"SHACK" HAS HISTORY
With its new manager, Mr. John
Wolfe, and its new redecorations, re
arrangement and additional booths,
the campus "Shack" is a surprise to
"new meats" who find its name rather
misleading. Here's how the Haskell
cafe got its nickname.
Years ago the "Shack" was an old
ramshackled cart pulled by a horse.
It was operated by students; and they
sold only sandwiches, candy, gum and
"pop." It had no tables or booths.
On special occasions such as football
games they drove the "Shack
to
the place and sold things there. Staff
members, who now eat regularly at
the "Shack," then ate upstairs in
Curtis hall.
Bernadine Agard, commercial, '48 and
Sammy Meyers were married this
summer and are living in Kansas
City, Mo.
Campus Guestbook: Maxine No
zhackum, '48, from Topeka, Kans....
S 2/c Harold Blalock, '46-47, from
Tuckerton, N. J....Mr. and Mrs.Vernon
Green, commercial '41, and their two
children from Washington D. C., where
Mr. Green works in the Public Health
department.

Members have

iasm already, she said. Two K.U. stu

international headquarters in Gen

Dewey Seeks Independent Vote,
Truman Blasts G.O.P. Smears,
As Candidates End Campaigns
_*
Republican Rally
Crowds 23,000
Into Garden
By Newbold Noyes, Jr.
Star Staff Correspondent
NEW 'YORK, Oct. 30—Gov.
Dewey wound up his presidential
campaign tonight with a bid for
the votes of independents and
doubtful Democrats.
He told more than 20.000 adherents,
in Madison Square Garden, that the
times call for better government—
“competent, warm-hearted, stable
government”—for America.
“Tonight,” he said, “I Invite every
citizen—wherever he lives, however
he works for a living, whatever his
party—Republicans. Democrats and
independents alike—to join with us
In this cause.
“I am grateful to the millions of
sincere Democrats and independents
who are for this victory. It is every
body's cause, for it means a better,
happier future for every American.”
Thousands Turned Away.
Thousands of New Yorkers, anx
ious for a look at the man they
think will be elected President next
Tuesday, were turned away from the
Garden when standing room space
and all seats were filled by 7 p.m.,
2'2 hours before the Republican
nominee began speaking.
While approaches to the building
teemed with would-be members of
the candidate's audience, an over
flow meeting for the disappointed
was ir. progress in Forty-ninth
screet, between Eighth and Ninth
avenues.
Police Commissioner Arthur W.
Wallander estimated that 23.000
persons crowded into the Garden:
that 125.000 saw' Gov. Dewey on the
way from his hotel to the arena,
and another 20.000 massed outside
the auditorium during the rally.
Gov. Dew'ey will make a short
radio talk Monday night, but to
night's address marked the real
end of his long drive for the presi
dency.
It contained no surprises. It was
a graceful, low-pitched little hymn
to God. country and teamwork, j
Sung against the crashing back
ground of a slambang. stops-out
VIRGINIA REPUBLICANS SEE
best chance to win since 1928.

PUTE polls indicating GOP vic

political rally, it had a strangely
unreal sound, like a harp playing
in a boiler factory.
v Show Lasts Six Hours.
The show was a good one—six
hours long. It began with an old
fashioned torchlight parade from
the headquarters of the Dewey
Warren Citizens’ Committee. 40 East
Forty-first street, one mile cross
town to the Garden. Marching,
throngs, burning red flares, and
more than 100 cars, trucks and
buses tied up New York traffic for
blocks. Senator Ives, Republican,
of New York, and the five Metro
politan County Republican chair
men occupied the lead cars.
The flag-drgped. floodlighted Gar
den building wras filled to over
flowing by 6:30. Behind the speak-]
ers platform, enormous pictures of
the Republican presidential and vice
presidential candidates flanked a
30-foot-wide model of the Capitol!
in Washington.
For four and a half hours, the
expectant and loudly applauding
audience was entertained with band
music, songs, vaudeville perf or’-1
mances and speeches. Political and
theatrical luminaries were intro
duced. Jinx Falkenberg and Gene
Tunney hit autographed tennis balls
into the crowd.
Speakers included Joe R. Hanley.
New York’s lieutenant governor and 1
Senator Ives. Thomas J. Curran,!
New York secretary of state, pre
sided.
Four-Minutt Ovation.
By the time Mr. Dewey, dressed
in a blue doublebreasted suit, step
ped onto the stage, the crowd was
In the mood. While the band blared
and the audience shrieked, stamped
and waved flags, he walked around
the stage shaking hands with the j
Republican leaders. It was 4 min
utes before the audience quieted
down enough for the candidate to
begin his address.
Thereafter, his words were lib
erally punctuated with the shouted
approval of his admirers, and he re
ceived another ovation at the end.

Dewey Victory Called Certain,
329 Electoral Votes Assured
Large Popular Vote May Help Republicans
Retain Control of Senate by Slim Margin
(The Star's Forecast of Vote by States on.Page A-5.)
By Gould Lincoln
Thomas E. Dewey will be elected President of the United
States Tuesday. He is expected to have not less than 329 votes in
the electoral college It takes 266 to elect.
The Dewey electoral vote may mount to nearly 400, depending
on how strong the Republican sweep may be. President Truman’s
electoral vote is not expected to go beyond 120-^-a'nd it may fall
short of this.
Gov. J. Strom Thurmond of South Carolina, the States’ flights
candidate, will have at least 39 electroal votes, and he may have
49. Henry A. Wallace, Progressive Party candidate, will receive no
electoral votes.
The American people are gambling for better Government, for
peace, for lower prices and taxes—none of which the Truman ad
ministration has been able to produce.
Senate is Greatest G.O.P. Hazard
Dewey will carry to victory, along with him, a marjority of the
House, probably with little change in size from the Republican
majority in the present Congress.
The Republicans face the greatest hazard in control of the
Senate. A large popular vote lead for Gov. Dewey, with many
“straight ticket” votes in a half dozen States where the Senatorial
races are close, will aid materially in retention of a slender
Republican margin in the upper house.
The probabilities are for a Republican Senate. The worst the
Republicans fear is a tie, with 48 Republicans and 48 Democrats.
So close, however, are a number of the Senate races that many
political observers are inclined to give the Senate to the Democrats.
Here is the lineup of electoral votes:
For Dewey, 329; leaning to Dewey, 35; total 364 electoral
votes. For Truman, 76; leaning to Truman, 44; total 120 electoral
votes. For Thurmond, 39 electoral votes.
Doubtful, Florida with 8 electoral votes.
Total vote fn the Electoral College, 531; necessary to elect, 266.
(A State-by-State breakdown of these figures is shown in
the accompanying chart on Page A-5.)
Four of the Democratic Presidential electors in Florida are
oh the ballot under Mr. Truman’s name and four under Gov.
Thurmond.
Named in the Truman column,"
and in the Thurmond column, are
also 4 elector candidates each with
no party designation. In the Dewey
and Wallace columns are 8 elector
candidates each.
If Tennessee is carried by Mr.
Truman, he will have 10 electoral
votes from that State. Two of the
Demopratic elector candidates are
pledged to Gov. Thurmond.
Trend Against Democrats.
In this election the great mass of
voters appears intent on putting the
Democratic administration out—as
inept and insufficient—and using
tne Republican Party and its can
didates as their vehicle. It is a vote
"against'’—which has been the case
in nearly every change of National
Government in the country's his
tory. It was so in 1932, when the
Democrats took over from the Re
publicans for a 16-year stretch in
office.
Aiding the Republicans, too. has
been a disastrous split in the Dem
ocratic ranks which suppsrted the
late President Roosevelt. Conserva
tive Democrats have left President
Truman In the South and Border
States because of his antisegrega
tion civil rights program and be-'
cause of his ultra New Dealism.
In the North and West, conserva
tive Democrats have left him on the
ground that he has gone overboard
for New Dealism. A radical wing

Tonight to Aid Truman
By th« Asiociatyd Prm
NEW YORK, Oct. 30 —Mrs. Elea
nor Roosevelt will speak on behalf
of the Truman-Barkley ticket over
a nation-wide American Broadcast
ing Co. hookup tomorrow night.
The 15-minute broadcast, spon
sored by the New York State Dem
ocratic Committee, will begin at 8:45
p.m.
Transit Riders Begin
Paying 13c Fare;
Tokens Are Back
Court Action Deadline
Passes Without Appeal
To Block Higher Rates
While the city slept this morn
ing, Capital Transit Co. street
car and bus operators filled their
changeholders with gilded tok
ens find began charging higher
fares.
The new fare schedules author
ized by the Public Utilities Com
mission went into effect at 5 a.m
the hour when streetcar and bus
riders are fewest
Large placards on cars and buses
informed riders of the new rates:
13 centf cash, three tokens for 35
cents, and $1.75 for the weekly
pass.
Transit Company officials could
not estimate how many persons
would be introduced to the new
fares today but pointed out that on
a normal Sunday they collect 500.
000 fares, and on weekdays approx
imately 1,000,000.
Other Fares Increased.
The increase in District fares
was accompanied by a boost of 3
cents on the connecting Capital
Transit routes with two Virginia
lines—The Washington, Virginia
and Maryland Coach Co., and the
Alexandria, Barcroft and Wash
ington Transit Co. Tomorrow fares
on the Silver Spring express (S-9)
line will go up 5 cents.
Threats of a court Injunction
to block institution of the new fare
schedules expired late yesterday
when the Americans for Democratic
Action and the Washington Com
mittee for Consumer Protection al

vision coverage will be combined

gram of election reporting Tues
day night.
All regular programs after 7:30

i by The Star’s radio stations—
WMAL, WMAL-FM and WMAL
TV—to make way for running
reports of the returns across the
Nation. The news stall of The
Star will join reporters and com
mentators of the American
Broadcasting Co. in preparing
and presenting the returns.
To assist radio listeners in fol
lowing the election results a* they
take shape, Tuesday’s editions of
The Star will carry a “score card”

tered as broadcast,

President Makes
Fighting Speech
To 'Hom’e Folks'
By Joseph A. Fox
Star Staff Correspondent
ST. LOUIS, Oct. 30.—The Tru
man prosecution rested tonight
in the “case of the people vs. the
special interests.”
Ending his 30,000-mile drive for
(votes (the President still calls it
22,000 and refuses to give the June
junket to the West Coast the bene
fit of political paternity), Mr. Tru
man came back to Missouri to make
his closing plea for support to home
folks.
With the exception of one-day s
I run in upstate New York, the Presi
dent's weather luck had been good
throughout the campaign. But it
deserted him as he hit his home
State. St. Louis glistened under a
thin drizzle of rain as he drove—
in an open car—the five blocks from
the railroad station to Kiel audi
torium for his speech.
Crowds Ar# Sparse.
Crowds were thin along the route
but the Chief Executive received a
cheering welcome from 13,000 Mis
sourians packed into the hall where
Gov. Phil Donnelly, a Democrat,
introduced him as “the next Presi
dent of the United States.”
In the auditorium—named for a
Republican mayor who for years was
a hair shirt to Missouri Democrats
—the President returned his final
indictment against the GOP, assail
ing the legislative record of the
80th Congress and the refusal of
“the Republican candidate” to “tell
where he stands.”
It was a fighting talk In which
he virtually threw away remarks he
had prepared in advance for de
livery, and it embraced attacks on
what he called the “McCormick
Patterson saboteurs” who he said
conducted a "smear campaign”
against him.
Calls Campaign ‘Vile
This "smear campaign,” he said,
started in the McCormick-Patterson
newspapers immediately after he
submitted hi* first 31-point domes
tic program to Congress in the fall
of 1945. He called the campaign
“vile” and said the newspapers’
writers took it up and wrote crit
icisms of him, not because they be
lieved them "but because they were
paid to do it.” y
The candidate made his big pitch
for Missouri’s 15 electoral voles,
with a denunciation of the Repub
lican record on farm and J*roor
legislation—parity prices aad'Taft
Hartley Act—then, he swung back
to his oft-repeated victory theme,
with a prediction that the Demo
crats would sweep the country Tues
day. It jvas obvious that Mr. Tru
man was thinking in terms of the
people who have turned out to see
him in the 36-state route he has
covered in the past 5 months.
Says Figures Came From People.
"People ask me where I get my
figures,” he said. "I get them from
the best possible sources—from the
people themselves."
As for the campaign of Gov.
Dewey who has been trailing Mr.
Truman for the last week, the Presi
dent repeated his charge that the
GOP nominee is depending on plati
tudes and double talk" to win, and
then told his audience “a fable." He
said:
“Centuries ago there was a poli
tician who lived in a palace which
today would be called Albany. One
day the people gathered in the
square in front of the palace and
called on him to enlighten them.
“He came out and raised his haftcl j
and said: ‘Do you know what I am
about to say to you?’
“The people, afraid of offending
the politician, said: '‘Oh. yes, we
know.’
“Well,” said the politician, “if you
know what I am about to say, there
is no need for me to speak.” And
then he went back into the palace.”
People Came Back.
“The people were disappointed,
for they expected big things,” the
President said.
So, the next day, according to
Mr. Truman, the crowd came again
and the politician appeared a second
time.
This time, the President’s story
went, the people were asked if they
knew what the politician in the
“Albany” palace was about to say.
‘“No, we don’t know,’ they were
quoted as replying.
‘Well,’ the politician was said to
have replied, ‘if you are that igno
rant, it is not proper that I should
speak',” the President recounted.
And the politician, Mr. Truman

Fairfax Girl, 8, Killed by Car
As She Runs to Meet Father
An 8-year-old Fairfax County girl
was fatally injured yesterday when
struck by a car in front of her
home on Route 50 a half-mile west
of Chantilly, Va. t
The girl, Beatrice Fay Hockman,
daughter x>f Mr. and Mrs. Alfred
D. Hockman of R. F. D. 1, Herndon,
was hit by the automobile as she
ran across the road to meet her
father. She was taken to Arling
ton Hospital where she died two
hours after the accident.
The driver, William J. Beall, 51,
of 1317 Ritchie place N.E., was
booked on a technical charge of
reckless driving and freed under
$500 li>nd pending a hearing in
Fairfax County Police Court No
vember 13.
Dr. W. C. Welburn,, Arlington
Couqty coroner, said he would hold
an inquest today. The hospital said
the child suffered multiple head*,
body and internal injuries. She
died at 6:15 pjn.

Acid Smog Kills 17,
Hospitals Jammed in
Pennsylvania Town
400 Believed Stricken
By Smothering Fumes;
Pittsburgh Rushes Aid
By the Associated Pratt
DONORA, Pa., Oct. 30.—The
board of health reported 17 per
sons died in this bustling mill
town today and that their deaths
were caused by a smothering
smog containing acid fumes.
Charles Stacey, chairman of the'
board, added: “We're up in arms
about it. We re gojpg to appeal to
State health authorities for help."
Mrs. Cora Vernon, executive di
rector of the American Red Cross,
said an emergency hospital was set
up and several persons treated.
Hospitals in the area, including the
Charleroi-Monessen hospital at
nearby Charleroi, were jammed to
capacity.
400 To 500 Persons 111.
“I would say there’s at least 400
or 500 persons sick from the smog
effects,” Mr. Stacey told the As
sociated Press. "However, I don't
think the situation will become
more critical—I believe it’s all un
der control now.”
Mr. Stacey helleved the smog
caused by a mixture of fog and in
dustrial smoke—contained fumes
coming from the plant of the
American Steel Wire Corp., a
United States Steel subsidiary.
Spokesmen for the company were
not available for comment.
Describing the smog, Mr. Stacey
continued:
"I’ve never seen anything quite
like it. We've had smog before but
today the situation really got bad
and it was difficult to breathe.”
Mr. Stacey said 15 deaths had
been reported earlier in the day but
two more persons had died tonight.
Believes Smog Is Lifting.
"All of them were directly attrib
utable to the smog as far as we
can determine,” he declared. "How
ever, there's one bright spot—the
smog seems to be lifting now and
I don’t believe there will be a great
number of additional fatalities.”
Mr. Stacey said all of today’s
deaths were attributable to the
smoggy conditions, adding:
"Ordinarily we only have about
two deaths a week here.”
Ear lief Rudy Schwerha, a member
of the health board, said nurses
and doctors were having difficulty
“keeping up with the situation.”
“The deaths seem to have hit
people who were suffering from
cardiac or asthmatic conditions,"
Mr. Schwerha said. "There’s no
doubt the smog has been the cause.”
James Lau, superintendent of
the Charleroi-Monessen Hospital,
said extra beds have been placed in
the corridors to accommodate pa
tients. He added:
"We can’t take another patient.
All day long I’ve been shuttling
back and forth between the hospital
and Pittsburgh getting extra equip
ment. We've got 11 oxygen tents in
operation tonight, compared to our
usual 3.”
Victims Died in Homes.
Supt. Lau explained that all vic
tims died at their homes early to
day. He said most of the victims
or their families apparently didn't
realize the seriousness of the situa
tion immediately.
One funeral home reported it had
received eight bodies within as
many hours. The other bodies were
taken to funeral homes In nearby
communities.
Mrs. Vernon, who gave the death
toll as 15, praised the small-town
doctors who are "doing everything
in their power to combat the emer
gency.”
Dr. A. S. Sickman, chief of staff
at the Charleroi-Monessen Hospital,
said medical authorities are inves
tigating to determine if fumes from
plants in the vicinity have anything
to do with the situation.
“At the moment, however, we're
concentrating on helping the sick,"
(See SMOG, Page A-9.)
Fog Halts Air Traffic
And Delays fcfotprists
Automobile traffic flowed to a
snail’s pace and all air traffic In and
out of Washington halted shortly
after 11 o’clock last night when
heavy fog blanketed the city.
Visability was recorded as “zero
zero” at the airport at 11:22 pun.
In some sections of the city the
fog was so thick motorists could not
see two car-lengths ahead.
Bandit Slays Two, Youths Shot
In Gunfight With Police Here
Theater Manager and Bystander Shot to Death
As Robber Sprays Street With Automatic Rifle
Two men were slain apd three
youths wounded, last night as
a fleeing holdup man sprayed a
street with automatic rifle fire
in a gun battle with three
policemen.
Police estimated more than 40
shots were fired near the intersec
tion of Second and F streets S.W.
The dead:
James E. Washington, 52, colored,
manager of the Rosalia Theater,
218 F street S.W., who was walking
toward his parked car with the day's
receipts from the theater.
Herbert Smith, 25, also colored, of
1224 Seventh street NW„ a by
stander.
The wounded:
Carl Shedrick, 10, of 829 Delaware
avenue S.W, whose right leg was
broken by a bullet.
Richard Lee Jefferson, 13, of 620

4,C J District Pupils
To Get New Treatment
Against Tooth Decay
Sodium Fluoride Method
To Be Demonstrated at
9 Schools' in November
By Coit Hendley, Jr.
The District Health Depart
ment will conduct a demonstra
tion of the new sodium fluoride
treatment, which cuts tooth de- j
cay in children by at least 40!
per cent, on approximately 4,000*
elementary school pupils during
November.
Dr. A. Harry Ostrow. chief of the
Health Department's dental section,
said the program will get underway
about November 15 in nine elemen
tary schools.
A traveling unit furnished b/ the
j United States Public Health Service
will do the work under direction of
1 Dr. Ostrow. The unit will be com
i posed of a dentist, two dental hy
gienists and a clerk.
Approval Expected.
Approval of the program still has
'to be obtained from the Board of
Education. The proposal will be
presented by School Supt. Hobart
M. Corning next week and un
doubtedly will be approved.
Schools to be used in the demon
stration are Bancroft, Barnard,
Bunker Hill, Key, Kingsman, Lang
don, Oyster, Raymond and Buch
anan.
The sodium fluoride treatment is
very simple, Dr. Ostrow said. The
child’s teeth are cleaned thoroughly
and a 2 per cent solution of sodium
fluoride is applied to the crown
surface of each tooth and allowed
to dry.
A total of four applications are
made at intervals of three days to
a week for maximum protection.

First Shipload of DPs
Lands in New York;
813 Start New Life
Emigrants Quickly Pass
Final Inspection; Clark,
McGrath at Reception
By Chalmers M. Roberts
Star Staff Correspondent
NEW YORK, Oct. 30.—A new
life of hope and freedom began
today for 813 displaced persons,
the first of 205,000 who will come
to these shores before mid-1950.
They rode up New York harbor
on the Army transport General
Black, watching for those familiar
landmarks they had often heard of,
but despaired of ever seeing. Today
they were real—the Statue of Lib
erty, the Manhattan skyline and
New York itself, port of entry into
the new world.
The Black docked at ►mid-Man
hattan pier just before 5 p.m. and
in a couple of hours the DPs had
passed their last inspection—cus
toms and immigration—and became
legal residents of the United States
of America.
“We really made it!” was the col
lective remark of these Europeans
i who today are almost all fleeing
Stalin and the Soviet Union. They
were able to come to America be
cause Hitler and the Nazis had
brought them into Germany during
thq war as slave labor.
“So Much It Overwhelms.”
Thousands of other ships have
steamed or sailed to these shores,
bringing almost 40,000,000 emigrants
to America since the Nation was
founded. But the General Black
was unique, for this is the begin
ning of an official gesture by the
United States to help solve the
problem of Europe's more than
530.000 homeless.
The DPS began to spread out
quickly over the United States.
Many are remaining in New York
(See DPs, Page A-4.)
Heated Ramps to Replace Steps
Outside New U. S. Buildings
New standards adopted for Fed
eral buildings of the future include
such inovations at> elimination of
outdoor steps, and removal of snow
and ice from ramps by radiant heat.
If steps are desired, the Federal
Works Agency announced yesterday,
they will be placed within the build
ing whenever possible.
Where outside ramps are exposed
to snow and ice, it'is proposed to
install piping for under-surface heat
ing to remove the slipping or skid
ding hazard during winter weather.
Such changes in design, to sim
plify and achieve economy in con
struction and maintenance, were
developed by conferences between
Maj. Gen. Philip B. Fleming, Fed
eral Works Administrator, and Pub
lic Buildings Commissioner W. E.
Reynolds.
New simple office furniture for

Federal buildings such as post offices
and courthouses has been designed
to reduce not only the first cost, but
also the maintenance, Mr. Reynolds
said. Old-fashioned ornament and
mouldings have disappeared, he ex
plained, and clean lines have
emerged to produce "more beautiful
and more efficient office tools.”
Every precaution against accident
will be taken, it was explained. In
addition to elimination of out-door
steps it is proposed to develop a type
of windows that can be cleaned from
the inside, instead of the outside.
Colors will be used to identify safe
or hazardous conditions in connec
tion with pipes in walls, telephone
wiring and ducts.
Standardization of equipment for
postofflee lobby windows, counters
and letter drops will be introduced,
to provide for mass production and
easier alterations.
Fall of Mukden
Feared Deciding
Blow in China
Chiang's Effort to Beat
Reds on Nation-wide
Scale Believed Lost
By th« Associated Press
NANKING, Oct. 30. Mukden
collapsed into Chinese Commu
nist hands today in the most
stunning government loss of the
three-year civil war, reliable
sources in this fearful capital re
ported tonight.
Many high Chinese and foreign
sources declared the civil war had
been decided with the fall of the
great Manchurian city. Quick re
tirement from the remaining small
holdings in southernmost Man
churia was predicted.
Bitter fighting may continue in
China-proper for months or even
years, the sources said, but Gen
eralissimo Chiang Kai-shek's effort
to defeat the Communists on a na
tion-wide scale has died. The
latest setback brought speculation
on the possibility of replacing
China's long-time leader.
Anarchic Confusion Reported.
Some unofficial quarters said the
Communists completely occupied
Mukden, swollen to nearly 2,000,000
population by refugees.
Later and equally credible re
reperts near midnight said the en
circling Communist armies had not
yet taken full charge but the last
government officials fled by car at
3 p.m„ leaving the city in anarchic
confusion.
(Associated Press Correspond
ent Spencer Moosa reported from
Peiping the Nationalist airforce
withdrew from Mukden. Public
radio communication with the
city ceased.
(Reports received in Peiping
said Communist posters in Muk
den urged the populace to calmly
await the arrival of the Reds.
The posters bore the seal of Gen.
Tso-hsiang, one of the most
prominent men in Manchuria
before Japan s invasion in 1931.)
Garrison Already Gone.
Recent pro government reports
have indicated the bulk of Muk
den’s 150,000-man garrison already
had left, attempting to fight its way
southwest or to escape via the South
Manchurian port of Yingkow, 115
miles from Mukden.
The government had no confir
mation of Communist claims to de
struction of' 12 divisions of this
force two days ago, but the official
denials sounded half-hearted.
(The Communists said 10 of
these divisions ware American
equipped and were the cream of
Chiang’s army. The government
has kept silent about the makeup
and equipment of its forces.)
Whereabouts of Cfen. Wei Li
huang, Manchurian commander, was
unknown.
The government still has hundreds
of thousands of troops under arms,
but the best quarters question their
will to defend themselves, let alone
to attack.
Some observers thought this pic
ture was painted too dark, and
banked on a possible lull during
which Gen. Chiang could pull his
shocked forces together.
Chiang Flies to Nanking.
Gen. Chiang, the president and
for more than 20'years the strong
man of China, flew back to Nanking
tonight from Peiping, his two-week
effort to recoup his northern mili
tary fortunes a failure.
This evidently strained and weary
man—now 62 years old—alone can
answer the question of what comes
next. Most reliable sources said his
return foretokened “drastic devel
opments.”
Official and diplomatic circles
buzzed with speculation whether he
might move his capital south to
Canton or even west to war-famous
Chungking.
Nanking is more than 750 miles
jby air south of Mukden, and Com
jmunist raids never have reached
I nearer than 100 miles north.
Tf riefections which have cost the
government so dearly in the past

Endurance Flyers Fail
Again After 92 Hours
By the Associated Press
PALMDALE, Calif., Oct. 30.—Two
California flyers failed today in their
second attempt to establish a new
endurance flight record.
Bill Barns and Dick Riedel said
they were forced to land because
ice formed in the carburetor.
They had kept their monoplane,
jSunkist Lady, in the air 92 hours
land 10 minutes in their second try.
Their first attempt, during which
they stayed aloft 106 hours, ended
October 24 when they were grounded
by fog. Mr. Barris and Mr. Riedel,
who are trying to break the record
of 726 hours set in 1939, said they
will make another attempt.
'Big 3' Elevens
In Clean Sweep;
Bowl Choices Win
Notre Dame, Michigan and North
Carolina—the Big Three of 1948 col
lege football—rolled to new triumphs
yesterday. All are undefeated and
untied thus far.
Notre Dame, led by Halfback Emil
Sitko, pulverized Navy, 41 to 7, be
fore 63.314 fans in Baltimore. "Lady
Luck” helped Michigan defeat stub
born Illinois, 28 to 20, and Charlie
Justice led North Carolina to vic
tory over Tennessee, 14 to 7.
The current favorites for the Rose
Bowl won, Northwestern defeating
Ohio State, 21 to 7, and California
downing Southern Cal, 13 to 7.
George Washington lost, 33 to 14,
to Lafayette at Easton, Pa. Howard
U. defeated Shaw University, 14 o
6. here. Georgia Tech defeated D~. j,
19'to 7: Georgia belted Alabama, 35
to 0; Army crushed Virginia Tech,
49 to 7; and Penn State routed Col
gate, 32 to 13.

Glenwood School
Forms Chapter
Of Beta Club
Plans for establishing a local
chapter of the National Beta Club,
a service-leadership organization for
Tiigh school students of America,
were made last Friday, at Glenwood
high school, according to W. P.
Whitesides, principal.
The National Beta club, with ap
proximately 1,550 local chapters
and over 36,000 members in high
schools in 16 states, is now in its
fifteenth year, and has been in con
tinuous operation since 1933.
Objectives of this non secret,
leadership-service organization are:
to encourage effort, to promote
character, to stimulate achievement
among its members and to encour
age and assist students to continue
their education after high school
graduation.
Sponsor for the organization will
be elected at the first meeting of
the club.
Miss Helen Thompson was ap
pointed chairman of the organiza
tion committee. Other members of
the committee are: Miss Willette
Holland and James Haney.
Permanent officers for this year
will be elected after an installation
meeting to be held at an early date.
A special program is being planned
for this occasion.
The local organization will be
composed of approximately 15 stu
dents who have fulfilled the require
ments necessary for membership in
the National Beta Club.

Teachers Outline
Legislative Program
Raleigh, Oct. 6.—The legislative
committee of the North Carolina
Education association announced to
day its suggested NCEA legislative
program for 1948-49.
The program has to be submitted
to the NCEA board of directors and
is yet to be discussed at each of the
association's six district meetings.
The program includes these pro
posals:
Teacher load Reduction of
teacher load to 30 pupils.
Salaries Minimum of $2,400 a
year for beginning teachers holding
"A" certificates andv 12 increments
of at least $100 a year for teachers
holding "A" certificates; minimum
of $2,900 for teachers holding grad
uate certificates with two years' ex
perience and 11 increments of at
least $100 a year for teachers hold
ing graduate certificates; propor
tionate increases for other profes
sional personnel and "adequate" in
creases for other school personnel.
Sick leave—10 days a year, on a
cumulative basis.
Health—Expanded health service
and health education for school chil
dren.
School construction—State aid in
construction and maintenance of
school building.
Attendance—An "effective" sys
tem for enforcement of the com
pulsory attendance law.

School, according to Richard A.
Shaw, principal.

proximately 1550 local chapters and
36,000 members in high schools of

year and has been in continuous
operation since 1933.

leadership-service organization are:
to encourage effort, to promote
character, to stimulate achievement
among its members and to encour
age and assist students to continue
their education after high school
graduation.

meeting to be held at an early date.

Thurmond Reception
At Charlottesville
Surprises Observers
By John V. Horner
Star Staff Correspondent
CHARLOTTESVILLE, Va., Oct. 8.
—Gov. Thurmond of South Carolina,
who told a States’ Rights audience
here last night that "no true Amer
ican” can support major proposals
of his opponents in the presidential
race, set out today in quest of votes
at Richmond, heart of Virginia’s
conservative Democratic organiza
tion.
On the third day of an intensive
campaign to capture the State’s 11
electoral votes, the States’ Rights
candidate was to deliver an address
at the Atlantic Rural Exposition
and to dine with political leaders
intimately associated with potent
Byrd-Tuck machines.
Although neither Senator Byrd j
nor Gov. Tuck has announced which
candidate he is supporting, the im
plications are strong in the com
pany Gov. Thurmond is keeping.
He and Mrs. Thurmond will be j
guests of Gov. and Mrs. Tuck at
breakfast tomorrow.
Reaction Is Mixed.
The reaction to Gov. Thurmond's
two-fisted speech last night at the
University of Virginia was mixed.
But' there was universal surprise
at the enthusiasm engendered
among the 1,400 students and towns
people who packed Cabell Hall. The
University of Virginia is not known
for tumultuous receptions to visit
ing speakers.
Former Gov. Colgate W. Darden,
Jr., university president, and over
night host to the Thurmonds, said
the crowd was the largest he had
ever seen in Cabell Hall for any oc
casion. It was considerably larger
than the turnout early last Satur
day for President Truman on his
special campaign train.
It was a thoughtful, attentive
group, about three-fourths students
—some, of course, too young to vote.
During the speech there was a
minimum of quiet heckling among
student friends, but this was not'
audible on stage.
Impromptu Attack Cheered.
The audience followed Gov. Thur
mond with restraint and applauded
respectfully during his prepared ad
dress on States’ Rights. But when,
at the end. he put aside his script
and launched into the fighting, im
promptu attack on President Tru
man, Gov. Dewey and Henry A.
Wallace, the crowd roared its ap
proval.
They gave out with thunderous
applause and shouts when he
summed up:
“The minority groups have made
a stooge of Henry Wallace, a mouth
piece of Harry Truman and a pup
pet prince of Tom Dewey.”
Says Truman Bolted Party.
They also demonstrated when
Gov. Thurmond promised that,
after the election, the States’ Rights
Democrats—not the pro-Truman
forces—would cull the "bolters”
from the ranks of the party. The
States’ Righters, he said, will see to
the "rebirth” of the party and its
restoration to “fundamental prin
ciples.”
“It’s Truman and his gang who
bolted the Democratic Party,” the
South Carolinian shouted, "not
those of us who are standing for
the rights of the States.
“The political bosses didn’t expect
the South to resist this assault
(Civil Rights legislation) upon the
Southern States,” Gov. Thurmond
told the audience. “In their mad
haste the bosses forgot that the
Southern people have a history of
loyalty to principles reaching back
to Ijhe very birth of the Democratic
Party.
"Now they know the error of their
ways. Now that they know the
strength of purpose of the States’
Rights movement, they are growling
in their anger like the dog that
didn’t get the bone.”
"Now let me tell you this,” he

said in typical political orator
fashion. The crowd tittered and he
yelled into the jnicrophone:
“Harry Truman has never been
elected President of the United
States and he never will be
elected!”
Practically everybody screamed
approval.
"And let me tell you this: Tom
Dewey can have PEPC in New York
State if he Wants it, but he better
not try to force it on the rest of
the States pf the union.”
Passes Wallace By.
"Now. what about. Henry Wal
lace?” he demanded.
Hisses and boos filled the air.
“Let’s just pass him by,” Gov.
Thurmond said cryptically. And a
storm of applause, lasting fully a
minute, greeted the remark.
In a blast aV Federal bureaucracy,
which he said was a product of the
shift in power from the States to
Washington, Gov. Thurmond de
clared that hundreds of billions of
dollars could be saved by govern
mental efficiency with the possible
reduction of one third in Federal
employes.
I His meeting here was sponsored
by the University’s Young Demo
cratic Club, which was organized
two weeks ago and has a member
ship of 250. Although a majority
of members are from Southern
States, its president, Earle Ellis of
Dania, Fla., said many reside in
the North, and other aections out
side the South.
The Thurmond supporters wore
red, white and blue campaign but
tons. They had strung up a States’
Rights banner across Main street
and before last night’s meeting,
Cecil Kilpatrick, 4120 Forty-second
street N.W., Washington, and Wil
liam Baylor, Richmond,' cruised
around town in a sound truck urg
ing attendance at the rally.
Some Student Critics.
Some of the students criticized
Gov. Thurmond's attitude towards
FEPC. These included Thor Ander
sen of Brooklyn, who said he is
a Republican, and Arthur Hawkins
of New York City, a young Demo
crat, who was stronger for States’
Rights before the meeting than
after it. But Carter E. McIntosh
of Conneaut, Ohio, and Dan Myers
of Haddonsfield, N. J., were 100
per cent for the speech. Mr. Myers
said he comes from a Republican
family.
One Charlottesville woman who
said she had planned to vote for
Gov. Dewey, disclosed that she was
converted to the Thurmond cause.
“But I do wish that Mrs. Truma^
would take off her hat,” she said.
“She would look so much better.
Oh, oh, did I say Mrs. Truman?
I mean Mrs. Thurmond.”
By and large the Charlottesville
appearance seemed to help the
States’ Rights ticket, particularly]
among Democrats unwilling to vote
for Mr. Truman and unenthusiastta
about Gov. Dewey.
The Thurmond party came
to Charlottesville from Lexington,
where the candidate spoke to the
students of Washington and Lee
University. Persons who attended
both college speeches said that, if
anything, the more Southern Lex
ington student body responded more
warmly.

Forrestal to Unveil Plaque
To Protestant Chaplain Dead
New headquarters of the General Commission on Chaplains
j at 122 Maryland avenue N.E. —Del Ankers Photo.
A plaque to the memory of
Protestant chaplains who died in
both World Wars wjll be unveiled
at noon November 3 by Secretary of
Defense Porrestal during the dedi
cation of a new headquarters for the
General Commission on Chaplains.
The headquarters building, at 122
i Maryland avenue NJE., is the former
home of the late Senator Hiram
Johnson of California.
The dedication will be held in
connection with the semiannual
meeting of the General Commission,
which processes applications for
Army, Navy and Veterans Adminis
stration chaplains’ bureaus. The
commission also serves as a liaison
agent between military chaplains,
churches and the Government. Es
tablished during World War I, it rep
resents 49 Protestant denominations.
Commission headquanters were
formerly in the Woodward Building,
Fifteenth and H streets N.W.
A memorial to “those ministers
of religion who as chaplains gave
their lives in service to God and
country,” the building is opposite the
Supreme Court Building. Names of
134 chaplains will be engraved on
the plaque.
The headquarters also houses the
editorial* and circulation offices of
The Chaplain and the Link, religious
publications edited by Delmar L.
Dyreson, associate director of the
commission. Dr. Joseph C. Hazen
is conflnission chairman. He will
preside at the meeting at 9:30 a.m.,
prior to the dedication.
Among those invited to attend the
dedication are Secretary of the
Army Royall, Maj. Gen. Luther D.
Miller, Army Chief of Chaplains;
Shore Society to Elect
Officers of the Eastern Shore So
ciety of Washington will be elected
at a dinner meeting at 6:45 pjn.
Tuesday In the Fairfax Hotel, 2100
Massachusetts avenue N.W. Also
on the program will be two moving
pictures, and music by Martha
Hedges, pianist, and Cecelia Hodges,
violinist.

of Health School for Handicapped

school.

Carl R. Gray, and Dr. Frederick E.
Reissig, executive secretary of the
Washington Federation of Churches.

Fort Dupont Legion Post
Plans Officer Installation
A parade from the south .end of
Sousa Bridge to 8t. Francis Xavier’s
School, 2700 O street S.E., will pre
cede the Installation of officers of
Fort Dupont Post 49, American Le
gion, at 8 pjn. Tuesday in the school
auditorium.
The Marine Drum and Bugle
Corps, the Elks Band, Citizens’ As
sociations and Legionnaires will
march in the parade led by Capt.
George H. Rothe, grand marshall.
Officers to be installed are: Law
rence B. Taylor, commander; Harry
A. Hampton, George G. Blackwell
and James L. Donato, vice com
manders ;-Jenevieve Sparshott, chap
lain; Howard D. Cramer, sergeant
at-arms; Charles B. Chronlnger,
quartermaster; Arthur W. Wood
worth, Judge advocate, and Charles
P. Weiss, historian.
Eastern High Students
To See Special Drill
A special demonstration of drill
formations by the 2nd Battalion of
the 3rd Infantry Regiment of Fort
Lesley J. McNair will take place
before the student body of Eastern
High School at 9 a.m. Tuesday.
The 35 white-helmeted Infantry
will be led by Lt. Fred Archibald
while Lt. Robert D. Glaser explains
the various evolutions to the stu
dents over a loud speaker system.
Tacoma Lessons Help
British Build Bridge
•y th« Auociatcd Pr«»
LONDON, Oct. 28.—A group of
British engineers, planning to build
the world’s largest suspension bridge
over the Severn River, are taking a
lesson from faults in the Tacoma
(Wash.) bridge which collapsed
eight years ago.
Scientists of the Department of
Scientific and Industrial Research,
a Government-sponsored body, have
tested model bridges in a wind
tunnel, reproducing the vibrations
which doomed the Tacoma bridge.
New designs for deck construc
tion, incorporating ideas used in
building airplane wings, now are
being tested and will be incorpo
rated in a scale model of the Severn
bridge. Details of the new designs
have not been published, but the
Ministry of Transport says they are
able to withstand winds up to 140
miles an hour. The Tacoma bridge

'Born-Again' Businessmen Hold
3-Day Evangelical Session Here
Some of the country’s big businessmen are leaders of the
30,000 "born-again” business evangelists in annual convention j
lat the Mayflower, Seen here are three founders of the move
ment. They are (left to right), Arnold Grunigen, jr., vice
chairman; R. G. LeTourneau, chairman, and Dr. N. A. Jepson.
—Star Staff Photo.
Started 11 years ago In Chicago
and headed for the last eight years
by a hard-fisted Texas industrialist,
the Christian Business Men's Com
mittee, an international organiza
tion, is one of the most unusual
evangelical groups ^ver to meet here.

Western Allies Cut Off
Gas Mains to Hall
Drains by Russians
•y th* Atiocisttd Frau
BERLIN, Oct. 23.—Large areas of
the Russian sector of Berlin were
without gas today after the Western
powers shut the mains at sector bor
der* to prevent seepage eastward
of supplies produced by works in
blockaded Western Berlin.
American spokesmen said the em
bargo against Eastern Berlin was
imposed for two reasons:
1. Oas pressure in Soviet mains
was so low because of gas plant
repairs undertaken there that sup
plies from hard-pressed Western
Berlin were being drained off.
2. Reprisal for firing of German
non-Cori!munist employes at head
offices of the city's gas works, which
are located in the Soviet sector. The
repairs in Soviet sector mains were
understood to be necessary because
of equipment damaged by the sub
stitution of Silesian coal in the place
of Ruhr coal, for which the plants
had been adapted.
Few Plants in Soviet Sector.
Russian supplies of Ruhr coal
have been cut off by the Western
allies coiinterblockade imposed in
reprisal for the Soviet land blockade
of Berlin.
The biggest gas works in the city
are located in the American, British
and French sectors. The Russian
sector, even if all its plants were in
top repair, could hardly produce
enough gas to maintain a normal
service.
Some Russian sector residents saW
the Soviets had promised them coal
rations to make up for the gas de
ficiency.
Germans Release 27.
Western Berlin also has been un
der severe gas rationing since the
Russians imposed their blockade in
June because all coal must now be
flown in.
Headquarters of Western Berlin’s
German police announced, mean
while, that the Communists had
released the .remaining 27 of 47
Western police and plainclothesmen
arrested during last month’s City
Hall riots. The release of the lint
20 was announced Thursday.

COMMUNITY PROJECT—A new venture in community recreation gets under way at Chevy Chase.
Miss Betty Maggio (left), recreation director of the Community Center, helps with the planning
at the old Brown School. —Star Staff Photo.
Chevy Chase Teen Core Club
Votes 'Smoking' in New Center
Nearly 90 teen-agers organized
;i the Teen Core Club yesterday at
1 the Chevy Chase Community Center
and got right down to business on
whether they will permit them
1 selves to smoke in the new recrea
tion center.
For two years citizens of the
CheVy Chase area have worked to
i turn the old E. V. Brown School.
McKinley street and Connecticut
avenue N.W., into a recreation cen
ter. Their eflofts were completed
recently and the young people
i wasted no time in moving in.
The teen-agers will be self-gov
erned and will hold their own
i “courts” for those who break their
rules.
i At yesterday’s organization meet
1 ing each boy and girl stood up, gave
his name and the school in order
that they could ‘‘get to know
each other.” Then the smoking
question was taken up.
By hand vote they decided to
allow smoking in the canteen room
1 where dances will be held and the
television set is installed.
Miss Betty Maggio, 23, In charge
of teen-agers’ activities and special
events, warned them about dropping
ashes on the floor, and said:
“We ll try it for a week and see
how it goes.”
The group voted to “be on their
honor” about smoking at the center
if they're not allowed to ^gmoke at
home.
Mississippi College Fire
Causes Million Damage
By the Associated Press
STATE COLLEGE, Miss., Oct. 21.—
A million-dollar fire has destroyed
40-year-old Lee Hall, main admin
istration building of Mississippi State
College.
Two walls of the four-story struc
ture crumbled here yesterday as fire
men from three towns fought the
blaze for nearly two hours.
Hundreds of students scrambled
in and out of the blazing structure
to help save equipment and records.
1 L. F. Mallory, college controller,
said it was understood the cause
was a flaming tar bucket atop the
structure where workmen were re
pairing the roof.
Jack Flowers, public relations
officer, said replacement of the
building would cost $1,000,000.

Burma Frees U. S. Flyer
After Month Detention
ly th« Associated Press f
RANGOON, Burma, Oct. 21.—Of
ficials said today that an American
flyer has been released from jail
after being held a month under sus
picion of unlawful activity in Burma.
Authorities arrested Frank J.
Greigware, 41, of Chicago, 111., after
he arrived from Bangkok in his own
plane.
They said Greigware is now being
questioned about illegal entry into
the country.

Wallace Emphasizes
Plans to Keep Party
Together for 1952
DETROIT, Oct. 18.—Henry Wal
lace, looking toward the 1955 elec
tion, moves his presidential cam
paign to Pittsburgh today.
He was still striving for every
possible vote for his Progressive
Party on Nov. 2, but he also was
taking every opportunity to ham
mer home this message:
"No matter what the vote is, on
November 2, I am not going to quit
this fight. This party is going to
keep right on going!”
Mr. Wallace bore down heavy on
that theme yesterday at three meet
ings in Detroit and Flint, Mich.,
fighting to offset the Anti-Wallace
leadership of the CIO United Auto
Workers.
UAW Reject Wallace.
That union, headed by Walter
Reuther, has rejected Mr. Wallace’s
third party and announced plant
to seek the formation of a “genuinely
progressive” third party after the
November election.
Last night in the Detroit Music
Hall, before a singing, cheering
crowd of nearly 2,000 which in
cluded many members of the anti
Reutber faction of the UAW, Mr.
Wallace blazed away at future third
parties.
He said "the time is now, not
tomorrow.’’
He said, “it is a queer kind of
thinking that says: Lock the polit
ical barn door after the election has
been stolen.”
Steelworkers’ Home Base.
In Pittsburgh, the Progressive
Party candidate finds himself at the
home base of another huge CIO
union which—like the UAW_re
jected him and came out for Presi
dent Truman’s re-election. This is
the CIO Steelworkers Union, headed
by Philip Murray, who also is presi
dent of the CIO.
The Wallace campaigners passed
a strangely assorted week end, flying
from the North to the South and
back again.
Saturday they flew 1,300 miles in
their chartered plane, starting in
Chicago and winding up in Detroit.
All this traveling was for the pur
pose of making one speech in Dal
ton, Ga. There Mr. Wallace ap
peared at a swinging, swaying,
stomping revival-type meeting spon
sored by the Church of God of the
Union Assembly.
500 in Arts and Sciences
Pledge Wallace Support
NEW YORK. Oct. 18 <£>).—About
500 men and women in the arts,
sciences and professions yesterday
pledged support of the presidential
candidacy of Henry A. Wallace.
Sculptor Jo Davidson, honorary
chairman of the National Council
of the Arts, Sciences and Profes
signs, an independent organization,
made the announcement.
The group sadi it supported Mr.
Wallace because "inherent in his
program” is “full realization of
progress and freedom for the people
of this Nation.”

A Navy-Notre Dame Victory

New Railroad Unit
Of Reserves Activated
The Fourth Military Railway
Service was activated last night as a
unit of the Army’s Organized Re
serve Corps, during a dinner in the
Hotel Statler.
The group, to be composed mainly
of employes of Southeastern rail
roads, is commanded by Col. Fred
IW. Okie, president of the Union
Railroad Co. of Pittsburgh. Present
at the ceremony were most of the 46
reserve officers who will constitute
the headquarters officer personnel
of the unit. Enlisted personnel
necessary to operate a railroad sys
tem will be recruited in the near
future, Col. Okie said.
William T. Faricy, president of
the Association of American Rail
roads, sponsoring organization of
the reserve unit, told the reservists,
“The performance of the railroads
in the last year is an indication of
what they can do if another war
should come.”
Addresses were made by Maj. Gen.
Hobart R. Gay, commanding gen
eral, Military District of Washing
ton, and Maj. Gen. F. A. Heileman,
chief of Army transportation.
A telegram from Veterans Ad
ministrator Gray, who commanded
a railway service unit prior to World
War II, was read to the officers.
Executive officer of the unit is
Lt. Col. W. T. Rice, superintendent
of Potomac Yards.
St. George Church
To Resume Schedule
The Rev. Hedley J. Williams, rec
tor. has announced that St. George
Episcopal Church, Arlington, will
resume its regular schedule of serv
ices with Holy Communion at 8 a.m.
tomorrow. Worship services are
scheduled at 11 a.m.
Mr. Williams, who recently re
turned to Arlington from sick leave,
said that the church school also
will resume its regular session, at
9:30 a.m. tomorrow, with classes for
all ages. On the third Sunday of
each month there will be a celebra
tion of Holy Communion for the
i church school, with instruction for
the intermediate and upper grades.

Reds Reported Urging
Czech-Soviet Merger
ly th« Auocialod Pr«t»
PARIS, Sept. 11.—Hubert Ripka,
former Czechoslovakian foreign trade
minister, charged yesterday a group
within his country’s Communist par
ty is seeking Czechoslovakia’s imme
diate entry into the Soviet Union.
A member of the National So
cialist Party, Mr. Ripka was ousted
from the cabinet with other non
pommunists in February. He
reached France in April. He an
nounced that he will work with the
“Council of Free Czechoslovakia,” to
be organized next month with head
quarters in Washington, London,
and Paris.
“The reports of Communists
working to make Czechoslovakia
one of the Union of Soviet Repub
lics have come to me not from
one, but from five or six cities,” he
told a news conference. "I was very
much astonished by them. Those
working for it are mdstly young
people, from the Communist youth
organization. Party officials are
doing their best to keep it secret.”
Mr. Ripka estimated Czechoslovak
political refugees total 12,000 to
15,000, including about 60 deputies
and eight former cabinet ministers.
Mr. Ripka said the council would
not seek recognition from the West
ern powers as an exile government
but will "interpret the interior
struggle in Czechoslovakia to the
outside world.”

sponding secretary and John Griffin, j
treasurer. Percy V. Templeton, re- j

ation of Citizens’ Associations.
The association voted to join the

zens’ Association with Mrv Hess
and Mr. Templeton as delegates.

nates will be elected at the October
meeting of the association.
The meeting was held in the Paul
Junior High School, with Mr. Tem
pleton presiding.

Losers at Arbitration
Often Are Too Critical,
Seitz Tells Truckers
Pevcr Seitz, general counsel of
the Federal Mediation and Concilia
tion Service, today scolded the
"chronic bellyachers and spoil
sports” in industry and labor who
attack the integrity of arbitrators
when an award goes against them.
He told the American Trucking
Associations, Inc., in convention
here, that such a practice weakens
the arbitration system- one of the
most effective instruments for pro
moting Industrial peace.”
Mr. Seitz said he was "appalled”
by the attitude of some persons after
they have called upon the Govern
ment to provide impartial private
citizens for arbitration duty.
“They do not hesitate,” he said,
"to attempt to ruin the well-estab
lished reputations of men who have
made great contributions to indus
trial peace, and whose services will
continue to be needed in a field in
which the most highly qualified in
dividuals are rare and hard to find.”
Convention at Three Hotels.
The ATA convention^ which
opened Friday. will continue
through Wednesday. Sessions are
being held at the Mayflower, Stat
ler and Carlton Hotels.
Today’s luncheon, at which the
International Harvester Co. is host
at the Mayflower Hotel, features
James Melton, star of the Metropol
itan Opera Comptny.
The association's Executive and
Resolutions Committees were sched
uled to meet late this afternoon to
prepare resolutions to be presented
for action later. New officers will
be elected Wednesday.
Ed J. Buhner, president of ATA,
in his annual report yesterday said
that the effect of the Taft-Hartley
Act has been all that its authors
hoped for so far as the trucking
industry is concerned.
Sees No 'Quickie’ Strike*.
The "quickie” strike has virtually
disappeared, he reported, and the
secondary boycott—of which he said
the trucking industry was the
“chief victim’ —no longer exists.
“All of you have heard about labor
leaders' determination to drive from
public life those legislators who sup
ported the Taft-Hartley law,” he
asserted.
‘‘It seems to me that in our own
Interests that program ought to
be matched by an equal determi
nation on the part of employers and
employes that these valuable pub
lic servents, regardless of party, be
kept in public office.”
Harvey Firestone, Jr., chairman of
the Firestone Time Rubber Co.,
told the convention yesterday that
American businessmen should be
come more active in emphasizing
the merits of competitive free enter
prise both at home and abroad.
"The record of the trucking in
dustry,” he said, "is a classic exam
ple of how a great oak can grow
from a little acorn when the climate
is right. Do not forget for a single
moment that matter of climate.
There are no oak trees at the
North Pole.”
“Roadeo” Ends at Armory.
The eighth annual “roadeo” of
the ATA came to a climax last
night at the District National Guard
Armory, where truck drivers In two
of the three classes were acclaimed
national winners. About 5.000 at
tended.
Grayson C. Thomas of Associated
Transport, Burlington, N. C., won
the ATA semi-trailer driving cham
pionship. Thomas Benane of Truck
ing, Inc., Detroit, took similar hon
ors in the straight trffck class.
Fifty-eight drivers from 29 States,
champions in the areas, took part in
the “roadeo.” Semifinals were held
Saturday cn Fifteenth street N.W.
between E street and Constitution
avenue.
Other winners, truck and semi
trailer class—Second, Vernon Han
cock, Pacyic Intermountain Express.
Kansas City, Mo.; third, Lloyd
Baker, Norwalk Truckline Co., Nor
walk, Ohio.

Riot of Armed Prisoners
Quelled in North Carolina
By the Associated Press
STATESVILLE, N. C., Oct. il.~
A riot of armed prisoners was
quelled in a prison camp near here
last night by reinforcements of
highway patrolmen and deputies.
The prisoners, all colored, held the
superintendent for half an hour and
a guard for an hour and a half
before releasing them unharmed
when prison reinforcements ar
rived. Twelve of the ringleaders
were taken to Central Prison in
Raleigh.
C W. Knox, superintendent of
the prison camp 6 miles north of
here, said it was not a premeditated
break, but started when a guard
tried to take a knife from one of
the prisoners who tried to attack
another at supper.

Soviet Soldiers Shoot
At Germans Trying
To Run Blockade
By tht Associated Brett
BERLIN, Oct. 11.—The Russians
warned again today of fighter flights
and gunnery practice in the air
corridors used by British and Amer
ican planes supplying blockaded
Berlin.
This is the second consecutive
week of Soviet air maneuvers which
some sources say are connected with
extensive Red Army war games.
Meanwhile, Russian soldiers guard
ing a road blockade shot at a
German truck and German passen
ger car attempting to pass through
the blockade from Hamburg. The
truck and its cargo were confis
cated, but the car and its occupants
escaped.
Traffic Controls Tightened.
The shooting came after the Rus
sians tightened traffic controls on
the outskirts of the blocaded city
to close leaks. They claimed to have
confiscated thousands of tons of
food from Western Berliners who
had obtained the food by barter
with Soviet zone farmers. The
Soviets call this trade “smuggling.”
The Russians admitted that im
portant Industries in their occupa
tion zone have been bit by the
Western powers' counter-blockade.
A report on the third quarter of
this year by the German Economic
Commission of the Soviet zone yes
terday conceded 'that production
goals were not being met in several
fields.
Lagging steel and metals produc
tion was blamed on lack of machin
ery which formerly came from West
ern Germany.
Steel Production Lags.
The counter-blockade of the Soviet
zone was ordered by the Western
military governors July 26 in retal
iation against the Russian blockade
of the Wetem sectors of Berlin.
The order halted freight traffic to
and from the Russian zone, cutting
Eastern Germany oft from supplies
of Ruhr coal and steel and machine
products from Western Germany.
The Economic Commission report
said steel production was running
far below the goal and said efforts

Crisis Worst in History,
Cardinal Spellman Says
ty th« Aiioclatod Pros*
NEW YORK, Oct. 11.—Francis
Cardinal Spellman says "It is folly
for us to deceive ourselves that we
are at peace.”
"There is no peace, and naught
we fought for has come to fruit,”
he said yesterday.
“We know that nations gird them
selves for war, a war of annihila
tion,” he declared. “The whole
world and every human in it today
face the greatest crisis in the his
tory of civilization.”
The New York Roman Catholic
prelate spoke at the seventh annual
religious service held by the city’s
Holy Name Societies at the Polo
Grounds.

Yalta Pact Defended
By Byrnes; Says Reds
Hade Mockery of If
ty Associated Press
SEATTLE. Sept. 7.—James F.
Byrnes told the lawyers of
America last night the “greatest
danger In the world today is the
danger of continued aggression
on the part of the Soviet Union.”
The former Secretary of State,
addressing the American Bar Asso
ciation Convention, accused Russia
of “violating nearly all” the agree
ments concerning Eastern Europe.
But, he told the delegates, “as
long as men confer with their prob
lems, even if their language is pro
fane. there is always hope of agree
ment.” He said America was united
in its opposition to aggression, but:
"It does not help us to move to
ward a mare peaceful world to
speak or act in terms that .might
give out the false impression that
we are only concerned with an all
out power struggle with Soviet
Russia."
Say* People Want No War.
Mr. Byrnes said he did not agree
with people who contend “we can
not build one world but must have
two worlds. I do not abandon
hope before this we have seen
governments change policies.
“We can be certain the people of
Russia, like the people of the United
States, do not want war.”
Referring to acts before the war’s
end, and specifically to the Yalta
agreement, Mr. Byrnes said:
“There is nothing wrong with
this Yalta agreement—the trouble
was that the Soviet government
later made a mockery of that
agreement.”
He asserted the Soviets found
these war agreements “such obstacles
that they had to violate nearly all
of them" in pursuit of their expan
sion policies in Eastern Europe.
Doubts They Were Gifts.
It has been these violations, Mr.
Byrnes said, which have made up
the basis of the protests sent to
Moscow by the western powers.
"If the agreements were conces
sions or gifts by Churchill and
Roosevelt to the Soviets, it is rather
peculiar that the Soviets had to
violate them,’’ he declared.
Mr. Byrnes noted that except in
one European area today “Soviet
control does not extend beyond the
lines its armies occupied." That
area, he explained, is the German
territory east of the Elbe which had j
been assigned to the Russians at
Yalta.
"No one of our military chiefs'
had any idea 'at the time of Yalta) I
that we would reach Berlin before
the Soviets did.” he said. "Later, I
when our troops withdrew to our
zone we were simply complying
with our agreement. While pos-1
sibly our representatives, civilian
and military, should have spelled
out in more details our right of ac
cess to Berlin, it is not true that;
our present difficulties in Berlin are
due to the fact that our rights were
not more meticulously defined.”
“Underestimated Patton.”
The Yalta arrangement con
cerning Prague. Mr. Byrnes con
tinued. “appears to have been ex
clusively a military decision." It j
was “generally assumed" that!
Prague would be reached first by,
the Russians but “we underesti-1
mated the prowess of Gen. Patton,”
he said. The reason why the United
States complied with a Soviet mili
tary request not to move forward
toward Prague was “to avoid col
lisions between American forces!
from the West and Soviet forces
from the East,” he explained.
“The Yalta agreements,” Mr.
Byrnes declared, "must be appraised
in the context of the circumstances
under which they were made. For
example, there was the agreement
regarding Poland. It was not a
question of what we would let the
Russians do, but what we could get
the Russians to do.”
Again, in conceding that major
postwar criticism has centered on
the Yalta agreements giving Russia
areas in the Far East, Mr. Byrnes
said:
"To be fair in passing upon the
wisdom of those agreements we
must consider the circumstances as
they were appraised by our military
leaders at the time and not the cir
cumstances as they developed after
the Nazi collapse."
Mr. Byrnes concluded with three
main suggested conditions for a
German peace treaty. They were
that, Germany should be enabled to
develop peacetime industries but
should be kept demilitarized for at
least a generation: the Ruhr in
dustries should be supervised by
Germany's immediate neighbors,
and Russia should be allowed rep
arations from current German pro
duction as compensation for fac
tories originally assigned to Russia
but later kept in Germany.
Although he admitted the agree
ments reached with Russia during
the war "were not perfect," Mr.
Byrnes added:
I “It is a grotesque distortion to
picture these agreements on our
part as the naive action of President
Roosevelt trying to play the part of
Santa Claus."
A realistic conclusion, he argued,
is that the war agreements gave
the Soviet Union very little that it
was not in a position to take with
out agreement.
Mr. Byrnes insisted the United
States must continue a bi-partisan
foreign policy and that the leaders
of the party out of power must be
advised before policies are deter
mined.
"Our bi-partisan policy is opposed
by the naive Mr. Wallace." he said.
“It is also opposed by the Commu
nists who realize it prevents them,
even in an election year from divid
ing America. But that policy is sup
ported by the overwhelming major
ity of Americans.
Nagasaki Spurs Hunt
For Rats by Releasing
30 With Metal Collars
By th« Aijociot#d Pr»»s
NAGASAKI. Sept. 7.—Rats
have been running roughshod
over Nagasaki, but now the
citizens are running after the
rats.
To spur the race, city fathers
caught 30 rats and collared
them with shiny metal. Then
the rats were tinned loose.
Each collared rat will be
worth 1.000 yen to its captor.
To make sure the metal col
lar is not the only quarry the
city officials offered a lottery
ticket for each uncollared rat.
The lottery prize is 500 yen.

AS JULIANA BECAME QUEEN OF THE NETHERLANDS Princess Juliana, who succeeded her
mother as Queen of the Netherlands, is shown on the dias in New Church, Amsterdam, yester
day as she toojc the oath of fidelity to the constitution. The oath was administered by Dr. R.
Kraneberg (foreground), president of the States-General. Queen Juliana’s husband, Prince Bern
hard, is sitting at right of the new Queen.
European royalty watched the formal Investiture of Queen'Juliana yesterday. Pictured (left
to right) front row: Princess Irene, daughter of Juliana; former Queen Wilhelmina, now Do

Bar Association Gets
Several Resolutions
Against Communism
By the Associated Press
SEATTLE. Sept. 7—The Resolu
tions Committee of the American
Bar Association convention here re
ceived several proposals yesterday
denouncing Communism.
Max Chopnick of New York pre
sented a resolution for a special
committee on measures to combat
Communism.
Edwin M. Ottenbourg of New York
proposed that any attorney “pub
licly or secretly" aiding the Com
munist movement should be ex
cluded from the bar association.
Robert B. Bolger of Philadelphia
suggested that the bar association
start a campaign to educate citizens
on the principles of the bill of
rights.
Dispute Over Housing.
A controversy over public housing
support was brought to light when
Horace Russell of Chicago intro
duced a resolution asking that the
management of the American Bar
Association Journal be told to stop
■supporting the “public housing
lobby."
Mr. Russell's resolution recited
editorial support which he said the
Journal had given to the Taft-Ellen
der-Wagner housing bill. It called
for the bar association to take a
stand for “private home-ownership
and private ownership of rental,
housing.”
Hawaii Statehood Favored.
Other resolutions submitted In-1
eluded support for Statehood for
Hawaii by J. Russell Cades of Hono
lulu; establishment of a system of!
"little assemblies” in each of the,
United Nations to discuss interna-1
tional problems, by Dorothy Frooksj
of New York and a declaration in
behalf of the preservation of democ
racy by Hatton W. Sumners, of
Texas, former House Judiciary Com
mittee chairman.
Nine lawyers were nominated for
the association's house of delegates.
Five will be elected. The nominees
are Alfred J. Schweppe. Seattle, for- j
mer dean of the University of Wash-!
mg Law School; Charles E. Lane,
Wyoming; John Kirkland Clark,
New York; Frederick M. Miller.
Iowa; Julian Skinner, Alabama;
Dean C. Davis, Vermont; Charles
M. Lyman, Connecticut; A. W
Dobyns, Arkansas, and R. G. Storey,
Texas.

2 D. C. Men Arrested
On Assault Charge
In Prince Georges
Two District men were arrested
by/ Prince Georges County police
yesterday on charges of attempting
to rape a 43-year-old Washington
mother of four children.
The men, Paul Courtney Puggit,
31, a refrigerator Installation man
of the 1100 block of Sixth street
S.W., and Prank P. Cowens, 41, a
cab driver of the 3300 block of Al
der place N.E., are held without
bond in the Marlboro Jail. Cowens
also is charged with transporting a
wcman for immoral purposes.
According to Prince Georges
County Detective John W. Siddall
the victim, a resident of Southwest
Washington, was left unclothed and
severely beaten near Bock road,
Oxon Hill, abont 3 am. yesterday.
She walked about two miles to
a house where she obtained a slip
and then continued about two miles
more to another house, where she
got other clothing and called police.
Her eye had been blackened and
her body was cut and bruised, Mr.
Siddall said.
She told police two men plicked
her up at her home about 6 pm.
Sunday on the pretense of taking
her to see Fuggit’s ill mother. De
spite her protests, she reported,
they went to a Kenilworth tavern
where they had several drinks and
then to an unoccupied new house
in Cheverly where Fuggit had in
stalled a lefrigerator. She said she
was assaulted in this house.
About 10 p.m. Sunday the three
went to a tavern on Livingston road
at Oxen Hill, but the woman denied
going in, the detective said. The
next she could recall was being
beaten and left on the side of a road,
he added.

Honors Daubantons
For Link With Holland
The congregation of the Chevy
Chase Presbyterian Church last
night paid tribute to the Daubanton
family which has provided it with a
most interesting link with the Old
World. Ch. J. H. Daubanton is
minister in the Netherlands Em
’bassy. With Mrs. Daubanton and
their son and daughter, he will leave
soon for Mexico, where he will be
head of the Netherlands Legation.
When Dr. J Hillnan Hollister,
the pastor of the Chevy Chase
church, wanted to adopt a war
damaged community after VE-Day.
the Daubantons suggested Hedel.
It is a picturesque farming hamlet
whose population left it as the war
anproached. It was defended by
the Germans and bombarded by the
•Canadians. When the population
returned, it was to a. ruined village.
The church adopted Hedel and its
families adopted Hedel families.
Clothes and money and farm tools
were sent from the church in great
boxes. A correspondence of con
siderable portions developed between
the families here and those in Hedel.
The Daubantons volunteered to
translate the letters. It grew to a
sizable task as the number of
adopted families increased to 170.
Mrs. Daubanton and her son and
daughter, who were in school here,
translated every outgoing letter from
English to Dutch and every letter
that came in from Dutch to English.
They were translating scores of let
ters a week.
Mrs. Daubanton visited Hedel two
years ago and on her return thrilled
the congregation with her report of
what the clothes and the tools and
the correspondence meant to the
people there. The village sent the
Church thousands of tulip bulbs
which bloomed this spring.
Dr. Hollister and his wife, who j
attended the World Council of j
Churches at Amsterdam last month,
visited Hedel. The people told hint
the village had recovered—that they!
wanted to continue the correspond-j
ence, bu: suggested the church's j
future gifts go to some other com- j
xnunitv more in need.
Translation of tly correspondence,
a task which has been lightened by
some translation work in Hedel. will,
be continued by Mrs. N. A. C. Slot.e
maker de Bruine. wife of the coun
selor of the Embassy.
A picture of the church at Hedel
as restored has been presented to
the Chevy Chase church. It was on
display last night. It was painted
by a Hedel villager
Five Steamship Lines
Accused of Monopoly
By the Associated Press
NEW ORLEANS. Sept 29—A suit
charging violation of the Sherman
antitrust, laws and asking millions
in damages has been filed in Federal
Court by Seatrain Lines. Inc.
against five steamship companies.
Named defendants are the Lykes
Brothers Steamship Co.. Standard
Fruit Steamship Co.. United Fruit
Co. and West India Fruit. <fc Steam
ship Co., all United States firms.!
and Empress Naviera deCuba, S. A..j
of Cuba.
Seatrain asks immediate payment,
of $1,800,000 for damages allegedly
sustained and pavment at the rate
of $21,000,000 a year as long as the
alleged violations continue.
The suit charges that the com
panies, as members of the Gulf and
South Atlantic-Havana Steamship
Conference, have contracts with
shippers under which the shippers
ran be penalized if they use any
water carrier not authomed by the
contracts. The penalties range from
15 to 25 per rent of the freight rates
on a shipper's traffic for a year, the
petition said.
The suit charges the shipping
companies with "an unlawful at
tempt to monopolize and
to restrain trade ,and commerce of
the United States with a foreign
nation."

Mrs. Cora Kennedy, Mother
Of Newsmen Here, Dies
Mrs. Cora B. Kennedy, mother of
Frank A. Kennedy, Washington cor
respondent for nine Southern news
papers, died at her home yesterday
after a week's illness.
Mrs. Kennedy, widow of Maj.
James S. Kennedy, lived with her
son at 1413 Crittenden street N.W.
She had been active in the Republi
can Women’s Club here.
Her son represents the St Peters
burg Independent. Jacksonville Jour
nal. Pensacola Journal and News.
Orlando Sentinel and Reporter-Star.
Ocala Star-Banner. De Land Sun
News. all In Florida, the Panama
City News-Herald and the H. L.
Peace Publications, New Orleans.
Funeral services and bunal will
be held Friday in Chambersburg.

Trial Set for Mitchum;
Latin in indictment
Protested by Attorney
By the As*ocioted Pres*
LOS ANGELES, Sept. 29.--Screen
Actor Robert Mitchum and two of
his co-defendants pleaded innocent
today to narcotics charges after his.
attorney failed in an attem)»t toj
j have the indictment dismissed on a
I legal technicality. Trial was set for;
i November 22.
Jerry Giesler. noted criminal law- j
yer representing Mitchum. asked
dismissal with the argument that a
portion of the indictment is not in j
the English language and is there-j
fore unconstitutional.
He referred to the section which j
charged the defendants with posses- j
sion and conspiracy to possess 1 flow
ering tops and leaves of Indian
hemp (Cannabis sativai.” i
j Superior Judge Thomas L. Am
brose overruled the demurrer and
ordered the defendants to plead.
All four are at liberty on $10,000
bail each.
Mitchum. 31: Actress Lila Leeds,
i 20: Dancer Vicki Evans. 25. and
Robin Ford. 31. real estate man.
!are charged with possessing mari
juana.
Tire four were arrested shortly;
after midnight last September 1 at
a secluded cottage in Laurel Can-;
yon. north of Hollywood.
Miss Evans did not enter a plea,
as her attorney asked for lime
to argue for dismissal on grounds
that evidence against her is insuf
ficient. This will be heard Octo-;
bj?r 7.
In arguing for the dismissal as
to Mitchum. Mr. Giesler said the!
charges might .as well be "in the!
•Japanese or Chinese language, or
even heiroglyphics.”
It was the intention of the fram
ers of the Constitution that persons
charged know the meaning and sig
nificance of indictments." he added.
"This indictment refers to flow
ering tops of hemp. I always
thought hemp was used to make;
rope."
This brought a laugh from the
packed courtroom and the judge.
“I can't pronounce these words,"
Mi'. Giesler said, after he stumbled j
over cannabis sativa, "and I don't!
know what they mean."
Cotton Production
Curbs to Be Dropped
Ay Ai»ocio*ed Pr#j»
The Government held out the j
promise of a big boost in cotton j
markets this season in announcing j
last night that next year's crop will j
be free of prewar production con- j
trols.
Secretary of Agriculture Brannan j
said it looks as though domestic and j
foreign demand for cotton during 1
the season ending next August 1
will total 13,000.000 running bales.
The market took only 11.266.000'
bales during the past season.
The bulk of the expected jump in
sales will be in the export market.!
Mr. Brannan forecast foreign ship-;
merits at 4.000.000 bales, compared
with only 1.968.000 the past season
Tins year's big crop had pushed
cotton supplies to a point which
indicated it might be necessary to
set planting allotments and market-,
ing quotas on next year's crop. The,
AAA Act of 1938 requires rtch con
trols when supplies exceed the so-!
railed "normal" supply by more than
7 per cent.
Mr. Brannao figured the supply
at 19.164.000 running bales. This in
cluded a crop of 14.858,000 bales and
a carryover of 4.306.000 bales. The
normal supply was determined to be
18.200.000 bales on the basis of a
i formula in the control law.
Thus, the Secretary said, the total
I supply is only 310.000 bales below the
level which w-ould have made con
trols mandatory.

D. C. Realty Board to Elect
Officers and Directors
Officers and directors will be
elected at a meeting of the Wash
ington Real Estate Board tomorrow
at the Mayflower Hotel. F. Moran
McConihe. president, said today.
After the. election board members
will hear a talk by Charles E.
Hoover, regional vice president of
the National Association of Real
Estate Boards, on "The Constitution
and What It Means." Mr. Hoover j
is a. real estate broken In Hunting
ton. W. Va.
During the meeting a gavel will
be presented to the board which
was received from the national as
sociation. Fashioned from wood
grown at Mount Vernon and Montt
cello, it has a steel name band
marie of metal from the deck of the
U. S. S Missouri.
Preceding the meeting will be a
buffet supper for active and asso
ciate members. A1 S. Gardiner, Jr„ I
is dhairman of arrangements.
Hiskey Enters Denial
Of Spy Plot Charge
By the Aisoeiated Pres*
LACROSSE. Wis„ Sept. 29. Dr.
Clarence F Hiskey today termed
charges that he conspired to give
atom bomb secrets to Soviet agents
‘false and de
void of factual
basis.”
Dr. H i s k e v's
statement was
contained In a
telegram to his
hometown news
paper, the La
c r osse Tribune.
Dr. Hiskey is a
professor of
analytical
ch e m i s t r y at
Brooklyn P o lv
technic I n s t i
tute.
The House
Dr. HiskeT,
Committee on Un-American Ac
tivities yesterday recommended spy;
prosecution of br. Hiskey and his'
first wife. Marcia Sand Hiskey. It
cited committee testimony by a
Manhattan District colonel to the
effect that Military Intelligence
was "convinced" Dr. Hiskey 'Was a
subversive agent.”
Dr. Hiskey said in the statement
that two years ago he had testified
"freely and completely to agents of
the Federal Bureau of Investigation
on the facts of the situation.” and
charged that the House committee
had "distorted the meaning of these
facts and have drawn unjustified
conclusions from inferences com
pounded on inferences.”
"1 would like to state unequivocal
ly that at no time have I partici
pated in any association or with any
individual, to commit or to plan acts
inimical to the interest of the Amer
ican Government or of a treason
able or espionage character," he
said.
Dr. Hiskey said he was issuing
the statement “in an effort to allevi
ate the damage to my reputation
which these allegations have caused
and to reassure my friends and col
leagues that these charges are false
and devoid of factual basis."

Backs Truman Ticket
By the Associated Pres*
PITTSBURGH, Sept. 29.—The 33
member International Executive
Board of the CIO-United Steel
workers of America has voted unan
imously to indorse the Truman
Barklev Democratic ticket, the
union said.
