Youngest Delegate, 99,
Likely to Head GAR
■y ttw A»iociot«d Prni
GRAND RAPIDS. Mich., Sept.
29.—The last comrades of the Grand
Army of the Republic soberly con
vened here today to name a new
commander-in-chief.
It was held likely that the office
would fall to 99-year-old Theodore
A. Penland of Portland, Oreg., now
senior vice commander.
Mr. Penland, youngest of six dele
gates, has been presiding at the
JGAR's 82d annual encampment. He
has filled in for 103-year-old Comdr.
Robert M. Rownd. who is ill at his
home in Ripley, N. Y.
The six veterans will take part
tonight in the annual parade. The
honored old soldiers will ride in
automobiles.

INDEPENDENCE, MO.—THE PRESIDENT REGISTERS—President Truman, stopping off here
on his Western campaign tour, signed the registration book in the courthouse Saturday night.

Martin Predicts G.O.P.
Will Gain 20 House
Seats in November
By the Associated Press
1 House Speaker Martin predicted
today the Republicans will pick up
about 20 House seats in the Novem
ber elections.
Here for a brief visit before start
ling on a Midwestern speaking trip,
|Mr. Martin told reporters:
“It's a Republican year: we’ll be
in firm control of both houses.’’
He said he does not share some
Republicans’ fears that the Repub
licans may lose its present Senate
slight margin. In fact, Mr. Martin
added, he would not be surprised to
see the party gain a Senate seat.
The Senate lineup now is 51 Re
publicans and 45 Democrats, with 18
Republican and 14 Democratic seats
at stake in November. The Repub
beans would have to lose four seats
for the Democrats to regain control.
Only a "Rough Guess.”
Mr. Martin emphasized that his
lestimate »f a 20-seat House gain
;was only a “rough guess.” He said
j*ie beligves Democrats not only will
lose seats to the Republicans but
also may drop several to Henry A.
Wallace’s Progressive Party.
At the start of the present Con
gress. Republicans held 246 seats,
Democrats 188 and the American
Labor Party 1. A controlling major
ity is 218. To lose control. Republi
cans would have to drop 29 seats.
From information he has received
in the presidential contest, Mr. Mar
tin said, “I can’t point my finger at
three States outside the Solid South
that President Truman will carry.”
He said his informants believe
Gov. Dewey will strike into the
South to cafry Virginia and Tennes
see. with a good chance to win in
North Carolina.
Meanwhile, it was announced
that Senator Vandenberg, Repub
|lican. of Michigan, chairman of
the Senate Foreign Relations Com
mittee, will make “one of the most
important" speeches of the Dewey
j Warren campaign Monday night,
October 4. It will be broadcast.
McGrath Cites Aid for Schools.
Chairman McGrath of the Demo
cratic National Committee said in
a statement that “the Federal Gov
ernment has spent nearly $10,
i000,000,000 for education during the
j past 16 years of national Demo
jcraiic administration.'’
‘America's States, counties and
i cities are still in control of public
education within their boundaries,”
Senator McGrath said. “But Amer
ican schools are in a’much stronger
position today than in 1939 as a
result of expanded Federal aid to
education programs developed by
the Democratic administration.”
American schools still need more
help, he said, and the “Republican
controlled Eightieth Congress” had
before it a bill "that would have
gone a long way toward meeting
the present war-born crisis in edu
cation.” While Mr. Truman urged
I its passage, he declared. Congress
didn't enact it “although it found
ample time to vote generous tax
I cuts for taxpayers in the upper in
come brackets.”

Big Terminal Markets

CHICAGO, Sept. 20. Cattle
flooded into the major stockyards
I of the Nation today in the largest

were generally steady.
A total of around 129,000 head
was recorded for 12 principal termi
nals. compared with same 143,000
on the most recent larger day.

Mississippi Group
Puts Forward Slate
Of Truman Electors
By the Associated Pres*
JACKSON, Miss., Sept. 20. —A
Mississippi slate of presidential j
electors pledged to President Tru
man was announced yesterday. j
The slate contains eight names.!
Philip E. Mullen of Oxford, chair-'
man of a group which made the
announcement, said a ninth name,
making up the State's full quota,
probably would be announced in a
few days.
A nine-man slate of electors
nominated by Mississippi’s Demo
cratic Party is pledged to vote for
J. Strom Thurmond, presidential
candidate of the States’ Rights
Democrats.
Mr. Mullen, in a statement ac
companying the announcement of
|the pro-Truman slate, said:
"Those who are becoming electors
are doing so entirely because of
principle, with the knowledge that
there are no political attractions in
volved.
"The most important principle in
volved is the right of the mass of
the people to vote.
"Without the qualifications of
this slate of electors, all citizens in;
Mississippi would have been dis- j
franchised, for they would have had;
no choice. Their votes would have j
been cast for them by less than 6.
half dozen people from each county
who took part in the State (Demo
cratic! convention.”
Mr. Mullen asserted that the,
elector group, nominated at a hotel
meeting in Jackson yesterday, ex
pects to qualify with the Secretary
of State within a few days.
William J. Primm, assistant to
Democratic National Chairman J.
Howard McGrath, said in Washing
ton last night the party national
committee will be ‘'very much
pleased” by the entry of a slate of
Truman electors in Mississippi.
It is something Mississippians
have done on their own. Mr. Primm
told a reporter who advised him of
the announcement of the slate. The
national committee, he added, is
naturally interested and ha? knowp
of plans for the move, but has not
done any organizing for it.
Arkansas House Member
Urges Truman Election Slate
LITTLE ROCK, Ark., Sept. 20
UP).—Representative Hays. Demo
crat, of Arkansas, yesterday pre
dicted that Gov. Dewey ‘‘probably
will be elected President of the
United States.”
But he urged the Arkansas Demo
cratic State Convention to pledge
its presidential electors to President
Truman and said he would vote for
Mr. Truman.
In an address over station XKLR.
Mr. Hays said, “I still prefer Mr.
Truman to Mr. Dewey and I prefer
the Democratic Party as a vehicle
of service for my State to the
Republican Party whose economic
policies have been set against us.”
The Arkansas House member crit
icized Mr. Truman’s decision to
sponsor a civil rights legislative
program and said it has “been a
costly one not only from the stand
point of political success but in im
pairment of his leadership.”
“I offer no plea on his behalf on
any other basis than the Solith's
future interest and the progress of
this nation as a unified economic
and political community," he added.

EN ROUTE WITH WARREN TO
TULSA, Okla., Sept. 20.—Campaign
orthodoxy is taking a beating from
California’s earnest and amiable
Gov. Warren.
The Warren brand of homespun
politicking is proving a shocker to
veteran politicians.
It has confused—but impressed—
partisan Republican audiences who
came to tear down the hall, and
then went away without having had
more than a bare chance to whoop
it up.
As the Republican vice presiden
tial special rolled across the Texas
Panhandle into Oklahoma today,
there was a growing conviction
among Gov. Warren's followers that
the Warren campaign this fall
might set a newr high in political
unconventionality.
Has No Counterpart.
There has been none of the classic
“Fourth of July" type of oratory
in Warren speeches.
There has been no distinctive
Warren counterpart to the drama
tic and angry Willkie forelock, the
biting Roosevelt sarcasm, or the
brown derby of A1 Smith.
Even Gov. Warren's utterances
have flouted the elementary politi
cal maxims of keeping all the credit
within the party.
"There are good Americans In
both parties,” he mildly tells his
audiences.
And again: “No party has a

Civic Problems, Civic Bodies
U. S. History and Civics in Our Schools;
Interesting Population Comparisons
By Jesse C. Suter
U. S, History and Civics, High School ‘Must’ Subjects
The Board of Education at its meeting Wednesday approved a pro
posal by School Supt. Hobart M- Corning raising the present requirement
of two semesters of American history and Constitution to two semesters
of history and one semester of United States Government and Constitu
tion. This will become effective with students enrolling in the tenth1
grade in February next.
Students already in high school will not be required to take the
extra semester. This concession was made so as not to disturb students 1
plans of studies. However, such students will be urged to take these
classes as electives.
The superintendent stated that the change is the result of a
y study begun by a special committee of teachers last February.
Making American history. United States Government and Constitu-j
tion "must” subjects in the high schools covering an aggregate of three
semesters, is highly gratifying to citizens who are deeply interested in
having the schools of the National Capital take the lead in study and
inculcation of patriotic Americanism. It is believed that if the teachers1
of history, government and civics enter enthusiastically and patriotically
into making these courses bright and interesting to the students, it will
surely result in continuing good.
This action by the Board of Education appears to promise a satis
factory solution of a portion of the problem of how best to spread
Americanism to combat the spread of Communism in our country.
Other subdivisions of this problem require careful study, planning
and implementing. There still remain a large number of adults who,
as far as Americanism is concerned, failed to acquire the requisite
knowledge and understanding during their schoQling.
Then there are the teachers of history, government and civics, who
surely should have an illuminating and patriotic refresher course to arm
and equip them fully for putting these subjects in a compelling and con
vincing manner in their classes.
Study Classes for Adults May Be the Answer.
Reaching the adults appears to be a task which might well be ac
complished through the formation of small study groups in various
neighborhoods throughout the District of Columbia. Citizens’ associa
tions. home and school associations, church groups and others might
well undertake the formation of such study circles. This is a most im
portant and much needed work, equaling in importance the civilian de
fense work during World War II.
True knowledge and understanding of United States history, United
States Government and civic obligations and privileges and the putting
of such principles into practice are essential to the preservation of the
Republic and the American way of life.
There is required a systematic educational campaign, which should
challenge the Washington Board of Trade, through its fine Committee
on Education, and the Federation of Citizens’ Associations, under its
committee of the same name, to assume leadership in this urgent
patriotic undertaking.
The Federation has already tackled one phase of the problem
through discouraging infiltration of subversive elements into the con
stituent organizations and the central body. Such action was largely
of a negative character and would be greatly strengthened by active
support of a constructive movement through American education to in
crease the knowledge and understanding of Americanism. This in
cidentally would contrast Americanism with the fallacies of Communism.
The result would be an aroused patriotism and increased devotion to
our country.
We cannot take America for granted and overlook entirely the fact
that this Government of the people, by the people and for the people
requires the actual understanding and participation of these people.
It is not a perpetual motion machine, but requires the steady application
of the best thought, patriotic devotion and human energy to keep it
going. These days we hear a good deal about “preventive medicine’’ and
should remember the old adage,—“An ounce of prevention is worth a
pound of cure.”

Are Citi/.en Area Councils Only Wheels Within Wheels?
The informal meeting last week of the Northeast and Northwest
Councils of citizens’ associations has drawn attention to these regional
bodies and revived interest in a question as to their value to the or-^
ganized citizen movement. It appears unfortunate that the meeting
of these two groups was considered by the press as a Joint meeting, when
a careful analysis shows that the meeting was really only a pre
Federation election caucus.
The press reports agree that presidents of either of these councils
were reluctant to preside over the meeting as the contemplated business'
was entirely beyond being a strictly sectional question.
However, there has been a revival of some of the doubts as to the
value of such groups, and questions such as these arise: Do the addi
tional duties performed by council officers and the holding of extra t
meetings pay in tangible results? Would not the energy, effort and
influence of such officers be more productive in the Federation of
Citizens’ Associations? Some members of the citizens’ associations re-!
cognize a real danger to the central body through the formation of
blocks, and thus control of the Federation through sectional pressure.
They can see an increased danger to the central body through the
combined action of two or more of these sectional councils.
mere are some wno Deneve me couneu can pmunn a vaiuauic
service in handling problems In which there is a common interest on the
part of member associations within the council area. But there is!
seen a danger of the council usurping the work of the Federation of
Citizens' Associations by reaching out. independentlv of the Federation,
in the handling of matters affecting the entire District of Columbia.
Very effect.ve and extensive teamwork has been done through the*
formation of joint committees from citizens' associations in a given
neighborhood to deal with a single problem. In such cases study.!
planning and persistent activity have resulted in constructive achieve
ment and complete accomplishment of the objective. The job being
done, the joint committee has been disbanded and not made a con
tinuous organization requiring attendance at meetings and the work
usually attached to the operation of such organizations.
The joint committee plan for teamwork appears to have an ad
vantage over the maintenance of a continuing organization like these
councils. Those who hold to such views feel that wheels within wheels
make a complicated machine which is burdensome to keep in operation
on the lines upon which it was designed to run.

Columbia as of July 1. 1947. This gave the*population of the District of
Columbia as 861.000, which leads the population estimates of each of

Naming the ‘Whitehurst Freeway’ Meets Popular Approval
The Commissioners of the District of Columbia, almost immediately
after the death of Capt. Herbert C. Whitehurst, named the partly com
pleted K street elevated highway the “Whitehurst Freeway.” General
Gordon R. Young, the Engineer Commissioner. In recommending this
action said that Capt. Whitehurst was responsible more than any other
man fpr the “magnificent” system of bridges and highways surrounding
and being built around Washington. Various citizens’ associations have
voted hearty approval of this action.
The associations also are expressing their approval of the selec
tion by the Commissioners of John N. Robertson as director of high
ways, which position was vacated by the death of Capt. Whitehurst.

Activities
The 12th annual Parent-Teacher
Institute, sponsored by the George
Washington University and the
District of Columbia Congress of
Parents and Teachers, will be held
at the University Hall of Govern
ment, Twenty-first and G streets
N.W., on October 6 and 7.
There will be meetings from 10
a.m. until 2:30 p.m. on both days
and a final session at 8 p.m. on
Thursday, when the workshop sum-'
maries will be given, so fathers who
have been unable to attend the day
sessions may be informed.
This series of meetings will in-;
elude down-to-earth workshops,
where PTA members and officers
may exchange ideas as to best
methods of presenting the work.
Mrs. John E. Hayes, first vice presi
dent of the National Congress of
Parents and Teachers, will come
from her home in Idaho to assist
in this school of instruction.
Parliamentary procedures will be
presented by Mrs. Arthur C. Wat
kins, parliamentarian and former
president of the D. C. Congress. Dr.
Charles E. Bish, principal of Mc
Kinley High School, will discuss
“Leadership Techniques" and Dr.
Leland Bradford and associates
from the NBA will demonstrate the
use of role playing In parent groups.
Dr. James Harold Pox, dean, School
of Education, George Washington
University, will also take part on
the program.
Greetings by Mrs. O. G. Hankins,
president of the D. C. Congress; Dr.
Clovd Heck Marvin, president of
the university; Mrs. John E. Hayes,
national PTA officer, and Dr. Ho
bart M. Corning, superintendent of
schools, will feature the opening
session.
Three workshops will be held each
morning, with a short session fol
lowing lunch for further questions.
Wednesday workshops will be on
organization (also ways and meansJ
budget, treasurer), program and
publicity, and welfare (also school
gardens). Thursday workshops will
include home and family life (also
legislation and library), education

Event to Aid Building Fund
Headquarters Staff of DAR
To Give Benefit Card Party
By Frances Lide
Hie stately corridors of Constitu
tion Hall will provide the setting for
a mammoth card party on the eve
ping of October 20.
Hostesses at the festive event will
be members of the national head
quarters staff of the Daughters of
the American Revolution, who have
chosen this means of raising funds
for the DAR building fund.
The staff has set a goal of $2,200
for its contribution to the building
project which includes construction
of an administration building and
remodeling of Memorial Continental
Hall. The estimated cost of the
entire project is $900,000.
Elaborate preparations are being
made for the benefit, which will be
pnder the general chairmanship of
Miss Janie Glascock. Plans call for
setting up 250 tables for cards or
other games, with attractive prizes
to be offered at each table.
A special feature will be the pres
entation of a fashion show by one
Of Washington’s leading department
stores, and other business firms also
are co-operating to make the party
success.
The corridors will be decked with
flowers supplied by a local florist,
ahd the festive atmosphere will be'
enhanced further by a program of
recorded music, which will be made
possible through the courtesy of a
music company here. Three radio
phonographs will be installed espe
cially for the occasion, so that the
music program can be heard simul
taneously in the three long corridors.
Mrs. Marguerite Schondeau, chair
man of music, also Is in charge of
the fashion feature, while others
working on various phases of the
benefit include Mrs. Katie Prince
Eskar, chairman of prizes: Mrs.
Dorothy Ross Mackey, refreshments
chairman, and Mrs. Elena Quilllan,
who will be in charge of a candy
sale.
Still other chairmen are Mrs. Ada
Walker, souvenir program; Mrs. Vir
ginia Burton, publicity; Miss Bessie
Bright, tables and chairs, and Miss
May Adele Levers, gift table.
Mrs. Jeannette Jackson is chair
man of tickets, which are available
at the administration headquarters,
1720 D street N.W.
Mrs. Roscoe C. O’Byrne, president
general of the DAR, heads the list
of patronesses, which includes most
of the top brass in the society. Mrs.
Rex Hays Rhoades, treasurer gen
eral, who also heads the DAR Build
ing Finance Committee, will be
among the guests, while other
patronesses'will be members of the
cabinet, national officers, honorary
presidents general, honorary vice
presidents general, national chair
man, past national officers and offi
cers of the Children of the American
Revolution.
As the date for the benefit coin
cides with that of the National Board
of Management's fall meeting, many
out-of-town Daughters are expected
to attend the fete. The board meet
ing is scheduled for 9:30 a.m. on
October 20.

Alumnae to Mark
Founders’ Day
Dr. Walter Clinton Jackson, chan
cellor of the Woman's College of
the University of North Carolina,
will address the annual founders’
day dinner of the Washington
Alumae Club at 7 p.m. Friday at
the Lafayette Hotel.
Mrs. Boydston Satterfield of At
lanta, president of the Alumnae As
sociation, and Mrs. Carleton Jester,
general secretary from the alumnae
headquarters, will be special guests.
The program will include colored
movies of campus activities.
More than 500 invitations have
been extended to alumnae in the
Washington area, Baltimore and
Annapolis.
New officers will be elected at a
short business meeting following the
dinner. Miss Eloise Banning is
chairman of the Nominating Com
mittee.
Mrs. William H. Haggard is chair
man of the Dinner Committee, as
sisted by Miss Virginia Tatum and
Mrs. D. J. Harris. Reservations
should be made before Tuesday with
Miss Mary Wood Wolfe.
Guild to Assist
With Open House
The Goodwill Guild is planning to
assist with the annual open house
of the Goodwill Industries on Wed
nesday and Thursday at 1218 New
Hampshire avenue N.W.
Mrs. Wiley Rutledge, guild presi
dent, has named Mrs. Willard Ed
wards as chairman of hostesses and
Mrs, Harry Rochester as chairman
of guides. The open house will be
held from 10 a m. to 5 p.m. Wednes
day and from 12:30 p.m. to 8:30 p.m.
Thursday.
Among those taking turns at the
tea table and guiding visitors
through the workrooms will be Mrs.
Sidney Hechinger, Mrs. Karl Fen
ning, Mrs. Howard Nichols, Mrs.
Charles Daly, Mrs. Elizabeth Mur
ray, Mrs. Samuel Herrick, Mrs. John
Ladd, Miss Cynthia Hill, Mrs. Fred
erick Halstead, Mrs. Claude Orchard,
Mrs. L. H. Macomber, Mrs. Francis
Goodwin, Mrs. J. Brent Clarke, Mrs.
Ernest Daniel, Mrs. Theodore I.
Messenger and Mrs. Louis Renfrow.
Goodwill Industries, which works
closely with other community and
national agencies serving the handi
capped, arranges this special two
day program each year in order to
give its contributors and their
friends an opportunity to see for
themselves how their Goodwill bags
are received and used.

D, C. Federation
To Hold Benefit
The District Federation of Wom
en's Clubs has completed plans for a
luncheon and fashion show to be
sponsored by its Finance Committee
November 3 at the Hamilton Hotel.
Mrs. Benjamin L. Schoenfeld is
general chairman of the affair,
which will .have as its theme, the
adaptation of today’s fashions from
the costumes of other countries and
early American styles.
The Finance Committee, which is
headed by Mrs. E. Morgan Pryse,
will use the proceeds for the philan
thropic work of the federation.
Assisting chairmen include Mrs.
Frederic N. Newburgh, patrons; Mrs.
William O. Gale, reservations; Mrs.
Marie Deal and Mrs. T. David Gates,
music, and Mrs. Ernest H. Daniel,
decorations.
The federation will resume its
monthly meetings on October 25 at!
11 a.m. at the Hamilton Hotel. The
departments of publicity and club
extension, will have charge of the
program.
Supper Meeting
Mrs. Tomas Cajigas. first vice
president of the Columbian Women,
will entertain the Executive Board
at a supper meeting at p.m. Tues
day at her home, 4605 Charleston
terrace N.W. Miss Ruby Nevins will
preside at the business session.
Community Clubs
Arrange Programs
A discussion on the proposed
Montgomery County charter will be
presented at the opening fall meet
ing of the Silver Spring Womans
Club on Wednesday. Plummer M.
Shearin, executive director of the
Charter Committee, will speak in
support of the proposal and Samuel
E. Bogley of Bethesda will speak in
opposition.
The meeting will be held at 11
a.m. in the social hall of the Wood
side Methodist Church, 8814 Georgia
avenue. A rebuttal will be allowed
each speaker and a question period
will follow with Mrs. George East
ment, club president, acting as mod
erator.

Dr. Walter Isard of Tufts Col
lege and the Massachusetts Institute
of Technology, has been appointed
an associate director of the Teach
ing Institute of Economics of Amer
ican University, the university an
nounced.
The appointment of Dr. Isard is
part of a program planned for the
study of the economic and social
consequences of atomic energy util
ization. the school added.

tor's degree at Harvard University
and has held post-doctoral fellow
ships of the Social Science Research
Council for the last three years, has
written extensively on economic
implications of the use of atomic
energy.

Knee-Hi Receives Safety Award
On Completion of Visit Here
Inspector Richard H. (Dick) Mansfield, The Star’s safety
director (left), presents a canine safety award to Knee-Hi, the
safety dog, as George E. Brunson, her master, looks on.
—Star Staff Photo.
Knee-Hi may not realize It, but
she's now the possessor of a novel
safety award.
Its the first canine safety award
ever made by Inspector Richard H.
(Dicki Mansfield, The Star's safety
director and school safety lecturer.
Inspector Mansfield presented it
to the young traffic-educated dog
upon completion last week of her
two-week Washington visit under
auspices of The Star.
It signifies, according to Inspector
Mansfield, that Knee-Hi has a
special proficiency In helping chil
dren to be careful when they are
on the streets. The dog, still only
10 months old, proved that in her
visits to a number of Washington
playgrounds and to Camp Ernest
W. Brown in Scotland, Md., train*
ing camp of the school patrol.
Knee-Hi made such a hit that
Inspector Mansfield is including her
in his chalk-talk “Safety Circus”
which he will take to the area
schools throughout the coming win
ter. In the “Safety Circus,” her
trainer will be a “Chinaman" named
"Long-time No-see.~
The real Knee-Hj, meanwhile, will
be visiting other cities to teach
school children how to avoid traffic
accidents, according to her master,
George E. Brunson of Cape May,
N. J. Mr. Brunson took an earlier
Knee-Hl to a number of cities in
the United States and Canada, in
cluding Washington, before her
death two years ago.
6 Transfers Ordered
By Salvation Army
Changes involving six Washington
leaders of the Salvation Army were
announced yesterday by Lt. Col.
W. W. Bouterse, divisional com
mander for the District. They are:
Maj. Maurice Smith, since 1928
assistant corps director at Seventh
and P streets N.W., to be command
ing officer of Negro activities at
Memphis, Tenn.
Maj. Clarence Hammer, com
manding officer of No. 5 corps. 715
I street N.E.. since 1944, to a similar
post at Baltimore.
Adjutant Edna Cooper, matron of
the Women's and Children's Emer
gency Home, 1608 New Hampshire
avenue N.W., since 1946, to the
Memphis, Tenn., Citadel.
Adjutant Lonnie Knight. Wash
ington No. 1 corps since 1944, to
lake charge of the Dallas, Tex.,
Citadel.
Capt. Leslie Hall, in charge of
Georgetown activities since 1946, to
be director of the music depart
ment at the Washington Divisional
Headquarters.
Capt. Howard Whitehead, assist
ant officer at No. 5, to be com
mander of the Georgetown.post,
The changes are effective imme
diately with replacements to be an
nounced next week, the Salvation
Army said.

dent of Venezuela, will be guest of

dent Romulo Gallegos took office.:

and articles on the economic, social j
and political problems of his i
country.
Checker Tournament
Opens at 'Y' Tomorrow
Fifty checker enthusiasts from
Washington and Virginia will meet
tomorrow In the YMCA, Eighteenth
and G streets N.W., for the annual
Old Dominion Checker Tournament.
This is the first time the contest,
expected to last two or three days,
will be held in the District. It is
sponsored by the District Checker
Club, which is affiliated with the
Old Dominion Checker Association.
Officers will be elected at a busi
ness meeting at 10 a.m. tomorrow,
after which play will begin.

Church Council Ends;
Leaders Praise Spur
To Spiritual Unity
ly th* Associattd Pr«ss
AMSTERDAM, Sept. 4.—The first
assembly of "the World Council of
Churches ended today with its
leaders satisfied the assembly had
done more to achieve spiritual unity
than any other in Christian church
history.
The assembly of many religious
groups representing 150 Christian
churches arrived at the conclusion
there is no such thing as a “just"
war and rejected both communism
and “laissez-faire” capitalism^ as a
means of assuring freedom and jus
tice in the world.
Willem Adolf Visser T. Hooft,
general secretary of the World
Council, said the groups were de
termined to stay together in what
amounts to a covenant between the
churches. He said such a cove
nant between the churches. He said
such a covenant would produce a
spiritual force in the world to com
bat intolerance and war.
Frank, Open Discussion Cited.
The general secretary, who Is a
member of the Dutch Reformed
Church, said the assembly had gone
even further than promoting a
world-wide church movement. He
said the conference had learned to
speak frankly and openly, with the
result that differences rising out of
varied denominational and national
backgrounds had not been so great
they could not be settled.
in aaaitiqp, ne said, theologians!
arrived at a better undertanding of
lay problems.
Council officials said the findings
which the assembly commended to
the churches for serious "study and
appropriate action” would provide
material for churches to reflect in
their teaching. The churches will
take no formal action on the pro
nouncements of the council, which
are intended only as guidance.
Charles P. Taft, Episcopal lay del
egate of Cincinnati, Ohio, gave the!
opinion that communism had be
come possible because of the lack
of social consciousness on the rest1
of the world. It was on his sugges-!
tion that the council accepted an
amended report which qualified a
previous flat rejection of Capitalism i
with the term “laissez faire.”
Lauds U. S. on Racial Issue.
Mr. Taft also felt the United
States need “not be ashamed over its
treatment of the racial problem, in
view of the racial question in South
Africa, Palestine and India. He said
he did not condone what had not
been done in the United States, but
that he believed the United States
had done “as good a job” as other
areas of the world confronted with
the same problem.
The assembly in its final pro
nouncement urged churches to speak
boldly in opposing "terror, cruelty'
and race discrimination," and to an-!

Dr. Reissig of Washington
Pees Record Impressive
AMSTERDAM, Holland, Sept. 4
(CDN).—Protestant dignitaries Con
cluded here today in Amsterdam
what is generally regarded as a
most successful conference.
The view of many of the delegates
to this first assembly of the World
Council of Churches at the close of
their two weeks of sessions seemed
to be expressed by Dr. Frederick
Reissig, United Lutheran Minister
from Washington.
Dr. Reissig said he considered that
the assembly had set up an impres
sive record both by staying together
and by reaching agreement on some
srtongly worded statements con
cerning present-day problems.
Emphasizing that the delegates
had come from both sides of the
iron curtain and represented all
races with their considerably varied
opinions on world problems, he de
clared:
“It was a risky thing to hold this
assembly. It could have broken up.
But we have stayed together.”

Civic Problems, Civic Bodies
Board of Trade Makes a Fine Start;
Community Chest Campaign “Kick-Off”
By Jesse C. Suter
Board of Trade Ready to Go on 1948-9 Season’s Jobs
The annual organization meeting of officers, directors and committee
chairmen of the Washington Board of Trade Tuesday afternoon and
evening was a well-attended and helpful occasion. The conference was
held in the Chinese Room at the Mayflower Hotel, where, upon arrival,
each member was provided with the Committee Operations Manual for
the fiscal year. The table of contents of the manual will give a fair idea
of its value to officers, directors and committee chairmen of this great
organization of 8.000 members.
An interesting foreword is followed by a brief history of the organiza
tion and a list of past presidents and the year of their service. The
current list of officers, directors and committee chairihen is next in order.
Other topics are past accomplishments, principles and objectives,
committee operations. Board of Trade actions, assistance available in
publicity and library material available. Then come the listing of the
entire personnel of the Board of Trade's staff, with their duties, the office
layout and suggestions for committee activities. Finally the manual for
each chairman gives the complete list of members of his committee.
The president of the board, Leon Chatelain. jr.. presided and
Introduced John A. Reilly, last year's president, who briefly sketched the
work of the organization during the fiscal year 1947-48.
President Chatelain outlined briefly the program containing the
major objectives for the current year as approved by the Board of
Directors. This program had been prepared by the Committee on
Planning Activities and has been explained rather fully in the news
columns of The Star during the past week.
Tentative Program Adopted for the Tear.
The program, briefly outlined, contains the following subjects:
1. District of Columbia Fiscal Affairs. Placed first on the list are
District of CoHihibia fiscal affairs. The first step to cure the immediate
fiscal problem is recognized as the enactment of the proposed retail
68lw* tax
2. Decentralization. The importance of successfully combatting the
threatened decentralization of business from the central business area
of the city is stressed. A number of committees co-operating with the
City Planning Committee are expected to devote major attention toward
minimizing the forces which seem to be major factors in the movement
of business to outlying sections.”
3. Tourist and Convention Promotion. The vigorous campaign of the
Greater National Capital Committee to increase the number of tourists
to visit Washington is to be continued, including bringing conventions to
Washington and preparing the way for a large attendance at the sesqui
centennial celebration in 1950.
4. District of Columbia Sesquicentennial. The board will take a large
part in the planning of proper observance in 1950 of the sesquicentennial
of the moving of the seat of the Federal Government to the District of
Columbia. The Board of Trade was primarily responsible for the enact
ment of the legislation creating the Sesquicentennial Commission.
5. Congressional Relations. The imnroving of the relations between
♦he local community and the Congress of the United States, inaugurated
four years ago. has met with great success. This very constructive
activity has shown such good results that it -will be carried on In a fine
program this year.
6 District of Columbia Form of Government. Under ^6) it is stated
that the Board of Trade did not look with favor upon the Auchincloss
bill for local suffrage. It recognizes that, a still further revised plan will
be introduced in the Eighty-first. Congress. It Is promised that such
proposals will be carefully scrutinized and the board will "continue to
exert unlimited effort to make sure that there will be no change in the
District of Columbia form of government, unless it can be clearly
demonstrated that the change would represent an Improvement over
What we now have.”
7. Service to Business. More business information and more business
gtatistics are expected to be developed during the year. These are to be
made available to the membership by means of an expanded publications
program.
There is also included a recommendation that a weekly radio program
be inaugurated. Through it the Board of Trade, it is said., would be better
able to support its committee activities and inform the Washington public
respecting board views on important issues.
The year's program is necessarily incomplete, as many problems in
which the Board of Trade is interested will from time to time be presented
In the form of recommendations from the numerous committees.
Vigorously pushing national representation for the people of the
District Is a perennial task of the Board of Trade. It Is recognized that
»nv worthwhile change in the form of local government must be preceded
by the granting to the people of the District of Columbia of.voting
representation in the Senate, House of Representatives and the electoral
college.
While the Auchincloss bill Is frequently referred to as home rule,
the board s attitude always has been that the nearest approach to ‘‘home
rule” In the District could come only through representation in the
Congress and votes for President and Vice President. The Congress and
the President are the real rulers of the District on behalf of the people
of the 48 States.
Committees Indicate Type of Activities.
The titles of the committees of the. Board of Trade give a fair
indication of the varying activities of the organization. These committees
are listed as follows:
American Ideals. Architecture and Architectural Awards, Auditorium.
Aviation. Citv Planning. Clean-up Washington, Congressional Relations,
Cultural Development, District of Columbia Form of Government.
Education. Employment and Security, Forums, Greater National
Capital, Highways. Industrial and Wholesale Interest, Insurance, Law
and Legislation. Libraries, Membership, Metropolitan Area Relations,
Mid-Winter Dinner, Military and Naval Affairs, Municipal Finance,
National Affairs.
National Representation. New Enterprises. Program. Public and
Private Buildings. Public Health. Public Order, Public Utilities. Railroads
and Transit Lines. Recreation. Retail Business. River and Harbor, Safety,
Spring Outing, Stadium, Traffic, Welfare, World Trade, Zoning, Sesqui
centennial.

Community Chest at Board o£ Trade General Meeting
The first general membership meeting of the Washington Board of
Trade on Thursday, September 30. at 8 p.m. in Constitution Hall, will be
combined with the '49 Community Chest "kickoff” for the business of
Washington.
The circular announcing this event, sent out by the chairman of tne
Program Committee, states: "The Washington Board of Trade proudly
presents this Double-header Four Star Plus Show which serves a dual
purpose: (1) As the First General Membership Meeting for the 1948-9
Administrative Year; <2) a ‘kickoff’ meeting for the 20th annual Red
Feather drive to rededicate the whole Chest idea of united giving,
establish public confidence in the Business Unit leadership in the October
campaign and bring these leaders in touch with Board of Trade members
bo that mutual understanding and teamwork may be encouraged.”
The principal speaker of the evening will be Henry Ford II, president
of the Ford Motor Co. and chairman of the Community Chests of America.
Chauncey G. Parker, director of administration, International Bank, and
general “chairman of the Washington Community Chest Federation
campaign, also will speak. Leon Chatelain, Jr., president of the board
and vice chairman of the 1949 Chest drive, will preside and introduce the
speakers. _ _
An entertainment feature will be furnished by the Connie B. Gay
Radio Ranchmen, plus outstanding radio entertainers from Washington s
local stations. Admission to the general meeting will be by special card
and not by the usual Board of Trade membership card.

Further Study of Proposal to Amend ABC Regulations
The District Commissioners have put aside for further study a
proposed new regulation which would compel all tavern and restaurant
licensees to maintain two sets of books, one covering the sale of food and
the other the sale of beverages.
The proposal originated with the Alcoholic Beverage Control Board
with a view to providing the means of determining whether or not
class C and class D licensees are selling more food than beverages, as
required by law.
Another provision of the proposed amendment would require every
class of licensee to keep daily records of all disbursements, to be at all
times available for inspection by representatives of the ABC Board.
A public hearing was held on the proposed amendment last July
and the corporation counsel was assigned to report it in a revised form
to the Commissioners. This is the form of the proposal on which the
Commissioners are making their further study. The first part of the new
regulation has the purpose of providing the control board with definite
means of ascertaining whether or not the requirement of the law is
being met by licensees in classes C and D.
In the Alcoholic Beverage Control Act ^District Code of Laws.
Title 25 Section 103 under •'Definitions.” the Alcoholic Beverage Control
Board is required to determine in its Judgment that certain conditions
are met by licensees.
Under paragraph <gL defining “clubs.” the law provides:
s shall satisfy the board that the sale of beverages intended is not
more than an incident to and is not the prime source of revenue from
auch space
Under paragraoh (j), defining ''hotels,’ the law, provides:
*hall satisfy the board that such dining room is intended for use
primarily as a place for preparing, cooking and serving meals and that
the chief source of revenue to be derived from the operation of such
dining room shall be from the preparation, cooking and serving of meate
and not from the sale of beverages
Under paragraph <n», defining “restaurants,” the law provides:
the chief source of revenue to be derived from the operation of
auch place shall be from the preparation, cooking and serving of meals
and not from the sale of beverages
Under paragraph tq>. defining “taverns,” the law provides:
a place where sandwiches or light lunches are prepared and served
for consumption on the premises in such quantities as to satisfy the
board that the sale of beer and light wines intended is no more than an
Incident to and not the prime source of revenue of such tavern.’
The public hearing on the regulation held in July appears to have
been well attended by licensees but civic files fail to disclose tHe presence
and participation of any representatives from civic groups.
■nils part of the proposed regulation appears to be perfectly
reasonable and a businesslike way of supplying essential information to
the ABC Board It would enable the board to uncover those places whtfce
prime source of revenue is from the sale of alcoholic beverages and where
the service of these beverages is not incidental to the sale of food as
required by law There appears to be no ground for a reasonable objection.
The'second portion of the proposed regulation, requiring the licensees
tn all classes to keep adequate books and records showing all disburse
ments would be of advantage to the board in determining the actual
ownership of beveraga businesses in the District and disclosing hidden
owners.

THE AUGUSTA COURIER, AUGUSTA, GA., SEPTEMBER 6, 1948
September Bth Will Be Liberation
Day In Georgia
Herman Talmadge will liberate Georgia on September Bth. He will
liberate Georgia from the most corrupt gang of politicians who have in
fested the State since the day when old Rufus Bulloch came down from
New York State and got elected Governor by the negroes, carpetbaggers
and scalawags.
On page 258 of Volume 3 of “Dictionary of American Biopraphy” the
following sketch of Rufus Brown Bulloch is given:
“Bulloch's entrance into politics was as a member of the Constitu
tional Convention of 1868. Congress ordered the adoption of the new
State Constitution. By disenfranchising the white element and em
powering the negro to vote this Convention would write into effect
the will of Congress. To this Convention Bulloch was elected. He
at once became the leader of the carpetbaggers and negro element of
the Convention and was elected Governor by this element. Bulloch
was charged by the contemporary democratic newspapers with every

To "BUY" Votes
The Rivers-Arnall-Thompson adminis
tration is as corrupt as the carpetbagger
and negro government which Georgia
had during the days of Reconstruction,
said Alex McLennan, an Atlanta lawyer
in an address over Radio Station WSB
on Friday night, August 27th, 1948.
In his address McLennan stated that
the history ..books say that Rufus Bulloch
was elected to office by the carpetbaggers
and negroes during the period of Recon
struction and that he sold pardons, in
fluenced the press by grants from the pub
lic treasury and in the building of rail
roads pilfered the State Treasury and put
the money in his pocket.
He drew a parallel between the ad
ministration of Rufus Bulloch and the
administration of Ed Rivers, Ellis Arnall
and M. E. Thompson by showing that the
three Acting Governors have sold par
dons, have corrupted the press by the
wasteful expenditure of public money,
and that they have swindled and pilfered
the State Treasury in the building of high
ways just as bad as old Rufus Bulloch did
when he was building State-aid railroads.
McLennan’s speech in full follows:
MY FELLOW GEORGIANS:
The subject of my talk tonight is “CAN
M. E. THOMPSON BE TRUSTED.”
Thompson has insisted that his record be
discussed. I am very pleased at this op
portunity to discuss his record. However,
in order to intelligently discuss Thomp
son’s record it is also necessary for us
to discuss the records of Ellis Arnall and
Ed “Asphalt” Rivers, because this pair
own and control M. E. Thompson.
In discussing the records of the Rivers-
Arnall-Thompson administrations we find
a striking parallel in the history of Geor
gia during the Reconstruction years of
1868 to 1871. Historians all agree that the
administration of Rufus Bulloch was the
most corrupt period in the history of Geor
gia. However, the history books cover
ing the present era have not as yet been
written.
I will read a few paragraphs from the
history book entitled “Dictionary of
American Biography”, Volume Three, be
ginning at Page 258, “Rufus Brown Bul
loch—Bulloch’s entrance into politics was
as a member of the Constitutional Con
vention of 1868. Congress ordered the
adoption of a new state constitution. By
disenfranchising the white element and
empowering the negro to vote this Con
vention would write into effect the will
of Congress. To this Convention Bulloch
was elected. He at once became the lead
er of the carpetbaggers and negro ele
ment of the Convention and was elected
Governor by this element. Bulloch was
charged by the contemporary democratic
newspapers with every known political
form of rascality. He was qharged with
the sale of pardons; with purchasing the
influence of the press by wasteful publica
tion of public documents; with gross cor
ruption in the payment of subsidies to
railroads; with selling State bonds and
appropriating the proceeds; with general
extravagance and corruption in every
department of his administration. The*
truth appears to be that Bulloch and his
crew instituted a carnival of public spoilia
tion. Through the device of issuing State
bonds to subsidize railroad corporations
they poured public money into their own
pockets.”

ed to Georgia' in the year 1859. Rivers, a
native of Arkansas, emigrated to Georgia
in the year 1919. Both of these men had

News, Glamour Abound at Gathering
Margaret Truman Tells Plans
At Junior League Luncheon
By Betty Beale
If there is one feminine social function in Washington that is
jacked with interest, it is the annual Junior League fall luncheon.,
It abounds with glamour, intellectual stimulation and all kinds of excit
ing news.
Yesterday Miss Margaret Truman was one of the hundreds of
members who met at the fchevy Chase Club tor this opening send-off.
Looking very chic in a navy blue suit and hai and fur scarf, her blond
hair curled becomingly short, the President's daughter told about her
future musical plans. She is going to do some operas, she said, "Lucia,”
"Rigoletto,” and perhaps "II Traviata,” either late this fall or the first
of the year. She wouldn’t divulge,'
though, with what opera company
she would sing nor where it would
perform.
From the looks of the club dining
room, the'Junior League of Wash
ington had turned out, as usual, in’
full force for this party. Mr. Walter
Kotschnig, the guest speaker, had a
large and attentive audience while
he talked for an hour on “The
United Nations and You.” He said
that he couldn’t look at both his au
dience and his notes with his new
glasses, but he preferred looking at
his audience- It was easy to see
why; some of the Capital’s prettiest
and smartest women were present.
Standing out on the inclosed
porch, where cocktails were served
before luncheon, was Mrs. George
Wheeler, chic in a black tailleur and
little black hat lined with white
satin and topped by snow white
birds Chatting with her were Mrs.
Charles McKenney and Mrs. William
J Flather III, whose pretty blond
head was capped in a red felt
chapeau.
Capitalites Present
There was Mrs. Christopher Sar
gent who was looking out for Junior
Leaguers recently transferred from
other cities; Mrs. Kenneth M. Hoef
fel, whose committee on public re
lations was'being generally helpful;
Mrs. Donald De Veau with Mrs.
Landra Platt, the latter in smart
dark green, sitting with a group of
friends; Mrs. John Henderson chat
ting in another group; Mrs. Millard
West introducing an attractive new
comer, Mrs. Charles Carothers of
Cincinnati, and Mrs. Donald Mc
Knew in a conversational group that
included Mrs. Frederick Bradley,
Mrs. Robert Clement Watson and
Mrs. John B. Hyde, mother of the
president of the Junior League, Mrs.
Herbert Peck Fales.
Still others exchanging the latest
W'ord with all their friends on hand
were Mrs. Edwin Tribble. Miss Cecil
Jones, Miss Rose Saul, Mrs. Thacher
Winslow in a striking leopard vel
vet skirt, hat and bag and a greige
jacket; Mrs. H. L. Rust, jr.: Mrs.
Frederick Allen, Miss Anne Carter
Greene with Mrs! David Karrick,
Mrs. Charles Runyon, most recent
ex-president of the league; Miss
Dorothea Villard and Miss Evelyn
Walker; Mrs. Charles Hewes with
Mrs. Robert Reeside, Mrs. Robert
Whitelaw Wilson, Mrs. Farnham
Blair, Mrs. Hill Carter, Mrs. Henry
B. Sayler, jr.; Mrs. Lester Buchanan
with Mrs. Daniel Borden, and many
many more, but there were too many
to see.
During luncheon, clothes from
the Thrift Shop and the Junior
League Outgrown Shop were
modeled by members. Both shops|
are the chief sources of the
league's financial support for their
welfare projects. The good-looking
low-priced costumes drew one round
of applause after another. With
Miss Bliss Schumann acting as
commentator, Mrs. Charles H.
Meigs modeled a green wool dress
maker suit with matching hat that
totaled $5.50; Mrs. Grove Seeley, a
beige crepe afternoon dress for $10;j
young Hunter Echols, 9-vear-old
son of Mr. and Mrs. Emmett
Echols, a tan herringbone suit for
$15, and 11-year-old Cornelia
Hoeffel, a blue wool coat with red
lining priced at $25.
Mrs. Pales introduced Mrs. Law
rence Groner, chairman of the
Thrift Shop board, who announced
that the newly decorated room
where clothes are sold at a com
mission will be open November 3.
In introducing Mr. Kotschnig,
Mrs. Pales said that the Junior
League is dedicated to educating
its members in the responsibilities
of citizenship and today the duties
of citizenship transcend national
boundaries.
Speaks on U. N.
Mr. Kotschnig, who is chief of
the Division of United Nations
Economic and Social Affairs in the
State Department, began by saying
that the question of today is, is the
U. N. machinery which has been
set up giving us a false sense of
security from which we will awaken
in another war, or is it leading to
peace?
Explaining the set-up of the U. N„
describing its failures and successes
to date, the speaker pointed to the
growing block of democratic nations
that can’t help but impress Russia
and her satellite nations. With ref- j
erence to the latter always toeing
the Soviet line, he told the story
that’s currently being bandied about.
A chief of one of the satellite dele
gations was walking along in Paris
with an umbrella over his head on
a beautiful, cloudless day. When
asked by an acquaintance why the
open umbrella, he replied, “I have
just been informed it is raining in
Moscow.” I
Russia has no intention of accept
ing any kind of agreement to make
disarmament possible, Mr. Kotsch
nig said, but without the clarifica
tion of the General Assembly, which
Senator Vandenberg has called “the
town meeting of the world,” we
would be in a bog of propaganda. The
Security Council, he added, has kept
the Big Five talking and as long as
they keep talking they will not
shoot, and time, he said, is on the
side of peace not war. The work of
the Economic and Social Council will
usher in a new era of international
relations. He ended by saying that
the U. N. has done more in three
years than the League of Nations
did in 20, and what we need most
for success is firmness and patiance.
Following his address, Mrs. David
St. P. Gaillard, in Red Cross uni
form, spoke about the need for do
nors for the blood bank; Mrs. Wins
low commented briefly on the educa
tion program for the coming year,
and Miss Agnes Birney and Miss
Schumann did a skit from “Play
time,” the League's weekly tele
vision show for children that begins
again on November 0.
Seated at the head table with Mrs.
Fales and the guest speaker were
Mrs. Alexander Guyol, second vice
president; Mrs. Gerhard Gesell, fin
ance chairman; Mrs. Frank J. Sher
idan III, assistant placement chair
man; Mrs. Winslow, education chair
man; Mrs. David Varner, first vice
president; Mrs. Lewis c. Coxe, league
chairman for the Community Chest
campaign: Mrs. George W. Petti
cord, Jr., treasurer and Mrs. Carlton
Skinner, secretary.

1Debate on Arms Aid
To Europe Forecast
By O’Conor in Talk
By Ruth Dean
The American people can prepare
themselves for discussions on the
desirability of providing Western
Europe with Trrrds for insuring its
defense against the dinger of pos
sible aggression, declared Senator
O’Conor, Democrat, of Maryland,
yesterday.
“It is openly admitted,’’ Senator
O’Conor told a luncheon meeting
of the Soroptimist Club, “that the
American, British and French oc
cupation forces in Germany, to
gether with the internal forces of
the Marshall Plan countries, would
be no adequate defense if Russia,
the one possible enemy to Western
Europe, should decide to go 'on the
march’ again for new conquests.”
The speaker suggested that as
the Western European countries
make progress toward economic re
habilitation, they will become more
tempting to any potential aggressor.
At the same time, he said, "the
passage of time makes the same po
tential aggressor better prepared for
any act of aggression that she might
deem wise.”
“It is lor this reason,” he de-j
dared, "that we will likely have to
determine whether it is prudent to
supply upward of a billion dollars
to Western Europe for new arma
ments and a co-ordinated defensive
program, as the only possible guar
antee of enduring peace both dur
ing the rehabilitation period and
afterward.”
Senator O’Conor pointed out that
with the economic situation Im
proving in Western Europe, "present
indications are that ECA will scale
down its request this coming year
for future allocations for economic
recovery.” This could admit the
possibility, he continued, of divert
ing these funds to arms purposes
“if it is deemed strategic to do so.”
“A decision must be made,” Sen
ator O'Conor asserted. "To require
Europe to develop adequate arms
insurance against aggression now,
while it is just struggling through
the first phase of economic rehabil
itation, might well interfere defi
nitely with long-range economic
plans.” He warned that the addi
tion of such a burden might defer
for years the attainment of that
minimum economic level of achieve
ment which is the goal of the Mar
shall Plan. “Because Europe is
beginning the upward climb we
must be careful not to load upon
these countries bufdens which
might overtax their entire strength,”
he said.
Turning to domestic affairs, the
speaker praised the efforts of the
Commission on the Organization of
the Executive Branch of the Gov
ernment, headed by former Presi
dent Hoover. "The results of their
survey should give the people of
the United States an assurance of
the reorganization of the Govern
ment on a less costly and more
systematic basis,” said Senator
O'Conor, who organized a similar
commission in Maryland when he
was Governor of that State.

MISS SORRELL.
Announcement has been made by
Mr. and Mrs. Clarence H. Sorrell of
Hyattsville of the engagement of
their daughter, Miss Jeanne Marie
Sorrell, to Mr. Robert Warren Dorr,
witn no date named for the wedding.
Miss Sorrell studied at Wilson
Teachers’ College and Meredith Col
lege in Raleigh.
Mr. Dorr was graduated from
Mars Hill College in North Carolina.
He received his A. B. degree from
the University of Maryland, where
he now is working for his M. A.
degree and meantime is teaching at
the Oak Ridge Military Institute in
North Carolina. He is the son of
Mr. and Mrs. Philip C. Dorr and
during the war served as an ensign
in the Navy.
Lady Franks to Pour

Lady Franks, wife of the British
Ambassador, will pour tea at an
informal reception to be held at the
Florence Crittenton Home and Hos
pital tomorrow following a rally day
program sponsored by the Board of
Managers.
Mrs. Reba Barrett Smith, daugh
ter of Dr. Kate Waller Barrett, co

Mission, will also assist at the tea
table.

executive secretary of the Council
of Churches and Christian Educa
tion ?f Maryland and Delaware.
Reports of the year’s activities by

Mrs. A. J. Moore will present a pro

Polio Vaccination
Seen Likely Soon
A prediction that it soon will be
feasible to vaccinate against polio
was made yesterday before the
Woman's Club 'ot Chevy Chase by
James Waldo Fawcett, editorial
writer of The Star.
“The University of Michigan is
working on experiments to that end,
and the success of those experi
ments may be announced any min
ute,” the speaker declared.
Mr. Fawcett, who had addressed
the club during the dedication of
its clubhouse 10 years ago, spoke at
the monthly meeting which marked
the anniversary of that occasion.
Discussing “Ten Years of Many
Gains and Few Losses," the speaker
declared that the decade had been
marked by remarkable progress in
medicine. In 1938 the presumption
of life for a new-born child was
47 years, he said, while today it is 62.
“We have prolonged duration by the
application of ‘organized common
sense,’ he maintained.
Following his talk, Mr. Fawcett
gave the club a new American flag
i to replace one he had presented
10 years ago.
I During the business session, mem
bers voted to approve che board's
action in appealing to the State Tax
i Commission to reverse the county
commissioners' decision to make the
club subject to taxation.
In another action, members voted
to give $200 to the Community Chest
fund in Montgomery County ar.d
$150 to the District fund.
All of the past presidents, except
four, were introduced to the mem
bership. Three who took a promi
nent part in obtaining the clubhouse
—Mrs. Frank A. Linzel, Mrs. J. T.
Maguire and Mrs. Fred M. Kirby
reviewed their experiences.
Colonial Dames
Dinner Tonight
A dinner tonight at the Shore
ham Hotel will be one of the high
lights of the 27th biennial council
of the National Society of Colonial

expected to attend.
The council meeting, which opened
Tuesday, will continue through to
morrow when officers will be elected.
The present president, Mrs. Ethel
bert Ide Low, has served for the
past seven years.

p.m. to midnight tomorrow under

Club of Prince Georges County. Mrs.
Mabel Wilkinson is chairman of

with two of the iron curtain nations
to fill one of the posts.
In addition, Canulle Gutt, chair
man of the Executive Board, pointed
out that countries formerly enemies
Joined in electing an Italian as a
director.
Mr. Gutt also annou iced the ad
mission of Siam to the fund. He
said the admission of Pakistan is
“on its way."
One of the new executive directors
elected is Bohumil Sucharda of
Czechoslovakia. The Yugoslav rep
resentative joined with Poland,
Czechoslovakia and Finland in Mr.
Sucharda's election. He succeed
J. V. Mladek of Czechoslovakia.
Italian Is Elected.
Guido Carli of Italy was re-elected
with the votes of Italy, Greece and
Turkey. It was this election Mr.
Gutt referred to when he mentioned
former enemy countries voting to
gether.
Re-elected executive directors wera
Ahmed Zaki Bey Saad of Egypt*
Louis Rasminsky of Canada, J. W.
Beyen of the Netherlands and S. G.
McFarlane of Australia.
Newly elected to the Executive
Board were Octavio Paranagua of
Brazil, who formerly had been an
alternate; Carlos A. D'Ascoli of
Venezuela and Ernest de Selliers of
BelgAm, who also had been an
alternate.
The Monetary Fund and its twin
international financial organization,
the World Bank ,are holding their
third annual meeting in Washing
ton at the Shoreham Hotel. The
meeting closes tomorrow.
The Governors of the World Bank
were to elect executive directors this
afternoon.
These nine directors serve with
five who are appointed by the
United States, the United Kingdom,
China, France and India.
To Elect New Chairman.
A new chairman, to serve as head
of both boards of governors, will
be elected tomorrow, to succeed
Wang Yun-wu, China's Foreign Min
ister, whose term of one year is
expiring. This post previously has
been held by the United States,'
and the Unite'd Kingdom and is ex
pected to go to one of the other
larger powers.
Considerable routine business re
lating to administration and com
mittee work of both units was ex
pected to be conducted today and
tomorrow by the representatives of
47 nations.
The annual report of the World
Bank was accepted late yesterday
by its Board of Governors, in exec
utive session, after having been pre
sented earlier in the day by Presi
dent John J. McCloy.
Mr. McCloy said afterward the
report met with general commenda- j
tions, most of which were ''hearty,”;
although there were some criticism
and suggestions.
"Sbvlet Bloc Criticizes.
Some of the criticism, he said,
came from spokesmen for Poland,
Yugoslavia and Czechoslovakia, in
the eastern Soviet bloc.. Their
criticism was characterized by the
bank president, however, as being
in “good temper.”
They charged, he said, that the
bank is not following as closely as
it should the ideals of its founding
fathers to keep politics out of its
operations. They also thought, Mr.
McCloy said, that too much refer
ence had been made in the bank’s
report to the European Recovery
Program of the United States.
Sir Stafford Cripps, British Chan
cellor of the Exchequer, vice chair
man of the board, presided at the
meeting. All sessions are being held
at the Shoreham Hotel.
Rites for H. H. Brayton,
Retired Printer, Are Set
Funeral services for Harvey H.
Brayton, 78. retired Government
Printing Office printer, will be held
at 2 p.m. tomorrow at the Lee funeral
home. Fourth street and Massachu
setts avenue N.E.
Mr. Brayton, who lived at 1412
Manchester lane N.W.. died Tuesday
at the Jolliffe Nursing Home, Silver
Spring, Md.. after an illness of two
months. Burial will be in Cedar Hill
Cemetery.
A native of Janesville. Wis., Mr.
Brayton came to Washington as a j
printer with the GPO in 1899. He j
retired in 1933 and worked in thej
warehouse at Woodward Lothrop's
for four years.
He was a member of the Columbia
Typographical Union.
Surviving are his widow, Mrs.;
Margaret J. Brayton; a son. Jean H.|
Brayton of Long Island. N Y a(
daughter, Mrs. Elva B. Walker of
the Manchester lane address, and a
brother, Warren Brayton, 1217 j
Jeffersop street N.W.
Alfredo Galli, 79, Painter
Of Europe's Nobilify, Dies
By the Associated Press
NEW ORLEANS. Sept. 30.—Al
fredo Galli, noted Italian portrait
painter, died suddenly in his apart
ment-studio last night. Dr. Fer
nando Carlomacho attributed death
to a heart attack.
The 79-year-old artist was known.
Internationally for his portraits, i
His subjects have included Euro- i
pean nobility and prominent per
sons of two continents.
A native of Florence. Italy. Galli
came to the United States in 1916
He became prominent here in the
1930s when he painted numerous
well-known New Orleanians. Por
traits of children were his favor
ites. His exhibit of youthful sub
jects at Baton Rouge, La., in 1935
attracted widespread attention.
He is survived by his daughter,
Mrs. Aldo Curioni, now in Cannes,
France.

WINNERS OF POLICE AWARDS—Pvt. Temple J. Dykes (extreme left), who joined the Metro
politan Police Department because he wanted “action,” receives a “Policeman-of-the-Month”
award from Francis E. Robey, president of the Brightwood Lions Club, as Pvt. Paul V. Minnick
(extreme right), who joined the police force for the same reason, receives a similar award
from Adelbert W. Lee. Inspector Floyd Truscott looks on. —Star Staff Photo,
Two Who Joined Police Force
For 'Action' Honored tor Valor
Determination usually pays off,
and you can take the word of two
Metropolitan policemen on that.
Both Pvt. Temple J. Dykes of No.
j 6 Precinct and Pvt. Paul V. Minnick,
who drives the No. 11 Precinct patrol
i wagon, decided to join the police
I force because they were tired of their
old jobs and "wanted to get a little
more action.”
Well, they got it—a fact that was
apparent yesterday when the two
policemen were given the depart
ment's monthly award of merit for
performance of duty during August.
Took Pay Cut for Job.
Pvt. Dykes. 25, took a pay cut
when he left his job as a lieutenant
with the Capitol police to join the
Metropolitan force. His avowed
goal was “some action,” and he de
cided that “pounding a beat” in the
city would be more likely to bring it
than nodding good morning to Sen
ators and Representatives from a
swivel chair.
He was right.
Five months after being sworn in.
he came face to face with an armed
hold-up man. The bandit tried to
steal a car at gun point, eluded
Pvt. Dykes who arrived a few min
utes later, and then was seen by
the policeman a few blocks away.
Pvt. Dykes called on the man to
surrender. Instead, the suspect lev
eled his gun. The rookie policeman
dropped to the ground, took careful
aim and was ready to shoot when
he saw a woman walk directly into
his line of fire.
Precious Seconds.
Seconds were precious. Pvt. Dykes
again called on the man to surren
der. His answer was the click of
the bandit's gun as he pulled the
trigger. But the gun was set in the
safety position. The hammer
clicked harmlessly.
The confused bandit dropped his
gun, and was led aw4y, murmur
ing “It's a good thing for you my
gun wouldn't work.
For his extreme bravery, for his
unwillingness to risk an innocent
life even at the peril of his own,
Pvt. Dykes was recommended for
the award for the month of August
by his commanding officer, Capt.
Archie M. Winfree, and by Francis
E. Robey, president of the Bright
wood Lions' Club and vice president
j of the Bank of Commerce and Sav
ings.
Pvt. Minnick, 33, had decided
that a life as a repairman atop a
telephone pole wasn't the most ac
tive in the world.
He joined the force in May. 1942,
was commended for clearing up
some housebreakings, participated
in some thrilling chases—and then,
last August 12, heard the radio
j call which really spurred him into
action.
"Investigate a man in the Ana
jcostia River off Benning Bridge."
i the radio blared. Pvt. Minnick sped
to the scene. Leaving his partner
on one side of the bridge, he went
i to the other.
j There he saw a hand, just disap
pearing under the river's surface.
Fully clothed, he stopped only to
strip off his gun and his cap.
Subdued After Struggle.
Jumping from the bank, he swam
to the victim's side, 40 feet out. The
drowning man was panic-stricken.
He struggled. But Pvt. Minnick,
j who later said "I couldn’t have
i swum an inch further,” subdued
the man, and swam with him back
i to shore.
While other police on the scene
called a Rescue Squad, Pvt. Minnick
l applied artificial respiration.
Both Capt. James E. Silvea, his
commanding officer, and Capt. Wil
jliam T. Murphy, head of the new
No. 14 Precinct, recommended Pvt.
Minnick for the award. So did Adel
bert W. Lee, a member of the Board
of Education, who yesterday pre
sented the policeman W'ith his medal
land certificate.
Two citations were given for the
month, because the Award of Merit
'Committee, composed of six news
papermen, was unable to decide
which act was the more worthy. _
I The policemen were honored dur
ing a meeting of the Lions Club at:
iHaeringer’s Buffeteria, 4723 Four
teenth street N.W.
Both officers blushed and pro
tested they had only done their:
duty. But, they admitted, as they
examined the pen and pencil sets j
given them by the Lion's Club, with
the approval of the District Com
missioners, it "sure was swell” to
get the awards.
Thomas Armat Dead;
D.C. Man Was Pioneer
Oi Movie Industry
Thcmas Armat, 81. a pioneer of
the motion picture industry, died
today at his home. Greystone, on
the edge of Rock Creek Park. He
had been in poor health for some
time.
Mr. Armat turned his attention to
motion pictures, then little more
than a curiosity, in the 1890s, when
he was already a successful Wash
ington real estate man. His in
ventions were credited with playing I
an important part in development
of a practical film projector. For
a time, he was associated in business
with Thomas A. Edison.
His work in helping to take the
motion picture industry out of the
"peepshow'' era was recognized last
March by the Academy of Motion
Picture Arts and Sciences. It
award him its coveted statuette, the
“Oscar.”
Mr. Armat is survived by his wife,
two sons, Thomas Armat. jr., and
Christoper Brooke Armat, and a
brother. Christopher Armat, of Lake
land. Fla
Funeral services will be private at
Mr. Armat's birthplace, Fredericks
burg, Va.
Herbert S. Bryant Retires;
41 Years With Smithsonian
Herbert S. Bryant, chief of the
division of correspondence and rec
ords at the National Museum of the
Smihtsonian Institution, retired to-;
day after 51 years in Government
service. He had been with the
Smithsonian for the last 41 years.
Mr. Bryant was born in Washing
ton in 1878. was graduated from
Business High School and attended
the Corcoran Scientific School, now
a part of George Washington Uni
versity, as a. special student. He
and his wife live at 304 Rittenhouso
street N.W.
He became a Patent Office mes
senger August 1, 1897, and four years
later transferred to the Past Office
Department as secretary' to the as
sistant general superintendent of
Railway Mail service. In 1907 he
went to the National Museum as a
clerk. At that time the natural
history building was under con
struction at Constitution avenue
and Tenth street N.W.
Since 1918 he has been chief of
the division of correspondence and
records. He was in charge of of
ficial correspondence, distribution of
museum publications and the regis
tration of thousands of specimens
from all fields.
Civic Style Show Planned
Miss Jody Miller, Miss Washing
ton of 1948, will be one of the
models participating in a style
show at the meeting of the West
over Civic Association at 8 p.m. to
morrow in the Westover Baptist
Church, 1125 North Patrick Henry
drive, Arlij^ton.
Gurney Asks Resumption
Of Relations With Spain
By th* Associated Press
MADRID, Sept. 30.—Senator Gur
ney, S. Dak., chairman of the Senate
Armed Forces Committee, advocated
today the re-establishment of "com
plete relations between Spain and
the other great powers,” Including
j the United States.
Senator Gurney made this state
ment to reporters after an hour's talk
at El Pardo Palace with General
i issimo Fr ancisco Franco. He added
that, in his opinion "economic re
covery of Spain must go hand in
hand with that of the other coun
tries of Europe.”
The United States. Britain and
many other nations withdrew Am
bassadors from Spain after the
United Nations adopted a resolution
in 1946 condemning Gen. Franco's
regime.
Senator Gurney arrived here Wed
nesday from Paris on the last leg of
a tour of Europe.
Mrs. Jascha Heifetz
Gives Birth to Son
By the Associated Pres*
HOLLYWOOD. Sept. 30.—Mrs.
Frances Heifetz, 36, wife of Violinist
Jasche Heifetz, gave birth to a 6
pound 10 ounce boy last night at
Cedars of Lebanon Hospital.
It was the first child for the 47
year-old virtuoso and the former
j Frances Spiegelberg, who were mar
ried in January, 1947. He was di- j
vorced from Florence Vidor, silent j
film star, in 1945.
3 Feared Dead in Crash
Of Plane in Virginia
By the Associated Press
KEYSVILLE, Va„ Sept. 30,—A
military plane, tentatively identi
fied as a National Guard craft from
a Georgia base, crashed and ex
ploded near here yesterday, killing
its occupants.
An Army spokesman at Camp
Pickett said the plane apparently
was from either the Marietta or
Warner Robbins Air Bases in
Georgia.
He added at least two persons had
been identified by papers found in;
the wreckage and checked against j
a loading list field at Mitchell Field,
N. Y., the plane's destination. The
spokesman said three men were be
lieved to have been in the plane.
The names of those dead were
not released.
Police Sergt. E. P. Landers said
the plane, flying low, struck the
ground and exploded about 300
yards from the farm home of W. H.
Townsend, approximately 6 miles
northeast of Keysville, on Route
685, near the Charlotte County line.
The impact dug a crater about
100 feet long in an open field, Sergt.
Landers said. He added that the
trench was about 20 feet wide and
8 feet deep at one end.

FBI Launches Inquiry
Into 'Quite a Few'
Draft-Dodging Cases
The Federal Bureau of Investi
gation today was investigating
“quite a few” cases of apparent
draft-dodging over the Nation
as Selective Service reported that
8,584,963 men, 18 through 25
years of age, had registered.
Locally there was still a flow of
delinquent registrants, but the Dis
trict officials had taken on action
other than to warn them that after
today they w'ould be placed at the
head of the induction list.
Regulations Cited.
William D. Leahy, District di
rector, pointed out that one se
lective service regulation provided
that “any registrant who is delin
quent shall, regardless of his age.
be selected and ordered to report
for induction before any other reg
istrant.” More than 1,000 have reg
istered late here.
“They just can't register at their
own pleasure and the obligation to
register is theirs,” Mr. Leahy com
mented. The number of tardy reg
istrants throughout the Nation was
believed to be small.
Of the total men registered, 2,147.
813 are single, non-veterans who
will form the pool from which the
Army will get its new manpower.
676,082 Born in ’29.
Officials said men born in 1929 ap
parently offer more draft eligibles
than any other age group, with a
reported total of 676,082 being single
.and non-veterans. Only 33,001 men
born in 1922 are in this classifica
tion.
i During the last wrar Selective
Service registered more than 40,000,
1000 men 18 through 45. A total of
24.000.000 registered for the World
War 1 draft.
River Terrace Group
Elects Area Delegates
The first set of area delegates
provided for by a recent amend
ment to its constitution was elected
last night by the River Terrace
Citizens’ Association.
The area delegates will represent
geographical areas of the associa
tion on its Executive Committee. It
is expected their presence on the
committee will facilitate handling of
minor problems of the group.
Delegates elected were: Edward
M. Rothschild. B. S. Dzuibinski,
Robert H. Wells, Howard E. Isaacs,
Howard V. Jones, Sam Krawitz,
James E. Briley, Joseph Hurwitz and
Mrs. Marv V. Smith.
The group elected its former vice
president, Robert F. Tibbetts, presi
dent for the coming year.
James R. Vernon was re-elected
treasurer. Other officers elected
were Bernard E. Roache. vice presi
dent, and Mrs. Howard E. Isaacs,
secretary.
Announcement was made of the
appointment of delegates to the
Northeast Council of Citizens’ Asso
ciations and the Federation of Citi
zens’ Associations by the Executive
Committee. They are Chester V.
Parker and Mr. Tibbetts to the
Federation, and Mr. Isaacs and
Frank Frazer to the council.
The meeting was held in the River
Terrace Church, Anacostia avenue
and Clay street N.E.

Survey, will be associated with him

Deaf Star Employe Who Piloted
Plane Across Nation Gets Medal
Shown at the White House presentation of a gold medal to
Rhulin A. Thomas, linotype operator for The Star, are (left to
right) Maj. Gen. Harry H. Vaughan, military aide to the Presi
dent; Mrs. Thomas, Mr. Thomas and their 12-year-old daughter
Ruth Mae* —Star Staff Photo*
Rhulin A. Thomas, 38, linotype
operator for The Star who is cred
ited with being the first deaf man to
fly an airplane across the country,1
today received a gold medal for the
achievement during ceremonies at
the White House.
The medal, an award sponsored
by the International Association for
the Deaf and the Missouri Associa
tion for the Deaf, was presented by
Maj. Gen. Harry Vaughan, militery
aid to the President.
Mr. Thomas, who was accompanied
to the ceremony by his wife and
daughter—both of whom also are
deaf—was told'’ by Gen. Vaughan
that “it was a distinct pleasure to
be acting for the President in mak
ing the award.”
Mr. Thomas replied in sign lan
guage, which was translated.by Wil
liam J. McClure, principal of the
Kendall School for the Deaf, a part
of Gallaudet College.
The deaf flyer made the coast-to
coast trip last year in order to greet
George Truman and Cliff Evans on
their return from their round-the
world flight in light planes. Neither
Mr. Truman, w;ho taught Mr.
Thomas to fly, nor Mr. Evans were
able to be present today. The
Star employe declared he accepted
the medal in behalf of all deaf
people and hoped more deaf people
wrould learn to fly and to drive auto
mobiles.
Mr. Thomas was introduced to
Gen. Vaughan by Truman Ingle,
superintendent of the Missouri

association. Plans to encourage ex
panded use of 16-mm. film will be'
discussed.

Reds Say U. S. Evades
Direct Reply on Korea
■y Associated Pros*
MOSCOW, Sept. 30—Tass. tha
official Soviet news agency, said to
day that the United States has “de
clined to make a direct answer” to
a Russian note proposing the with
drawal of both American and Rus
sian troops from Korea.
The United States delivered a
reply to the Soviet government
Tuesday stating that the question
of withdrawing American troops
from Korea is "part of a broader
question."
The presidium of the Supreme
Soviet announced September 19
that all Russian troops will be evac
uated from Northern Korea by Jan
uary 1. 1949. and expressed the hope
the United States would follow suit
in Southern Korea.
The State Department announced
in Washington September 30 in
effect that American troops would
be kept in Korea until the United
Nations gave the word to with
draw’ them. It pointed out that
the United Nations Commission on
Korea, which the Russians had not
permitted to enter their occupation
zone, would report to the U. N.
General Assembly during the cur
rent session in Paris.
SEOUL, Korea, Sept. 30 OP).—
American Ambassador John J.
Muccio told a Korean news confer
ence today he can not envisage any
agreement between the United
States and Russia that would per
manently divide Korea,
j The country is now divided into
American and Russian occupation
j zones with a government in each
(zone.
