Dewey Seen Certain
To Win With Popular
Vote Fairly Close
House to Stay Republican
But Outcome in Senate
Still Is Uncertain
By David Lawrence
Oov. Dewey will be elected by a
substantia! majority In the electoral
college He la assured of a mini
mum of 266—necessary for election
—and his majority will range from
75 to 100 electoral vote*.
The House of Representatives will
remain Republican by a comfortable
maioritj but the Senate will be or
ganized by the Republicans by the
narrowest margin In recent history.
The reason for Gov. Dewey's vic
tory is that which has emerged In
many presidential elections—the peo
ple vote against the man in the
white House when things do not
go well with the household budget
or when there is a feeling that a
President Is really not equal to the
job.
The campaign just closed was
wholly a pro-Truman or anti-Tru
man affair. The question of what
Oov. Dewey said or did was not the
issue. The Republican nominee
satisfied the regblar Republican
voters and Mr. Truman supplied him,
in addition, with a substantial num
ber of Democrats and Independent
voters who simply could not bring
themselves to vote for the Demo
cratic nominee. This trend against
Mr. Truman was manifest last sum
mer when so many leaders in his
own party tried to persuade him to
withdraw. Some other Democratic
nominee would have made the race
closer and might have won.
The very trend that revolves
around the economic well-being of
the individual gives Mr. Truman a
substantial vote in the farm .com
munities, especially In the West be
cause of the high prices for crops.
The protest, however, against the
cost of living which usually is
blamed on the President in office
meant a high vote for Gov. Dewey
In the urban communities. Because
conditions are not uniform in all
sections, Mr. Truman will make a
better showing on popular vote than
on electoral votes. Less than 4,000,
000 votes out of 51,000,000 will sep
arate the two contenders.
This is one reason why the Sen
ate race is so close. The Senate at
present has 51 Republicans and 45
Democrats. Because only one third
of the Senate plus any vacant seats
are up for election every two years
and because so many of the seats
happen this year to be in the South
and West, a few contests can swing
the Senate either way. Inasmuch
as only six seats separate the par
ties, if the Democrats gain four
they would have 49 and the Repub
licans 47 of the 96 seats.
Reports from the 8tates where 33
seats are up for election, and the 33d
seat where there has been a va
cancy that has to be filled, show
that the pivotal contests are to be
found in Montana, Idaho, 4lew
Mexico, Wyoming, Colorado. Iowa,
Oklahoma, Minnesota, Kentucky
and West Virginia.
The Democratic Party strength is
expected to be maintained in Idaho,
Colorado and New Mexico. Inas
much as Oklahoma is formally
Democratic, a change from the Re
publican to the Democratic column
is anticipated or a net gain of one
for the Democrats.
This narrows the contest to six
seats—Kentucky and West Virginia,
in the area east of the Mississippi
River, and Montana, Wyoming,
Iowa and Minnesota in the West.
Neither Candidate Sure.
It is an old rule in politics that
if the top of the ticket is running
strong he carries so many “straight
ticket” voters with him that the
“split ticket” vote is overcome.
Neither candidate for the presidency
is running strong enough in three
of the Western 8tates—Montana,
Wyoming and Minnesota—to drag
in a senatorial candidate “by the
coattails” as the saying goes in
politics. In Kentucky, however, Mr.
Truman could sweep in the Dem
ocratic nominee while Gov. Dewey
could carry in the Republican in
West Virginia. It is significant, on
the other hand, that in neither
Kentucky nor West Virginia is there
any conceding being done as the
predictions vary with the experts
interviewed.
It looks, however, as If Senator
Cooper, Republican, is strong enough:
on his own to win in Kentucky andj
as if Senator Revercomb, Repub-'
Hean, will squeeze through in West
Virginia on the “coattail” vote
which means the Republicans will
retain these two seats.
It looks also as if Senator Mur
ray, Democrat, will lose in Montana!
which offsets the Democratic gain1
in Oklahoma.
This leaves Iowa, where Senator
Wilson, Republican, will be carried
In by the Dewey vote, and Wyoming,
where a similar close squeeze for
the Republican incumbent may be
expected. In Minnesota the odds
favor the Democrat in place of
Senator Ball, Republican.
This would make the Anal tally
SO Republicans and 48 Democrats.
Thus the Republicans could still lose
in Wyoming or West Virginia and
have a 49-to-47 lead but if both
States are lost to the Republicans
and the “coattail” vote does not ma
terialize for the Republicans as ex
pected by many veteran observers
it would mean a tie at 48 to 48 and
the vote of the Vice President, Gov.
Wwren, would swing the Senate in
to Republican ranks. It seems clear,
therefore, that the Senate will be
organized as to committee chair
manships and the like by the Re
publican Party.

large part the basis for the above
predictions. It is compiled from the

papers in the doubtful States. The

candidates seemed “sure” of elec

This Changing World
Victor in Tomorrow’s Election to Get
Blueprint of West World Organization
By Constantine Brown
The blueprint for ft gigantic or
ganization of the Western world, to
provide greater resistance to Rus
sia's pressure, will be presented to
the victor In
tomorrow’s gen
eral election.
The theme of
this blueprint,
prepared, it is
reliably reported,
by the chancel
lories of West
ern European
and Western
Hemisphere gov
ernments, is the
pooling of all
manpower, in
dustrial and raw
material re
sources of the
ComUntlno Brown.
free nations, and their use for peace
or war, depending on how the
U. S. S. R. acts in the future.
The whole thing is predicated, of
course, on the likelihood that the
cold war will continue for some time
and that a shooting war will be
avoided.
From the logistic point of view,
the U. 6.‘ S. R. and its satellites
are far better off than the Axis
was during World War II. The
blockade of Germany, Italy and
Japan by the Allies contributed to
a great extent to the defeat of
those powers, since it deprived them,
especially near the end, of vit&l raw
materials and essential supplies for
their war activities.
Don’t Need Sea Trade.
Russia and her satellites are in a
much better position. They are not
dependent on seaborne trade in any
way. Today they have a greater po
tential of raw materials, such as oil,
iron ore, manganese and food, than
the loosely *knit coalition of West
ern nations.
Ideological diplomacy, a hangover
from the days of our honeymoon
with Moscow, still plays-an import
ant part in the Western powers’
conduct of International affairs,
with the result that up to this day
we have made no real effort to
improve relations with strategically
important Spain.
While our relations with some of!
the South American republics, such'
as Argentina, have been placed on
somewhat saner basis than a year
ago, there is no real co-operation
between all the nations which are
the target for the Kremlin’s con
spiracies.
We are not ad well prepared as
the Soviet bloc Insofar as raw ma
terials are concerned. In order to
remedy this situation discussions
have quietly taken place to. organ
ize the Western nations into an
effective group with standardized
armaments and the means for easy
interchange of raw materials and
manufactured products.
Argentinians Change.
The Argentinians, for instance,
who were badly kicked around by
the United States in the days when
Spruille Braden was assistant sec
retary of state in charge of Latin
America relations, have foreseen
that Western diplomacy would take
a new turn and have let bygones be
bygones. They realized that in this
coming fight for the survival of the I
Western civilization personal pique
must be forgotten. The activities of
Juan Bramuglia, the Argentine for
eign minister, in the United Nations
have fully -demonstrated the new
trend.
If Moscow does nothing to pre
vent an armed clash in the near
future, it is hoped that the con
solidation of all resources of the
Western nations will help bring the
Kremlin to its senses. This new
bloc will have a population far
greater than that of the Eastern
bloc. It will control a larger quan
tity of raw materials and will have
far greater technological know-how.
In time of war, when mobiliza
tion of resources otherwise would be
somewhat slower than that of the
already mobilized Eastern bloc, the
existence of a Western Economic
organization "Will enable the United
States and its Allies to make a far
greater war effort than the Soviet
bloc can make.
If by some miracle common sense
prevails in the Kremlin, the merger
of Western resources could make a
great contribution to the economic
and political rehabilitation of the
world.
The blueprint, while having as its
immediate objective the consolida
tion of the Western forcefc, can have
far-reaching consequences in war
or peace.
On the Record
Peace Seen Possible Only if Neither U. S.
Nor Russia Gains European Goptrol
By Dorothy Thompson
When Oen. Omar Bradley said
last Thursday that although the
possibility of war was grave, there
is an even greater probability that
America ana the
Soviet Union
would live in a
state of tension
for a generation
or more, I felt
that he was
choosing the bet
ter of the two
alternatives but
doubting his
own hopes.
No "state of
tension” such as
now exists can
conceivably en
dure for many
more months.
Derrthy THmimai.
When a stalemate is rfache*i be
tween two highly armed great
powers, either one must retreat or!
a settlement be achieved on an
entirely new basis.
It is inconceivable that, under
present circumstances, either of the
two great powers will, or even can,
retreat. Each is a prisoner of the
situation that has developed out
of the war and wartime events and
policies. Russia and the United
States each has justifiable reasons
to regard the other as a menace.
If one could rise above this planet
to look down on the struggle with
utmost objectivity, one would, I
think, observe that in the pattern
of power relationships as they exist
at present, each of the protagonists
in this drama is acting from identi
cal motives and fears. Each fore
sees that one more step from the
opponent might create a situation
where it would be impossible to
organize a defense and one would
be left at the mercy of the other.
Defense Is Key.
Since the elemental condition for!
the existence of any state is its
capacity to defend itself, the issue
becomes one of life and death re
gardless of the forms and contents
of the states in question.
The basic cause of World War I
and of the present cold war is the j
failure of the peacemakers, In both
cases, to recognise some funda
mental facts regarding power rela
tionships. The Congress of Vienna,
following the Napoleonic wars,
managed after many failures and
adjournments to construct a peace
system which endured, in all funda
mental matters, for nearly 100
years. This was because its en
gineers. regardless of ideological
differences and conflicts of national
interest, recognized the first axiom
of enduring peace between sovereign
states. This axiom can be so stated:
“The condition for a lasting peace
Is the construction of such a sys
tem of power relationships that it
is to the Interest of all the powers,
victor and vanquished, to preserve
it, while no power or combination
of powers can anticipate overturn
ing it with success.”
Trie Versailles system broke1 down
because the power on which it rest
ed in Eastern Europe was a fiction,
and because it was not to the in
terest of two powers—both at that
time weak but potentially strong—
to preserve it, since it was directed
against them both. Each had a com
mon interest to destroy it as well
as a calculable opportunity. These
powers were Germany and Russia.
The system began to crumble in
1922 when Russia apd Germany
clasped hands at the Genoa Con
ference, and exploded in 1939 when
Stalin give Hitler the green light.
Thus, despite the fact that World
War I had arisen out of a struggle
between Germanic and Slavic in
terests in Southeastern Europe, a
changed power situation led them
to combine against a condition
tolerable to neither.
Conditions Must Be Changed.
Satisfactory international rela
tions obviously are impossible if a
condition exists or threatens under
which one great power is in a posi
tion to destroy the other. Here we
come to the present impasse. If the
Soviet Union adds Germany to her
power potential, she can win
Europe and be in a position to
destroy us. On the other hand, if
the United States can control West
'era Europe and Germany, she will
be In a position to destroy the
'Soviet Union.
Both, the United States and the
U. S. S. R. must therefore, in con
sideration of basic security, prevent
the other from accomplishing this
objective. The retreat of either will
lead to the accomplishment of the
objective by the other. The at
tempt of both to reach the same
objective will lead to war. War
only can be averted, therefore, by
the relinquishment of the objective
by both contenders, under condi
tions which make It impossible for
either to reopen the contest, and to
the interests of both to preserve
the conditions.
Is this possible t
It is not possible If, as American
and Russian policymakers both
seem to believe, either is out for
world domination.
But if we may assume that the
hysterical recriminations and polit
ical tactics of both parties arise
from the impasse Itself, and would
change were a solution to be found,
then it is entirely possible. It would
require not minor compromises, but
agreement regarding the goal to be
achieved (the security of both) and
the means for achieving it which
do not, in reality, afford any wide
range of choices.

McLemore-^
Japanese Describes
His Nation's Difficulties
By Henry McLemore
How does an enlightened Jap
anese feel about the occupation
and the vast change which his
country is ur.d:rgci:\' I asked
Kazutaka, Watanabe about this
and he graciously agreed to
write an arztcle or two for me.
Mr. Watanabe is managing di
rector of the Institute.of Politics
and Economy at the present time,
and is a graduate of Colgate Uni
versity, class of 1925.
By Kazutaka Watanabe
TOKYO, Japan.—All of us ln|
Japan are In, a state of vacuum.
We thought our Emperor was In
fallible, our "glorious” army and
navy undefeatable, and our father
land should never be "soiled by for
eign enemies.”
But, alas! All proved to be un
true. Things which we had always
revered, valued and cherished were
all thrown away like broken tiles.
We lost our faith and trust in every
thing The sense of certainty was
mercilessly shattered. What can it
be then that we can be sure of?
What can it be that we can depend
on? We are cast Into a vacuum.
The time-old principle of feudal
ism—“make the people depend on
the government; don’t let people
know things”—lasted firmly until
the surrender. The simplest way
to gdVem is to command, the
easiest way to live is to obey. We
had been long made to stand under
the celling lamp and depend on it.
As long as we stay under it we have
light, but the minute the light goes
out, we are in total darkness of
chaotic emptiness. t
Every One Here Has Lantern.
In America every person carries k
a lantern in his hand, however
small It may be, and finds his way
wherever he goes. He is more used
to his lantern than to stand under
the ceiling lamp. But here In
Japan we have never been given a
lantern of our own.
Therefore, when we are left in
darkness to find our way we feel
extremely uneasy and nervous. It;
is so strange and uncomfortable not
to have a lamp over our heads. We
become very anxious to go to a room
where a lamp is hung from the ceil
ing and where we can feel secure in
light.
It is clear that we cannot remain
in this state of vacuum. It must be
filled with something, good or bad.
Democracy is to be put in there, to
be sure. We all know it is the finest
form of life.
Japs Have Mach to Learn.
Until the Americans occupied
Japan, the Japanese knew little
about how to think for thegiselves.
They still have so much to learn.
To the average Japanese it is so
much easier to have someone tell
him what to do, what to think
some one to give tym a whole pat
tern of life. That is why communism
must not be allowed to gain a fool
hold in Japan. The Communists
Come in with ready-made schemes
and even though they are evil
schemes the average Japanese is
tempted to follow because it takes
all responsibility of thinking off his
shoulders, just like the old a:.„ j be
fore the war.
The Japanese have a craving for
some cure-all medicine., that will
remove them from this state of
suspension they are now in. De
mocracy will take many years, and
the Japanese are too impatient.
As I said before, we are a peo
ple in a state of vacuum.
What is going to fill this dread
ful emptiness?
Will it be democracy?
Or will it be communism?
I and millions of Japanese hopo
and pray it will be democracy, given
to us by America.

Grounds Plans
Laid for 1950
History Pageant
Area at Monument
And Sylvan Theater
To Be Revamped
Government officials today laid
plans to improve the Washington
Monument Grounds and revamp the
Sylvan Theater for the projected
sesquicentennial pageant in 1950.
At the same time, the National
Capital Sesquicentennial Commis
sion promised to sponsor "the great
est lesson in American history and
American traditions ever put before
the American people.”
The National Capital Park an$
Planning Commission has prepared
a model of the Monument Grounds
to provide for additional walks and
trees. Congress will be asked for
funds to do the work before the
sesquicentennial celebration.
Maj. Gen. V. S. Grant III, chair
man of the' planning commission,
yesterday showed the model to the
Fine Arts Commission, meeting at
the Interior Department. The Fine
Arts Commission expressed ap
proval of the idea but asked that
even more walks and trees be pro
vided.
Consultations Planned.
H P. Caemmerer, secretary of the
Fine Arts Commission, said his group!
and the planners will be in periodic
consultation over the improvement
program.
Meanwhile, the National Capital
Parks is considering a $250,000 pro
gram to redevelop the Sylvan Thea
ter on the Monument Grounds,
according to Harry T. Thompson,
assistant superintendent.
Paul Green, Pulitzer prize winner
and pageant author, is considering
the writing of a pageant for Wash
ington's 150th birthday. The re
developed Sylvan Theater would;
have facilities for scenery storage,
workshop and dressing rooms, an
enlarged stage, a new sound system
and semipermanent seating for
3 000 persons.
While Mr. Thompson said the
outdoor theater can seat about 3,000,
it was designed many years ago
and does not possess up-to-date
facilities.
If Mr. Green decides to write the
pageant, it would be produced during
the entire summer of 1950 as a
feature of the sesquicentennial cele
bration, officials explained.
"Freedom” to Be Theme.
Meanwhile, the sesquicentennial
rommission, through its director,
Edward Boykin, announced that the
theme of the sesquicentennial ob
servance will be Freedom.
Dr. Luther Evans, Librarian of
Congress, in indorsing the plans, de
clared:
"We shall be celebrating the en
during national unity of a great
people whose ability to govern them
selves is here exemplified. We
shall be celebrating the irresistible
force of the idea on which this Re
public was founded—the concept of
free association of sovereign States,
whose national unity was and is ex
pressed in this their Federal City,
the District of Columbia.”
M. P. Kills Prisoner Trying
To Escape at Fort Myer
An Army military policeman shot
and killed Pvt. Robert H. Smith, 20,
of Jamestown, N. Y., w'hen he at
tempted to escape from a guard
house at Fort Myer, Army authori
ties revealed yesterday.
Pvt. Smith, the Army said, had
been released to the military au
thorities by the Washington police j
after serving a four-month sentence
for stealing an automobile. He was
being held for further investigation
when he tried to escape last
Wednesday.
The escape was attempted from a
building on the Fort Myer reserva
tion where Pvt. Smith was being
detained under guard. The soldier
died at Walter Reed Hospital several
hours after the shooting.
Army authorities identified the
guard who shot Pvt. Smith as Pvt.
Gerald G. Wilkins of the 3d Infan
try Regiment, whose home is in
Marshfield, Wis. Pvt. Wilkins is
being held in “technical custody,”
the Army said, but no specific
charge has been placed against him
Lt. Col. William F. Nee, public
information officer for the Military
District of Washington, attributed
the five-day delay in releasing the
story to the fact thjt an investiga
tion of the shooting was under way
and the full facts had not been
determined.

night, approved, with minor reserva

land National Capital Park and

Virginia Denies Lack
Of Equal Recreation
Facilities for Negroes
By th« Associated Press
RICHMOND, Va„ Sept. 14 —State
recreational facilities provided lor
Negroes “have been relatively equal”
to those provided for whites consid
ering “the relative demand,” the
Virginia attorney general's office
said yesterday.
In a brief filed in Federal Court
here, answering a Negro’s suit
charging discrimination, the office
said that on substantial demand, the
State would not refuse to provide
additional State park and recrea
tional facilities for Negro citizens,
similar to those provided for whites.
Meanwhile attorneys for the Surry
County School Board put the finish
ing touches on a report of progress
in equalizing facilities in white and
Negro schools, to be presented to
Federal District Court in Richmond
tomorrow.
Arlington Case Arises.
In Arlington, another test case in 1
the “equal facilities” issue popped
up yesterday when a group of Negro
children sought, and were refused,
enrollment in Washington-Lee High
School for whites.
The pupils, who have attended the
Hoffman-Boston school for colored,
claimed that recent improvements
in the school were "unsatisfactory"
and failed to bring facilities to the
level maintained for white children.
It was the third time groups of
Negro pupils had sought registration
at white high schools in Virginia.
Last week, similar instances oc
curred at Botetourt High School,
Gloucester, and at King George
County High School. In all cases i
the Negroes were refused admission
because of the segregation statute.
Deadline Is Tomorrow.
Tomorrow is the deadline for the
Surry County “equalization'’ report;
before Judge Sterling Hutcheson in
Federal District Court.
In the recreation facilities suit,!
the attorney general's brief said j
the State is willing to provide ad
ditional park and recreational
facilities, "including overnight ac
commodations for Negro citizens
similar to those provided white
citizens to meet proportionately the
actual demand for such facilities.”
The plaintiff, Maceo Conrad Mar
tin of Danville, named in his suit—
“on behalf of the Negroes of Vir
ginia”—the State Department of
Conservation and Deve'opment. the
Division of Parks and Randolph
Odell, commissioner of the Division
of Parks.

A mechanical shovel closes the last gap In the dike which surrounds a rough rectangle of

Hains Point Slipping Into River;
Army Begins Reclamation Job
By Charles J. Yarbrough
Haips Point, reclaimed from a
Potomac River swamp for one of
Washington’s favorite playgrounds,
Is slipping back to the river.
To reclaim it once more and at
the same time resume war-delay
ing dredging of the rival channel,
Army engineers are ready to begin
pumping silt into a huge diked area
on the point’s northwest section.
The dike, 18 feet high around
50 acres of the now-unused area,
is being closed today, and pumping
will begin immediately after the
President's cup Regatta. The dredg
ing will be along the approximate
course of the regatta.
The operation will be the first
i phase of a long-range plan calling
for almost complete covering of the
327-acre East Potomac Park with
river silt to build it higher above the
water level, National Capital Park
and Planning Commission officials
said.
Created in Same Manner.
It was created in exactly that
manner, beginning in 1890, and more
than 20,018,000 lineal yards of silt
were pumped into the swamps that
are now East and West Potomac
Park. Hains Point, named for En
gineer Col. Peter Hains who had
charge of the project, has been
sinking back into the river marsh
under it at the rate of a half inch
per year.
Officials estimated that by 1990
the entire tip of the point would
have been back under water.
Heavy rains, unusually high tides
and strong upstream winds flood the
tip now and leave water standing
in the lower spots of the entire area
for days.
The overall reclamation plan,
which officials estimate will take
years, will mean burying, section by
section, parts of four golf courses,
a score or more tennis courts, roads
and sidewalks. How rapidly the
project progresses depends on the
Army Engineers, how swiftly the
river channel fills and how fre
quently it is dredged.
Despite its dampness, the point
has been a popular spot for driving
since young dandies raced their
horses and rubber-tired buggies
around the "speedway’’ which
rimmed the morass.
Completed in 1914.
As it stands now, the park is builtj
from river silt and from dirt ex
cavated from the Tidal Basin, which
had been cut to a depth of 8 feet
in 1894. It was completed in 1914.
but Haines Point was ringed by a
sea wall as early at 1894.
Cost of the East and West Po
tomac Park projects was over $2,
500,000. During World War I much
of the Hains Point area was turned
into war gardens. Troop barracks
dotted its flat fields. Its real de
velopment began immediately after
the war. By 1931, its 327 acres were
taken up with golf courses and the
Washington Tourist camp in the
southwest corner.
The teahouse near the tip of the
Point was built in the early 1920s.
The continued settling, much
more rapid in the early years, has
necessitated constant re-filling. More
than 1,800,000 lineal yards of silt
went on the peninsula in 1912, and
several hundred thousands yards I
have been poured on since that
time, Park officials say.
Despite this, the river is still try
ing. The tip and its water-side road i
has been under water 27 times in
the last 20 years.
National Capital Parks is trying1
to get it out of reach.

The Falls Church City Council

of the Rosslyn Gas Co. rates.

cil up to its required size of nine
members. The council size was In
creased when Falls Church assumed

August 16 and the three additional
ccuncilmen were appointed by the
other councilmen. Those sworn in
last night were John R. Eakln,
Samuel J. Dennis and Lee M.
Rhoads.
Mayor Albert Orme urged that
the Board of Trustees of the Falls
Church teen canteen report to the

nishings be insured for $5,000.

berlane and Spring streets at an
estimated cost of $8,000 was ap
proved. Mr. Dunn estimated that

ment.
Six standing committees were ap
pointed. The chairmen appointed

finance; H. H. McCarthy, annexa

and grounds; Mr. Chanel, utilities,

Virginia Buses'
Daytime Service
To Be increased
Company to Abolish
Trips to Garden City
After 7 at Night
Effective tomorrow, the Washing
ton, Virginia Maryland Coach
Co. will increase daytime service
on all bus lines between the Dis-
trict and Virginia points and will
abolish night service to Garden
City.
The Garden City service will be
eliminated after 7 p.m. because pat
ronage has not been satisfactory,
J. F. Stokely, traffic manager of
the company, said today. Uke other
lines, however, it will share an
increase daytime service, wtih one
more bus edch hour through the
day.
The Garden City line, via Lee
highway and Key Bridge, was in
augurated May 19. Under the new
schedule, the last bus for Garden
City (3-G route) will leave Eleventh
and E streets N.W. at 7 p.m. on week
days and at 6:55 p.m. Saturdays.
Falls Church Service.
Mr. Stokely said the principal in
crease in service will be for patrons
of the 3-F route to Falls Church
via Lee highway and Key Bridge.
Besides reducing the headway, or
time between buses, by the addition
of a bus every hour during the day,
the company has added two after
noon rush hour express buses from
the District to Falls Church.
Mr. Stokely said the express trips,
leaving the Washington terminal
point at 5:05 p.m. and 5:35 p.m.,
would pick up passengers at all
stops within the District but would
proceed nonstop to Falls Church
after reaching the bridge.
Saving of 10 Minutes Expected.
The traffic manager estimated
that the express trips would cut
from 10 to 15 minutes oft the present
scheduled running time of 49 min
utes to Falls Church in the late aft
ernoon.
On the 3-F Falls Church line, Mr.
Stokely said, headways which have
been from 12 to 15 minutes will be
cut to 10, and those which have
been 30 minutes will be cut to 20.
On the 3-B route, Glebe road and
Lee highway, the company is adding
two buses every hour all day through
7 p.m., making the daytime head
way 10 minutes instead of 15.

Miss Elizabeth Meigs,
Long District Court
Aide, Dies at 85
Miss Elizabeth Martin Meigs, 85,
known as "Miss Lizzie” to hundreds
of older lawyers, court attaches and
judges at District Court, died yester
day. She had been in the Home for
Incurables for eight years.
Miss Meigs retired in 1941 after
nearly 50 years of clerkship in Dis
trict Court. She had been ip charge
of the Lunacy Division, joining St
when it became a separate division
in 1895.
She entered court service in 1891,
the third generation of her faimly
to serve there. In 1863 President
Lincoln called her grandfather. Re
turn Jonathan Meigs, from Nash
ville. Tenn., to help reorganize the
court and be its first clerk. It was
then known as the Supreme Court
of the District of Columbia.
Born in New York.
Her father. Return Jonathan
Meigs, also came here at that time
from New York, where she was born.
He served as deputy clerk under his
father.
Miss Meigs was said to be the
first woman assistant clerk in any
Federal court and was advised to
sign her name E. M. Meigs to avoid
startling her correspondents.
Before entering the home, Miss
Meigs lived at the Portner Apart
ments. She was a member of the
Columbia Historial Society.
Leave* Two Sisters.
She is survived by two sisters,
Mrs. Henry Olds of Woodside, Md.,
and Miss Jane Boyd Meigs of San
Francisco. Also surviving are five
nieces, Miss Elizabeth Naylor, Chevy
Chase, Md.; Mrs. James Lauder
dale, Washington; Mrs. Philbrick
McCoy, Van Nuys, Calif., and Mrs.
Mary Fraser and Mrs. Margaret
Benedict of Silver Spring. A
nephew, Maj. Gen, Robert Olds,
died five years ago. Among her 14
great-nieces and nephews is Maj.
Robin Olds, U. S. A. F., now in
Eifgland, husband of Ella Raines,
movie star.
Graveside services for Miss Meigs
will be held at 11 am. tomorrow
In Oak Hill Cemetery.

saboteurs derailed two miners’ trains

One train left the tracks near

swinging a cane, was caught by the photographer as he walked along a rain-swept street today.

the Aubagne-Le Barque line near
Marseille. No casualties were re
ported in either derailment.
Communist union leaders called a
24-hour general strike for today in
the Valenciennes region, where the
back-to-work movement has been
the strongest. It was only partially
successful. Railway service and
trolley cars operated normally under
police protection.
Dynamite bombs were thrown at
the railway station in Fresnes-Sur
Escaut, but no serious damage was
reported!
In Lorraine, second most impor
tant of the French mining areas, the
government claimed about 34,500
workers would be at their jobs to
day—a complete staff for a normal
work day.
Two of the nation s largest ports
were tied up by strikes.
At Bordeaux, dock workers walked
out to protest the use of trgops to
unload coal ships. Sailors from 58
ships went on strike in Marseille.
Both walkouts were led by the pro
Communist General Labor Federa
tion <CGT), but the Marseille dem
onstration was not linked directly
to the coal strike.
Work in the big Mediterranean
port of Sete also was halted by a
sailors’ strike. Their union is de
manding a cost of living bonus of
3,000 francs ($10) a month pending
wage adjustments.

The Republican Absentee Voters
Bureau, 1430 K street N.W., will stay
oped until 9 o'clock tonight for late
voters. Mrs. Faith. Gwynne Fisher,
in charge, said the bureau was busy
until 9 o'clock last night. She said
the voting at the bureau exceeded
that of previous years.
The Democratic Ab&ntee Voters
Bureau, 1336 New York avenue N.W.,
likewise was helping voters in their
late balloting. Tonight it will be
open to receive and announce
returns.
Republican Groups Join.
Tonight the Republicans, under
leadership of the Republican State
Committee of the District, will re
ceive what they already refer to as
the “victory returns” at the Hotel
Statler. Joining in will be the Young
Republicans, the Dewey-Warren
Club, the League of Republican
Women and others.
The Young Republicans will be in
charge of large boards on which
returns will be posted as they are
received on news service wires.
Not only will television coverage
of election night events be shown,
but the program at the hotel will
be televised four times during the
evening, it was announced. The
pickup will include a televised con
versation between Hugh D. Scott,
1r., chairman of the Republican Na
tional Committee, who will be at the
Statler, and Herbert Brownell, Jr.,
Republican campaign manager, in
New York.
A community sing will be televised
at 10:48 p.m.
The Democrats will be receiving
returns at the National Committee
headquarters in the Ring Building.
Cabinet members and party lead
ers who are in Washington are
expected to be present.
There will be boards for posting
returns. Returns from the sena
torial campaigns will be under the
direction of Leslie J. Biffle, secre
tary-treasurer of the Democratic
Senatorial Campaign Committee.
Oapt. Victor Hunt Harding, exec
utive secretary of the Democratic
Congressional Campaign Committee,
will direct the posting of returns on
Representatives.
Clubs to Provide Returns.
Many cltibs in the District area
also made preparations to provide
returns for election night gath
erings.
No program has been planned by
the States’ Rights Democrats in
Washington. W. Austin Seay, man
ager of the organization’s Washing
ton office in the Mayflower Hotel,
said, however, he and C. C. Carlin,
jr., would appear on a television
political roundup program at. 9:30
o'clock tonight.. Mr. Carlin is editor
at the Alexandria Gazette and a
member of the States’ Rights Exec
utive Committtee in Virginia.
The District Progressive Party
will hold an open house beginning
at 8:30 p.m. election night at their
headquarters, 1722 Connecticut
avenue N.W.
Party spokesmen said several pri
vate parties will be held at the
homes of Progressive and Young
Progressive Party members.
Sam Schmerler, executive secre
tary of the District Progressive Par
ty, said he expects the open house to
run into the wee hours—‘‘until every
one is satisfied how the election is
going.”
He said a television set and radio
will be installed at headquarters to
receive election programs, Including
a talk by Henry Wallace.
Progressives Stage Parade.
An election-eve plea for District
suffrage was registered last night by
a 40-.car Progressive Party motor
cade through downtown Washing
ton.

Washington Woman Spends $5.
In Last Drive for Presidency

PHILADELPHIA, Nov. 2.—A wom
an who bills herself as America's
only woman candidate for President
spent $5 out of her own pocket last
night to dome to Philadelphia for a
last-hour appeal for votes.
Her “candidate special” was a bus
a'hd her hall was Philadelphia
streets.
Agnes Waters. 55. carries her own
identification cardboard placards
hanging front and back—and they
bear the announcement:
“The Pistol Packin’ Mama—Agnes
Waters, Only Woman Candidate for
States will be voting tomorrow—but
not you," the procession wound for
an hour and a half through some
67 city blocks.
At a number of intersections
party workers alighted from cars to
distribute pamphlets.
Slogans were plastered in large
signs on all the automobiles: “Home
Rule for the District”: “Government
by appointment is undemocratic”:
"Take freedom off a train—give it
to the people.” Some of the posters
attacked segregation “Jim Crow
Must Go—Give It a Body Blow—
Get D. C. Vote." One promised.
"Wallace and Taylor stand for civil
rights in D. C."

He was patient throughout all the
posing and light changing.
"Who did you vote for?” a re
porter asked as he emerged from
the voting booth the last time.
“Well,” Mr. Dewey said, "I know
of two votes we got anyway.”
Mrs. Dewey wore a black wool two
piece suit edged with Persian lamb
on the collar, while her husband was
natty in a gray double-breasted
outfit.
The ceremonies completed, Gov.
Dewey returned to his 15th
floor suite at the Roosevelt, and the
last, long wait for the election re
turns will begin.
Mother With Candidate.
The Republican nominee believes
he will be elected President today.
But he will not rest until he knows
he has been.
In addition to his wife, other
members of his family will be stand
ing by him during the hours of
waiting. His mother, Mrs. George
M. Dewey, met him here yesterday
when he arrived by automobile from
his Duchess County farm. She came
by train from Owosso, Mich.
His sons, Tom, jr., 16, and John,
13, are to arrive this afternoon
from their school, Albany Academy,
where they have been given the' day
off.
Gov. Dewey will receive the elec
tion returns by radio and telephone
in his rooms.
In a 5-minute ra<jio talk last
night the New York Governor called
on Americans to "vote regardless of
how you vote” and then to unite
behind their administration.
Stresses Privilege of Vote.
"Over much of the world,” Gov.
Dewey said, "men and women have
been denied the right to vote.
"fevery one of us, as citizens of the
United States, will be the envy of
the earth as we freely choose the
kind of government we want.
“Tomorrow we will register our
opinions according to the dictates
of conscience. The next day we will
close ranks and go forward, shoul
der to shoulder, remembering that,
whether we are Democrats or Re
publicans on election day, we are
good Americans every day.”
Gov. Dewey said the issue of the
election is whether this is to be a
“strong, united Nation.” There he
sounded the keynote of his 16,000
mile campaign. Over and over
again since September 19 he has
said his first purpose is to unify
America. Under President Truman,
Gov. Dewey has told the Nation,
America is not united.
|“I believe with all my heart that
we Americans have made up our
minds we are going to have a strong,
united Nation—that we will meet
the challenge of this time,’’ he said
in his speech last night.
Promises Lasting Peace.
"We shall build a lasting peace
in the world, upon the rock of jus
tice and human decency. That,
above all other objectives, will be
our goal. We will not rest until we
have achieved it.”
The candidate referred briefly to
such campaign issues as conserva
tion, social security and the prob
lem of world peace. Of the latter,
he said:
"Firmly, and patiently, with an
honest offer of friendship to all na

Gov. Dewey delivered his radio talk
at the end of an hour-long program
signing off the Republican cam
paign. The broadcast included a
short speech by Gov. Warren, Gov.
Dewey’s vice presidential running
mate, who spoke from Oakland,
Calif. More than 30 stage, film and
radio stars parti9ipated.
Gov. Dewey, accompanied only by
Mr. Lockwood and Mrs. Dewey,
spoke from an eighth floor studio
of the National Broadcasting Co. in
Rockefeller Center.
After his speech he went to
another studio two floors below to
thank Irene Dunne, Robert Mont
gomery, Ray Milland, William Gax
ton, James Melton and other per
formers, who had contributed their
part of the show from there. He
told them “it was terrific—a swell
show."
Truman
(Continued From First Page.)
to be back in Washington Friday.
One of the most strenuous cam
paigns ever undertaken by a Presi
dent seeking another term came to
a quiet end last night when Mr.
TYuman, in a Nation-wide radio
broadcast, not only urged people
to go to the polls, but frankly ap
pealed for Democratic votes.
His running mate, Senator Bark
ley of Kentucky, also addressed the
radio audience and talked more of
Mr. Truman than he did of his own
bid for the vice presidency.
He hailed the President as the
man who "has brought strength to
our economy, expansion and per
manence to our influence and hope
to hopeless men and women
throughout the world."
Faced Great Tasks.
“No man called suddenly to the
presidency of this Nation ever faced
a great task," Senator Barkley de
clared. “Search the history books
and tell me whether any of them
or all of them combined were con
fronted with anything approaching
the great questions that confronted
the successor of Franklin D. Roose
velt.”
Mr. Truman sat at a cloth-covered
table near a grandfather clock and
a green tile fireplace in the glow of
two electric lights fashioned after
old-time kerosene lamps.
“I have told you the truth as God
has given me the wisdom to see the
truth,” he said to the vast audience
throughout the country. "Go to the
polls tomorrow arid vote your con
victions. your hopes and your faith
—your faith in the future of a Na
tion that under God can lead the
world to freedom and peace.”
Referring to his far-reaching cam
paign travels, the President re
marked
1 “Maybe you would like to know
why I made this fight for the
people. I will try to tell you. It
is a matter of the things I believe
,in.
Urges Democratic Votes.
Speaking slowly and softly, he
paid tribute to Senator Barkley, who
had Introduced him from Paducah,
Ky. He departed to some extent
from the tradition that final wind
up speeches should deal only with
the duty of citizens to vote. The
President appealed directly for sup
port of his own party as “the party
of the people.” He called the Re
publican Party the “party of privi
lege.”

Moses Resents Plan
For U. S. Department
Of Public Works
A plan to create a new Depart
ment of Public Works with cabinet
status and to include in it the pres
ent civil functions' of the Armyi
Engineers Corps has been presented
to the Hoover Commission.
The proposal was made by one of
the commission's “task forces” head
ed by Robert Moses, New-York park
commissioner.
The Moses proposal fcalls for the
new department to take over nearly
all Federal construction activities
now performed by a number of
agencies, and also to perform the
rivers, harbors and flood control
activities now under the direction
of the Army Corps of Engineers.
Would Assign Army, Navy Officers.
Spokesmen for the Hoover Com
mission, which is studying the re
organization of the Government,
say that the plan at the same time
calls for the assignment of Army,
Navy and Air Force officers to the
proposed new department to aid in
its activities.
The spokesmen refused to specu
late on whether the plan would be
approved by the commission, but
they pointed out that former Presi
dent Hoover, the commission's
chairman, made a somewhat similar
proposal to Congress when he was
in the White House.
If the commission does favor the
plan, it will be submitted to Con
gress early next year along with
the commission's other recom
mendations for. Government reor
ganization.
Opposition Expected.
It's expected that considerable op
position may develop to the part
of the plan which would take over
the civil functions of the Army en
gineers. In the past, opponents of
such a plan have contended that
the civil function activities give
Army engineers fine practical train
ing for important military engi
neering projects that they could get
in no other way.
Mr. Moses is said by commission
spokesmen to feel that these activi
ties by military engineers can con
tinue under the new plan with their
assignment to the proposed new de
partment. The New York park
commissioner also feels that the
new department is needed to co
ordinate flood and harbor control
work with reclamation and power
development projects, and that the
Army Engineer’s activities at pres
ent form only one part of the over
all program.
Rites for Father Deihl
Set at Marist Chapel
Funeral services will be held at
11 a.m. Thursday in the Marist Col
lege Chapel, 3901 Harewood road
N.E., for the Rev. Joseph A. Deihl,
S. M.. 53, first American priest sta
tioned in the Samoan Islands. He
died in Mercy Hospital, Chicago,
yesterday.
The requiem mass will follow reci
tation of the divine office at 10:30
a.m„ at the college, where Father
Deihl studied. Burial will be in the
Marist plot at Mount Olivet Ceme
tery.
Bom in Atlanta, Father Deihl at
tended both the Marist Seminary
and the college here and was or
dained in 1930.
He volunteered for service at the
Marist missions in the Pacific
islands and was assigned to aid
Bishop Joseph Demand. S. M., vicar
apostolic of Samoa. Father Deihl
served in the area for 28 years,
wrote magazine accounts of the
missionary work there and became
an authority on the islanders.
Father Deihl, who returned last
summer because of poor health, was
known to thousands of servicemen
in the Pacific theater for his work
at the Catholic Club he established
at Apia, Samoan port of call en
route to the Solomons, New Cale
donia. New Hebrides, Australia and
New Zealand.

3 Men Wait Trials
On Bootleg Charges
Three men charged with liquor
law violations over the week end to*
day awaited Municipal Court jury
trials on December 2.
Checking a report that a man
named “Bobo” was Selling liquor out
of the back of a car at Second and
E streets N.W., Pvt. John A. Wentz
went to the intersection on Sunday
Pvt. Wentz said he found there a
man in an automobile who was will*
lng to sell him liquor. But instead
of making a sale on the spot, the
man invited him into the car and
drove him to an address in the 400
block of New Jersey avenue S.E., the
M
officer said, where a purchase was]
made.
Pvt. Went* saig he was then
driven back to Second arid E streets.
The pause there tots only momen
tary, however. The man was or
dered to keep going to police head
quarters.
Charged with keeping and selling
liquor without a license is Edward
L. Christman, 37, of the 400 block of
New Jersey avenue S.E. He pleaded
not guilty and was released under
$500 bond by Judge John P. Mc
Mahon.
The same judge set $1,000 bond in
a second case, involving James Wil
son, 32, colored, of the 2400 block of
Nichols avenue S.E., and John A:
White, 22, colored, of the 1600 block
of Fort Stanton road SB. Both
pleaded innocent.
The men were charged with keep
ing liquor without a license and
with possessing untaxpd liquor. Both
were arrested last Saturday at the
Nichols avenue address, after police
had made purchases with marked
money, it was reported.

four freight cars carrying fresh
fruits and vegetables from the
United States arrived in Montreal
yesterday. The shipment marked
the first day of relaxation of Can
ada’s ban on importation of Amer
ican produce.

There seems to be no
doubt that Tom Dewey
will be elected next
month, at least the
betting fraternity
seems to be unable to
find any one willing
to wager that he will
not. As this opinion
seems to be unanimous
maybe we Arizonans
should be thinking
seriously about how
we are going to vote
for Representative in
Congress and for
Governor. I believe
It would be wonderful
if two Republicans
from this State are
in the next Congress
I cannot help believe
that two Democratic
Congressmen from
Arizona would be in
about the same fix as
Republicans were
during the Roosevelt
administration when
the House was predom
inately Democratic. I
doubt if there is a
Democratic voter in
the State but that
will agree privately
that Republican
Representatives will
be able to do much
more for the State in
Washington during the
next four years than
Democrats, and I
suppose that there
will be few that will
admit after election
that they voted for a
Democratic congress
man. This state needs
federal help so badly
that it seems utter
foolishness to send
men back there to rub
a GOP administration
the wrong way. A
Republican governor
can go to Washington
and do us plenty of
good; he talks the
same language as the
members of the
administration. It
would be a waste of
the taxpayers’ money
*to send a Democratic
governor expecting
to get any favors, so
we’d better be smart
and vote for our
own interests.
BISBEE, ARIZONA, THURSDAY, OCTOBER 28, 1948

gest crowds, while Dewey gets the

versial subjects. Very confusing.

red Cow, told him what was neces

BILL SAYS—
“On next Tuesday
Show your faces
In this nation’s
Voting places.”
—PETE
Last Friday morning we stepped
on a big air liner in Miami, Flor
ida, and headed West. We changed
planes at Dallas and arrived at
Tucson before 8 p. m. This in
spite of the fact that we spent
nearly three hours in the Texas
city. It is realiy something to tra
verse the country in so short a
period. We attended the national
convention of the American Le
gion and after devoting the first
few days to official duties, found
time to do some sightseeing and
eat some of the finest sea food
that we have ever set a tooth in
to. The weather was fine except
for a few showers and we saw and
visited with many oid friends and
acquaintances.
The desert dweller, who has
not been in the lake country for
years, is a bit amazed when he
views the pattern spread be
neath him over Florida. Thou
sands of large and small bodies
of water and a maze of canals and
inland water ways spread over
the entire state. And of course the
peninsula itself has the Gulf of
Mexico on one side and the At
lantic ocean on the other. Water,
water, everywhere—and nar^ a
drop to spare us out here. These
arid regions will blossom some
day—and the water will come
from the seas that surround us.
The desalting and conveying are
merely technical details that will
be overcome by the ingenuity of
men.
Broiled red snapper, kingfish,
blue fish and pompano are def
initely not on the menus of the
average Arizona restaurant. They
are plentiful* in Miami right now,
however, and the prices are ac
tualty reasonable. Oysters on the
half shell were 35 cents for six
at two of the places we visited
and one popular spot listed: “All
the lobster you can eat for $1.75.”
We did very well, thank you, and
will be content with our local cuts
for a while.
Arizona cannot compete with
the coast states in the sea food
deal but we could have fresh
citrus fruit juices available for
our tourists and winter resi
dents if someone would make
an effort. Fresh orange juice is
on tap in practically every eat
ing place in Florida, from the
lowest lunch counter to the
highest priced hotel dining
room. Thousands of juice stands
line the highways and the
downtown streets of the cities.
We did not see an ounce of
canned citrus juice served any
where. Here in Arizona, in the
shade of our orange and grape
fruit groves, fresh juice is vir
tually unobtainable at restau
rants and other eating places.
Sounds like a good deal for our
Chambers of Commerce in co
operation with Merchants asso
ciations, etc.

insky in a U.N. pow-wow at which

to agree to destroy our atom

Three American boys killed fly
ing supplies into blockaded Ber
lin. There will be more. Mean
time I see where a leading Demo
crat says that it seems to be a
mystery just WHO is responsible
for U.S.A, troops being isolated
in Berlin, completely surrounded
by gun-totin’ Commies. He thinks
it might have been a Republican
named John Winant. I ain’t no
mystery-solver, but here’s a clue
for anybody that wants to figger
it out for himself: the agreement
that nut us in this Commie-corral
in Berlin was made with Joe Sta
lin at sa conference at a place
called Potsdam. Both newspapers
and official records bear out the
fact that a Democrat named
Harry Truman was there, and
there are rumors that it was he,
not John Winant, who was Presi
dent of the United States and
Commander-in-Chief of the Army
and Navy at the time.
Stupid?
Anyhow, the Democrats are
telling us, it’s STUPID to be con
cerned over WHODUNIT now that
the fat is already in the fire. The
trouble with us stupid Republicans
is that we feel like folks ought to
know w'ho made such a tragic
mistake, even though he didn’t
mean to, for fear he might get re
elected President of the United
States and make some more like
it.
It Checks

ors, or who have foolishly associ
ated with and aided same in their

munism’s openly avowed aim. In

er acted upon by the Justice De
partment of Mr. Truman’s ad
ministration.
But I also happen to know a

also. There was an otherwise in
telligent gal in our town who for
years quite openly ran with the
Communist wolf pack. In conver
sation she talked the Party Line.

umns of The New Masses and

Voice of the People: More pow
erful than the pen or the sword
is the voice of the people—or it
could be, if the people were not
inarticulate jackasses.
Here we are in a crisis such as
the world never knew before, our
whole civilization threatened with
destruction by a handful of ruth
less men whose voices are shout
ing lies, threats and hatred, yet
we remain dumb when we, the
billions of us could shout them
down—we could almost whisper
them down.
In Russia, where the people’s
voice is hushed by police and
banishment to slavery in Siberia,
one can understand their reluct
ance to speak. But if enough of
them spoke at once, their masters
would be powerless they could
not execute millions of people and
jail other millions wi.hout wreck
ing the economy of Russia, with
out wrecking their own plans to
conquer the world.
But here in America and in
many other countries where the
people do have a voice, they are
strangely silent when they should
be loudly shouting for peace, for
justice—drowning out the voices
of Vishinsky and others.
If our voices were turned
against the Russians, not our own
government, in a loud protest—
not a threat of war, but with
enough force to let the Russians
know that millions of us are uni
fied in our demands for peace
with justice and freedom for all,
they would hear—and as more
millions of voices throughout the
world, even those of the Russian
people, caught up the cry —the
men of the Kremlin would heed.
What are YOU going to do
about it? Let yourself be silently
led to slaughter, or will you raise
up your voice and let your would
be executioners hear your protest?
Political Predictions: Don’t let
that caption fool you I’m not
predicting who will be elected
President. It looks like Dewey in
a walk, according to the public
polls and straws in the wind, but
the world is in such a state of flux
and uncertainty, who can tell what
might happen before November 2?
We might find ourselves, by some
strange quirk of fate, saddled
with Truman again.
That, I wouldn’t like—and my
one vote, come what may—if I
can still vote, will be against such
a possibility. We need something
better than a “Charlie McCarthy”
President in these perilous times
—we deserve something better
than a grinning dummy who
mouths the words of not one, but
a dozen or more “Edgar Bergens”
—having no real mind of his own.
As was said here when F.D.R.
died and Truman took over, he
would make a fair Mayor for
Kansas City, where the Pender
gast machine “made” him, but he
does not have the stature for a
President.
As for Tom Dewey, he is far the
better man, in my opinion, but he
will learn much after—when and
if he gets to the White House.
But, men —voters—propose, and
someone throws a monkey wrench
in the voting machines—Stalin,
Vishinsky—maybe “an act of
God” or some other unforeseen
thing. I wouldn’t even predict the
weather these days if I were head
of the Weather Bureau.

The lose resulting from the

Stores’ warehouse in Durham'

not as heavy as first reported,
according to a statement by

president. After a careful ap-‘
praisal, the loss in merchandise
and fixtures has been tentative
ly determined to be between 1
$1,250,000 and $1,500,000. This I
loss was covered by insurance.!
The warehouse building, owned
by the North Carolina Ware
house Corporation, was leased
by Colonial.
Fortunately, due to the strate-1
gic location of other company j
warehouses, Mr. Allen continued, j
there will be nßinterruption in j
the supply of merchandise to'
the stores formerly serviced by j
the Durham warehouse. Reg-!
ular scheduled deliveries were
started to these stores from the
company’s warehouses at Nor-i
folk, Richmond, and Greenville, j
S. C., within twelve hours after
the fire. All stores will continue
to receive ample stocks of all
merchandise, including perish
ables, regularly carried.

served.

David was bom near Taylor's
Bridge, the son of the late James

Da vi d and farmed in that area for

1911 and 1913 from the Seventh
Senatorial District and was elected
a director of the National Bank of
Smyrna in 1917, serving as its vice
president since April 1936. On

die d October 15, 1938. Surviving

Taylor's Bridge; Mrs. Dudley
Kemp of near Smyrna; Miss Nora
David at home and Senator Wilson
a. David of Taylor's Bridge; three
sisters, Mrs. Molly Parsons of
Frankford; Mrs. Fannie Wallace of
Wilmington, and Mrs. Laura Whit
tington of Bear, Del; also three
g randc hil dr en, Virginia, Billy and
Blanche Kemp. Funeral services
were held from his late residence
Saturday, September 18th with the
Rev-John l. j ohnson and Rev.Wal
ter L Beckwith officiating. Mr.
David S fellow bank directors and

PAGES WASHINGTON, D. C., SEPTEMBER 12, 1948
_ ___ 1 111 1
West Sector
Of Germany
Doing Well
Allies May Abandon
Idea of Uniting
Two Zones
By Sigrid Arne
Western Germany is coming along
*o well that State Department ex
perts say now they don’t think any
compromise will be made on Ger
man administration in order to get
the Russian zone into a once-again
united Germany.
Germans in the American-British
zone are paying more at their own
bills than they did a year ago. They
are actually making a profit on the
manufactured goods they sell
abroad, although they’re still in the
red on food.
German "ministers-president” of
the 11 states in Western Germany
have done well enough that the
western Allies plan to turn over
more of the governing job to the
Germans shortly (but with con
tinued Allied control).
Some Illustrations
The long-time official American
hope is to g-ft all of Germany—the
Russian zone as well—into a unified
government. But now there is little
sentiment at the State Department
to toss its growing successes in Ger
many overboard just for the sake of
getting the Russian zone in.
The real test of German rebuild
ing is the money the Germans make
selling to other nations compared
with the money they spend and
the bills they run up in other na
tions.
The best stones or what is hap
pening come out the American
British zone, where the United
States and Britain decided in early
1947 to pool their efforts. They
formed a Joint Export-Import
Agency to supervise what the Ger
mans bought abroad, what they
sold, where they hunted new mar
kets, the prices they charged.
The first half of this year the
United States-British zones sold
$229,000,000 worth of goods to 77
different nations and territories.
In that time they spent $163,000,
000 for the machines and raw mate
rials they needed to turn out the
goods.
Interesting Contracts
On that operation German ex
perts here feel it fair to say the
Germans are already selling at a
profit, three years after the war.
But they have to import much
food. That comes high: $459,000,
000 the first half of this year.
So, actually the two zones spent.
$622,000,000 and earned $229,000,
000 on their foreign accounts.
That means they earned roughly
40 per cent of what it takes them
to live and keep their factories
going. But that is double a year
ago.
Some interesting contracts have
been written for German goods.
An American concern is buying
$1,500,000 worth of German dyes.
The Americans will furnish $475,
000 for machines and raw materials
to get the job going. The Germans
will pay that bill when the dyes
are delivered. The Americans get
the dyes. The Germans make
about a million profit. And they
have the machines to turn out
more dyes.
India will exchange goods with
Germany—but she would also like
German technicians for some jobs
in India. She would like to send
Indian nationals to Germany for
industrial training.
Through every sale of German
goods or labor the driye is to get
for the JEIA account the foreign
money which the Germans need to
buy abroad.
Can She support Hersclt.
For example:
JEIA collects the. royalties paid a
German singer who makes records
for a Swiss concern. JEIA pays h^r
in German marks.
A German specialist from Weis
baden is fitting glass eyes in a
Swiss hospital. His pay of $50 a
day goes to JEIA, which deposits
marks to his home account.
A canary breeder gets a JEIA
okay to import seeds for his birds.
When the birds are old enough
they'll be flown to a New York
dealer. He will pay JEIA in dol
lars and the German will get marks.
JEIA insists that Germans charge
near the going world price for their
goods. Germans who apply for ex
port licenses must present at least
three world price quotations to get
JEIA's okay.
The big question is: “Can Ger
many become self-supporting, and
when?” At first it was hoped 1949
would turn the trick. Now experts
figure on 1951.
A Political Parallel
There is a parallel growth on the
political front.
At London, in June, the United
States, British and French govern
ments agreed that it is time the
Germans begin to run their own
government under supervision.
They agreed to call a meeting of
the German ministers-president in
the near future, and give them the
power to call a constituent assem
bly. Delegates to the assembly will
be elected from the 11 western
states.
The assembly will be asked to
write a constitution for Germany
that will:
1. “Allow all German states to
•ubscribe as soon as circumstances
permit.” That leaves the gate open
for the Russian zone.
2. Set up a federal form of gov
ernment which will protect the
righta of individual states and the
freedoms of the individual.
If the draft constitution gets the
okay of the Allied military gover
nors, it will go to the German peo
ple for a vote.
•Distributed be the Associated Press) A
Democracy's Road Ends at the Top of the World
__ 4
Russia Is Suspicious
of Its Norwegian
Neighbors
(Few newspapermen have ever
-penetrated the desolate, bleak
regions of Finnmark, far above
the Arctic Circle, at the Nor
wegian-Russian frontier. Nat A. j
Barrows, foreign correspondent j
for The Star and the Chicago
Daily News, herewith describes j
his observations and experiences
in that area of great strategic j
importance.)
By Nat A. Barrows
KIRKENES, North Norway.—
Here, on the Barents Sea only a
short air hop from Murmansk, the
road of Western Democracy comes
to its end at the top of the world.
This is it—the farthest north
frontier of the nations outside the
Russian orbit.
The line dividing Norway and
Russia is merely the middle of a
river or a cleared space cut through
the birch-and-evergreen woods.
But if this frontier line were as
solid as the granite peaks towering
above the Finnmarken fjords It
would not separate the two coun
tries more effectively.
What is it like up here, nearly 250
miles above the Arctic Circle, at the
edge of Soviet Russia?
What Is going on along this vi
tally strategic border? What are the
undertones?
Border Line Dangerous
It is clear enough, first of all. that
the Russians just over there to the
east have been exceedingly busy
in the brief weeks of summer this
year.
Even the Kirkenes children play
ing in the wreckage left by Allied
bombs and German firebrands know
the high lights:
How “large numbers" of Russian
troops have been carrying out ex
tensive maneuvers these past few
weeks.
How fighter planes and bombers
are frequently seen from the hills
as they return to nearby bases that
obviously are spread through the
Kola Peninsula, on the Russian side
toward Petsamo.
How the sound of four-engine
planes is wind-borne into Norway
from the Salmijarvi area, just over
the frontier.
How camouflaged Russian soldiers
crawl through the border under
brush in a strange and nerve rack
ing game of “cops and robbers."
I had the story from many of the
most responsible, level-headed peo
ple in Kirkenes and other parts of
this Varangerfjord district. I
They keep strictly away from the
immediate frontier line. Too many
Norwegians have been involuntary
“guests” of Soviet Russia for acci- j
dentally stepping across the line
while bird-hunting or fishing in the
Pasvik River.
But from the hills and peaks that
make Finnmark one of the most
rugged areas in the world they
have seen Red Army troops in field
maneuvers and seen Red Army
planes “by the scores."
Iron Curtain Drawn
Except for the 20 known Moscow
trained Communists among the 2.500
people of Kirkenes, there is not the
slightest contact between the natives
of the two countries.
Once, before Finland lost t lie
Petsamo corridor by the 1944 arm
istice terms, the Norwegians here
crossed the border and traded ex-j
tensively in Salmijarvi, the Boris
Gleb district and Petsamo.
Today, the Finns who were there
in 1944 have disappeared. The
curtain of iron has been drawn.
A handful of Norwegians have
penetrated this curtain against their
will.
Here are .some of the experiences
I nave heard in Kirkenes:
Three Norwegians were swimming
in the Pasvik River this summer
and crossed to a little island in the
middle, part of which was in Rus
sian territory.
They cavorted about the island
gayly in an escapist reflex from the
frightful 40-below cold and stir
crazv darkness that imprisons Finn
mark in the winter. Unwittingly
they ventured a few' feet into'me
Russian side of the island.
Others in the party, on the Noi
wegian bank, saw what happened
then.
Soviet soldiers, wearing camou
flage suits, rose up from the under
brush and immediately laid down a
crossfire just behind the swimmers
to prevent their return to Norway,
only a few yards away.
Other Russian soldiers forded the
river, took them captive 'and led
them away.
Business Men Abducted
After being blindfolded they were
taken somewhere on the Kola
peninsula and kept for five days
as a lesson, they were told, not to
violate Russian territory.
A motorboat owned by a business
firm in Kirkenes was making a
coastal survey at the frontier, just
west of the 31st longitude.
The crew ventured across into
Russian waters and was promptly
seized by a Russian gunboat.
All were forced aboard the gun
boat, blindfolded and taken along
the coast to Murmansk, the motor
boat in tow.
In due time, the worried owners
asked the No. 1 Communist in
Kirkenes, Gottfried Hoelvold, if he
could find out what had happened
to the boat. Hoelvold, by devices
best known to himself, had the
answer in two hours.
The men and the boat were safe
in Murmansk, he said, and would
be returned in a few weeks. They
were.
I went out one morning to the
end of the road from the free
democracies, in the hills beyond
Elvenes, and saw for myself the;
tinnmark, shown in the shaded area, is many mues auvve me amne li/uc, vu-j.
became a Russian neighbor when the Soviet annexed the Petsamo Corridor u.r.cr ic: ..:s of
the armistice with Finland. The vessel sketched off Tromso marks the spot where the German
battleship Tirpitz was sunk by British bombers.
Young Oslo Businessmen Who Bought the Hulk of Battleship Tirpitz
For $8,000 Have Got Their Money Back and Are Sure of Big Profit
TROMSO. Norway.—There she lies, rusty bottom
up—the mighty German battleship Tirpitz of 1944—
the hulk of this third year of uneasy peace.
From this far north island and the fjords and
peaks, she looks like a bloated old whale over there
in the cove where she hoped to nurse her wounds
and hide out from Allied bombers.
Alongside, the Tirpitz reveals herself as the
strangest machine shop this land of the ,midnight
sun has ever seen. f
Salvagers have cut down through the overturned
hull and set up their workshops, open to the sky,
using as their flooring what would be the over-,
head bulkhead of the bottommost compartment if
the old battleship were upright.
A group of lucky young fellows in Oslo got the
45.000-ton hulk of the battleship for exactly 40,000
kroner, W'hich in American dollars comes to a mere
$8,000.
They got their money back almost as soon as
they could cut through the hull.
This is the reason a battleship hulk originally
worth many millions went for such a trifle:
The more money Norway received for selling the,
Tirpitz to salvagers the more money would be de
ducted from Norway’s claims for war damage.
Already one diesel engine has been removed, in
good shape. Now they're pulling out the miles of
copper wiring.
As they cut into one watertight compartment
after another, the salvagers are finding German
sailors who died slowly from suffocation after the
capsizing trapped them at their posts.
How many died with the Tirpitz that night of
November 10, 1944, and how many remain inside
the hulk, probably will never be knowm.
Because they found complete blueprints of the
battleship when they explored inside, the salvagers
will avoid many compartments.
Perhaps 1.100, certainly 1,000, died when RAF
Lancasters found the Tirpitz here behind Tromso
and blasted her out of the w’ar.
The Tirpitz is dead but the sound of her music
still lives here. In one compartment the salvagers
found hundreds of musical instruments intact.
These they have sold at small cost to the people
here.

Except for three Norwegian sol
iiers standing unhappily at the
rontier blockade, the expanse of
rare rock hills above valleys of
:vergreen and birches was utterly
without sight or sound of human
ife.
On the Russian side, the dirt road
wound along the lake, past a steep
rill, and disappeared in the woods
rehind a secondary blockade.
There was no sign of the Russian
guards.
“Wait a little and you'll see,” said
he Norwegian officer with me. “It
will be like children playing cow
oo.vs-and-Indians."
Two flagpoles stretched above
he two frontier bars, the Russian
oarrier painted in alternating
stripes of red and green, the Nor
iverian barrier in yellow and black.
They were exactly 3 meters, or
oughly 9 feet, apart.
Except for the path cut like a
fire-strip through the woods, the,
actual frontier line is designated
only by sets of posts, one red and
green, the other yellow and black,
always placed so that one set is in'
sight of another in the distance.
“Listen," said the officer.
We heard a slight crackling of
twigs in the hillside underbrush
above us perhaps 200 feet away.
“That’s one of them, crawling
around on his stomach, watching
us,” said the officer.
Politicians Strange, Too
“You see, they came down to the
barrier yesterday and raised the
Russian flag on their flagpole. That’s
the way we tell one another when
we want to have a conference about
some minor detail concerning the
frontier, say, about a broken market
post or a cow that has strayed.
“We raise the Norwegian flag on
our side if we want a conference
with them.
“We never have any conversation
with them because they reject even
the slightest gesture, such as a
wave of the hand in the morning
or any attempt to be decently pleas
ant in this miserable isolation.
"Anyway, they rateefl the flag
and now we've been waiting for
nours and still no sign of them—
openly.”
One of the soldiers pointed up the
hill.
I finally made out what he saw.
It was a Russiari soldier in a green
camouflage-covering lying prone,
eyeing us.
The political brothers or these
Russian soldiers, across the line in
Kirkenes, are equally strange in
their activities.
Here, at the edge of the Soviet
Union, the handful of Norwegian
Communists living in Kirkenes must
adopt different tactics than Com
munists elsewhere.
These people know too much
about Marxist ambitions and the
Red Troops Play "Cops
and Robbers" on
the Border

Jong-range aims for world domina
tion. The usual smear-and-Ue
propaganda, then, would be useless
here. v
The appeal is subtle, through in
nocent little parties for the yaung
people, by a mimeographed bulletin
that comes out nearly every week,
by attempts at stressing Russia's
help to the Allies in wartime.
There is no attempt, here, to foster
a vicious campaign against Marshall
Plan help to Norway.
Actually, the Communists are
worried.
Lose Remaining Delegate
They lost eight of their nine dele
gates in the December election of
the mine and mill workers at the
town’s single big industry, the Syd
varanger iron ore producers. In
this summer's election they lost the
remaining delegate.
These 650 men in the mines and
in.the mill do their own thinking.
They do nci respond to Communist
appeals to selfish interests.
The Russian sword of Damocles
hangs over their heads. Thus,
Gottfried Hoelvola and his hench
men hate eased the Communist line
to something almost as gentle as a
spring picnic.
They're playing their own ver
sion of crawling through the under
brush in camouflage suits.
They lie low and they bide their;
time.
Kirkenes is something more than
merely the farthest north town
nearest Russia.
It is the site of the heaviest con
sistent bombing given any wartime
target except Malta. It is the scene
of deliberate mad destruction by ihe
Germans.
And today it is the place where
2,500 men and women lift them
selves above terrible memories of
yesterday and terrifying fears of
tomorrow, and build anew in their
ruins.
Watched Homes Kurn
The Allies bombed them over 500
times when this was a major base
for German attacks on the Mur
mansk convoy run. They fled into
shelters at exactly 1,017 air-raid
alerts.
The Germans on October 23.
1944, burned, dynamited and knocked
down what the bombs had spared.
They stood on the hills that
night, these stolid Norwegians of
Kirkenes, and watched the great
bonfire of their homes. They saw
the Russian slave-prisoners driven
like cattle by the retreating Nazis
and they saw the weary and sick
fall in the snow and die.
They fled into caves and they hid
in'the iron ore mines back in the
iiife
And somehow they survived that
winter of 1944-5 and got through,
most of them, until V-E day.
Many already had been forcibly
deported south by the Germans;
the others, after liberation, re
mained away only until the return
ing Oslo government could get them
tents and material for reconstruc
tion.
There are almost 60.000 such
people in this bleak Finnmark, so
lovely in summer for the tourist
venturing ashore briefly from a
steamer, so cruel in winter to every
thing that dares to exist here.
The sight and the sound of war
continues.

United Nations Facing Big Problems at Paris
By Robert N. Cool
At mianignt on septemoer zz a lu-acre tract in raris win Decome
the temporary capital of the world. In the Trocadei o section, within
sight of the Eiffel To wet, 1,500 delegates from the 58 United Nations
countries will take up such problems as the control of atomic weapons
and other devices for mass extermination.
They will consider a world police force advocated by U. N. Secre
tary General Trygvie Lie, and the smaller Security Council may de
bate the No. 1 headache of postwar days: The "cold war’’ between
the Soviet Union and the western powers which has reached a crisis
in Berlin.
The setting for these broad discussions will be the General As
sembly of the United Nations. It is expected to last from four to
eight weeks. Concurrently the potent Security Council and other
executive bodies will be in action.
U. N.’s General Assembly (it has been called the town meeting of
the world) meets in regular session once each year. Last November a
special session was called on Palestine. It led to the partition of the
embattled former British mandate, and creation of the State of IsraeL
To the General Assembly each member nation sends five repre
sentatives, many alternates, advisers and experts. Including reporters,
about 4,200 persons will officially attend the Paris gathering.
Their work will affect the futures of some 2,000,000,000 persons—
the total population of thMlcbe.

Such is the East-West deadlock over atomic controls. Russia
and the western powers may be expected to attempt to convince the
other countries that they were not to blame for failure of the U. N.

Secretary General Lie has warned the Assembly In advance on

The delegates are likely to hear an appeal by the large princely

over Kashmir is already before the United Nations.

attempt to press their case at Paris. j
