Wallace May Hit Hardest
In House and Senate Races
Democrats and Unions Fear Third Party Will
Split Congressional Contests and Put
‘Reactionaries’ in the Saddle
By Chalmers M. Roberts
In the Presidential election of 1912. a widely published cartoon made
a point, which is even more true in this election year of 1948. The car
toon used the three party symbols: GOP elephant. Democratic donkey
and the bull moose of Teddy Roosevelt s third party.
The cartoon lined up the three party symbols with mathematical
signs between them to read this way:
Republicans divided by Bull*
Moose equals Democrats.
That was exactly what happened
on election day. Roosevelt split the
Tait vote and Wilson won the
Presidency.
This year the same sort of thing
is obvious for all to see. It is Demo
crats divided by Progressives plus
Dixiecrats equals Republicans.
In that 1912 election, however, the j
political mathematics were true;
only on the national scale. This1
year the same sort of result may
be had in many a race for the
‘Senate and House. The-Dixiecrats
are playing no part in this particu
lar split; their fight is strictly with
President Truman over the Presi
dential electoral votes of the South.
GOP May Sweep House
The split which may seriously
weaken the Democratic Party in
!Congress is being engineered by the,
Progressive Party of Henry A. Wal
lace, To many a Democrat and es
pecially to those remnants of the;
New Deal still taking part in poli
tics. the really serious situation is
in the senatorial and House races.
Conversely, Republican prospects
for a sweeping party victory in the
House are brighter than ever and
the GOP chances of retaining con
trol of the Senate are likewise im
proved, though nothing like they are
for the House. j
It is lor exactly tnis reason inai
labor groups, the American Federa
tion of Labor, the Congress of In-
dustrial Organizations and the in-;
dependents, are concentrating their j
campaigning on the House and Sen
ate races. After all, they reason, if
the Eightieth Congress was, as Pres
ident Truman has repeatedly said,
the second “worst” Congress in his
tory, the thing to do it to throw out
some of the old members and re
place them with members more In
clined to your own point, of view. J
Green’s Admonition
In his Labor Day address at,
Akron, Ohio, President William
Green of the AFL warned against a1
repetition of what.happened in the
1946 election, when "only orie third
the qualified voters in America”!
went to the polis to name the
Eightieth Congress.
I .Green's admonition Is ‘Use
your Vote” and he is being seconded
[and echoed by every other labor
[leader intent on improving '•.the
quality of Congressmen.
Now, it appears, the Wallace Pro
gressives will make the job even
tougher than the regular one of
getting out the vote on November
2. They are putting up third party
candidates In many important races.
It looks like Democrats divided by
Progressives equals Republicans.
When Henry Wallace first started
his third party he made great pro
testations that he was interested
in electing a liberal President—him
self—and a liberal Congress. He and
his friends said they would back
all liberal candidates and enter their
own Progressive Party members
where there was no liberal candi
date.. The definition of a "liberal”
varies considerably, but at least two I
liberal groups, the CIO and the
I Americans for Democratic Action.
have proauced check lists of voting
records in the last Congress to de-!
i termine just how "liberal" a Sena
tor or Representative was.
me tests coverea a gooa many
/mints, but break down this way:
On foreign affairs a vote for the
Marshall Plan and for the full ap
propriation for its first year was a
liberal vote. On domestic affairs
votes against the Taft-Hartley labor
law and against overriding Presi
dent Truman's veto were liberal
votes. Likewise, liberal votes were
those for public housing as embod
ied in the Taft-Ellender-Wagner
bill and for extension of such
things as rent control.
The CIO News recently issued a
summary showing that it rated 56
Congressmen at 80 per cent liberal
or better—those who voted “right”
from the CIOs standpoint on 8
out of 10 issues.
Yet the Progressives have entered
some 80-odd candidates in congres
sional contests, in many cases
against these “liberal” congressmen
—almost without exception Demo
crats.
Blunt Language
The result appears so likely to be
catastrophic to the liberal cause
that one CIO union, the Textile
Workers' Union, came out last week
with a pamphlet printed in red and
black and titled: "Third Party—Red
Ally of Reaction.*'
The opening paragraphs express
in blunt language the feelings of
many other liberal groups, both
unions and other groups. Up to now
no one had had the courage to put
it quite like this:
“The third party, headed by
Henry A. Wallace, is trying hard to
bring about a reactionary victory
in the November elections. This
would be true even if Wallace him
self withdrew from the presidential
race.
“The real story of the third party
does not concern the presidency;
that's just a detail. The real story
can best be read in the 80-odd con
gressional contests where third party
candidates have been entered so far. j
“It is a shocking story of an evil;
scheme—a scheme to divide and
confuse American liberals in such
a way that they will bring defeat
upon themselves.
“These are words,^ and words
easily written. But the facts sup
port the words in every detail."
The “facts" the union then offer*
mmmarize its views of 83 congres
sional contests in 13 States, where
third-party candidates have been
entered;
“In 17 cases, incumbents with fair
to perfect liberal records are in dan
ger of defeat because the third pauy
will draw off a part of their support.
“In 19 cases, bright prospects of
replacing reactionary incumbents
with liberals have been dimmed by
the injection of third-pa;ty candi
dates.
"In 13 cases, reviving liberal hopes
have been completely crushed by the
third-party division.
“In only seven cases is the third
party candidate the only alternative
to reaction; and all seven are in
California, where the desertion of
voters to the third party primary
helped reactionaries capture both
major party nominations under the
State's cross-filing system.
“In 21 cases, the political comple
tion of the districts is so pronounced
that the third party will have little
impact. In the remaining cases the
situation is not clear."
State* to Watch
Since the pamphlet was prepared,
however, the Progressives have been
eliminated from the ballot in Illi
nois, one of the important State*
about which the union is objecting.
But the third party will play havoc,
the liberals fear, in these States:
California, Indiana, Maryland, Min
nesota, Missouri, New Hampshire,
New Jersey, New York, Pennsyl
vania, Washington and Wisconsin.
Both the Textile Union and th*
CIO News list various specific dis
tricts and candidates to prove their
points that the Wallaceites are out
to eliminate the liberals irom Con
gress. The only explanation of
such a policy, if it truly is the Pro
gressives’ aim, is that they^re fol
lowing the Communist Party line
that the worst enemy of a Com
munist is a liberal who wants to
correct the social or economic con^^^
ditlons which lead to the chaos^BBj
which communism thrives.
The CIO News, by being apecino
In a recent issue, drew a hot retort
from the Wallace campaign man
ager, C. B. Baldwin, former Farm
Security Administration head in
Washington. He accused the CIO
Of being ‘‘false and reckless-’ in
charging the Progressives with be
ing bent on defeating liberal Con
gressmen.
Mr. Baldwin said that of the 56
Congressmen whom the CIO called
JO per cent or more liberal "there
will be few whom we will oppose.''
The CIO and the Textile Union
throw the book at Mr. Baldwin in
reply. Here are some examples:
Upposition lor Dill
Minnesota: Senator Ball. Re
publican, who started out as a labor

disfavor as Senior Taft of Ohio,
is facing tough opposition from
Mayor Humphrey of Minneapolis,
the leader in the liberal group which
wrote a strong civil rights plank
into the Democratic platform at th§
Philadelphia convention. On every
issue except the Marshall Plan,
Henry Wallace would be likely to
agree with Mayor Humphrey. But
Mr. Wallace considers the Marshall
Plan a prelude to war. So there is
a third party candidate: onetime
Gov. Elmer Benson. If the Re
publican vote this November is a
sweep, it could very well be enough
to put Senator Ball back in the

Maryland: Last year CIO Presi
dent Phillip Murray indorsed Ed
ward A. Garmatz to fill a vacancy

indorsement served as a test of the

tivities by the labor press since it
I was carried by the CIO News. Mr.
Garmatz was elected, but in No
vember he will face both a Re
publican and a Progressive.
Pennsylvania: In a Philadelphia
House district the Democrats are
running former Congressman Wil
liam Grrtn. Jr„ a man with what
the CIO calls "a perfect labor vot
ing record,” against Representative
Sarbacher, the youthful Marine
hero who won on the Republican
(fcket last time and who, the CIO
says, "voted as wrong as the oldest
Republican member.” The Pro
gressives have entered Philip Stern,
Strongest in1 New York
New York: Here, where the Wal
lace strength is at its peak, the Pro
gressives have indorsed several
Democrats—six says Mr. Baldwin,
only four says the CIO. But It la
running its own members against
such “stalwarts,” to use the Textile
Union's word, as Representatives
Bloom and Multer. Others being
opposed are Representatives Keough,
Somers, O'Toole, Lynch and Buck
ley* Democrats, and Jayts, the lone
Republican, considered a "liberal*
by the labor groups. Pormer CIO
general counsel Lee Pressman,
whose name has turned up in
the Un-American Activities Com
mittee hearings, is himself op
posing Representative Multer as
the third party candidate. The
CIO News says the result is to
make "almost certain’ the election
of Republican "reactionaries.”
Taken together, the liberal group*

BULLETIN
PARIS </P).—The United Na
tions announced today that
the Security Council will meet
Thursday on the invasion of
Hyderabad by India. The
session, called at the request
of Hyderabad, will be the first
U. N. meeting in the Palais de
Chaillot, where the General
Assembly convenes next week.
By the Associated Press
NEW DELHI,'Sept. 14.—Indian
troops invading Hyderabad to
day captured the town of Rajasur,
halfway from their jumping-off
point of Sholapur to Hyderabad
City, heart and capital of the
princely state.
Another tank-led column striking
from the northwest captured the
important rail town of Jalna on the
Nizam's state railroad after a pen
etration of 70 miles into Hyder
abad. Jalna is 250 miles from the
capital.
Rajasur is 90 miles to the west
and in the area where the Indian
army had found its hardest resist
ance.
The Indians driving in from the
east captured Suriapet, 90 miles
east of Hyderabad City.
Strong Resistance Met.
Troops in the northwest also cap
tured Daulatabad. also a commu
nications center. 60 miles west of
Jalna, against "strong resistance.”
The successes were recounted in a
Defense Ministry communique is
sued shortly after noon when the
invasion was less than 36 hours old.
The Indian communique said an
other column advancing north from
Sholapur drove 70 miles and reach
ed Yerinana, 100 miles south of
Jalna
The Important town of Osmana
bad, 40 miles inside Hyderabad and
165 miles northwest of the capital,
was taken en route.
This force and the one striking
from the northwest apepared to be
swiftly enveloping all the northwest
corner of Hyderabad in a large
pincers.
Airfields Are Bombed.
The communique said Indian
planes bombed airfields employed
in gun running from Pakistan.”
The fields attacked were not named.
The planes also dropped leaflets.
Troops which captured Rajasur
were declared by the ministry to
have scored a lightning advance.
Thev captured two 25-pounder can
non in their push toward Hyderabad
City and its suburb, Secunderabad,
which the Dominion of India has
vowed to garrison until what is
described as disorder and anarchy
is put down.
India surrounds the south cen
tral state and has demanded its ac
cession Hyberabad is as large as
Minnesota and has 16.000,000 people,
four-fifths of whom are Hindus. The
fabulously rich Nizam, a Moslem,
has refused to accede.
India invaded the state from all
four sides at dawn yesterday. Col
umns (Hyberabad said 10 were
striking into the state* from the
north and south appeared to be
making slower progress. Hyerabad
City is only 70 miles from the south
ern frontier with Madras Province,
however.
An army official in Madras said
progress is being made toward Se
cunderabad and Hyderabad City in
all four sectors. He said heavy cas
ualties were inflicted on forces of
the Nizam and the private army of
Razakars in the northwest around
Jalna, where opposition was de
scribed as "very stiff.”
India Admits Loss of 100.
India acknowledged in a morning
communique one of its invading
columns in the w’est last 100 killed
and 150 captured. (Hyderabad said
it inflicted 1.000 casualties on the
Indians in the first day of the in
vasion.
The army spokesman at Madras
said an Indian "police force” occu
pied 50 Hyderabad villages and 5(X
square miles in the Peddapurair
border areas, capturing 100 Ra
zakars.
(A military headquarters spokes
man said "the Indian advance con
tinues in all sectors of Hyderabad
despite opposition in some areas.”
(The Hyderabad radio gave the
distance of the penertations by
Indian troops thus far as: South

By the Associated Press
BERLIN, Sept. 14.—Gen. Lucius
D. Clay said today: “I don’t
think war is just around the
corner.”
The United States military gover
nor made the statement to Amer
ican newsmen who came to Berlin
on a special Air Force flight from
the United States to view the
British-American air lift operation
supplying Soviet-blockaded Western
Berlin.
Gen. Clay added that he doubted
the Russians “wall make Berlin the
final issue on the entire German
problem.”
The American commander pre
dicted that before any forceful
actions were attempted to break
the 82-day-old Soviet blockade, the
United States would exhaust "all
other means at its disposal” to reach
agreement.
Gen. Clay refused to discuss
whether bringing the Berlin ques
tion before a Council of Foreign
Ministers would mean "negotiation
under duress.”
He left the impression, however,
that the United States at least would
not be prepared to discuss any other
larger German issues with the Rus
sians at such a conference before
the blockade was lifted.

Ex-Premier ol Bengal
Succeeds Jinnah as
Hecid of Pakistan
Nazimuddin Takes Oath
At Karachi as Acting
Governor General
By the Associated Press
KARACHI. Pakistan, Sept. 14.
—Kwaja Nazimuddin, former
Premier of Bengal, took the bath
today as acting governor gen
eral of Pakistan.
He will fill the vacancy caused
by the death Saturday of Mahomed
Ali Jinnah. founder and first gov
ernor-general of the new Moslem
dominion.
Today’s cabinet announcement,
|issued at noon said:
‘‘On advice of the Prime Minister
lor Pakistan, His Majesty the King
is pleased to appoint Kwaja Nazi
muddin acting governor-general of
Pakistan in the vacancy caused by
the sad demise of Quaid-e-Azam
(great leaden Mahomed Ali Jin
nah."
Educated in England.
Mr; Nazimuddin, 54. was educated
at Harrow and Cambridge in Eng
land 8nd later became a member
of the English bar. He had been a
member of the government in his
native province of Bengal at various
times since 1927.
He was Premier of Bengal from
1943 to 1945 and became Premier of
East Bengal after partition last
year. He once was knighted by the
British crown but renounced the
title at a meeting of the Moslem
League in Bombay in 1946.
As education minister in Bengal
in 1929, Mr. Nazimuddin fought for
compulsory primary education. For
10 years he was a member of the
working committee of the All-India
Moslem League, the organization
which Mr. Jinnah led and which
fought for and won the Moslem
nation of Pakistan.
Was League Representative.
He was a member of an Indian
food delegation to the United States
in 1946 and represented India at the
last League of Nations meeting at
Geneva.
A keen sportsman, he was presi
dent of the India Hockey Federation
from 1938 to 1940. His favorite
sports were described as cricket,
hockey, football and tennis, but he
also was reported to be an ardent
fisherman and to be Interested in
shooting.
2 U. S. Flyers Missing
In Russian Zone
By the Associated Press
FRANKFURT, Germany, Sept.
14—Two American air lift pilots
bailed out over the Soviet zone
j early today and have not been
heard from since.
Air Force headquarters said they
were ferrying a C-47. laden with
food, from Wiesbaden to Berlin,
when the pilot radioed at 4:20 a m.
that their engines had failed and the
ship was losing altitude.
The message said the crew was
abandoning ship in the vicinity of
Langensalza, about 20 miles inside
the Russian zone. It said one mo
tor had cut out and the other had
begun to quit.
Nine Americans have died in three
j fatal crashes since the air lift over
the Soviet blockade of Berlin began
|in June.

Washington Leads Big Cities
In Aggravated Assault Cases
Washington led all other cities
with more than 100.000 population
in the number of aggravated as
saults reported to police in the first
six months of this fear, the uniform
crime reports of the Federal Bureau
of Investigation indicated today.
A semi-annual bulletin listed a
total of 1.867 such assaults here.
Next highest was Detroit, with 1,881.
Other crime in the District was
tabulated as follows; Larcenies un
der $50. a total of 4.858 during the
six months: larcenies $50 and over,
1,007; burglaries and housebreaking.
2,381: automobile thefts, 32: rob
beries, 676, and murders, 30.
In releasing the bulletin. FBI Di
rector J. Edgar Hoover noted that
nationally, crimes in rural areas in
k
creased 3.8 per cent in the first half
of 1948 over the comparable period
of 1947. and declined in urban areas
1.8 per cent.
Only two cities were reported as
having more petty larcenies mnder
$50> than Washington. They are
Detroit, with 8.979; Los Angeles,
with 8.376.
Washington tied with Dallas for
Uth place among the 83 cities in
the number of murders. New York
led with 155.
The District had the fifth highest
number of robberies, the fifth high
est number of burglaries and house
breakings. the fourth highest num
ber of grand larcenies and the
eighth highest number of automo
bile thefts.

ling all questions at public hearings

ican—the most un-American we

try in which scientists are expected

The smashing Republican vic
tory in Maine yesterday was
hailed today by Herbert Brow
nell, jr., Dewey campaign man
ager, and by other Republican
leaders, as forecasting a sweep
ing victory for Gov. Dewey of
New York over President Tru
man next November.
It disproved, they said, the Demo
cratic claim that the bigger the vote
the better for Democratic candi
dates. The total votes cast in the
Senatorial and House elections in t,
Maine yesterday were records in
size,
Mrs. Margaret Chase Smith, the
first woman ever elected to the
Senate without previous appoint
ment to that office, defeated her
Democratic opponent. Dr. Adrian H.
orolten, 154.587 to 61,837. with the
returns virtually complete. The
total vote cast in this race was
216.524, compared to a total of
166,274 in the Senatorial race in
Maine in 1946.
Since Maine always holds these
congressional elections in September
—almost tw’o months in advance of
the presidential election, the count,
is a Presidential year Is comparable
to that in a nonpresidential year.
There was no Maine senatorial elec
tion In 1944.
Record Majority for Maine.
Furthermore, Mrs. Smith's lead
over Dr. Scolten was approximately
94,000. This is a record majority for
any election in Maine, upsetting a
20-year record of 82.481 established
in the 1928 gubernatorial contest.
Representative Hale, Republican,
in the first congressional district,

Democratic opponent, James A. Mc
Vicar, former Democratic State

years ago, Mr. Hale won by only
12,599 in a total vote of 65,353. In
1944, a presidential year, he won by
26,101, and the total vote was 69,340.

ber. The official results in Maine

overwhelming majority.

ported,’’ it now appears that Mr*.

74 per cent of the votes in the race
for United States Senator (the As
sociated press reports fix the per
centage at 71.4 per cent). Her elec
tion to the Senate as the first
woman to win a Senate seat in her
own right without prioi service
under appointment is incidentally
evidence of the Republican Party
policy which calls for full and equal
participation by women in political
affairs.
“Other Republican candidates
won by impressive majorities. The
Maine results indicate unmistakably
that we will have a Republican
President after January 1. 1947.”

ren ticket, wired Mrs. Smith:

6 Colleges to Benefit
Under Hughes' Will
Six colleges and universities are
among beneficiaries under the will
of Charles Evans Hughes, former
Chief Justice, which was filed today
with the register of wills here,
The will made specific cash be
quests of $160,000 and left the bal
ance of the estate to his son and
two daughters.
The educational institutions men
tioned are Brown University, willed
$25,000, and five bequeathed $15,000
each—Wellesley College, Colgate
University. Vassar College, Amherst
College and Barnard College, New
York City.
Justice Hughes, who died August
27 at his summer home on Cape
Cod, also left $25,000 to the Asso
ciation of the Bar of New York,
$15,000 to the New York County
Lawyer*’ Association and $5,000 to
his secretary, Wendell W Mischler.
The Legal Aid Society'of New
York City was bequeathed $15,000.
The son, Charles Evans Hugh*?,
jr., was given Justice Hughes’ law
library.

whether it will hear the Lawson i
case. This will be announced in the
course of its term, which runs nine
months. Important labor issues also

work.

are considering.
Lawson already has an appeal

Court of Appeals here, but he
j asked the Supreme Court to take
over instead.

Supreme Court petition said, “the
case involves inquisition by means

well depend the nature of our fu
ture society, whether the time has

ciples.”

335.000 Coal Miners
Strike in France
By th* Associated Press
DOUAI, Prance, Oct. 4.—A strike
of some 335,000 French coal miners
that could paralyze the nation’s in
dustry if prolonged began quietly
today.
At two mines near here groups
of pickets from the Communist
dominated General Confederation of
Labor (CGT) gathered at mine en
trances. Neither police nor soldiers
were in evidence.
The walkout, in protest against
wages and living costs, threatened
to cripple coal fields in the north of
France and in the Pas de Calais
area.
The strike was called for an “un
limited duration’’ by the Commu
nist-led Federation of Miners. The
non Communist workers’ force,
claiming 120,000 members, said it
would Join the strike “effectively
but not actively.” The non-Com
munist French Confederation of
Christian Workers agreed to strike
for only 48 hours.
The National Federation claims
320.000 members and the Christian
Workers 30,000. The various claims
•f union membership are overlap
ping and confusing. Many miners
carry more than one union card.
Peru Crushes Revolt
By Navy at Callao;
Port Under Control
Casualties May Be High;
Aprista Party Blamed in
Sailor-Civilian Uprising
By the Associated Press
LIMA, Peru, Oct. 4.—Govern
ment troops held full control
over the port of Callao today
after smashing a bloody revolt
by sailors and civilians.
The insurgents, who siezed and
used the full firepower of warships
and heavily armed naval instal
lations at Callao, Lima’s port,
launched the revolt early yesterday.
By late last night, government
infantrymen, supported by tanks
and fighter planes, had rooted the
rebels out of their fortified strong
holds and apparently had captured
them all.
No Indication of Casualties.
There was no official indication
of the number of casualties or of
how many persons took part in the
revolt. All accounts indicated, how
ever, that the number of dead and
wounded was large. An army auth
ority said there were so many cas
ualties that trucks probably would
be used to augment ambulances in
carrying them away.
The government of President Jose
Luis Bustamente Rivero said in a
communique it had-, confirmed that
the opposition Aprista Party had
‘‘inspired and directed” the revolt.
The communique said many armed
civilians affiliated with Aprista were
arrested.
“Comdr. Enrique Aguila Pardo,
who is well known because of his
Aprista leanings, headed the rebel
lion aboard some warship,” the
communique said.
Civilians Attacked First.
The rebels’ first attack occured
about 3 a.m., Police Col. Felix Pie
lago Stagnaro said, when about 40
civilians, well equipped with navy
arms, attacked a Callao police sta
tion. A police squad routed them
Col. Pielago said.
In rapid order, sailors and civil
ians seized the naval academy,

RedsChallenged
By Austin on
Atomic Control
Answer to Russian
Switch Is Demand
For Serious Work
By the Associated Press
PARIS, Oct. 4.—Warren R.
Austin of the United States chal
lenged Russia today to say if she
is ready to agree to effective
international control of atomic
energy.
He opened, In the Political Com
mittee of the United Nations As
sembly, the Western powers’ drive
to answer Russia’s latest switch in
tactics in the atomic energy debate.
Mr. Austin said he hoped the time
for throwing “old tomato cans and
dead cats” is over. He added that
he hoped the delegates could get
down to real work.
Answers Violent Attacks.
His answer to violet attacks by
Russia’s Andrei Vishinsky and
Dmitri Z. Manuilsky of the Soviet
Ukraine was that he would try to
do nothing to make the committee’s
job harder.
Mr. Vishinsky, Russia’s deputy
foreign minister, startled the com
mittee Saturday with a resolution
calling for the U. N. Atomic Energy
Commission to continue work. He
proposed that the Assembly call for
a treaty banning atomic weapons
and a second treaty establishing
effective controls, both to take
effect at the same time. He hinted
Russia might have the atomic bomb.
Mr. Austin, the chief American
delegate, asked Russia what is
meant by “effective” control in the
Soviet resolution. He said the
United States and a majority of
the Atomic Commission want ef
fective controls. He said if both
mean the same thing the task is
simple.
He said he believed, however, the
Russians did not mean to accept
the majority and were not “pre
pared to go ahead in the same
direction” as the rest of the com
mittee.
Doubts Readiness for Control.
The American said Russian re
marks in debates indicate they ac
tually are not ready for effective
international control. He said the
Russians still adhere to national
ism.
“There is a chasm that must be
bridged in their debate and their I
resolution,” he said. It may
be that after the delegates have
thrown all the bricks they have to
throw there may be a way to bridge
the gulf” between the Russian and
United States viewpoints.
Mr. Austin declared the United
States Will go a long way to get
unanimity. He went on that Rus
sia has indicated she will not ac
cept a Canadian resolution endors
ing the majority report of the U. N.
commission, or a Syrian suggestion
that the commission continue work
on the basis of that report.
He called for adoption of the
Canadian resolution, as did Sweden
and New Zealand.
The committee’s afternoon session
was called off so leading delegates
could attend the Security Council

Over Atlantic in r19 Dies
By th* Associated Press
SWANSEA, Wales, Oct. 4. —Sir
Arthur Whitten-Brown, 62, navi
gator of the first airplane to fly
the Atlantic Ocean nonstop, died to
day.
Sir Arthur and Pilot John Alcock
opened the era of transatlantic
flying in June, 1919, when they flew
a converted, World War I Vickers
Vimy bomber from Newfoundland
to Ireland. Sir John was killed
in an air crash in Prance later
that year.

British Stop Wrecking
Of Air Raid Shelters
By the Associated Press

LONDON, Oct. 4.—Preparedness
minded Britain has stopped general
demolition of its wartime air-raid
shelters.
A home office spokesman said to-
day that except for shelters which
have been condemned as dangerous
all of these refuges from air attack
will be left standing for the time
being.
No general instruction has been
issued, the Home Office said, but
suspension notices have been sent
to “certain areas.” The spokesman
said no more detailed information
was available.
No statistics were available on
the number of shelters still in good
condition. Thousands—chiefly sur
face shelters erected in busy city
streets and parks—have Ibeen des
troyed.
Federal Reserve Plans
Antitrust Hearings on
Transamerica Corp.
Uses 1914 Clayton Act
Powers for First Time;
Sessions to Be Public
By the Associated Press
The Federal Reserve Board
announced today it will hold,
public hearings here October 12
on antitrust charges against the
Transamerica Corp. of San Fran
cisco, the Nation’s biggest bank
holding company.
The announcement The first
official disclosure of the case by
the Reserve Board—said that
Transamerica itself had requested
that the hearings be made public.
Headed by A. P. Giannini of the
widely known California banking
family, Transamerica has an in
terest in more than 500 banks and
industrial concerns in five Western
states.
Until Transamerica requested
public status for the case, the
Reserve Board had refused any
official statement on the antitrust
charges, although^ lied the initial
complaint last June 24.
Credit Monopoly Charged.
The complaint is a civil charge
that Transamerica has built up a
"credit monopoly.” through placing
directors on the Boards of Banks in
the huge Bank of America chain in
California and other banks in
Oregon, Washington, Arizona and
Nevada.
It was filed under the Clayton:
Antitrust Act, which permits the
Federal Reserve Board to make the
charges and to sit as a "court” to
pass on tha evidence. If It finds
against Trans-America." the board
can order the concern to get rid of
all its stock in banks.
Never Defore in its 35-year history
has tne Reserve Board tried to use
its powers under the Clayton Act,
which was passed in 1914.
Congressional hearings have re
vealed that, as far back as 1945.
The Justice Department was
studying the possibility of antitrust
action against Trans-America under
the Sherman Act, but doubtful it
could win such a case.
Not in Court Now.
In the pending proceedings, the
Reserve Board has moved without
going through the Justice Depart
ment or the courts, although it can
use the latter to enforce any order
it may issue for a company to get
rid of bank stocks.
Transamerica's Board Chairman
Giannini and Marriner S. Eccles,
whom President Truman demoted
this year from chairman to plain
member of the Reserve Board, have
long been engaged in a public battle
over a bill sponsored by Mr. Eccles
to let the Reserve Board regulate
bank holding companies.
The bill has been considered in
several sessions of Congress, but has
never been passed.

FENWAY PARK, Boston, Oct.
4.—The Boston Red Sox, who re
claimed a lost cause by tying for

regular season, met the Cleve
land Indians here today in the
American League’s first playoff
to decide the championship.

34,000 fans packed the stands.

first, but Boston tied it up.

surprise starter for the Red Sox.

pitchgr a secret until the last pos
sible minute, finally named South
paw Gene Bearden (19-7) to face the
Sox. The rookie from Arkansas, who
has won his last six starts, was
working with only one full day’s rest.

last Saturday. He holds a 2-1 edge
on the Red Sox for the season.

BOSTON, Oct. 4 UP).—Staid old

that its Red Sox were goners, got as
excited as any town could today
with renewed hope for a city World
Series.
Fans who as recently as last
Thursday were accused of giving up
on the men of McCarthy stormed

kill in the playoff game with the
Cleveland Indians. Before the start
ing pitchers were announced the
betting odds were 10 to 7 in favor
of the Red Sox.

all night in quest of 8,500 bleacher
seats. They huddled under blankets
in 40-degree temperature, built cam*
fires in the streets and generally
reacted in a fashion that would

'Truculent Turtle' Lands
In Chile, Fails to Set Mark
By the Associated Press
SANTIAGO, Chile, Oct. 4.—The
United States Navy bomber "Tru
culent Turtle” arrived here yester
day on a flight from Patuxent, Md.,
interrupted by a landing at Anto
fagasta because of radio trouble.
The landing at Antofagasta was
700 miles short of the Turtle’s goal.
The plane had covered about 5,500
miles in an attempt to set an inter
American distance record.
Mother Rescues
Son, 3, Floating
Faceup in Pond
A quick-thinking mother saved
her 3-year-old son from drowning
today.
Mrs. Marabel Mason found the
child floating face up in a fish pond
at her home at Paint Branch Farm,
near Colesville, Md. She got him
out of the water and revived him
with artificial respiration. The
pond is 3 feet deep.
The boy, Leon Roger Mason, is
recovering at Montgomery County
General Hospital, Sandy Spring,
where he was taken by the Sandy
Spring rescue squad.
The boy’s father, Leroy W. Mason,
is a plumbing contractor.
Police and hospital attendants
credited Mrs. Mason >ith saving
Leon’s life.

WASHINGTON NEWS
WEDNESDAY, OCTOBER 20, 1948
Apathy Afflicts
Both Parties in
Fredericksburg
Neither Side
Excited Enough for
Committee Talks
(This is one of a series on the
political outlook in Northern Vir
ginia.)
By Alex R. Preston
Star Staff Correspondent
FREDERICKSBURG, Va., Oct. 20.
—Neither the Democrats nor the
Republicans are sufficiently excited
about the election to call meetings
of their committees.
In addition to pronounced apathy
within the core of each major party,
the local picture is highlighted by:
Public indorsement of Gov. Thur
mond of South Carolina. States’
Rights candidate for President, by
one of the most influential political
figures here.
A political rally—the only one
held, or planned—for Representa
tive Scott, chairman of the Republi
can National Committee, was at
tended by leading Democrats as well
as Republicans. Mr. Scott is a na
tive of Fredericksburg.
The only conclusion reached after
making the rounds among persons
who usually know about such
things is that Representative Bland,
Democrat, has little to worry about
Fredericksburg’s vote. Mr. Bland
is opposed by Stanley G. Adams, Re
publican, and J. Luther Kibler, In
dependent, in the First Congres
sional District which extends from
here all the way to Newport News
and across Chesapeake Bay to the
Eastern Shore.
GOP Win Seen Likely.
From what leaders have to say,
however, it is indicated that Fred
ericksburg probably will vote Re
publican this year for the second
time in history. The city followed
most of the rest of the State in 1928
when Virginia voted for Herbert
Hoover over A1 Smith.
The last time Gov. Dewey ran for
President in 1944, however. Freder
icksburg gave him only 698 votes
against 1.092 for President Roose
velt. Mr. Bland, incidentally, got
1,166 to 371 for Walter Johnson, his
Republican opponent, that same
year.
Milton B. Graves, insurance deal
er, who is chairman of the Demo
cratic Committee, said members “do
not think they need to be called into
a meeting.”
"The Democratic party is split,
right to the committee itself,” he
said. “As a matter of fact, the split
goes further. It goes right into
aome families.”
Elliott E. Brooks, real estate dealer
who is Republican Committee chair
man, said no G. O. P. rallies have
been held since the one for Repre
sentative Scott and that “none is
planned” by his committee.
“We’ve got things rolling pretty;
much our way now,” he explained.
“There’s no use of stirring up things
and getting the Democrats mad at
us with a lot of talk.”
Attitude Is Common.
This attitude has been noted
among many Northern Virginia Re
publican leaders.
Mr. Brooks conceded that Mr.
Bland will be elected by the total
1st District vote but promised some
setback for him in the Fredericks
burg area. He said Senator Rob
ertson, Democrat, will have a more
difficult time getting the district
vote from Robert H. Woods, Pearis
burg, State G. O. P. chairman who
also is running for the Senate.
Former State Senator C. O’Conor
Goolrick, a leader of the Demoratic
organization, is the avowed sup
porter of Gov. Thurmond. Mr. Gool
rick, incidentally, is to speak to
night at the Municipal Building in
Culpeper while Representative Scott
addresses a University of Virginia
Young Republican Club rally at the
same time in Charlottesville.
Mr- .Goolrick said he did not in
dorse Gov. Thurmond with the idea
of actively entering the campaign.
“A lot of my friends feel that
rather than vote for President Tru
man they will vote for Gov. Dewey,”
he said.
Disagrees With Poll.
He disagreed with a recent na
tional political poll'which indicated
Gov. Thurmond would receive only
8 per cent of Virginia’s vote. Mr.
Goolrick estimated Gov. Thurmond
will get about 15 per cent of the
State and local vote.
Mr. Goolrick said he felt all pub
lic officials “of importance” should
make their position known in the
presidential race. He would not
mention which public officials he
had in mind but Gov.. Tuck and
Senator Byrd are among the last to
refrain from committing themselves.
Mr. Goolrick agreed that some
significance could be attached to
Gov. Tuck’s introduction of Gov.
Thurmond at a recent Richmond
rally and also to the presence of
Mrs. Byrd, wife of the Senator, at
a Thurmond banquet in Richmond.
Effort to Reorganise Party.
‘The Thurmond movement,” he
said, “is not an attempt to create
a new party. It is an effort to re
organize the Democratic Party. The
Democrats of the South have got
to show they they can not be treated
with the utmost contempt. They
deserve to have their place in the
sun.”
The Negro Vote is not considered a
decisive factor in the 1st District by
either major party. Gloucester
Counfy in the district is one of two
whic recently figured in Negro’s at
tempt to obtain equal school facili
ties through court action. The other,
King George County—which is
closer—is in the 8th District.

A BEAR WITH SPECTACLES—The spectacled bear is the way
zoologists list this new bruin, acquired yesterday by the Zoo.
The name is taken from the white rings around the bear’s eyes.
This species is native to the Andes region of South America and
is the only bear found in the southern half of the Western Hemi
sphere, according to Dr. William M. Mann, Zoo director. The
newcomer, an 18-month-old female, is a gift of the San Diego
(Calif.) Zoo. —Star Staff Photo.
Some Food Items to Sell at Cost
In Center Market Campaign
Center Market City merchants *ill I
introduce next week a plan to sell
six food items at cost for three days
of the week.
The plan was evolved by the 90
independent merchants operating
stands at the big market at Fifth
and K streets N.W., with the aim of
benefiting both customers and mer
chants.
In cutting the prices of six items a j
week, the merphants reasoned cus- j
tomers taking advantage of them j
would remain to do their shopping
for other foodstuffs.
Operating on a rotation basis, six
merchants will offer as many items
at cost Tuesday through Thursday
each week. On Mondays, news
papers will carry six coupons, one
for each item, and customers will be
required to present them in making
their purchases. Customers will be
restricted to the use of one coupon
for each purchase.
Next week the merchants will fea
ture eggs, poultry, potatoes, round
steak, bacpn and butter. The list of
prices has not been completed, but
potatoes, usually selling at five
pounds for 25 cents, will be offered
at nine pounds for a quarter.
There are 33 meat stands, 17 fruit
and vegetable stands, nine grocery
and delicatessen stands, seven poul
try stands and five bakery stands at
the market, which covers an acre
and was established in 1874.
Merchants said customers would
not be under obligation to buy
items other than those offered at
cost.
After-Dark Violators
Of Horn-Blowing Law
Get Special Attention
Motorists who sound their horns!
at night to attract friends indoors!
are due for special attention in the
police department's antihom-blowing
effort.
This was the order to all precinct
commanders late yesterday from
Acting Supt. Floyd A. Truscott.
It came as the list of arrested
horn-blowers continued to mount.
Three more were arrested in the last
24 hours, Capt. L. T. Johnson of the
traffic division reported today, bring
ing the total in the nine-day-old
campaign to 26.
Inspector Truscott's order, which
called for an arrest, violation notice
or warning for every violation of
the horn regulations, declared:
"The Commissioners of the Dis
trict of Columbia have formally and
wholeheartedly indorsed the anti
horn-blowing and unnecessary noise
campaign now being sponsored and
promoted by The Evening Star. It
is our duty and responsibility to do
everything possible to eliminate or
alleviate this bad practice cm the
part of many motorists by a good,
sound, consistent and continuous
enforcement program.”
The order cited as particularly
objectionable drivers who sound
their horns for motorists ahead to
move “the instant the green light
comes on,” as well as the late-hours
horn-blowers.
The only. excuse for using the
horn under the regulations, Inspec
tor Truscott’s order pointed out, is
when it is a "reasonable warning of
danger.”

Elect Col. Kenyon
Col. Howard N. Kenyon, U. S. M.

Prince Georges Jury
Asks Stiff Penalty for
Liquor Sale to Minors
The Prince Georgefc County grand
jury has urge4 a crackdown on
sales of alcoholic beverages to
minors. It also has recommended
caution in spending for school con
struction.
In its report, submitted yesterday,
the Jury asserted it felt that the
“consumption of alcoholic bev
erages by minors is one of society'6
most serioti* problems, frequently
resulting in juvenile delinquency
and crime.
“Sales of such beverages to minors
should' not be tolerated,’’ the jury
declared.
As an enforcement measure, the
jury “urgently recommended" that
violators of this regulation, “when
apprehended, be vigorously prose
cuted and severely dealt with, and
that, after conviction, the licenses
involved be either suspended for
substantial lengths of time or, in
aggravated and proper cases, be
permanently revoked."
Four county taverns, cited in five
separate charges during the past
few weeks, still have cases pend
ing before the court for alleged
violations of this law. One of
these, the Club Hillbilly, Seat
Pleasant, was named twice in 48
hours by Roy D. Bright, county
parole and probation officer.
On the school money question
the jury asserted:
“There appears to be somewhat of
a public demand for more and
better public schools. Although it
seems to appear that the public
schools of the county may be some
what crowded, it is the feeling of
this body that the present is no
time for the expenditure of any
public funds other than what is
absolutely necessary for the main
tenance of reasonably good county
government, and that any and all
spending which is not absolutely
essential should be deferred for the
time being.”

Decision of 1804
Cited in Brief on
D. C. Litigation
High Court Prepares
For Oral Arguments
On Case Friday
The struggle between the Feder
alists and Thomas Jefferson in 1804
might have influenced Chief Jus
tice Marshall in taking a “cautiously
restrictive approach" to the rights
of District citizens in Federal
courts outside the District, the Su
preme Court was told today.
This reference to a 144-year-old
ruling came in a written brief as
the high court prepared to hear
oral arguments Friday on the con
stitutionality of the 1940 Gwynne
Act.
Tlie act allowed District citizens
and corporations, as well as those
of Hawaii, Alaska and other Ter
ritories, to sue and be sued in civil
cases in Federal courts anywhere
in the United States.
The Federal court in Baltimore
and the United States Court of Ap
peals in Richmond held the law in
valid last year.
The Supreme Court is asked—by
the Justice Department among oth
ers—to declare it constitutional.
The court is asked to rule that Fed
eral courts everywhere give District
citizens the same status as is ex
plicitly guaranteed to people of
other States and to aliens—includ
ing aliens living here.
Involves Contract Case.
The pending case involves $10,000
which the National Mutual Insur
ance Co. of the District of Columbia
seeks to recover from the Tidewater
Transfer Co. of Virginia on charges
of breach of contract.
But the constitutional issue is re
garded as so important that the
Justice Department, without com
menting on the merits of the $10,000
dispute, has intervened as a friend
of the court.
Solicitor General Philip B. Perl
man. in his written brief, today
described the 1940 Gwynne law as
"a belated response to Chief Justice
Marshall1! suggestion” in 1804.
He urged that the la# be upheld
not only as being in line with the
Constitution, but 'also as a means
of ending "an unfair discrimination
against citizens of the District.”
Chief Justice Marshall Is some
thing of a pivotal figure in the argu
ments. He held that the First Judi
ciary Act of September. 1789, did not
give Federal circuit courts jurisdic
tion in civil suits between District
citizens and those of the several
States. He remarked that this was
“extraordinary,” but that It never
theless was a subject "for legislative,
not judicial consideration.”
See Stand for Legislation.
Attorneys arguing in behalf of
such rights for District citizens do
not denounce the Marshall ruling.
They do not concede that he "in
tended to reflect on the lack of con
stitutional authority” for Congress
to extend those rights. They declare
on the contrary, that his stand gives
strong support, even at this late date,
for power of Congress to enact
legislation such as the Gwynne Act
of 1940.
The brief prepared for the insur
ance company, however, declares
even if Chief Justice Marshall
did mean to say Congress lacked
authority to help District people in
this way. his view in 1804 should be
re-examined in 1948 by the Supreme
Court.
Noting that the Washington popu
Iation now is close to 1,000,000
whereas it had only 3,000 people,
including transients and foreigners,
in 1804, the brief suggests:
“The contemporaneous struggle
between the Federalists and the
followers of Thomas Jeqfferson (in
1804) and the resultant opposition to
the Federal courts may have in
fluenced Marshall’s cautiously re
strictive approach in that case.
The changed economic and political
scene requires a different approach
to and conclusion for the problem.”
Ruling In Lower Courts.
In the pending case, the lower
courts held the Gwynne Act un
constitutional mainly on the ground
that the District is not a “state”
within the meaning of the constitu
tional section for establishing the
judiciary; that Congress lacked
authority to vest Federal courts with
Jurisdiction in civil cases between
District citizens and those of other
States, and that the exclusive legis
lative authority of Congress over the
District is territorially limited to
the District.
The Justice Department brief con
tends: That the District is a "state”
within the meanihg of the Judicial
power section of the Constitution
just as it is a “state” within the
application of treaties, taxation,
statutes and other constitutional
provisions. That the Gwynne Act
was a proper exercise of the Judi
cial power as defined In Article III
of the Constitution.
That it was an appropriate exer
cise of a far-reaching power of
Congress to legislate for the Dis
trict.
Maintaining that District citizens
and corporations should have access
to Federal courts equal to that
granted those of States and to
aliens, attorneys for the Insurance
company argued;
That framers of the Constitution
had no intention of discriminating
against future residents of the Dis
trict.
That citizens of the District orig
inally were citizens of States and
were entitled to the same equal
legal processes.
That the Federal Government’s
authority to legislate for the Dis
trict is national in character and
not confined to the Washington city
limits.

Congress Committee
'Sloppy' Subpoenas
Hit at Kamp Trial
The Nation’s lawmakers some
times use sloppy subpoenas to sum
mon persons before Congressional
committees, opposing counsel agreed
yesterday in arguing before the
United States Court of Appeals.
They disagreed, however, as to1
the effect such laxity should have
on the appeal of Joseph P. Kamp.
Mr. Kamp was sentenced in Dis
trict Court to four months in Jail
and a $100 fine was suspended. He
was convicted of contempt of Con
gress in refusing to produce before
a House special committee investi
gating campaign expenditures rec
ords of the Constitutional Educa
tional League, Inc., of which he was
executive vice president. The in
cident took place in October. 1944.
Kamp was convicted last March
16. A previous trial ended in a mis
trial in December, 1946.
Argues Trial Improper.
John J. Wilson, representing
Kamp, argued his client had been
brought to trial improperly, since
the subpoena served on him was
directed to the House sergeant-at
arms and didn’t command Kamp,
directly, to do anything.
Mr. Wilson also contended the
indictment was faulty because it
didn’t allege Kamp was in posses
sion of the records the committee
wanted.
The subpoena didn’t even read
like a subpoena, Mr. Wilson insisted.
It had more of the appearance of
a warrant, he said.
Mr. Wilson admitted his client,
“being a man of common sense,"
understood what the committee ex
pected of him. He wouldn’t have
known, though, if he had inter
preted the wording strictly, the
lawyer argued.
Assistant United States Attorney
William Hits, appearing for the
Government, contended that Kamp
didn’t fail to produce the papers be
cause he didn’t comprehend the
subpoena. He recalled Kamp had
told the committee he would not
reveal the contributors to his or
ganization because he did not con
sider it a political group and felt the
committee had no right to inquire
into its affairs.
Admits Writ Poorly Drawn.
“Ill concede, frankly, that it is
not a well-drawn subpoena," Mr.
Hitz said.
"Do you have any notion why they
use this form instead of a regular
subpoena?” Judge E. Barrett Pretty
man inquired.
“No, your honor, I do not,” Mi.
Hitz replied.
Judge Henry W. Edgerton noted
subpoenas of the same form has
served the purpose in the past, al
though other types also had been
issued by congressional committees.
Judge D. Lawrence Groner sug
gested it might prove to be "a pretty
reckless and careless method of
coming into court” if persons sum
moned were to be expected to draw
on knowledge beyond the letter of
a subpoena in interpreting it.
The court took Kamp’s appeal
under advisement.

Medical Bureau Handles Calls
For Doctors at 2 P.M. or 2 A.M.
By George Beveridge
Have you ever been unable to
locate the family doctor—or antf
doctor—when you needed him most?
Ten years ago, there probably was
good reason for spending frantic
moments thumbing through the
telephone directory to find a doctor.
If you go through such wasted mo
tion now—whether it’s at 2 pm. or
2 a.m.—you probably haven’t heard
of the Medical Bureau of the Dis
trict Medical Society.
The bureau has two main pur
poses. One is to handle calls for
doctors. The other is to reach your
doctor for you or, if he isn’t avail
able, to get another one—fast.
The bureau’s busiest times, when
doctors are least available, are be
tween 6 and 9 pm. daily and on
Thursday afternoons, according to
a recent bureau survey. Doctors,
like other persons, have to take
some time off from the job. And
many of them choose Thursday
afternoons.
350 Doctors Listed.
The bureau Is set up to take over
any time the doctor can’t he reached
at his office or home. It’s business
is carried on at a three-operator
switchboard at its headquarters,
1718 M street N.W., where more than
1,000 calls a day are handled
through the number—Republic 6100.
About 350 of the District’s 1,300
practicing doctors are listed with
the bureau and the switchboard
takes calls on direct lines from the
offices and homes of about 360
others. If these 600 doctors can
be reached, the bureau operators
know where. If they can’t, the
bureau has a list of alternate phys
icians who are available.
Most dramatic of the bureau’s
services is its “emergency” list from
which the operators must get a doc
tor on the job within 10 minutes
of the time an emergency call comes
in. according to J. Richard Connelly,
who directs the bureau’s activities.
100 on Emergency List.
More than 100 doctors, listed ac
cording to the areas in which they
live, are on the emergency list for
night calls alone.
Often, though, the operators are
called on for more than merely to
locate a doctor. Bureau officials cite
the call received by Mrs. Helen
Workman, 413 Crittenden street
N.W., as a good example.
Several weeks ago, a woman called
to report that she had taken an
overdose of sleeping pills. While
calling the police rescue squad on
another line, Mrs. Workman said
she kept up a running conversation
with the woman because she knew
it was important in such cases for
the victim to remain conscious.
‘T talked to her for more than 10
minutes about everything I could
think of," the operator said. “Every
once in a while the telephone would
slip out of her hand and she collap
sed at just about the time the police
gpt there.”
Doctors Recommended.
The woman was rushed to Gal
linger Hospital, where she was re
ported in good condition the next
day, Mrs. Workman said.
The bureau operators stress that
no treatment is available at its
headquarters. They also determine
whether patients are able to pay for
private care. Those who are not
able are referred to the Health De
partment or Hospital Service
Agency. The bureau was begun in
1940, with 100 doctors, and is still
growing.
Another service has beert set up
for people who call in to ask that
a doctor be recommended. Often,
Mr. Connelly said, the request is for
“the best doctor in town.”
To meet this rather delicate prob
lem, the bureau has classified all
the society's members according to
specialties or general practice.
About one-third of the doctors are
in the latter group.
Acta as Information Service.
The operators suggest names of
three doctors—never one—from the
classified lists and let the callers
make their own choices.
The bureau also acts as an in
formation service to provide general
data on medical facilities here tb
about 75 callers every day.
Like every switchboard, the
bureau’s gets its share of unusual
calls such as one from a woman
who wanted the name of the doctor
“who drives a black Cadillac” and
another from a woman who wanted
the doctor to "treat my little dog.”
This call was referred to the Animal
Rescue League.
2 Alexandria Officers
Out in Health Shakeup
One Alexandria health official re
signed yesterday and another was
discharged in a “personnel shift"
which was forecast at a meeting of
the Health Board Monday night.
City Manager Nicholas A. Cola
santo said he discharged Roland M.
Orrison, a sanitarian, who was in
charge of the pound and scavenger
service. Mr. Orrison had been with
the city 17 years.
Sanitary Engineer Keith Wade,
head of the health department
sanitation division, submitted his
resignation yesterday afternoon to
Mary Karne, acting health officer.
Mr. Wade, a health department em
ploye who ranked second to the
health officer, said he had planned
to resign for some time.
He said his resignation was not
prompted by the recent survey by
State health officials, which was
sharply critical of the workings of
the Health Department sanitation
division.
Just before publication of the
health survey report, City Health
Officer W. Lewis Schafer, jr., re
signed. Dr. Schafer also denied that
-his resignation was prompted by
the health report.
Citizens'Group Indorses
Site for Cardozo School
The Central Northwest Citizens’
Association last night indorsed a
proposed site for a new Cardozo
High School building at W street
and Georgia avenue N.W.
Dr. Emmett Williams, chairman
of the Education Committee, pro
posed that letters be sent to Garnet
C. Wilkinson, first assistant super
intendent in charge of colored
schools, and to the high school
principal, in favor of the new site.
The resolution was passed at the
meeting in Garnet Patterson Junior
High School, Tenth and U streets
N.W.
A site at Thirteenth and S streets
N.W. also has Men considered for the
building. Dr. Wilkinson said today,
and neither has Men decided on.

Draftees' and Veterans' Guide
.By Maj. Thomas M. Niol
If you’re wondering about farm
deferments and whether you’ll get
one, you’ve got company.
A short quote from a letter I got
makes a good starting point: “I find
that farm deferments will be made
only on short-term emergency basis,
however justifiable the case may be,”
writes a Jackson, Mich., correspon
dent. ‘‘Any comments?”
“You find exactly right” would be
the first comment I could offer. Be
cause that's the whole story on farm
deferments—they will be made on a
short-term basis, no matter what the
case may be.
H6w short? One year, to be exact.
After that, the local board reclassi
fies the registrant. If conditions
havn't altered, it may, and probably
will reclassify him right back into
Class II-C—deferred for agricultural
activities.
Here is a paraphrase of the selec
tive service regulation on farm de
ferments:
Any registrant employed in the
production for market of a substan
tial quantity of agricultural products
necessary to the maintenance of
the national health, safety or inter
est will be put in Class II-C if:
1. The registrant really is engaged
in that activity, or would be but for
seasonal interruptions.
2. He cannot be replaced.
3. Drafting him would cause a
material loss of effectiveness in his
particular farming operation.
So, right away you want to know
answers to these .questions:
1. What is a “substantial quantity”?
2. How does one define “necessary
to the maintenance of the national
health, safety or interest”?
3. What is a “material loss of
effectiveness”?
Right away I have to say that
these questions do not have answers
today. They may never have answers
which follow any kind of a pattern.
For instance, a substantial quan
tity in one part of the country might
be different than in another. Also,
a substantial quantity of one agri
cultural product might be different
than a substantial quantity of
another.
So, yho decides these things?
Your local board. If they think you
may rate a farm deferment, or if
you ask for one, they will look at
the facts in your case and answer
the questions as they see fit.
I think there will be a good many
farm deferments, because there are
a lot of draft-age people working
on farms. The exact/ age number
is not known, but a reliable estimate
of the number of single, nonvet
eran, draft-age farm workers is
250,000.
There’s little doubt but that many
of these 250,000 will gain draft defer
ments.
So now you know all the “ifs”
about agricultural deferments. To
get the facts you'll have to wait and
see what your draft board does, and
watch for new regulations.
(From UF HewafMturai.)
Freeway Road
Progress Told
ro Keystone Club
Will Extend Across
District Shortly,
Robertsoif Says
The Whitehurst Freeway will ex
tend across the District in the near
future, J. N. Robertson, new di
rector of highways told the Advisory
Committee of the Keystone Auto
mobile Club at its first board meet
ing of the season at the Shoreham
Hotel last night.
The present highway from
Twenty-seventh street to Key Bridge
is now 76 per cent completed and it
is hoped that it can be finished by
next March 1, he said. The second
phase of the project will be a high
and low level roadway from Twenty
first street N.W. to Twenty-seventh
street with a tunnel under Washing
son told the board.
This will be followed by work on
a surface roadway from Connecti
cut avenue and'K street to Twenty
first street connecting with the other
two projects, he said, adding that
the remainder of the freeway to
the District line will be worked out
later.
Completion in November, 1949.
The new Highway Bridge will bo
completed in November, 1949, after
which the old Highway Bridge will
be used for a time before being re
placed with a new structure, Mr.
Robertson said.
The South Capitol Street Bridge
also will be completed in 1949, ho
told the board. The American Steel
Co. will begin erecting steel for
the superstructure about December
15.
The Dupont Circle underpass is
now 26 per cent completed and will
be finished on schedule in March.
1950.
Plans for an underpass at Capitol
Hill also are in the making, he said,
but it will be some time before it
is completed.
The much-discussed Arizona
avenue project will be started soon
and the work of filling the ground
from Canal road to Wisconsin av
enue for the beginning of the work
will start soon, he said.
The greatest problem in the
Highway Department, he saia, was
the resignation of employes for
higher pay in Federal Government
jobs and private enterprises. Mr.
Robertson said he hoped salaries
could be raised, but he said that if
they are, it is going to handicap
his department for want of sufficient
funds to continue highway work.
Disagree on Proposal.
The board could not agree on th#
proposal to have one full-time muni
cipal court Judge devote his tim*
exclusively to traffic cases. Report*
indorsing the plan were heard from
citizens’ associations and civic clubs,
but the judges of both District Court
and Municipal Court were opposed
to it. After some discussion the
matter was referred back to com
mittee.
C. Melvin Sharpe was authorized
to mime a committee to study tha
use of special insignia that can be
seen in the dark, for policemen
on traffic duty. He also was au
thorized to appoint a committee to
study the proposal of the Capital
Transit Co. to reroute some tracks,
Ernest R. Brett, manager of th*
Washington Keystone Club and sec
retary of the Advisory Board, told
the committee of .the new trafflo
regulations and said a committee
should appear before the Commis
sioners when the new regulations
are considered in January.
Maj. Gen. David I. McCoach,
former District Engineer Commis
sioner, was appointed chairman o4
a committee to study the new reg
ulations. Serving with will be Dr.
H. C. Dickenson, Randall Caton,
Edward C. Baltz, W. S. Canning
and Mr. Brett.
Two Men Held in Theft
Of Bus in Alexandria
Alexandria police today were
holding two men for investigation
in connection with the theft of
sightseeing bus from the 1700 bloc*
of Price street, Alexandria.
Police said the men have been
identified by two Arlington officers
as the pair seen by the latter in
the bus as it was being driven on
Arlington Ridge road near Glebe
road, about 15 minutes after it was
stolen yesterday.
The men were not arrested at
that time, however, because a look
out had not yet been broadcast for
the vehicle, owned by the Monu
mental Motor Tour Co., Baltimore,
police explained.
According to Alexandria police,
the bus had been parked in front
of the home of the driver, Thomas
P. Jones.
They said a boy playing nearby
saw two men park their yellow con
vertible car about 100 feet away,
walk back to the bus, climb through
an open window and drive await.
About four hours later, police said.
Pvt. Robert Peacher received a call
at police headquarters from a man
seeking the whereabouts of a yellow
convertible.
Pvt. Peacher asked the man to
come to the police station and iden
tify the car. When the man and a
companion arrived at the station*
they were placed under arrest.
The bus was found early today in
the 100 block of West Alexandria
avenue, about five blocks from where
it was taken.
Defective Oil Stove Fire
Damages Apartment
A fire started by a defective oil
stove did extensive damage today to
a three-room second-floor apart
ment in a house at 745 Second street
N.W.
The fire burned out one room and
damaged a hallway in the three
story brick building. It was brought
under control quickly by firemen
from three engine companies and
one truck company which responded
to a box alarm. No one was in
jured.
John Benton, colored, was listed
by police as the tenant of the apart
ment. Mrs. Delia Lanahan, 5320
Kansas avenue N.W., was lilted as
the building owner.

House Plants Must Be Petted
Sunlight, Watering and Temperatures
Are Major Control Factors to Watch
By W. H. Youngmon
Failure or near failures of house
|\nts may be traced to one or
jLre of the following conditions;
\ack of, or too much, sunlight.
Over or under watering.
Temperatures too high.
Chilling draughts.
Escaping fumes and gases.
Now control of these factors is
not difficult but control is exceed
ingly important to successful cul
ture.
In an apartment, oftentimes, only
one or two exposures exist; and,
where the window gardener is con
fronted with limited facilities, it is;
well to select plants which will
thrive in those exposures.
An African violet, for example,
cannot stand strong sunlight. A j
window facing the south has too;
much sunlight for this sensitive!
plant. It should be placed in a less!
exposed situation during the sum
mer or a curtain used to reduce the
strength of the sun’s rays.
We see geraniums trying to grow
where they receive insufficient sun
light, hence are scrawny and
anemic. A fern placed in a sunny
window will develop the same ap
pearance. A cactus placed in a
south window that is shaded by
trees will not thrive, nor will a
shade-loving fuchsia thrive in front
of a hot, dry window where the sun
pours in most of the day.
No Hard and Fast Rule. k
There is no hard and fast rule to
Judge the suitability of a given
window space for plants. The win
dow gardener will need to study the
particular situation and then select!
plants accordingly. Some experi- j
mentation with curtains may be
necessary to determine the control
needed.
The moisture given to house
plants has much to do with their
health and vigor. Overwatering
tends to exclude air from the soil
and to stunt plant growth. Lack of
moisture effectively prevents them
from obtaining food, for plants can
take in the elements of plant growth
|nly in solution. The problem,
therefore, is to supply sufficient
moisture without the soil becoming
water-logged.
Various rules haye been offered as
guides in the watering, but such
rules do not allow for the variations
in requirements. Some plants, being
more succulent than others, have
higher moisture requirements. The
African violet, for instance, needs
much more moisture than does an
aloe or a kalanchoe.
Put Humus in the Soil.
The soil mixture should contain1
sufficient humus to hold the mois
ture needed, but contain enough
sand to permit excess moisture to
quickly drain away. Plants, such as
the African violet, should be planted
In a soil containing at least 2 parts j
of leafmold or compost, 1 part sand
and 4 part garden loam.
Azaleas, hydrangeas and gardenias
need a soil mixture containing peat
moss or at least a goodly portion
(approximately one-third of the
humus should be supplied in that
form. Gardenias, for convenience,
I are sometimes planted in a mixture
of half peatmoss and half garden
loam
The soil mixture for cactus, in-;
eluding the Christmas cactus, sanse-1
vieria, peperonia, etc., should con-!
tain X part of clay-loam, sucn as
most of us have In pur gardens and
X part of sand. A small amount of
humus in the form of compost might
well be added and some ground
limestone (a tablespoonful to a 6
inch pot of soil) is usually needed
for these dry land plants.
For ferns, begonias and other
fibrous-rooted plants the soil mix
ture might well consist of 2 parts of
garden loam, 1 part sand and 1 part
of rich compost or well-rotted ma
nure. These plants do not as a rule
require a sweet soil and the lime
stone should be omitted.
Still another mixture, one that Is
adapted to heavy rooted plants with
large foliage, such as aspidistra, is
made from a mixture of 3 parts of
good garden loam mixed with 1 part
of well-rotted manure or rich com
post. If the plants require an acid
soil some flowers of sulphur may be
added to this mixture, while for
sweet soil a small amount of ground
limestone will reduce the acidity.
Keep the Air Moist.
A second point in meeting the
moisture requirements of our plants
is to keep them in a humid atmos
phere. Too often the air in our
homes is so dry as to be injurious to
human health. If we would only
place our potted plants on a layer
of gravel in a large container and
keep the gravel wet, the moisture in
the air about the plants would be
greatly increased. Some gardeners
using this method have successfully
grown and flowered orchids in their
homes.
Most house plants resent high
night temperatures. Perhaps that
is why geraniums and begonias
thrived in the days of the base
burner. We banked the fire in the
evening and by morning the house
was apt to be “plain cold.” As a
rule most house plants need a night
time temperature of 60 to 65 degrees.
The daytime temperature does not
seem to affect their health as much.
This implies the desirability of turn
ing the thermometer back to 60
degrees before going to bed. Only
the orchid family seems to require
a lower night-time temperature than
that.
A few plants such as the gloxinia,
gardenia, and azalea thrive best
with a daytime temperature of less
than 65 degrees. Such plants should
be kept in a separate room since
few people are comfortable at that
temperature.
Try to place the window garden
where it will be protected from
strong draughts and during the win
ter especially protect them from
wintry blasts whenever the door is
opened.
It is practically impossible to have
fixtures so perfect that no gas can
escape. Plants should be kept in
rooms at some distance from the
gas fixtures.
House plants as a rule need to be
repotted at least every other year.
When repotting, remove the top
layer of soil from the root ball.
Put the plant in a size larger pot
than they were growing in and pack
good rich soil (qf the recommended
mixture) around the ball of roots.
A small amount of soluble plant
food may be given once a month
in the water, or a plant tab may
be buried in the soil.
This Week in the Garden
Chrysanthemums are making a1
fine showing. Those without this
hardy perennial might well lookj
about and select varieties to order
next spring. Not all varieties are
winter hardy in this area, so it is
well to inquire about that point. The
ones most likely to be injured or
lost during the winter can be car
ried over in a cold frame. It is not
the cold but the moisture that does
the damage.
a a a a
Remove the tops of spent hardy
asters and other perennials that
seed too freely. A plant out of place
is a weed, and the hardy asters are
apt to fall in this class.
a a a a
Napthalen flakes can be used to
keep moles and mice away from the'
bulb beds, but mix them well" with
the soil and preferably around the
outer edges of the planting. Too
heavy an application or too close to
the bulbs may result in the volatile
gases injuring the tender new
shoots.
a a a a
Pull and place old corn stalks in
the compost pile, or spade the stalks
under.

Chickweed is getting started for
winter growth, but this can be con
trolled by spraying with 2-4D. By
the way, 2-4D does not seem to be
injurious to ro6e bushes, but, of
course, we should not get the spray
on the foliage if it can be avoided.
L. W. Kephart, weed specialist of
the Agriculture Department, re
commends the amine form. It is
less likely to cause injury to nearby
plants than the more volatile ester
form.
a a a a
Gather grasses, berries and other
plant material suitable for winter
bouquets now. Tie in bunches and
hang them head down to dry until
needed.
t a
Pile a thick layer of oak leaves
around azaleas, rhododendrons,
laurel and other broad-leaved ever
greens. If necessary, place some
brush Mi the leaves to make them
stay put.
a a a a
Buy and plant dog’s tooth violets
(Erythroniums), Wake Robin (Tril
lium grandiflorum) and other bulbs
now for the shady wild flower
garden.
a a a a
Still time to buy and sow seeds of
the hardy annuals.
a a a a
November is rose-planting time.
Order for November delivery.
a a a a
Common ryegrass sown In the
vegetable garden will provide a good
crop of green manure to turn under
next spring. It also is a satisfactory
grass for winter and if kept cut will
make a good turf for one year. It

More Farmers Adopt
Conservation Plans
During the last year, 10.033 more
farmers in the 12 Northeastern
States adopted complete farm con
servation plans, according to the
Soil Conservation Service Northeast
ern regional headquarters.
The region Includes Maryland,
West Virginia, New York, Pennsyl
vania. Maine, Delaware, Connecti
cut. Massachusetts, New Hampshire,
New Jersey, Rhode Island and Ver
mont.
In Maryland, officials announced,
plans were completed for 1,058
farms, bringing the total number of
farms planned to 6,877.
Officials said flood control opera
tions in the Potomac River Basin
were carried on in six subwatersheds
having a combined area of 82,290
acres. Four of these watersheds
were in West Virginia, one in Mary
land and one was partly in Penn
sylvania and Maryland.
Work was planned for 11 addi
tional subwatersheds, it was said,
and operations are expected to begin
this year.

By Peter Boggs
We have a new puppy in our
house—a Chesapeake Bay retriever.
I was watching him the other eve
ning doing a thing practically all
dogs do; namely, turn around two
or three times before they lie down.
Perhaps you have seen your dog do
this and have wondered about the
reason for it.
For years before they were civil
ized, dogs were creatures of the
wild, existing as best they could.
Many of their actions today are
throw-backs to that era. For ex
ample;
A dog buries a bone because his
ancestors buried food they killed,
after eating what they wanted, so
that other dogs wouldn’t find the
remains. Later, they would return
and finish it. These same ancestors
would turn around to mat down the
stiff grasses and brush for a bed
whenever they wanted to rest or
sleep. They also turned around for
another reason. They were testing
the direction of the wind, and al
ways lay so that the wind blew
directly at their noses, bringing to
their highly developed sense of
smell the odor of any approaching
enemy. Regardless of where your
dog is going to lie down, nine times
out of ten he will turn around once
or twice before doing so. This habit
has been handed down through
the ages.
It has become a rather widespread
practice to mix charcoal in a dog’s
food In the belief that by doing so
one is greatly improving the ani
mal’s health. However, this item is
of little value in any animal’s diet
unless it is heated to drive out
gases. Charcoal is not a food, but
it has its advantag# if given in
small amounts.
Queerly enough, the pads of your
dog's feet get much more wear and
tear when he Is walking on a leash
(especially over pavements) than
when he is traveling three times
the same distance unleashed and
at such pace as he may choose for
himself.
Q. My dog has rickets. How did
he develop this terrible thing?—P. S.
A. Rickets are caused by a defi
ciency of bone salts. Contributary
factors might be worms, improper
feeding, absence of exposure to sun
light, damp quarters or lack of
proper exercise.
Q. I am able to secure a lot of
tripe inexpensively. Is this good to
feed an English setter?—D. R. S.
A. Tripe, the stomach lining of
animals, is excellent to feed to any
dog. When I feed it, I usually boil
it first in milk, but it can be fed
j raw to your dog.

By James Birchfield
customs made him so homesick he
could hardly carry out his mission.
It was like that for days. But
one morning he was awakened by
a chorus of guineas that wiped out
his feeling of loneliness.
A guinea, they say, makes the
best farm “watchdog'’ there is. No
stranger can come on the place, no
matter the hour, without causing
a barnyard rumpus. And the clatter
usually keeps up until the visitor
departs or enters the house.

Pall, with its bright colors and
sharp frosts, is marking the end of
the country season. Autumn may
be a long, drawn-out affair, but to
the countryman the crop season
ends quickly and suddenly with the
first killing frost.
The fall and frost mark a new
beginning for most of us in the
country. It is a time of cleanup
before the drab, dark days of winter
not far away.
But there is another benefit from
the first frost. There is nothing
tmore assuring than to look out over
the garden the morning after the
first heavy frost and to realize that
Nature has completed for you a
job that until then seemed impos
sible.
Next year, you are sure, will be
different. But again it will be the
frost that will keep the weeds from
taking over.

We watched our calf the other
day and saw her suddenly grow up.
It was the day she was 6 months
old, and she was being turned into
the pasture with her mother.
She couldn’t understand why all
of her friendly overtures to the cow
were met with total disinterest,
backed up by a pair of horns. And
after several attempts to become a
baby again, she hung her head and
w'ent off to cry in a corner of tht
lot.
It was not long before her spirits
were up again and she was frisk
ing about as gay as a cricket. But
no longer was she interested in her
mother. In a few minutes she had
grown up. t
Carden Club Activities
Plan* for observance of National
Flower Week November 7 to 14 are
growing. Most clubs are co-operat
ing in the plan to dramatize with
floral displays the joy and beauty
flowers add to the art of living.
A chairman from each garden
club is being chosen to help in plan
ning distribution of flowers to hos
pitals, banks, schools, churches, the
aters, homes of shutins and similar
places. Fifteen of the 18 clubs in
Montgomery County, under the
leadership of Mrs. Ernest Klinge of
the Silver Spring Garden Club have
signed.
A list of area leaders was pre
viously published, to which should
be added Mrs. Roy Crampton, pres
ident of the Forest Hills Garden
Club, who is chairman for the Dis
trict. She asks each club to recom
mend important locations where
flowers should be placed.
During the week the members will'
harvest their gardens for flowers
and the commercial growers and
florists of the area, under the gen
eral chairmanship of T. J. Witt,

Pointers on Planting
Roses in November;
Soil Mixture Vital
It’» stylish, horticulturally, and
good sense, too, to plant rose bushes
in November.
Roses will thrive in fairly heavy
soil If well drained. Well-drained
soil requires no subdrainage. Be
careful not to expose the roots of a
bush to drying air.'
The following general rules apply:
Unpack plants as soon as they ar
rive from the nursery and soak the
roots in a. bucket of thick muddy
water for six to eight hours before
planting. If planting has to be
delayed for a few days, cover roots
with soil and keep covered, tops and
all, with wet burlap or straw.
Preparation of Soil.
The most important point in
planting roses is preparation of
soil to a depth of 24 inches: dig hole
large enough for roots to be spread
out with 4 to 6 inches at bottom.
Roses will grow in soil that grows
good vegetables but the ideal mix
ture for finest bloom and growth is
soil plus 15 per cent rotted manure
and 25 per cent peat moss, and a
cupful of special rose food mixed
through the soil.
Plant a rosebush so that the graft
will be at ground level of the bed.
Pack soil firmly about roots and
when hole is half filled with soil,
pour in a bucket of water, then fill
with soil. The top few inches should
