Vahart Wheat Yields
By the Associated Press
BLACKSBURG. Va., Oct. 30
Questionnaires returned by 86 Vir
ginia farmers who grew Vahart
wheat this year showed it averaged
27.8 bushels per acre in yield. In
1948 between 35,000 and 40,000
bushels of Vahart seed were pro
duced in 80 counties.
Maryland Farming Advances
Milk Production at All-Time High;
Marketing Is More Efficient
Maryland is making greater
strides in agriculture than ever be
fore.
According to the annual report
of the State Extension Service the
number of cows milked and the
number of pounds of milk produced
has reached an .all-time high; im
provements have been made in the
$200,000,000 job of marketing Mary
land farm products; 4-H Club and
other youth work in the State has
completed a banner year, and many
soil conservation problems have
been solved as 90 per cent of the
farm land in the State came under
soil conservation districts.
The report, issued by Dr. T. B.
Symons, State director of Extension
Service, points out that Maryland
farmers made many adjustments in
a return to peacetime agriculture
and faced the problem of producing
food for other countries of the world.
Changes in Cropping System.
He said changes in State cropping
systems on Maryland farms has
been greater during the last few
years than during any other period
in history.
Particularly important, the report
stated, have been adjustments in to
bacco marketing, in farm machinery
and buildings, in the production of
poultry and in the fight to control
insects.
To assist tobacco farmers to mar
ket their crops, a State Tobacco
Authority, with certain regulatory
functions, was established; a Fed
eral grading system was launched,
and a move was made to promote a
more favorable parity price for State
growers.
As Maryland farmers moved
toward greater mechanization, more
emphasis was placed on the care,
repair and maintenance of farm
equipment. A building plan service
was perfected from which fanners
receive plans for constructing or
remodeling farm structures.
Broiler Industry Aided.
The Extension Service h&s aided
the Eastern Shore broiler industry
in outlining feed-saving methods,
and has taken the lead in combat
ing the Japanese beetle threat to
State agriculture.

Cold Frames Are Versatile
Harvest Season Gan Be Prolonged;
Tender Varieties Winter There
By W. H. Youngmon
Much has been written on cold
frames and their importance to the
home gardener. Often he finds it
easier, however, to buy the plants
from a commercial grower and so
dismisses cold frames from his
gardening thoughts. In so doing he
is overlooking the other uses of cold
frames and their values.
Cold frames are useful in pro
longing the harvest season, they are
important for carrying tender, va
rieties of chrysanthemums over
winter, and bulbs for forcing may
be started in the cold frame as
easily as in a greenhouse. Other
uses will depend upon the size and
care given them.
Several local gardeners regularly
transplant from the garden small
plants of lettuce, beet, endive, cel
ery, Chinese cabbage, etc., to their
cold frames before the nights get
frosty. There they continue to reach
maturity until long after Thanks
giving time, sometimes until the
Christmas season. We may also sow
radishes, lettuce, etc., in the cold
frame where they will grow into
tender, useful crops. Spinach takes
considerable room and should not
be sown unless ample space is
available.
Standby tot Head Lettuce.
In the spring the earliest sowings
of quick-maturing crops may be
made in the cold frame where they
will mature several weeks ahead of
seed sown in the garden. If for
some reason the plants do not reach
maturity in the cold frame they
may be transplanted into the gar
den. This is a desirable practice
for early harvests of beet and let
tuce. A cold frame is the only de
pendable place in which to grow
head lettuce, which must be far
enough advanced when transplanted
to the garden to reach maturity
before hot weather. Usually this
means the seed should be sown in
the cold frame around the first of
February.
In this area most of our seedling
perennials, if large enough to be
transplanted to the border by mid
September, survive our winters with
little difficulty. Pansy plants set
out in the border by the middle of
October usually come through the
winter with only moderate losses.
But there are some plants so tender
that they seldom survive our win
ters unprotected. A cold frame is
a necessity if you wish to carry a
tender variety of chrysanthemum
over winter. Wee Dot, most of the

Here are some points to remember
about a cold frame:
It must be protected against the
inroads of field mice.
Some watering and ventilation
will be needed during the winter,
but overwatering is likely to be in
jurious.
The soil should be well drained to
give the crowns of dormant plants
the needed protection.
The cold frame should be pro
tected from cold winds, either by a
wall or by dirt banked up on all
sides.
Good for Bulb Care
It is not necessary to have a cold
frame for the forcing of early spring
flowering bulbs, but it Is a good use
to make of the one you have. Bulbs
potted In the fall need to be.stored
in a cool, moist situation where they
will become well-rooted and devel
oped so that when brought in to
heat and light they will make
sturdy growth. Without a cold
frame we must store them in a well
drained, protected situation, where
they may be kept from freezing by
a cover of leaves. Freezing does not
hurt the bulbs but it does make
bringing them indoors difficult.
Bulbs potted in late fall may not
make sufficient growth for forcing
unless stored in a cold frame in thr
heat of the sun for root growth.
Plunge the pots In the soil for
sand) so that the tops may be cov
ered with the soil. In a poorly con
structed or exposed cold frame it
may be necessary to pack the space
under the glass with leaves or ex
celsior to give the needed warmth.
However, leaves and other mulch
ing materials make a home for mice
—and mice do so enjoy feeding on
juicy tulip bulbs.
Cold frames, if deep enough and
well constructed, may be used to
house gardenia and camellia plants.
Such use, however, does require
substantial construction and regu
lar attention. Ventilators need to
be raised on warm sunny days, and
during cold weather it may be nec
essary to spread a protective cov
ering over the glass to conserve
Suggested, dimensions for a
cold frame.
ithe heat. While the gardenia and
{the camellia will withstand freezing
temperatures they cannot be ex
pected to survive cooking on bright
sunny days and then freezing as
soon as the sun goes down. Ample
supplies of moisture also mtiat be
provided for good growth.
Some Other Considerations.
Cold frames may be made in many
sizes. If old windows are used the
j frame should fit them. Commercial
sash is available in the standard
3x6.size, although a special 3x3 size
is easier to handle. The materials
can vary from old packing crates to
brick and cement. Some dismantle
and store over summer; however, if
the frame is needed for summer
seed sowing and propagating, a
permanent type of construction will
pay. Treat with a wood preserv
ative like creosote or cuprinol.
Select a site well drained, easily
accessible, protected from strong
winds and with a southern exposure
if possible.
A good soil mixture, one con
taining a generous supply of peat
moss or compost and sand, is nec
essary for good growth. This layer
of especially prepared soil need not
be more than 2 inches deep for
seedlings, but for propagating
shrubs, growing perennials, for stor
ing large perennials and for grow
ing vegetable crops, a 6-inch layer
is desirable. Covering the entire
layer with a half inch of sand to
prevent the damping-off disease is
an old practice among seed growers.
We have been discussing cold
frames, a glass covered, Inclosed
frame without heat, for the han
dling of plants in the late fall and
early spring. A little experience
with one and a taste of its values
soon converts the home gardener to
glasshouse gardening. It takes only
a few feet of electrical cable to
change the cold frame into a hot
bed with a wider range of uses over
a longer period.
A Retaining Wall
Gan Be Planted, Too
A planted retaining wall is an
excellent variation from stone or
concrete. Here are the steps rec
ommended by A. C. Pfander, super
intendent of buildings and grounds,
New York Botanical Garden:
1. A trench about 10 or 12 inches
deep and slightly wider than the
desired wall to be filled with cinders.
2. The first row of rocks to be
pitched to the bank about 1 inch
to the foot.
3. To prevent forward tumbling
during heavy frost, the wall to be
given a 2-inch lean to the bank for
every foot of height.
4. The soil used between the rocks
to be screened so that a soft medium
density Is maintained.
5. Each rock to be firmly bedded
in soil to achieve permanent steadi
ness.
6. Hie layers of soil between rocks
not to be more than inch thick.
7. The soil behind the rocks to be
well packed to avoid settling.
8. Rock garden and alpine plants
of the smaller types, such as Dian
thus sempervivum or many of the
sedums planted as seedlings or
small divisions, will eventually cover
most of the wall with large mats
and cushions of color that change
the Impression of stone to softness
and grace.

P. D. Digges (right) and his farm manager, Carl L. Pear
ton, look over the herd that helps pile up record production
figures on a 65-acre farm in Fairfax County. Mr. Digges said
his farm grossed 117,000 last year. —Star Staff Photo.
Small Farm Runs Smoothly
Digges* 65 Acres in Fairfax County
Is Well Supplied With Machinery
It isn’t the amount of land you
have, but how you use it!
At least, that is the theory of P. D.
Digges, whose 65 acres near For
estville, in Fairfax County, are
making production records.
Mr. Digges is president of the
Model Farms Dairy at 4115 Kansas
avenue N.W. A lew years ago he
got the idea that the same kind of
efficiency employed in a dairy
could be applied equally well in
making a good farm, and so it has
proved.
“It isn’t every 65-acre farm that
will gross 617,000 a year,” said Mr.
Digges. “But we are showing them
here what it takes to do just that.”
Mr. Digges is quick to tell you
that much of his success has been
due to his manager, Carl L. Pear
son, who takes the same interest
in the small farm as the owner, and
many improvements stem from his
ideas.
For example, power failures never
interfere with milking. Mr. Pear
son and Mr. Digges got together
one day and decided that the intake
manifold of the tractor would pro
vide enough suction to work the
milking machines in an emergency.
They bored and threaded a hole
in the manifold and placed a valve
in the hole. Now, when the power
goes off Mr. Pearson backs up the
tractor to the bam window, con
nects a long rubber hose to the
milking vacuum system, and goes
on with the milking.
The Digges farm supports about
24 milking cows, Holsteins and
Guernseys, and about 12 head of
heifers that are coming on. The
farm keeps a herd sire and man
ages to sell a few animals each1
year. The farm also supports a
few pigs. But the farm program
naturally centers around the milk
ing herd. This year Mr. Pearson
made 140 tons of silage on seven
and one half acres of corn and
has made more than 80 tons of
good legume hay.
“That corn was so good,” Mr.
Digges said with a note of pride,
"that people came from miles
around to see it. We measured
some of the stalks and they went
18 feet, 3 inches tall. That’s not
bad on any land.”
Although the Digges farm is be
low average size, it is completely
mechanized. The fact that he does
not have to depend on neighbors’
equipment not only makes for more
friendly relations in the community,
he thinks, but also enables him to
do farm jobs when they should be
done.
In addition to his smaller ma
chinery, Mr. Digges and four
neighbors went together to buy a
threshing machine. This season
when much grain was lost because
of bad weather their grain was
saved in good shape.
Although he has invested heavily
in machinery, Mr. Digges does not
consider It top heavy. He believes
adequate machinery is necessary
on even a small farm where pro
duction and income are high.
Like his tAy dairy. Mr. Digges
hopes that Grafton Farm, named
for his mother’s home place in
Fauquier County, will become a
model and guide to better farm
ing in his section.
Mice Poisons Tested
Mice—of the meadow and pine
species—have been doing serious
damage lately by girdling and kill
ing apple trees. But newly developed
poisons are being tested in Virginia
and West Virginia orchards this
month by an Interior Department
biologist.

Rose gardens and test gardens

The clubs are sponsoring the
planting of roses this fall, most of
them urging that orders be placed
so that the bushes will be received
by November 20. Fall-planted roses
can become well established by
spring- During the winter, when
the plants appear dormant, their
roots actually make growth when
ever the soil temperature rises a
few degrees above freezing. This
leads to larger and better blooms
than might otherwise be obtained.

Perhaps you may decide it would

the shape and form of your beds
Life on 40 Acres—Corn Crop Above Average
By James Birchfield
Now that the corn is down, we
can see the edge of the woods that
borders our back field. There is a
fringe of dogwood, deep red in the
brisk fall air and it lends color to
the landscape as we look back from
our small hilltop.
I feel distant, somehow, to the
corn. The cutting and the shocking
went by me and I must wait for
the husking to give me a sense of
kinship with our first grain crop.
Jane and Eugene Dennis managed
the cutting and there was something
of a record, I believe, made on our
forty. For in one day’s working they
managed 23 shocks. And that, with
part of another day's work, man
aged the crop.
Although it will take the husking
to tell the full story, apparently we
haven’t done 4b badly with our first
corn crop. Even with the wet sum
mer and the water that seemed to
collect in our cornfield, it seems
that we may have a fairly good,
yield.
Nothing like the corn that wasi
grown on an island in the Potomac
River, nor as good as Tweed How
ser’s across the way, but some ears
are long and fat. I think it will
beat the State average yield of
around 30 bushels to the acre.

We have had a minor tragedy—
our first. Our calf, so proud last
week after taking reserve champion
at Richmond, managed to cut a
gash at the base of one of her
small horns.
We don’t know how It could have
happened, and for a time it seemed
that she might loose her left horn.
We have kept the wound clean, and
although the horn is loose, we hope
now that she will come through
all right. Somehow, Jerseys with
out horns do not look like cows
to me.

This week has been one of haul
ing lumber and getting ready to
build a chicken house. A friend
let us have an old building that had
been torn down, and from it we
will have more than enough to
build a good, tight house.
The building job is yet to come,
and it will be interesting to see
what finally emerges from our plans.
To one who has never tried it, j
there is something formidable about
building a house—even a small one
for chickens.
If we really do it, and it stands,'

Profit From Hogs
Increased by Use
Of Better Facilities
ly tho Associated Pross
BLACKSBURG, Va., Oct. 16
Better housing and feeding facili
ties for hogs will do much toward
boosting profits of Virginia swine
breeders, Agricultural Extension
Service specialists believe. These
suggestions are made:
If size of the enterprise indicates
us^ of a central hoghouse, it should
be provided with floors that can be
kept clean and easily disinfected.
Movable houses, extensively used
because of low cost per unit and
adaptability to sanitation measures,
will aid in disease control. They
can be moved to clean ground be
for or after farrowing.
The farrowing house should have
a 50 to 60 degree temperature
range. Use of brooders will reduce
chilling of small pigs.
A farrowing pen should he pro
vided for each sow. A 6-by-8-foot
pen is the minimum for a small
sow, while 7-by-8 pens may be
used for sows under 350 pounds.
Guard rails should be provided on
at least three sides.
Hoghouses and feeding areas
should have floors built for warmth
and freedom from dampness, dust
and drafts. The surface should have
just enough slope for drainage and
ease in cleaning and disinfecting.
If self-feeders are used, there
should be a minimum of five square
feet of feeding floor per pig with
additional space for shelter.
Rat control calls for foundation
walls extending about 18 inches be
low ground.
All feed troughs should be mov
able and have smooth Inside sur
faces for ease in cleaning.

This Week in the Garden
Lily-of-the-valley makes a desir
able edging for beds of ferns and
other shade-loving plants. They will
thrive along a north wall if planted
in a soil well supplied with humus
—decayed leafmold, compost or
woods earth. A few plants, set out
at 12-inch intervals, will soon pro
vide the mass effect that is desirable.
Established plantings should be top
dressed with well-rotted manure or
rich compost.

Look over the trees and shrubs
for injured branches or bruises that
might develop into decay. Clean
them up, removing diseased woodT
paint with a tree dressing and pro
tect them until healed. Many valu
able trees are ruined by neglect of
small wounds. If tree dressings are
not available prepare your own by
mixing bordeaux powder with lin
seed oil. When this has dried suffi
ciently coat with an asbestos cement.

It is not too late to give the straw
berry bed a dressing of rich compost
or well-rotted manure. This side
dressing 1011 leach down to the
roots where they can reach it when
ever in active growth.

Lift blooming chrysanthemums
and place them in the perennial
border where they will make an
effective display until freezing
weather. Or, pot the smaller plants
and place them on the porch or in
some other sheltered situation.

Tulip planting should not be fur
ther delayed. Bulbs planted now
will give the best results next spring.
Other bulbs, except bulbous iris and
the anemones, should go into the
ground without delay. They make
considerable root growth in the fall,
and some top growth below the sur
face of the soil. If the soil is too
cold this development will not take
place.

The failure of peonies to bloom
is frequently due to planting at toe
great a depth. In our heavy soils
they should not have more than
2 inches of soil over their buds. In
planting in a well prepared hole
we should strive to cover them not
more than 1 inch, this is to allow
for settling of the soil and plants.

In the warm days that usually
follow the first killing frosts is a
good time to give the dormant
deciduous trees and shrubs an ap
plication of dormant strength lime
sulphur spray. Pall applications of
dormant sprays are not generally

The failure of lilacs to bloom may
be due to several causes: 1. The
common lilac is a notorious slow
bloomer, often not beginning to
flower until 5, 6 or 7 years after
planting. 2. Scale insects may be
taking too heavy a toll of the plant’s
strength. 3. Too much shade. 4.
Allowing too many suckers to draw
upon the plants strength—5 to 7
are sufficient. 5. Lilacs prefer a
sweet soil, but are tolerant and will
thrive and bloom in an acid soil.
Add ground limestone occasionally.
6. If cultivating be sure it is dona
shallowly. 7. The lilac borer is not
a common pest, but if present will
destroy blooming age canes.

WASHINGTON AND VICINITY
WEDNESDAY, OCTOBER 20, 1948
Apathy Afflicts
Both Parties in
Fredericksburg
Neither Side
Excited Enough for
Committee: Talks
(This is one of a series on the
political outlook in Northern Vir
ginia.
By Alex R. Preston
Star Staff Corr#ipond«nt
FREDERICKSBURG. Va., Oct. 30.
—Neither the Democrats nor the
Republicans are sufficiently excited
about the .election to call meeting*
of their committees.
In addition to pronounced apathy
within the core of each major party,
the local picture is highlighted by:
Public indorsement of Gov. Thur
mond of South Carolina, States’
Rights candidate for President, by
one of the most influential political
figures here.
A political rally—the only one
held, or planned—for Representa
tive Scott, chairman of the Republi
can National Committee, was at
tended by leading Democrats as well
as Republicans. Mr. Scott is a na
tive of Fredericksburg.
The only conclusion reached after
making the rounds among persons
who usually know about such
things is that Representative Bland.
Democrat, has little to worry about
Fredericksburg's vote. Mr. Bland
is opposed by Stanley G. Adams, Re
publican. and J. Luther Kibler, In
dependent, in the First Congres
sional District which extends from
here all the way to Newport News
and across Chesapeake Bay to the
Eastern Shore.
GOP Win Seen Likely.
From what leaders have to say.
however, it is indicated that Fred
ericksburg probably will vote Re
publican this year for the second
time in history. The city followed
most of the rest of the State in 1928
when Virginia voted for Herbert
Hoover over A1 Smith.
The last time Gov. Dewey ran for
President in 1944. however. Freder
icksburg gave him only 698 votes
against 1.092 for President Roose
velt. Mr. Bland, incidentally, got
1,166 to 371 for WalteV Johnson, his
Republican opponent, that same
year.
Milton B. Graves, insurance deal
er. who is chairman of the Demo
cratic Committee, said members "do
not think they need to be called into
a meeting.”
"The Democratic party is split,
right to the committee itself," he
said. “As a matter of fact, the split
goes further. It goes right into
some families.”
Elliott E. Brooks, real estate dealer
who is Republican Committee chair
man. said no G. O. P. rallies have
been held since the one for Repre
sentative Scott and that “none is
planned” by his committee.
“We've got things rolling pretty
much our way now," he explained.
"There's no use of stirring up things
and getting the Democrats mad at
us with a lot of talk.”
Attitude Is Common.
This attitude has been noted
among many Northern Virginia Re
publican leaders.
Mr. Brooks conceded that Mr.
Bland will be elected by the total
1st District vote but promised some
setback for him in the Fredericks
burg area. He said Senator Rob
ertson. Democrat, will have a more
difficult time getting the district
vote from Robert H. Woods, Pearis
burg. State G. O. P. chairman who
also is running for the Senate.
"Neither Mr. Bland nor Senator
Robertson have been here,” Mr.
Brooks said. "Bob Woods has been
working hard with radio talks and
public appearances here and in the
Northern Neck counties.”
Former State Senator C. O’Conor
Goolrick. a leader of the Demoratic
organization, is the avowed sup
porter of Gov. Thurmond. Mr. Gool
rick, incidentally, is to speak to
night at the Municipal Building in
Culpeper while Representative Scott
addresses a University of Virginia!
Young Republican Club rally at the
same time in Charlottesville.
Mr- Goolrick said he did not in
dorse Gov. Thurmond with the idea
of actively entering the campaign.
"A lot of my friends feel that
rather than vote for President Tru-:
man they will vote for Gov. Dewey," j
he said.
Disagrees With Poll.
He disagreed with a recent na
tional political poll which indicated
Gov. Thurmond would receive only
8 per cent of Virginia's vote. Mr.
Goolrick estimated Gov. Thurmond
will get about 15 per cent of the
State and local vote.
Mr. Goolrick said he felt all pub
lic officials “of importance" should
make their position known in the
presidential race. He would not
mention which public officials he
had in mind but Gov. Tuck and j
Senator Byrd are among the last to
refrain from committing themselves.
Mr. Goolrick agreed that some
significance could be attached to
Gov. Tuck’s introduction of Gov.
Thurmond at a recent Richmond
rally and also to the presence of
Mrs. Byrd, wife of tht Senator, at
a Thurmond banquet in Richmond.
Effort to Reorganize Party.
‘The Thurmond movement,’’ he
said, “is not an attempt to create
a new party. It is an effort to re
organize the Democratic Party. The
Democrats of the South have got
to show' they they can not be treated
with the utmost contempt. They
deserve to have their place in the
*un.”
The Negro vote is not considered a
decisive factor in the 1st District by
either major party. Gloucester
County in the district is one of two
whic recently figured in Negro’s at
tempt to obtain equal school facili
ties through court action. The other,
King George County—which is
closer—is in the 8th District.
The daily newspaper here, pub
lished by Mayor Josiah Rowe, has
not affirimatively indorsed any can
didate although it often has been
critical of the Truman administra
tion. The municipal elections are
conducted on a nonpartisan basis
and about 1,800 voters participated
in the last one in June. This set a
record although about 2,500 are
qualified.
Mr. Rowe said he believes few
Fredericksburg Democrats will vote
for President Truman, without, how
ever, tipping his own hand on how
be stands.
A BEAR WITH SPECTACLES—The spectacled bear is the way
zoologists list this new bruin, acquired yesterday by the Zoo.
The name is taken from the white rings around the bear’s eyes.
This species is native to the Andes region of South America and
is the only bear found in the southern half of the Western Hemi
sphere, according to Dr. William M. Mann, Zoo director. The
newcomer, an 18-month-old female, is a gift of the San Diego
(Calif.) Zoo. —Star Staff Photo.
Falls Church Council
To Meet Friday to Act
On Proposed Budget
The Falls Church Council will
meet Friday night to act on a pro
posed budget for the current fiscal
year calling for an increased tax
levy of 30 cents per $100 valuation.
The Council will decide at that
time whether to approve for public
hearing the $265,485.87 outlay for
operations and capital improvement
for the period of September 1. 1948.
to June 30. 1949. Future fiscal years,
in accordance with recommendations
by the State, will run from July 1 to
June 30.
Expenses for the 12-month fiscal
year 1947-8 were $215,626.16. Last
year's tax rate was $1.50.
County Tax Is $3.05.
Falls Church property owners this
year will pay a county tax of $3.05
in addition to the Falls Church levy.
The proposed budget is the first to
be considered since Falls Church
became a second-class city August
16. It calls for salary increases of
approximately 10 per cent, according
to Councilman Samuel J. Dennis,
acting chairman of the Budget Com
mittee. Also in the budget are pro
visions for employment of a city
engineer, a sanitary engineer, a
planning engineer and two addi
tional policemen.
The proposed salary for City At
torney La Rue Van Meter, who also
is commissioner of revenue, is $6,500.
One-third of the commissioner of
revenue's salary is paid by the State.
Annexation Studies Cost $14,000.
Annexation studies are allotted
$14,000 in the proposed budget—
$2,500 for a preliminary survey and
$11,500 for a "final study’’ if the
preliminary study warrants further
action.
Capital improvements in the 1948
49 budget are estimated at $45.
666.25, a decrease of approximately
$5,000 from 1947-48. Sidewalks are
planned for South Washington street
and South West street. Streets to
be improved include Oak street,
Spring street, Park avenue and No
land street.
An additional auto for the police
department and a traffic light for
the intersection of Lee highway and
Hillwood avenue are included in the
proposed budget.
Montgomery G. 0. P.
Hears Blast at Truman

Representative Fellows, Repub
lican, of Maine, told a Montgomery
County Republican rally last night
that President Truman had failed
to co-operate in purging Com
munists and fellow travelers from
the Government.
He appealed to 500 persons in the
Silver Spring armory to help “clean
house" with their votes November 2.
Communist infiltration in the
Government departments also was
criticzed by D. John Markey. chair
man of the Veterans' Division of
the Maryland Republican State
Central Committee.
Representative Beall. Republican,
a candidate for re-election from the
6th Maryland district, reviewed the
record of the Eightieth Congress.
The rally was opened by State!
Senator Rov Tasco Davis. Brooke
Johns, president of the Board of
County Commissioners,1 was mas-!
ter of ceremonies.
Among those who spoke were:
Wal*er Dawson, chairman of the
Republican State Central Commit
tee for Montgomery County, and
Galen L. Tait of Bethesda, former
chairman of the Maryland Repub
lican State Central Committee. The
rally was sponsored by the Dewey
Warren-Beall Club of Montgomery
j County.

'Reasonable Time' Set
By County to Move
Home Blocking Traffic
The Montgomery County Com
missioners yesterday gave Thomas
W. Perry. Bethesda fuel dealer, a
reasonable time" to move his house
out of a Bethesda street where it is
blocking traffic.
The action reversed a stand taken
last week when the board gave Mr.
Perry until 8 a.m. last Saturday to
move the house, which had bogged
down in the center of the street
while being shifted the three blocks
from Wisconsin avenue to Cordell
avenue.
Commissioner Wesley T. Sauter
said yesterday the one-and-a-half
j story structure, which has been in
i transit about three weeks, is in the
7200 block of Arlington avenue and
is "inching along."
j The Commissioners also directed
that a letter be written to Jack
Eaton, Bethesda area building in
spector. requesting him not to
grant permits to move houses on
county streets "over which he has
no jurisdiction.”
The board issued a permit to the
J. Enos Ray kindergarten to hold
classes in the Takoma Park muni
cipal building. J. Ernest Hawkins,
board clerk, said the application has
oeen approved by R. p. Frye, the
’town building inspector. M. E. Me-’
Bride, Takoma Park fire chief, and
Dr. L. R. Burnett, town health of-:
fleer. j
No opposition was voiced at the
hearing, which was attended by
about 20 mothers.
The Commissioners had granted a
permit to the kindergarten about
a month ago, but rescinded it last
'week after Mrs. Alma Opal. Ta
koma Park, complained the board
had failed to hold a hearing.
The following traffic regulations
were approved:
fin parking on the east side of
iWest avenue from Bradley lane to
Ridge street, Chevy Chase, and on
the east side of Maple Ridge road
from Old Georgetown road to Park
lane. Bethesda. One hour parking
on the north side of Miller avenue
to a point 214 feet west of Wiscon
sin avenue. i
The boaid also ordered stop signs
erected at Glendale road and East
Leland street, Chevy Chase.
The resignation of Policeman
John L. Jones, Bethesda, was ac
cepted. Mr. Jones said he was re
signing because of ill health.

Traffic Death Charge
Dropped at Trial
Manslaughter and reckless driving
charges against a Colmar Manor
'Md man were dropped on request
of the State yesterday, in the midst
of a trial before a 12-man jury of
Prince Georges County Circuit
Court.
A motion to nolle prosse the
charges against James E. Boehman,
37, former taxicab owner, was made
by Assistant State’s Attorney Her
vey G. Machen after the trial had
lasted three hours.
Judge Charles C. Marbury. who
i was presiding at Upper Marlboro,
j expressed gratification "at the spirit
!of fairness” shown by Mr. Machen.
1 The case was up on appeal from
j Police Court. Mr. Boehman had
been fined $500 for manslaughter
and $100 for reckless driving in con
i nection with an accident which re
sulted in the death last March of
Joseph Blue, 23, of 26 Channing
street N.W.
The victim and Mr. Boehman
were riding in the defendant’s taxi
cab on Route 5, near Clinton, Md.,
when it ran over a rut and struck
two poles. The defense contended
Mr. Blue was driving.

The way has been cleared for

land Fair, which opened Monday

missioners yesterday transferred

partment.

temal or charitable groups. Bingo
games at the fair are being held in

I Meanwhile, entries in virtuallj
every department, except Shorthorn,
and Hereford cattle, were to be

The only Judging tomorrow will

horn cattle classes. On Friday,

cattle will be judged, while the

row also is Federated Woman's Club

j present.
Heavy Penalties
Urged in Sale of
Liquor to Youths
Prince Georges Jury
Also Warns County
On School Spending
The Prince Georges County grand
jury has urged a crackdown on
sales of alcoholic beverages to
minors. It also has recommended
caution in spending for school con
struction.
In its report, submitted yesterday,
the jury asserted it felt that the
“consumption of alcoholic bev
erages by minors is one of society's
most serious problems, frequently
resulting in juvenile delinquency
and crime.
"Sales of such beverages to minors
should not be tolerated,” the jury
declared.
As an enforcement measure, the
'jury “urgently recommended” that
violators of this regulation, “when
apprehended, be vigorously prose-1
cuted and severely dealt with, and
that, after conviction, the licenses
involved be either suspended for
substantial lengths of time or, in
aggravated and proper cases, be
permanently revoked.”
Four Taverns Cited.
Four county taverns, cited in five
separate charges during the past
few weeks, still have cases pend
ing before the court foi alleged
violations of this law. One of
these, the Club Hillbilly. Seat
Pleasant, was named twice in 48
hours by Roy D. Bright, county i
parole and probation officer.
On the school money question
the jury asserted:
"There appears to be somewhat of
a public demand for more and
better public schools. Although it
seems to appear that the public
schools of the county may be some
what crowded, it is the feeling of
this body that the present is no
time for the expenditure of any
public funds other than what is
absolutely necessary for the main
tenance of reasonably good county
government, and that any and all
spending which is not absolutely
essential should be deferred for the
time being.”
The jury recommended, however,
that "adequate school facilities be
i provided as soon as conditions war-'
rant, and such facilities can be
provided without increased taxa
tion.”
Jail Inspected.
School Supt. G. Gardner Shu
gart, when informed of the report,
jsaid most of the current school
construction requests are ••essen
tial.”
The jury noted that general con
ditions of the county “appear to
be very satisfactory.” The members
inspected the jail and the county
courthouse at Upper Marlboro, the
almshouse at Ritchie and the County
Service Building. Hyattsville.
The Police Department was com
mended in the report, the jury say
ing that it "appears to be doing!
an efficient and satisfactory job
of law enforcement, with possibly a
somewhat limited personnel."
In Its 12 day* of duty the jury
Investigated 176 cases involving al
leged violations or criminal laws, the
report said. It returned 137 indict
ments. refused to Indict in 27 cases
and continued 12 for further lnves-t
ligation.
Judge Thank* Jury.
The report was signed by Joseph
S. Haas of Mount Rainier, foreman,
and Mrs. Mary White of Colmar
Manor, secretary. Judge Charles C.
Marbury thanked the 23 jurors for
their service and then dismissed
them.
I Among Indictments returned yes
terday were those against two men
already lodged in the Hyattsville j
Jail for assaulting women. They
are Robert Lee McKinney. 28. of the
3600 block of Otis street, Mount
Rainier, indicted for rape and lesser,
counts in the assault of a 54-year
old Riverdale woman October 6, and
Richard P. Carrunchio. 26. ef Jersey
City, N. J., Indicted for assault with
intent to rape a 18-year-old Laurel
girl October 8.
Arraignments will be made at 1
pm. Friday.

nal point. still must say where

merce Commission order to increase

tween Washington and Virginia
points.

Lofstrand Opposes
Bond Proposal for
Silver Spring Hospital
Opposition to a proposed $1,000,000
bbnd issue for construction of a
200-bed hospital in Silver Spring
was voiced yesterday by Anders R.
Lofstrand, jr„ chairman of the
Montgomery County delegation to
the General Assembly.
The proposal was in a resolution
adopted last week by the construe
tiQn board of trustees of the Silver
Spring Memorial Hospital Associa
tion. Inc.
It called for presentation of a
bond issue bill at the 1949 session of
the General Assembly. The issue
would be amortized by a tax not to
exceed 10 cents for a $100 assessed
valuation on property in the 13th
Election District.
Favors Hospital for Area.
While emphasizing he is in favor
of a hospital in the Silver Spring
area. Mr. Lofstrand said:
"I oppose the $1,000,000 bond issue
because, under present costs, plans
for erection of this building are in
excess of $4,000,000. Therefore, the
$1,000,000 issue would be inadequate,
even taking Federal aid into con
sideration.”
He claimed sufficient- funds for
the project would entail a tax in
excess of 20 cents per $100 valuation
to amortize the debt. County resi
dents already are paying their full
share of taxes and "we should seek
means of reducing taxes, not in
creasing them,” Mr. Lofstrand
declared.
Calls Proposal Inefficient.
He also contended a 200-bed hos
pital in Silver Spring would be ‘'in
efficient." adding that the county
"should have a hospital centrally
located and large enough to take in
Silver Spring, Bethesda and Rock
ville, such as was proposed some
time ago.”
The tax to pay for a bond issue
large enough to finance such a
project, Mr. Lofstrand said, should
be levied on all property in the
county and not on one section. He
said he would “not oppose” a refer
endum on such a proposal on a
county-wide basis.
The hospital association now owns
a 27-acre tract on Forest Glen road
between Georgia avenue and Sligo
Creek parkway and has received
$200,000 in pledges for a building
Changes Made in Bill.
At the same time, Mr. Lofstrand
said changes have been made in a
proposed bill providing for an elected
board of education. Board members
now are appointed by the Governor.
One of the changes provides for
a special election in April, 1951, if
the proposal is approved in a refer
endum in November, 1950. The bill
originally called for a special elec
tion in December. 1950.
In addition, it was decided to
eliminate a provision for the board
to include an "upper county” resi
dent. Mr. Lofstrand said the dele
gation feared this might result in
the establishment of school districts.

Medical Bureai
For Doctors at 2
By George Beveridge
Have you ever been unable to
locate the family doctor—or any
doctor—when you needed him most?
Ten years ago, there probably was
good reason for spending frantic
moments thumbing through the
telephone directory to find a doctor.
If you go through such wasted mo
tion now—whether it’s at 2 p.m. or
2 a.m.— you probably haven’t heard
of the Medical Bureau of the Dis
trict Medical Society.
The bureau has two main pur
poses. One is to handle calls for
doctors. The other is to reach your
doctor for you or, if he isn't avail
able, to get another one—fast.
The bureau's busiest times, when
doctors are least available, are be
tween 6 and 9 p.m. daily and on
Thursday afternoons, according to
a recent bureau survey. Doctors,
like other persons, have to take
some time off from the job. And
many of them choose Thursday
afternoons.
350 Doctors Listed.
The bureau is set up to take over
any time the doctor can't be reached
at his office or home. It’s business
is carried on at a three-operator
switchboard at its headquarters,
1718 M street N.W., where more than
1.000 calls a day are handled
through the number—Republic 6100.
About 350 of the District's 1,300
practicing doctors are listed with
the bureau and the switchboard
takes calls on direct lines from the
offices and homes of about 260
others. If these 600 doctors can
be reached, the bureau operators
know where. If they can't, the
bureau has a list of alternate phys
icians who are available,
j Most dramatic of the bureau’s
services is its "emergency” list from
which the operators must get a doc
I tor on the job within 10 minutes
of the time an emergency call comes
j in. according to J. Richard Connelly,
who directs the bureau's activities.
100 on Emergency List.
More than 100 doctors, listed ac
I cording to the areas in which they
live, are on the emergency list for
1 night calls alone.
Often, though, the operators are
j called on for more than merely to
locate a dQctor. Bureau officials cibe
i the call received by Mrs. Helen
Workman, 412 Crittenden street
N.W., as a good example.
Several weeks ago. a woman called
to report that she had taken an
overdose of sleeping pills. While
calling the police rescue squad on 1
another line, Mrs. Workman said
she kept up a running conversation
I with the woman because she knew j
it was important in such cases for
the victim to remain conscious.
"I talked to her for more than 10
minutes about everything I could
think of.” the operator said. “Every
once in a while the telephone would
slip out of her hand and she collap
sed at just about the time the police
,%ot there.”
Doctors Recommended.
The woman was rushed to Gal-'
linger Hospital, where she was re
ported in good condition the next
day, Mrs. Workman said.
The bureau operators stress that
no treatment is available at its
headquarters. They' also determine
whether patients are able to pay for
private care. Those who are not
able are referred to the .Health De
partment or Hospital Service
Agency. The bureau was begun in
1940, with 100 doctors, and is still
growing.
i Handles Calls
P.M. or2 A.M.
Another service has been set up
for people who call in to ask that
a doctor be recommended. Often.
iMr. Connelly said, the request is for
"the best doctor in town.”
j To meet this rather delicate prob
lem, the bureau has classified all
|the society’s members according to
'specialties or general practice.
About one-third of the doctors are
in the latter group.
Acts as Information Service.
The operators suggest names of
three doctors—never one—from the
classified lists and let the callers
make their own choices.
The bureau also acts as an in
formation service to provide general
data on medical facilities here to
about 75 callers every day.
Like every switchboard, the
bureau’s gets its share of unusual
calls such as one from a woman
who wanted the name of the doctor
"who drives a black Cadillac” and
another from a woman who wanted i
.the doctor to "treat my little dog.”
This call was referred to the Animal
Rescue League.

Draftees' and Veterans' Guide
i 'By Maj. Thomas M. Niol
If you’re wondering about farm I
deferments and whether you’ll get
one, you’ve got company.
A short quote from a letter I got
makes a good starting point: "I find
that farm deferments will be made
only on short-term emergency basis,
however justifiable the case may be,”
writes a Jackson, Mich., correspon
dent. “Any comments?”
"You find exactly right” would be
the first comment I could offer. Be
cause that's the whole story on farm
deferments—they will be made on a
short-term basis, no matter what the
case may be.
How1 short? One year, to be exact.
After that, the local board reclassi
fies the registrant. If conditions
havn't altered, it may, and probably
I will reclassify him right back into
Class II-C—deferred for agricultural
activities.
Here is a paraphrase of the selec
tive service regulation on farm de
ferments:
Any registrant employed in the
production for market of a substan
tial quantity of agricultural products
necessary to the maintenance of
the national health, safety or inter-1
est will be put in Class II-C if:
1. The registrant really is engaged;
in that activity, or would be but for
seasonal interruptions.
2. He cannot be replaced.
3. Drafting him wouldv cause a
material loss of effectiveness in his:
particular fanning operation.
So, right away you want to knowj
gnawers to these questions: I
1. What is a “substantial quantity"?
2. How does one define "necessary
to the maintenance of the national
health, safety or interest”?
3. What is a “material loss of
effectiveness"?
Right away I have to say that
these questions do not have answers
today. They may never have answers
which follow any kind of pattern.
For instance, a substantial quan
tity in one part of the country might
be different than in another. Also,:
a substantial quantity of one agri
cultural product might be different
than a substantial quantity of
another.
So, who decides these things?
Your local board. M they think you
may rate a farm deferment, or if
you ask for one, they will look at
the facts in your case and answer
the questions as they see fit.
I think there will be a good many
farm deferments, because there are
a lot of draft-age people working
on larms. The exact age number
is not known, but a reliable estimate
of the number of single, nonvet
eran, draft-age farm workers is
250,000.
There's little doubt but that many
of these 250,000 will gain draft defer
ments.
So now you know all the “ifs”
about agricultural deferments. To
get the facts you'll have to wait and;
see what your draft board does, and
watch for new regulations.
(From UP) Newdnturei.)
Two More Out
In Alexandria
Health Shakeup
Colasanto Discharges
Man 17 Years on Job;
Kieth Wade Resigns
One Alexandria health official re
signed yesterday and another was
discharged hi a "personnel shift”
which was forecast at a meeting of
the Health Board Monday night.
City Manager Nicholas A. Cola
santo said he discharged Roland M.
Orrison, a sanitarian. Who was in
charge of the pound and scavenger
service. Mr. Orrison had been with
the city 17 years.
Sanitary Engineer Keith Wade,
head of the health department
sanitation division, submitted his
resignation yesterday afternoon to
Mary Karne, acting health officer.
Mr. Wade, a health department em
ploye who ranked second to the
health officer, said he had planned
to resign for some time.
Not Prompted by Survey.
He said his resignation was not
I prompted by the recent survey by
State health officials, which was
sharply critical of the workings of
the Health Department sanitation
division.
Just before publication of the
health survey report, City Health
Officer W. Lewis Schafer, jr:, re
signed. Dr. Schafer also denied that
his resignation was prompted by
the health report.
It was disclosed that Mr. Orrison
first was given an opportunity to
resign, and when he failed to do
so he was discharged.
Mr. Colasanto said, "We are en
deavoring to improve the depart
ment and to get the best possible
1 results from our employes.”
Dismissed* After Report.
He said Mr. Orrison was dismissed
as a result of the State report.
The city manager had indicated
at the meeting of the Health Board
Monday that he anticipated several
changes in personnel in the Health
Department.
Meanwhile, only one doctor has
been interviewed for the position of
health officer. October 29 has been
advertised in medical journals as the
deadline for receiving applications
for the job. Miss Karne, whose
regular position is supervisor of
nurses, was praised in the State
report.

Two Men Held in Theft
Of Bus in Alexandria
Alexandria police today were
holding two men for investigation
in connection with the theft of a
sightseeing bus from the 1700 block
of Price street, Alexandria.
Police said the men have been
identified by two Arlington officer*
as the pair seen by the latter In
the bus as it was being driven on
Arlington Ridge road near Glebe
road, about 15 minutes after it was
stolen yesterday.
The men were not arrested at
that time, however, because a look
out had not yet been broadcast for
the vehicle, owned by the Monu
mental Motor Tour Co., Baltimore,
police explained.
According to Alexandria polio®,
the bus had been parked in front
of the home of the driver, Thoma®
P. Jones.
They said a boy playing nearby
saw two men park their yellow con
vertible tar about 100 feet away,
walk back to the bus, climb through
an open window and drive away.
About four hours later, police said.
Pvt. Robert Peacher received a call
at police headquarters from a man
seeking the whereabouts of a yellow
convertible.
Pvt. Peacher asked the man t«
come to the police station and iden
tify the car. When the man and a
companion arrived at the station,
they were placed under arrest.
The bus was found early today ia
the 100 block of West Alexandria
avenue, about five blocks from w’hera
it was taken.
Motorist Awarded $2,500
In Suit Against Alexandria
R. L. Steinberger, 1200 Janney’s
lane. Alexandria, yesterday won a
$2,500 damage judgment against the
city of Alexandria for injuries he
said he suffered last spring in an
automobile accident caused improp
erly maintained streets.
Mr. Steinberger. a Government
physicist, who sued for $10,000 dam
ages, won the $2,500 verdict in Alex
andria Circuit Court.
He told the jury he was a pas
senger in a car driven by his wife
when it struck a hole in the 400
block of Quaker lane. The car went
out of control, he said, struck sev
eral other large holes and lurched
into a telegraph pole.
As a result of the accident, he
said, he suffered permanent in
juries.
