C—4 SUNDAY, October 10,~1948
A Good Day's Work
It seems to The Star that the men who
supplied their skilled craftsmanship and
their own labor, and the employing con
cerns which supplied the materials for
renovation of Barney Neighborhood House
have done something very fine, for which
they merit the thanks of the community.
Their accomplishment was in itself
dramatic. In a single day they have trans
formed the old Barney house, left it clean
and shining with bright paint and restored
woodwork. But the symbolism of their
accomplishment is even more valuable^
They gave an example of teamwork in
a community, every man giving his best
for a day to help put a neighbor on his
feet. They did something that is in the
tradition of the old days in this country,
when men put more emphasis on their
responsibilities as cilizens than on their
rights, knowing that the two are insepar
able and one cannot survive without the
other.
Their neighbors, in this case, were the
families—and especially the children—of
the area surrounding 470 N street S.W.,
a neighborhood for which Barney House
for many years has stood as a sort of
second home. But in a larger sense they
were working for all their neighbors in
Washington who need the willing help of
those whose good fortune enables them
to provide it.
The demonstration at Barney House was
a joint undertaking of the American
Federation of Labor’s craftsmen in the
Washington Building Trades Council and
the contractors belonging to the Wash
ington Building Congress. The union
organized the teams of artisans; the con
tractors provided the materials. They
pooled their efforts in a common cause,
cheerfully, with great good nature and
quick results. If the same spirit could
be duplicated throughout the city, what
a push-over the Community Chest drive
would be! If every citizen gave the fruits
of one day’s labor to his neighbors, as the
team at Barney House did, there would
be no “problem” in meeting chest deficits.
For there would be no deficits.
There are signs that the same idea is
apreading elsewhere. Already the firm
and all the employes of William Baker,
Inc., have decided to devote a whole day’s
labor and the profits thereof to the chest
campaign. They doubtless will be joined
by others. For there is untold satisfaction
to all who labor in such a cause. And
should the spirit of such examples spread
throughout the community »the end of the
Red Feather campaign would leave be
hind it a better city and happier citizens.
Implementing Trizonia*
At the London Conference of the three
Western Powers it was decided, against
strong Russian protests, to combine their
respective occupation zones of Germany
for administrative and economic purposes.
This involves two distinct problems. The
first of these concerns the native German
regime to be set up under Allied control.
The second, which has hitherto attracted
little public attention, is the character of
the over-all military government which
will follow consolidation of West Germany
into a combined area informally known as
“Trizonia.”
The practical importance of this latter
phase is greater than might at first sight
appear. The zonal division of Germany
at the close of the late war entailed the
setting up of separate military govern
ments by each of the occupying powers.
And these occupational regimes had prac
tically a free hand in their respective ad
ministrative methods. The result has been
wide divergencies, alike in theory and in
practice, saddling Germany with costly
foreign administrations, to say nothing of
the numbing effects of those elaborate
alien bureaucracies upon German eco
nomic recovery.
It is with these unfortunate conse
quences in mind that our Government has
proposed a plan for revamping the whole
system on an efficiency basis. Our plan
is to make the proposed West German
military government tripartite from top
to bottom while drastically reducing its
size and scope. It would abolish the Amer
ican, British and French Zones for all but
garrison purposes, and confine Allied ex
ecutive intervention in German affairs to
the central military administration.
The practical benefits from such an ar
rangement are obvious. Drastic staff
reductions would give considerable relief
to Allied taxpayers, especially ours, who,
directly or indirectly, are footing most of
the bill for occupational costs. West Ger
man economy would likewise benefit nota
bly from the cutting of alien bureaucratic
red tape. Lastly, West German public
opinion would be deeply gratified by being
vouchsafed most of the things it hopes for
from the, “occupation statute” which will
define the division dl powers between the
future West German government and the
occupation authorities.
Unfortunately, the American plan is
meeting with strenuous objections from
both the French and the British, neither
of whom want to give up their zonal pow
ers and pe«uisites. And there can be
little doubt that one reason is reluctance
to lose so many lucrative Jobs. With the
French, there is in addition a political
motive. French policy opposes the crea
tion of anything like a reunified Germany,
and desires if possible to retain special
control over those portions of Germany
which she now occupies. The indications
are, therefore that, while Britain may be
amenable to American proposals, stubborn
French opposition can be anticipated.
All this shows that the Implementation
of “Trizonia” may not be easy, despite the
Allied agreement in principle arrived at
some months ago. It is to be hoped that an
efficient program can be elaborated. Our
taxpayers are certainly concerned in the
outcome.
The Vinson Episode
The statements which have been made
by President Truman and Secretary Mar
shall tend to discredit published reports
that Mr. Truman was considering sending
Chief Justice Vinson to Moscow to seek
a settlement of the Berlin problem.
Neither the President nor Secretary Mar
shall say that the Berlin dispute was spe
cifically excluded from the matters which
Mr. Truman thought the Chief Justice
might discuss with the Russians. But they
are in agreement that the primary pur
pose of the Vinson trip, if it had been
made, would have been to discuss the atti
tude of the Soviet government on atomic
energy control.
Whatever the precise facts as to this may
have been, there is ground for gratifica
tion that the President consulted General
Marshall before going ahead with plans
for a public announcement of the planned
Vinson trip.
The men who are directly responsible
for negdtiations with the Russians,
whether those negotiations relate to
atomic energy control, the Berlin crisis, or
world peace in general, have spent weeks
and months and years trying to find some
basis for agreement with the Soviet gov
ernment. They have failed, and there Is
no reason whatever to suppose that the
Chief Justice, whose place after all is on
the Supreme Court, would have enjoyed
any greater success.
Both the Berlin situation and the atomic
dispute are now before the U. N. They
have been taken there after failure of
the most exhaustive efforts, in conjunction
with the other Western powers, to find
some basis for an agreed settlement with
the Russians. For this Government to
embark now on any unilateral course of
action, especially in the midst of an elec
tion campaign, could only serve to under
mine the United Nations and destroy
whatever slim chance there may be of
achieving constructive results from the
appeal to the U. N. to which we have
committed ourselves.
As a Nation we can be glad that Mr.
Truman consulted his Secretary of State
before going through with this ill-con
ceived project, and that he accepted Mr.
Marshall’s recommendation that it be
abandoned. Now that we have taken our
case to the U. N., we had better let that
agency handle It without benefit of any
new “missions to Moscow.’*
Dr. Bush's Resignation
The impact of the resignation of Dr.
Vannevar Bush as chairman of the Na
tional Military Establishment’s Research
and Development'Board is eased not only
by the selection of Dr. Karl T. Compton
as his successor but by the assurance that
Dr. Bush will continue to work closely with
the Government in the field of science.
Fortunately, both Dr. Bush and Dr. Comp
ton were wartime assbciates in the atomic
bomb project, in radar research and
in other scientific studies which helped
shorten the war and save lives. Dr. Comp
ton is eminently qualified to take over
leadership of postwar military research—
a program which largely is a continuation
of studies begun during the war.
Dr. Bush told the President that he
favors rotating the civilian chairmanship
of the Research and Development Board
in order to afford room for "new points of
view.’’ Furthermore, he said in his letter
of resignation, he believes it is a good
idea to bring military members of the
board into contact with a wider group of
civilian scientists. The plan is sound, for
it precludes "one-track’’ thinking by a
group on whose broad vision may depend
military decisions regarding new weapons
and new techniques of transcendental im
portance to the Nation. There was broad
thinking in this field during the war,
thanks in major degree to the influence of
such men as Drs. Bush and Compton.
Through this bold approach came the
remarkable progress which our men of
science made in the realms of nuclear
fission, electronics and the like. Smaller
minds could never have achieved what the
wartime Office of Scientific Research and
Development achieved in so little time.
It is reassuring, therefore, to know that
Dr. Bush’s advisory services will c«ntlnue
to be at the disposal of the Government,
even while he applies himself actively to
his job as head of the Carnegie Institute
here. And it is good to know that his
successor is a similarly distinguished
scientist, thoroughly familiar with the
challenging problems which confront the
Research and Development Board.
Flare-Up in Peru
Although the Callao revolt has been
quickly and thoroughly suppressed, It can
not be dismissed as a negligible episode.
The rising was apparently well planned and
included most of the navy, plus organized
civilians, who temporarily gained control
of Peru’s chief port city that lies only a
few miles from Lima, the capital. The
rebels fought stubbornly and were over
come only by the superior weight of the
army, which remained loyal to the gov
ernment. The tenacity of both sides, which
caused heavy casualties, Implies sharp
political differences within the country.
Indeed, the Callao rising had a predecessor
less than three months ago, when a local
revolt in a southern provincial city was
nipped in the bud by energetic action of
the authorities.
Under these circumstances, President
Bustamente seems to have accurately
described the current situation as one of
“acute general crisis.” The tension in
Peru’s national life revolves around the an
tagonism between the so-called Apra Party
and its political opponents. The moving.
Spirit In Apra Is Hava de la Torn, a per
sonality internationally known for his tal
ented writings as well as for his ideas.
Apra’s program is a peculiar sort of so
cialism, inspired in part from the civiliza
tion of the Incas, which was a functional
collectivism well suited to the Indian
temperament. Among other radical re
forms, Apra advocates subdivision of large
landed properties. However, it has no
connection with international communism,
to which it is specifically opposed.
Apra’s domestic radicalism naturally an
tagonizes the conservative and moderate
elements, while its opposition to com
munism gains it the hostility of the local
Reds. Nevertheless, Apra was winning
such popular support that, in the general
elections of 1945, it took an anti-Apra
coalition to put into the presidential chair
the present incumbent, Dr. Jose Luis
Bustamente, for a six-year term.
It is this government, conservative in
character, that has been blocking the
efforts of the Apra opposition. The Presi
dent categorically charges that these re
volts were inspired by Apra in order to
gain by force the power that it had been
denied at the polls. Large-scale arrests
of Apristas are taking place and a thor
ough judicial investigation has been an
nounced. It remains to be seen just where
the responsibility for the trouhle will be
placed.
An Improved B-36
£atest news from the Strategic Air Com
mand is reassuring to those who have been
concerned over reported1 failure of the
much-touted B-36 bomber to live up to
expectations of its Air Force sponsors.
There have been published rumors that the
huge plane, which dwarfs even the great
B-29, had proved to be so slow that it
would be a “sitting duck” in combat, that
it was so unwieldy that it could operate
only from special airfields and that it had
other “bugs” that affected its value as a
bomber. In fact, there have been reports
that the B-36 would be converted into an
aerial “tanker,” to refuel B-29s and B-50s.
To convince the critics that their lears
are unfounded, the Air Force staged a
special demonstration by the Eighth Air
Force at Fort Worth, Texas, last week.
Aviation reporters found a greatly im
proved B-36 on display. And they heard
enthusiastic praise of the plane by General
George Kenney, soon to relinquish com
mand of the Strategic Air Command to
become head of the Air Force Air Univer
sity, Mid from other superfortress experts.
The officers made it plain that if they
formerly had any idea of using the B-36
as a tanker instead of a bomber, they have
no such thoughts today.
The apparent change in attitude toward
the great ship arises as the result of a
number of noteworthy revisions made in
the pKfne. Its speed has been stepped up,
through installation of improved power
plants, so that its speed is now rated as
well above three hundred miles an hour in
normal flight and above three hundred and
fifty at its planned bombing altitude of
40,000 feet. The modified power plants
also have increased the plane’s effective
bombing range, with prospects that fur
ther modifications within a year will enable
it to carry ten thousand tons of bombs
more than twelve thousand miles. Since
the Eighth Air Force is being trained for
atomic warfare, it is assumed that the
B-36 could deliver an atomic bomb great
distances via the polar regions.
Another of the B-36’s limitations has
been corrected by the extension and
strengthening of its landing gear. This
change, it Is said, will enable the B-36
to take off and land on any field now
available for B-29 operations. Before this
alteration was made the craft could be
handled on only four fields. We have B-29
fields in Great Britain, Germany and
Japan, in addition to those in this country
and Alaska. There are fields in other parts
of the world large enough to accommodate
the Improved B-36.
This encourages belief, therefore, that
the eighteen B-36s now in operation and
the others coming off the production line
will materially strengthen our aerial
striking forces at a time when air power
is of vital importance.
Communism in Schools
It is hard to see how the Baltimore
School Board could have done otherwise
than approve the dismissal from the teach
ing force of Mrs. Regina Frankfeld, an
avowed Communist.
The complaint against Mrs. Frankfeld
rested, not on any ground of incompetency
or any showing that she had tried to im
plant Communist doctrine in the minds
of her pupils, but solely on the proposition
that an avojved and practicing Communist
has no place in the public school system.
The correctness of this view was chal
lenged on various grounds, but there is
no doubt that most people will agree with
the position taken by the school board.
Oile of the board members read what
he said was an excerpt from an article in
the publication, the Communist, entitled:
“The Schools and the People s Front.” This
excerpt read: “The party must take careful
steps to see that all teacher comrades are
given thorough education in the teachings
of Marxism-Leninism. Only when teachers
have really mastered Marxism-Leninism
will they be able skillfully to inject (it)
into their teaching at the least risk of
exposure and at-the same time to conduct'
struggles around the schools in a truly
Bolshevik manner.”
That is typical Communist doctrine, j
Perhaps Mrs. Frankfeld never subscribed j
to it and never practiced it. But when
questioned, she said: “I am a member of
the Communist Party. I admit it. I am
proud of it.” This statement may be ad
mired on the ground of its frankness,
although, without it, there was ample evi
dence of her Communist affiliation. Thq,
issue, however, is not a question of Mrs.
Frankfeld’s candor. The only question is
whether an avowed and enthusiastic Com
munist has any business teaching in the
public schools. The answer given in Balti
more is no, and that is the right answer.
The passing of the last of the Ingersolls
evokes many memories of the famed
dollar watch; the one that attracted police
to the scene, if wound in a bank doorway,
after dark.
A medical observer of the Bikini tests
says there's no escape from the atomic
bomb. 80 don’t run to the nearest exit;
you’ll only find yourself in a radioactive
alley. j
Spires of the Spirit
Handling Handicaps
Great Achievements Have Resulted From* Courage
in Face of Physical Difficulties
By the Rev. Frederick Bro wn Harris, D. D Lift. D.
Life’s music depends not on the strings which
have snapped, but on what is done with the
strings that are left. Performing before a
rapt audience at a concert in Paris, Ole Bull,
the great violinist,
I realizing that his A
string had broken,
quickly transposed
the composition and
finished it on three
strings amid the
deafening applause of
the crowd. He did
not pity himself nor
quit. He knew how
to handle the1 handi
cap. Life’s highest
art is to take second,
third, or even fourth
best and triumphantly
make of it something beautiful and useful.
An oyster takes that which is at first *n
irritation of which it cannot be rid and by
some mystic alchemy changes it into an object
of irridescent beauty, a pearll
There are just two things any of us can
do with limitations which narrow the scope of
our activities. We supinely can submit to them
or we superbly can surmount them. Handi
caps can prove either stumbling blocks or
stepping stones. Limitations in one direction
often serve to open gateways to even wider
fields. If you take a handicap with glorious
morning face, it may take you to heights which
otherwise never would be scaled. The blind
Milton’s early lament was: "Does God require
day labor, light denied?” But out of that
darkness came the radiant treasures of
"Paradise Lost” and "Paradise Regained.”
Every bottle of Pasteurized milk at our door
speaks of a handicapped cripple, who is now
employed in the ministry of healing by every
doctor and every hospital in all the world.
Pasteur's congenital poor health and his early
paralysis did not disqualify him for the
monumental service he was to render to suffer
ing humanity. Brother is he of all those
who by rugged determination have not been
mastered by handicaps, but have become
masters of them.

How free from encumbering handicaps
Handel must have been as he soared into
the seventh heaven and gave to the world
the "Hallelujah Chorus”! But the facts are
that his health and fortune had reached the
lowest ebb. His right side was paralyzed. His
money was gone. Threatened with imprison
ment, he vu tempted to give up the light.
Beset by such circumstances he rebounded to
compose the epic “Messiah.” Every time an
enraptured throng listens to the “Hallelujah
Chorus,” they are employing a handicapped
man.
William Wilberforce was so small physically
as to be called a shrimp. For a score of years
that diminutive man was in such wretched
physical health that at the doctor’s orders he
had to take opium just to keep going. He
wasn’t much, but what there was of him he
threw in front of the entrenched British slave
trade. And, in spite of giant opposition, more
than any one man that pigmy stopped it. In
Westminister Abbey the inscription at his
grave reads: “The Attorney General of the
unprotected and the friendless.” That is what
he did with his handicap. Henry Fawcett,
when' both eyes were rendered sightless by a
hunting trip accident, declared within ten
minutes after that terrible happening that he
was resolved not to allow it to make any
difference to his life. Without eyes he worked
his way through Cambridge, was made pro
fessor of political economy at his alma mater
and became Postmaster General of England,
developing over there the parcel post system
a generation before it was introduced in
America. He took hold of his handicap by
its real handle and turned it into a challenge.
And who can think of handicaps without
the name of Helen Keller being written across
the sky? Blind and a deaf mute, yet she has
changed all impediments into pearls.

What would the world, be without handicaps
harnessed to its most inspiring tasks? All the
world employs Helen Keller. Every social
reform employs William Wilberforce. Every
government employs Henry Fawcett. Every
college employs Milton. Every choir employs
Handel. Every hospital employs Pasteur. The
records show that the handicapped are the
most employable and with capabilities rein
forced have most to give. By sheer ability and
capability the handicapped have hired them
selves to the ages. They have ever been among
the immortal legion who have served best. In
maimed bodies are packed values and skills,
fidelity and zeal, quality and excellence, which
are among the Nation’s richest resources.
The President's Committee on National Em
ploy the Physically Handicapped Week, with all
its allied agencies, is lifting up the torch of
justice and the banner of mutual advantage
to employer and employe. It is a crusade to
change weights to wings.
Looking Ahead Some Shadows of Coming Events
Are Noted Here and A broad
(By the World Staff of the Associated Press.)
LONDON.—With tension growing Britain is
hustling its plans for protecting the civilian
population if war comes. A committee of
experts has just about completed the blueprint
of a comprehensive civil defense Mil. It will
be presented soon after Parliament reconvenes
October 26. The work has been under the
supervision of the Home Office, which had
charge of domestic front problems during
World War H's devastating bombings. Over-all
plans are in three parts: 1. Mobile columns of
regular and reserve troops which could be
wheeled into action against invading troops or
disloyal groups. 2. Regional corps with a wide
variety of duties ranging from heavy rescue
work to manning air-raid command posts.
3. Strictly local groups assigned such tasks as
first aid. Still unsettled are questions of safe
guarding children, aged and ill persons and
nonessential women. Under present plans
they would be taken out of port cities and in
dustrial areas to the country—later, perhaps,
overseas to the dominions. This, however, still
is tentative.
Meat
Don't let the current drop-In livestock and
meat prices fool you into believing that plenti
ful supplies and more favorable consumer
prices are here to stay. The current situation
is only temporary. It reflects a normal fall
increase in farm sales of hogs. Such sales will
continue rather heavy for the next three
months. Then they will slump, with price*
advancing again—perhaps to peak levels. No
permanent relief in supplies and prices is ex
pected before 1950. By that time farmers will
have expanded livestock production by *a large
margin. The Agriculture Department predicts
the per capita supply of all meats next year
will average possibly five pounds less than this
year's average of 145 pounds. This will still
be considerably above the prewar level of 134
pounds.
Social Glitter
NEW YORK.—Some of the orchids-and
champagne flavor of prewar days will return
with this year's debutante season. Several
coming-out parties comparable to the fabulous
ones that made headlines a decade ago are
planned. Most social and select of the group
debutante parties will be the Grosvenor ball in
the Ritz-Carlton ballroom, November 27. Big
gest will be the 13th annual debutante cotillion
and Christmas ball, December 20, when some
100 buds will make their bows at the Waldorf
Astoria. Capital socialites also look for the
most brilliant and lavish season in many a
year. Tipoff was a spectacular reception given
by the French Ambassador and Mme. Bonnet
this past week. Expensive liquors flowed freely,
the food was out of this world and the women's
gowns and jewels would break a maharajah.
The Mom
LONDON.—Prof. P. M. S. Blacklett, who was
a member of the government's recently dis
banded Advisory Committee on Atomic Energy,
is coming out soon with a book saying in effect
that Russia would be foolish to accept the
American proposals for control of atomic
energy. He writes that it would mean that
Russia would have to pin-point her heavy in
dustries for a “potentially hostile America,”
that Russia would have no guarantee that
America would destroy her own atomic power,
that an atomic authority might retard Russia's
atomic development.
Unification
A step forward In armed services unifica
tion: Each service has agreed to admit de
pendents of members of other services to its
hospitals regardless of whether the other
services have hospitals in the same area. And
it will cost a flat rate of $1.75 per day.
Aviation Briefs
The Air Force would like some of a new jet
night fighter to be announced soon by the
Navy, but it doesn’t have the money. The
plane is 10.000 pounds lighter than a similar
type already ordered by the Air Force. The
State Department’s aviation division may be
abolished and its work distributed among other
divisions dealing with transportation.
Gl Insurance
An informed person in the Veterans’ Admin
istration says that to avoid any possible rise
for poetical purposes the size of the OX tyjpur
ance dividend kitty won't be disclosed until
after election day. The national service life
insurance fund now totals $6,959,000,000. Esti
mates of surplus to be split up as dividends
out of this fund run up to *2,000,000.000. Early
in September VA said it probably would dis
close the surplus early this month. Now it
says, officially, that rechecking of errors will
delay the statement until mid-November.
Fifty Years Ago
The spotlighted news in The Star of Octo
ber 1, 1898, concerned turmoil in the Flowery
Kingdom. A late despatch from
Trouble in Shanghai said that it has been
Chino “semiofficially announced” that
the Emperor of China had com
mitted suicide on September 21. In London it
was understood that his imperial majesty had
died after signing decrees “whirti placed the
Dowager Empress at the head of affairs.”
Difficulties in Peking included arrest and ban
ishment of “all the English-speaking secretaries
and the principal members of the Chinese For
eign Office." Mr. Mortimer, belonging to the
British mission, was “insulted and attacked by
a mob, which stoned him and covered him with
mud”; and the Chinese secretary of the United
States Legation was similarly treated and had
several ribs broken. A cable from the American
Minister at Penang dealing with the situation
in China explained: “Six alleged conspirators
executed by order of the Empress yesterday.
Kang, leading reformer, adviser of the Emperor,
escaped in a British vessel.” What actually was
happening was a conservative revolution. It
succeeded—without actually murdering -Kuang
Hsu, the weak but liberally inclined occupant
of the throne. Kept prisoner, he did not really
die until 1908. Meanwhile, the Boxer Rebellion
and many other troubles developed.

A plague of false alarms had been bothering
the Washington Fire Department, and The Star
for October 3. 1898. contained a
Folse lengthy story about how the police
Alarms bad managed to catch some of
the mischief-makers. The officers
nabbed three suspects in the act of pulling a
box about 2:30 o’clock in the morning. One.
of the apprehended men was drunk “and also
had an extra supply of liquor in his picket.”
Each defendant was fined $40, “with a term of
four months on the farm each in default of
payment.” Another individual before Judge
Scott on two charges of a similar nature was
a 12-year-old colored boy, who admitted his
guilt but said: “Some white boys told me to do
it.” The judge, “in consideration of the boy’s
age,” imposed a penalty of $20 or 60 days in
each case.

Many columns of space in The Star for
October 5, 1898. were devoted to chronicling
the triennial convention of the
Episcopalians Protestant Episcopal Church in
Assembled America which opened that day
at the Church of the Epiphany.
The initial service was conducted by the Right
Rev. Henry B. Whipple, Bishop of Minnesota,
famous as the “Apostle to the Red Men.” He
was assisted by the Right Rev. Henry D. Neely,
Bishop of Maine; the Right Rev. Richard H.
Wilmer, Bishop of Alabama, and the Right
Rev. Daniel S. Tuttle, Bishop of Missouri.
Distinguished laymen in attendance included
J. Pierpont Morgan of New York, Chief Justice
Melville Fuller, former Senator George F.
Edmunds of Vermont, Robert Treat Paine of
Boston and David B. Lyman of Illinois. One
of the items on the agenda was a proposal to
change the name of the church to the American
Catholic Church.

Col. Theodore Roosevelt, opening his cam
paign for Governor of New York at Carnegie
Hall, New York City, was re
T. R. on the ported in The Star of October
Stump 6, 1898. as having said: “The
guns of our warships in the
tropic seas of the West and the remote East
have awakened us to the knowledge of new
duties. Our flag is a proud flag, and it stands
for liberty and civilization. Where it has once
floated, there must and shall be no return to
tyranny or savagery. We are face to face with
our destiny, and we must meet it with a high
and resolute courage.” A “tremendous crowd
cheered every utterance o( the gallant
Rough Rider.”
New Strategy Evidenced
By Detractors of Dewey
Enemies Cease Open Disparagement
But Still Work to Defeat Him
By Frank R. Kent
One of the Interesting phases of this cam.
paign Is the suffering of the confirmed jour
nalistic and political Dewey haters, nearly all
of whom are New Dealish, Left-wingish and
ClO'ish in their natures and records. Few of
these can write a piece or make a speech with
out using the word “liberal” from two to 20
timjss.
Tliese are the individuals who ever sine*
1940 have sneeringly referred to Gov. Dewey
as “Little Tom,” repeated with relish the Ethel
Barrymore story of the bridegroom on the
wedding cake and gleefully circulated the
venomous remark of Mrs. Kenneth Simpson
that "You have to know Tom Dewey well
really to dislike him.” Largely, inspiration for
this petty form of disparagement came from
the White House during the Roosevelt regime.
It was typical of the late Mr. Roosevelt’s atti
tude toward those who opposed him. Since
the Republican Convention, however, this
group has ceased to harp on these strings.
One reason, of course, is the strong probabil
ity that Mr. Dewey will be elected President
on November 2. Another is that the Dewey
record and performance make these stories
seem silly and continued effort to treat him
with contempt undoubtedly would react upon
those who try it. So that form of detraction
has gone into the discard. And those who most
conspicuously practiced it are now crowding
the periodical and daily press with articles
■bout Dew'cy's extraordinary ability as an ad
ministrator and the amazing competence of
the devoted coterie of friends who constitute
the “Dewey team.”
But none of this means that this transmitted
enmity has disappeared or that those who
cherish it look forward to the Dewey election
with pleasure. On the contrary, the prospect
causes considerable pain, evidence of which is
to be found in new lines of attack, plainly de
signed to make trouble if and when he becomes
President. Some of this is absurd, very' little
of it is important and a lot of it is malicious.
Several examples can be given. First, there is
the hopeful insistence that while Gov. Dewey
is sure to be elected, the Democrats will regain
control of the Senate and Mr. Dewey will thus
be powerless to put through a program. This,
of course, is a possibility, but it is a remote one.
Tne fact is that if Dewey is elected by anything
like the landslide indicated by the polls, he
will pull through the Republican Senators in
all the States now classified as “doubtful.”
In brief, a decisive Dewey victory means a
Republican Congress in both branches. Every
experienced politician knows this, and most of
the talk about a Democratic Senate comes from
persons who, for one small reason or another,
would like to see Mr. Dewey handicapped after
he gets In. Second, there is the hopeful in
sistence that Mr. Dewey and Senator Taft are
bound to clash, with resulting administration
futility. This, too, is a remote possibility. The
facts are that Gov. Dewey and Senator Taft
are now on exceedingly good personal terms.
A serious clash is the last thing either would
want. Clearly, it would be disadvantageous
politically to both. No one expects Senator
Taft to be a blind follower of President Dewey
or to agree with him on every point. But thera
are so many reasons why a real rift, such as
suggested, would be bad for both these intelli-*
gent and patriotic men, bad for their party and
bad for the country, that those who know them
best regard the idea as exceedingly farfetched.

Third, there is the hopeful insistence that
the so-called “Old Guard" in the House and
Senate will capture the Republican Party and
frustrate all of Dewey's plans. This, of course,
utterly ignores the facts that, as President,
Mr. Dewey will have a tremendous lot of Fed
eral jobs and Federal favors to distribute
through Republican members of Congress; that
these jobs and favors are of great political
value in the States and districts; that mem
bers of Congress who try to nullity the plans
of a new President automatically cut them
selves off from such jobs and favors; that
the hostility of a popular President would
be a great liability to members of Congress
who come up for re-election in 1950.
Fully aware of these things, few detached
and experienced observers believe that if
elected Mr. Dewey will have any real diffi
culties with his own party. They take no
stock in the notion of a great Dewey-Taft
feud. As for the Old Guard “capture” of
the party, that is even more farcical. Nearly
all these suggestions are the product of wishful
thinking. Most of them are predicated upon
the false assumption that Mr. Dewey is a
small, stupid man who will not know how to
use the presidential power as a party leader.
Of course, he will have trouble with some
members in Congress. Every President has
that. But that he will not be able to get
along with his own party in the legislative
branch is certainly not borne out by his
eight-year record at Albany.
Neverthless, these and similar forecasts will
continue. Most of them w’ill seem ridiculous—
if they have not been forgotten—by the time
they are scheduled to be fulfilled. Right now
the silliest one to appear in print is the state
ment that Gov. and Mrs. Dewey are jealous
of Gov. and Mrs. Warren because the lovely
Warren daughters have been getting more
and better publicity than the not-so-lovely
Dewey sons. That should get the prize. How
ever, it is only a little more absurd than tha
contention that all the rich Democrats (and
there are plenty in office here* are patriotic
and self-sacrificing fellows, while all the rich
Republicans want to “shoot the poor"—a
shoddy phrase which the professional “liberals’*
love to roll off their tongues and pens.
Changes
IN* the Top«k» C»»itaJ:
Members of the Kansas Press Association la
Topeka for their annual meeting represent
both the older editors with their wealth of com
munity experiences and those of the younger
set who are forging new and better paths for
the future. As an editor and his newspaper
grows, so does his town, generally spearing.
W. W. Graves, who has begun his 53rd year
aa publisher of the St. Paul Journal, explained
this clearly when he wrote the following: “The
town, too, has made its changes. It war
slightly larger than now, but it is a better
town now than then. The old board sidewalks,
where there were any sidewalks, have given
way to flne concrete walks that reach nearly
every part of the town. The muddy streets
have given way to surfaced thoroughfares. The
houses are more modern and the business
activities have kept pace with the times.
"The present editor welcomed the first tele
phones, the first steam engines, the first
tractors, the first gasoline engines, the first
natural gas, the first electricity, the first
picture show. All kinds of lodges flourished
here, but few are left.
"Carload after carload of apples were shipped
from here then, but we have to get our apples
somewhere else now, even for table use. Home
made butter was shipped from here by the bar
rel; but now no farm home-made butter is
sold on the local market. Coffee came in bar-
rels and had to be roasted and ground at home.
Calico and dress goods came in rolls and all
dresses were made at home or at the local
dress shop. Basketball was unheard of but
the town had a flne baseball team that held
i its own widths best of them.”

Saddler Seeks
Featherweight
Crown Friday
New York—(AP)—Joe (Sandy)
Saddler will be the new feather
weight champion, if you can be
lieve Charley Johnston’s litera
ture on the No. 1 contnder.
It took the Johnstons, Charey
and his brother Bill, two years
to get their 22-year-old leather
pusher in the same ring with
Willie Pep, the popular champion
from Hartford, Conn.
“All Saddler figures to gain by
!he match is the championship,”
says Bill Johnston. “Certainly
le’s not going to get rich. That
ivill come later if he beats Pep.
Although the 20th Century
Sporting Club expects the Masi
son Square Garden bout to draw
a $70,000 gate, Saddler’s 10 per
cent probably will amount to no
more than $5,000. Pep is laying
his title on the line for 50 per
cent of the net receipts.
Sadder, a native of Boston,
has been fighting professionally
four years. He has scored 62
knockouts in 92 pro fights, six
of which he lost Saddler fought
13 times in the Garden and won
each bout. Pep has won all but
one of 11 Garden fights. His on
ly defeat there—it’s the only de
feat on Pep’s record—was a
gainst Sammy Angott in 1943
when Saddler was still an ama
teur. They meet on Friday, Oct.
29.
As a simon pure. Saddler won
Police Athletic League titles in
New York in four divisions—85,
90,95, and 100 pounds. He won a
Golden Gloves crown as a ban
tamweight.
As a professional he knocked
out six featherweight titleholders
—Leonardo Lopez in Mexico
City, Oscar Calles of Venezuela,
Young Tanner of Trindida, Mi
guel Aceveda of Cuba, Kid, Bar
lie. He has also knocked out
crack lightweights from Bogota,
Caracas and Panama.
“It was just luck how I signed.
Saddler to a professional contrac
for my brother Charley,” says
Bill. “One Monday night I went
out to Jamaica Arena to arrange
a wrestling show, one of the 17
I promote weekly. They had am
ateur fights that night and I saw
Saddler. He looked good and
wanted a manager.
“I made a date to visit his
father. I was smoking a big cigar
when I met Sandy's father. His
father said if I signed Sandy I'd
have to put out my cigar.
“There’s no smoking in my
house,’ said Sandy’s father.
“Well, I put out the cigar and
Sandy’s father made the boy pro
mise four tings before he’d sign
They were: 1—No smoking while
he’s a fighter. 2—Be in bed by
10:30 at night when possible. 3—
No intoxicating liquors. 4—Quit
fighting when he marries.
“Sandy agreed on all counts.
“Next thing I know Sandy’s* fa
ther says he can’t sign the con
tract. that he's not Mr. Saddler.
‘You're Sandy’s father, aren’t
you?’ I said. 'I sure am,’ said
Sandy’s father, ‘but my name’s
not Saddler.’.—.-
‘Then what is it?’ I said.
‘Moses Defendant,’ answered
Sandy's father.
“The name Saddler was* cross
ed out and we—I had a lawyer
with me—inserted the name ‘Mo
ses Defendant.’
“Sandy’s father is a follower
of Father Divine and had his
name changed legally.
“Sandy, who has three brothe
and three sisters, idolizes his fa
ther and the boy really deserves
the championship.”
Johnston then opened his safe,
pulled out the contract on which
every paragraph is initial
ed “MD” and said:
This boy is really a hard hit
ter. I think he’ll win for his fa
ther. Not many fighters have fa
thers like Moses Defendant.”.

Airport Work
Races Winter
Work on Palmer’s airport was
racing with winter weather this
week, as a survey crew headed by
Victor Rivers, project engineer,
completed work on the east-west
runway. The runway ditching
has been held up awaiting the
running of these final survey
lines.
It is the hope of the Palmer
Airport Association to complete
the ditching before cold weather
sets in, thereby assuring that the
strip will remain in usable con
dition through the bad weather
months.
Mr. Rivers inspected the en
tire airport construction recent
ly and reported that the work
“shows satisfactory progress and
a reasonable return per dollar for
the money involved in the con
struction program.”
Mr. Rivers said that by August
31, $20,009 had been spent on the
port this year. Territorial High
way Engineer Frank Metcalf had
allotted a total of $33,500 to the
Palmer airport for the construc
tion planned this year. The sum
is expected to be entirely ex
hausted before winter ends the
airport work.
Four Leave Valley
Ted and Leola DeLand, son and
daughter of Mrs. L. Z. Scott of
Palmer, left the Valley last week
to spend the winter Outside.
They flew to Philomath, Ore.,
where they will visit with an
aunt. Leola expects to return to
Palmer next year, to conclude
her final year in school at Pal
mer. Accompanying the DeLands
on the plane Outside were two
other Palmer-ites—Glen Miller
and Don Parks who were plan
ning to visit relatives in North
Dakota and in Nebraska.

FIRST FROSTS FOLLOW
FLEEING SOUTHERNERS
Word coming from Kirk Stone,
now comfortably at home in
Madison, Wis., reports that he is
still somewhat climatically con
fused. After waiting, in company
with all Valley residents, for the
first killing frost of the year
here, he resignedly welcomed
winter with the coming of that
frost in mid-August of his Val
ley residency.
Now. six weeks’ later, he finds
he’s got it all to go through again
back at Madison. However he’s
again been introduced to the
winter of 1948 for a second time,
for Madison received its first
killing frost on the evening of
Oct. 4.
Mr. Stone expressed some dis
appointment at the dwindling
numbers of questionnaires which
are reaching the Madison ad-
dress. He says it’s most impor
tant that he have a good idea of
the Valley point of view when he
consults with his Department of
Interior superiors, and he’s prom
ised to faithfully present that
point of view.
Arrangements have been made
to have all questionnaires for
warded, so Mr. Stone again re
quests that all colonists who have
not yet returned the blanks
make every effort to do so as
soon as possible. Their co-opera
tion may mean much to the en
tire Valley’s future.
The first fall meeting of the
Matanuska Valley Health Coun
cil will be held tomorrow, Oct.
21, it was announced this week
by Council president, Mrs. Clyde
King, Sr. The meeting will be
held in the Health Center and is
scheduled to open at 8 p. m.
All clubs are urged to have a
representative at this first meet
ing of the year, when officers
will be elected and plans will be
formulated for the year’s work.

A—1» THURSDAY, October 14, 194R
Montgomery's Charter Upheld
By upholding the constitutionality of a
disputed section of the proposed Mont
gomery County charter, the Maryland
Court of Appeals has cleared the way for
a decisive vote, by county resident® on the
home-rule question. The court has left
no room for doubt as to how much of the
charter is to be voted on. It will be the
entire instrument, not a truncated ver
aion of it, or a version containing a pro
vision which might later be declared
It is fortunate that the high State court
has disposed of the constitutional issue
in such forceful and unequivocal terms
and before the election rather than after
the charter is adopted. And there seems
to be little doubt that a majority of the
citizens are in favor of adoption. Had
the appellate tribunal been less explicit
in dealing with the debated portion of the
document, many voters who otherwise
would have supported the plan would have
had misgivings. They might have had
difficulty in making up their minds to
vote for a charter containing a clause
which the Circuit Court had held to be
unconstitutional and which the higher
court had left clouded with doubt.
Since the Court of Appeals is Maryland’s
court of last resort, the validity of the pro
vision for a budgetary executive session of
the proposed County Council a month after
the regular legislative session of the coun
cil has been definitely established. This
provision had been attacked successfully
before the lower court, which ordered the
section deleted before the charter was
submitted to the voters. The Circuit Court
had held that a State constitution re
quirement that all “legislative” acts by a
county councif must be performed within
the period of a month-long legislative
session precluded any appropriations in a
later executive session. Under the charter,
the council would hold its regular legisla
tive meeting during May and then would
meet in executive session in June for
budget-making and appropriating.
In overruling the lower court’s opinion
that budgeting and appropriating are “leg
islation” and hence could not be done
except in the May meeting, the higher
court contended against a narrow con
struction of the constitution's language,
To give a “straight and unnatural mean
ing” to “legislation” would be to “emascu
late” the charter, Chief Judge Marbury
pointed out.
Thus the chief technical issue raised by
opponents of the charter has been wiped
out and the home-rule plan now will go
to the voters oh its merits. That is the
way this question, so vitally affecting the
future of the county, should be decided.
General Eisenhower's Inaugural
In his inaugural address at Columbia
University, General Dwight D. Eisenhower
was speaking not merely to the faculty
and student body assembled there but
also to the Nation at large. Specifically
it was to the whole country that he de
clared: “The common responsibility of
all Americans is to become effective, help
ful participants in a way of life that blends
and harmonizes the fiercely competitive
demands of the individual and of society.”
A citizen, he insisted, must be free to make
the most of himself and of his opportuni
ties; the society must 'be stable—“other
wise it is nothing but a temporary truce
with chaos.” But "freedom for the indi
vidual must never degenerate into the
brutish struggle for survival that we call
barbarism” and “neither must the sta
bility of society ever degenerate into the
enchained servitude of the masses that we
call statism.”
This Is good American doctrine in the
General’s own engaging language. He was
talking in the tradition of Washington
and Lincoln when he reasserted the fun
damental principle of liberty and order.
But his mind was not focused on the past.
He faced the issues and the problems of
the present and the future as they not too
often are faced—the dangers of “too great
a concentration of power in the hands of
any individual or group,” “too much power
in centralized government,” “social explo
siveness and a demand for revolutionary
change,” “selfish retaliation,” “loss of unity
through demagogic appeals to class self
ishness, greed and hate.”
General Eisenhower reviewed those
perils courageously, remembering how the
English historian Macaulay in 1857 had
written to an American friend: “When a
society has entered on this downward
progress, either civilization or liberty nlust
perish. Either some Caesar or Napoleon
will seize the reins of government with a
strong hand; or your republic will be as
fearfully plundered and laid waste by bar
barians in the twentieth century as the
Roman empire was in the fifth; with this
difference, that the Huns and Vandals
who ravaged the Roman empire came
from without, and that your Huns and
Vandals will have been engendered within
your own country by your own institu
tions.”
But the General had an answer for that
prophecy of disaster. The day foretold
by Macaulay—and forecast likewise by cer
tain more modern pessimists—“can never
come,” he affirmed, “if in our educational
system we help our students gain a true
of our society, of the need
for balance between Individual desires and
the general welfare, and of the imperative
requirement that every citizen participate
intelligently and effectively in democratic
affairs." Americans who never will matricu
late at Columbia or in any other institu
tion of higher learning can appreciate the
challenge implicit in these words—words
which offer a new hope and a new de
termination for “the greatest temporal
power yet seen upon the earth, a .power
dedicated to-the betterment and happi
ness of,all mankind.”
German Reparations
The much-debated problem of German
reparations has again been emphasized by
the brief trip to Paris and Ldndon of Paul
G. Hoffman, Economic Co-operation Ad
ministrator. It is understood that Mr.
Hoffman made this quick transatlantic
flight to hold first-hand conferences with
the French and British Foreign Ministers
on the best way to iron out divergencies of
viewpoint on the subject. As matters
stand, our Government favors a reduction
of the reparations program, whereas the
British and French desire it to be main
tained, at least in principle. The practical
aspects of this issue are far-reaching. They
involve the whole problem of European
recovery and American aid in that
endeavor.
Altered concepts regarding German rep
arations reflect the profound changes in
the attitudes and policies of the victorious
great powers since -the close of the late
war. The original idea' was to make de
feated Germany indemnify the victims of
its aggression more efficiently than after
its defeat in World War I. At Versailles,
Germany was assessed a bill for damages
expressed in terms of money. This bill
was not only far in excess of Germany’s
ability to pay but ran into insurmountable
difficulties of exchange and transfers. The
result was German bankruptcy with rela
tively slight material gains for its creditors.
Mindful of that melancholy precedent,
the victors in World War II determined
that Germany should pay this time in
taagibles, especially industrial plants and
machinery, which should be dismantled
and shipped abroad on a predetermined
basis. Originally, the victors were agreed
that Germany’s industrial stature should
be much reduced, which left a wide margin
for reparations out of its vast industrial
structure. But a number of factors soon
entered the picture which worked against
the carrying out of the full program.
Russia and Poland unilaterally seized Ger
many’s eastern provinces, including the
great industrial center of Silesia. Also,
they and other eastern countries expelled
many millions of Germans to the shrunken
fceich. Again, Russia exploited its zone
of occupation without consulting its West
ern allies, though claiming a share of
reparations from the western zones. All
this, together with Russia’s refusal to live
up to the Potsdam agreement for treating
occupied Germany as an economic unit,
paralyzed Germany’s industrial life, reduc
ing Europe’s former industrial heart to
the depths of a depression which, in turn,
crippled* economic recovery in all Ger
many’s neighbors.
This became Increasingly important to
America when we assumed the burden,
first of maintaining our own zone, then
part of the British zone, and finally of
aiding all Western Europe under ERP. It
is the American contention that a reduc
tion of transfers of productive equipment
from Germany is needed if Germany is
to be put on a viable economic basis and
the recovery of Western Europe is not to
be gravely hampered.
France and Britain dissent from this
contention, though for somewhat different
reasons. French opposition is the stronger,
France fearing any notable revival of‘Ger
man heavy industry. It is to be antici
pated, therefore, that Britain may be more
amenable. Yet the issue requires con
structive handling, consistent with our
interest as the country carrying the bulk
of the load.
One versed in the Intricacies of the
feminine coiffure can probably distinguish
between the short, feathered bob and the
appearance of the average high-school
miss on arising.
Commonwealth Conference
The rapid evolution of that unique po
litical organism, the British Common
wealth and Empire, is attested by the
changed composition of the conference of
Prime Ministers now meeting in London.
These conferences usually take place every
other year. Since the last one, held in
1946, momentous developments have oc
curred within the Commonwealth. Three
new dominions have been added to the
roster. These are India, Pakistan and
Ceylon. And those newcomers are very
different in character from the charter
members.
Hitherto, the dominions have been es
sentially British, or at least European, in
their makeup, the French of Canada and
the Du&h of South Africa being the only
notable exceptions to the general British
rule. The new trio are not only non-British
and non-European in their racial makeup;
they are also tentative in their common
wealth membership. India and Pakistan,
especially, chose dominion status largely
because they could get along with each
other more smoothly within the Common
wealth than they could if they had be
come wholly independent nations, as
Burma elected to do. And of course every
member of the Commonwealth is free to
quit the association whenever it wants to.
All this naturally raises the question as
to whether some new form of association
may be evolved to cover both the oldfcr
dominions, with their intimate links with
Britain and deep devotion to the Crown,
and those newer dominions whose mem
bership rests on expediency alone.
Nevertheless, this gathering of Premiers
is concerned-with certain problems of vital
common concern. One of these is defense
in a troubled and uncertain world. Here,
again, the trend is toward devolution.
Impoverished postwar Britain is no longer
able to carry the main burden of Com
monwealth armaments. Commonwealth
strategy must be based upon regional
arrangements. This trend is not" only
between dominions, as the arrangement
between Australia and New Zealand for
joint defense of the South Pacific, but also'
between sections of the Commonwealth
and the United States, best exemplified by
the understanding between Canada and
ourselves. Another strategic problem con
cerns the attitude of the dominions toward
the Western European Union, of which
Britain is a member. On the economic
side, this union likewise poses the problem
of empire trade and finance as affected by
Britain’s closer economic association with
countries of the European continent.
These and other matters will form the
basis for the discussions that are now
going on. It should (be emphasized, how
ever, that these talks*will be exploratory
and Informal in character.' There is no
hard-and-fast agenda, no public debates,
no binding decisions. In conformity with
Commonwealth tradition, this is' a sort
of “family gathering” for exchange of
views and helpful suggestions. Common
wealth flexibility is thus maintained.
Leroy W. Herron
An earlier illness this year brought about
Leroy W. Herron’s formal retirement in
July from The Star as Advertising Director.
But his relationship with The Star was to®
intimate a thing to be severed by retire
ment. Death came to him while return
ing from a trip to a business meeting in
Ohio which he had attended of, his own
volition because, of his love for the work
and associations of a lifetime. He did not
And it easy to adjust himself to leisure or
rid himself of responsibility. He was hap
piest when actively at work and there is
some satisfaction in the thought that in
his final hours he may have considered
himself as still being on duty.
He came to The Star more than half a
century ago when his father, the late
J. Whit Herron, was active in the manage
ment of the paper’s business affairs. His
death thus ends a family connection that
goes back nearly seventy years, when his
father began work; in 1879, as a clerk in
the business office. To the son, as to the
father, The Star is indebted for a deep
loyalty, the influence of a clean and manly
character and many years of happy and
delightful companionship as coworker in
development of this newspaper.
Colonel Herron’s attainments as an ad
vertising executive are attested in part by
the honors that,came to him at the hands
of his business colleagues throughout the
United States. He enjoyed a wide circle of
friends in Washington. He was a good
citizen and a successful civilian. But he
was also distinguished as a soldier, and
from the time of his boyhood he loved and
honored the military traditions of disci
pline and allegiance to country. He fought
in two wars, commanding a regiment in,
France, and he was one of the enthusiastic
builders of the District National Guard.
Popular and respected as a leader of men,
one of his great disappointments was that
age prevented him from- going on active
duty once more in the Second World War.
His associates at The Star will not forget
the moving eloquence with which he spoke
to members of “The Star family’’ in uni
form at a dinner given in their honor after
the war, and the pride he took in their
service.
With the members of his family and
the great number of his friends The Star
shares a loss, but also the pleasant
memories of a good and useful life.

This and That
By Charles E. Tracewell
Templeton Jones was showing off his new
television set to a neighbor.
They were watching the football game, when
in came Mrs. Jones.
“And where have you been, my dear?” asked
Jones.
"Out on the front steps, talking to Mrs.
Brown.”
"These ladies," smiled Jones. “Always gos
siping.”
It was the sort of thing one will say, not
meaning it, exactly.
“I suppose you men never gossip,” said Mrs.
Tones. Mr. Jones grinned, the neighbor grinned.

The two men kept looking at the screen.
Templeton Jones finally asked, “Well, did
you find out anything?”
“It wouldn’t interest you,” replied his wife.
“You must have talked about something.”
“We weren’t talking much, Just watching.”
Temp Jones kept his eyes on the screen, but
he asked, “Just what were you watching?”
Mrs. Jones was deliberate, even siow, in
answering.

“Well,” she finally said, “there was a police
car down the block.”
“These women—”
That was what Jones started to say, but
suddenly he snapped to attention.
He took his eyes off the end of the big tube,
jumped to his feet, and walked over.
“A police car?” he asked. “What was it
doing?”
“I doh’t know.”
“Where did it go?”
“Oh, down the block somewhere?”
“Don’t you know whose house?”
“Well, I think it was the Greens’ house.”
“Did the cops go in?”
“Yes, they went in, all right. But why are
you so interested? Only women, I believe you
said, are interested in such things.”

Templeton Jones subsided in his seat, but he
couldn’t keep his attention on the screen.
“What else did you see?”
“Not much.”
“Something must have happened.”
“There probably did, but I don’t know what.”
Jones tapped on the set.
"Did they bring anybody out?”
“Two officers went in, and two officers came
out.”
“Didn’t they have anything with them?”
“Well, onfe of them had a piece of paper in
his hand.”

“And when he came out—”
*>He had the same, piece of paper in his
hand.”
1116 small figures on the television screen
were running up and down the field, but
Templeton Jones had forgotten all about them.
"Now, I wonder," he mused, “what the cops
were doing at the Greens’.”
It was a question no one there could answer.
Mrs. Jones, however, who knew her hus
band better than most persons, was not through
yet.
“By the way,” she said, “you probably won’t
be interested, but there is a moving van com
ing down the block, too.”
“A moving van—now I wonder which house?”
“Oh, that old one that hasn’t been occupied.”
“Have you seen the people?”
“Yes.”
“How many?”
“Oh, several, and a nice looking dog, and a
nice cat in a collar. Now you had better go
back to the football game. ’Hiat’s all I know,
for the present.”
"These women,” grinned Templeton Jones.
"Always gossiping."
Letters to The Star
Three Rs’ By-Products
To the Bditor of The Star:
With reference to the letter of Ida R. Brown,
It seems to me one very Important Item has
been overlooked—namely that “progressive”
education is not a methodology but an ideology
which uses what may appear to be activities
as its method. The controversy on progressive
education is entirely a question of objectivity,
for, to be progressive, the development of com
munication skills or scientific skills (the 3 R’s)
are a possible and incidental by-product only
which may or may not result from the de
velopment of a particular philosophy.
But don’t take my word for it—I am not an
educator. Let me refer you to Dr. Boyde H.
Bode, Professor of Education, who says: "In
academic language, the progressive school is
a place where children go, not primarily to
learn, but to carry on a way of life.” Also
Prof. W. H. Kilpatrick who says: “We must
not begin by setting up certain traits as items
of knowledge or certain habits or skills as
ends to be realized and then hunt' about for
some experience to teach them.” In other
words,- we cannot intend that little Johnny
will read, write or spell as the result of what
we do but may only hope that he will also
and incidentally acquire such skills.
I believe that the public should devote more
interest to what is being produced for our
educational dollar and should put at the top
of the must list for background reading “Pro
gressive Education at the Crossroads” and
“Modern Education” by Prof. Bode. Also
"Foundation of Methods” by Prof. Kilpatrick
may be interesting. CARL W. BINKER.
‘Thirty-Niners’ Defended
To the Editor of The BWr:
Like many other American newspaper
readers, I have been following the Ilse Koch
case and the ensuing debate with the greatest
of interest. But having been both bom and
educated in Germany as well as served, as
an American citizen, with the United States
Army and with the American administration
in Germany, I have also been deeply shocked
by some statements in Leon B. Poullada’s
lengthy dissertation on the case. I take par
ticular issue with his allegations concerning
what he calls the “Thirty-Niners.”
I, too, am one of these “Thirty-Niners,”
inasmuch as I left Germany in the thirties be
cause I happened to disagree with Hitler. But
just because of this, I feel that I and others
like me were doubly qualified to represent
America and the United States Government
in postwar Germany. We did not go there
in order to give vent to any feelings of cheap
revenge, as Mr. Poullada implies (nor, for that
matter, to deal in the black market or for any
other “get rich quick” scheme). It is strange
that from 'other quarters just the opposite
charge has been made: Namely, that “we”
have been far too friendly and lenient towards
our German “brethren.”
As a matter of fact, serving with Military
Government in Germany or in related assign
ments, we simply believed that, knowing Ger
many and the Germans and German mental
ity as we do and also having embraced Amer
ica wholeheartedly, we might be able to do
more in the way of developing and executing
the policy of USMG than many a native fel
low-citizen. There are many quite prominent
figures ampng Mr. Poullada’s so-called “Thirty
Niners”—aside from the unnamed thousands
of GIs, officers and Military Government offi
cials and employes—who prove Mr. Poullada's
insinuation to be what it really is: malicious
and, in its intolerance and unfairness, basically
un-American.
Aside from the dubious merits of the Koch
case, there is one more point definitely in
favor of the “Thirty-Niners,” and the de
plorable handling of the Koch case substanti
ates this point: We read with ever-growing
apprehension of "the Prauleins winning the
peace” by indoctrinating our gullible represent
atives over there, high and low, with Nazi ideas,
Instead o{, as it should> be, the other way
around. Whatever the case pro or con on the em
ployment of recent citizens of German back
ground in USMG may be, one thing is sure:
With their insight into the German mind, they
would not have fallen for this, and one may
venture to say that many a trouble we are
facing in Germany today might have been
averted had the powers-that-be made a better
and wider use of their specific knowledge and
experience. ERNEST S. CARSTEN.
Stalin and World War II
To the Editor of The Star:
It was with great interest that I read Con
stantine Brown’s article of October 10 in which
he charged that Stalin manipulated the West
ern Powers into the last war so that com
munism would inherit what remained of those
countries after war, chaos and political in
stability had done their work.
On July 22, 1939, the Pittsburgh Press pub
lished a letter to the editor written by me in
which I expressed the hope that the democra
cies of the world would conduct their foreign
affairs in such a way that it would be possible
for them to stay on the sidelines in the com
ing war between fascism and communism.
My contention was then and is now that
Stalin through the Communist cells in the
British labor unions and the Confederation
General de Travailleurs influenced British and
Trench foreign policy to such an extent that
Chamberlain and Daladier were forced to com
mit themselves to war to stop further German
territorial aggrandizement. Some of the com
ments after Munich bear out this premise;
for instance, the Confederation General de
Travailleurs in October, 1938, declared against
the Munich pact at their exercises commemor
ating the Octobrist revolution, in Russia.
Winston Churchill and Sir Stafford Cripps in
July and August of 1939 played into Stalin’s
hand by advocating war against Germany if
Hitler marched into Dantzig and the Polish
corridor. Stalin knew that the French and
British commitments were such in September
of 1939 that by making a pact with Hitler, it
would free German armies for their march
into Dantzig and the Polish corridor. Stalin
knew that as long as these segments of former
Getanan territory remained in Polish hands or
free, the7 causus belli for Western Europe
existed. That Winston Churchill and Sir
Stafford Cripps were hypocritical about Poland
is proven by their willingness to barter Polish
freedom and Polish land for Russian promises
at the conferences between Stalin, Churchill,
and Roosevelt. The world heard no‘further
about freeing Poland from the Communist
grasp from either the British labor groups or
the Confederation General de Travailleurs,
E. M. WARNER.
Says Franco Threatens Democracy

ings.
However, your editorial “Our Spanish Policy”

I make particular reference to the statement

Joining in this censure was far from a mistake.
It was in keeping with American Ideals of con
demning anti-democratic forces. Besides the
resolution was aimed at one man, and one man
only, Franco, an avowed enemy of democratic
government today as he was on December 19>

1946, an4 on another memorable date, Decem
ber 7, 1941.
Franco made no bones of his sympathies for

As a threat to world's peace, Franco today'
means nothing. But as a threat to democracy,
he is still as dangerous as ever. Not as dan
gerous as Stalin, but certainly not to J»e under
estimated, If we are at a point wh£jp we Heed

fuehrer, we should go along with “Our U. N.

It Should Have Been ‘Pounds’
To the Editor of The Star:
An editorial in the October 10, issue of The
Star entitled "An Improved B-36” says:
•'noteworthy revisions and further modifica
tions within a year will enable it to carry ten
thousand tons of bombs twelve thousand miles.”
A noble weapon, indeed!
But do you not think that the Strategic
Air Command, from which you probably got
your Information, is being overly modest in its
claims for this world-shaking and unique ve
hicle? Only ten thousand tons, forsooth!
And, in view of the above statement, your de
duction that "the B-36 could deliver an atomic
bomb great distances via the polar regions”
just takes our breaths away and amazes us no
end.
Tut! Tut! Tyt! Some of us who have salt
in our blood know that two plus two equals six
and a quarter—or could you have been slightly
wrong in your figures? A. G. BOWERS.
(Editor’s note: The figures were right,
but the unit of weight was wrong. Pounds
were meant, nol tons.)
Penalty for Diplomats Who Fail
To the Editor of The Star.
Referring to the crisis with Russia, let me
say that many years ago I read this comment
on such situations: “In event of war, the am
bassadors who failed on both sides to preserve
•peace should be hanged.”
I think that if that were the rule now, our
leaders and conferees and the Russians would
make far more earnest efforts to keep the peace.
PAUL B. JOHNSON.

Mr. Vinson Out of OrderP
To the Ecutor of The Star:
President Truman's determination to send
Justice Vinson to plead before the throne of
Stalin has brought forth unfriendly criticism
from both political friend and foe. However,
no criticism has been aimed at Mr. Vinson.
Was he not equally guilty of consenting to
commit a diplomatic blunder 'of the first
magnitude? He knew that he was about to
go over the head of our Secretary of State.
Presumably, he knew that the matter had been
submitted to the United Nations.* He also
knew that he was not obliged to accept the
assignment. If he felt that his exalted posi
tion would make an Impression on Mr. Stalin,
he and his associates would do well to employ
Mr. Gallup to find out just where they stand
in the eyes of the American people. The result
of the poll might induce them to stick to the
law. J. FRED PARSONS.
Steel in the White House
To the Editor of The Btar:
Much is being said concerning an expenditure
of $1,000,000 for remodeling the White House at
this time of short material supplies and high
costs of construction. Edward Egger, writing to
The Star, states that the.White House is the
same 132-year-old structure, built when steel
and concrete reinforcements were both un
known and unobtainable.
Mr. Egger undoubtedly is unaware of the fact
that in 1902 President Theodore Roosevelt
secured an appropriation from Congress in the
amount of $830,000 to cover complete remodern
ization and refurnishing of the White House,
and steel beams replaced the wooden ones.
The purchasing value of a dollar in 1902 was
over twice what it is today.
WALTER L. CARTER.
Compliment for Mr. Brown
To the Editor of The Star:
I read consistently the Constantine Brown
column in The Star and like it very much. Mr.
Brown, in my opinion, is an able and gifted
columnist. His articles are classic examples
of intelligent and distinguished newspaper
work. They are uniformly well-written, in
teresting and informative. His extraordi
nary grasp of international affairs is astound
ing, and through his mastery of the art of im
parting in clear and concise style and his inter
pretations of world issues he is contributing
immeasurably to the efforts of those forces
dedicate to the ideal of promoting a more en
lightened public opinion. The Star is for
tunate indeed to have such a talented gentle
man cm its staff. L- A* c
‘Native Daughter’#’ Complaint#
To the Editor of The 8t»r:
Is Washington bowing to the golden idols?
The ruthless way in which beautiful trees are
cut down along picturesque and historic streets,
the absolute heaxtlessness with which beautiful,
historic homes are tom down to make—of all
things—parking lots, are deplorable, to say the
least. With the housing shortage so bad, it is
unspeakable to tear down so many residences
fb make room for parking lots or office space.
We native residents count it a privilege to
live in the Nation’s Capital, even though we
have no voice In our own government and are
fiasswi with lunatics and criminals! One
often wonders whether sane of the “foreigners”
who come from other cities and States and
take over the management of our city do not
rightfully belong in that class though they
straddle the fence and vote “back home” while
they rule here with an iron hand.
The older residents who have lived here and
worked for the Government for many years,
buying their own homes, hoping for a measure
of security in the “sunset years” of life—what
peace do they have today? They continually
are harassed by telephone calls asking if they
want to buy cemetery lots or do they want to
sell their homes.
Let’s restore the dignity and beauty which
should characterise the "Capital of the World”
and start working for peace right here at home
before we tackle the bigger Job of world peace
and security. NATIVE DAUGHTER.
The Political Mill
Michigan Labor Chief*
Fail to Slow Up Dewey
G. O. P. Candidate Seen Well in Lead,
Helped by Vote for Wallace
By Gould Liucoln
GRAND RAPIDS, Mich., Oct. 14.—Tha
Dewey-Warren band wagon is rolling in Michi
gan notwithstanding the efforts of the l^bor
leaders—on whom the Democrats place their
reliance intthis State—to slow it up and halt
it entirely. Pour years ago the late President
Roosevelt d^eated Gov. Dewey in the State
by 32,000 votes—a narrow margin In a total
vote of 2,200,000. Today, Gov. Dewey is well
in the lead in his race against President Tru
man, and he is being helped, too, by the vote
which Henry A. Wallace, the Progressive Party
candidate, is expected to poll. These Wallace
votes will be taken in overwhelming number
from the voters who ordinarily would support
Hhe Democratic ticket. If Mr. Wallace gets
40,000 or 50,000 votes, it is obvious what ha
will do to the Truman-Barkley ticket. Tha
Wallace vote is falling off, however.
The CIO leaders have not given up. They
point to a large registration—for which they
claim credit—and murmur that if the vote
comes out in large numbers they still will have
a chance to keep Michigan in the Democratic
column.
Their great difficulty lies in the fact that
there is no such enthusiasm among the Work
ers for Mr. Truman as there was for Franklin
Delano Roosevelt. It is difficult to stir them
up. The AFL is to all intents and purposes
working hard for Mr. Truman, too. The an
tagonism between the two great unions, how
ever, is maktng itself felt in some parts of
the State, and regular Democrats resent the
CIO’s taking command.
Wallace Strong in Detroit.
Wayne County (Detroit) is the Democratic
stronghold which gave Mr. Roosevelt a lead
of 238,000 votes in the election four years ago.
Mr. Wallace’s greatest strength in the State
lies in Detroit. In addition, there is reason to
believe that Mr. Truman’s lead there—even
with Mr. Wallace out of the race—would be
materially reduced as compared to that of Mr.
Roosevelt.
Mr. Dewey, although he is Governor of New
York, is a native of Michigan, and this does
him no harm' in his race here. He is the
Michigan boy who made good. With him at
the head of the ticket this year, it is expected
Republican congressional and State candidates
will ride along to victory, too.
Senator Ferguson, known before his election
to the Senate as the one-man grand Jury who
cleaned up Detroit—he was a Judge at the
time—undoubtedly will win under his own
power in the senatorial race. His Democratic
opponent is former Representative Frank Hook,
who served five terms in the House. Mr. Hook
represented the 12th district in the upper
peninsula, the first Democrat to be elected
from that district. He was a 100 per cent
New Dealer and made himself solid with or
ganized labor. Senator Ferguson, howeyer, has
continued to gain in stature as the chief Re
publican inquisitor in the Republican Eight
ieth Congress, and he is expected to win easily.
Democratic hopes in Michigan center largely
ground the party’s candidate for Governor—
G. Mennen Williams. Mr. Williams is a grad
uate of Princeton in the class of 1933 and of
the law school at Michigan University three
years later. During the last war he served
in the Navy, finally as lieutenant commander,
with 10 battle stars to his credit. He was
aboard four of the large aircraft carriers. He
has been a Democratic member of the Liquor
Control Commission, Assistant Attorney Gen
eral of Michigan, and in thd years 1939-40 a
special assistant to the Attorney General in
Washington. _ j;
Sigler Victory Seen.
Mr. Williams is pitted against Gov. Kim Sig
ler, who was elected in 1946 by a big votk.
Gov. Sigler will win again, it is expected, not
withstanding certain ructions in the Republi
can Party stemming in part from the enmity
which Attorney General Eugene Black has for
Gov. Sigler. Mr. Black was responsible in the
first instance for the grand Jury investigations
of contributions made to the Republican cam
paign funds by automobile dealers in Michi
gan. The claim is that the dealers were shaken
down for financial aid, and that some of the
contributions were made by corporations, in
violation of the Federal Corrupt Practices Act.
Four persons have already been indicted in
connection with contributions made to the Re
publican 1946 campaign. The Democrats are
making as much as they can of the scandal as
an issue. t
The 1946 elections left the Democrats with
only three members of the House of Repre:enta
tives from Michigan to fourteen for the Re
publicans. The Republicans will probably hold
nearly all of these—perhaps all—although there
are hard fights in a couple of the Detroit dis
tricts. The only Republican incumbent who
fell by the wayside in the primary elections is
Representative Jonkman of the Grand Rapids
district. He was defeated by Gerald R. Ford,
Jr., war veteran, a former star football player
at the University of Michigan who later studied
law at Yale and was an assistant football coach
there. Ford will be elected easily. The CIO
and the AFL indorsed Mr. Ford in the primary
campaign—in order to strike at Representa
tive Jonkman. They are taking no stand in
the present contest, however.

Q. Has the new state of Israel adopted an
official language?—B. W. T.
A. The official language of Israel is Hebrew.
Formerly Palestine had three official lan
guages, English, Hebrew and Arabic.
Q. What is done with the money paid for
admission to the Olympic games?—N. E. B.
A. The Amateur Athletic Union of the
United States says that the money paid for
admission to the Olympic games by spectators
is the property of the Olympic Organizing
Committee—for 1948 the British Organizing
Committee—but must be devoted to the further
promotion of amateur sport. The competing
countries do not share in the receipts or receive
any financial help toward their expenses.
Q. What is the oldest capital city in the
United States?—L. L.
A. Santa Fe, New Mexico, founded in the
winter of 1609-10 by the Spanish Governor
Pedro de Peralta, is the Nation’s oldest seat
of government.__
Andrea in Blue
The six-year-old in chiffon smelling sweet
With lavender invades my study, sure
Of praise, on pretty tiptoe poised to greet
This iron-gray sailor with a kiss. J hold
Her close, thank God He-lets these arms
immure
Her straight slim pride, my lamb within
the fold!
This moment 1 recall the cobalt sea
Off Malta, Persian faience, porcelain
That -seems to float on air, dreams lost
to me
Yet never far, now imminent and fair
As her blue eyes, and humbly once again,
i Touch Ups in fealty to noonday hair.
JOHN ACKERSON.
