C—4 SUNDAY. Octobar 31, 1948
Journey s end
There will be few lamentations at the
passing of the 1948 political campaign.
Instead, most people will feel a sense of
relief that at last it is over.
v For one thing, it has not been a cam
paign which has provided either an ex
citing or an edifying spectacle. The
’political speech seems to be losing its
charm. Perhaps we are a Nation which
is coming of age, or it may be that the
new role of the United States as the fore
most world power, and our growing aware
ness of the real responsibilities which ac
company that role, tend to make us more
Indifferent than formerly to the things
political candidates say when seeking
office. In any event the last of the major
speeches has been made. The torrent of
words has subsided, and there is little point
in reviewing the things that were said or
left unsaid. If there is any one in the
United States who can read or listen, and
who still does not know what the candi
dates have said, he will not be interested
in any resume at this eleventh political
hour.
There may be more purpose in trying to
appraise the effects of the campaign. If
political oratory can divide the people of
the United States, if political appeals can
set one group against another, then there
may be reason to believe that the campaign
has hurt us. But any such assumption
would be a dubious one. For the political
speech, when it takes the form of some of
those delivered by the President, or by Mr.
Wallace, or Governor Thurmond, is de
signed not so much to create division as
to capitalize on division that already exists.
J3ometimes this strategy is successful in
the vote-getting sense. But its effect is
likely to be a temporary one. When an
election is over the American people tend
to get back about where they were before
the speechmaking started, and that prob
ably will be as true this time—so far as the
speeches are concerned—as it has been in
the past.
It is possible, however, perhaps even
probable, that a by-product of this cam
paign, having nothing to do with the
speeches, will be a permanent realignment
#f our political structure.
No such change is apt to come from the
Wallace movement. Whatever chance
♦here may have been of a lasting third
party emerging from that quarter seems
to have evaporated, possibly because Mr.
Wallace has talked his cause to death.
Nor is there much reason to look for any
new alignment in the Republican Party.
Xhe change, if there is going to be a
change, will come in the future relation
ship between the Democratic Party and
the South. There are two schools of
thought on this, one holding that the left
wingers will fall away after the expected
defeat of President Truman and that the
old-line Democrats will again climb back
In the saddle. But there is Just as much, if
not more, reason to believe that the pendu
lum will swing the other way, that the
national Democratic Party will be forced
itill farther to the left by the pressure of
political realities. If that happens, if the
future emphasis is to be on a party that
appeals primarily to the labor vote, the
Jewish vote and especially the Negro vote,
ft is hard to see how the Southern Demo
crats will find an acceptable place in it.
And if they cannot return to the
Democratic Party they have no feasible
alternative but to attempt to organize a
permanent third party, built initially at
least around the Thurmond movement. To
speculate on the likelihood of this cannot
be much more than guesswork. But it is
reasonable to believe that if any lasting
change comes out of this election it will
fee along these lines.
As for the election itself, the only real
area of doubt seems to lie in the Senate
race. Unless all of the signs are wrong,
Governor Dewey will be elected President
and will have a safely Republican House.
But the Democrats are very hopeful that
they will be able to take over control of
the Senate.
It will be extremely unfortunate if it
Works out that way. Governor Dewey, if
he is going to be chosen President, ought
td start out with a Congress controlled by
his own party. He ought to have this op
portunity to put into effect whatever pro
gram he may have, and the political lineup
ought to be such that responsibility for
success or failure can be clearly fixed. A
Democratic Senate and a Republican
President could be expected to produce no
other result than to muddy the waters
and weaken our political effectiveness when
it is imperative that we be strong.
Reading by Ear
ilt is gratifying to learn that science is
not too busy with other things these days
to devote attention to the problem of de
mising new aids for the blind. The latest
fj'dpeful news for those who cannot see
Sqmes from New York, where Radio Cor
poration engineers have demonstrated a
remarkable device that enables the blind
to read by ear rather than by touch. The
device, based on the principle of the
electronic “eye,” scans the printed letters
and translates them, by actual reproduc
tion of the human voice, into sound. Thus,
when the “eye” passes across the letter A,
an electronic impulse activates a selector
mechanism which causes a recording tape
to announce the letter by name. Each
letter causes the machine to respond with
the correct letter, by voice.
Unfortunately, the machine is so com
plicated and expensive at present as to
preclude its use by individuals. However,
further research is planned. This may
lead at least to a better and cheaper in
strument capable of being installed in li
braries or institutions for the blind, for
general use by those who can hear but can
not see. Such a revolutionary aid would
be a boon to the blind, contributing not
only to their pleasure but to their earn
ing powers. The possibilities are suffi
ciently intriguing to warrant intensive
efforts to perfect a more practicable ver
sion of the laboratory-tested model. The
time may come when reading by ear will
replace reading by touch for all the blind
whose sense of hearing remains unim
paired.
Politics and Palestine
The world has now witnessed another
flip,-flop in America’s Palestine policy. Last
Thursday, everybody in Paris, including
ouf own delegation, had no doubt that we
would support the Chinese-British resolu
tion to create a Security Council subcom
mittee to study the possibility of imposing
sanctions on Israel if it refused to with
draw its forces from the Negeb. The next
day, however, the situation was reversed,
apparently on the strength of a message
received from President Truman. As of
the moment, the net result is that con
fusion reigns, with nobody—again includ
ing our own delegation—seeming to know
exactly where we stand.
This is all rather typical. Under Presi
dent Truman our policy on Palestine has
been made up of an unedifying series of
on-again-off-again decisions in which the
State Department has found itself obliged
to perform somersaults before the eyes of
the world in keeping with directives from
the White House. Thus, last November,
against the counsel of a large and well
informed body of opinion in the depart
ment, the United States voted for parti
tioning the Holy Land between the Arabs
and Jews. The following April, it reversed
itself and came out in favor of an unreal
istic proposal for a temporary U. N. trustee
ship over the country. A month later, with
a suddenness that took everybody by sur
prise, Mr. Truman extended diplomatic
recognition to Israel on a provisional, de
facto basis. Then, within the past month,
after Secretary Marshall had indorsed it—
presumably with his approval—the Presi
dent said we would not support the Berna
dotte plan for a revision of the boundaries
previously recommended for the new
Jewish state. Our latest switch is thus in
character with the record as a whole.
Of course, in fairness to the President,
it must be said that the Palestine problem
is so enormously complex that it makes
decisions difficult and uncertain. That
-consideration to one side, however, and
wholly apart from the merit or lack of
merit of some of our reversals, the fact
remains that no nation in the world has
dealt with this issue as skittishly or as
unpredictably as our own. As the consti
tutional fountainhead of our foreign policy,
Mr. Truman is responsible for that. It
would be naive, moreover, to suppose that
in dealing with the matter, in the midst
of a presidential election year, he has not
had in mind—just as Governor Dewey has
had in mind—the large and very important
Jewish vote in the pivotal 8tate of New
York.
In other words, stated bluntly, domestic
politics—and not simply the difficulties
and uncertainties of the problem itself-—
has played a key part in making our
approach to the explosive Palestine ques
tion look like anything but good and high
minded statesmanship. Certainly, when
our foreign policy is conducted in a manner
of that sort, we must expect serious injury
to our prestige abroad. Now that the elec
tion is almost over, one can only hope—
perhaps forlornly—that there will be an
end thereafter to all such American gyra
tions in the councils of the world.

Moscow Eyes Japan
Soviet Ambassador Panyushkin’s com
plaint against certain alleged actions of
General Douglas MacArthur and our occu
pational authorities in Japan is, by itself,
neither novel nor especially newsworthy.
Other complaints and allegations have
been made from time to time, all of them
countered by our State Department as
equally unfounded. This latest Soviet
action does, however, suggest that Moscow
may be preparing a diplomatic initiative
toward Japan similar to those which it
appears to be launching in Korea and Ger
many. The essence of this pattern seems
to be an attempt to end military occupa
tions by the Western Powers under condi
tions which would favor Soviet aims.
Mpscow has never liked the terms on
which Japan was occupied by the victors
in the last war. It tried hard to muscle in
on the occupational setup in Japan on the
lines of the Potsdam agreement toward
Germany. Rebuffed in this, Moscow has
endeavored to throw sand in the well-oiled
gears of Japanese recovery by various
means, including encouragement of the
small but ebullient Japanese Communist
Party. It should be remembered that
Soviet Russia has a seat on the eleven
nation Far Eastern Commission which ex
ercises an over-all supervisory role toward
the military occupation of Japan under
the terms of the armistice agreement, al
beit the occupation itself Is virtually an
American affair. That commission includes
representatives of all the nations with
which Japan was officially at war, covering
states like Australia, New Zealand and the
Netherlands as well as the major powers.
And it is to that body that Panyushkin
addressed himself.
Moscow’s only success thus far has been
the negative one of blocking the negotia
tion of a peace treaty for Japan. General
MacArthur has repeatedly voiced the de
sirability of such a settlement, and last
year our Government took the initiative
in proposing a peace conference on the
full eleven-nation basis. To this, how
ever, Moscow demurred. It insisted that
peace making for Japan be done solely by
the Council of Foreign Ministers, restricted
to the five “Great Powers”—America,
Hfttain, China, France and SoviefARussia.
1« proposal would not only hawfrozen
out the lesser victors but would have en
abled Moscow to exercise a veto under a
unanimity rule. So the Russian plan was
emphatically rejected. Yet, without Rus
sian participation, a peace settlement with
Japan was deemed impracticable.
Unlike the German situation, however,
this deadlock was not unfavorable to us.
In Japan we control the situation and can
mould Japanese recovery much as we see
fit. And the success of our recovery pro
gram is increasingly repugnant to Moscow.
Hence, it is by no means unlikely that
Soviet diplomacy may be preparing to
raise the Japanese issue once more, if
only for diversionary and propagandist
purposes. Such an initiative would be
logically linked with Soviet moves in
Korea and with the situation in China,
where the Communists appear to be get
ting the upper hand. Under these circum
stances, Moscow’s attitude toward Japan
will bear watching.
Stalin and the Truth
Swift and unqualified denials by France,
Britain, the United States and spokesmen
for the six “neutral” members of the Se
curity Council leave no room for doubt
that Prime Minister Stalin’s “interview”
with Pravda has done violence to the facts
of history.
The facts have to do with agreements
regarding the lifting of the Berlin block
ade. Mr. Stalin has accused Britain and
the United States of having violated the
one that was reached in Moscow on August
30. He has accused them, too, of rendering
“null and void” another which he claims
was drawn up during the recent mediation
efforts in the Security Council.
But if documentation means anything,
there cannot be the slightest doubt about
what happened to the August 30 agree
ment. For the record—particularly as set
forth in.the American “White Paper” a few
weeks ago—offers abundant proof that
that agreement was broken not by Brit
ain and the United States, but by Marshal
Sokolovsky, the Soviet military governor
in Berlin, who torpedoed it just as soon as
the time came to make it effective. As for
the later “agreed draft decision” men
tioned by Mr. Stalin, we have the word
of all other parties concerned, including
the six “neutral” Council members who
attempted mediation, that no such under
standing ever existed.
In view of all this, it must be said of Mr.
Stalin that either he is guilty of resorting
deliberately to brazen falsehoods or he is
the innocent victim of misinformation
handed him by his subordinates. If the
latter is true—which is quite possible,
especially in a bureaucracy like the Soviet
dictatorship’s—then he had better find
out who among his underlings is responsi
ble for it. If he does not, he may learn to
his sorrow one of these days that some
lower-echelon cabal has fed him lies ca
pable of plunging Russia and the world
into war.
That said, it can only be added that if
Mr. Stalin is himself responsible for these
grave falsehoods, he would be well-advised
to give careful thought to the history of
Herr Hitler and others who once thought
it smart to employ the colossal lie as an
instrument of national policy.
A Precious Diary
In a ramshackle camp at Kill Devil Hill
on the coast of North Carolina on the
evening of December 17, 1903, a man sat
writing in a diary. He was not the sort
of man that people would notice on the
street. Back in Dayton, Ohio, his family
was known as “just plain folks like every
body else." When a lad he had started
a printing business. Later in partnership
with his brother Wilbur he had run a
bicycle shop. In 1896 the death of Gustav
JLilienthal, a German engineer who had
tried to imitate the wing movements of
birds, led the pair to speculate on the
feasibility of flying. Three years later they
built some model gliders. In September,
1900, they successfully operated one of
these on a narrow strip of sand dividing
Albemarle Sound from the Atlantic.
Now they had a new contraption, weigh
ing 750 pounds. Storms delayed the tryout.
But finally everything was ready, including
the weather. The result as the original
aviator reported it in his journal reads:
When* we got up .a wind of between 20 and
25 miles was blowing from the north. We got
the machine out early and put out the signal
for the men at the station. Before we were
quite ready, John T. Daniels, W. 8. Dough,
A. D. Etheridge, W. C. Brinkley of Manteo
and Johnny Moore of Nags Head arrived.
After running the engine and propellers a
few minutes to get them in working order, I
got in the machine at 10:35 for the first trial.
The wind according to our anemometers at
this time was blowing a little over 20 miles
(corrected) 27 miles according to the Govern
ment anemometer at Kitty Hawk. On stop
ping the rope the machine started off increasing
speed to probably 7 or 8 miles. The machine
lifted from, the track just as it was entering
the fourth mile. Mr. Daniels took a picture
just as it left the tracks.
Such was Orville Wright’s impression of
the beginning of modern aeronautics—
nothing flowery, nothing pretentious; just
a simple narrative, characteristic of an
ordinary chap who knew what he was
doing and was content to be nothing more
than he was. The journal in which he
wrote is one of the treasures of the Library
of Congress. Shortly the plane which first
proved that heavier-than-air aviation was
practicable will be installed in the Smith
sonian Institution. It is on its way back
from England, where for many years it
was an object of pilgrimage in the British
Museum.
Regarding the candidates, the great
John L. Lewis has spoken, and so has
Harold Ickes. Since, however, these strong
individuals cancel each other vout, the issue
must be decided by us groundlings.
Among the loot offered by a give-away
show is a popcorn machine with which you
can set up in business. If, in addition, you
can find neighbors who will pay to see
your home movies, you’re a showman.
French Reds, calling on the workers of
the world to strike, don’t mean Russians,
as there is no room for this foolishness
under Moscow’s equivalent of the Taft
Hartley Act.
One of the Navy’s little newspaper's
thinks the chicken crosses the road for
fowl purposes—a nifty which must go back
at least to the battle of Mobile Bay.
La Belle France is bluntly told to work
or perish, a choice as unpalatable as that
other grim tossup, “Wallace or war.”

Spires of the Spirit
On Going to the Polling Places
A Message in Advance of a National Election Day
Stresses the Duties of Individuals
By the Rev. Frederick Brown Harris, D. D., Litt. D.
One or the most sublime sights on this tom
and tortured globe is America going to the
polls. One of the most disturbing things in
the life of the Republic is that millions who
have the right to vote,
a right bought for
them at crimson cost,
betray and deny their
birthright by failure
to exercise it. With
the future at stake,
when the votes are
counted they do not
count.
As election day ap-
proaches, every man
and woman worthy
to be called American
is keenly aware of
the price with which
this privilege or untrammeiea irancnise was
purchased. Amid tinted trees, under the bluest
of skies, in the warm, fall sunlight at Arling
ton less than a week from election, 20 flag
draped caskets in two mournful rows, at wait
ing graves, were mute reminders of the last
full measure of devotion paid by boys who
bravely died far from home, in tangled jungle
and on lonely isles. They fell that this kingly
scepter never might be snatched by tyranny
from the hand of the common man. As the
volleys were fired and taps sounded with the
low undertone of mothers’ sobs, we knew, alas,
tha# again the ruthless tyranny of the police
state is on the march, prostituting the sacred
name of democracy, making so-called elections
but the rubber stamp of a dictator’s will. But
in our blessed land of the free on the Tuesday
after the first Monday in November, turning
from imposing National and State capitals, our
reverent gaze is on the unadorned yet sacred
booths set up across the continent in which,
free from sinister, spying eyes, every man is
alone with his own conscience, none daring to
molest or to make afraid.

But not only when as free men we in secret
mark the ballot and record our choice of pol
icies and leaders do we register a vote. Every
day, every one of us is voting. Every dawn puts
a ballot in our hands. We choose, and make
our choices felt. In everything that has to do
with private and public life we constantly are
casting our ballot. The impact ql our per
sonality, the weight of our influence is either
a part of the problem or a part of the solution
of all that relates to our ailing social order.
With all the matters up for decision in the
commonwealth of humanity, every man must
face the question, what am I voting for with
my life? For instance, Robert Browning pic
tures all the Beatitudes of Christianity. Prac
ticed, he declares, they will solve every question
in the world and out of it. But the pertinent
question asked by that poet-prophet is, Does
this'Christianity have your vote to be true if
it can? Ah, that is the vital question with
regard to everything good and pure and true
and excellent: Does it have your vote to be
true if it can?
We are living in a world baffled, bored and
betrayed by the things in which it has put its
trust. The gods currently worshiped among
us mostly resemble the deities once decreed by
the Roman Senate, but they now are created
by Hollywood. What are these gods? Physical
strength, social prestige, bodily pleasure, po
litical power, financial success—these things
made pre-eminent have brought us to the very
edge of chaos and catastrophe. They have
fashioned a noisy materialism and a coarsen
ing of mental life. They have cultivated a
secularism that denies that man is born to
serve the spirit. In one area^of life after an
other these false gods have emptied the uni
verse of divine meaning and substituted human
ingenuity. Man has been trained to live in
the cellar of his being and to make that the
living room of his house.

The world is entering upon a period of crisis
and threatened collapse, where we are being
forced to take sides. Gen. MacArthur’s words
from the surrender deck in Tokyo Harbor
ought to be written on the doorposts of all our
houses: “We have been given one more
chance. The issue now is of the spirit, rather
than the flesh. It is spiritual and theological.”
In this decisive day of the “one more
chance,” what is your vote? On the ballot in
every man’s hand are those solemn words of
Scripture: “I have set before you life and
death, the blessing and the curse. Choose life,
then, that you and your children may live.”
Most Americans readily would agree to the
assertion of an increasing chorus of thinkers
in all realms, that only a return to true religion
can keep us from impending doom. Even so,
believing that, millions do not vote.
"But my vote would make no difference!”
Listen to Bonaro Overstreet:
“You say the little efforts that I make will do
no good;
They never will prevail to tip the heavy scale
Where justice hangs in balance.
I do not think I ever thought they would;
But I am prejudiced beyond debate
In favor of my right to choose which side
Shall feel the stubborn ounces of my weight.”
That is your vote—the stubborn ounces of
your weight. Those stubborn ounces count.

(By the World Staff of the Associated Press.)
A Secret Service detail will join Gov. Thomas
E. Dewey Tuesday night if and when—but not
before—voting returns make it apparent he
has been elected President. The service has
had the duty of protecting Presidents-elect as
well as Presidents ever since 1913. It has never
lost one. Before it took over, assassins killed
three Presidents in a 36-year span. The service
doesn't make any pre-election arrangements
with possible winners. It simply has its detail
ready to move in without previous announce
ment. Members of the detail that walked in
unannounced on Franklin D. Roosevelt—about
10 o'clock the night he was first elected—said
Mrs. Roosevelt met them and received them
very courteously. Size and membership of the
detail that would take over on Mr. Dewey is
kept secret for security reasons. It is a good
bet it would be made up chiefly of men from
the service’s New York City office. If Mr.
Dewey is elected the service will have to keep
watch on both him and President Truman, and
their families, until Mr. Truman goes out of
office January 20.

War Dead
PERTH, Australia.—United States Army
divers are preparing to search Darwin Harbor
for the United States destroyer Peary, sunk in
an air raid February 18, 1942. This is a major
task in the Army's job of returning American
war dead to U. 8. soil.

NEW YORK.—A leading producer of acety
salicylic acid has upped the price 3 cents
a pound. This may mean higher prices for
aspirin.
Atlantic Pact
National resources experts are attempting
to figure out what it would cost us if the
United States joined with Western European
Powers in a military “Atlantic pact.” They
feel it might mean one or all of the following:
No more tax cuts, an actual increase in taxes,
allocation of the most important materials.
Small Planes
Production of personal aircraft (carrying up
to four persons) fell during the first three
quarters of this year. Compared to the same
period last year, figures were 13,775 and 5,990;
dollar value, $45,000,000 and $23,400,000.
Latin Woes
While there is no apparent tieup among the
disorders striking several Latin American
countries just now, prospects are that Latin
America is in for a long period of political and
economic unrest. Here are current highspots
which to United States experts add up to long
term ferment: Mexico is in serious financial
trouble. Argentina is heading for it. Panama
has just put down a revolt. So have Paraguay
and Bolivia. And Peru is in the throes of its
second within a month. Chile and Brazil are
rounding up Communist agents. Several Central
American countries are accusing one another
of plotting agatyist them. Bloody clashes con
tinue between partisans- of Colombian political
parties.
Flying Briefs
Newest homey touch in Air Force fighter
planes: Electric pop-out cigarette lighters in
cockpits. Air Force is studying emergency
hatch requirements in troop carrier planes.
A tiny budget has squeezed naval fighter train
ing to the point that planes get only enough
fuel fqr one hour’s flight a week.

Lt. Gen. Robert L. Eichelberger, commander
of occupation forces in Japan for three years,
was asked whether Communist revolts would
break out there if United States troops with
drew before a strong Japanese police force
were set up. He replied emphatically: "It not
only could happen, but it would happen.’’
Other authorities on Japan share his opinion.
It looks like%a long time before occupation
troops will be pulled out of Germany—although
Gen. Sir Brian Robertson, the British military
governor, suggested it the other day as “the
only wfey to solve the German problem.” Au
thoritiflFhere say the United States would like
to see Russian troops out of Eastern Germany.
But we would consent to a general withdrawal
only on condition that German elections be
held under some sort of international super
visiori. Russia has refused that in Korea.
Fifty Years Ago
The Star of October 21, 1898, told an inter
esting story of the vagrant habits of the Right
Rev. Leighton Coleman, Bishop of
Tromp Delaware, whose “genial humanity
Bishop put him on a friendly footing with
all schools and classes, making for
him a large place in the hearts of the people.”
Dressed in a long mackintosh, heavy shoes and
a slouch hat and carrying a staff, the church
man-historian roamed over the mountains and
through the valleys of different States, sleep
ing in farmhouses or bams, paying his way by
repairing clocks and umbrellas. “He traveled
in all sorts of weather over all kinds of roads,"
the average day's journey being about 20 miles.
Few, if any, of the persons he encountered on
his tramps had any idea that he was a prince
of the church, a profound scholar and a de
voted practical philanthropist.

Four columns of closely printed type were
used in The Star for October 24, 1898, to report
the unveiling of the Peace Cross
Peace Cross in the grounds of Washington
Unveiled Cathedral the previous day. All
the delegates, clerical and lay,
attending the General Convention of the
Protestant Episcopal Church in America were
present at the ceremony. Just before the start
of the program President McKinley arrived on
the scene. The Right Rev. Henry Yates Sat
terlee opened the sacred proceedings by saying:
“Your excellency, reverend fathers and
brothers, 1 welcome you to the first service
of the Cathedral of Sts. Peter and Paul.”
Deputy Warden J. B. Nourse actually uncov
ered the monument. The principal address of
the occasion was delivered by the Right Rev.
William Croswell Doane, Bishop of Albany. Ar
rangements for the dedication were in charge
of the Rev. Alfred Harding, destined to be Dr.
Satterlee’s successor as second bishop of the
diocese. The cross was designed by Robert
William Gibson, who also was responsible for
the design of the original building of the Na
tional Cathedral School for Girls at Wisconsin
avenue and Woodley road.
Gen. Lord Kitchener, announced The
Star for October 29, 1898, had returned to
London and was being over
Kitchener in whelmed with social invitations.
England He also had been asked by two
s publishers to write a book “and
an American agent has been trying to get
him to consent to a lecturing tour in the
United States.’’ But “the commander of the
Anglo-Egyptian forces had declined all these
propositions.” The dispatch continued: “The
statement that Gen. Kitchener served in the
French Army during the war of 1870-71 was
confirmed this week by an interview with his
stepmother at Dinan. She said that he had
just received his commission in the British
Army and went to France on a holiday to visit
his father who had settled at Dinan. Young
Kitchener, in spite of the opposition of his

commission, joined the Dinan Mobiles and set
out against the Germans. At Laval he ascended
in a balloon to reconnoiter the enemy’s posi
tion and caught a severe attack 'of pleurisy,
which compelled him to return to Dinan and
cut short his career in the French Army.” Lord
Kitchener, drowned in the sinking of the
Hampshire, June 5, 1916, was one of the cas
ualties of World War I.

Board Sets New Policy
On Picket Line Tactics
Ruling on Union Responsibility for
Members’ Violence Stirs Labor
By Gerard D. Reilly
The National Labor Relations Board’s im
portant decision on the subject of union
responsibility for the violent acts of strik
ers and pickets is receiving careful analysis
in industrial relations circles. This opinion
was rendered by, the board last week in
the Sunset Line Twine case, in which a trial
examiner’s report dismissing a complaint
against the CIO Longshoremen’s Union was
reversed.
This was the first instance in which the
board has construed the new provision in the
Taft-Hartley Act which makes it an unfair
labor practice for labor organizations or their
agents to “restrain” or “coerce” employes. The
insertion of this provision in the act was one
of the most controversial issues raised in the
Senate when it was offered in the form of a
floor amendment to the committee bill by Sen
ator Joseph H. Ball of Minnesota, as Senator
Wayne Morse of Oregon had persuaded the
committee to omit it.
Some years previously the Senate had re
jected a similar amendment to the Wagner Act
on the theory that since mass picketing and
violence amounted to a breach of the peace,
such conduct was a matter for local police reg
ulation rather than congressional action.
History had demonstrated the fallacy of this
assumption. In virtually every major strike in
recent years in which the companies involved
attempted to operate their plants, violence on
the picket line was a normal incident. The
reluctance or inability on the part of local
police forces to preserve order became so no
torious that in numerous instances striking
unions would have the effrontery to issue passes
which entitled maintenance or clerical person
nel to enter a plant during a strike and re
garded the issuance of such .documents as a
generous concession on their part.
The result was that until the Wagner Act was
amended H had come to be almost taken for
granted that employes exercising their statu
tory right to refrain from engaging in con
certed activity had no redress if they were
reckless enough to attempt to cross a picket
line. By 1947 this fact was so well known that
it is difficult to understand the opposition which
the Ball amendment excited Unless its oppo
nents were of the belief that labor unions, un
like any other group of private citizens, have
a right to use physical violence to advance their
objectives in economic controversies.

The first test of the effectiveness of the new
statute occurred early last fall when Local No.
6 of the Longshoremen's Union called a wage
strike at a small company engaged in the
manufacture of fishing line. The plant, situ
ated in a town about 40 miles north of San
Francisco, employs about 100 persons, most of
them women. After the strike had been going
on for a few days the plant reopened and four
girls went back to work.
When they left the plant they were jeered
at by the pickets, called obscene names and
followed by pickets in automobiles who threat
ened them with violence. On later occasions
the business agent of the local threatened one
of the nonstriking employes and exchanged
blows with another. Subsequently, as more
employes started to return to the plant, large
crowds of people led by the union officers and
pickets blocked the driveway to the factory
parking lot and would have effectively pre
vented any cart from entering had it not been
for the intervention of the local police.
The company filed a charge, whereupon the
regional office, after an expeditious investiga
tion, issued a complaint. The union made no
serious attempt to dispute the facts which
have been summarized here, but nevertheless
Trial Examiner C. W. Whittemore, to the great
surprise of all persons familiar with the case,
found that'there had been no violation of the
act. His findings were issued last January and
General Counsel Robert N. Denham immedi
ately filed exceptions.
Except for holding that the hurling of epi
thets at the girl workers did not constitute
“restraint or coercion,” the majority opinion
of the board supported the general counsel’s
position in every major respect. Both the local
and the international were held responsible for
the illegal behavior of the pickets and hence
guilty of unfair labor practices.
Two aspects of the decision are somewhat
questionable, however. In finding that the
pickets had not violated the act by jeering and
shouting obscene terms at the young women
who resumed their employment the board held
that this verbal conduct was privileged under
the provision of the act which states:
“The expression of any views, argument or
opinion, or the dissemination thereof, whether
in written, printed, graphic or visual form, shall
not constitute an unfair labor prac
tice if such expression contains no threat
of reprisal or force or promise of benefit.
WWW*
It Is clear that all that this language at
tempted to do was to protect the right of em
ployers and unions to make whatever argu
ments they saw fit as long as they stopped
short of threats or bribes. But the calling of
such names as would ordinarily lead to breach
of the peace hardly falls under the category
of the expression of “views, arguments or
opinions.” The board itself has recognized this
in cases where the alleged restraint has con
sisted of an employer interrogating an em
ploye about his union membership.
The other aspect of the case was the debate
between the majority and the minority over
the weight which should be given to an admis
sion in the answer filed by the international
union. The minority, consisting of Chairman
Paul M. Herzog and Commissioner John M.
Houston, had fully agreed with the majority
as to the responsibility of the local for the
illegal conduct of the picket line, but found
the evidence of the international’s participa
tion too meager to impute responsibility to it.
The majority relied in part on the fact that the
international in its answer had admitted that
it was a co-sponsor with the local of the strike
—a ground characterized by the majority as
overreliance on the technical niceties of
pleading.
In answering this contention, the majority
properly pointed out that this admission re
lieved the general counsel from the necessity
for putting in any evidence to show the joint
responsibility of the international as well a*
the local, but they went on to say "if the in
ternational had offered evidence to show that
it was not a co-sponsor of this strike the mem
bers of the board majority would not disre
gard that evidence.”
This was an unfortunate suggestion. The
requirement that an answer be filed stems
from the Administrative Procedure Act as well
as the board’s regulations. The purpose of the
requirement was to eliminate the taking of
unnecessary evidence, and in view of the tre
mendous backlog of Labor Board cases, no pre
trial device which is intended to narrow the
scope of the issues and hasten a final decision
should be lightly disregarded.__

disguised when they went out to do mis
chief. They did succeed, however, in landing

James Fay, 49, Dies;
Representative From
New York for 2 Terms
Firmer Representative James H.
:Fay, Democrat, of New York died
early today in New York City after
an illness of three months. Mr. Fay,
49, represented
the 16th New
York district
from 1939 t o
1941 and from
1943 to 1945. At
the time of his
death he oper
ated an insur
ance and adver
tising agency.
Born in New
York City, Mr.
Pay was edu
cated there and
received his de
gree of law at
the Brooklyn j»«*m h. Far.
Law School in 1929. He served
with the 165th Infantry in World
War I, losing his left leg by shell
fire. Later he became trustee of
the World Association of Amps—
war veterans who lost either an
arm or a leg.
The loss of his leg. however, was
no drawback to the genial Mr. Fay,
who in September, 1938, defeatea
Representative John J. O'Connor,
then chairman of the House Rules
Committe. He won by 553 votes,
directing his campaign from a hos
pital bed. White House choice in
President Roosevelt’s Democratic
congresional "purge,” Mr. Roosevelt
was elated over his victory.
On December 31, 1942, Mr. Fay,
his wife and son and a relative were
rescued from their burning home in
New York by firemen, who carried j
them down 75-foot aerial ladders.
Mrs. Fay suffered burns on the j
hands and face and the others were
treated for smoke poisoning and
slight burns. A short circuit in
Christmas tree lights was blamed
for the blaze.
Prior to Mr. Fay's congressional
service, he was deputy commis
sioner of hospitals in New York j
An old-time Tammany man, he
later was appointed first deputy of
the third New York internal reve
nue district. At one time he was
secretary to the board of trustees of
Bellevue and allied hospitals.
Bar Group to Debate
Speedy Arraignments
Whether the law should be,
changed to require immediate ar
raignment of accused persons, will
be debated by the District Bar As
sociation at 8 p.m. Tuesday in the
I Mayflower Hotel.
The association will consider the
report of its civil rights committee,
which split seven to three on the
proposal. The majority recom
mended that arrested persons get
an immediate hearing, at any hour
of the day or night.

William McNair, 68,
Colorful Ex-Mayor of
Pittsburgh, Dies
ly the Associated Press
ST. LOUIS, Sept. 10.—An Inquest
will be conducted tomorrow in the
death of William N. McNair, 68, for
mer Mayor of Pittsburgh, who col
lapsed and died in Union Station
last night.
A dinner audience was waiting to
hear him and he had just put hfc
wife on a train for Houston, Tex.
Her train was 90 miles away when
she got the news, and, after waiting
two hours at Bismarck, Mo., she
came back on another train.
Apparently it was a heart attack
that killed the veteran Pennsyl
vania Democrat. He had made a
luncheon talk and had not been ill.
Mrs. McNair is flying «to Pitts
burgh this afternoon after joining
a daughter, Mrs. Robert Morrison,
who is flying from Houston.
Burial will be in Pittsburgh, but
the time has not been set.
Mr. McNair was elected mayor in
1933—the first Democrat to win the
office since 1905. In 1944 he was
elected to the Pennsylvania Legis
lature and currently was campaign
ing for another term.
A six-footer with a winning po
litical manner and a flair for pub
licity stunts, Mr. McNair was swept
into office with a plurality of 2,000
more votes than President Roose
velt received in Pittsburgh in the
1932 election.
One of his first actions delighted
his supporters and attracted na
tional attention. This was his an
nounced intention of setting up
offices in the lobby of the City Hall.
He soon abandoned this idea after
discovering the lobby would be too
cold and that it would interfere
with his working efficiency.
Another of his stunts was to offer
a bottle of whisky to Mrs. Ella
Boole, president of the Women's
Christiap Temperance Union. On
one occasion he spent an hour in
jail for ignoring a court ruling.
Admired by Zioncheck.
In an era of curious and outland
ish political behavior, Mr. McNair
more than held his own with such
publicity headliners as the late Rep
resentative Zioncheck, Democrat, of
Washington, who once went wading
in the pool of the sunken garden
at Rockefeller Center in New York.
Mr. Zioncheck became such an
admirer of the Pittsburgh Mayor
that he suggested him for Presi
dent. But Mr. McNair was as cool
to the idea as he was to Mr. Zion
check, whom he ignored when the
Representative visited Pittsburgh,
wrestled with a Turkish grappler
and gave Pittsburgh 12 hours of
wild fun-making.
Mayor McNair had a cure-all, he
thought, for city financial burdens,
something resembling the single tax
of Henry George,"but he never suc
ceeded in working it out. He re
signed on October 6, 1936, in the
midst of financial chaos resulting
from the City Council's refusal to
approve his appointee for city
treasurer.
Roper Suspends Poll;
Sees Dewey Victory
Republicans were warned yester
day by their national chairman not
to get overconfident because a poll
taker said victory in November was
so assured he planned to make no
more reports on the contest.
Elmo Roper, conductor of the for
tune survey, announced in the New
York Herald Tribune that the na
tional Republican ticket was so far
ahead that only a “political con
vulsion” could defeat Gov. Dewey.
Mr. Roper said he had decided
after years of study that a winner
“clinches his victory early in the
race” and that /presidential cam
paigns rarely alter the outcome.
He said he planned to make no
more resports on his presidential
surveys “unless something really in
teresting happens.”
Hugh Scott, chairman of the Re
publican National Committee, ex
pressed gratification at the Roper
statement, but added:
“Despite Mr. Roper’s announce
ment this is no time to relax
our efforts to build up the biggest
majority in history.
“While I have every confidence
that the Dewey-Warren ticket will
be elected, I never regard an elec
tion as over until election day. Over
confidence can lose an election.”

If Mr. Truman had all the breaks
and the Wallace candidacy were
withdrawn, the situation in States
like Minnesota, California, New
York and a few others, would be
close. In such case, if nearly all
the Wallace voters switched to Mr.
Truman, the latter’s re-election
would be within striking distance.
A total of 87 electoral votes might
be added to the 210 referred to
above, making the possible Truman
total 297. This, however, seems un
likely.
Gov. Dewey’s lead in a large bloc
of States with many electoral votes
is so substantial that if later figures
confirm the first results it will be
difficult to overcome it in the few
remaining weeks. It is now expected
that Gov. Dewey will win with at
least 304 electoral votes. He might
win 397 if he captures all of the
close States. Therefore, at this
point in the campaign, the result]
looks like Gov. Dewey by 304—397
electoral votes.
The strength pf the Dewey
Warren ticket lies all across the
northern tier of States. In the
populous eastern and central States,
some of which were formerly Roose
velt strongholds, the Republican ra
tios are significant. However, two
States in New England are at pres
ent close. The Wallace vote in Cali
fornia makes the Republican lead
definite. Washington and Oregon
apparently will also be Republican.
It should be emphasized that this
analysis is based upon the results
of a poll taken before Labor Day.
Since then, Mr. Truman has spoken
to huge fcrowds in Michigan and
has begun' a very intensive personal
campaign which may win him large
numbers of votes.

Dr. T. J. Le Blanc Dies;
Was Medical Professor
ly the Associated Press
CINCINNATI, Sept. 10. Dr.
Thomas John Le Blanc, 54, professor
of preventive medicine at the col
lege of Medicine, University of Cin
cinnati, die<J yesterday alter a long
illness. He was a native of Cheboy-1
gan, Mich., and spent his boyhood
in Sault Ste. Marie.
Dr. Le Blanc was decorated by the
governments of Japan and Mexico
for achievements in his profession.
He was head of the institute of hu
man biology at Tohoku Imperial
University, Senday, Japan, in 1927
29. He was given a gold medal by
Mexico for his work in yellow fever
control.
R. Frank Jarrett, 84, Dies;
Poet and Philanthropist
ly tha Associated Press
ASHEVILLE, N. C.. Sept 10.—R.
Frank Jarrett, 84. poet, innkeeper,
composer and grain speculator, died
today In a Dtllsboro hospital.
A prominent figure in the political
life of Western North Carolina for
more than half a century, Mr. Jar
rett was a widely-known philan
thropist. having contributed liberal
ly to Mars Hill, Wake Forest and
Brevard Colleges, the Oxford (Ma
sonic) Orphange, and Baptist
churches.
His best known work was the
poem. "Occoneechee, the Maid of
the Mystic Lake.” He published a
volume titled “Back Home and
Other Poems.”
Ernest H. Bradley Rites
To Be Held Tomorrow
Funeral services for Ernest H.
Bradley, sr„ 74, of 1222 B street S.E.,
a retired Government worker who
died Wednesday at his home, will be
held at 10:30 a.m. tomorrow at Lee
funeral home, Fourth street and
Massachusetts avenue N.E. Burial
will be in Congressional Cemetery.
A native of Washington, Mr.
Bradley was the son of the late Rev.
and Mrs. John R. Bradley. He was
educated in schools here.
In Government service for more
than 40 years, Mr. Bradley retired
in 1942. At that time he was ad
ministrative officer in the budget
and finance office of the Secretary
of Agriculture.
Despite his age, Mr. Bradley
studied Spanish after his retirement
and was an "ardent opera fan,”
members of his family said.
He is survived by his widow, Mrs.
Eleanora E. Bradley; a daughter,
Mrs. Harry A. Ockershausen: four
sons, Albert R., Ernest H. jr., R.
Alan, and Charles F. Bradley; two
sisters, Mrs. Mary Wright and Mrs.
Maude Dikeman, and a brother, J.
Thomas Bradley, all of Washington.

Capital Transit has estimated that
track changes would cost $250,000
if the underpass plan is adopted
and the Potomac Electric Power

intersection to break up the bottle
necks. One plan would set up double

diana avenue connecting Pennsyl
vania avenue tracks with route 40
car tracks at Fifth and D streets
N.W.
The other plan would eliminate

N.W. east of Ninth street includ

tween Independence avenue and C

VIENNA (/P). Employment in

Rifes Set Tomorrow
For Lloyd C. Shumac
Funeral cervices for Lloyd C
Shumac, assistant to the Roister
of the Treasury, will be held at 10
a.m. tomorrow in the Hines funeral
home, 2901 Fourteenth street N.W.
Mr. Shumac, who was 54, died
Wednesday in Mount Alto Hospital,
where he had been under treatment
since April.
He will be buried Sunday in his
home town, Meyersdale, Pa.
A veteran of World War I, during
which he served overseas with the
15th Engineers. Mr. Shumac Joined
the Treasury Department in 1919.
He worked in the Register's office
until his illness. In 1922, he was
appointed chief of the division of
destruction. He was named to head
the canceled securities division in
1942 and became asistant to the
Register in 1945.
Mr. Shumac was educated in
Meyersdale.
He lived with his wife. Mrs. Mar
garet R. Shumac. at 5019 James
town road, York Village, Md.
Also surviving are a step-daughter,
Mrs. James B. Whittaker of Green
Acres, Md.; Mr. Shumac's father,
Wilson J. Shumac of Meyersdale; a
sister, Mrs. Robert Kiem, also of
Meyersdale, and two step-grand
sons.
Reburial Services Today
For Lt. Alfred S. Hazard
Reburial services for First Lt. Al
fred S. Hazard, 25, who was killed
in a plane crash March 10, 1945.
in the Philippines, were to be held
at 2 p.m. today in Rock Creek
Cemetery..
Lt. Hazard, a native of Takoma
Park, Md., was graduated from Cen
tral High School and was attend
ing the University of Maryland
when he entered the Army in July,
1941. His mother, Mrs. Maude
Hazard, lives at 7333 Piney Branch
road, Takoma Park, Md.
An infantry officer with the 32d
Division, Lt. Hazard was killed when
the plane that was taking him to
a hospital on Leyte crashed into
a mountain. He had been wounded
on Luzon.
Surviving, besides his mother, is
a sister, Mrs. Edith Hazard Barth ol
the Piney Branch road address.

Faunce, 85, retired Norfolk and
Washington steamship captain, will
be held at 10 am. tomorrow in

Eleventh street SF. Burial will be

S.W.
Following his retirement as a cap
tain for the Norfolk and Washing
ton steamship line, he did work for
boat owners all along the waterfront,
painting and repairing their craft.

all done.. Did you raise egg
plant? If so, here’s a couple of
different ways to fix it.
SCALLOPED EGGPLANT
One large eggplant, 4 table
spoons butter or other fat, 1
green pepper, chopped, 1 small
onion, chopped, 1 quart canned
or chopped raw tomatoes, 2
teaspoons salt, pepper, 1 cup
bread crumbs. Pare the egg
plant and cut it into small,
even pieces. Melt 2 tablespoons
of the fat in a skillet, add the
green pepper and onion, and
cook for a few minutes. Add
the tomatoes, eggplant, salt and
pepper, cook for 10 minutes,
and place the mixture in a
shallow greased baking dish.
Melt the remaining fat in a
skillet, stir in the bread
crumbs, and sprinkle the
crumbs over the eggplant. Bake
in a moderate oven for 15
minutes, or until the egg plant
is tender and the crumbs are
browned.
STUFFED EGGPLANT
One large eggplant, 4 table
spoons melted butter or other
fat, 1 Mt cups bread crumbs, 1
small onion, finely chopped, 1
cup chopped string beans,
peas, or other left over veget
tables, 1 teaspoon salt, pepper.
Wash and cut the eggplant in
half lengthwise. Remove as
much of the white portion as
possible without breaking the
shell Cut the pulp into small
pieces, cook in a small quan
tity of hot water for 10 min
utes. or until tender and drain.
To the melted fat add the bread
crumbs, stir until well mixed
and reserve half of the crumbs
for the top. To the remaining
crumbs add the other ingre
dients and the egg plant pulp,
mix thoroughly, pile lightly in
to the egg plant shells, sprinkle
the top with crumbs, place in
a shallow baking dish, pour a
few tablespoons of hot water
around the shells to keep them
from sticking, and bake in a
moderate oven for 30 minutes.
Serve in the baking dish

Dear Mrs. Pease:
You need letters? Here is
one. The youngsters all in
school again. how very
quiet each day this first week
is! Don’t you find yourself
looking up the road long be
fore it’s time for them to come?
Mothers, those of you who
have youngsters beginning
school, here is an idea that
will bring joy to both the
youngsters and yourself. Not
only today, but tomorrow,
and the next tomorrow. It
could be done not only for
the beginners in school but
for any age child and it's
a fine keepsake and it costs
so little.
Get each child a scrap book.
Paste in it all pictures, school
invitations, awards, newspaper
clippings, party items, pro
grams, report cards, etc., in
fact everything of interest that
touches that child throughout
the years. Keep these scrap
books up each year. Work to
gether. Be sure to jot down
the names of persons in the
pictures. It’s strange, yes, un
believable, how one forgets
names, yes even faces, 30 years
1 later.
What beautiful reminis
censes these scrapbooks do
bring. My youngest's scrap
book of his first year con
tains: First grade picture.
school group picture, his
teachers' picture, the news
paper clippings of her mar
riage that year, picture of
each member of the family,
his sister's graduation pic
ture. newspaper clipping of
her commencement, banquet,
school activities, school hon
ors, invitations to parents to
patrons day, program, PTA.
picture of the first calf he
owned and himself, pictures
and items that touched him
throughout the year. his
birthday and Christmas
cards, report card, and item
about the first school pic
nic.
Ticklers By George
‘‘That the only luxury he’s got. His back itches.”
So on, each year through
grade school? What a book of
treasure today as he enters
high school. We begin his high
school scrapbook with a pic
ture of himself as he starts
out the first day.
Try this out and you will
enjoy every minute of this pro
ject, mother and child work
ing together

Last Monday morning Miss

O’Neill’s new flouring mill

ery being set up this week by

rels per day. Mr. Darr informs

him the greatest prosperity it1
is possible for a man to re

40 YEARS AGO
September 10, 1908
N. B. Nesbit returned last
week from Burwick N. D.,
where he had been judging the
North Dakota field trials. Mr.
Nesbit says the trials were a
mong the most successfull he
ever attended, birds being
very plentiful. Adm. Gilchrist
received all three moneys in
the derby, while W. E. Mc
Michael, who formerly ran a
barber shpp here, handled the
dog that got second money in
the all age. In December, Mr.
Nesbit goes south to judge the
Texas. Oklahoma and Tenn
essee trials.
Southern Holt county loses
one of its pioneers and re
spected citizens in the death
of James LaRue, who passed
away at his home in McClure
precinct yesterday morning at
the age of 79.
In his younger days Mr.
LaRue was one of the pro
minent men of this section
of the county and served one
or two terms on the board
of supervisors. Funeral
services were held
at the home today, Thurs
day). and the remains taken
to Ewing, where they were
shipped to Tekamah for
burial.

Something to Remember
It is a good thing that the District grand
Jury has consented to let Representative
J. Parnell Thomas give his side of the
j story of alleged irregularities in his con
j gressional payroll.
Mr. Thomas was not entitled to a hearing
j as a matter of “right,” as he had claimed
in his letter to Attorney General Clark. A
I grand jury investigation is not a trial, and
the grand jury is its own judge as to who
shall be heard and who shall not. Still,
to have refused Mr. Thomas a hearing in
contravention of the usual practice in cases
of this kind would have lent some color
to the claim that this investigation is
politically inspired.
Mr. Thomas has already outdone him
self in this respect, contending that the
Department of Justice, in initiating the
investigation of his payroll records, was
indulging in "cheap Pendergast politics.”
This is a wholly unwarranted slur on the
Attorney General and, incidentally, on the
grand jury. For whatever the fact may
be in some States, or in other investigative
agencies that might be mentioned, grand
juries in the District of Columbia are not
instrumentalities for cheap political in
vestigations. The record on this point is
too clear for dispute. Mr. Thomas can rest
assured that the investigation of the com
plaint against him will be conducted
fairly, and, if there should be an indict
ment, that he will receive a fair trial.
Nevertheless, the decision by the grand
Jury to hear Mr. Thomas after the election
is a sound one. It will put at rest sus
picions which otherwise might have arisen,
and for all any one knows it may set
for Mr. Thomas an example which he will
deem worthy of emulation. For months
now the House Committee on Un-American
Activities, which Mr. Thomas heads, has
refused to give Jw4iearing«4o Dr.JEdSiard
U. Condon, director of file Bureau of
Standards. Dr. Condon is entitled to a
hearing because the committee has ques
tioned his patriotism. But though month
after month has gone by, the committee,
possibly because it has no evidence to sup
port its accusation, has found one excuse
after another for putting off the promised
hearing.
Maybe it will be different after Mr.
Thomas is given a hearing by the grand
Jury. Perhaps he will remember that a
man named Condon is Just as much en
titled to a square deal as is a man named
Thomas.
A Grand Alliance?
The grimly compelling logic of events
lends powerful support to the idea of a
grand alliance between Canada and the
United States on one hand and free
Europe on the other. The idea—reportedly
the central theme of the meetings now
being held in Paris by the foreign ministers
of the five-nation Western Union—may be
bitterly opposed by many Americans, but
it la so in keeping with the dangerous
character of our times that something very
much like it probably will be high on the
agenda of our next Congress unless Soviet
policy meanwhile changes markedly for
the better.
According to authoritative reports, what
the members of the Western Union—Lux
embourg, Belgium, the Netherlands,
France and Britain—are aiming at is a
mutual defensive arrangement in which
these features would be dominant: (1)
Ever-increasing economic, political and
military co-operation among themselves;
(2) a broadening of their common front
to include other free European nations;
(3) an over-all security pact between
them and Canada and the United States,
and (4) a commitment by us not merely to
join with them against the threat of ag
gression but also to supply them now with
i armaments on a lend-lease basis.
This idea is not something that the
Western Union has thought up unilaterally.
It has long been implicit in our developing
bipartisan foreign policy. Indeed, the
essence of it is contained in the historic
i Vandenberg resolution. For that resolu
j tion—which the Senate adopted last June
by the overwhelming vote of sixty-four to
four—specifically advises the President to
pursue a course in which the United States
will encourage, support and “associate”
Itself with regional defensive treaties not
only in the Western Hemisphere but beyond
as well. The grand alliance now under
consideration is in line with the Senate’s
action; if it comes into being—and our
own security will demand it if Soviet
policy continues menacing—it will be the
military counterpart to the Marshall Plan
for European recovery.
Such an alliance, of course, would rep
resent a far-reaching new departure in
our peacetime foreign policy. In the past,
except during wars, the United States has
consistently shied away from entering
J mutual defensive pacts with powers out
5 side the Western Hemisphere. But things
sj 'are not as they used to be. We are living
i in a foreshortened world; the age of the
atom and supersonic flight is here; the
oceans around us have ceased to be pro
3 tective barriers, and we cannot be safe as
1 long as our friends abroad face the danger
l of being overwhelmed, one by one, by
direct and indirect Soviet aggression. The
simple /fttct is that we cannot abandoii
them without gravely imperiling ourselves.
That is why the Senate, in the Vanden
berg resolution, is on record as solemnly
advising American participation in alli
ances like the one being discussed now*
And that is why the next Congress, on'
top of vast outlays for other pufpoSes, may
be called up to revive military lend-lease
at a cost, according to some estimates,
that may run as high as $2,000,000,000
yearly. The prospect is not a heartening
one. But neither is Soviet policy hearten
ing. The bleak truth is that as long as
that policy remains what it is, we and
other free peoples will have to labor hard
together and share costly burdens to be
safe. Liberty does not come cheap.
Our Navy Today
It is a radically reorganized Navy, with
new types of ships, new weapons and new
responsibilities, to which the Nation pays
its respects on this Navy Day of 1948. It
is a Navy far different from that envisioned
by Theodore Roosevelt, whose birthday
anniversary coincides with the Navy’s an
nual open-house observance. What would
the advocate of big battleships, big guns
and the “big stick” policy say if he could
see our fighting fleet today—a fleet with
only one battleship in commission?
Vice Admiral Earle W. Mills, chief of the
Bureau of Ships, has made it plain that
the day of the heavy-gunned dreadnought
which began in the early 1900s is drawing
to a close. Battleships were not even
mentioned in his description of our pres
ent naval building program. In their
stead, as the core of the battle force, will
be the large aircraft carriers of World
War II remodeled for broader service. And
topping these later will be a 65,000-ton
supercarrier, especially designed for the
atomic age.
in snort, the carrier has replaced the
battleship as the backbone of the fleet.
The long-range plan is for a force of
twenty-eight fast carriers, built around
the much-debated supership, the CVA-58.
The present 45,000-ton carriers of the Mid
way class and the 27,000-ton Essex class
carriers will be improved with heavier
armament and strengthened flight decks,
to enhance their striking power. This
carrier task force will be capable of
launching atomic bombers, if necessary, in
support of and in co-ordination with the
Air Force’s strategic bombers. Further
more, it has been demonstrated that the
floating bases can> serve as mobile plat
forms for rockets as large as the V-2. One
carrier already is being used exclusively
for rocket research.
Coincident with its emphasis on carrier
borne airpower, the Navy is devoting
special attention to submarine-borne sea
power. The chief reason for this is that
the only fleet which poses a threat to the
United States is that of Russia—and it is
composed largely of submarines, many of
them of the most advanced type. Russia
has been building up her submarine forces
relentlessly since the war. The latest edi
tion of Jane’s Fighting Ships says that the
Russian navy is putting its main reliance
on submarines, instead of on carriers or
other craft. Hence, our own naval experts
are devising countermeasures to meet this
under-water threat. Antisubmarine task
forces will have the job of seeking out and
destroying enemy submersibles before they
can reach our ships or shores.. Our own
schnorkel-equipped submarines will take
part in this mission, while other deep
diving vessels will conduct offensive opera
tions or stand picket duty with radar
detectors—to guard against surprise at
tack by air or sea.
Thus, despite the too-rapid demobiliza
ton of the postwar period, America’s Navy
is beginning once more to evolve as a
potent, modernized fighting force—dedi
cated to speaking softly but ready when
called upon to join with its fellow mem
bers of the unified defense team in wield
ing a big stick whenever necessary. The
mere existence of this potentially powerful
Army-Navy-Air Force team should help to
curb the ambitions of any aggressor. Until
better means of preserving the peace can
be found, our Navy, in common with the
Army and Air Force, must continue to be
strong and watchful.

Mother love Is that which restrains the
sorely tempted parent from chucking the
eight-year-old, muddy football suit and
all, into the washing machine.
A behind-the-iron-curtain analyst says
the Kremlin will never forgive Tito. For
