Our Price Outlook
Last August, in a statement defending
the Republican anti-inflation program,
Senator Taft took the rather cheerful view
that there were “many reasons to believe
that we are reaching a stabilized price
level” and that the next Congress probably
would not have to legislate new measures
to curb rising living costs;
The moderate optimism thus voiced by
Mr. Taft two months ago is still very much
open to question, but figures on the con
sumer price index—the latest available—
suggest at least the possibility that time
may prove him to have been right. For
the index, as compiled by the Bureau of
Labor Statistics, shows that in the monthly
period ended September 15 the over-all
cost of living stopped rising after having
climbed continuously for five months.
In fact, despite the usual seasonal boost,
food prices dropped a bit during the
month, and were it not for the fact that
this was offset by slight increases in such
other items as rents, fuel and electricity,,
the index as a whole would have shown
a decline, instead of merely halting its
upward trend. In other words, as of Sep
tember 15, it was no higher than on August
15, but remained at the same level, stand
ing still for the first time in almost six
months.
But although this tends to support Mr.
Taft’s view, Americans would be well
advised to keep their fingers crossed. Cer
tain factors in the picture serve as
warning signals against too-hasty or over
hopeful conclusions that we may now be
entering a period when consumer .prices
will mot only flatten out on “a stabilized
price level” but actually will begin to drop.
That is a real possibility, of course,
especially because our exceptionally good
crops and tremendous industrial produc
tion are bringing the supply situation more
and more into line with demand. Never
theless, a look at the world scene should
be enough to dissuade anybody from pre
maturely assuming that the worst days
of our inflation are at an end.
Thus, wholly apart from the fact that
the Federal Reserve Board recently de
clared that inflationary factors still domi
nate our economy, we must keep in mind
that international peace is in a critical
state. If it does not improve, we shall be
obliged not many months from now to
consider increasing our armaments out
put for a big and costly new lend-lease
project to help our allies abroad. Added
to our own tremendously expensive defense
program, that would almost certainly have
a substantial effect on,our supply-demand
price situation.
In short, although that situation may be
approaching stability now, events overseas
could upset it so severely that the next
Congress would be compelled to try head
ing off a new wave of inflation. There is
reason to hope for the best, of course—and
indeed the best may happen—but Mr.
Taft’s August optimism is still subject to
serious “ifs."
Who Are These Men?
It is hard to realize that Mr. Truman’s
Chicago speech was delivered by a Presi
dent of the United States. For it is the
sort of speech that one would expect only
from a man who had plumbed the depths
of irresponsibility and panic.
Mr. Truman would have us believe that _
there is a Republican conspiracy against
our free society. He would have us believe
that Mr. Dewey is merely a “front man”
for sinister figures in the background, and
that a Republican victory would install in
the White House, not a Hitler, not a
Mussolini, not a Tojo, bvjt all three rolled
Into one.
Who are the architects of this plot
against the Nation? Or as Mr. Truman
himself put it, who are the men behind
the forces that “threaten our way of life’’?
Needless to say, having posed the question,
the President did not answer it. Like
Henry Wallace, when he was going about
the country ranting against unnamed
“American Fascists,” Mr. Truman is con
tent to call names, but is not willing to
name names.
The chances are that this sort of thing
is not going to do irreparable harm, for
the American people have too much sense
to be taken in by it. But it is bound to do
some harm, and in that connection there
Is one thing Mr. Truman would do well to
keep in mind. Back in the depths of the
depression, when many people feared for
the future of the country, Mr. Truman’s
predecessor in the White House made a
statement in which he said that we have
nothing to fear but fear itself. That was
a statesmanlike comment, and Mr. Tru
man would do well to reflect upon its impli
cations as he goes about the unworthy
.business of trying to manufacture fear for
the sake of votes.
The word from Tokyo tells of a Jap
liqueur distilled from onions. So much
for science, and Its stand that there is no
reply to the atomic bomb.
Candidates are people who, once united
on the need for unity, thereupon proceed
to go their separate ways.
This and That
By Charles E. Tracewell
"OAKTON, Va.
"Dear Sir:
"We have a robin, which the children
brought in, suffering from a broken wing.
"It has been subsisting on a diet of angle
worms and dogwood berries which we gather
for it. What are its chances for recovery? And
how soon?
"What other foods can we offer it? May I
say that your column long has been a favorite
of this family. We have enjoyed reading it
and have gained much of value from it. More
power to you. "Regards, L. G.”

If a bird can be kept from injuring itself or
being injured by cats, dogs or children, the
chances are good for its pulling through.
A wing injury always is serious, much more
so than a broken leg. The leg may be put in a
splint, but an attempt to do the same with a
wing is fraught with danger.
Perhaps the best procedure is to confine the
bird as much as possible, so that it cannot
make much attempt to fly.
This will allow the injured wing to set itself,
in a sense. This is what nature does, in the
wild, and it often works out well.
Robins are comparatively easy to handle.
A cardinal is a bird of another color. No
matter how much injured, a redbird will fight
back, being a true wild creature.
Often it is impossible for a human to hold
an injured or ill cardinal.
Often people take them away from cats, to
discover that, no matter the extent of the in
jury, the bird flutters its wings, struggles and
pecks, so that it must be released.
The robin is more sedate in action. One
would not say it seems to know- that one is
trying to help it, but the result is much the
same. The bird allows much familiarity, and
is glad to get food.
Robins in captivity do nicely on earthworms,
Insects of all kinds, bits of beef and liver, the
yolk of hard-boiled egg, small pieces of bread
and milk, pablum and milk, raisins, pieces of
cut apple, even slices of coconut cake.
Raisins may be considered standard robin
fare. They are good to put out in February,
when the birds first arrive here, and when
there still may be snow on the ground.
Often it will be necessary to scoop a level
place in the snow.
It is hard for District residents to realize
that 75 years ago there were few robins here
about.
Since then it has become one of four favorite
birds, almost as plentiful during spring and
summer as the English sparrow.
Perhaps as many as a dozen robins stay here
all winter, most of them to be found in shel
tered areas of Rock Creek Park.
When a sick robin is taken in, sometimes it 1
is good to give it a little warm milk in which
one drop of whisky has been put. No more
than one drop should be used in a teaspoonful
of milk, and of this not more than a few drops
given.
A large dose is of no avail.
Usually an injured bird needs rest, warmth
and freedom from outside interference.
An attempt may be made to give it water
with a medicine dropper.
Food may be given in the same way, but
usually a bird that has enough reserve power
to get well will show it by picking up food for
itself.
It is best to put some of the foods on a paper
on which some loose earth has been sprinkled.
No attempt should be made to give a sick
bird grit. Plain garden earth will do. It will
contain all the small particles birds need for
digestion. Too much attention to wild things
in captivity is worse than too little.
A robin with a broken wing will need from
four to eight weeks for mending, provided the
break is of the sort that can repair itself with
nature’s expert aid. "Nature cure” is the only
kind the wild things'have in nature. In civil
ization, as we like to call it, they do best when
nature is imitated.
Often the best we can do for them is to let
them alone, keep them warm and protected
and supply them with foods as far as feasible.
A bird with a broken wing has a chance, and
should be givea^t.
I Letters to The Star
Helping the Alcoholic
To the Editor ot The St»r:
Permit me to comment on your editorial,
"Some Progress Due,” of October 17. The
editorial, which devotes itself to the establish
ment of clinic service for alcoholics in the
District of Columbia, states that, "practically
nothing has been done to get the work start
ed.” I feel that it is very unfortunate to leave
the reader of your paper with the impression
that nothing has been done toward the estab
lishment of a clinic for alcoholics here in the
District, which you refer to as a “new de
parture.” Historically, it may be of interest
to the reader to know that psychiatric ap
proach in the treatment' of alcoholics is noth
ing new ani has a history of more than 50
years’ practice back of it. Here in the Dis
trict, the local Health Department, through
its Bureau of Mental Hygiene, has dealt with
the alcoholic for more than three years. It
has rendered service to more than 1,600
patients and has apparently achieved cures
or good results in at least 60 per cent of these
patients.
So far as the establishment of the particular
clinic for alcoholics under the Hebert Act is
concerned, the difficulty in establishing this
arises from the interpretation which certain
people are putting upon the meaning of this
act. The recommendation that the clinic be
developed, as your editorial says, as a “sepa
rate entity” is, manifestly, unsound. It would
immediately identify the locale for the clinic
service for the purpose for which it exists
and, in that way, identify those that come
to the clinic as alcoholics; that is, to say the
least, bad psychiatric service. We have had
ample experience with clinics so housed as,
for example, in the treatmpnt of venereal
disease patients. The matter became a crying
scandal and certainly is a procedure that
should not be repeated. On this point it
might be interesting to the public to have
the opinion of such well-known authorities in
the field of psychiatry as Dr. William Men
ninger, formerly head of the psychiatric pro
gram of the Army, who writes me as follows:
‘‘From my point of view it would seem
highly important that you have one mental
hygiene service and that the alcoholic clinic
be a section or a part of this. It seems
extremely unwise to me to set up a so-called
alcoholic clinic as separate and apart from
another division of psychiatric outpatient
work. I think this does an unfairness to the
patients concerned because obviously alcohol
ism is merely a svmptom and even though
a common one, the nature of the diagnosis
and treatment of the underlying personality
disorder is the same as in any other psy
chiatric case. Therefore, to me it seems an
artificial division. Furthermore, it would seem
to me that for the advantage of the patient
it would be much wiser to have him go to a
psychiatric clinic rather than to an alcoholic
clinic.”
Gallinger Co-operation Needed.
The commissioners, fortunately, have recog
nized the unwisdom of setting up the clinic
for alcoholics as a separate entity because,
administratively, such clinic service needs the
co-operation of Gallinger Hospital psychiatric
service, of the Hospital Permit Bureau, of the
Laboratory, of the Public Health Nursing Serv
ice, of the psychiatric service, of the account
ing and personnel service, all in the Health
Department. An independent clinic requiring
all these services would represent needless
duplication and increased cost to the taxpayer.
However, while it has been decided that the
clinic should be established in the Health De
partment, the director of the clinic is still
described as a person who should function
with absolute independence of the Health De
partment. Such a setup would be extremely
awkward to say the least. However, honest
effort has been made to secure a director for
the clinic service as defined. Not only was
the local Civil Service appealed to, but also
Saint Elizabeths Hospital, the Public Health
Service, the Army, the Navy, the Veterans'
Bureau, 15 leading universities having psychi
atric service, the American Psychiatric Associa
tion, the local Medical Society, in addition
‘to the advertisements placed in the medical
Journals. Outstanding in the reaction was,
of course, the point which your editorial also
mentions, the inadequacy of the salary offered.
However, some local psychiatrists whom we
hoped to secure for the service, declined to
have anything to do with it when they learned
of the proposed limitation of the service to
alcoholics only.
In this connefction it may also be of interest
-to your readers to learn thdt the Connecticut
Commission on Alcoholism ("Yale Clinic”)
is suffering from the same handicap that we
are here? namely, that it ha? not yet found
a director for its clinic. In fact, the director
of the District Health Departments mental
hygiene clinic service, who is also in charge
of the clinic work for alcoholics here, has
been offered the directorship of the Connecti
cut service which he has, however, declined to
accept.
The suggestion which you say has been made
that the standards for the directorship be
lowered in order to fill the position is, to say
the least, of doubtful value. The absurdity
in the local situation is that the funds that
have accumulated for the care of the alcoholic
are denied to an existing and functioning
clinic on the basis of an unfortunate and
impractical bit of legislation, at least as this
Act is presently interpreted.
From the above it should be clear that really
quite a good deal has been done to help the
alcoholic. More, of course, could be done by
simply repealing the impractical stipulation
in the present Act so that funds available
under it could be applied to the psychiatric
clinic service in the Health Department.
GEORGE C. RUHLAND, M. D„
Health Officer.
Sympathy for Mercy-Speeder
To the Editor of The Star:
Have you been deluged with letters of pro
test concerning the treatment of the poor
man who was imprisoned and fined for ex
ceeding the silly District speed limit while
rushing a desperately ill child of a neighbor
to a hospital? I hope so. I consider his pun
ishment the most outrageous abuse of police
power I have ever heard of outside of the
Soviet Union.
The District attitude on speeding does not
make sense. I have held a driver's license since
1926, driven thousands of miles each year on
volunteer motor corps work and my own busi
ness. Only twice during the 22 years have I
been spoken to for speeding, both times on the
rural end of Sixteenth street, burning up the
road at all of 38 miles an hour. Both times
my license was suspended with considerable
hardship, yet there was no charge of reckless
driving or danger to any one either time.
The driver of the sick child exceeded the
limit at 40 to 45 miles an hour and could have
menaced no one—a parody of Justice and
mercy. Could I pay the fine?
E. McSHERRY.

Barred From Teaching in D. G.
To the Editor of The Star:
I have been greatly interested in recent
articles concerning the scarcity of teachers in
Washington schools and the subsequent articles
putting part of the blame on the examination
as a means of selecting (one might say ex
cluding) teachers.
Once was enough for this writer. Previous
to the war I had spent 15 years In school work
In Ohio, the last eight of which were as prin
Letters for publication must bear
the signature and address of the
writer, although it is permissible for
a writer known to The Star to use
a nom de plume. Please be brief.
cipal of a respectable school system. I hold
the master of arts degree from a great uni
versity. Returning to civilian life in 1946, after
40 months of service in the Navy, I decided to
try to teach in the Washington schools.
I was offered a job, teaching in a junior
high school. The salary was not enough to
live on, but that is not my point here. The
point I would emphasize is that I was to be
given a temporary job, and further that I
could not even »take the examination until I
had gone to school and taken a two-semester
hour course in “Methods in Junior High
School.” It sd happened that I had neglected
to take a course by that particular title when
doing my undergraduate work, so I was barred
from teaching in the city schools, in spite of
the fact that I had had eight years’ experi
ence supervising the same kind of school, to
say nothing of several years of successful
teaching in the same field.
I submit that so long as the local school
system operates on its present system of se
lecting teachers there Will continue to be a
shortage of good teachers. J. E. D.
Some Senator* Dixiecrats?
To the Editor of The St»r:
Which political party will organize the
Senate of the next Congress is a most prom
inent question of the present election cam
paign. Speculation about whether Democrats
or Republicans will be elected to the Senate
from Kentucky, MoWaea, Oklahoma, West
Virginia and other doubtful States is running
strong. Always the discussions assume that
the Republicans must win at least enough
Senate seats to make their total Senators not
less than 48 in order to control the Senate.
One important point is never mentioned.
We are so accustomed to only two political
parties in the Senate that no thought is given
to the possibility of a half-dozen or more Sen
ators in the 81st Congress being neither Demo
crats nor Republicans.
Suppose the Republicans as a result of the
election November 2 have only 46 Senators.
The Democrats then would not necessarily
have 50 and so control the Senate. The Sen
ators from Alabama, Mississippi, South Caro
lina and perhaps other States might refuse
to be classed as Democrats. States which
elect Senators and presidential electors on
States’ Rights tickets scarcely could be claimed
by the Democrats.
Then with only 46 or fewer Senators the
Republicans still couid have a majority in the
Senate. This factor of third-party Senators
should be considered in all speculations on the
control of the Senate in the 81st Congress.
GEORGE F. MILLER.
Pensions for Needy Only
To the Editor of The Star:
In reference to your editorial "War Pen
sions for Everybody,’’ October 23, why is It
that there never is a peep out of your editorial
staff when well-fixed financially, higher
bracket military and civilian pensions are
handed out, some at full salary after only 10
years of service?
I am for economy in all branches of Gov
ernment service, and I think there should be
no pensions for any one unless proof of
financial need is shown to exist. I often have
wondered just what these near-millionaires
do with their pensions.
Why does The Star criticize the action of
the American Legion in helping these World
War I vets who are getting along in years?
Your editorial should have been entitled
"War Pensions for Everybody Except World
War I Veterans.’’ HAROLD J. OLIVER.
U. N. and Russia Subversion
To the Editor of The Star:
There is a primary weakness in the United
Nations setup which hitherto has been Ignored
or overlooked. That weakness owes its origin
to the naive ideas concerning Soviet Russia
held by some of our policymakers at the end
of the last war.
To begin with, the success of any interna
tional body depends basically on the freedom
of action of the individual nations that are
members; there must be no coercion on the
part of larger units; there must be complete
freedom of decision; there must be complete
freedom of discussion, press and individual
decision inside the member nations in order to
have a free and democratic international body.
This was ignored or overlooked by our policy
makers at San Francisco. To make matters
worse, they played into the hands of Russian
strategy by the implementation of the veto.
This in itself showed their inconsistency.
They spoke during the war of unity of pur
pose; repudiated nationalistic ties and isola
tionist arguments by castigating all doubts of
sovereign nations’ culpability and international
justice, then they legalized these doubts by
the retention of the veto power by the members
of |he permanent council.
To add insult to injury, they proceeded to
make deals; Jockeyed for political advantage
and temporary prestige for the United Nations
and themselves by giving the Russian satellite
states two votes in the Assembly, knowing or
being naively unaware that independent
thought and action were completely shut off
for these two units of the Communist world
state.
Your cartoon of October 24 reiterates the
present fallacy of that idea in the democratic
nations of the world: That the United Nations
is the hope of peace. My contention Is that
Russia wants talk until by subversion in the
permanent member nations of the Council of
the United Nations (i.e. France and China), she
will be able to control the vote of their dele
gates; then by deception, coercion and subver
sion, the Communist conspiracy will go to work
on the members of the Assembly gradually
through economic measures, political and inter
national embargoes, subversion from within,
plus the financial and economic strains upon
the economy of the United States from the
Marshall Plan, military commitments and just
ordinary deflation inherent in the capitalistic
system, our economy will fail, and we will be
prey to the Communist legalistic maneuvers in
the United Nations. These will come in the
nature of press censorship, atomic energy con
trol, race questions, etc. The United States
and those democracies that will be left will
have to accept these or veto. It will not be
like the veto of Russia. It will have moral
calumny attached. We will either have to
accept or leave the United Nations. Then
Russia’s Communist leaders will hsure the
reason for a Communist holy war to wipe out
capitalist aggressors with the legalistic sanction
of the United Nations. We will have repudi
ated our child. E. M. WARNER.
The ‘Catatonic’ to Blame
To the Editor of The Stir:

Stars, Men and Atoms
Half of Man's Body Seen
Changed Every 2 Months
Speed of Physiological Processes
Reported by Oak Ridge Scientist
By Thomas R. Henry
A man gets at least half a new body every
two months.
This astounding conclusion has come from
study of physiological processes, some of which
take place with extreme rapidity, with radio
active isotopss now being manufactured by the
Atomic Energy Commission, says Dr. Paul
Aebersold, chief of the isotope division of the
commission’s plant at Oak Ridge, Tenn.
These isotopes—particularly exploding so
dium, phosphorus, iodine and carbon—now are
being furnished to medical laboratories all over
the world and the discovery of the astonish
ingly rapid rate of cell renewal results from
a large number of experiments designed for
other purposes.
Speed Unrealised Before.
It long has been known that most body cells
are replaced many times during the average
lifetime, but physiologists hitherto have not
realized the speed at which the rebuilding of
a human being takes place.
At least 76 per cent of a man is water. Work
with heavy water has shown, Dr. Aebersold
says, that one-half the water in the body is
turned over every two weeks. Only an infini
tesimal amount of water that composed the
great bulk of the body of any man or woman
a month ago is in the same individual today.
The isotope experiments have shown that
about half the atoms of sodium, potassium and
sulphur which constitute a considerable part
of the nonwater part of the body are replaced
by new ones every month. It requires about
two months for half the carbon to disappear
and be replaced.
Teeth and Bones Change.
Some other atoms, like calcium in teeth and
bone, are exchanged very slowly, but still it
is unlikely that the average adult has much
of the same teeth and bones present in child
hood.
The human body, Dr. Aebersold points out,
consists of about 10,000,000,000,000,000,000,000
000,000 atoms of all sorts. While precise cal
culations are difficult because of the extreme
complexity of physiological processes, it is al
most certain that more than half of these are
rejected and replaced by new ones each two
months. Whether any of them remain with
the individual from birth to death is quite
debatable.
Those rejected are released to the general
“atom bank'’ of the universe.
There hardly can be any question, Dr. Aeber
sold says, that every person who reads this
has in his body a few millions of the atoms
that were once temporarily part of the body
of Julius Caesar, or the Queen of Sheba or
George Washington.
Shock to Aryans Seen.
Persons bom pure-blooded Aryans, he point*
out, may get a shock to learn that their bodies
today are made up of considerably more Negro,
Chinese or Russian atoms than of pure Aryan
atoms.
The atoms are the building stones of mole
cules which, in turn, are the constituents of
cells, the microscopic, diversely shaped “bricks''
which make up all living matter. Not all th*
atoms in a molecule or molecules in a cfell
are replaced at the same time. The human
body is like a house w’hich is not actually
rebuilt at any time but on which repairmen
are always at work, so that at the end of a
year or so there is essentially nothing of the
original structure left.
The isotopes are revealing, Dr. Aebersold
said, that the physical process of living is
dynamic far beyond any previous conception.
Experiments with radioactive sodium, lor
example, have shown that at least 60 pound*
of this element, probably in units of single
atoms, pass back and forth across the wall*
of the blood vessels every 24 hours.
Bate Is Not Constant.
The rebuilding, Dr. Aebersold points out, does
not go on at a constant rate all over the
body and for all types of cells. This may be
one reason why the personality does not
change and why there is a continuity of
memory. The rate of rebuilding can be fol
lowed by incorporating isotopes in cells, chiefly
through -food, and finding the rate at which
new cells appear.
A precision has been attained by the use of
the isotopes which hitherto would have been
incredible, he says. It now is possible to detect,
for example, 1,000,000,000,000,000,000th (a
billionth of a billionth) of a milligram of radio
active iodine and millionths of a billionth of a
milligram of various other elements. This 1*
at least a thousandfold greater precision than
can be achieved by any other method of chemi
cal analysis.
The lower limit for detection, he says, seem*
to be about 10,000 atoms, which would consti
tute a grain of matter far below visibility with
the most powerful microscope.
Questions and Answers
A reader can set the answer to any question of
fact by writlns The Washington Star Information
Bureau. 318 I street N.E.. Washington 3, D. C.
Please inclose 3 cents for return postage.
BY THE HASKIN SERVICE.
Q. Where is the headquarters of the Reor
ganized Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day
Saints?—G. G.
A. This denomination has headquarters in
Independence. Mo. It was founded in the 1850a
by members of the original Mormon church
who would not accept the leadership of Brig
ham Young and the idea of polygamy and
other alleged innovations.
Q. Which is the largest city of the South?
—H. J.
A. The Bureau of the Census says that
Baltimore is the largest city in the South,
the population at the last census (1940) being
852,100. Nearly half the people of Maryland
live in the city of Baltimore.
Q. When a person attempts to swim across
the English Channel, may he rest at intervals
by floating or by hanging on to a boat?—
G. D. R.
A. The swimmer is permitted to float when
he is tired but he may not hang on to a boat.
Q. Was John Paul Jones originally buried
in France?—C. M. T.
A. After a long search instituted by Gen.
Horace Porter, American Ambassador to
France, the burial place of John Paul Jones
was discovered in 1905 in the old St. Louis
Cemetery in Paris. In 1906, the body was
conveyed by a United States squadron to An
napolis, Md., where it was buried with naval
honors.
Q. Is one end of an orange sweater than the
other?—E. McD.
A. The blossom end of an orange is sweeter
than the stem end.
These Things I Love
All things upon this earth I love:
Weed and towering hollyhocks:
Raucous crow and gentle dove:
Nocturnal skunk and wary fox.
My heart is like the ambient sky
Curving to the fertile ground.
My thoughts are skimming birds that fly
Above still waters without sound.
These humble verities I prize—
All lowly things that live and grow—
Lilting song and laughing eyes
As full of stars as sparkling snow.
HARRY ELMORE HURQ.

Both Parties Declare
For Supports
WASHINGTON, D. C.—Govern
ment farm price supports, as an is
sue in the presidential campaign, ap
pear to have been minimized by pro
nouncements from both major can
didates during their campaign
swings across the country.
Both President Truman and
Gov. Thomas E. Dewey, his Re
publican opponent, agree to the
principle that the government
should step in to protect farm
prices when necessary to avert
collapse.
The choice between the two can
didates appears to narrow down to
which one can best administer a
program of government agricultural
supports.
Farm price supports, which had
been under attack in some segments
of the metropolitan press, make up
the major issue on which all farm
organizations agree.
Rather than increasing the cost of
living as some critics have implied,
the farm leaders contend the re
verse is true—that supports encour
age ample production, sufficient
enough to keep food prices from
soaring to the levels they have
reached in some other countries.
This was the official view taken
by officials of the three major farm
groups at a recent joint meeting in
Washington last month.
Maximum production, these top
spokesmen for the American Farm
Bureau federation, the National
Grange and the National Council of
Farmer Co-operatives emphasized,
means lower food prices for con
sumers.
And in taking their chances on
maximum production, farmers have
to have some sort of guarantee that
the bottom won't drop from under
them.
With apparent agreement all
around on price supports, farm lead
ers in Washington are giving atten
tion to other agricultural issues,
such as reorganization of USDA,
which are expected to come up in
the 81st congress.
As yet, no farm organization has
definitely set its strategy regarding
the next congress which will be
elected in November.
Issues are being studied, and late
surveys will be made.
The legislative program of the
American Farm Bureau Federation
probably will be outlined at its De
cember convention. National Grange
policy is being formulated at a
series of executive committee meet
ings.
While there are technical differ
ences among the organizations, they
are unified on price supports— a
floor below which farm prices can
not drop.

steps to adjust agriculture to its
proper position in the nation's
future economy, to keep agricul

4-H, HOMEMAKER'S COUNCILS
PLAN ACHIEVEMENT DAYS
A YEAR AGO
THIS WEEK...
Student activities were making
headlines in the Valley Frontiers
man just a year ago this week.
Palmer students had completed
work on a new basketball floor
in the gymnasium, and school
heads were busy framing rules
for the community use of that
gym.
Palmer men and women were
planning basketball teams, too.
The Shamrocks were organizing
under the sponsorship of Matt
Perkins. Palmer High squads
were getting their initial prac
tice sessions in the great Alaskan
out-of-doors.
Torrential fall rains had wash
ed out roads at Moose Creek and
had slowed traffic at Chugiak on
the Palmer-Anchorage Highway.
Robbers attempted a break-in
at Hartley’s Garage, but were
frightened away before they
could investigate the cash box.
Rusty Dow again opened a
trucking line with headquarters
in Palmer and the West Side Ga
rage announced that it would
soon be ready for business.
Mrs. Walter Zeile was named
temporary public health nurse,
pending the appointment of a
new nurse.
Palmer’s airport was reported
nearly finished, with the east
west strip nearing the point
where it would be usable. The
Matanuska Hotel was roofed
during the last week of October,
and workmen rushed the job of
getting the siding up.
Palmer students planned the
first school dance of the year
and Valley Granges at Palmer
and Wasilla scheduled a joint
social evening. Guests listened to
two hours of music at the first
fall meeting of the Music Appre
ciation Club in the rectory of St.
Michael’s Church.
Ronnie Meehan and Freddie
Cook took their place on the
Frontiersman staff as Palmer
newsboys.
Parker Lester, local Extension
Service agent, is taking a holi
day this week. Mr. Lester’s vaca
tion will be short and he does
not plan to leave the Valley.

Achievement Day plans for 4-H
and Homemakers’ clubs were dis
cussed at last week’s Council
meetings. No definite date has
yet been set for the 4-H Achieve
ment Day, which is tentatively
planned for some time in No
vember.
Lynn Sandvik, Mrs. Harold
Dinkel, Mrs. Lou Sandvik and
Miss Marilyn Johnson were nam
ed to a central committee to or
ganize plans for the day. They
expect to announce time, place
and date in the near future.
In the meantime, 4-H Club
members were reminded that
record books are due on this
year’s work no later than Oct. 15.
Any club member who turns in
his record book after that date
is not eligible for a pin. accord
ing to Miss Johnson.
Homemakers’ Council mem
bers tentatively selected Oct. 27
as the date for Homemaker’s
Achievement Day. It will be held
in the Grange Hall, following a
potluck luncheon at noon. Each
club will be responsible for a
number on the day’s program.
Final plans for the day will be
outlined at the next Council
meeting, to be held on Oct. 18.
At that time new officers will be
elected and installed.
Program plans for the coming
year were discussed last week.
Further and more definite plans
will be brought before the Coun
cil at the October meeting.
Student Paper
To Be Out Friday
This year’s staff of the Banana
Belt Breeze, student publication
of Palmer High School, was burn
ing the midnight oil this week to
meet a Friday deadline. The
year’s first issue is scheduled to
be on the streets by after-school
hours on Friday.
Staff positions were still not
definite at the Frontiersman’s
press deadline. However it is cer
tain George Hermon will be over
seeing the efforts of Breezes re
porters as editor. Roberta Hem
mer is to be his right-hand man.
All future issues of the Banana
Belt Breeze are scheduled for
publication on the last Friday of
each month.
EKLUTNA HOUSECLEANING
SLOWS VALLEY DINNERS
The power dimout experienced
in the Valley last week was caus
ed by the annual housecleaning
at Eklutna dam. Each year the
city of Anchorage shuts down the
Eklutna .sub-station for several
days while a clean-up crew works
on the dam gates.
During the shut-down all of
the Valley’s power is brought
from Anchorage over a small
transmission line, with a result
ing high loss of voltage. The
MVFCA Diesel plant was operat
ed during the four-day period, in
order to pick up the voltage
needed for Valley use.

Council Discusses 'Drop-Inn* Vandals;
Protest To Rent Control Board
Vandalism at the Drop-Inn, Greenbelt’s youth center, was a
subject of discussion at Monday’s meeting of the Town Council.
Councilman Allen D. Morrison made public the growing discourage
ment of the parents’ board and the inability of the board or
recreation department so far to keep the Drop-Inn’s patrons fromj
defacing the property and from petty thievery.
Town Manager Charles T. McDonald agreed to take a hand in
solving the problem and will meet with the parents’ board in thq
near future.
Rent Increase Protect
Formal protest of the proposed
rent increase in the form of a reso
lution was ordered sent to the rent
control board in Silver Spring,
Maryland. Introduced by Mayor
Thomas J. Canning, the resolution
makes the following points: the
proposed increase ranges from thir
ty to fifty percent over the present
rates established under OPA review
in 1942; the increase is admitttedly
not necessary because of any in
crease in operating costs; there is
no housing in the area to which
Greenbelt can be fairly compared;
therefore the Council concludes that
the increase at this time is request
ed to inflate the sale price; and the
Council goes on record as vehe
mently opposing the increase and
requests an opportunity to appear
before the rent control board.
Studies Leave Problems
Manager McDonald reported on
a study he is making of leave regu
lations at the request of tthe coun
cil. It was agreed that those em
ployees with accumulated annual
leave of 75 days or more be request
ed to take leave before the end of
the year and be reimbursed for
leave not taken over 60 days. A
formal resolution of leave regula
tions will be presented at the next
council meeting, Mr. McDonald
said.
The Council studied the current
financial statement and discussed
the present status of discussions
with PHA over town boundaries
and with the County Board of Edu
cation over charges to the county
for use of the centerschoolbuilding
if the building is leased to the
town by the government.
GCS Picks 4 Directors
Passes By-Law Changes
Four directors were elected
unanimously to the Greenbelt Con
sumer Service board of directors,
and several by-law changes were
made at the quarterly membership
meeting which met last night.
Reelected to the board were Mrs.
Carnie Harper and Paul Kasko.
New directors elected were Henry
Walter and James Wolfe.
Among the by-law amendments
passed was a provision to allocate
20% of the net savings to the per
manent surplus reserve. A flat 5%
of the remainder of the net savings
will hereafter be paid yearly as div
idends on capital stock. All direc
tors of GCS will be elected for a
term of one year, beginning with
next February’s annual meeting.
Thomas Ritchie, treasurer, re
ported that GCS was in a good fi
nancial condition. The working
capital ration is 50%.
Membership committee chairman,
William Nicholas, announced that
GCS had 2501 members as of Au
gust 30, with an average investment
of SB7.
Door prizes were awarded at the
beginning and end of the meeting.
Mrs. Bob Morrow, wife of GCS’
comptroller, won an electric kitch
en clock. Your reporter, Mrs. S.
'Rubin, won a Co-op Pressure
Cooker.
Wodak At Health Office
Dr. Hans Wodak is substituting
for Dr. William Eisner as public
health officer for the next few
weeks, according to Town Manager
Charles T. McDonald. Schedules
maintained at the clinic will remain
as usual.
Mayor Heads Group
To Fight Increase
Mayor Thomas J. Canning was
elected chairman of the Greenbelt
Rent Protest Committee Tuesday
night in a follow-up of Friday’s
mass meeting.
The 19 residents who attended
the meeting for the purpose of es
tablishing a formal committee,
named Michael Salzman, president
of Greenbelt Mutual Home Owner
ship Corporation, as vice-chair
man; June Ringel, news editor of
the Cooperator, as secretary; and
Benjamin Goldfaden, of the
GMHOC board of directors, as
treasurer.
National Support Sought
Frontal assault in the commit
tee’s attempt to enlist national sup
port will be in the form of a tele
gram to J. Howard McGrath, chair
man of the Democratic National
Committee, asking for a conference
between Democratic representa
tives and representatives of the pro
test committee. It was suggested
that if no reply were received with
in a few days, the Republican Na
tional Committee be similarly ap
proached.
Pen Pals
Individual letters from residents,
it was agreed, should be written
to Walter R. Heath, Rent Director,
400 E. Lombard Street, Baltimore,
Maryland; and to Congressman
Lansdale G. Sasscer at Upper
Marlboro, Md. It was also suggest
ed that all citizens write letters to
the editors of their hometown pa
pers, in an effort to put the issue
before the reading public all over
the country.
The committee voted to send let
ters, accompanied by copies of the
resolution adopted at Friday’s meet
ing, to the following: President
Truman; Tighe E. Woods, federal
housing expediter; Raymond Fo
ley, administrator, Housing Home
and Finance Agency; W. L. Tliomp
son, area rent representative in the
Office of the Housing Expediter;
See MAYOR, page 5
Voters To Register
On September 28
All prospective voters in Green
belt who have declared their inten
tion to become citizens of the State
of Maryland are notified to regis
ter at the Greenbelt Fire House, on
Tuesday, September 28, between
the hours of 9 a.m. and 9 p.m.
Certificates of declarations of in
tention must be presented at time
of registration. The final registra
tion day will be October 5, at the
same place and during the same
hours.
Registration Streamlined
Alexander L. Buscher, president
of the Board of Elections, states
that registration formalities will be
streamlined in order to prevent un
necessary delays and long waits.
If persons who have either lost or
misplaced their certificates of dec
larations of intentions will notify
at once R. L. J. Carmody, president
of the Greenbelt Democratic Club,
51-J Ridge Rd., by letter or post
card only, giving full name and ad
dress, an effort will be made to ob
tain duplicates at the County
Clerks Office at Marlboro.
Charter Meeting
Postponed To Monday
An open hearing on charter
changes will be held next Monday,
September 20, at 8 p.m. in the town
office, instead of tonight as tenta
tively announced two weeks ago.
Any resident with suggestions
for changes in the town charter
may be heard at this time, Mayor
Thomas J. Canning announced. He
urged that all citizens interested
in improving this basic document
of our town government attend the
meeting.

To Talk At Women
Voters League Tea
The League of Women Voters of
Prince Georges County will launch
this year’s activities with an Open
Membership Tea and Reception
honoring as guests Honorable
Lansdale G. Sasscer, Mrs. Sasscer,
and Mr. and Mrs. C. Maurice
Weidemeyer, candidates for Con
gress from the Fifth Congressional
District of Maryland, on Thursday,
September 23, at 8 p.m. in the
Maryland Room, Home Econom
ics Building of the University of
Maryland.
This meeting will be an oppor
tunity for voters of the Fifth Con
gressional District to hear both
Mr. Sasscer, Democratic nominee
for re-election to the House of
Representatives and Mr. Wefde
meyer, Republican nominee, who
will give short talks on the posi
tion of their political parties. Rep
resentative Sasscer has served in
the House of Representatives since
lie was elected Fbruary 3, 1939,
and has been a member of the
Committee on Armed Services. He
also served as a member of the
Maryland Senate from 1922 to
1938, after having prarticed law in
Southern Maryland. Mr. Weide
meyer too, has been practicing law
since 1936, maintaining offices in
Annapolis and the District of Co
lumbia. His law practice was in
terrupted while he served in the
Armed Forces of World War II
from 1943 to 1945. During that
time Weidemeyer was- successful
in 1944, in winning over two op
ponents, the Republican Nomina
tion for Congressman in the 'Fifth
District. At the present time he is
Chairman of the Republican State
Central Committee of Anne Arun

given by the Prince George League

public, and guests will be able to
meet the candidates and their wives
personally and talk with them, as

Greenbelters Fill Gym To Protest
PHA*s Proposed Rent Increase
Greenbelters turned out over 500 strong Friday night to pro
test Public Housing Administration’s proposed new rent schedule.
Forced indoors because of bad weather, residents thronged the cen
ter school auditorium giving enthusiastic support to the town lead
ers who have initiated the fight against increased rents.
Initiated by the Cooperator, the meeting was held with thei
active support of most of the town’s community leaders. Mike,
Salzman, president of the Greenbelt Mutual Home Ownership Cor
poration, chaired the meeting after introduction by Cooperator
editor Sally Meredith.
National Legion Head
Will Visit Greenbelt
Greenbelt American Legion Post
No. 136 announced this week the
visit of National Commander Jas.
F. O’Neil to Greenbelt on Thurs
day of next week, September 23.
An extensive program has been
planned, and residents of Green
belt are invited to participate in the
activities. This date has been offi
cially proclaimed “James F. O’Neil
Day” by action of the Town Coun
cil at its August meeting.
National Commander James F.
O’Neil of Manchester, New Hamp
shire, together with staff officers of
the Washington headquarters of
the American Legion and his per
sonal aide, Ernest Jones' of Omaha,
Nebraska, will arrive in Greenbelt
shortly after 3 o’clock in the after
noon. The official party will be
greeted by Mayor Thomas J. Can
ning, members of the town council
and officials of the Greenbelt Post,
at which time Commander O’Neil
will be presented with an engrossed
copy of the official proclamation
and the key to the city.
F. 8.1. Agents To Show Skill
The party will then make an ex
tensive tour of the town under the
guidance of PHA Manager Charles
M. Cormack and Town Manager
Charles T. McDonald. The tour
will end at the Gun Club adjacent
to the disposal plant, where a group
of special agents of the FBI will
stage on exhibition of the use of
rifles, pistols and sub-machine guns
in law enforcement activities.
Police chiefs of adjoining towns'
have been invited to attend this
demonstration. Commander O’Neil
is especially interested in police
work,.due to his position as Police
Chief of the city of Manchester.
A banquet will be served for the
official party and guests- in the com
munity building at 6:30 p.m. Cater
ing will be by Greenbelt Consumer
Services, under the direction of
John Coleman. Guests will include
Major General Milton A. Reckord,
adjutant general of the State of
Maryland, who will welcome the
National Commander on behalf of
Governor Wm. Preston Lane.

Salesmanship
Charging that the increase is be
ing sought by PHA solely to get a
better sales price for Greenbelt,
Michael Salzman, president of the
Greenbelt Mutual Home Owner
ship Corporation, urged residents
to write letters of protest and to
get all town organizations to pass
resolutions protesting the increase.
Mayor Thomas C. Canning de
scribed in detail a meeting between
the town council and PHA repre
sentatives held August 19 in Wash
ington. PHA men from the re
gional office in New York and from
Washington were not as familiar
with the rent proposal as PHA
Community Manager Charles M.
Cormack who was fully prepared
with two printed rent schedules
and a three page legal size explana
tory statement which he passed out
to those present. Mr. Canning
said that the only reason given by
PHA for the proposed increase was
to make rents in Greenbelt compar
able to rents in the surrounding
area.
“Considered”
In answer to questions from the
Council, PHA said they had taken
into consideration transportation
costs, the lack of hospital facilities,
that they h§d “spot-checked” the
houses in Greenbelt, and that
“countless numbers” of other fed
erally owned communities had had
rent increases. Asked if there
would be any changes in the pres
ent maintenance policy, Mr. Cor
mack indicated there would be “if
we get the money,” Mr. Canning
reported. PHA officials admitted
the proposed rent increase for
Greenbelt was far in excess of the
increase contemplated for the sis
ter towns, Greendale and' Green
hills.
The council requested PHA to
give further study to the matter
with a view to keeping the rents
at a lower level than shown on the
schedules. The mayor reported

Health Ass’n Meets
At School Tuesday;
Reception For Staff
The September 21 membership
meeting of the Greenbelt Health
Association will be held at 8:30
p.m. in the home economics room
of the Center school, Abraham
Dury, president of the Association,
announced Tuesday. A business
session, with reports on the Asso
ciation’s progress and status, will
precede a special report on the rec
ommendations made for the local
association’s set-up by Dr. Hard
wicke of the National Cooperative
Health Federation. Time will be
set aside during the meeting, Mr.
Dury said, for membership com
ments and recommendations and
discussion.
The business session will be fol
lowed by a reception for the staff,
expected to begin at 10:15, at which
refreshments will be served. Mr.
Dury emphasized that members
are urged to be present, and that
non-members are welcome, particu
larly new residents who wish to
get acquainted with the members
and staff of the Association.

ELECTION JUST AROUND THE CORNER
In 1944 there were 48,000,000 ballots cast for
Presidential candidates. Reliable estimates indicate
that there will be 55,000,000 voters in this year's
election. Evidently what battle there is left to be
fought out concerns House and Senate seats. Both
the President and Governor Dewey have been giving
a good deal of their time in the closing days of the
campaign helping their Senate and House candi
dates.
There is not much hope among party leaders
that President Truman will be elected. Truman
supporters in the National Capital claim 229 elec
toral votes in twenty-four States. On the other
side of the house the Dewey forces claim 28 states
with a total of 334 electoral votes, which is -68
more than is required to gain the election. Thur
mond and Wallace tickets are whittling down the
Democratic strength. Well, we will not have long
to wait.
LOCAL BIGHTS
Many National groups disagree with the prac
tices of the Federal Government in providing funds
for all sorts of Tom, Dick and Harry local projects.
The Federal Government has become a sort of a
wild-cat big banker.
One common comment is to the effect that the
Government is almost a socialistic State. Are we
retracing our steps and getting back into the prac
tices of the old time New Deal.
Still, we will never be Communists because
the people of this country are never going to turn
over our Government to a small bunch of unscrupu
lous leaders. We went far enough in that direction
when the voters elected the late Mr. Roosevelt
four times.

Clarksville

Scotland Neck —The Clarks
ville Literary Club began Its
1948-49 program with a delight
ful meeting with Mrs. Douglas
Temple at her home near here.
Attractive arrangements of fall
flowers were used in the Temple
home.
Mrs. J. D. Hall, incoming pres
ident, presided and after Mrs.
James L. Vernon, newly elected
secretary-treasurer had read
the minutes of the last spring
meeting, welcomed Mrs. J. L.
Josey, who returned to the club
after a leave of absence.
The program committee, com
posed of Mesdames J. D. Hall,
James Livermon and A. S.
Daughtridge, handed out the
yearbooks. The course of study
this year will be the French nrf/
el and Mrs. J. D. Hall discussed
the program and gave back
ground for its study.
Mrs. Wade H. Dickens read
from nthe General Federatio
Clubwoman (the magazine of all
federated clubs) the address that
Gen. George Marshall had given
at the annual convention oi uie
Federation of Women’s Clubs in
Portland, Oregon last spring.
Mrs. Temple, assisted by Mes
dames Shields Alexander and
Allen Lassiter then served a sa
lad course with coffee and pecan
pies to the following members:
Mesdames Irwin Clark, Wade H.
Dickens, Balfour Dunn, J. D.
Hall, C. H. Herring, J. L Josey,
W. A. Kitchin and C. H Smith.

School Clubs
Are Formed
Scotland Neck—The extra cur
ricula activities at the Scotland
Neck Schools went into full

ganization of the various clubs.

Roanoke Rapids is pleased to

ning and two children. The Run
nings are Canadians. Mr.
Running is sulphate plant sup
erintendent of the paper mill
here.
Kenneth Running, eldest son

ed school here for the first time.
We know he will like our chools

student.

named their grandchildren. Ca
roline, aged seven, is “Wapps”,
Constance, aged six, is “Sukie”
and Katherine Charles, only one
and a half, is “Tumbleweed”.
We were very sorry to hear of
the death of Mr. Willie Shell.
All his many friends will miss
him coming by every day with
his fresh berries, fruits and veg
etables to sell.
Mrs. Charlie Butler Jenkins is
now in the routine of her new
pdsition as superintendent ol
nurses at the Roanoke Rapids

ual arangemenrts of dahlias at

Mrs. C. A. Wyche made a sug
gestion that a garden section be
included in this column. She says
she will be glad to contribute to
such an idea. Since Mrs. Wyche
has for a long time been recog
nized for her knowledge and
ability with flowers her contribu
tions would be valuable we are
sure.
Mrs. Wyche further adds there
is no such thing as a “green
thumb”. One has to have some
knowledge of a plant and, in ad
dition, must give it proper care
for good results.
The other night after dinner
while sitting by a warm fire
(which feels plenty good these
cool nights) Grandaddy was tell
ing “Granny” a joke. “Well,” he
says, “That’s like that yourtg
woman who was playing bridge.
She jumped up and said ‘My
goodness! I forgot my husband.
He was sleeping so well when I
left home I hated to wake him
up to give him his sleeping pills.”
Granny—true to form said
“What did she want to wake him
up for if he was already sleep
ing?”
JUST BETWEEN US
By Nancy Mottram
Take Your ‘Ribing’
Like A Good Sport;
Silly To Be Irked
They just don’t understand
you. Sometimes kidding gets un
der your skin, and you’re sure
they were disguising some sut
ting cracks with a laugh. It sends
you into the blues for days and
no matter how quiet you are,
how sulky your attitude, no one
takes the time to apologize and
coax you to smile.
Kidding is a great American
custom and joking is part of any
teen-ager’s makeup. There just
aren’t any ulterior meanings in
the chit-chat that’s tossed
around when the guys and gals
are in a good mood. Now and
then that cheerful sparring might
develop into more than b con
test to see who can draw the
most howls, and an intentionally
cruel remark thrown, in but it’s
rare.
You want to be in the center
of the circle and give and take
with the rest of them. Instead,
you always seem to be on the
fringes of the group. A word
comes your way occasionally,
but you don’t feel as though the
gang couldn’t have a£ good a
time without you. Maybe that’s
why you take the kidding that
comes your way as meant to
make you uncomfortable. You’ve
got the wrong idea, and unless
you turn over a new leaf by
switching your attention away
from yourself, you may have
good reason to believe that the
rest are "picking on you.’’
People love to exercise their
wit on someone who’s an easy
target. They pick a fellow or
girl who’ll take everything that
comes his way but who has a
comeback. You don’t have to
ruffle your feathers and decide
the world’s against you because
your’re the butt of a current
joke. The most-in-demand mem
ber of the crowd is the lad or
lass who can take humor with
a grin and pop right back with
another laughing remark.
If you expect someone to take
time out to explain that it’s all
in fun and to please wipe that
long look off your face, you’re
out of luck. It’s every man for
himself and you’re old enough to
be on your own with people. It’s
the same way with teasing. In
nore it and they’ll begin search
ing for a victim who’ll let it
bother and annoy him. The re
action is what counts, and if
yours is a laugh you’re on the
right side.
Anyway, it’s better than being
passed over entirely and getting
as much attention as though
your chair were empty. It’s not
hard to switch your ideas about
humor around. Once you join
the game, you’ll discover that
your suspicion that you were the
"goat” was all out of focus.
Learn to laugh with them—and
the laugh won’t be on you.
Miss Mottram wants to help
with your problems. Write her j
in care of this newspaper.

Bird Club Will
Meet Tuesday at
Wheeler Home
The Roanoke Rapids Bird Club
will hold its first meeting of
the new year on Tuesday even
ing at eight o’clock at the home
of Mrs. D. L. Wheeler on Bol
ling Road, it was announced to
day by Mrs. T. L. O'Briant, sec
retary-treasurer of the club.
Mrs. O'Briant said all mem
bers are especially urged to be
on hand for the meeting, at
which new club officers will be
elected for the year.

168 Attend
First High
School PTA
The first meeting of the high
school P.T.A. for the school year l
was well attended. One hundred I
and sixty-eight parents and tea
chers gathered in the high school j
auditorium, on Thursday evening
at eight o’clock to hear a pro
gram on “Good Health for North
Carolina Children.”
The program chairman, Mrs.
David Clark, announced the
theme for the year “United for
Youth”. She then presented Dr.
Henry Cutchin, who gave an out
line of basic community health
services, many of which Roanoke
Rapids already has. Dr. Cut
chin stated that Halifax County j
has one of the best health de
partments in the state and cer
tainly the best county health doc
tor in North Carolina, Dr. Ro-'
bert Young. Others on the pro
gram were Miss Martha Rob
Cherry, of the high school faculty 1
discussing proper nutrition; Dr.
R. A. Daniel, who discussed im
portance of dental care;
Mrs. Kathleen Suiter, school j
nurse who spoke on the school
health program; Mrs. Sam
Brown who spoke on summer re
creation. Mrs. Zeb Denny gave
the devotional for the evening.
During the business session
with Mrs. I. E. Ready, president.1
presiding, reports were heard
from the following committees
hospitality. Miss Martha Rob
Cherry, chairman; Publicity,
Mrs. George Pappendick, chair
man; and Mrs. J. E. Mausteller,
chairman of tne room repre
sentatives reported that this
group was well organized and
hoped to do good work this year.
Mrs. Sam Bunn, chairman of the
membership committee reported
184 paid members so far. Mrs.
Jessie Harrell reported 57 mem
berships from the sixth grades.
Mrs. H. S. Lov. president of the
city council P.T.A. announced the
district conference is to be held
in Roanoke Rapids on Wednes
day, October 20.
At the conclusion of the meet
ing. refreshments were enjoyed
in the library.

Mrs. Jones Releases Statement
On Gold Star Mother's Month
Mrs. N. T. Jones, president of
the local Veterans of Foreign
Wars Auxiliary and Department
Chairman of the Gold Star Mo
thers of North Carolina, has is
sued the following statement in
connection with Gold Star
Mothers month.
“I am very happy at this time
to serve the gold star mothers
of North Carolina. This month
is set aside for gold star mothers.
wives, sisters, and daughters, for
membership.
“The Halifax Auliliary of the
V. F. W. will meet on Tuesday
night, October 19, at 8 o’clock,
at 1307 Hamilton Street. If there
is a mother, wife, sister or'
daughter who wishes to come to
this meeting, we will be glad to,
have you. We need your help to
carry on the great work we are
doing in the hospital at Oteen.
“Now’ you may ask what is a
gold mother? She is the mother
of one who was regularly en
lsted, drafted, indicted or com
missioned, and who was accept
ed for and assigned to active1
duty in the Army, Navy, or Ma
rine Corps of the United States.
“Do you know the significance
of the gold star, ana wnai con
stitutes eligibility for the gold
star membership? I should like
to clarify this for you. The gold
star signifies that the blue star
has been turned to gold, by the
death while in service during ei
ther of the World Wars, of the
person for whom the blue star
was worn. Let us be mindful
always that our gold star mo
thers have made a great sacri
fice, that we, you and I, might
be free people.
“We can do so very much to
help them, the mother, wives,
sisters, and daughters, of our
war dead, and they can help,
too. if they join our auxiliary
and work for those who aren’t
able.
“This auxiliary has 110 men at
Oteen whom we help. That is
why we are pleading for your
membership. We. whose sons,
husbands, brothers and fathers
came back, could not choose a
more opportune time, than now,
to throw our arms around them
to comfort and cheer them, when
their fallen heros are being
brought back for their last, long
rest.

Is Attending

Mrs. C. Jenkins, supervisor
of nurses at the Roanoke Rapids

in Wilmington.

day convention at which the
theme will be nursing care in
community health.”

SATURDAY, OCTOBER 30, 1948
“Blame Call and praise-all are two blockheads Franklin
TRUMAN IS THE MAN
Tuesday, November 2 will be election day. On this
occasion the man who will rule the greatest nation in the
world for the next four years will be selected. That man
will become President of the United States of America.
There will be four candidates for the office of Presi
dent, and a possible 50,000,000 or more citizens will cast
their votes. The candidates are: Governor Thomas E.
Dewey of New York, Republican; Governor J. Strom
Thurmond of South Carolina, Dixiecrat; Henry A. Wal
lace, former vice-president of the United State, Progres
sive, and last, but by no means least Harry S. Truman,
now President of the United States.
We do not feel that it is necessary to say anything
about the Progressive Party candidate or the Dixiecrat.
As we see it the race will be between the major parties,
the Republican candidate, Mr. Dewey and the Democrat,
Mr. Truman.
We have no fault to find with Mr. Dewey, we believe
if elected, he will serve the country well. However, we en
dorse Mr. Truman and hope that he will be elected and
serve another term in the White House.
Mr. Truman succeeded one who in our opinion was
the greatest president in the history of the United States
—Franklin Delano Roosevelt. He took office when the
country was engaged in the most disastrous war in the
history of the world.
The war ended and this nation, generally speaking,
is back to normalcy.
True it is that the European situation at the present
time is a serious one, and peace seems again threatened,
but our country is united on its foreign policy and the
election as we see it will have little or no affect upon that
situation.
Mr. Truman, like every other human being, has
made mistakes, but he has served the country well and
should be returned to office.
The Democratic party split wide open over Mr. Tru
man’s Civil Rights Program and because of that, it seems
certain that he will lose the support of several states. If_a
record making vote is cast, he may be able to overcome
this loss. We hope he will.
There are those who believe Mr. Truman’s Civil
Rights Program was a political move to secure votes. Sup
pose it was, there is little harm in that. What ever the
purpose, the Civil Rights program is a just and righteous
cause and one that should be appreciated especially by
those it seeks to benefit.
Dr. Channing Tobias gives some very good reasons
why he is supporting Mr. Truman. Read them carefully
in this issue of the Miami Times.
On February 2, 1948 Mr. Truman had this so say:
“We cannot be satisfied until all our people have
equal opportunities for jobs, for homes, for education, for
health and for poltical expression, and until all of our
people have equal protection under the law.”
We again say, we endorse Mr. Trpman and suggest
that our readers cast their votes for his election.

High Spots of the
Agricultural News
Farmers Union meet—Farm price
support and a progressive farm pro
gram will receive much attention at
the annual Farmers Union conven
tion in Great Falls Oct. 20-23, Don
Chapman, state president, said. An
attendance of 1,500 is expected at
the Civic Center arena.
James G. Patton of Denver, na
tional FU president, is scheduled as
the keynote speaker of the conven
tion. He will make the convention's
opening address with a review of
problems facing American agricul
ture, Chapman said.
New officers will be elected and
resolutions dealing with the Mon
tana and national Farmers Union
program will be considered.
Other speakers scheduled include
Emil Syfestad, St. Paul, FU Central
Exchange general manager, discuss
ing the FU oil refinery expansion
program; J. E. Wells Jr., St. Paul,
FU Livestock association manager;
M. W. Thatcher, St. Paul, Grain Ter
minal association manager; Glenn
Talbott, Jamestown, N. D., North
Dakota FU president; C. E. Huff,
Denver, director of national FU in
surance, and Chapman.
R. E. A. meets Officers of the
Montana State Rural Electric Co
operative association were re-elected
at the closing session of the group's
two-day meeting in Great Falls.
They Were Resner Blikken, Opheim,
president; C. G. McDermott, Fair
field, vice president, and Harold
Ebaugh, Havre, secretary-treasurer.
Directors are Eugene Pike, Mis
soula, and George Lackman, Bil
lings, both re-elected.
The rural electrification adminis
tration is in the greatest period of
expansion it ever has known, Ad
ministrator Claude R. Wickard,
Washington, D. C., told representa
tives at the annual meeting.
In the three months ending June
30, compared with the same period
last year, 50 percent more people
were connected with R. E. A. lines,
he said, and in the same period 80
percent more miles of lines were
energized. In 1947, 320,000 addition
al farmers were added and figures
just released show 68.6 percent of
the farms of the United States are
electrified.
R. E. A, reports show 49.9 percent
of Montana farms now electrified
and 52.5 percent of Wyoming farms.
World record —The world record
price for commercial calves was
topped when Allen Fordyce's Bar 13
ranch steer calves sold for $76 a hun
dred at the second annual Sheridan,
Wyo., feeder cattle sale, County
Agent Pete Jensen announced.
The pen of 25 calves, champions in
the steer calf division of the show,
was purchased by experiment sta
tions of Ohio State college, Kansas
State college and Oklahoma A&M.
Selling for $7,322.60, the lot aver
aged $282.90 a head.
Average weight was 385 pounds.
Heifer calves consigned by the Bar
13 also were judged champions of
the show and brought $34.50 at auc
tion.
Sleeping sickness—Federal public
health service scientists in Hamilton
are working on equine encephalitis,
or horse sleeping sickness.
Drs. Carl M. Eklund, laboratory
senior surgeon, and James M. Bren
nan, medical entomologist, are doing
field work in North Dakota and lab
oratory research in Hamilton.
Their operations base is the Ham
ilton laboratory where they success
fully fought Rocky mountain spot
ted fever.

Results in the contest are based
on the ‘frequency rate”. That
rate is a computation of the num
ber of disabling injuries for each
million work hours. A “disabling
injury” is one that prevents the
worker from being on the job the
next day or for his next shift.
In the 1947-48 contest, on which
results just have been announced,
The Ohio Fuel Gas Co., had a fre
quency rate of 1.78, with only 11
disabling injuries in 6,191,276
hours work by employees. The
average accident frequency rate
for the 16 companies competing
was 11.57.

Presented In Recital
MISS JOYCE ELAINE AMES
Featuring the “Carnival of Vienna”, by Schuman, the “Scherzo
in Flat Minor” by Chopin, and the first movement of Beethoven's
“Third Piano Concerto in Minor” Miss Joyce Elaine Ames was
presented in a piano recital at the Arts Club, 2017 Eye street, N. W.,
Washington, D. C., October 8, by her teacher, Prof. Francis Garzia
of the National Symphony Orchestra. Mr. Garzia played the second
piano for the concerto number, which closed the program.
“Prelude and Fugue in F” by J. S. Bach opened the perform
ance which began at 8:40 and was followed by “Hungariarf Dance
Number III’’ by Brahms, “Sonata in Major” by Mozart, “Scherzo
in E Minor” by Mendelssohn and the “Second Arabesque” by De
bussy.
Joyce, who is 15, has studied the piano for over six years, and
received generous applause after each number. As an encore she
played the popular “Tarantella” by Pieczonka. At intermission she
receive generous gifts of flowers, presented by well-wishing friends
and relatives.
A student at McKinley high school in Washington. Joyce lives
with her parents, Mr. and Mrs. Richard D. Ames, at 4205 Queens
bury road, in Hyattsville. Mr. Ames will be remembered as a
former editor of The Journal. He and Mrs. Ames, the former Miss
Martha Guiler of Summerfield route, were living on Belford street,
in Caldwell, at the time Joyce was born.

(Paid Political Advertisement)
The-------------
Truth About
Joe Ferguson
TEAM
HE OPERATES A POLITICAL MACHINE
AT TOP LEVEL
HE RUNS A VOTE-CATCHING SOFTBALL
HE COLLECTS A FERGUSON “FLOWER
FUND”
HE HAS THREE STATE CARS
(From the Akron Beacon Journal, Monday, October 18, 1948)
Probably the most controversial political figure in Ohio today is
Joseph T. Ferguson, auditor of state. He checks the legality of all public
money spent.
He is running for another term and he is of special interest in the
Akron district. He is the only Democrat in the state house, and he was
elected to his job from Barberton.
His Republican opponent at the November election is Roger W.
Tracy, whose father, Joseph, long held the office.
Tracy, a Columbus man, is youthful, a trained auditor, and generally
well liked in his home-town. He is ambitious to follow in the footsteps
of his well known father. The elder Tracy once was Ferguson’s boss.
THE STATE AUDITOR’S post is only slightly less important than
that of governor.
In one respect the state auditor rates above the governor. Ferguson
can check upon the governor’s expense any time he wants to, but only
under special circumstances, and a never-used law, can the governor get
an audit of the auditor’s office.
Nobody can audit the auditor, but the auditor checks public fund
spending in all the more than 3,000 branches of government in the state.
There are a lot of Republicans in Columbus who would like to audit
Ferguson and write one of those critical reports that state examiners
write about others.
FERGUSON HAS unlimited ambition. Friends say he already is
planning on how to get into the governor’s office and how to stay in.
This explains, perhaps, why Ferguson operates a political machine
at top level.
Two of his best salaries go to B. J. DeGeorge, who is listed as “per
sonnel director,” and Clarence J. Doyle, on the payroll as Ferguson’s
secretary.
DeGeorge gets $5,375 a year and spends part of his time collecting
a “flower fund” which is a 2 percent levy against the salaries of more
than 400 employes. He ran a wall paper store at Barberton before Fer
guson put him on his present job.
THIS FUND would amount to about $19,000 a year if all the help
paid. The attorney has ruled against such funds, however, and quite a
number of examiners, termed “disloyal” by Ferguson, are not paying.
DeGeorge also serves Ferguson as a political trouble shooter, and an
entertainer of visiting political firemen.
Doyle is bulky, pleasant, and a master of political prose. He syndi
cates a column in praise of his boss which runs in Democratic weeklies
throughout the state. He also, in company with an associate, publishes a
series of neighborhood papers which are distributed throughout the
Columbus district.
4s
LIKE DeGEORGE, Doyle has plenty of personality. He came to
Ferguson after serving two terms, one in the penitentiary for issuing a
bad check, and one in the reformatory for embezzlement. He is the
office publicity chief.
The two jobs are unique in state government. They arc not more
unusual, however, than the auditor’s soft ball team, which is used frankly
for its vote-catching ability.
A check of the July and August state payrolls shows the names of
eight team members. Some of these athletes get the higher pay schedules.
JOHN NOLAN, team manager, was receiving $15 a day Neil Mur
phy, $13.64 a day John C. Brown and Harry Cullen, $10.65 Emil Almay,
$9.45 .Lawrence Whalen, $9,034 Tod McKinney, and Dick Southard,
$8,425 a day.
Ferguson expressed surprise that so many men were getting state
pay. “We wouldn’t average over five players on the payroll,” he said.
The auditor says the team played at night, which left their days free
for work in his office. Columbus newspapers reported the team traveled
more than 5,000 miles this year.
The auditor of state says the public reaction is good. “That team
was once runner up for the national championship. It has drawn a 5,000
turnout in predominately Republican counties,” he says.
ANOTHER ACTIVITY that has caused some unrest is the number
of Ferguson relatives on the payroll. Brother John is chief examiner for
highway division at $5,775 a year brother Leo gets $14.11 a day in the
liquor department brother-in-law Fred Cooney receives $8.68 a day as
a liquor department examiner.
Son James gets $8.36 as an assistant examiner, and son Tom gets the
same amount in the summer when he is not in school at Ohio State
university.
Two daughters, Anna Marie and Theresa were on the payroll prior
to their marriages.
Another advantage of office is that Ferguson has three Buick autos
assigned to his office by the state. They have what appear to be private
license numbers on them. There are no “State Car” labels on them.
TWO OF THESE cars are seen frequently at the Ferguson home at
night. One traveled 3,400 miles between Aug. 6 and Sept. 15.
On Sept. 8 this car got 19 gallons of gasoline at the state garage. On
the 9th it was back for 24 gallons. On the 10th it got 15 gallons, and used
from 13 to 16 gallons a day for the rest of the period.
State use of autos is much more free than that of local officials.
There is no requirement that the cars account for the time used on pri
vate business. Ferguson has no private auto.
ISSUED BY TRACY-FOR-AUDITOR COMMITTEE
James B. Richards, Secretary, Columbus, Ohio

I always put in a bad month
immediately following the an
nouncement of a new “How to”
book. At the moment I am strug
gling with an almost uncontroll
able craving for Dale Carnegie's
new book on how to stop worry
ing, and it is only by the greatest
strength of will and by planning
my shopping so that I always
pass bookstores on the other side
of the street, that I have so far
kept temptation behind me. Not
that I am fooling myself. Within
another week I shall probably
lurch into a bookshop, my eyes
staring, my hands shaking, grasp
the nearest counter for support,
and cry, “Quick! A copy of
Carnegie’s ‘How to Stop Wor
rying’ and water on the side!”
The point is, I am a push-over
for books on _ self-improvement.
Let somebody "write a “How to”
book on any subject and it will
instantly awaken the conviction
that I am the world’s most wit
less and maladroit man in that
particular regard and I will want
that book and will buy it. I may
even get around to reading it.
At the .moment, however, I am
trying to break this habit. I am
not trying to taper off; I am try
ing to break it entirely and I am
using Mr. Carnegie’s book in the
effort. I have the feeling that if
I get by “How to Stop Worrying”
the next such book will be easier
to resist and eventually we may
look forward to the day when I
will cease to come staggering
home on pay day with my arms
full of books instead of groceries.
A happy day for my family, I’ll
tell you that.
How those lovely people my
family, I mean—have borne with
me through this trial and how
they have worked to help me
conquer the affliction! Referring
to themselves
as Bookaholics
Anonymous they never weary of
providing me with complaints
and counsel and just yesterday
they proposed a course that of
fers a ray of hope. They suggest
ed that I look at all the other
“How to” books in .my library to
discover whether or not they had
really done me any good. To this
I glumly agreed and they were
soon pawing among the shelves
for the results of the past book
buying binges.
The first one they handed me
was a DOOR cauea now to DNis
At once an old torn ligament in
my right leg began to twinge
and I had to confess that this
volume, so eagerly purchased
some year* ago, had led to noth
ing but splints and arnica.
The children next approached
me with a book titled “How to
Raise Children” and then, as I
began to turn the pages, they
launched into a bitter quarrel
and began to dash bric-a-brac at
each other. There is no telling
how largely they might have
damaged the house and them
selves had I not seen the object of
the demonstration and screamed
at them to behave. This they did
and, their point being made, they
quietly relieved me of “How to
Raise Children” and returned it
to the shelves.
By this time my nerves were
frayed, understandably so I
think, and my wife had to trot
along at my side with her next
offering. “Please stop pacing
back and forth and biting your
“I
fingernails,” she implored.

sion’, ‘How to Relax’, and ‘How
