Maybe You'll Vote
How long did it take you to cast your ballot by voting machine
in the primaries?
Can you cut that time down to one minute and 36 seconds in
the November elections—making your selections for national,
state and local offices and voting “yes” or “no” on the various
levies and bond issues?
If you can and if several other “ifs” are disposed of satis
factorily, everybody who wants to vote November 2 may do so.
But if anything goes wrong many voters—perhaps thousands
of them—are going to be disappointed.
Consider the statistics, the facts—and the “ifs”:
There are 399 voting precincts in the county with an esti
mated registration of 200,000 voters, an average of 500 voters to
a precinct. Of these precincts 267 are in the city of Dayton.
The county owns 300 voting machines and plans to place 198
of them in voting places in the city.
Since some of the precincts have more than 500 registrations,
manifestly, to maintain the 500 average, some must have less
than 500. Let us, to be conservative and on the safe side, use
450 as the average of precincts using voting machines.
The polls will be open 12 hours on election day. That will
mean, in order to accommodate the 450 voters, each machine will
have to take care of 37.5 persons an hour for each of the 12 hours.
That figures one minute and 36 seconds per person.
To take care of that many voters there must be a steady, even
flow of citizens every minute of the 12 hours. But voters don’t
arrive at the polls in that fashion. Most persons who are em
ployed vote between the hours of 6 and 8 a.m. and 4 and 6 p.m.
During the rest of the day, ordinarily, there are spurts and dead
periods.
In the primary election hundreds of persons did not get to
vote. Some few of them complained and the newspapers carried
stories of men and women who, confronted by long lines of wait
ing voters, became discouraged and left without voting. Other
hundreds, perhaps thousands, simply went on home without vot
ing and said nothing about it. But if they are deprived of a vote
the second time they are not likely to be so quiet about it.
So everybody, mathematically, will get a chance to vote if the
voters arrive at the polls at the rate of 37.5 persons an hour for
the 12 hours; if nobody takes longer than one minute and 36
seconds to make his choice, and if no mechanical troubles arise
to put any of the machines out of commission for one minute.
And what can be done about it? This: the law does not say
that voting machines must be used in all precincts. Nor does it
limit the machines to one for each precinct.
If, instead of trying to spread the 300 voting machines over as
many precincts as possible, the board of elections would install
two or three in each voting machine precinct, instead of one, all
voters could be accommodated. The rest of the precincts could
use the old-fashioned—and less confusing—paper ballots. For
some inscrutable reason the law will not permit both machines
and paper ballots in the same precinct.
So unless the members of the board of elections want to risk
bringing the wrath of countless indignant voters down on their
heads they will “bunch” the voting machines and let the rest of
us use paper ballots.
The situation, of course, would be helped if non-employed
voters would cast their ballots after 8 a.m. and before 4 p.m., and
if all employed’persons who can be spared from their jobs or
suffer the loss of pay would take off a half day to vote.
But the election board would be wise not to count on this
miracle, for it’s not likely to happen.

Traffic Control
Sanity Returns
To South Main
A measure of sanity was re
turned to traffic control in Day
ton Tuesday night, when, thanks
to Mayor Louis W. Lohrey, the
city ordered operation of the two
traffic lights in front of the Na
tional Cash Register plant on
South Main street only during
rush-hour periods. As a caution
the signals will flash red and
amber during the remainder of
the day.
Mayor Lohrey, acting upon
complaint of hundreds of Dayton
motorists who contended con
tinuous operation of the lights
impeded traffic, insisted no spe
cial consideration be shown the
N.C.R. He contended that no
good purpose was served in forc
ing traffic to slow down to 15
miles an hour in front of the
plant where, except during rush
hours, there is practically no
cross traffic.
Captain Paul Price contended,
that continuous operation of the
lights had stopped speeding on
South Main street. Observation
of motorists is that drivers, irked
by the unnecessary
“funeral
pace” of traffic, really stepped
on the gas after passing the
plant, creating more speeding
than obtained before the lights
were placed in 24-hour opera
tion.
Now that Mayor Lohrey has
become
interested
in
traffic
control, it is to be hoped that he
will direct his attention to other
sore spots in Dayton where traf
fic light timing is tying up the
movement of vehicles, causing
delay and congestion unneces
sarily. Lohrey seems to be the.
only man in city hall who is
thinking sanely about traffic and
is placing public convenience
above the prevalent insane trend
toward regimentation.
$125,292 More
Montgomery county resi
dents had $125,292.76 less
money to jingle
in their
pockets during the week end
ing September 11, for that was
the amount they paid in sales
tax to swell the huge surplus
in the state’s coffers. That
week’s contribution increased
the sales tax payments by
county residents to $5,460,
212.98 for the year, as com
pared with only $4,839,642.33
during the corresponding pe
riod of 1947.
Report of the state treas
urer showed a balance of
$225,713,481.60 in the state
treasury on September 17.

HAIL OUR YOUNGEST
READER!
Our youngest reader is undoubt
edly Richard Gerry Winger, 7
months old infant of Mr. and Mrs.
Robert Winger.
Richard, his proud parents de
clare, gazes intently at every copy
of the Arizona Post he can get
his hands on. When he finally sees
something he doesn’t like —he
tears up the paper.
Concert Series Os
Chamber Music To
Op en On Sunday
Because a small group of per
sons want to hear more chamber
music more often and because
they want to hear music not often
played, the Arizona Friends of
Music has been formed under the
leadership of such persons as Har
ry Richel and Arthur Blair. Oth
ers serving on the executive board
are Sam Fain, Madeline Schutz
bank, George Rosenberg, Stuart
Udall and David Patrick.
As a non-profit organization,
they are scheduling a series of
four concerts. If subscription vol
ume permits, additional concerts
will be given at no additional cost
to ticket holders. Subscription
rates were purposely placed at the
low rate of $12.50 for 2 tickets,
with the privilege allowed mem
bers to bring an occasional guest.
The first concert will be held
at 4 p.m. Sunday, Oct. 17 at the
Woman’s Club, West Alameda
St. The artist trio will be the very
well-known Copinsky, violin; Kal
man Bloch, clarinet; Frances Mul
len, piano.
Season subscription may be se
cured from Blair’s Bookshop, 1028
N. Park or by sending your check
to that address, payable to Ariz
ona Friends of Music. Individual
concert tickets will not be sold.

ness and peace have kissed each other.”

Citizens Of Area Are Urged To
Register So Tliev Mav Cast Vote
Tomorrow the registration books will be open
again offering citizens of this section a duty and a
privilege of which all too few will take advantage.
Within a short time there will be another Gen
eral Election in the nation, and it is expected gener
ally that a lot of votes will be cast. North Caro
lina, however, will probably do little voting in the
General Election because most people feel that the
real election was completed in the Democratic pri
maries in the Spring. For that reason the1'
away from the polls in droves, little realizing that
primary votes are not even considered when it c„...es
to appointment of privileges for the people—all those
are based on votes cast in a general election.
One of the chief troubles with the citizens of our
own countv is that they have too little interest in
their right’ to the ballot* They lose sight of the fact
that the right to the free ballot was one of the basic
issues that" their forefathers sought in the American
Revolution of 1776.
Most of the people take it for granted that Kerr
Scott has already been elected Governor and J. M.
Broughton has already been elected to the United
States Senate without considering that these men
will not be the elected officials of this state until the
November General Election ballots are tabulated. It
is probably a certainty, that in North Carolina that
the men who have already won the nomination in
the Democratic primaries will be elected. It is the
vote which is cast- however, to make that election of
ficial that is going to be considered when the duly
elected officials of the state and nation start con
sidering how many voters have bestirred themselves
enough to take an interest in the public issues facing
Tomorrow the registration books in Halifax and
surrounding counties will be open again to allow those
v hose names are not already there to qualify as
voters in the November voting.
It is the duty of every person in this section who
considers himself a citizen of his township, county,
state or nation to see to that he is correctly regist
ered: and it is a duty and a privilege of these same
people to shake off the political apathy which has
encumbered our state for so long by going to the
pells and voting for somebody for something on
November 2.
Clergyma 11 Explains That Other
Ministers Do IVot Understand U. S.
Dr. Ralph \V. Sockman, well known clergyman,
recently returned to this country from the Amster
dam Conference of churches, with the observation
that the opposition to capitalism by delegates was
based upon misunderstanding of that system as it
exists in the United States.
The clergyman points out that the resolution con
demning Communism and Capitalism had been re
vised to refer to “laissez faire” capitalism. He points
out that Americans “were in a minority group” be
cause most of the other delegates live under some
kind of socialized government. He commented: “They
don’t know what our American-capitalism really is.”
This very interesting statement indicates that
the conference, in connection with the resolution as
to capitalism, was dealing with a subject-matter of
which most of the delegates were nuiformed. This
points again to the folly of religious organizations
passing resolutions upon secular subjects and indi
cates that it might be well for the church, individu
ally and collectively, to confine itself to the inter
pretation of spiritual matters.

OCTOBER 8/ 1871, at 9 25 P M., flames rose up
in Patrick O'Leary’s barn just north of the river
in Chicago. Just how it started is a matter of
legend and conjecture. But before its spread
stopped, on Oct 9. it had enveloped an area of
1900 acres. Some 13,000 buildings were de
stroyed. and nearly 100,000 persons were made
homeless. The loss figured in money was $200,
000.000—the equivalent today of a billion dollars.
The loss of life, fortunately and almost miracu
lously. was small.
When fire struck that night, most of Chicago's
60.000 buildings were wooden: pine The city had
sprung up rapidly to 300.000 population, and
there was little substantial construction There
were no effective fire prevention regulations, and
an inadequate fire department Drought had lay
on the city all summer; the rainfall was less than
30% of normal for the season, and for weeks hot
winds had swept in from the prairies. The build
ings were dry as tinder. The waterworks and
the water supply both were inadequate. The situ
ation was made to order for a conflagration such
as the country had never had before.
It was not put out; it burnt itself out.
But one result was more attention to fire pro
tection in American cities. And each year, coin
cident with anniversary of the Chicago Fire, the
U. S. observes Fire Prevention Week.

needleman but has taken his out

L. and into and out of the C. I.
O. And back into the A. F. of
L. again He saw no wrong in
that. In each instance, his con
duct constituted dual unionism
and or disruption in the first
degree but was convered by the
privilege which permits a union
to flout its own moral laws.

These five, however, subject U*

indefinite purposes and how the

tween the purposes.
The union did not promise that

made in his name. The union

cess. And where union employ
ers farmed out work to contrac

to the union with respect to in

ber wasn’t eligible for any “bene
fit.”

uiuuuy muraer. ineymigni nave

THE MARION PROGRESS
A WEEKLY NEWSPAPER DEVOTED TO THE BEST INTERESTS OF THE PEOPLE OF MARION AND McDOWELL COUNTY
ESTABLISHED 1896 MARION, N, C., THURSDAY, OCTOBER 14, 1948 VOL. 53 No. 13
Marion PTA
Meet Attended
By More Than 200
The Marion Parent-Teacher as
sociation held its first evening meet
ing Tuesday with more than 200
mothers, fathers, grandparents and
teachers present.
Mrs. R. R. Cook, membership
chairman, spoke on attendance in
crease, and reported on the mem
bership drive, stating that 270 paid
memberships had been received
thus far. There were three grades
in the elementary school who had a
hundred per cent membership and
one in high school.
These pupils, together with their
teacher, received a pass to the
movies. These were Mrs. Effie Mor
ris, second grade, Mrs. Marshall
Dark's fourth grade, Mrs. Leola
Wright's sixth grade and Mrs. Pat
Davis', ninth grade.
Mrs. Sam Yancey, president,
thanked the teachers, grade moth
ers and committee members for
their woi-k during the past weeks in
completing the drive successfully.
ivirs. josepn ryatt, ways ana
means chairman, announced plans
for a Hallowe'en Carnival to be
held in the high school building on
Saturday 29, at 7:30 o'clock.
Mrs. Homer Beaman, program
chairman, presented P. J. Story,
who introduced Charles'' Elledge,
principal of the Marion High school.
Elledge spoke briefly on his teach
ers and the severe handicaps under
which he said they were laboring,
but commended them for the man
ner in which he said they were car
rying on under this teacher load.
He stressed the importance of
keeping the children in school every
possible day and said the farther a
boy or girl goes in school, the great
er will be his or her earning capaci
ty and the better citizen he will
make.
He then gave a humorous pro
gram including several poems, jokes
and an original reading.
Mrs. Yancey asked that the sug
gestion box, which has been install
ed in the elementary school over
the telephone, continue to be used
and said the suggestions received so
far would be studied and acted up
on, if possible.
Mrs. David Gray's eighth grade
won the attendance prize in the
grammar school with 71 per Cent ol
parents present and Mrs. Pat Davis
minth grade won for the high school
with 39 per cent of parents. Mrs
Clarence Mcintosh won the dooi
prize, a cake donated by Mrs. Paul
Story.
GILBRETH SPEAKS
AT ROTARY MEETING
Hal Gilbreth, of the Y'eterans Ad
ministration office in Asheville
spoke on "National Employ the
Physically Handicapped Week," al
the meeting of the Marion Rotarj
club Friday.
Ben Hendrix, of Shelby, was
visiting Rotarian.
7,530 X-Rays
Made To Date
In This County
The two mobile x-ray units oper
ating in this county had made a total
of 7,530 x-rays through last Satur
day, Mrs. Wade Britt, chairman oi
the McDowell County Tuberculosis
association, announced this week.
Unit two, operating in rural and
industrial areas, completed its work
last Wednesday. In addition to the
numbers previously reported, this
unit made 45 x-rays at W. J. Baker's
store, 93 at the Prison Camp, 152 in
Bridgewater community.
Last week unit four, stationed in
front of the court house, made 275
x-rays Tuesday; 177 Wednesday;
186 Thursday; 208 Friday, and 294
Saturday.
This unit will complete its work
in this county on Saturday, October
16. Between now and Saturday the
Marion Junior Woman's club, spon
sor of the project, hopes that the to
tal number of x-rays may be
brought to at least 10,000.
Consecrated Bishop
The Rev. Matthews George Henry,
formerly of Charlotte, who was
consecrated third bishop of the
Episcopal Diocese of Western North
Carolina in a colorful and tradition
al service at Trinity church in Ashe
ville Wednesday, September 29.
Bishop Henry will visit this parish
Jon Sunday, November 7, for a ser
vice at St. John's church,

BATTiSTA
PRESENTED
IN CONCERT
j Joseph Battista, American pian
ist, performed brilliantly before an
appreciative audience in the first
concert to be sponsored by the Mar
ion Comjmunity Concert association
jat the Marion High School auditor
j ium Wednesday evening.
The 30 year old musician was,
born in Philadelphia of Italian par
1 ents, graduated from Philadelphia
public schools, winning the All-Jun
ior High Contest for pianists en
route. Later he won a scholarship
I for the Philadelphia Conservatory
of Music, his first contact with Olga
Samaroff as teacher and guide.
After a year at the Conservatory
he won a Fellowship in the Juilliard
Graduate School and came to New
York. There he worked in Mrs.
Samaroff's piano master-class at the
same time studying languages, theo
I ry, ensemble, etc.
He won the Youth Contest of the
Philadelphia orchestra and the a
ward was an appearance under Eu
gen Ormandy in the Rachmaninoff
j C minor Concerto. He made his
debut January 18, 1939. Soon after
awards he won the Pennsylvania
State Contest of the National Fed
eration of Music clubs.
I nie year uei-urt; tjiiueieu
ithe war, Columbia Concerts held a
contest to further good-will between
the United States and Brazil and
Battista won the reward—a trip to
Brazil and a tour of the country
arranged through the pianist Guio
mar Novaes.
I He sailed in July, 1941 to Rio de
Janeiro as musical emissary of his
country, remaining in Brazil two
months, playing before cultural so
cieties, schools of music, colleges,
over the air, with symphony orches
tra, and in recitals of his own.
On the threshold of internation
al recognition, he returned home.
Pearl Harbor came, and before long
Battista was in uniform. For three
years he turned his back on his ca
reer and gave his talents to a coun
try at war.
His program here last night was
varied and displayed his abilities as
a versatile musician. The program
i included compositions by Bach,
j Beeithoven, Rachmaninoff, Chopin,
j Schubert, Barber and others.
I Responding to encores at the
j close of his program, he presented
j a humorous selection, and a num
j ber, which he explained was typical
ly South American.
Battista was introduced by P. J.
Story. Following the concert he re
j ceived a group of admirers back
stage, lead by an enthusiastic crowd
J of juvenile autograph hunters,
j Battista is booked for a number
of concerts this year. He perform
I ed recently at the Transylvania
I Music Camp, Brevard, at Statesville
and a few years ago in Raleigh.

American Legion
Has District Meet
The Fifth District of the Ameri
can Legion held its first meeting of
the year at the Royal Cafe last Fri
day evening with Division Com
mander C. B. Dobson as host.
Dobson spoke on "Planning,
Thinking and Acting" after a gen
eral discussion had been given by
the District Commanders.
Rev. George H. Needham was
elected Membership Chairman of
Division Five and C. M. Stepp was
named oratorical chairman of this
Division.
Commanders of districts '29
through 35 were present at the
meeting.
Hunting Season Will
Open October 15 on
Six Types Of Game
Friday, October 15, will be a big
day for local sportsmen as it marks
the opening of the deer, bear, squir
rel, oppossum, raccoon and Russian
wild boar season, according to T. W.
Gowan, game warden.
The official hunting dates and the
bag limits are as follows:
Bear—October 15 to January 1,
day bag limit 2, season lim;t 2.
Deer—November 15, 16 and 17,
day bag limit 1, season limit 1. This
is for male with visible antlers.
Wild Boar—October 15 to Janu
ary 1, day bag limit 1, possession
limit 2, and season bag limit 2.
Oppossum and Raccoon—October
15 to February 15, with dog and
gun, no limit.
Rabbits—November 25 to Janu
ary 10, day bag limit 7, possession
limit 14, and no season limit.
Squirrels—October 15 to January
10, day bag limit 6, possession 12,
season bag limit 100. (This applies
to all except red squirrel or boom
er)
Quail—November 25 to January
10, day bag limit 8, possession lim
it 16, season bag limit 100.
Turkey—November 25 to Janu
ary 10, day bag limit 1, possession
limit 2, and season bag limit 6.
(Gobblers only).
Grouse—November 25 to January
10, day bag limit 2, possession 4,
season bag limit 10.
Mrs. Penland Dies
After Brief Illness
Funeral services for Mrs. Marie
Evelyn Whitson Penland, 24, who
died Friday morning after a brief
illness, were conducted at Garden
Creek Baptist church Sunday after
noon at 2 o'clock by her pastor, the
Rev. Chas. C. Parker. Burial was
in Oak Grove cemetery.
Surviving are the husband, Lat J.
Penland; one son, Lat James Jr.;
mother, Mrs. Marion A. Whitson of
Marion; five sisters, and three
brothers, Mrs. Carl McKinney, Miss
Ruby Whitson, Walter, and Edward
Whitson of Marion, Mrs. Clarence
Duncan, Marion R. F. D. No. 4, Mrs.
Paul Morris of Glenwood, Mrs. Cecil
Moody of Old Fort R. F. D. No. 1,
and Herbert Whitson of Corpus
Christi, Texas.
Stameys Purchase
Business Property
The building occupied by the
Roseland Florist on North Main
street has been purchased by Mr.
and Mrs. Nelson Stamey of Mor
ganton from Mr. and Mrs. L. D.
Greene. The Roseland florist is
owned and operated by the Sta
meys.

CLUB ANNOUNCEMENT
Home Demonstration club
members throughout the coun
ty are urged to turn io book
reports immediately so that re
ports may be completed for

5, Miss Jean Steele, home dem
onstration agent, announced

WILDLIFE CLUB
URGES HUNTERS
TO JOIN CLUB
The McDowell County Wildlife
club passed resolutions asking the
State Wildlife Resources Commis
sion to launch a state-wide program
to encourage hunters to join their
local Wildlife clubs, at a meeting
held in the community building here
Monday night.
The resolutions climaxed a round
table discussion led by S. L. Home
wood, president. It was pointed out
that members should publicize the
fact that every person receiving a
hunting license is eligible for mem
bership in the McDowell County
Wildlife club. During the discus
sion members said that by joining
the Wildlife club hunters help to re
store game in McDowell county and
are naturally more interested in
seeing that the game is protected.
They also mentioned members' in
terest in enforcing game regulations
pertaining to the bag- limit.
Dr. J. F. Jonas was appointed
chairman of a nominating commit
tee and Cato Holler and W. E. Mor
gan were named members of the
committee.
They were instructed to submit a
list of candidates at the November
meeting and in December officers
for the coming year will be elected.
Prior to the program a fish fry
was prepared by the members under
the direction of Fate Lentz and E.
D. Ricks.

Navy To Honor Dead
In Rites October 24
The Navy will conduct a me
morial service Sunday, October 24,
for men who died in World War II,
it was announced by the Navy De
partment.
Relatives and friends of naval
personnel who died in World War II
are asked to send one flower, pre
ferably wild or home-grown, to the
Navy Public Information Officer at
Cherry Point or Wilmington, so that
it may be taken out to sea and scat
tered on the waters as a part of the
memorial program.
The flowers should arrive prior to
midnight Saturday, October 23,
Mrs. J. E. Melton, president of the
American Legion Auxiliary, an
nounced yesterday.
Democratic Precinct
Meetings Announced
i
Chairman S. J. Westmoreland
announced yesterday that a Demo- j
cratic precinct meeting for Glen
wood, Higgins and Bracketts will be
held at Glenwood high school Thurs
day night, Oct. 14, at 7:30 o'clock;
also precinct meetings for Old Fort
precinct 1 and 2 and Crooked Creek
will be held at the high school build
ing in Old Fort Friday night at
7:30.
Young Democrats
Plan Barbecue
And Rally Here
The Young Democrats of Mc
Dowell county made plans to hold a.
barbecue on the Marion High school
field on October 16, at a meeting
held in the Democratic headquar
ters Thursday evening.
The Marion High School band
will parade Main street and con
clude at the field where the barbe
cue will be served free to all at 6:30
o'clock.
Major L. P. McLendon, of
Greensboro, former president of the
North Carolina Bar Association, and.
Clarence 0. Ridings, solicitor of this
district, will be speakers.
The rally is county-wide and is»
expected to attract hundreds of
Democrats.
Registration Books
Opened Saturday For
The General Election
Registration books for McDowell,
county's general election of Tues
day, November 2nd, opened Sat
urday, October 9th in each of the.
county's 17 precincts. The books*
will remain open on October 16th„
and the 23rd in the respective pre
cincts. Voters may register at their
polling place on those two days.
At other times during the week, be
tween those dates, voters may pre
sent themselves to the registrar at
his home and be registered.
Saturday, October 30th will be*
challenge rday and the election will
be held November 2nd.
All voters who have become of
age since the last election may reg
ister and those who have moved
from one precinct to another, or
who have moved to McDowell coun
ty from another county or state will,
be required to register.
Attention of ex-service men is di
rected to the fact that if they have
never registered, but voted while in
service, they must register. The
provision for voting while in service
was a temporary measure and the
name of the voter did not go on the»
registration book.
Registration for municipal or
special elections do not qualify one*
to vote in the general election.
Instruments Filed
During Past Year
The Register of Deeds office com
piled a report this week of the work
accomplished during the past year.
According to figures released by
Miss Ruth Lackey, register of deeds,
there were 3,071 conveyances filecL
in that office from October 1, 1947
to October 1, 194B. During the same*
period the office issued 183 mar
riage licenses, 50 delayed birth cer
tificates were recorded, 13 marriage
licenses from other states recorded.
50 income tax licenses filed and 45>:
beer licenses issued. From January
1, 1948 to August 1, 1948 the office*
issued 710 birth and death certifi
cates.
Mary Price
To Speak Here
Next Saturday
Mary Price, progressive candidate*
for Governor will speak at the court
house in Marion at 2 p. m., Satur
day, October 1&, according to an
nouncement this week.
She was born on a tobacco farm:
in Rockingham county, graduated
from the University of North Caro
lina, was secretary to Walter Lipp
ma.i on the New York Herald Tri
bune. From 1945-48 she served as
executive secretary of the Commit
tee on Human Welfare of North
Carolina.
Miss Price is chairman of the Pro
gressive party of North Carolina,
member of the Presbyterian church,
member of the U. D. C. and th.3
CIO.

Greenbelt Cooperator
AN INDEPENDENT NEWSPAPER
Published Every Thursday By The Greenbelt Cooperative Publishing Association, Inc., 8 Parkway, Greenbelt, Maryland
Vol. 13, No. 5 Greenbelt, Maryland, Thursday, September 23, 1948 Five cents
Jimmy O’Neil Day Celebrated
As Town Fetes Legion Head
“James F. O’Neill Day,” sponsored by the Greenbelt Post No.
136 of the American Legion, was inaugurated this afternoon by the\
arrival of National Commander James F. O’Neil and his staff.
After being officially welcomed by Mayor Thomas B. Canning and
presented with the key to the city, the official party was conducted
on a tour of Greenbelt.
FBI Here
An exhibition of the use of wea
pons in law enforcement activities
was staged at the rifle range by
special agents of the FBI. Police
officials of surrounding towns were
special guests.
Commander O’Neil and official
guests are being entertained at a
banquet in the Community Build
ing at 6:30 this evening. Guests
will include Major General Milton
A. Reckord, who will welcome the
National Commander on behalf of
Governor Wm. Preston Lane.
The program for this evening in
cludes a review of Battery “A”,
224th F.A. Bn., Greenbelt’s own
National Guard Unit, under the
lights at Braden Field at 7:30 p.m.
followed by a concert by the
Greenbelt Community Band in the
auditorium of the Community
Building.
Public Reception
A public reception iwill follow
the concert to which all residents
are invited. Among the prominent}
guests who have accepted invita
tions are Past National Command
ers Harry Colmery, Paul H. Grif
fith and Lynn U. Stanbaugh of the
American Legion; National Execu
tive Committeeman Harry A. Allen,
Department Commander John D.
Carman and Department Adjutant
J. Nelson Tribby of the Depart
ment of Maryland and Department
President Mrs. T. Frank Lynch of
the American Legion Auxiliary.
Congressman Lansdale G. Sas
scer, State Senator L. Harold Soth
oron. Other state and county
officials and officials of adjoining
towns are also expected to attend.
Commander O’Neil will deliver
the main address of the evening fol
lowing the reception. Refresh
ments will be served by the Ladies
Auxiliary of Greenbelt Post No.
136..
The celebration will conclude
with a dance to be held in the new
American Legion Auditorium from
10 p.m. to 1 a.m. Music will be
furnished by Walter Hubbell’s or
chestra. Admission to the dance
will be by wearing the “Jimmy
O’Neil Day” booster button, which
may be obtained at the door. Pro
ceeds from the sale will be used to
defray all expenses.
Grips Will Collect
Rent Protest Letters
A letter-writing campaign, spon
sored by the Greenbelt Rent Pro
test Committee, will be climaxed
Saturday night when young Green
belters between the ages of 10 and
17 will participate in a “scavenger
hunt” for letters.
All residents were urged this
week by Mayor Thomas J. Can
ning, chairman of the committee,
to write to Walter R. Heath, Rent
Director, 400 E. Lombard Street,
Baltimore, Maryland, whose office
makees the final decision on Public
Housing Administration’s applica
tion for an increase in Greenbelt
rents.
“GRIPS” (Greenbelt Rent In
crease Protest Scavengers) will as
semble at thte Cooperator office
Saturday night at 7, and be assign
ed routes to cover in picking up
letters to Heath. Stamps will be
provided by the committee, who
will also mail the letters.
“GRIPS’ expenses, the mayor
said, would come out of the money
contributed to the committee fund.
Health Association Holds
It’s Quarterly Meeting
The Greenbelt Health Associa
tion quarterly meeting, attended
by 50 members, last Tuesday night,
was devoted primarily to a review
of the last six months’ progress of
the Association, and a summary of
its present status. Dr. Abraham
Dury, president, pointed out to the
members that the Association now
had a permanent two-man staff,
and that the board of directors was
working on plans for revision of
the extra charge fees, and purchase
of new equipment as soon as feasi
ble. He challenged the member
ship, pointing out that the next
move was up to them, and asking
that they now assume their “single
major responsibility—that of pro
moting the Health Association to
others in the community, so as to
increase thte membership strength.”
A goal of 450 members was set by
Dr. Dury so that medical coverage
to the membership could be in
creased, and aditional equipment
could be purchased for use by the
medical staff.
Thomas Ritchie, treasurer, re
ported to the membership a loss of
$769 for 1948. Only $25 of this had
been incurred since April 1, he
stated, which also included the sum
mer months when income normal
ly drops. He pointed out an im
provement in the ratio of liabili
ties to assets, and stated that the
Health Association is a going con
cern, and can adequately support

Center PTA Plans
Social Meeting
The Center school PTA is plan
ning a gala social evening for their
meeting Tuesday, September 28.
After a short business meeting at
8:30 p.m. in the school auditorium
at which time the objectives of the
PTA for the year and the new
homeroom mother program will
be outlined, the teachers will be in
troduced and the group will adjourn
to the social room where games by
Ellen Linson will be featured and
refreshments will be served. Mrs.
Phillip Crofford, hospitality chair
man in charge, promises home
made cookies and coffee.
On Wednesday, September 29, at
2 p.m., Mrs. Weagley. supervisor
of cafeterias, will speak to a group
of cafeteria mothers in the Center
school auditorium to explain the
Type A lunch which will be served
this year. The cafeteria will open
October 1. Mrs. White and Mrs.
Coleman will be in charge.
A meeting will be held at 3:30
p.m., Friday, October 1 in the Cen
ter school auditorium for all Cen
ter school mothers and teachers, to
explain the new homeroom mother
program. Afternoon programs of
the Psychology of the School-Age
Child will be discussed. ‘Lesson
Trouble,’ ‘The Truth about Report
Cards’ and ‘What to do about;
Quarreling Among Children’ are
some of the topics outlined in this
program. Individual homerooms
will meet with their teachers after
wards to elect various officers. It
is hoped that a large number of
mothers will attend so that they
can set up the type of program they
wish.
Come To Grips
With Rent Protest
On Saturday Night
Are you between the ages of 10
and 17? Yeah? Then you’re eli
gible to join the GRIPS.
GRIPS, that is: Greenbelt Rent
Increase Protest Scavengers.
Once a Gripper, you’re eligible
for free refreshments, and in line
for one of those prizes to be hand
ed out Saturday night.
You see, kids, to make it as pain
less as possible for your old man
to get that letter in to Rent Direc
tor Walter R. Heath! the Greenbelt
Rent Control Committee is offering
to stamp and mail it for him.
Here’s where you come in: to
make it even easier, you go around
and pick up the letters and take
them to the committee. They’ll be
waiting for you down at the Co
operator office, with refreshments!
and prizes for the Grippers bring
ing in the most letters.
Here are the rules: Report to
the basement of 8 Parkway at 7
p.m. You will be assigned a cer
tain section of town, which you may
cover either alone or with com
panions. Although letters will not
be counted until 10 p.m., you can
bring them back before that if you
’ve completely covered your terri
tory. Refreshments will be there
for you whenever you get back.
You don’t have to hang around for
the count, since we hope it will take
hours. Prizewinners will be an
nounced in next week’s Coopera
tor.
Drop-Inn Resumes
Program October 4
The Drop-Inn, Greenbelt’s youth
center, will open for its fall program
on Monday, October 4, it was an
nounced this week by the adult ad
visory board. The youth center
will be open each weekday after
noon from 3:30 to 5 p.m. Evening
hours will depend on results of a
questionnaire sent this week to the
junior and senior high schools with
the cooperaton of Prncipal John
Speicher. The youth center mem
bers helped to prepare and dis
tribute the questionnaire. One
question included is whether par
ents would be willing to serve as
hosts and hostesses at the center.
Ervin C. Dietzel, 8122, will take
calls from adults who will serve.
Needs A Piano
The youth center advisory board
met Tuesday evening with Town
Manager Charles McDonald, Rec
reation Director Sam Fox, and
Eileen Labukas, his assistant. Un
der discussion were methods of im
proving jantorial and snack bar
services. The canteen needs a
piano, and any town organization
or individual having suggestions
for obtaining one should contact
John Teel, president of the advisory
board, 3606, or the recreation de
partment.
The youth center is available for
use in the evenings by young peo
ple’s organizations; the Boy Scouts,
Catholic Youth Organization, and
Community Church and Lutheran
youth groups have already made
arrangements for meeting there.
Other groups interested in obtain
ing the use of the center should
get in touch with Mrs. Labukas at
the recreation department, 6966.
At next week’s meeting Mrs. La
bukas plans to report on the year’s
operation of the center by the
young people, supervised by the
recreation department and the ad
visory board. Most of the actual
running of the place has been in
the hands of the members, who
came out in the black and even
with a small surplus, in spite of the
loss incurred when the snack bar
was broken into recently. Includ
ed in the report will be the results
of the questionnaire, and the deci
sion on evening hours.
Council Will Consider Changes
In Town Charter Next Week
Proportional representation as a method of voting in town*
elections is under consideration by the council as a result of a sug
gestion made Monday night by Delbert Mesner, one of several citi
zens attending an open hearing on charter changes.
Rent Examiner Cuts
Boost at Brentwood
Greenbelters interested in the
fate of other housing projects whose
landlords applied for rent increases
read with interest this week the
news stories on increases at Brent
wood Village (439 units). Brent
wood Gardens (171 units), and
Brentwood Development Corpora
tion (126 units).
Citing increased operating ex
penses, the landlord asked 11.32%,
15.05%, and 19.01%, respectively,
for the three developments. Ex
aminer William F. Mansfield, who
conducted hearings on the applica
tion, found that some increases
were evident! but cut the respective
percentages to 6.02, 9.08 and 3.95.
New rents in the Brentwood de
velopments, located near Four
teenth Street and Rhode Island
Avenue, N. E. (approximately one
hour nearer downtown Washing
ton than is Greenbelt), range from
$37 per month for two rooms and a
bath to $92.75 per month for five
and a half rooms and two baths.
Old rents ranged from $35 to SBS.
Increases applied for by PHA for
Greenbelt range from 16% to 35%.
They would raise some rents as
high as SB9 per month.
Tuesday Meeting To Set
AdultEducationProgram
The Adult Education program in
Greenbelt will get under way at a
meeting to be held in Room 225 in
the Center school on Tuesday, Sep
tember 28, at 8 p.m. Courses are
usually offered in general educa
tion; English or other languages
for which there is a sufficient de
mand; music appreciation; public
speaking; typing; shorthand; book
keeping; art; ceramics; child study;
parent education; sewing and
home economics. There is a regis
tration charge of SI.OO for the year.
Everyone interested in adult edu
cation courses is urged to attend
this meeting. If fifteen people are
interested in a subject that is not
listed above, such a course can be
given. If an insufficient number of
people are interested in a subje.ct
for the class to be offered in
Greenbelt, arrangements can be
made to take the subject elsewhere
in the County. Full information
will be available at Tuesday night’s
meeting.
A tentative schedule of subjects
will appear in next week’s Coopera
tor. Classes will open the first week
of October.
Greenbelt Band
Takes First Place
Led by high-stepping drum ma
jorettes, the Greenbelt band won
the SSO first prize as the best-ap
vearing band at the 26th annual
convention of the Prince George’s
County Volunteer Firemen’s Asso
ciation on September 11, at a pa
rade on the Baltimore Boulevard 1
at Cottage City.
Leading the parade was the
Washington Police band which
captured second prize.
In addition to the SSO in cash,
theh Greenbelt band was awarded
a silver loving cup. According to
John Kramer, director of tthe band,
the cup will be inscribed and dis
played in a prominent- place in the
Center.
Under the present system of plu
rality election, particularly when
slates dominate the campaign, citi
zens backing the losing candidates
feel they have no representation
on the council, Mr. Mesner said.
He urged consideration of the Hare
system of proportional representa
tion to guarantee minority groups
a voice in the conduct of the town’s
affairs.
Councilwoman Betty Harrington
spoke in favor of P.R., adding that
she is opposed to slates even,
though she ran on one at the last
election.
In the event the council favors,
P.R., the question of its adoption
would best be decided by referen
dum, Councilman Frank Lastner.
stated.
Boundary Changes Discussed
Town boundary changes were
discussed with town solicitor John
S. White. Public Housing Admin
istration seeks to include within
the town line a strip of land in the
north end of Greenbelt which is
legally the property of the Depart
ment of Agriculture, although;
Greenbelt homes are built on it and.
are considered part of the com
munity. There is no objection toi
its inclusion, but the council raisedi
real objection to PHA’s attempt to
remove from the town boundaries
four pieces of privately-owned
land, one of which is Schrom’s air
port. At present they are includ
ed in the town limits but are not
under town jurisdiction.
No Late Registration
Elimination of late registration
for voting in town elections will be
recommended by the council. Also
under consideration is the establish
ment of a board of review to pass
on dismissals of town employees
and clarification of several sections
of the charter.
Council members present at the
meeting were Mayor Thomas J.

GCS Board of Directors
Frank Lastner was re-elected

er Services board of directors at a

Juniors Seek SSOO
Equipment Fund
This year will be the first that
boys between the ages of 9 and 15
will have a chance to participate in
football. The boys have three
coaches to help them, Pete Cook
son, H. M. Goode and Richard
Lewis. Through Donald Wolfe
and the Recreation Department an
effort will be made to raise SSOO to
equip the boys.
The boys are entered in the
Prince Georges Boys Club League.
Those willing to help the boys

"j hM
VOTE!
So Labor
May Know
As they say out at,the horse em
poriums where you put two bucks on
a nag’s, nose and then regret it,
they’re in. the home stretch now.
The stakes are high—mighty high
—and don’t* let anybody tell you
they’re not.
Just look atwjio’s betting on whom
and you can get a pretty good idea
of what’s involved.

Let’s start out with Tom Dewey,
the little man who wants to move
from Albany to Washington and oc
cupy the White House.
The big money is on Dewey be
cause he appeals to those who have
big money.
It’s almost as simple as that.
He’s the darling of the conserva
tives—the men who publish. the. big
newspapers and run the J»ig corpora
tions.
A large number of reactionaries are
riding his bandwagon, too.
Dewey is the champion of those
who love the Taft-Hartley Act and
hate what they loosely term “social
^legislation.”
He’s the fellow who has been cam
paigning on the theory that it’s’ best
to say almost nothing about almost
everything.
i
Harry Truman is on the “other”
side of the fence—the side occupied
by organized labor and by the “little
folks” who make up the bulk of. our
population.
He’s been campaigning on the
theory that it’s best to say something
v about everything in which the people
jjre interested.
And he hasn’t been guilty of quib
bling or double-talk.
He’s for repeal of T-H for a high
er minimum wage, for expansion of
social security, for real civil liberties,
for action to control inflation and
build houses.
He’s running on a platform indors
ed by labor—a platform that doesn’t
contain the-vague promises made by
the ,GOP platform committee.

Henry Wallace is riding a left
wing nag that is. stumbling badly
after making a most inauspicious
start.
Regardless of what his original in
tentions were, he got himself so bad
ly tangled up with the Communists
that he’s “theif boy” now.
His chief contribution to the cam
paign has been to make a lot of
noise and to split a portion of the
liberal vote from Truman and some
of the good pro-lhbor senatorial and
congressional candidates.
His chief supporters are those
violently opposed to the Marshall
Plan and fayoring “friendship” with
Russia.

Strom Thurmond, the Dixiecrat
presidential candidate, is the product
of a Southern revolt against Tru
man’s civil rights program.
He’s backed by those who have
exploited race and religious prejudice
in their fight against trade unions.
And there’s big money Repub
lican money behind his campaign.
Thurmond’s chief contribution has
been to capture a portion of the
Southern vote that would have gone
to Truman.
He’s a party bolter who, for all
practical purposes, is working for
Dewey.

It’s not difficult to put candidates
for the Senate, House and state of
fices in their proper categories mere
ly by learning who their chief backers
are.
From, labor’s standpoint, many of
these races are extremely important
_because labor must operate under
laws enacted by our legislative
bodies.
The Taft-Hartley Act, remember,
was a product of Congress and not of
the White House.
The numerous anti-labor state
laws now on the books were passed
by legislatures and not by governors.

Yes, the stakes are high, and the
battle over them is one well worth
fighting with vigor.
There’s not a lot of time left in
which to fight, but there are still im
portant things to be done between
now and the close of the polls on
November 2nd.
There are still door-bells to be
rung and campaign leaflets to be
^'distributed.
There’s still the Job of getting to
the polls those whose laxiuesa or lack
of interest might keep them from
voting.-
And there’s the important job of
Casting your own ballot.
Lot’s do our part of- the job well.

Anti Picketing Ruling
Under TaSt Hartley
Law Kills Labor
Add peaceful mass picketing to your fast-growing list of
Taft-Hartley “Thou Shalt Nots.”
This drastic ruling was made by Irving Rogosin, an NLRR

CIO Electrical Workers and the Cory Corp., Chicago manufacturer
_ _ coffee brewers, electric and gas
Production Up:
So Is Price
The latest- issue of Consumer Re
porjs poses an economics question
to its readers.
“In August 1948-, dairy farmers in
the New York area produced more
milk than in August 1947. Consum
ers, however, drank less milk," the
magazine of Consumers Union said,
asking: “With supply up and demand
down, what happened to prices?”'
And the answer: “Despite the well
known law of supply and demand, con
sumers had to pay more for their
milk; the price was 3V& cents a quart
higher in August of 1948.”
Buckmaster Drops
Trip to England
Economic Cooperation Administra
tor, has announced that Victor
Reuther, educational director of the
CIO Auto Workers, went to England
with the joint Anglo-American Pro
duction Council.
Reuther took the place of Presi
dent L. S. Buckmaster of the CO
Rubber Workers who notified Hoff
man that pressure of duties in Akron
made it impossible for him to attend.
stbves and other products.
Drawing the T-H ban on “coercion"
to the extreme, Rogosin ^.decided that
‘force of numbers alone, even where
picketing is conducted peacefully, has
an intimidatory and coercive effect on
employes otherwise willing to cross
a picket line. Picketing of such char
acter exceeds the bounds of peaceful
persuasion and is not privileged or
protected as free speech.”
If the interpretation is upheld by
the NLRB and by the courts, employ
ers will have been handed an almost
fool-proof strike-breaking formula.
This is how it would work:
Unions have traditionally employ
ed the mass picket line as their main
defense against strikebreakers. With
mass picketing outlawed, strikebreak
ers would be virtually free to swarm
into the plant. Once enough men
were on the job, the employer would
call for a representation election.
Strikers replaced by strikebreakers
would be barred from voting under
T-H. The anti-union replacements
would vote the union out, the strik
ers, denied a voice in the decision,
would lose their unions, their strike
and their jobs.
(Continued on Page Pour)
An Election Day Message
You would fight a foreign enemy which threatened to take
your right to vote away from you.
Yes, we have neglected that right to vote stayed at
home on election day.
Organized labor forgot to vote its full strengthen 1946
Result: A United States Congress that forgot organized labor.
If organized labor had voted in 1946, we wouldn’t have the
Taft-Hartley Law today.
This pernicious legislation is just beginning to get its
teeth into organized labor.
Just a couple recent decisions, already, made, show what
“CAN HAPPEN” and are merely samples of what “WILE
HAPPEN” if organized labor does not vote.
Do you know that if you go on a strike (even though you
have followed every legal step required)) to enforce a wage de
mand, the employer may hire scabs to take your place and you
are OUT? Well the Taft-Hartley Law says he can.
You can READILY see that your strike weapon under the
Taft-Hartley Law. has become a bundle of twigs.
Do you know that under the Taft-Hartley (aw, mass pick
eting is an unfair labor practice and is therefore subject to
Federal injunction?
Do you see the significance of these two decisions? With
mass picketing prohibited, it will be easy for the employer to
hire REPLACEMENTS while you are on strike to get higher
wages, put the REPLACEMENTS to work, and you can start
looking for another job.
These are the kinds of law we get when organized labor
LETS the other fellow do his voting on election day.
Your failure to vote is'a vote for anti-labor legislation.
Don’t be found sleeping on election day—on November 2,'
1948. Those who like the Taft-Hartley Law will vote right
down the line.
If members of organized labor vote on November 2, the
flood of anti-labor legislation can be checked.' If we fail to vote,
there is more stuff like the Taft-Hartley Law coming.
Be fair with youself your family your country.
VOTE ON NOVEMBER 2.
If every eligiblii voter in the United States who favors
the election of President Truman will go to the polls and
register his choice in the ballot box, President Truman will
win by one of the greatest landslides in American history.
But the election forecasters predict that only about hall I
of the voters will take the trouble to go to the polls.
Of the number jwho refrain from voting, past exper
iences indicate that a vast majority of the stay-aways
will be members of Organized Labor and just rank and
file citizens who actually have more at stake in the elec
tion than anyone else.
Hence, the prediction: Dewey will win!
Frankly, most of these Dewey' election predictions are
part of a well-played plan to influence the results of the
election.
This propaganda is designed to make as many Truman
supporters stay away from the polls as possible, or to switch
o Dewey.
It is designed to make Trumanites say: “My candidate
can’t win anyway, sq why should 1 waste my time going to
the polls d
The propaganda’of the “polls** also is designed to appeal
to the narrow-minded type of voters who •'likes to ride a
-winner," regardlessiW w hom he might be- ..i
Such persons, it is contended, will cast a vote for the
candidate they believe will win, regardless of bow disastrous
his election might prove to be.
Most of the election forecasters predict that Governor
Dewey will receive just about half of the total vote cast.
This would win him the election, as the remaining vote
would be split among several candidates, but with President
Truman receiving the great bulk of it.
If only half of the eligible voters go to the polls, that
means that approximately one-lourth of the voters will
choose the President. Is that Democracy?
But don’t sell President Truman short!.
He is Labor’s candidate:
He is the candidate who places human rights of the rank
and file.citizens above the property rights of big business.
President, Truman will stand or fall next Tuesday on
his determination to do something constructive for Or
ganized Labor and the great masses of American people.
Is Organized Labor going to “sell him down the river"
Something To Remember!
WHERE TRUMAN STANDS
He’s for a war on inflation.
He’s for strong civil liberties.
He’s for Taft-Hartley repeal.
He’s for greater Social Security.
He’s for £RP and a just peace.
WHERE DEWEY STANDS
He’s for Big Business.
He says he’s for ERP, but—
He’s backing isolationist senators.
He's for Taft-Hartley.
He would scrap the New Deal.

week that no pay raises can be ex
pected next year if the Republicans
win a victory at the polls Tuesday.

enforcement of the Taft-Hartley Law
after election will make strikes so in
effective that further pay increases
are impossible. It was signed by
President L. S. Buckmaster, Vice
President H. R. Lloyd and Secretary
Treasurer Charles E. Lanning. The
letter follows:
“Do you want a wage increase, next
year?
“Well, if you do, you had better
VOTE NOV. 2—because you may not
be able to do anything about that
wage increase next year if the ene
mies, of labor win at the polls.
“This is not election scare-talk.
It’s a hard, cold fact, Here’s the
reason:
“The National Labor Relations
Board, acting under the Taft-Hartley
law, has decreed that if workers go
on strike for wage increases, employ
ers can replace them with strikebreak
ers—and these strikebreakers can
rote in NLRB elections while the
union workers who hare been replac
ed will not be allowed to vote. Natur
ally, the strikebreakers will vote to
throw out the union!
“It’s easy to figure how that will
put a crimp in union plans for wage
increases.
“And that’s not all In another de
cision, the NLRB has said that mass
picketing is illegal, regardless of

Marysville Alttmntis
Elected to Board
Of Chicago Company
William J. Zeiter, Chicago, has
been elected a member of the
board of directors and secretary
of the Security Mutual Casualty
Company and took over his new
duties Oct. 1. He is a former
Union County boy and a gradu
ate of Marysville High School.
Zeiter is a member of St. John’s
Lutheran church in LaGrange
and is chairman of the church’s
board of education. Since 1946
he has been president of the In
ternational Walther league, world
wide organization of Lutheran
youth, and also is chairman of
the board of directors of the
Wheat Ridge foundation, a charit
able organization that operates a
tuberculosis santitarium at Wheat
Ridge, Colo., and a nation-wide
program of medical social service.
Home office of the Security
Mutual Casualty company is at
1U5 S. LaSalle st., Chicago.

Air-Parcel Post
Begins Service
With the inauguration of nation
wide—worldwide air parcel post
yesterday, the United States can
boast of the most modern and ex
peditious delivery service in the
world, according to Postmaster
James N. Wolfe of Greenbelt. Md.
Transit time will be reduced to a
fraction of that required by other
modes of transportation.
Postmaster Wolfe pointed out
that air parcel post packages will
receive the same particular con
sideration shown air mail in rout
ing. In addition, identical doorstep
deliver}* will be given to air parcel
post as is afforded other forms of
mail, making the oirborne mail
service unique in every respect.
Stickers Available Soon
He added that distinct air parcel
post stickers, printed in red, white
and blue and bearing the likeness of
a winged package with the earth’s
globe underneath, will soon be
available without cost at the Green
belt Post Office to apply to out
going packages.
While international air parcel
post service has been available from
the United States and its posses
sions for several months, domestic
airborne service is being launched
for the first time in history.

Oldtimers To Play
Shamrocks Sat.
The Shamrocks will try to beat
their old teammates of past years
this Saturday at Braden Field. The
oldtimers have been working out
each Saturday for the past few
weeks to see if they can get back
to their playing days. The game
should be a good one, as the old
timers will have most of their team
together that has won the County
Championship several times.
The benefits of this game will go
to the Rescue Squad.
Probable lineup for this game:
Oldtimers —Coaches, Craig and
Shea; pitchers, Boggs, Holochwost;
catchers, Mullen, Todd; Ist base,
Moore; 2nd base, Davis; short stop,
Guyer, Nuzzo; 3rd base, Russo;
left field, McDonald; center field,
Andrus, Zerwick; right field, Hol
ochwost, Holland.
Shamrocks —Coach, Wolfe; pitchy
er, Loomis, Scott; catcher, John
ston; Ist base, Randolph; 2nd base,
Lynch; short stop, Clark; 3rd base,
Mothershead, Anacker; left field,
Benefiel; center field, Sargies, Eck
hardt; right field, Sommers, Cragin.
Umpires for the games will be
Chief Panagoulis behind the plate
and Mayor Canning on the bases.
McDonald wil throw out the first
ball.
Council Meets Sept. 13
The next meeting of the town
council will be held Monday, Sep
tember 13, instead of September 6,
Labor Day. Tonight the council
acting as the charter committee is
meeting with Bernard Groves,
PHA general council, for a discus
sion of town boundaries.
Mayor Canning announced Thurs
day, September 16 as a tentative
date for an open meeting on char
ter changes.
ter changes.
Local Schools Open
Next Wednesday
All Greenbelt schools will resume
operation for the fall term on Sep
tember 8. Children should report
to their old classrooms in the Cen
ter and North End schools on the
first day at 9 a.m. for assignment
to new rooms. Registration of new
pupils will be held between 9 and
11 a.m. in the school auditorium.
Parents should accompany the chil
dren registering for the first time,
and should bring birth and vaccina
tion certificates, as well as transfer
records from schools previously at
tended.
Registration for the Center kin-'
dergarten will take place on Sep
tember 10, between 2 and 3 p.m. in
room 121. North End kindergar
teners will report to their groups
on Monday, September 13. Lists
for the groups will be in the foyer
of the North End school Wednes
day and Thursday, September 8 and
9. Children who have registered
for kindergarten, but have not had
a physical checkup, should report
to the public health office Septem
ber 3 between 9 and 10 a.m.
Dismissal time will be 3:15 p.m.
except for the first day, which will
probably be early. First graders
may leave at noon, and second
graders at 2:30 p.m. until further
notice.
North End pupils should plan to
have lunch at home until the cafe-'
teria opens, on a date to be an
nounced next week.
Greenbelt High School begins its
fall term at 9 a.m. on September 8,
and will be in session daily until
3:30 p.m. The cafeteria will serve
lunches beginning with the first
day. Bus routes established last
year will be continued. About 700
students are expected, not counting
100 seventh-graders from Berwyn
and Beltsville.
GHA Has New Doctor
Last Wednesday marked the first
day of Dr. William O. Self’s new
duties with the Greenbelt Health
Association as full time staff phy
sician. Dr. Self, a graduate of
Duke University Medical School,
will be the obstetrician and general
practitioner for the Health Asso
ciation. His experience includes
surgery and obstetrics for several
years in a 180-bed hospital in Penn
sylvania. Dr. Self recently finished
a two-year fellowship in the depart
ment of surgery at Johns Flopkins,
having previously taken his intern
ship at Johns Hopkins Hospital.
During his fellowship, he worked
under Dr. Blalock, who co-develop
ed the Blalock-Taussig operation
for blue babies.
Dr. Self has purchased a home
in nearby Rogers Heights. GHA
members will have the opportunity
to meet him September 21, at the
general membership meeting.
Dr. Max W. Bloomberg will con
tinue on the staff of the Health As
sociation as pediatrician and also
do general practice.
DON’T TELL PHA
A meeting will be held next
Friday night, September 10, of
all citizens interested in seeing
that their side of the rent pic
ture is presented at any hearing
of Public Housing Administra
tion’s petition for higher rents
in Greenbelt. The meeting will

insure sufficient room for all in

NCJW Plans Weiner Roast
Mrs. Dorothy Terkeltaub, Ways
and Means chairman for the Green
belt section of the National Council
of Jewish Women has announced
committee plans for a weiner roast
to be held at the lake on September
11. Plans tentatively include en
tertainment. Members and their
guests are invited to attend.
GCS Membership Meets
On September 15 th
Agenda of the Greenbelt Con
sumer Services, Inc., quarterly
membership meeting will be mailed
to members within a few days, ac
cording to GCS Assistant Manager
Bassett Ferguson. The meeting,
which will be held at 8:15 Septem
ber 15 in the Center ouditorium,
will be devoted, in great part, to
the remaining agenda of the June
7 meeting, which was adjourned
because of lack of a quorum.
By-law changes to be considered
include six for tax purposes. Of
these, most important is the change
in the method of establishing pa
tronage returns so that they will
not be taxable. Minor changes
clarifying existing by-laws will also
be discussed.
A study of administrative costs
will be presented, along with area
reorganization plans, proposed to
increase efficiency by combining
certain buying and administrative
practices with other cooperatives
in the area.
Four new board members and
the entire three-man auditing com
mittee will be elected. Nomina
tions must be submitted within
four days after notice of the meet
ing is mailed.
Door prizes will be given at the
beginning and ot the end of the
meeting.

There’s Another 01’ Load
Cornin’ Down That Road
Greenbelt experienced one of its
heaviest traffic loads in a long time
during the past week, according to
Town Manager Charles T. McDon
ald. Construction work on Cres
cent caused an exceptionally heavy
share of regular in-and-out traffic
on Southway, he added.
The American Legion carnival
increased the ordinary traffic prob
lem each evening. Parking was re
stricted to space along the road
way, since the Legion parking area
was completely taken up by carni
val visitors.
In addition, hot weather brought
a large number of outsiders as well
as local residents to the swimming
pool. This addition alone, accord
ing to the town manager, would
fill the center parking area, but in
addition the District softball elim
ination and championship games
were being played. Consequently,
every parking spot near the center
.of town was taken.
Extra police details were provid
ed toi handle the problem. Direc
tor of Public Safety George Pana
goulis expressed his appreciation
to residents for their care and co
operation in making the extra hea
vy week a safe one, with no traffic
difficulties reported.
We Went To Naylor Gardens
Or PHA Is Off The Beam
By Sally Meredith
Curiosity led the Cooperator editor and another interested
citizen Tuesday to Naylor Gardens, housing project cited by PHA
officials as “comparable” to Greenbelt in establishing the proposed
new rent schedule.
Twenty-five minutes after boarding a street-car at 14th and
Pennsylvania Avenue, we arrived at the 750-unit development.
(An hour and a half after leaving 14th and Pennsylvania Avenue,
one can reach Greenbelt.)
Baby Playgrounds Close
With Tot Talent Show
The closing activity of the baby
playgrounds’ summer- program
was the Tot Talent Show held at
the Center school auditorium Tues
day, August 24. The children gave
two performances so their mothers
and Dads could see the results of
the activities in which they have
participated this summer.
Each playground decorated a
table, and displayed samples of
work they have done during the va
cation period. Each playground
participated in the talent show. Se
lections were rather varied in style
and fell into the following catego
ries: recitation of poems, clarinet
selections, baton tricks, tap dances,
collection of songs, Mother Goose
rhymes and a short skit of the
Three Bears.
Tumblers Take Over
The final act of the program was
taken over by the advanced tum
bling class. The girls, ranging in
ages from five to fourteen, have
concentrated their efforts this sum
mer on forming pyramids and in
dividual stunts. They opened their
act with the “Opening of tfle Rose,”
and followed this with several pyr
amids, individual stunts, double
stunts and triple stunts. The show
was ended with the tumblers doing
the “Closing of the Rose.”
The four playground directors
have been under the direction of
the recreation' department. These
girls are Mary Burns, Marion Ben
son, Barbara Lloyd, and Abbye
Edelman. In adition to the regu
lar playground activities, the di
rectors have planned special events
for every Friday including: vehi
cle parade, costume parties, nature
hikes, talent show, musical parades
and swimming parties.
The arts and crafts articles made
in the, classes were judged before
the doors opened.

Community Chorus Plans
First Meeting of Season
The Greenbelt Community Chor
us opens its fall actiitvies with a
meeting September 13, at 8:30 p.m
in the Center school, Fred Pfeif
fer, president of the chorus, an
nounced today. All last season’s
members and any other Greenbelt
songsters who are interested in
joining are urged to attend. Two
concerts are planned for this year’s

For information on the chorus, and

Brick— Brick— Brick
Beautifully constructed red-brick
buildings, housing from 10 to 28
apartments each, stretched as far
as we could see, surrounded by
close-cropped, clean lawns. We
later learned that all maintenance
of lawns, including mowing, water
ing and cleaning is done by the
management. (Close your eyes and
think of the vista that greets you
on entering Greenbelt through
Southway.)
An examination of the bus sched
ule in the lobby of the administra
tion building showed that buses
run from 5 to 15 minutes apart, de
pending on the time of day, at a
fare of 10 cents each way. (Capital
Transit allows Greenbelters to
boad buses 15 or 20 minutes apart,
with a one-way fare of 35 cents.)
Phones All Over
Also on the bulletin board was a
mimeographed list of services pro
vided. These included at least one
washing machine for each building,
and public phones in 17 of them.
(There is a public phone in Green
belt, too.)
We asked a few questions, and
got very informative answers.
“How well would you say main
tenance calls are taken care of?
Shades, carpentering, that sort of
thing?”
In answer, we were .shown a sev
en-month report of calls received,
and answered. Every call received
during that period was answered
promptly. (Fill in your own con
trast material from your Green-,
belt file.)
“How often is trash removed?”
“Daily.” (Twice a week in Green
belt.)
“Did you use Greenbelt as com
parable housing when you asked
for —and got! —a rent increase?”
“No.” (The word alone could
not possibly convey the shock that
the very idea seemed to give.)
Paint Shortage Over
We then borrowed keys to a
couple of apartments for a personal
look-see. Nowhere on the way did
we see littered lawns; and in the
entrance foyers of the -buildings)
there was no debris. The first
apartment had two bedrooms. It
was naturally (for Naylor Gardens)
being painted, since new tenants
will soon occupy it. All on one
floor, there were two bedrooms, a
living room, a dining alcove, kitch
en and bath, with hardwood floors
throughout.
The living-room is 12’ by 18’,.
with a 9’ by 10’ dining alcove ad
joining one end with an archway
between. From the alcove you en-,
ter the kitchen, which is large,
enough for the usual cupboards,
stove, refrigerator and work table,
plus a small breakfast table if de
sired. The landlord furnished the
i linoleum.
i The main bedroom (IF x 14’),
has a spacious closet with full
length mirrored door. The second
i bedroom is 10’ x 13’ and also has
way up. There is in addition an en
a combination shower-tub, with tile
all the way around the room, half
way up. There is in adition an en
trance hall, a deep coat closet, and
a linen closet.
Management Cleans!
A trip to the basement showed
huge storage rooms for each ten
ant, in addition to laundry rooms.
The large back yard was complete-

OAK HARBOR—A full slate of

ities.
First day of the fair will be de
voted to children®' activities, fea
ture will be the annual Kiddies

youngsters and their pets from all

Junior fair day, a joint 4-ff arid

Park, steer, dairy cattle and poul
try judging events, a major steer
sale and a representative agricul
tural exhibit at the Oak Harbor
Armory.
Third day of the fair will fea
ture the Grand l'arnde, an event

flouts from all areas of the county.

Bound To Jury
In Stabbing Case
Calixto Gonzales, a Mexican
worker, who has been living in
Danbury township, was bound to
the grand jury under $500 bond
when arraigned in Justice E. C.
Brough's court last, Thursday night
on a charge of cutting or stabbing'
with intent to wound or kill. He
is held in county jail in default of
bond.
The charge resulted from a stab
bing episode on Sept. 3 whnn Gon
zales is said to have inllicted a
hand wound, necessitating hospital
ization, to his nephew, Raymon
Flores.

liQD Will Hire Men
Subject To Draft
ERIE ORDNANCE DEPOT
This installation will continue to
hire men who are subject to the
draft under provisions of the Se
lective Service Act as Ion# as
there exists the present urgent
need for mechanical workers here,
it is announced by Colonel J. E.
Mclnerney, commanding officer.
Contrary to rumors stating other
wise, this depot will employ all
these youn,g men who show proper
qualifications and training, Col.
Mclnerney emphasizes.
Automatic re-instatements rights
to re-employment, with all periodic
pay increases and promotional
privileges accruing to hig credit
during his absence, are assured
permanent federal employees who
are called into military service
under the Selective Service Act of
l!48.
At the present time, there are
approximately 50 employees at this
depot who are eligible to be cabled
into military service, nnd of this
group, the majority hold proba
tional-permanent civil service
status, assuring them of their jobs
here on their return from active
duty.
Employees with temporary-in
definite appointments, while not
edigiMe for the same reinstatement
rights a8 permanent employese will
be given preference for jobs here,
if there are openings and if the de
pot is permitted at that time
hire temporary employees.
All men vho are hired under
temporary-indefinite appointments
are converted to probational-per
manent appointments as quickly
as necessary requirements axe met,
usually a period of from six to
eight weeks, it is explained.
Although federal agencies have
no provisions for a cash bonus for
emplryees furloughed into military
service, all federal employees do
receive a ftimp sum payment for
their unused, accumulated annual
leave, which amounts to an aver
age of $20 per month.

TO THE CITIZENS
OF PORT CLINTON:
Reproduced below are the contents of a letter sent September 22,1948 to
electric customer* in Port Clinton
H. A. fcOWGILL,
AUTOMOBILE
MONDAY THROUGH FRIDAY
Something Different Everyday
Viee-President
As division manager of your electric company, I am writing you to lay the card$ on the table
and tell you frankly wtiy my Company finds it necessary to ask for an increase ia domestic and
commercial electric rates in Clinton by an average of about 10%.
of us—you and we alike—have been faced with the problem of increasing prices ever
since 1938. The materials and services needed to provide you with electricity have been going up
and up.
We have been successful in holding the line up to the present time in spite of these price
increases. Our success was made possible by:
1. The largest volume of business in our history.
2. Savings resulting from our refinancing program.
3. Recent expenditures for more efficient power production equipment which has helped to
hold down production costs.
Now, however, costs have reached the point where we can no longer absorb them with our
present electric rates. We, therefore, had no choice but to go before the Port Clinton Council and
ask them to review our situation and grant a reasonable increase in electric rates for residential anrt
commercial service.
Our large Industrial customers already are carrying their share of the burden of high costs.
The price? of electric power sold to them has been increased by more than 10% to cover increased
coal costs alone.
All* over the nation, many electric companies have been forced to increase electric rates.
These were necessary to safeguard the dependability of electric service and to insure facilities
for future electric demands.
The Ohio Public Service Company regrets that its record of over 27 years of rate reduc
tions must be broken with this request for an increase. We hope, however, that all fair-minded
citizens of Port Clinton will understand our situation and will agree that conditions beyond our
control make a rate increase necessary.
The Ohio Public Service Co.

TRUMAN ON TOUR
But for Mr. Truman’s campaign
tour it would never have been
possible to prove to the country
how small a part Mr. Truman ac
tually plays in the great office
which he holds, says the Wash
ington Post.
On this trip he will have been
away from Washington 16 days,
all of them from early morning to
late at night devoted to speeches,
parades, handshaking, and to
conferences with local candidates
and local bosses.
There is not even an attempt to
pretend that the President of the
United States is directing affairs,
is making the decisions, is forming
or conducting policy.
Mr. Truman was touring the
downtown shopping district in Los
Angeles while the Western Allies
were dispatching their note to
Moscow, while Secretary Marshall
was addressing the United Na
tions, while in every capital the
responsible men were in anxious
consultation.
There is, one might say, a cer
tain honesty in admitting that Mr.
Marshall. Mr. Lovett and Mr.
Bohlen, Mr. Forrestal, Mr. Drap
er, Mr. Royall, General Clay and
Mr. Douglas have been handling
the great conflict, which may
mean peace or war, that they have
not had guidance and leadership
from the President, and that
therefore his absence from Wash
ington makes no difference.
SAGEBRUSH SALVE
By Mac
“Well, well, Old Timer,” said
the Bartender in the Slag Dump
Cocktail Lounge and Social Cen
ter as the old Gulcher walked in,
“Just in time to tell me how the
election’s going.”
“Just as if you didn’t know,”
said the Old Geezer as he as
sumed his familiar position with
one foot on the brass rail. “Broth
er, it’s been a long time, sixteen
long years, and I dono yet how
I’ve lived through it. I don’t look
the same and I don’t feel the
same, but thanks be, I’ve lived to
enjoy a few years more of safe
and sane administration in Wash
ington.”
“That isn’t the way I heared it
from Mr. Truman,” said the Man
Behind the Bar. “He doesn’t like
pessimists and he told some of
friends so. He says that some of
them told him he couldn’t be
elected Senator and he made it,
fighting his own way, and I hope
to tell you he’s fighting his own
way now.”
“You said it, my fran,” said the

none of his advisors have told
him to call names and make the
cracks that he made on his West
ern trip. They told him to be
dignified as becomes a President
of the United States, but he evi
dently said, ‘To hell with dignity,

Learn About Communism: The
Committee on Un-American Ac
tivities of the House of Represen
tatives is putting out five book
lets on the subject of Commun
ism. The first in the series is TOO
Things You Should Know About
Communism.” The other four
booklets will be 100 Things You
Should Know About Communism
in Religion, In Education, In La
bor, and Communism in Govern
ment.
Only 500,000 copies of the first
booklet were printed, and they
went fast —so did the appropria
tion for these booklets. So this is
a tip to individuals and patriotic
organizations who might want to
order more copies through the
Government Printing Office,
Washington, D. C. or have them
printed privately.
The country should be flooded
with these booklets you might
write your congressmen to appro
priate the necessary funds for
this. It will be money well spent,
to combat the most evil force
force which has ever confronted
America, and the world.
While it is true that such infor
mation won’t convert Communists
to democracy they simply shut
their minds to all opposition, but
there are millions of people who
do not know Communism for the
evil thing that it is for the
menace it is to every freedom that
we know. Particularly young peo
ple, of school and college age.
The address of the Committee
on Un-American Activities is
Room 226. House Office Building,
Washington 25, D. C. If you love
America, write them for copies of
these booklets which you can dis
tribute in your community, and
request that enough will be print
ed.
Hollywood Thrillsters: The re
cent arrest of several Hollywood
movie stars for smoking marihu
ana stinks more than the narcotic
weed itself. It proves that these
To My Friends

people set themselves apart from
other humans as being privileged
characters The Movie Kings

A Los Angeles Police psychiat
rist, J. Paul De River, is quoted

ed movie heroes is quoted as say
ing.

mantic stimulant it’s a well
known fact that Hollywood people
are jaded. They’ve tried every

in ‘reefers’. It’s the only way they

romance.”
No doubt there will be an at
tempt made to whitewash the
whole stinking affair. The stu
dios are puting on the sob act al
ready for Mitchum in whose pic
tures they have invested much

sters consider themselves above

en in a recent interview I heard on

Turner had received bad publicity
from her many marriages. He re
plied that the only bad publicity

ture.
That seems to be the way they
feel about it. But have they for
gotten Fatty Arbuckle and many
others who went into oblivion be
cause they were too greedy for
thrills?

BACK YARD PHILOSOPHY
By LAL
I always have to laugh when I
see that pitying—“ You’ll learn,
you poor fool!”—look an old mar
ried man gives when he hears
a young supercilious bride-groom
to-be expanding on HIS ideas of
married life, how HE shall be x the
master of his home. These old
boys, travel weary along the road
of matrimony, learned their les
sons long ago. They know the
quicksands of marital battles. They
know women fight with their
brains and emotions as well as
their tongues and tears.
One day a long-time married
man came into my store and said,
“I want some paint. My wife and
I decided to renovate our house.”
I gave him the color charts, he
chose the colors he wanted and
left in a happy daze. Within a
couple of hours, he was back with
the paint and said, “Ma’am, can I
exchange this paint for some oth
ers?” I was way ahead of him.
It’s been bad planning on my part from
start to finish and, I’m ashamed to say, we
had no reserve to fall back on.
Like a lot of other people, we never
expected to feel the pinch and didn’t prepare
for it
I know this kind of letter doesn’t help you
meet your own expenses, but please bear
with us a while longer. I will make every
attempt to settle up soon.
Thanks for your patience.
Sincerely,.
John Doe
I knew he had bought without con
sulting his wife, so I had expected
him to come back. He took a col
or chart with him and then re
turned. We got the colors she had
checked. His only comment was,
“You know, I learned a long time
ago to let a woman have her way.
It’s lots easier on a fellow.”
I have read about the French
ladies and how they are supposed
to be subservient to their hus
bands—AND I have read what
happens when they don’t like
what their husbands do! He brags
he is Master of his house!
Look at the meek down-cast
eyed Chinese wife with her hus
band. She doesn’t fool him. He
knows she is boss in his house if
she is a mother of his son.
And speaking of married men,
I read the other day that the war
department picked married men to
plan the strategy of the “War of
Nerves.”
Honesty is, of course, the best
policy, but there are still many
who Are still satisfied with less
than the best.
