Excavation Starts
For Culvert
Excavation for the construe
tion of a new cement culvert to
start in front of the B B bar,
run under the street and the
Belmont and beyond, was begun
this week by Patrick Lynch and
Co.
The culvert in front of the B
B and the one by^the old com
missioner’s office must be com
pleted before the paving of the
streets can begin.
The big shovel of Patrick
Lynch and Co was in operation
Thursday removing the dirt and
and logs that had been there as
a fill for years.
Ralph Staupp will build the
forms and pour the concrete for
the culvert in front of the B B
as he did for the job in the other
end of town.

Fishing Open At
Karluk, Red River
Fishing in the Karluk and Bed
River aieae opened again yester
day morning at 6 am, Mark
Meyer, Fish and Wildlife agent,
announced.
He said there are enough fish
iu the Red River area, but that,
the opening in the Karluk area
is merely a test to see if there
are sufficient salmon tnere. The
fall escapement, at Karluk, set at
350, 000, has reached only 117
000 to date.
The rest of the Kodiak Island
area will open on September 10
and reman open until Sept. 30 I
Ouij thr e canneries Grinifs
Whiz Fish and Cape Douglas,
will be open in September Two
floating canneries, the local Is
land Seafoods Co and that
owned by Bill King, will also o
perate this month.
The Kodiak Is. salmon pack
to date is valued at $12,250,000
That figure is the cannery price
to the wholesalers.
The annual heating on fishing
regulations, conducted by the
Fish and Wildlife Service, is set
for September 15, at 7:30 pm in
the Union Hall.
The hearing will be conducted
by Seton Thompson, chief of the
division in Washington, D C.,
Clarence Rhode Alaskan head
of the service, and George Kelez
of Washington, fisheries super
visor for the territory.
The hearing is open to all and
any interested persons are urged
to attend, Mr. Meyer said
Mr. and Mrs. Eugene (Dick)
Lightfoot have purchased Lot 2
Block 13 from Edna Kraft.

munity Center, seeking operat

Local Firm Buys
Dry Dock
Kodiak has a floating dry dock
capable of handling boats up to
125 tons. The floating doek is
the property of Alvine and Gib
son.
Until this week the doek was
the property of the Protbero
Co. and was being towed to
Seattle, by tbe M. S. Foremost
for use there.
Rough weather forced Fiank
Protbero, skipper of the Fore
most, to turn back to Kodiak
and dispose of the dock. A soow,
also in tow, was lost.
“It’s the only one on tbe is
land and with 2 or 3 days work,
it should be ready for use’’, Mr.
Alvine said.

Mrs. Underwood Dies;
Widow of Senator
Was Noted Hostess
Mrs. Bertha W. Underwood, widow
of Senator Oscar W. Underwood of
Alabama and former owner of his
toric Woodlawn estate near Mount
Vernon, died yesterday in a hospital
in Philadelphia. She was about 75.
She had been a patient at the
hospital for more than three years.
Immediate cause of her death was
pneumonia.
Mrs. Underwood was a prominent
Capital hostess for many years, both
at Woodlawn and at the Under
woods' earlier home at 2000 G
street N.W.
Her husband, who died in 1929,
was a member of Congress from
1895 until 1927, and served as Dem
ocratic floor leader of both the
House and the Senate.
1924 Convention Recalled.
His name was presented to the
i Democratic National Convention in
I New York in 1924 by Alabama as
its favorite son candidate for the
Presidential nomination. The con
vention lasted for several weeks,
with 103 ballots, each of which was
led by the Alabama delegation’s
I casting “24 votes for Oscar W.
Underwood.” The nomination finally
was won by John W. Davis.
Mrs. Underwood, the former
Bertha Woodward of Birmingham,
was the Senator’s second wife. They
were married in 1904.
Senator Underwood bought the
1127-acre Woodlawn estate, once the
property of Nellie Custis, in 1925.
The estate had been presented by
George Washington to his adopted
daughter on her marriage to his
nephew, Lawrence Lewis. Washing
ton prepared the plans for the 20
room mansion, which was built
about 1803.
Sale of the estate to the Immacu
late Heart of Mary Mission Society,
a Catholic order, was approved by
the Fairfax County Circuit Court in
August, but the society has agreed
to forego its right to buy it in favor
of the recently formed Woodlawn
Public Foundation. Inc., which seeks
to preserve the estate as a public
shrine.
$170,000 Purchase Price.
The foundation has until January
3 to raise the purchase price of
$170,000. If it fails to buy the
pioperty by that time, it again will
be made available to the mission
society, according to Mrs. Under
wood’s attorney, H. Cecil Kilpatrick.
Mrs. Underwood’s death does not
affect plans for disposition of the
estate Mr. Kilpatrick said today.
Furnishings of the mansion were
sold at public auction in August.
Mrs. Underwood is survived by a
niece, Mrs. Eugenia Woodward of the
Anchorage Apartments; a brother,j
A. H. Woodward of Birmingham,1
chairman of the board of the Wood
ward Iron Co., and a number of
nieces and nephews in Alabama.
She had no children.
Funeral services and burial will
be in Birmingham tomorrow.
Static

Episcopalians in Virginia
Nominate 5 for Suffragan
By the Associated Press
i RICHMOND, Va., Oct. 27.—Five
Episcopal leaders were nominated
today for suffragan bishop of the
Diocese of Virginia at a special di
ocesan council meeting here. One
of them will be elected.
Nominated were:
The Right Rev. John Boyd Bent
ley. vice president of the national
council, former Bishop of Alaska
and a native of Hampton.
The Rev. Robert F. Gibson, jr.,
dean of the Theological Seminary,
University of the South, Sewanee,
Tenn.
The Rev. W. Lehigh Ribble, rector
Of Grace and Holy Trinity Church,
Richmond.
The Rev. Frederick J. Warnecke,
rector of St. Mark’s Church, Rich
mond.
The Rev.*Moultrie Guerry, rector
of St. Paul’s Church, Norfolk.
Bishop Bentley and Dr. Gibson
were nominated by the council's
I Nominating Committee. The others
were named from the floor.

Federation Offers Plan
To Strengthen Police
In Prince Georges
A four-point program aimed at
enlarging the Prince Georges Coun
ty police force and also at increas
ing its efficiency is under study to
day by the county commissioners.
The program, including a recom
mendation that nine men be added
to the force on a temporary basis
immediately, was presented to the
commissioners yesterday by a six
member delegation from the county
Civic Federation.
Besides requesting that the police
department be increased at once
from 42 to 51 men, the maximum
authorized by a State legislative
act of 1945, the group urged that
the commissioners request the Gen
eral Assembly to authorize another
increase to a size “commensurate
with the needs of the rapidly in
creasing population.”
Detailed Accounting Asked.
Also recommended for economy i
reasons was the selection of orily
one central service station, instead
of several throughout the county as
at present, to service and maintain
operation of county police cars. The
committee, in its fourth recom
mendation, urged that a detailed ac
count be published annually, show
ing the disposal of finance, includ
ing those derived from the disposal
of police and other automobiles and
of other property confiscated during
the year by the police.
Yesterday’s delegation was headed,
by Daniel M. Greene, chairman of
a committee of 12, designated by j
the Civic Federation in August to
conduct a study of police needs, and
Walter F. Mulligan, federation pres
ident.
While they took the recommen
dations under advisement, the com
missioners indicated they plan no
immediate action to comply with
any of the proposals.
Lack of Funds Cited.
“We would be glad to go along
with them, but we don’t have the
funds at this time with which to
do it,” said Harry W. McNamee,
newly elected president of the Board
of Commissioners.
In other business, the commis
sioners yesterday reiterated their
stand against permitting members
of the county police force to sell
tickets or chances of any kind
throughout the county. The action
was taken after C. Gepard Buscher
of Berwyn Heights appealed to the
commissioners on behalf of the
Prince Georges County Boys’ Clubs.
Mr. Buscher had asked that bounty
police be allowed to sell subscrip
tions during the clubs’ current emer
gency drive for $20,000.
The board elected Mr. McNamee
as chairman of the board of direc
tors of the Prince Georges General
Hospital, Cheverly, to replace the
late William A. Carson.
Street Bids Ordered.
County Engineer Arthur W. Tay
man was directed to prepare plans
and specifications and to advertise
for bids for improving two streets
in the Special Improvement Dis
trict No. 29, Bradbury Heights.
Construction of 26-foot bituminous
surface roadways with accompany
ing drainage facilities on W street
between Fifty-first and Fifty-third
streets, and Fifty-first street be
tween V and W streets, was ap
proved when no opposition was reg
istered during a public hearing at
Upper Marlboro yesterday.
Permission to operate co-operative
kindergarten schools at 4201 Galla
tin street and 5204 Forty-second
place, both in Hyattsville, was
granted the Hyattsville Kindergar
ten Association, despite a petition
opposing it, signed by property
owners of 14 homes in the com
munity. A similar permit to operate
a day nursery at 5992 Suitland road,
near Morningside. was granted
Audrey Holmes. James L. L. Rea
was given permission to operate a
gas station at 5421-5431 Defense
hwy., near Gladensburg.
A tax exemption, effective next
January 1, was approved for the
Potomac Conference Corp. of Sev
enth-Day Adventists for church
property at 4105 Fifty-fourth street,
Decatur Heights.
Arlington Rites Tomorrow
For Corpl. Donald E. Gray
Corpl. Donald E. Gray, USMCR,
killed in action by a Japanese
mortar barrage on Peleliu Island on
September 16, 1944, will be reburied
in Arlington Cemetery at 2 p.m. to
morrow.

ernment Printing Office employe.

Naval Air Station, and three sisters,

Gray of the Monroe street address

'Three Babies Die as Fire
Sweeps Tennessee Home
By tha Associated Prast
LAWRENCEVILLE. Term., Oct. 27.
—Three babies, the oldest less than
2 years old, perished in a Are which
destroyed their frame home last
night.
Kerosene used to kindle a Are in
a stove started the blaze that
brought death to Glynda Mae
Frazier, 22 months, and Sherry Kay
Frazier, 5 months, daughters of Pvt.
and Mrs. Norman Frazier, and 7
month-old Steven C. Hufford, son
of Sergt. and Mrs. Delbert Hufford.
The mothers of the victims and
the grandmother, Mrs. Ivan Frazier,
41, were burned when they made re
peated attempts to reach the chil
dren.
Mrs. Hufford said a “terrible ex
plosion” ripped through the house
soon after the kerosene was used to
start the Are.
Firemen found one child on the
Arst Aoor and the other two in the
basement, where they had fallen
when the ground Aoor collapsed.
Sergt. Hufford is stationed in
Japan with the 15th Air Force and
Pvt. Frazier is on duty in Texas.
$15,000 D. C. Saving Seen
With Ambulance Interns
The placing of interns on Health
Department ambulances will save
the District at least $15,000 a year,
John P. Sanderson, director of the;
Hospital Permit Bureau, reported
to Health Officer George C. Ruh-;
land today.
The saving will come from the,
interns treating the patients in j
their homes whenever possible in
stead of taking them to Gallinger
Hospital.
During the first three months
of the fiscal yeaV which began last
July, 23 per cent of ambulance cases
were treated in the homes making
hospitalization unnecessary, Mr.
Sanderson reported. The estimated
cost of this 23 per cent of the 1,714
patients involved at $9 a day
amounts to $3,591 for one day’s care
in a hospital, he said.
“It can be safely assumed,” Mr.
Sanderson said, “that in many in
stances a patient, once admitted to
a hospital, would remain longer
than a single day.”
Previously Health Department
ambulances were manned only by
a driver and a first-aid assistant,
and all patients were taken to the
hospital so a physician could
diagnose their cases.
Corporation Commission
Ruled Exempt on Pay Scale
By tht Associated Press
RICHMOND, Va., Oct. 27— Attor
ney General J. Lindsay Almond,
jr., has ended a long-simmering
feud between the State Corporation
Commission and the State budget
office by declaring the commission
not subject to supervision under
State personnel laws.
In an opinion released by Gov.
Tuck today, Mr. Almopd held the
corporation commission to be a
judicial agency. As such, he said,
it is exempt from provisions of the
1926 State Personnel Act.
The Virginia Supreme Court has
ruled that the personnel act would
be unconstitutional if it were con
strued to apply to legislative and'
judicial departments of the State
government, Mr. Almond recalled.
Therefore, he said, the commis
sion itself has the authority to fix
the compensation of its employes
within the lump sum amounts ap
propriated to it by the General
Assembly, without the written ap
proval of the Governor or the
budget director acting for him.

$74,987 Banked
In China Before
War Now Nets $2
By the Associated Press
SHANGHAI, Oct. 27.—The $14,987
(United States) deposited in a Chi
nese bank by a German national
before the war is worth nine gold
yuan—less' than $2—Chief Justice
Hsu Fu-kee of the Shanghai District
Court ruled today.
Albert Thordsen deposited the
American currency with the Cheki
ang Industrial Bank before Pearl
Harbor. At war's end, it was con
fiscated! on grounds that Mr. Thord
sen was an enemy national.
Mr. Thordsen protested. The Min
istry of Foreign Affairs said he could
get his money back—at the official
rate of exchange as of April 30, 1946.
That was 29.00,000 old yuan at 2,000
to $1. Since then, however, fhe rate
has deteriorated to 3,000,000 to $1, or
nine of the new gold yuan. The Dis
trict Court upheld the ministry’s de
cision.
Ex-GI 'a Lot Happier'
After VA Loan Advice
Jay V. Smith, who came to Wash
ington and the White House to ask
why he couldn’t get a GI business
loan, told Veterans’ Administration
officials today he would return to
Greenwich, Conn., “a lot happier":
because of advice they gave him
The 23-year-old Navy veteran,
holder of the Purple Heart for in
juries received when the cruiser
Atlanta was sunk in 1942, was told
he might get a loan of $4,000 for his'
restaurant business if he “went
about it in a more businesslike wav.”
“We pointed out to him," a VA
spokesman reported, “that he
couldn’t get a loan direct from the
Government and that before he!
could expect to get a loan from a]
bank under the GI program he
would have to submit a more de
tailed financial statement and busi
nesslike proposal. We telephoned
the Connecticut area and found
there was one bank in particular!
that specialized in the form of loan
he wanted. He said he was a lot
happier now.”
Mr. .Smith went to the White
House yesterday and declared he
would “come back and sit it out if
I don’t get any satisfaction.” He
talked for 45 minutes with Maj.
Gen. Harry H. Vaughan, the Presi
dent's military aide, who referred
him to the VA. He did not return
to the White House but informed
VA officials he planned to leave for
Greenwich today.

gent's funeral were to include Cor
coran Thom, Walter Bruce Howe,
I Dr. Charles Marlatt, Rear Admiral
Herbert S. Howard, Ralph Jenkins,
John Johnston and Carlile Winslow.
A wreath of boxwood from Mount,
Vernon was to be placed on her,-,
grave by the Ladies’ Association,.;

Thursday, October 21, 1948
WHERE DOES MR. TIMBERLAKE STAND?
In his speech before the Woman’s Club of Clarke County the
other day, Stephen D. Timberlake, the Republican^ candidate for the
House of Representatives from the Seventh District in Virginia, could
only criticize the record of the present incumbent, Burr P. Harrison.
Indeed it appeared as though Mr. Timberlake was conducting his
campaign and attempting to capitalize on what he would term the
“mistakes” of Mr. Harrison’s record.
Not once did the speaker outline his platform—what he stood
for—before the women attending the meeting. All of that was left
up in the air, except Mr. Timberlake did say that he thought a two
party system in Virginia would be beneficial, and a Republican Con
gressman from the Seventh District would lend much to the cooper
ation and support of a Republican President, assuming that Dewey
will be elected.
Of course, he would say that. He being a Republican can see the
wisdom of the Republicans gaining a foothold in an usually Demo
cratic state. Granted that a two-party system is good for the state,
such a situation can exist only if the Republican candidates can offer
a more beneficial platform to attract the voters. It follows then that
it is up to the candidates themselves to develop the second party.
But Candidate Timberlake did not once say how he stood on any
of the vital issues facing the voters and America today. So how can
he expect to garner votes when the voters do not know where he
stands. It is all right to stand up and criticize a candidate if you are
going to offer something better or offer constructive criticism, but
when Mr. Timberlake stands up and criticizes Mr. Harrison and offers
his audience no proof that he would be a better man for the job, he
defeats his own purpose and is way off base.
From his speech before the Woman’s Club, the only apparent
reason Mr. Timberlake gave that he should be voted into office was
that a Republican President should have a Republican Congress if
he is to have the support and cooperation. This is a weak argument
and defeats Mr. Timberlake’s statement that a two-party system is
good for Virginia. If the system is good for Virginia, then why
wouldn’t it be equally as good for the executive and legislative
branches of the government?
Mr. Timberlake offered criticism of Congressman Harrison’s
vote in Congress on the bill to submit to the states an amendment to
the Constitution to limit presidential terms to two terms. He also
cited Mr. Harrison’s opposition to the tax reduction bill, and his op
position to the Marshall Plan. But what Mr. Timberlake ddin’t say
was that Congressman Harrison voted for a subsequent measure to
appropriate funds for the Marshall Plan, and stated that he would
oppose any tax reduction bill until the budget was balanced and
there was on hand sufficient funds to carry on governmental opera
tions.
Congressman Harrison’s record is an open book, and he stands
squarely upon it. But where does the Republican candidate stand?
Certainly no one at the meeting last Thursday knows, for he didn’t
say. We do not for one moment doubt that he wasn’t sincere when
he said he should be voted into Congress because there would be a
Republican President. But that is a pretty weak reason for electing
a man to any office, or don’t you think so, Mr. Timberlake?
RE-ELECT ROBERTSON
The Courier has said little editorially concerning the campaign
of Senator A. Willis Robertson who is seeking re-election against
Republican Robert H. Woods. The Senator seeks his first re-election
to the Senate, but it is not his first political campaign, having been
elected to the House of Representatives in 1932 and re-elected time
and again until he was named to fill the vacancy caused by the death
of Senator Carter Glass.
Senator Robertson’s record in the Halls of Congress is impres
sive and one which the entire State of Virginia may justly be proud.
He has been consistent in supporting business, farm and labor meas
ures that are good, not only for his constituents, but for the country
as a whole. Fearless and independent, he has brought great distinc
tion to Virginia through his unselfish service to a nation that has at
times seemed hopelessly lost in the throes of indecision.
He is a conscientious and sincere law-maker, and while his ideas
are progressive, he adheres to the basic tenets of the Constitution.
He is against the President’s civil rights program and a firm believer
in the American system of government. His record in public life
started as a member of the Virginia State Senate in 1922 to 1928, and
he served as chairman of the State Commission on Game and Inland
Fisheries from 1926 to 1932, taking his seat in the House of Repre
sentatives in 1932, and elected to the Senate in 1946.
He has persistently had the courage of his convictions and has
refused to be swayed by high-powered lobbies on vital questions.
As a worthy representative of Virginia, his record supports his aspi
ration to re-election. That he will be returned to the Senate we do
not for one moment doubt.
Letters To The Editor
Editor, The Courier, sir:
It was indeed strange to hear pre
sident Truman’s Secretary of Labor
—Maurice J. Tobin of Massachusetts
—praising that eminent Virginian,
George Mason, over a Virginia radio
station recently. No two men could
have so little in common. In quoting
from the Bill of Rights fathered by
George Mason, Mr. Tobin deliber
ately omitted the loth Amendment
of the U. S. Constitution—namely,
that; “The powers not delegated to
the U. S. by the Constitution, nor
prohibited by it to the states, are re
served to the states respectively or
to the people.” This is the States’
Rights doctrine so valiantly upheld
today by those Southern Statesmen
Governors J. Strom Thurmond of
South Carolina and Fielding Lewis
Wright of Mississippi. We of the
South must rally to the defense of
constitutional government as oppos
ed to the totalitarian ideas of the
parties of Truman and Dewey.
Thomas Jefferson’s concept of
government was: “The true theory
of our Constitution is surely the wis
est and best, that the States are in
dependent as to everything within
themselves, and united as to every
thing respecting foreign nations.
This is the ideal for which the Con
federate soldiers fought for four
bloody years only to he defeated by
the strongly centralized Federal gov
ernment in Washington such as ex
ists today under Harry Truman.
George Mason opposed giving the
U. S. President too much power be
cause he foresaw the day when an
unscrupulous man in the White
House could misuse that power as
Harry Truman dared to do in advo
cating legislation to force Federal
control over purely local affairs. In
fact Truman believes so strongly in
this totalitarian idea that be reward
ed Tobin for his strong advocacy of
this idea at the Philadelphia Con
vention by giving him the post of
Secretary of Labor.
Is the public memory so short
that we have already forgotten the
hatred and criticism heaped upon
the South at Philadelphia by Presi
dent Truman’s mouthpiece, Maurice
Tobin, who now seeks to win our
favor? To refresh your memory, may
I remind you that Tobin was the first
speaker to attack the Southerners’
appeal for a states’ rights plank in
the 1948 Democratic platform. The
jeers and insults heaped upon our
Southern representatives at the Phil
adelphia Convention should not be
so soon forgotten. All true Southern
ers who honestly believe in the doc
trines of George Mason and Thomas
Jefferson snould vote for the States’
Rights Democrats for president and
Vice President of the U. S.—namely,
Governor J. Strom Thurmond of
South Carolina and Governor Field
ing Lewis Wright of Mississippi.
Sincerely yours,
A Young States’ Rights Democrat
-0
Editor, The Courier, Sir:
How deeply I wish that everyone
in our county had been in Winches
ter last Saturday night to hear the
address made by Senator Taft. Party
regulars, Dixiecrats, and Republi
cans found much common ground in
the body of this splendid speech.
His urge to all Southern Demo
crats that their only reasonably
course was to vote for Thomas E.
Dewey and Earl Warren was both
potent and persuasive. Obviously, ho
stated, if they vote for Thurmond,
their votes will be thrown away for
he cannot be elected. If their pur
pose is to defeat Mr. Truman, the
positive way is to vote foe Mr.
Dewey. If the opposition to Mr. Tru
man is divided between Dewey and
Thurmond, it is possible that Tru
man may carry some of the states
in which he has a distinct minority
of the popular vote. For Virginians
to cast tneir votes in such a way
that the indirect effect would give
their electoral vote to Mr. Truman,
whose every governmental policy
they now disapprove, would be tra
gic.
Thinking Virginians should pond
er one more point before casting
their ballots. None of us believes in
or can defend a one-party system.
We did not approve of it in Germany
before the war; we certainly do not
now approve of it in Riissia. Yet
here in the South we have been sad
dled for years by a one-party mach
ine. Safely “in the bag” and receiv
ing no favors from the Democratic
Party when they are in power, and
purely opposition states on whom it
is useless to expend patronage when
the Republicans are in the domin
ant position, the South, in the past
has found itself neglected both poli
tically and economically.
It is no accident that freight rates
on manufactured articles in the
South carry a higher freight rate
when shipped North, than similar
articles manufactured in the North
carry when shipped to the South.
And it is also no accident that the
scholastic standing of our public
schools is generally inferior to those
in the Northern states.
The greatest contribution to good
government in the State of Virginia,
and to the economic Improvement of
the entire South, is a strengthening
here of the Republican Party. Look
at Washington and offer your fer
vant thanks for a two-party system.
What about it, Virginians, we need
your help.
A. r. Dunning, Chairman
Clarke County Republican
Committee.

More Cities Join
Fire Prevention
Week Program
NEW YORK—More people of all
racial groups, regardless of race
or color, in more cities, towns,
and villages will join this year’
n observing National Fire Pre
vention Week, October 3-9, than
ever before, the National Board
of Fire Underwriters announced
this week.
One cause of the increased in
terest in this 37th annual obser
vation is the work done by the
President’s Conference on Fire
Prevention at its meetings last
year.
The conference encouraged the
selectio rrof Fire Safety Commit
tees in more than 500 cities.
Fire Prevention Week, which will
call attention of all citizens to
local fire hazards, originated in
the Middle West, where more than
40 years ago many communities
held an annual “clean-up” day.
Various people noticed that this
yearly campaign reduced the num
ber of fires, and several towns be
gan referring to the annual event
as Fire Prevention Day. Later they
settled upon October 9, the an
niversary of the great Chicago
fire of 1871, as the date.
In 1911, the Fire Marshalls As-
sociation of North America sug
gested that the 40th anniversary
of the great fire be set aside na
tionally to alert the American peo
ple to the increasing dangers from
fire. Twenty-two governors acted
upon the suggestion.
IN 1920, President Wilson pro
claimed October 9, as National
Fire Prevention Day. President
Harding made the observance a
National Fire Prevention Week,
and each succeeding President has
issued a similar proclamation.

District Answered
The past week many questions
I concerning the Independent
School District for Kodiak have
arisen. The following questions
and answers are published with
the hope that the issue may be
clarified:
1. What dodfc the term "In
dependent” mean?
A. The term "Independent”
means that the District is a
separate aDd legal entity and
that the District and its 5 man
governing board are independent
of the city council and the Mu
nicipal government.
2. Does the creation of an
Independent School Bistrict re
lieve the city of its obligation
to the school?
A It does NOT. The city will
continue to pay »ts share of the
school funds
3 Does the Territory still
make its 80 percent refund on
i he cost of operation?
A Yes, it will make this
refund j
4 What Territorial fund- does
the Independent School District
-tand to lose?
A It will lose the $30 tuition
oer pupil that is paid by (he
territory on each pupil living
beyond (he city limits.
5. Will taxes raised in the
Independent School District
amount to as much or more j
than the $30 paid by the terri-1
tory?
A. Yes.
6. I live outside the city limits
and have no children. Isn’t it
unfair to ask me to pay taiceB?-^
A. It is generally agreed that**
all citizens of a, community am
benefited by the school. Our
American theory of taxation is
not based upon benefits received
but upon ability to pay.

asked Commissioner of Educa
tion, Dr James C. Ryan whea
the Independent School District
was formulated for Juneau. Tb»
answers given to the above
questions were made by Dr,

Rededicaiion Of Ogle's Ridge Church
Held Sunday With Filling Program
Robert T. Secrest was the principal speaker, Sunday, when
a large number attended the rededication services for the Ogle’s
Ridgt church, Sunday, October 10, in the Middleburg community.
It will be remembered that during the past summer, Mrs.
Charles Schneeberger spearheaded the efforts of that community
to raise sufficient money to repair and improve the church in
such a way that a rededication became possible. Mrs. Schnee
berger is especially appreciative of the response which she re
ceived and the trust placed in her by a number of former mem
bers and ministers. It was large
ly through her personal efforts
that the services last Sunday
were possible.
Secrest spoke on “The Cause
of Juvenile Delinquency" and
pointed out that in most in
stances, the parents are to blame
for delinquency in children. He
said that the parent and not the
child should be punished.
Ministers who took an active
part in the morning and after
noon services were Rev. Lowell
Kiger, of Marietta Rev. Lenley
Addis, Middleburg Rev. Clair
Gray, Chandlersville and Rev.
Ola Teeters, Hoskinsville. Other
ministers who were given recog
nition included Rev. Walters, of
Ava Rev. Stille, Belle Valley
Rev. William Gray. Coal Run
and Rev. John Grimes, Sarahs
ville.
Others appearing on the pro
gram included Mrs. Cora Lamp,
who favored with a reading the
Gray sisters with several music
al selections Dickie Ryan, of
Marietta, vocal solo Emil Bar-
nett, Caldwell, vocal solo An
nabelle and Ethel May Clark,
vocal duet Marlene Teeters, vo
cal solo Rev. and Mrs. Lenley
Addis, Kathryn and Irene
Schneeberger, quartet selections
and those who spoke briefly ex
pressing their appreciation for
the much needed improvements.

BOOTH FESTIVAL
The annual booth festival
be held in the First Methodist
church here on October 19. All
churches of Noble county, Bever
ly and McConnelsville will be
represented. The produce and
canned goods will be sent to
White Cross hospital and the
cash will be equally divided be
tween the Elyria and Berea
homes.

ATTEND MEETING
Five persons from the Noble
county area attended the tri
state- tobacco meeting held in
Huntington, W. Va., Tuesday.
Those interested persons at
tending the meeting included:
Lewis Davidson, William Archer,
Rupert Kress, Edwin Crum and
County Agent Floyd Henderson.
The group left at 7:00 a. m., Tues
day morning.
ATTEND SALE
Mr. and Mrs. Earl Tarleton and
son, Tommy, attended the annual
O. I. C. spring gilt and boar sale
held Saturday evening at Circle
ville. At the sale they learned
that a sister to their latest pur
chase, sold for $110, which was
only $2.50 less than the grand
champion gilt sold at the sale.
HEADS REPUBLICAN WOMEN
Mrs. Willis Hupp has been
named as head of the Women's
Republican Organization in Noble
county. Mrs. Nelle Startzman
will have charge at the Repub
lican headquarters in the base
ment rooms under the Marquis
store on West and Spruce streets.
Other chairmen throughout the
county have not as yet been
named.

Robert Harrison Wilson, son of
James and Adaline Dougherty
Wilson, Was born February 9,
1873, and departed this life Sep
tember 27, 1948.
He was married to Ora Hull on
December 24, 1896, and to this
qhion was born one daughter^
Mrs. Angie Bates, of near Mt.
Ephraim.
He leaves to mourn his loss, his
wife and daughter, two grand
sons, Robert Bates of Canton and
William Bates, of Fort Dix, N. J.,
one granddaughter, Patricia
Bates, of the home two great
grandchildren, Tony and Dianne
Bates, of Canton two brothers,
John, of Byesville and Ambrose,
of Akron.
Two sisters and one. brother
preceded him in death.
Having lost his sight several
years ago, yet he bore his afflic
tions with patience, never com
plAining and always cheerful to
those about him. He was a kind
husband and father
sadly missed by his
many friends.

Of Course NOT
The annual fall meeting of the
Noble County Teachers Associa
tion will be held Friday after
noon, October 15, at 1:30 p. m.,
in the Caldwell high school audi
torium.
Clarence Carter, president of
the association, will preside over
the meeting and be in charge of
electing officers for the following
year.
Professor Frederick McKelbey,
supervisor of the center of educa
tional service, Ohio University,
will give the keynote address.
At the meeting officers for the
following year will be elected
and delegates to E. O. T. A. and
O. E .A. will be selected. Reports
will be presented by local repre
sentatives and several important
items of business will be dis
cussed.
All Noble county schools will
be dismissed at noon, thereby
giving all teachers adequate time
to get to the meeting by 1:30. All
teachers are expected to be pre
sent at the meeting.
Helen Ogle, teacher in the For
est Grove schools is secretary of
the organization. __

Reports Gas Supply
Will Be Curtailed
The gas suoply in Noble county
and Caldwell will again be cur
tailed for the coming winter, ac
cording to word received by of
ficials of the Permian Gas Co.,
and the Ohio Fuel Gas Co.,
The Public Utilities Commis
sion of Ohio states that the con
tinuing unprecedented demand
for gas and the inability of the
utilities and their suppliers to
provide facilities to make addi
tional gas available to consigners
in the state has created an emer
gency affecting the health, safety
and welfare of the people which
will continue to prevail during
the wintei' of 1948-49.
For practically all companies
in the state, the commission ruled
that no additional space heating
consumers would be permitted
for the coming winter.

SHORTS AND MIDDLINGS—
Ohio 4-H club members started a
total of 67,459 projects in 1948. State
club leader W. H. Palmer says this
was an increase of 8.5 per cent over
the 1947 total. Stark county club
sters with 1,506 projects head the
list, and Clark, Medina, Scioto,
Columbiana, Licking, Wayne, Hamil
ton, and Montgomery counties follow
in that order.
Less than five cows out of each
UDO in Ohio are tested for milk and
butterfat production, which means
that 95 per cent of the cows are fed
on faith that they will pay a profit
on the feed and on the labor of car
ing for the herd.

Morgan the Raider
p'EN. JOHN H. MORGAN had the
distinction of leading Confed
erate troops to the farthest point
north during the Civil war.
On June 11, 1863, his division ol
about 1,500 men crossed the Cum
berland and started north. After
several skirmishes with Union
troops, he reached the Ohio river
on July 7, captured two steam
boats, drove off 300 Federal militia
and two Federal gunboats, and
crossed into Indiana.
This brought him into the heart oi
enemy territory and his compara
tively small force was now pursued
by thousands of Feder?ls from the
Kentucky camps. Being cut off from
all sources of supply, all possibility
of aid and even of communication or
information, his further successes
are the more remarkable.
Descending on Corydon, he dis
persed 4,000 militia drawn up to
bar his way. Then he moved on to
Salem, where he tapped telegraph
lines and listened to the
ders to Federal troops
him.

From Salem he went
Ohio, destroying and burning, in an
attempt to cripple Union transpor
tation and deprive them of supplies.
After threatening Cincinnati, he
went around it and reached Camp
Shady where he destroyed many
Federal army wagons. He finished
his dash through Ohio with 25,000
Federal troops in close pursuit.
.Near Lisbon, Ohio, he was forced
to surrender to Maj. George W.
Rue. A monument now marks the
spot, commemorating July 26, 1863,
when the Confederate cavalier
reached the farthest northern point
during
the Civil war.

{irafcik-Klingbiel Marriage
isa Mary Katherine Krafcik,
fiaughter of Mr. and Mrs. Stephen
Krafcik, State street, become the
lbride of Floyd Klingbiel, son of
Mrs. Fred Klingbiel, in a ceremony
performed by Fr. Karl Brand at
10:M0 Saturday morning in the
priest's home.
The bride wore a gown of white
Slipper satin, with round neckline,
lace peplum and long train. Her
bouquet of baby mums and steph
anotis was carried on a mother-of
jearl prayerbook.
Miss Ethel Kern was the maid
Of honor and Miss Clarice Die
trich the bridesmaid. Their gowns
Of taffeta were fashioned alike with
tight bodices and hoop skirts, Miss
Kern's of fuchsia was compliment
ed by II bouquet of yellow baby
mums, and Miss Dietrich's of palm
green by rust baby mums. The
groom's attendants were James
Basiley as best man and Keith
Montgomery as usher.
A wedding breakfast was served
in the Krafcik home and a recep
tion was held in the Erie Gardens
Community house in the afternoon.
The young couple then left on a
wedding trip.
The groom is associated with his
mother in the Klingbiel Florists
business nnd the bride is employed
in the office of the Stevens Garage
in 6ak Harbor.
D. Of A. K. to Meet
Mrs. Henry Cowgill will be the
hostess to the DeLery Portage
chapter of th? Daughters of the
American Revolution for the No
vember meeting, the last one of
this'year. Wednesday evening. The
time will be taken up with a talk
by Mrs. Marshall Hixler, of Fre
mont, who will speak on the finan
cing and budgeting of the D. A. R.
The next meeting will be held in
January.

Takes Toledo Bride
In Trinity Episcopal church in
Toledo the marriage of Miss Jan
ice Bartelbaugh, daughter of Mrs
Fenton Gilbert, Toledo, and Thorn
as P. McRitchie, son of Mr. and
Mrs. William Wolf, Port Clinotn
took place Saturday afteronon
Rev. Arthur Hargate performed
the ceremony. The bride was given
in marriage by her cousin, Gaylord
Bancroft.
The bride's gown was of candle
light satin with a scalloped off
the shoulder bertha with iridescent
seed pearls and sequins. Her il
lusion fingertip veil fell from
lace tiara. She carried a bouquet
of white roses.
Bonnylynn Bartelbaugh, brides
maid and sister of the bride, wore
a gown of pastel green. Her flow
ers were yellow pompons. Dick
Hostrup was best man and James
Wolf and James Mars were ush
ers.
The bride's mother chose a cop
per-toned gown with brown acces
ories. Both had corsages of white
carnations.
A reception for 200 .guests was
held in the Hillcrest Hotel after
the ceremony. The couple left on
a trip south, to return this week
end. Mr. and Mrs. McRitchie will
ive at 222 Washington street.
Both are graduates of Toledo Uni
versity, while she is a member of
Delta Delta Delta sorority and
he a member of Alpha Phi Omega
fraternity.
Contract Club Resumes
Mr. and Mrs. Oliver True enter
tained their Monday Night Bridge
club at its first session of the sea
son, Monday evening. In the games
of contract bridge Oliver True and
Charles Bygden tied with" high
score for the men and in a draw
ing Mr. Bygden remained the win
ner. Mrs. Bygden was the winner
for the ladies.

Saturday Afternoon Weddinf
At a quiet wedding at which
only the Immediate family mem
bers were present, Miss Anna
King, South Railroad street, and
Franklyn Eaton, son of Mr. and
Mrs. JeBse Eaton, West Third
street, were united in marriage
in St. John Lutheran church Sat
urday afternoon at 2 o'clock, Pas
tor John Braun officiating.
Mrs. Donald Laimon attended
her sister as matron of honor and
3rd class Petty Officer Robert
Eaton was beat man.
The bride and her sistef wore
street length taffeta dresses and
carried bouquets of chrysanthe
mums. The best man wore his
navy uniform.
A dinner for 18 was served in
the home of the groom's parents.
During the evening a reception
was held in the Bay township
community hall. The couple left
on a wedding trip through the
Smoky Mountains. The groom
works as a carpenter.
Craft Items On Sale
Members of the Peace Craft club
met in the, home. of Mrs. Edwin
Hammond lust Wednesday evening.
At present the members are mak
ing doll clothes and accessories for

METHODIST WSCS
MEETS AT CHURCH
LAST MONDAY
The WSCS held its regular meet
ing of circles one and two at the
Methodist Church Monday afternoon
at 3:30.
The meeting was opened by singing
"A Charge To Keep." accompanied
by Mrs. W. M. Russell at the piano.
Mrs. Lena Stanton was in charge of
the devotional and read the Bth Cha
pter of Mark, closing with a prayer.
Mrs. A. B. Endsley gave a most
Interesting portion of the study book,
"On Our Own Doorsteps" by the Rev.
Frank S. Mead, which deals with,
Hawaii, the missionaries and their
work and other developments.
Circles One and Two went to their
respective places for their business
meetings. Mrs. Raymond Arick, ch
airman, presided over circle one and
Mrs. Guy Harris, in the absence of
Mrs. L. C. Downs, chairman, pre
sided over circle two. At the close of
the business meetings a social was
enjoyed by the following: Mrs. A. B.
Endsley. Mrs. W. M. Russell. Mrs.
Letha Nulfon, Mrs. Jack Kennedy,
Mrs. Wm. Ellis. Mrs. Hattie Capple
man. Mrs. Eva Bums. Mrs. Guy Har
ris. Mrs. Carolyn Hennes. Mrs. J. J.
Bell. Mrs. Sadie McKeown, Mrs. B. C.
Floyd. Mrs. Sarah Blackburn, Mrs.
Tillie Varn. Mrs. Lena Stanton. Mrs.
Raymond Arick. Mrs. J. A. Franklin.!
Mrs. C. M. Barnett. Mrs. Neva Aiken,
Mrs. Berry D. Bourn and Mrs. Janie
Nunn.
BAPTIST YOUTH
AUXILIARY MEETS
The Young People's Auxiliary of
the First Baptist Church met at the
church at eight o'clock last Monday.
Mrs. Gus Schultz, Auxiliary leader,
presided over the meeting.
This was an organizational meeting
and the following officers were elec
ted: President, Ellen Huckaby: Vice
president. John Romine: Secretary
and Publicity Chairman. Melvina.
Smith: Treasurer. Bunny Eppele:
Program and Poster Chairman Clint
Kimbrough. Remaining officers will
be elected at a future meeting.
The Auxiliary will meet every sec
ond and fourth Monday night each
month. All young people are urged
to attend the meetings.
Refreshments were served after
the meeting and a social hour was en
joyed by the group.
Those attending were Ellen Huck
abv. John Romine. Bunny Eppele,
Clint Kimbrough. Virginia Tomkin
son. Melvina Smith. Mrs. Gus Schultz
and her son. Clinton.

NUPTIALS ARE
ANNOUNCED
Mr. and Mrs. Paul Chorvat of
Brooksville have announced the mar
riage of their daughter, Martha, to
Mr. John Edward Ott, son of Mr. and
Mr. Charles Ott of Wilkes-Barre.
Pennsylvania.
The ceremony took place at the
Shrine of the Sacred Heart in Wash
ington, D. C. on August 28th. The
Rev. R. Paul Repetti officiating. Mrs.
Ott had as her matron of honor her
sister, Mrs. B. B. Burns of West Palm
Beach, and Mr. Ott was attended by
his brother, Mr. Anthony Ott, of
Wilkes-Barre, as best man.
A reception was held for immediate
friends and relatives after which the
couple left for a tour of Washington,
D. C.

MR. AND MRS. RICHARD FRANCIS TOMBRINK
United in impressive service in Catholic church
MISS LOUISE RICE BECOMES BRIDE IN
COLORFUL CEREMONY AT ST LEO ABBEY
In the presence of a large gathering
of friends and relatives Miss Mar
garet Louise Rice, daughter of Mr.
and Mrs. Thaddeus Stephens Rice,
became the bride of Richard Francis
Tombrink, son of Mr. and Mrs. Ber
nard Edward Tombrink, Sr., of Wa
tertown, Mass, in a very beautiful
ceremony performed by the Rev.
James Hodge in the Abbey Church
at St. Leo, Florida, on Wednesday
afternoon, September 15th, at three
o'clock.
Baskets of yellow and white chrys
anthemums, white gladioli, pink pom
pons with greenery and tall ivory
tapers, burning in cathedral candel
abra on the altar, cast a soft light
over the scene.
The musical interlude, preceding
the ritual, was presented by Donald
Kreusch, organist, and Mrs. R. P.
Marshall of St. Petersburg, vocalist.
The bride was attired in an original
model of old ivory satin, long fitted
torso draped above the normal waist
line which folded under a bertha of
Rose-Pomt Duchess lace, which was
incrus+ed with seed pearls outlining
the rose design. The skirt draped in
long graceful folds falling from a
chou at left side front, which is em
phasized with seed pearls as is the
outline of long draped train which
falls from a draped bustle. The lone
fitted sleeves are outlined at the hand
with pearls. The long bridal veil
repeats the pearls in twisted strands
and tiny oranee blossoms are tucked
in high ruching from which falls
bands of Chantilly lace outlining the
veil. She carried a shower bouauet
of white fugi chrysanthemums, li!y
of-the valley and white Pom Pon
chrysanthemums, tied with white
satin ribbon. Her only jewelry was a
diamond studded platinum verist
watch, a gift of the groom. Mr. Rice
gave his daughter in marriage.
Mrs. George Olney Craig of Orlan
do. a cousin of the bride, was matron
of honor. She wore a floor length
peacock blue taffeta gown, featuring
a basoue bodice with full gathered
skirt, her gloves were a matching
blue and she wore a blue ruffled hat.
centered with flowers. She carried a
cascade boquet of yellow spider chrys
anthemums tied with yellow satin;
and rainbow ribbon. Her only jewelry
was a string of pearls. She wore goldl
sandals.
Miss Carol Emerson and Mrs.
''rs. Bill Crow of Gainesville and Mrs.
T. G. Scott Jr., of Forsyth, Georgia
were bridesmaids. Miss Emerson and!
I'rs. Crow wore pansy purple floor!
length gowns of taffeta, featuring a
basque bodice with full gathered
skirts, gloves of matching purple and
purple ruffled hats, centered with
'lowers. They carried cascade bou
ouets of pink fugi chrysanthemums
tied with pink satin and rainbow
ribbons. Mrs. Sprngstead and Mrs.
Scott wore identically designed gowns
in sun gold with matching gloves
and hats and carried cascade bou-j
quets of bronze fugi chrysanthemums
tied with yellow satin and rainbow
"ribbons. All the bridesmaids wore
gold sandals.
Flower girls were the Misses Mar
garet Ellen Craig, cousin of the bride,
from Orlando Florida, and Karin
Rogers of Arlington, Virginia. They
were dressed in simple white taffeta
floor length dresses, featuring a bas
que bodice with full gathered skirts'
in their hair they wore wreaths of
w^ite flowers tied w th taffeta ribbonl
of peacock blue and wore gold san
dals. their only jeweby.was a rhine
stone butterfly, a gift of the bride,
They carried gold baskets filled with
rose leaves.
Bernard Tombrink, Jr., of Wash
ington, D. C. attended his brother as
best man. Ushers were Leon White
hurst, Jr., Brooksville, Ira Webb,
Dade City, George O. Craig, Orlando,
and Eugene L. Rogers of Arlington,
Virginia.
The bride's mother wore a green
marquisette gown over a darker
green satin, with white accessories.
Her corsage was a white orchid and
her only piece of jewelry a string of
pearls. The bridegroom's mother
chose a fuchsia floor-length crepe
dress with victorian blue accessories,
her jewelry was a matching set of
garnets. She wore a ruby lipped

Goo! rick Announces Support
Of Thurmond-Wright Ticket
Former Senator From Fred
ericksburg Repudiates Na
tional Democratic Leaders
Richmond, Va., Sept. 11—Form
er State Senator C. O’Conor Gool
rick of Fredericksburg, a staunch
Democratic party campaigner for
many years, repudiated the
party’s present national leader
ship yesterday and announced his
support of the Thurmond-Wright
ticket for President.
“The time has arrived,” he said,
“for Southern Democrats to let
the managers of the national
party know that they are sick
and tired of being kicked around
by those who are for the time
being in control.”
Goolrick said he would “do
what I can to aid the Thurmond
Wright campaign in Virginia.”
His statement made him one of
the few present or former Assem
bly members who thus far have
taken a public stand for the
States’. Rights Democratic cause.
State Senator I. Paul Wailes, of
Amherst, threw his support to the
States’ Righters earlier this week.
Other Assembly members who
have declared themselves have
done so for the Truman Barkley
slate of the national party.
Goolrick has been generally
classified as an, independent with
in the Democratic party’s Vir
ginia factions, at times siding
with the dominant organization
and at times against it. Hereto
fore a steadfast “regular party”
man, he has been a delegate to
every State convention for 30
years or more, several times has
served as a presidential elector,
and has campaigned actively for
practically every Democratic
presidential candidate over a
period of some 40 years.
It will, he pointed out, be the
first time he ever has voted
against the regular Democratic
nominee.
Goolrick denounced the present
national leadership as being “es
sentially that of a coterie of big
city bosses in the North and West
who pratically carry in their
pockets the delegations from their
respective States to the national
convention.” He said they are
men who “care little for and
know less of the real principles
of the party and its founders.”
He had a “so what!” for the
argument that a vote for Thur
mond is in effect a vote for
Dewey.
Predicts Dewey Victory
“Dewey will be elected in any
event,” Goolrick said, “but by
widespread support of Thurmond
the South can administer a much
needed rebuke to those in the
party who treat it and its cus
toms with contempt, and regain
for itself the respect and in
fluence which it so richly deserv
es at the hands of the Democratic
party, of which it has been the
mainstay for long years.”
Goolrick said he was “bitterly
opposed-’ to the “so-called civil
rights program,” but that his op
position to the national ticket was
not based entirely on this score.
“I am just as strongly oppos
ed to the repeal of the Taft
Hartley law, which Truman ad
vocates, thus repudiating a
majority of his own party in
Congress which voted for this
law. I am likewise opposed to his
latest scheme to socialize medi
cine, and I regard as utterly un
sound his suggestions to halt in
flation, while at the same time
he urges upon Congress the ap
propriation of added billions for
various purposes, many of them
economically unsound.
“It is more important in my
opinion, to preserve the integrity
of the Democratic party in the
South, to restore it to its former
place in the councils of the na
tional party and to resist the
preseht effort to dictate racial
relations in the South by those
seeking political profit therefrom,
than it is to maintain so-called
party regularity by voting for a
thoroughly incompetent candidate
for the presidency who, on Labor
Day, further demonstrated his un
fitness for the office by the dema
gogic speech delivered by him in
Detroit.”

Stuart Spurns
“Dixiecrat”, Bid
For Support
Richmond, Va., Sept. 11—“I’m
voting the straight Democratic
ticket from top to bottom,” State
Senator Harry C. Stuart declared
today in announcing that he had
spurned overtures to aid {he
States Rights presidential ticket
in Virginia.
“The record of the Democratic
party since it took over the ad
ministration of national govern
ment in 1933 has been so good
that it is unthinkable to me that
I should vote any way other than
for President Truman-Barkley and
the straight ticket, top to bot
tom” said the Russell-Tazewell
Buchanan County Senator who is
recuperating from a gall bladder
operation at his home at Elk
Garden.
Stuart said four consecutive
Democratic administrations have
“licked a terrible depression, won
a world war, and we are in pro
cess of having the best balanced
peacetime economy in the history
of the nation. Certainly there have
been some mistakes, but they have
been trifling, like mere specks of
dust in the wind compared to the
great good that has been accom
plished.
“Virginia, too has been blessed
with good government through the
Democratic party, and I don’t pro
pose for one minute to help weak
en the Democratic party in Vir
ginia by giving any comfort what
ever to the phoiiy ‘Dixiecrats.’.”
Stuart said he had declined
“with all the firmness at my com
mand, a request that I join a j
‘Dixiecrat’ tour of the State.”
Stuart is serving his third term
in the Virginia State Senate.
He is a nephew of the late Gov
ernor Henry Carter Stuart and is
one of Southwest Virginia’s larg
est cattle and farm operators. 1

Governor Tuck Names
Appeal Board For Draft
Five Members Of Selective
Service Appeal Board Is
Announced
Richmond, Va., Sept.—A five
member Selective Service appeal
board was announced yesterday by
Colonel Mills F. Neal, State Selec
tive Service director, to serve as
Virginia’s central appeal body on
the draft.
Named to the board by Gov
ernor Tuck, and approved by
President Truman, were Dr. Doug
las VanderHoff, of Richmond, phy
sician; Tschamer D. Watkins, of
Midlithian, agriculturist; Guy B.
Hazelgrove, of Richmond, attor
ney; John Hopkins Hall, Jr., State
Commissioner of Labor, represent
ing labor, and Lewis G. Larus, of
Richmond, industrialist.
Colonel Neal said Selective Serv
ice regulations provide that State
appeal boards should reflect in
their personnel the activities of
the area which they are to serve.
Appeals from classifications
made by any of Virginia’s 129 lo
cal boards will be forwarded to the
State body by the appeal agent
of the local board concerned.
Establishment of the single up-1
peal agency marks a departure
from the practice followed in
World War II, when several re-1
gional appeal boards functioned I
in each State.
A total of 82,358 Virginians
signed up for the draft in the pe
riod between August 7, when reg
istration began and September 7,
Colonel Neal said yestreday. The
figure did not include data from
29 local boards on the most re
cent report.
Approximately 240,000 Virginia
men between the ages of 18 and
25 are expected to ibe registered
by next Saturday, when the regis
tration period closes.
Colonel Neal said the registrar
situation had improved in some
localities but was still unsatisfac
tory in others as the selective
Service system moved towards the
final week of the peacetime draft

Ever count the pieces in one of Grandma’s pies?
Eager, young eyes may have counted only
one. But there was always enough to go around,
it no one took more than his fair share.
Your party-line telephone service is some
thing like that. So please be a thoughtful
party-line "neighbor.” Remember to let the
other fellow have his share of the service.
Road closed to all calls
A single telephone receiver “oft the hook’’
can tie up a whole party line, so that no
one can make or receive any calls.
Won't you check your telephone now and
then, especially if there are small children
in your home? And please be sure to re
place the receiver carefully after every call.
It will help you and everyone on your line
to better telephone service.
A little teamwork docs the trick
Teamwork on a party line
means answering the telephone
promptly allowing an interval
between calls so that others may
use the line not breaking in
on someone else's conversation,
except in a real emergency.
This sort of "give and take**
makes party-line service better
for all. The Chesapeake Po
tomac Telephone Company of
Virginia.

LOCAL GIRL TO
REPRESENT STATE
IN 4 H CONGRESS
Kathleen Mullins, the fifteen
year-old daughter of Mr. and
Mrs. Newton Mullins of Georges
Fork, will represent the state of
Virginia in the canning demon
stration at the National 4-H Club
Congress to be held in Chicago,
November 28 to December 2.
This young lady won this hon
or by taking the area, district and
state awards in canning. She won
the last honor in competition
with girls from all sections of the
state, some of them college stu
dents.
Kathleen leaves for Chicago
November 26. While there, she
will be entertained by the Inter
national Harvester Company. In
addition to the usual banquets
and dances, she will take part in
sight-seeing tours of the city. She
will also appear on a radio pro
gram.
During this year, Kathleen has
canned 800 jars of food and has
helped her mother with another
200 jars.
Bee Vanover, of the Ramey
Flats section, was in town Wednes J
day to pay his taxes and his sub j
scription to The Herald and to
hear the election news.
Gilmer Fleming Is
County’s Corniest
Farmer
(Contributed)
Gilmer Fleming, winner in the
100 buslyfl corn contest, received
a check for fifty dollars this
week, accordin gto E. C. Smith.
This contest was open to any
farmer in Dickenson County.
However, only twenty entered
the contest. Mr. Fleming’s yield
was 121 bushels per acre. His
corn was planted in sod land on
April 22. The land received 1000
pounds of 4-12-4 fertilizer per
acre in addition to 100 pounds of
Ammonium Nitrate when the
corn was knee high. Mr. Fleming
had worked out his corn once be
fore most of the corn in Dicken
son County was planted. The
drought in August did not injure
his yield. He planted U.S. hybrid
yellow corn and had around 14,
000 plants per acre. Corn rows
averaged 40 inches wide and
the plants 12 inches in the row.
The second place winner was
M. C. Baker, who conducted a hy
brid corn demonstration of six
varieties. Mr. Baker said the
Funks 5154 white and 111. 200 yel
low performed the best on his
farm. Yield of the acre plat was
115 bushels. Mr. Baker received
a check for $40.00. Third place

AFTON SLUSS DIES
IN CAR WRECK
THURSDAY
Afton Sluss, 23 years of age,
was killed instantly last Thurs
day on Trace Fork when the car
he was driving ran into a heap
of gravel and turned over twice.
With him were Buddy Younts and
Ralph Ratliff. Younts was severe
ly cut about the head and Ratliff
sustained a broken arm.
It appears that young Sluss had
been to Coeburn and was driving
ra‘her fast when the accident oc
curred.
Afton Sluss was bom at Haysi
December 20, 1925, a son of Char
lie Sluss. For some years his par
ents have lived on Mullins Ridge.
He had been employed in the
mine at Lick Fork for nearly a
year before his death.
Young Sluss entered the army
Feb. 26, 1944. He was a heavy
mortar crewman with the rank of
fifth grade technician. He was
awarded the army occupation
medal (Germany), the good con- j
duct medal, the European theatre
ribbon and World War II Victory
Ribbon. He was discharged May
5, 1946.
Surviving members of the fam
ily are the parents, a brother,
Clarence Sluss; and three sisters,
Mrs. Garland Mullins, Irene and
Trulah Sluss.
Funeral services were held at
the Pound River Church of the
Brethren on Saturday, with Rev
erends Keither Wilson and Fay
ette Fields in charge. Services
were held at the home on the
night before by Rev. Harrison
Mullins. The local posts of the
American Legion and VFW at
tended the funeral.
Aged Georges Fork Lady
Passes Away
Wednesday
Mrs. Selina Mullins, a vener
able and highly respected lady of
Georges Fork, died at her home
early Wednesday after an illness
of about one year.
Funeral services will be held
a; 1 p. m. Friday. It is is expect
ed that Jack Kiser and Emory
Vanover will conduct services.
Burial will follow in the old Mul
lins cemetery on Georges Fork.
Mrs. Mullins was a daughter of
William and Diadema Dale and
was born in the Hurricane sec
tion of Wise county 81 years ago
the 13th of last April. She mar
ried John B. Mullins of Georges
Fork, 63 years ago, and they set
tled on that creek. Mr. Mullins
died several years ago.
She joined the Primitive Bap
tist church more than 40 years
ago, and lived a faithful and de
vout member until death. In her
later years she attended different
churches at every opportunity
and believed that unity should
prevail among all Christian peo
ple.
She is survived by six sons and
daughters: Creed Mullins, Mrs.
S. M. McFall and Newton Mull
ins, of Georges Fork; Dr, R, L.
Mullins, of New Jersey; Mrs, Nor
man Crouse, of Pound, and Mrs.
J, D. Nicewonder of McClure.
One son preceded her to the
grave several years ago.

FUGATE SPEAK
HERE SATURDAY
Thomas B. Fugate, Democratic
candidate for Congress, and Con
gressman John W. Flannagan
spoke to an audience of 300 at
a Democratic rally in the court
house Saturday afternoon.
Congressman Flannagan led oft
with the usual speech about bring
ing the jam to the lower shell'. He
also pitched into Eugene Worrell,
the Republican candidate for
Congress, asserting that Worrell
did not know the issues and did
not know in general whnt he was
talking about. In some way or
other, Flannagan and .Worrell
have got into a controversy about
hillbilly m us i c. Flannagan
brought down the house with the
statement that he had often danc
ed all night on rough floors or in
yards to the strains of mountain
music while Worrell had glided
around polished floors to the met
ody of “Waltz Me Around Again,
Willie.”
Candidate Fugate gripped the
attention of the audience and
held it without a ripple through
his speech. At the close he got a
great and prolonged cheer. He
came out strongly in favor of fed
eral aid to education.
Mrs. Cyntha Boatright, head of
the Democratic women’s organi
zation of the Ninth District, and
Hubert Peery, Fugate’s campaign
manager, talked briefly on the
topic of getting out the vote. Sam
Wright, Democratic county chair
man, presided.
Worrell To Speak Here
It is understood that Eugene
Worrell, Fugate's Republican op
ponent, will speak in Clintwood
Thursday at 7:30 p. m. He will
tour the county during the day,
with short addresses at Haysi,
and Clinchco.
J. C. SMITH IS OLDES
PRACTICING ATTORNEY
SOUTHWEST VA.
Attorney Cowan Smith is much
interested in the results of some
figuring he and Judge Skeen
have been doing. When these two
hung out their shingles as lawyers
in Clintwood in 1895, there were
from 175 to 180 lawyers in South
west Virginia. Of all that num
ber, Cowan remains the only
living practicing attorney. Now
78 years of age, he has practiced
longer than any lawyer in South,
west Virginia.
Juke boxes and pinball mach
ines in the places of Joe Fleming,
Taylor and Mrs. Nannie Suther
land were robbed Monday night.
Thus far, the robber is at large.
Fleming’s plare is just over the
hill from Clintwood on the Fre
mont road; Taylor’s is at the old
Peggy Ann Rose stand; Suther
land’s is above McClure. The
haul is not estimated, in money
and goods, above $300.00.
Local officers and state police
worked on the case all day Mon
day. The net result of their work
was a dim description of a man
seen the day before, spending
nickels and driving a car with
Ohio license plates. This man, it
appears, “cased the joints” Mon
day. At one place, he said he was
looking for rooms to rent. At the
others, he was just a passing
stranger with nickels to spend.
He also visited Berlin Fleming’s
place in Clintwood.
The doors had apparently been
forced open with some tool like
a wrecking bar. The haul at Flem
ing’s was estimated at $200. The
other losses were much less. The
robberies seemed carefully plan
ned, even though the take was
small for a night’s dangerous
work. It is said the owner, who
leases the machines on a 50-50
basis, checks them weekly and
that he was due Wednesday. But
why would a slick operator work
for such small wages? Oh well,
who knows?
PROSPECTORS DRILL FOR
OIL AT NORA
N. C. Sutherland, good citizen
of Stratton, was in to renew his
subscription to The Herald last
Monday. He reports that there is
a great deal of interest around
Nora in the shaft being sunk on
the old Frank Smith place by the
Clinchfield Coal Corporation. It
is supposed that search is being
made for oil. Mr. Sutherland says
the report is that the drilling will
go down a mile.

ington and Charles H. Smith were
married. The couple stood in the

roses The double ring ceremony

A reception was held following
the ceremony. The dining laoie was

sticks on eacn side. Punch and cane

Wanda Wilcox. Reporter
The FIRST GRADE, taught by
Mrs. Doty Porter and Mrs- Emmett
Reese, has 25 pupils enrolled, and
Mrs. Porter is glad to have such
a .nice group. The children have
learned how to color, during the
past week, and are going to get three
new workbooks. They are learning
how to read and write their names,
are making an alphabet and nursery
rhyme booklet. Under the direc
tion of Mrs. Doty Porter, the class
has learned a nmber of new songs.
All the new pupils, and the entire
class, say they like school very
much.
The SECOND GRADE, taught by
Mrs. Paul Lindholm. is glad to wel
come four newcomers: Jimmie Wil
burn of Sidon. Jimmie Ellisop of
Pickens. Betty Ellington of Minter
City and Billy Rae Carr or Potts
Camp school. They are also glad to
welcome Joanna Hoff and Ed Thur
mond back to school after having
been quarantined against the
measles.
The THIRD GRADE, taught by
Mrs. Bill Schneller. also welcomes
four newcomers: Pete Brown of
College Hill, Edwin Earel of Ebe
nezer, Evelyn Spell of Ebenezer,
and Lamara Abies of Coxburg. The
third grade has 42 enrolled this year.
They are making decorations of
squirrels and sunflowers,
Tho FOURTH GRADE, taught by
Mrs- Matthew O'Reilly, has a num
ber of pupils that have never attend
ed Lexington school before. From
Bethesda five of these come. One
is from Ebenezer and one from
Hazlehurst..

Long-Time Resident
Pays Advertiser Visit
Ed Stigler of Tchula reports that
fishermen from the Gulf Coast have
been catching up to 15-foot alliga
tors in Tchula and Horseshoe lakes,
fne hides, he said, sell for $1.50 a
foot.
Mr. Stigler, who has been a long
time resident of the county, said he
worked for the Advertiser when Mc
Kinley was assassinated, and that
the message of his death was relay
ed by Sam Sample, telegrapher, of
Ebenezer.
He also recalled the first tele
phone line in the county wdiich was
from Durant to Castalian Springs,
and says “You couldn’t make people
believe you could talk to anyone."

PARENT TEACHER ASSN.
HONORS FACULTY MEMBERS
The Brooksville Parent Teach
er Association entertained ap
proximately one hundred persons
at an informal reception last
Thursday night at the Woman’s
Club building, in honor of the
faculty of the schools.
Greeting guests as they arrived
were Mrs. Bennie Fannin, local
P.-T. A. president; Mrs. W. S.
Miller, secretary; and Mrs. John
D. Law, hospitality chairman,
and her committee, Mrs. Travis
Slayden and Mrs. Geo. Allen.
A/ delightful music program
consisting of vocal and piano
solos by three talented school
girls and accompanied at the
piano by Mrs. Loie Hall was pre
sented. Miss Joy Fannin sang
‘‘Four Leaf Clover,” by Brownell,
and ‘‘When Church Is Out,” by
Carrie Jacobs Bond, followed by
Miss Jayne Weeks playing Rud
olph Friml’s ‘‘lndian Love Call”
on the piano and concluded with
Miss Martha Slayden’s vocal ren
ditions of “Night Wind,” by Ern
est R. Ball and “Smilin’ Through”
by Arthur A. Hand.
Mrs. Fannin introduced Louis
L Fisher, County Supt. of Schools
who after a short welcoming ad
dress, introduced Miss Marguerite
Neal. County Supervisor, and W.
A. Biggart and N. G. Mangin,
Principals of the High and Ele
mentary schools, respectively.
Messrs. Biggart and Mangin in
turn introduced the faculty of
their schools, many of which were
new-comers to Brooksville.
After a short get acquainted'
session between the parents and
teachers, refreshments of green
tinted punch and assorted cookies
were served with Miss Jo Carol
Law presiding at the punch bowl.
The refreshment table was over
laid with a linen cloth and cen
tered by a low ibawl of mums and
glads in shades of peach, white
and lavender, and flanked on
either side by green candles in
crystal candelabra. Large bou
quets of white and yellow chrys
anthemums and graceful potted
plants, placed at vantage points
throughout the hall, completed
the decorations.
PRESBYTERIAN CONFER
ENCE WILL BE HELD
IN BROOKSVILLE
District No. 3of the Woman’s
Auxiliary of St. John’s Presby
tery will be held in the Brooks
ville Presbyterian Church next
Monday, Sept. 27th., beginning at
10:30 a. m. Mrs. L. D. Burdine,
of Leesburg, District Chairman,
will preside over the conference.
The worship program will be
conducted by Mrs. M. G. Lyerly.
A solo number entitled, “I Come
To Thee,” by Roma, will be ren
dered by Mrs. H. J. Brinkley.
Mrs. T. L. Slayden will give the
welcome address. Mrs. W. A.
Fulton, Jr., local Auxiliary presi
dent, will give the achievements
attained by the Auxiliary of the
Brooksville Presbyterian Church.
Reports will also be given by
the presidents of the other Auxil
iaries in the district which in
clude the Presbytery of Brooks
ville, Inverness, Bushnell, Wild
wood, 'Leesburg, Oakland and
Umatilla.
Mrs. H. B. Hutchinson, of Tam
pa, who is president of the Auxil
iary of' St. Johns Presbytery, will
give the main address. There
will be other outstanding speak
ers, including Mrs. E. F. Mont
gomery, of Lake City, who is
president of the Woman’s Auxil
iary of the Synod of Florida.
Lunch will be served by the
Brooksville Presbyterian women
at 12:30. Miss Mary Birt Alison
is in charge of the luncheon
which will be held in the Legion
hall. All of the Brooksville Pres
byterian women are invited to be
present.
YOUNG PEOPLE’S
AUXILIARY MEETS
AT BAPTIST CHURCH
The Young Peoples’ Auxiliary
of the First Baptist Church met
at the church at eight o’clock
Monday. Mrs. Gus Schultz, the
Auxiliary leader, presided over
'the meeting.
This was an organizational
I meeting and the following officers
were elected: President, Helen
Huckaby; Vice President, John
Romine; Secretary and Publicity
Chairman. Melvina Smith; Treas
urer, Bunny Eppele; Program
and Poster Chairman, Clint Kim
brough. Remaining officers will
be elected at a future meeting.
The Auxiliary will meet every
second and fourth Monday night
in each month. All young people
and the fifteen sixteen year old
boys are urged to attend the
meetings.
Refreshments were served after
the meeting and a social hour
was enjoyed by the group.
Those attending were Ellen
Huckaby, John Romine, Bunny
Eppele, Clint Kimbrough, Vir
ginia Tomkinson, Melvina Smith,
Mrs. Gus Schultz and son, Clin
ton.
BROOKS OUT OF HOSPITAL
Friends of Jack Brooks will be
glad to know that he has return
ed to his home at Bailey Hill
after being confined to St. Joseph
hospital in Tampa where he re
cently underwent an operation.
He is much better and is fast re
cuperating.
BAPTISTS TO HOLD
PROMOTION DAY
Promotion Day for tne Baptist
Sunday School will be held Sun- j
day when members of the present
classes will be advanced to the
next age class and many will be
promoted to the next department.
The departments which will grad
uate members are the Nursey,
Beginners, Juniors, and Interme
diate. An appropriate program
is being planned for a general
assembly of the entire school at
which time the certificates of pro
-motion will be given to those
graduating to older departments.
All Sunday School members are
urged to be present for it is hoped
that an attendance equal to the
enrollment will be reached.
MISS MARTHA CHORVAT
WEDS IN WASHINGTON
Mr. and Mrs. Paul Chorvat of
Masaryktown, have announced
the marriage of fheir daughter,
Martha Christina, to John Ed
ward Ott, son of Mr. and Mrs.
Charles Ott, of Wilkes Barre,
Pa. The ceremony took place at
the Shrine of the Sacred Heart in j
Washington, D. C., August 28th.
The Rev. Paul Repetti officiating.
Mrs. Ott had as her matron of
honor her sister, Mrs. B. B Burns
of West Palm Beach, and Mr. Ott
was attended by his brother, Mr.
Anthony Ott of Wilkes-Barre, as
best man.
A reception was held lor imme
diate friends and relatives after
which the couple left for a tour
of New York State. They will
make their home in Washington,
D. C.
The bride is a graduate if the
Hernando High School, Class of'
1940, and has many friends in
Brooksville who will wish for her
abundant happiness during her!
wedded life.
MISS MARY FRAZEE
ENTERTAINS QUARTERDENS
Miss Mary Frazee entertained
the Quarterden Club at her home
Wednesday evening. She used the
season’s yellow pompom chrys
anthemums in beautifying the
party rooms.
There were two tables of bridge
and following the game, prizes
w’ere awarded Mrs. Joe Mason for
high score, Mrs. James White
hurst second high and Mrs. John
D. Law, the cut award.
The hostess served a delicious
salad plate and coffee.
Guests attending included Mrs.
John D. Law, Mrs. James White
hurst, Mrs. George Allen, Miss
Lula Hope, Mrs. John McKethan,
Mrs. Joe Mason. Mrs. T. L. Slay
den, Miss Mary Birt Alison.
Mrs. E. J. Allen, of Annapolis,
Md., is the guest of Mr. and Mrs.
Hilliard Allen at Garden Grove.

Mr. and Mrs. R. R. Lingle spent
several days at Clearwater Beach
the past week.

Gainesville, are announcing the
birth of a son, Wilburn Carlos, at
the Hernando hospital on Sept.
14th. Weight 7 lbs. 9% oz.
Mr. and Mrs. George W. Bellah,
Jr., of Hernando, are announcing
the arrival of a daughter at the
Hernando hospital Monday, Sept.
20th. This fine baby weighs 7
lbs. 6 oz., and has been named
Vanera.
Mr. and Mrs. Carey Baxley, of
Inverness, are announcing the ar
rival of a fine daughter, bom Sat
urday, Sept. 18th. at the Her
nando hospital. The baby has
been named Sandra Lee and she
Weighs 6 lbs. 5 oz.
PHILATHEAS TO MEET
The Philathea Class of the
Methodist Church will hold its
regular meeting and covered dish
supper Tuesday evening at the
Florida Power kitchen at seven
o’clock. Members are urged to
come.

SAFETY CAMPAKN MARKS SCHOOL OPEMMG
Originator of the Schoofboy Patroi. Sergt. WiHiatn Font#, of
Akron, tooks on approvingly as Wiitiam O'Neit. right, president of
The Genera) Tire Rubber Co., and sponsor of the "Give The Kids A
Brake" safety campaign, to be staged as schoot opens this Fait,
expiains the theme and hope that iie back of the campaign to Ji^o"y
Penoff kneeiing; Frankie La Kocca. and Eddie Butter, hotding "Stop
sign. Jimmy. Frankie and Eddie are cub scouts and members of the
Schoothov i'atrot and they wit) take an active part in the pian to
reduce accidents as young America returns to the ctassrooms.
30,000.000 rrgr<) To
"Give The Kids A Hrake**
Backed by the National Safety
Counci) and sanctioned by the
Nationat Association of Police
Chiefs of America and the nationa)
headquarters of the Parent-Teacher
association, is the "Give The Kids
A Brake" safety campaign ptanned
by The General Tire Rubber
Company to coincide with the re
opening of schools throughout the
country.
Every possible medium will be
used to carry the message of
safety, not only to the children
themselves but to the adults in
whose hands rests the safety of
our young people.
!n lauding the company and i*sj
distributors, Ned H Dearborn,!
National Safety Council prestdent!
said: "The General Tire Rubber
Company's Fall campaign is. to;
my knowledge, the greatest safety
program undertaken by a mer-j
chaadising organization. This is;
precisely the type of public servtce
industrv ran best perform to save
human life in America from the
scourge of accidents, and we con-!
gratulate and thank you for a
splendid enterprise."
Among those hading General's
program was Sergt. William Fouts,
of the Akron, O., police force, one
of the originators of the Schoolboy
Patrol, which has served such a
useful purpose in reducing acci
dents among school children.
"We are proud to have the
Schoolboy Patrol glorified in
General's campaign," Sergt. Fouts
said, "These youngsters have
proved invaluable helpers in the
cause of safety."
The "Give The Kids A Brake"
poster has been honored with
second prize in the annual exhibit
of Outdoor Advertising Art.
Speaking for the company,
William O'Neil, president, said:
"We are honored to have been
asked by the National Safety
Council to undertake this program.
Our youngsters are a little reckless
at this time of the year, so it is
our responsibility to protect them.
If this nation-wide campaign re
duces tragedies, we will fee! the
progrant has served a useful
purpose."
Jean G. Gifford
Weds War Veteran;
To Live in Florida
Miss Jean G. Gifford, daughter of
Mr. and Mrs. Harry Gifford of Chan
man "street, became the bride of Km
Sanchini of Putnam and New Brit
ain at a ceremony perfromed Satur
day morning in the Methodist church
by the Rev. Percy F. Smith, minister.
Virginia Gifford attended her sister
as maid of honor and William Sim
mons served as best man. Ushei
were Douglas Lee and Louis Vezina
The bride was attired in a vintag"
suit with black accessories and wore
a white corsage. Her sister wore a
green suit with matching accessorie
and wore a corsage of yellow roses.
After the ceremony a reception wa
I held in the church parlors and later
the couple left for a trip to Florida,
where they will make their home.
The bride was formerly employed
at the plant office of the Southern
New England Telephone company
here and her husband was employed
at deFlorez airport in Woodstock. He
is a graduate of Putnam Technical
School and served with the armed
forces in the ETO during the past
war. She is a graduate of Putnam
High School.
MARLAND NAMED AS DAIRY
STAFF MEMBER AT UCONN
Richard E. Marland of Danielson,
well known in the Putnam area, is
one of five members named to the
staff of the College of Agriculture
at the University of Connecticut, ac
cording to President A. N. Jorgen
sen.
Marland was named assistant pro
fessor of dariy gnanufacturing. He is
a graduate of the University, B.S.,
in 1942 and has a M S. degree from
Michigan State College, 1944.

St. Mary’s Post
Will Nominate
Officers
Terence McGarry, present com
mander of St. May’s Post, No. 1213
Catholic War Veterans, announcer
today that a nominating’ committei
has been appointed to select a slatt
of post officers for the coming year
The committee will present its list oi
nominees at the next regular meet
ing Monday, November 1st.
The first meeting of the post year
which starts in October, was held
Sunday morning in St. Mary’s hall,
Meetings had been suspended for the
summer months. A program for the
coming year was discussed.
It wras reported St. Mary’s Post ol
Putnam is one of the largest and
most active posts in the state. It wa?
chartered in February 1947 with 40
members. It has since grown to an
organization of over 100 members.
Officers for the coming year will
be elected November 1st. An instal
lation ceremony will be held at a
special dinner meeting at a later date.
