CLUB WOMEN LOOK ON THE SERIOUS SIDE
BENEFIT PLANS were made by members of the BVA, pictured above, at a recent press party
given at home of Nadine Cole. The club members plan to stage a gigantic club review at
Earl Carrolls, with proceeds going to aid needy entertainers. Picture shows Earl Carter,
Duke Williams, Avanell Harris, Camille Cannady, Joseph Morris, Lillian Cumber, Libby Clark,
Anita Turner Williams, Wini Orr, Ruth Mills, club president; J. T. Gipson, Suzette Harbin,
May Johnson, Qoris Pitts, Louise Franklyn, George Brady, Mrs. Brady and Nadine Cole. (See
story in theatrical section.) —Sheffield photo.
Esther T. Greenly Chairs
42nd Convention of State
Association Colored Women
SAN DIEGO (NPB). —Accord
ing to “oldest observers,” the
42nd Annual Convention of the
California State Association of
Colored Women, one of the larg
est and most powerful organiza
tions of its kind in the west, was
one of the most instructive and
entertaining in Channel City his
tory. Mrs. Esther T. Greenly,
state president of the body, pre
sided over the meet.
The Rev. Mr. Charles Hamp
ton, pastor of the church where
the convention was held, extend
ed the convention a hearty wel
come on behalf of official San
Diego. Hostess club groups to the
convention included the Societtes
Charity and Social club, The Gar
denettes Charity and Social club
and the Jolly Matrons Charity
and Social club. The three
groups did all, and more than
was expected in the way of
clearing the decks for official
action and providing suitable
means for comfort and enter
tainment during the three day
meet. Mrs. Gertrude R. Hicks,
executive secretary of the south
ern section of the group, pre
sided over the thre groups dur
ing the setting up period.
Mrs. Greenly was drafted by
the convention to serve as its
head for another term. The body
voted to hold its 1949 meet at
Sacramento. The convention
honored the Negro Women’s Ci
vic Improvement Club of Sacra
mento with a trophy cup for out
standing achievement because of
its having purchased the beau
tiful club house there. Mrs. Viola
Brooks accepted on behalf of her
organization. Mrs. Sadie Guest,
MISS CAROL BRICE, whose triumphant career has won for her the famed Na u m be rg
Foundation Award, and favorable comment from the country's leading conductors and music
critics, is pictured above with Serge Koussevitsky, famed conductor of the Boston Sym
phony Orchestra and Fritz Reiner, conductor of the Pittsburgh Symphony Orchestra. Miss
Brice, will be sponsored by her Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorors on Oct.-24, at Philharmonic
Auditorium. (See Social Set).
of Victorville, won the trophy for
the “most outstanding” superin
tendent of the year.
Scores of San Diegans opened
their doors and laid out heavily
laden tables to the visitors. The
Clay Rancho was the scene of a
splendid dinner affair. It was
richly adorned by the presence
of Agnes Knight, a most charm
ing hostess. Among those pres
ent at that affair were the Rev.
Chaplain Mabel L. Oliver, Mes
dames Mabel V. Gray, La Gullia
Shaw, Neadras Miller, Leola B.
Wilson and others. The affair
terminated with a luncheon giv
en officers of the convetionn by
Mrs. Hattie Payne, a most
dazzling and pretentionsly ser
viced affair.
Margaret Bigby on
Staff of Pasadena
Y.W.C.A.
Mrs. Margaret Bigby of Pasa
dena has been appointed a mem
ber of the staff at the Pasadena
YWCA. Mrs. Bigby is in charge
of the desk during the morning,
in the central registration de
partment. This brings the num
ber of race staff members up to
three.
Mrs. Bigby is a native of Seat
tle, Wash., and has just become
a resident of Pasadena.
PALS CLUB
Mrs. L. Lee of 1353 E. 43rd

Club recently. The ladies enjoy
ed meeting and also a very

President of
Nevin PTA Takes
Action
Mrs. J. M. Wise, Nevin Ave.
P.T.A. President met with her
executive board this week in the
principal’s offiCte to ratify the
years program and complete
plans for the annual member
ship campign. Nevin P.T.A.
needs the membership of every
parent for there is much work
to be done, declared Mrs. Claude
C. McAdams, membership chair
man. We are pleased to an
nounce that Mrs. Frances Rogers,
School nurse will be at the
school every Monday and Thurs
day, and every third Wednesday
with Dr. Clifford Gordon, school
physician.
Founder of Wands
Visits Coast
Major Grace Lee Steven of
Chicago, Illinois, was honor
guest at a dinner party given
by Mrs. Rosa D. Moore of Pasa
dena at her lovely home, 1132
Forest Ave. Mrs. Steven is the
founder of the Women’s Army
of Natipnal Defense (WANDS)
for the Pacific Coast. Mrs. Moore
is captain of the Pasadena
Chapter.
September 30, the members
and friends of the chapter pre
sented Major Steven with a beau
tiful leather and suede album
designed and inscribed by Mrs.
Moore.
After 3 months of traveling on
an extensive tour which included
Victoria Vancouver, B. C., and
other points through the great
northwest to Seattle, Washing
ton, where she attended the
Women’s national Federation of
Clubs, Major Steven, after spend
ing a few days in Los Angeles,
left the city Monday for Chicago.
WANDS OFFICIAL—Major Grace Lee Stevens of Chicago,
Illinois, is pictured above with Captain Rosa D. Moore of
Pasadena, as she looks over the lovely leather album pre
sented her by the chapter. (See story) Vera Jackson photo.
ADDING THE FEMININE TOUCH to the plans of the benefit cocktail party being spon
sored by the Association for the Abolition of Second Class Citizenship will be the wives of
the members who will serve as hostesses. The affair will be given Sunday afternoon,
October 17, in the garden of Mr. and Mrs. Mitchell Miles, 3715 W. 27 street. Hostesses
pictured above, left to right, are Mmes Jefferson D. Fowler, I. H. McClelland and Henry
Lewis.
Local Iotas to Host National
Confab Here in August; New
Auxiliary Honored at Tea
Alpha Phi members of lota
Phi Lambda sorority entertained
Sunday at tea honoring the
charter meeting of their auxil
iary organization, made up of
mothers and sponsors of the
members interested in the pro
gram of lota Phi Lambda.
k The lovely affair was held at
the West 35th street home of
Mr. and Mrs. Walter Mullens.
General chairman of the day
was Mrs. Marion B. Jackson,
president of the Alpha Phi
chapter. She explained in detail
the work of lota Phi Lambda on
a national and local scale. Plans
for the 1949 convention to be
held here in Los Angeles were
mentioned, with emphasis on
the part that the auxiliary
would play in preparing for this
meet that will mark the 20th
anniversary of lota Phi Lambda
the only national business wom
en’s Sorority of color.
Amog the charter members at
tending the Sunday affair were
Mmes. Edith Lee, Ly 11 ya n
Beavers, Arnetta Hoyt Brown,
Leila Mullen, Martha Wither
spoon, Helen Watkins, Esther
Cormia, Jean Hamilton and
Olva E. Ellison. At the next
meeting of the group slated for
the second Sunday in November,
officers will be elected and a
constitution will be set up. The
auxiliary will swell its member
ship at the close of each of
ficial initiation, allowing the
mothers and friends of the new
lota sorors to take active part in
the program.
Mrs. Charlotta A. Bass, na
tional honorary member, was
speaker of the afternoon. Need
for a serious program of work
was stressed by this veteran
Editor, Publisher, when she
spoke of the field that is al
ready crowded with many or
ganizations whose sole purpose
is to give parties.
Among chapter members as
sisting were: Lucille Reynolds,
Frankie Osborne, Margaret
Goode, Mary Frances Peterson,
Evelyn Brown, and Jessie Mae
Beavers.
Phys Art Lit Mor
Opens Club Year
With Breakfast
The Phys-Art-Lit-Mor club, one
of the city’s oldest and most cul
tural groups, had its opening
meeting of the season during the
breakfast hours at Alena’s Tea
Room recently.
The program was very infor
mative, with Mrs. Wilma Chap
man in charge. Among the wo
men participating were Mrs.
Ethel Thomas, Mrs. Nellie Cor
lisle and Mrs. Jamison Barnes,
who spoke on “The Women in
the World Ttoday.” Members
and friends found the talk very
interesting and enthusiastically
received it.
The president, Mrs. Belle Per
due, was introduced, and high
lighted the morning program
with a very good synopsis of the
year’s work. She introduced the
officers for the ensuing term, and
welcomed the visitors to this
opening session. Many outstand
ing club workers of Los Angeles
those present at the delightful
affair.

dent; Mrs. Verna Smith, Mrs. Ursula Hirschwitz, business manager; Mrs. Iva Culpepper,
and Mrs. Blanche McKee, not pictured.

The Homemakers club held its
regular meeting with Mrs. Bar
bara Haynes as hostess. Elabo
rate plans were completed for
the clubs seventh annual
Bazaar.
A colorful carnival will be the
festival setting this year and
will be held on November 7.
Bus and Pro
Fashion Review
Huge Success
Federated Business and Pro
fessional Women’s Leagues of
America presented its first an
nual fall fashion revue recently
at the home of Mr. and Mrs.
Albert Grace. With the setting of
fall flowers the Business and
Professional Women stepped into
the spotlight with one of the
most unique fashion shows ever
to be given in the Angel city.
Francois Andre’ was there with
his Hollywood Star models;
Frances Winston with her Tots
and Teens; Jewel Roberts,
Beulah Johnson and Robbie
Slocum showing hats; Arkin and
Freeman displaying furs; Gladys
Hill and Louise Cox Hill with
hair styles; Fannie Buckley and
Anna Mae Gatson modiste’; and
jewelry from Brown’s Jewelry
Shop.
Mrs. Norva R. H. Patterson
was chairman assisted by
Fannie Buckley, Jewel Roberts
and Emma Brown. The Mistress
of Ceremonies was Peggy Hop
kins who in turn introduced the
Master of Ceremonies War Per
kins.

Allied Arts League
Christens New Dance Studio
Of Noted Jeni Legon
It was a gay and festive meet
ing of the Allied Arts League, of
which Mrs. Dorothy Johnson is
president, w'hen that group of
socially prominent patrons of the
arts christened the new westside
dance studios of Jeni LeGon at
2140 South Vermont Sunday
morning.
With a record attendance, the
league members heard an il
luminating lecture on the “Inter
relationship of the Arts” by Dr.
A. Bill, of the California Art
School. Lively discussion followed
the address by Dr. Bill.
Mrs. Helen Garrott and Miss
Fay M. Jackson were joint hos
tesses of the meeting, while Mrs.
Betty Vaughn presented the pro
gram. At the conclusion of the
meeting, members were escorted
throughout the spacious studio
rooms of the enterprising Miss
LeGon where children and adults
of all ages and races may take
part in classes in the ballet and
ballroom dancing under expert
teachers.

nah Mays, corr. sec’y.; Mrs.

community leaders such as com

are bringing to the boys and

dance which will be beneficial to
them in professional life or sim
ply through health and spiritual
betterment.”

Ethel Valencia and others.

The Jolly Crew Social club en

ocr_text:
Tigers Hit Stride
In Defeating
McGill 27 To 13
The Tigers of Moss Point hav
ing completely outplayed and
outclassed the McGill eleven of
Mobile Friday night by a score
af 27 to 13 will be hot after their
third win in a row as they take
on a strong Big Eight team from
Natchez tonight at 8:00 o’clock
kickoff time on the Moss Point
field.
Moss Point scored in every
period except the first Friday
night. McGills opening kick-off
was taken by Son Rhodes on his j
10 returning to the 10, where the
game moved back and forth with
little headway for either team
until a fumble by Moss Point
was recovered by McGill on their
own 38 to end the first quarter.
J. Partridge on the first play
of the second period broke
through the Tiger line and raced
62 yards for the first score of the
game. Try for placement was
blocked.
An exchange of punts after the
ensuing kick-off evened the
game up, with Pat Gardner tak
ing Duke’s punt on the midfield
stripe, running back to the 30
where he lateralled to Bobby
Rouse, who took the ball into
the end zone standing up.
Norman Wells’ kick was taken
by Partridge on his, own 10 re
turning to McGill’s 35. Runs by
Partridge and Dooley moved the
ball down to the 4 of Moss Point,
where V. Dooley cracked over
center for the tally. Dooley's
placement was good.
Dooley's kick-off was taken by
Pat Gardner on the 5 and re
turned to the 34, but a pass of
Maggie Hammond was intercept
ed by Partridge returning to his
own 46. TWo plays later Bobby
Rouse reached up and pulled in
McGill’s pass on the 40 and lat
eralled off to Maggie Hammond
moving the ball to the 28. Son
Rhodes picked up 6 yards and a
pass by Maggie Hammond to Son
Rhodes moved the ball to the 1.
Maggie Hammond on a quarter
beck sneak went over for the
score. Norman Wells evened the
game up at the half by booting
the ball between the uprights.
Second Half
‘Norman Wells’ kick-off was
taken by Partridge on his own
15 returning to the 18. Great de
fensive play on the part of J. T.
Shepherd forced the Yellowjack
ets to punt out on the 45 of Moss
Point. Pat Gardner, Son Rhodes
and James Myers moved the ball
to the 155 of McGill, but McGill
held and quick kicked out with
Pat Gardner taking the ball on
the 35 returning to the 22 of Mc
Gill. Son Rhodes picked up 11
yards and Pat Gardner went
I-—-■—
I
through the line for the score.
Norman Wells' placement was
good.
l^orman Wells’ kick-off was
taken by Partridge on his 10
returning to the 46. A fumble on
the next play was recovered by
J. T. Shepherd. Runs by Son
Rhodes and Pat Gardner moved
the ball to the 5 of McGill, where
the Jackets took over and punt
ed out, taken by James Myers on
the 45 handing off to Pat Gard
ner who returned to the 37 of
McGill. Maggie Hammond’s pass
es to Flim Colville and runs by
James Myers, Pat Gardner and
Son Rhodes moved the ball to
the 5. An off side penalty against
McGill moved the ball to the 1
foot line. James Myers cracked
over center for the score. Again
Norman Wells split the up-rights.
The game ended with the ball
in McGill’s possession due to an
intercepted pass on their own 3.

Gulf Hills Women
Golfers Tie With
Pascagoula Players
A highly successful tournament
took plaee Thursday afternoon
of last week at the Pascagoula
Country club with the players so
evenly matched that a tie oc
curred between the Biloxi women
of the Gulf Hills club and the
Pascagoula players, the score be
ing 24 1-2 points.
The low score, 95 points, was
made bv Mrs. Felix Herrill of the
Gulf Hills club.
The Pascagoula club women
have been invited to play a re
turn match at an early date. In
the meantime, November 4 has
been set as the date for ancrther
tournament between the Pasca
goula and the Gulf Hills women
here.
The tournament Thursday was
preceded by a luncheon at which
the visitors were honor guests.
The men of the Pascagoula club j
were guests of the Gulf Hills club
Sunday.
The party was composed of
Charles Weaver, Carl Brinkman. I
John Furr, Joe Morris, Charles
Cornell, Jim Moran, Barney Al-
vis, Lorenzo Humphreys and N.
E. Kruse.
Work on the Washington Mon-;
ument in Washington, D. C. was j
suspended in 1854 when the shaft
was 150 feet high and was not
resumed until 1880.
Rebels Run Wild
In Piling Up
50 To 0 Score
The Pascagoula Pantners will
step out of the Big Eight Con
ference play this week to take
on the strong Picayune High
school Maroon Tide tonight at
Picayune, after dropping a 50-0
decision to the Natchez Rebels in
a game played last Friday night
on the Natchez gridiron.
The Rebels lived up to pre
season predictions in rampaging
against the Panthers wit.) Fred
Foster, Reb back, completeing
14 out of 19 passes against the
Panthers for a total of 198 yards
to turn in a brilliant performance.
Foster also accounted for one
of the eight Natchez touchdowns,
as he recovered the opening kick
off, booted by Arthur Grimsle.v,
end, in the Pascagoula end zone.
Foster Hurls Aerials
Foster passed to End Billy
Parks twice for touchdowns.
One scoring aerial covered 41
yards while "the other was good
for eight. Another Foster pass
to "Bubba” Kaiser, back, was
good for 28 yards and a marker.
Kaiser led the scoring parade
for the night, adding two other
touchdowns to the one he gain
ed credit for by’receiving Foster’s
toss. He ran 40 yards across the
goal with an intercepted aerial
in the last quarter and scored in
the second stanza on a two-yard
plunge.
Gene McGehee intercepted a
Pascagoula aerial at midfield and
returned it for a touchdown and
Paul O’Malley tallied on a one
yard plunge to wind up the
night’s scoring.
Despite the fine passing of
Leonard Trehern, Pascagoula
was unable to advance past the
Natchez 20. Trehern completed
nine out of 14 attempts for 93
yards. The Panthers mustered
only 63 yards on the ground for
a total offense figure of 156 yards
while the Rebels were gaining
163 rushing for a grand total
of 361 yards. Natchez made 15
first downs to Pascagoula’s eight.
Two Prizes Won
In Trout Rodeo
For Opening Week
The opening day prize was won
by E. E. Turner of Pascagoula
and the first week’s prize was
captured by A. G. Carlisle of
Ovette in the Speckled Trout Ro
deo being conducted here by the
Jackson County Hunting and
Fishing Association, A. M. (Bud)
Pelham, president, announced
this week.
Mr. Turner won a De-liar scale
and fish knife for his entry of
20 3-4 inch speckled caught in
Lake Masse with a baby Mae
West on the opening day. Mr.
Carlisle hooked his prize winner,
21 3-4 inches, with a pole and
line and live shrimp in Bayou
Graveline and was awarded an
aluminum tackle box.
Mrs. A. F. Johnson of Pasca
goula has registered the largest
fish of any of the women anglers,
Mr. Pelham said. Her record is
20 1-4 inches.
Entries are pouring in, Mr. Pel
ham declared, and the competi
tion is expected to get exceed
ingly stiff by the time the con
test closes December 3. All prizes
will be awarded December 7.

PLAN FOR BANQUET
The Young Men’s Business club,
in regular session Monday night
continued discussions on plans
for the annual Moss Point High
School football banquet.
President Billie Schaub an
nounced that the date was set
for Friday night, December 10,
1948 at the Moss Point High
school cafeteria, 7:30 o’clock. The
Moss Point Woman's club will
serve.
Coach Johnnie Vaught, head
coach, University Mississippi,
will be the featured speaker.
Methodist Women
Observe World
Day Of Prayer
In observance of the World
Day of Prayer the members of
the Woman’s Society of Christian
Service of the First Methodist
church held an all day meeting at
the church.
A program on "Needs In Tlie
Mission Field” was given under
the leadership of Mesdames W
N. Branch and E. H. Bacot.
This was interspersed with
music given by the Youth Choir
under the direction of Mrs. Ralph
Farrell.
Lunch was served in the sub
story during the noon hour
recess.
The direct loss from forest fires
each year is estimated at more
than $32,000,000.
County Fanners To
Elect Committeemen
For Convention
Farmers in Jackson County
will receive notices within the
r.fcxt few days about annual
elections of community and
county farmer committees, Ellis
W. Hamilton, chairman of the
County Agricultural Conserva
tion (AAA) committee, said to
day.
Each farming community will
elect committeemen and alter
nates, as well as a delegate to
the county convention where a
three-man county committee will
be elected. Announcement of
the definite dates, hours, and
places for holding the elections
will be announced later.
i Mr. Hamilton said that 'arm
ers eligible ure those who are
I participating in tne 1948 agri
cultural cans6rvation program
;or who have a contract with the
'Federal Crop Insurance Corpora

operators, tenants, and share
croppers on farms where the con
servation program is being car- j
ried out.
In announcing the coming'
elections, Mr. Hamilton urged,
that all ,eligible farmers vote.
Since the committeemen are re
sponsible for developing, adapt
ing. and administering national
farm programs to meet local’
1 problems end needs, it is im
portant that the men elected
really represent the choice of the
OES Chapter Has
Pot Luck Supper
And Initiation
Extra festivity marked the
meeting of the Pascagoula chap
ter of the Order of the Eastern
Star in the Masonic hall Tues
day at 7:30 p. m. when initiation
of officers was followed by a
pot-luck' supper.
Fall leaves were effectively
used in decorating the hall, and
the table where supper was
served with Halloween colors
and motifs.
The meeting was conducted by
Mrs. Myrtle Cotton, worthy ms
majority of farmers in the com
munity.
tron and Forest Mcgehee, worthy I
patron.
It was announced that the
chapter had been invited by the
Ann Grayson chapter to meet
with it in Biloxi. November 9.
As this will coincide with the
meeting dote of the Pascagoula
chapter the latter has been can
celled and the next meeting will
be held November 23.
There were a number of guests
in attendance, among them be
ing/Mrs. Clotile Cirlot past dis
trict deputy of the state chapter,
Mrs. Nell Stouter and Fritz Lan
der, worthy matron and worthy
patron of the Moss Point chap
ter, to which Mrs. Cirlot also be
longs.
In the temperate zone, most
birds have two molts a year.
Women Of Calvary
Baptist Church Hold
All-Day Meeting
Instead of meting in circlej
this week the Woman's Mission
ary Society of Calvary Baptist
church held an all day meeting
at the church Tuesday.
The time was given to the
study of the book, "The Holy
Spirit in Missions" which was
ebly taught by the Rev. M. S.
Varnado, pastor of the church.
At noon a lunch was served in
the church annex.
Thirty-five members of the so
ciety took part in the study.

Take My Word for It
By Frank Colby
A Fine Point of Uaage.
A newspaper editor has sent me a
headline which, he says, “set off a
stormy but futile word battle in our
editorial department.’’ The head
line reads:
Pew Doubt Dewey Won’t Win.
The story that follows is to the
effect that most local G. O. P. mem
bers are confident of Dewey’s victory
in November.
“Will you give us your interpreta.
tion as to what the headline actually
means?’’ the editor asks.
Use of “doubt” followecWby a nega
tive statement Is a common misuse.1
|We often hear such expressions as:1
“I doubt if it won’t rain today; I
doubt if he won’t be there; he says
he doubts if he'll not be able to
come,’’ etc.
The collect wording is: I doubt
that it will rain today; I doubt that
he will be there; he says he doubts
that he will be able to come.
Now the correct wording for the;
headline should be: Few Doubt
Dewey Will Win, for this means that
almost all the members are sure that
Dewey will be elected, that only a
few entertain any doubt about it.
But as the headline was printed—
Few Doubt Dewey Won’t Win—it
states literally that practically all
the members are certain of Dewey’s
defeat, but a few members doubt, it.
This, of course, is contrary to what
is brought out in the story itself.
It is not a question of grammar,
but of logic and clarity of expression.

•Since the misuse is rather common,
today's column should make good
material for classroom discussion.
Teacher—Will it rain today?
Student—I doubt it.
Teacher—You doubt what?
Student—I doubt that it will rain
i today.
Teacher—Does any one else doubt
that it will rain today? (Three
hands go up.) That's 4 out of 50
Just a few. Now let’s write it
on the blackboard: Few doubt that
it will rain today.
In such a sentence the word douht
means "do not believe.” Therefore
“doubt that it won't” is equivalent
to "do not believe that it won't,’
which is a double negative.

Opening lead, king of spades.
Perhaps the most fascinating oi
all plays at the bridge table is the
squeeze. If the technique of its
execution is difficult, the defense
against its operation calls for even
greater accuracy. This hand was
shown to the reader yesterday with
the query as to whether he would
prefer to play or defend against the
six-diamond contract, with the
opening lead of the king of spades.
The deal was originally played in
a team-of-four match, with total
point scoring, and the bidding pro
ceeded in precisely the same man
ner at both tables. The opening
lead also was the same. The player
representing one team took the
opening lead and drew two rounds
of trumps, discarding the seven of
hearts. He deferred drawing any
more, for he was undecided what
discards to make from dummy. He
then led a low heart and the queen
was taken by East’s ace. A spade
'was returned and ruffed by declarer.
Trumps were drawn, dummy dis
carding a heart and a spade. The
king of hearts was cashed and the
closed hand re-entered with the
queen of clubs. Now four cards
from the end, dummy held three
clubs and the jack of spades. West
held three clubs and the queen of
spades. When declarer led his last
trump it was impossible for West
to make a safe discard.
At the other table the play pro
ceeded In the same manner for the
first three tricks, but at the fourth
trick, when the heart was led and
the queen played from dummy. East
made a brilliant diagnosis. It was
clear that declarer had six solid
diamonds, and the only other side
cards he could have were the king
and queen of clubs. It was evident
therefore that 11 tricks were avail
able to him, but that th#12th could
be developed in a squeeze against
West, who is known to have the
queen of spades and probably holds
a club stopper.
East refused to win the trick,
since he was certain that declarer
could not have a singleton heart.
The reason for the refusal was this:
It is a fundamental principle that
no squeeze can be operated until all
inevitable losers have been con
ceded. By refusing to pfe-mit de
clarer to concede his losing trie':
early in the play, East spiked the
enemy’s guns.

Dr. Walter Schutz went to Day
ton last week at the call of the exe
cutive secretary of the department
of world missions of the Evangelical
United Brethren church. Dr. Schutz
was in St. Joseph, Mo., Sunday, to
begin a fifteen-day speaking tour in
the midwest.
Word has been received here by
relatives telling of the return trip
of Miss Ruth Hilty, daughter of Mr.
and Mrs. Harvey Hilty, of near
Pandora. Miss Hilty has been doing
relief work in Holland and Germany
for the past three years-under the
Mennonite Central Committee which
has headquarters in Akron, Pa. Miss
Hilty is scheduled to sail for the
United States Sept. 24. She will
arrive in the states October 4.

Bear Creek were overnight
guests at the Bert Romine home
Mohday. They went on to Liv
ingston Tuesday to attend a
pure-bred Aberdeen Angus sale.

Mr. and Mrs. Russell Atkins
are the parents of a baby boy
born Tuesday in a Billings hos
pital.
Mr. and Mrs. Jim Schober left
last week for Rochester, Minn,
where Mr. Schoer will go
through the clinic.
Mr. and Mrs. Marvin Turner
and Mr. and Mrs. Robert Turner
of Sheridan spent the week end
in Hardin.
Mrs. Peg Sargent of Billinas
spent the week end in Hardin.
Mrs. Nellie Wemple and her

nia, accompanied by Mrs.

Dairy Industry Has
Golden Prospect For
Future In Montana
Montana’s dairy indstry has
only one way to go—up, and
the prospects for its future are
as golden as its butter, accord
ing to Thomas A. McMaster,
chief of the dairy division of
the state department of agri
culture.
One of the biggest boosts
promised the dairyman is the
huge population increase on the
west coast, offering a ready
market for Montana dairy pro
ducts. The same population in
crease has proved a boon to
Montana stockmen.
At present, however, McMaster
said, Montana dairymen can not
take advantage of the hungry
west coast market because milk
production in this state is at
a low ebb. Most of the milk
produced in the state now is
being sold as fluid milk—drink
in- milk, that is—as the price
for it is better than for milk
sold for butter and cheese.
In 1934 there were about 190,-
000 dairy cows in the state.
Three years ago there were
157,000. A couple of years ago
there were 137,000. Now there
are about 130,000. They are bet
ter cows—the culls have gone
into hamburger—but they can’t
produce the milk of 190,000
cows.
Why so few cows? Well, a lot
of former dairy men have been
lured into the farming or stock
raising business. It’s been faster
money with less work. The
manpower situation has been
bad on the dairies. It’s early
rising, long-hour work, and the
cows have to be milked every
day in the year.
Butter production is down
about 50 per cent in Montana,
but the state still makes almost
enough for its own needs. The
export has been cut down.
Cheese production is way off.
Usually butter and cheese are
made in the spring when there
is more fluid milk available
than needed. Now it’s all needed
as fluid milk.
Dairy cows cost an average
of $230 now, as against SIBO a
year ago, and it takes about
three years to get going in the
dairy business, so it isn’t the
right deal for the suitcase spec
ulator. But it has a golden out
look for tlhe real dairyman.
B
Lake Association
Tops for August
Bozeman, Oct.—With an av
erage of 31.4 pounds of butter
fat per cow, the Lake county
No. 1 dairy herd improvement
association topped all associa
tions in the state for production
during the month of' August,
acording to J. O. Tretsven, ex
tension dairy specialist at Mon
tana State college.
In second place for the month
was the Lewis and Clark county
association with an average of
30.5 pounds butterfat per cow, i
and ranking third was the Bit
ter Root No. 1 association with
an average of 30.1 pounds.
Tretsven’s monthly report also
shows that association members
in the state culled a total of
118 low producing cows from
their herds during August.
State Farmers Union
Meet October 20-23
The annual convention of the
Montana Farmers union will be
held in Great Falls Oct. 20-23,
acording to State President D.
W. Chapman. Nearly 1,500 dele
gates from 200 locals and 33
county units are expected to at
tend. James G. Patton, Denver,
national FU president, will be
the keynote speaker
Much of the convention’s at
tention will be given to farm
price supports and a progressive
farm program. New officers will
be elected and resolutions deal
ing with the Montana and Na
tional Farmers union program
will be considered.
n
County Attorneys
Elect Officers
At the close of Montana
County Attorneys’ association’s
annual convention in Boulder.
Hugh J. Lemire of Miles City
was elected president. Paul J
Murphy of Stanford was elected
vice president, and O. Louise
Replogle of Lewistown was re
elected secretary.
The attorneys’ group asked
the State Legislature to clarify
gambling laws, particularly
those covering slot machines
and non-profit social clubs.
Next year’s convention will be
held in Helena with Helena and
Helmville acting as co-hostesses.

HELENA, MONTANA, OCTOBER 1, 194
Tom J a Davis Declares War
On Post Office
However adept one may be as a mixer, a hand-shaker, a
toastmaster, a socialite, it is no token that he can grow sudden
ly into the stature of a United States Senator.
One would think that when Mr. Davis' campaign card was
so puerile as to gain a smile from the entire country by being
laughed at by The New Yorker, a journal of sparkling sar
casm, read all over the United States, his committee would cen
sor his public speeches.
Not so.
At Livingston, for state-wide publication, Mr. Davis said
some days ago that he was opposed to ALL forms of Socialism.
The most charitable view to take of that statement is that Mr.
Davis did not know what he was talking about; that he does
not know what Socialism is, or what imperatively necessary
forms of American industrial life it embraces.
Mr. Davis: The Highway system of every state is purely
Socialistic. Are you opposed to good roads? If you are elect
ed Senator, will you object to Federal aid for our highways?
The Public School system is Socialistic. Do you oppose free
education? Education aid to veterans is-Socialistic Housing
aid also?
The Forestry Service of the United States is Socialistic. It
was begun by Theodore Roosevelt and Gifford Pinchot. Do you
wish it abolished?
Old age pensions are Socialistic. Shall they be eliminated,
Mr. Davis?
Rural Electrification is a classic form of Socialism. Do you
oppose that, Mr. Davis? And if you do, and are elected, will
your opposition take the form of no further extension, or of can
celation of existing lines?
The Home Owners Loan Corporation is a Socialistic affair.
Has its work been beneficial or not?
The floor under farm prices, that has lifted farming from
recurrent foreclosures to financial comfort and stability, is the
invention of Henry A. Walace when Secretary of Agriculture.
That surely is Socialistic, Would you withdraw the plan for an
ever-normal granary"?
The Bureau of Mines is Socialistic. Must that go also, Mr.
Davis?
The Reclamation Service is Socialistic. That brings us
really to what you thought you were saying, Mr. Davis. You
are opposed to the Reclamation Service wherever and in what
ever way it may serve the people in preference to serving the
power monopolies of the United States.
Government quarantine of 'Foot and Mouth' disease is So
cialistic by nature. Would you place responsibility for that
with private industry?
In your shallow thought that you are opposed to all "Social
ization" do you include closing our Public Library, 'firing' the
Fire Department, closing the County Hospitals, eliminating the
City Health departments?
In the Universities the prime example of Socialism explain
ed to the pupils is the Postal departments of this, and all other
civilized nations.
Are you 'agin' that also?
Finally, Mr. Davis, all modern warfare is waged by Com
munistic effort. If the spokesman of capitalism, Mr. Truman,
or the favorite of New York bankers, Mr. Dewey, gets us into
a war a
Germany for a World War, as they armed Hitler, shall we
carry on that war by. purely Communist methods, "all for one,
and one for all", or shall we turn the job over to the Standard
Oil company, that four months after "Pearl Harbor" was still
sending oil to a South American port for re-delivery to Germ
any?
It requires deeper thought to be a useful Senator of the Unit
ed States than to be a cheer leader at a Rotary meeting.
—LOWNDES MAURY, Butte
SHALL WE SMILE?
The Republican candidate for senator in Montana is making the
nation laugh. Why not join in the jest?
To your ramparts Lucifer and Moloch,
Sound your bugles, beat your drums,
Tremble devils, Davis comes!

of high food prices was re
vealed as the Department of Justice
announced filing of anti-trust suits
against three of the largest U. S.

Named in the action were the Inter
national Harvester Co., J. I. Case Co.
and Deere Co. with nine of its sub
sidiaries. Each of the three is charged
with suppressing competition by
agreements forced on retail dealers.
In every case the monopoly prac
tices have pushed up the prices of
farm machinery. Cost of equipment
has been one of the main factors in
creasing the farmer's costs of produc
tion and resulting in a bigger food
bill for the country.
Trusts Kill Off Small Competitors
As pointed out by Assistant Attor
ney General Herbert A. Bergson, "the
average farming community can sup
port only one or two farm machinery
dealers."
panies carries a full line of equip
ment. By forcing its full line on the
dealers. Each is able lo enforce high
prices and kill off small competitors.
Since, in the words of the Depart
ment of Justice, the "exclusive ar
rangements limit the range of
selection which farmers might other
wise have in the purchase of farm
machinery" the monopolies enjoy full
One of the
Each of the three com
protection.
The object of the suits, according
to Bergson, is "to restore competition
in this industry and thereby enable
manufacturers of competing lines of
farm machinery to procure a substan
tial number of addition market out
let."
The suits if successful will permit
farmers to start lowering their prices.
However, little hope of immediate
price relief was held out. Anti-trust
suits generally take years as they are
fought through the courts. Until a
final court judgment is reached, the
monopoly practises continue.
DEWEY'S CHOICE ON
LABOR FRONT SLAPPED
BY WILLARD FRASER
"The contempt of the Republican
party for the working men and wom
en of American has been illustrated
again and again. The latest mani
festation of this utter disregard for
the rights of labor has been shown
by the appointment of Barak T. Matt
ingly by Governor Dewey, as his (Gov.
Dewey's) campaign labor chairman,"
Demo Congressional candidate, Wil
lard Fraser asserted in Roundup last
Friday evening.
Mattingly Wanted Taft-Hartley
Law for Missouri
"Mattingly is a St. Louis and
insurance lawyer, with a notorious
anti-labor record in Missouri. He has
been an opponent of minimum wage
legislation, and favored a little Taft
Hartley law. How would you work
ing-men like to have him as the next
secretary of labor?
Fraser continued, "I have also been
reliably informed that if a Republican
congress is elected, one of the first
laws they intend to pass is one re
quiring union treasuries to pay full
wage to employees kept from their
jobs by labor difficulties. By the past
voting record of my opponent, it is
obvious that if he should be elected,
he would support this, and all other
anti-labor and anti-social legislation."

Farm price supports and a progressive farm program will
get lots of attention when more than a thousand Montana farm
ers gather at the Civic Center in Great Falls, October 20-23, to
transact the annual business of the Montana Farmers Union.
Officers for the coming year will be elected and the FU pro
gram which is eyed so closely by reactionaries and liberals
alike will be hammered out to furnish guidance on the course
the organization will follow#.
for another year in these per
ilous times.

NFU President James G.
Patton will keynote the con
vention with an address on the open
ing day when he will review the
serious problems facing American
agriculttire. Mr. Patton spent several
weeks early in the summer in Paris
where he met with the International
Federation of Agricultural Producers
on the perplexing problem of farm
surpluses and food shortages and
means of eliminating both were con
sidered.
Members will be brought up to date
on developments with the Farmers
Union Central Exchange by Emil
Syftestad, general manager,
FUGE
has just embarked on a refinery ex
pansion program and Mr. Syftestad
will have important information on
the whole supply picture for farmers,
Montana farmers will have an op
portupity for the first time to meet|
J. E. Wells, Jr., who resigned as Co-1
operative Bank Commissioner to be
come general manager of the Farmers
Union Livestock Association. Inter
est in the regional livestock co-opera
tive is high in Montana since the as
CONDON STRESSES
IMPORTANCE OF
KINDERGARTEN
Mary M. Condon, democratic candi
date for state superintendent of pub
lic instruction, stressed the need for
revitalizing Montana's education, be
ginning at the kindergarten level, at
a reception held in her honor by the
Custer County Democratic Women's
Club on .Saturday, September 25 in
Miles City.
"The continued disregard of the
need for a kindergarten program for
Montana and the attitude that such
a program is one of the "frills" of
education is deplorable. The experi
ence of the kindergarten is in many
respects the most important part of
the child's education. It helps him to
make the adjustment to leaving the
shelter of his babyhood and making
his first appearance out in the cold
cruel world. He should not be ex
pected to make the complete adjust
metn that is required by entering
first grade without first having
learned to get along with his fellows.
Most of the grief and hatred for
school that characterizes some of our
school children today could have been
prevented by a kindergarten program.
It is especially necessary now that
our "war babies", many of whom have
been living a pretty hectic five years
with all of the post war problems of
their parents, are reaching school
age. The expense should not be an I
I
such things cannot be j
obstacle
measured in dollars and cents."
REFERENDUMS 51 AND 52 COMMITTEE COMPOSED
OF HEAVYWEIGHT MONTANA CITIZENS
Forty well-known Montanans will
head the campaign for Referendums
51 and 52, it has been announced in
Helena by Albert Erickson, campaign
manager. Referendum 51 seeks re
enactment and increase of the univer
sity millage tax from 3 mills to 6
mills and Referendum 52 is a $5,000,
000 bond issue for new buildings and
improvements at the six units of the
university of Montana.
Most of the men and women on the
state comhiittee are heads of Mon
tana organizations and represent farm
organizations, labor groups, veterans,
civic clubs and come from all sections
of the state.
The commitete urged a favorable
vote on Referendum 51 and 52 "be
cause enrollments have increased
81% since 1940, operational costs hâve
risen, and if we are to insure ade
quate college educations to Montana
youth, sufficient support and facilities
must be provided."
Members of the Referendum com
mittee are:
John S. Barovich, Miles City; Nels
Bach, Sidney; Mrs. Melville Black
ford, Lewistown; Lyman Brewster,
sociation has gone into processing via
the rendering plant program. Many
stockmen in the Farmers Union as
well as others will hear a good talk
from Mr. Wells on the future of co
operative livestock marketing.
M. W. Thatcher, general manager
of GTA, will have a prominent place
on the speaking program. With the
price support program for agriculture
slipping away, members can expect
Bill Thatcher to fire both barrels on
the recent doings of the politicians.
Speaking on education day is
Frances Leber, director of education
for the Eastern Division of the Farm
ers Union and editor of the paper for
that group. Mrs. Leber is a dynamic
platform speaker.
North Dakota Farmers Union presi
dent, aggressive Glenn Talbott is
scheduled for a talk on the member

j

ship drive. Glenn is all steamed up
Ion what is happening to farmers
1 these days and will bring a vital mes
sa e to Montanans.

C. E. Huff, director of the National
Farmers Union Insurances will be on
hand to tell the folks about the rapid
ly growing program of security for
farm people and how farmers are
waking up to the potential financial
strength they have when they pro
vide their own insurance.
Singing, dancing, dramatics and dis
cussions will enrich the convention
and make this thirty-third annual
meeting one to remember.
—Montana F. U. News
Its time our people got a break',
Bonner said, "by having their state
government run for their benefit."
TAKE LIQUOR OUT OF
POLITICS, DEMANDS
JOHN W. SONNER
"The last annual report of the Mon
tana Liquor Control Board for the
years 1943 to and including 1946
viewed in the light of reports prior
to those years, shows on its face that
politics must be squeezed out of our
liquor business if the taxpayers get
what they are entitled to under our
state monopoly control system," as
serted John W. Bonner, Democratic
candidate for Governor. Wednesday,
in Helena.
"As proof of this statement," Bon
ner said "in 1940 the percentage of
profit was approximately 25.4% on a
business of approximately $6,000,000.
In 1946 the profit was only 17% oh a
business of approximately $17,000,
000
In other wordé, during the days of
adverse economic conditions the tax
payers made twice as much profit on
1/3 less business than we are making
today. This is indeed a deplorable
condition especially when we need
money for the betterment of our state
and our citizens are continually asked
for more taxes.

Panthers, Tigers Lose To Murphy And
Biloxi; Play McGill Gulfport Tonight
_O.
Murphy Defeats
Panthers By
Score of 23-0
The Pascagoula Panthers will
take on another Mobile team to
night when they meet the Mc
Gill High School Yellowjackets
at 8 p.m. at the new War Mem
orial Stadium on Market Street.
The Panthers suffered their
second straight defeat of the
season last Friday night when
they lost a 23-0 tussle to Murphy
High School of Mobile, in a game
played on the latter’s gridiron.
The game marked the third
straight triumph of the season for
the Mobile team.
A big factor in the Murphy win
was Johnnie Howell, quarter
back, who not only did a good
job of running the team and
played an outstanding defensive
game, but who also tossed passes
that accounted for two of his
team's three touchdowns.
After a scoreless first period
Howell tossed to Julius Hared
mann on a 59-yard touchdown
play that broke the visitors' back.
After this pass the Pascagoula
club were put in a hole and never
were able to scramble out and
get back in the ball game.
Pascagoula had pecked away
at the Murphy forward wall for a
couple of first downs in the ini
tial period but never was able to
get within striking distance of
the Panthers’ goal line. In the
second quarter the visitors quick
kicked and the ball rolled dead
on the Murphy 21-yard line.

Lucedale Tigers
Take To Air To
Defeat OLV Team
The Panthers of Lucedale pull
ed the claws of the OLV Eagles
Thursday night by a score of 31
to 7. The Panthers scored in
every period of the game, through
a passing attack that OLV
couldn't seem to break up.
The Eagle’s won the toss and
chose to kick off. B. Mansfield
back, booted the ball down to the
Panthers’ 20 yard line where Joe
Larrimore took the pigskin and
latter^lled to Curtis Hebert who
rolled down to the Eagle's 30.
The next play Leemon Mc
Henry passed to Jimmy Goff who
on the 5 yard stripe. The touch
down was tallied on the next play
by a pass from McHenry to
Jimy Berry. Try for extra
point was low.
J. Cauley took the ensuring
kick on his 20 and moved upfield
to the 40, but on the next play F.
O'Brien fumbled and Lucedale
recovered, and on a series of
passes by McHenry to Jimmy Goff
went over the double stripe once
more. Try for extra point was
short.
The Eagle’s tried the air and
Jimmy Goff of Lucedale inter
cepted B. Mansfield’s pass and
ran for another score. Place
ment was short.
The Eagles’ kicked off to start
the second half. Jimmy Berry
took the ball, but was smothered
on his own 15. Again the Pan
thers of Lucedale took to the air,
Jimmy Goff to Joe Larrimore to
another touchdown.
The lone OLV touchdown was

Tigers Lose
To Indians By
Score of 14-0
After taking a scalping from
the Indians of Biloxi, by a score
of 14 to 0 Friday night, the Mass.
Point Tigers of Coach Shields
will still be looking for a win
tonight against the Commodores
of Gulfport. This game should
be a hard fought contest from
start to finish with both teams
looking for their first win in Big
8 competition.
The Tigers outplayed and out
classed the Indians in practi
cally every department except
scoring in Friday night’s game,
but couldn’t seem to hold on to
the ball. The Tigers rolled up
14 first downs to 3 for Biloxi.
Moss Point won the toss and
chose to receive. Broussard
kicked to Pat Gardner, who took
the ball on his own 10 and mov
ed to the 25. After moving to
the 47 .Son Rhodes was forced
to kick. After the exchange of
punts Ellzey Burch tossed the
pigskin far into the end zone to
the waiting arms of Charles
Boone. Broussard’s placement
was good.
At the start of the second
quarter Norman Wells passed
from the Moss Point 42 to James
Clark on the Biloxi 40. One of
the Indians interfered with Clark
and interference with the receiv
er was ruled against Biloxi, mak
inging the play good. Pat Gard
ner picked up 5 yards, then J. T.
Shepherd on a beautifully exe
cuted run picked up 14 more
yards and a first down. Pat
Gardner and Son Rhodes carried
the leather to the one. On the
next play Pat Gardner tried
through the line, but fumbled
the ball, which was recovered by
John Radich of Biloxi. Burch
kicked out of danger to the 37.
Two plays later Norman Wells
fumbled and James Boudin cov
ered the ball. At the end of the
half the ball was in Moss Point’s
possession on the 35 of Biloxi.
CALL BACK TOUCHDOWN
Moss Point kicked off to start
the second half to the 20 of
Biloxi where Broussard took the
oval to the 40, but the Tiger lino
held and Burch was forced to
kick, kicking out of bounds, near
the goal line. J. T. Shepherd
kicked out of danger, but Duplain
took his kick on the Biloxi 40
and ran for a touchdown, which
was nullifield because of a clip-
ping penalty against Biloxi. As j
the quarter ended the ball rested
on the Moss Point 45 in the Red
skins hands. Four plays after
the start of the last period Burch
j heaved the oval once again from
j the 24 into the waiting hands of
I Roland Marion for another touch
down. Broussard split the up
rights once more, making the
score 14 to 0 in favor of Biloxi.
The end of the game found the
ball in the hands of the Indians
on their own 40 due to a pass in
terception by Marion.
The most outstanding player
for the Tigers was J. T. Shepherd
who played a whale of a game
on both offense as well as on de
fense. Others playing a great
game were Son Rhodes, Flim
Colville, C. E. Roper, Gene
Young, Pat Garnder and Nor
man Wells.

CALIFORNIA SUPREME COURT
VOIDS RACIAL MARRIAGE BAN
Lawyer Condemns
Legislature For
Expelling Members
The members of the Progressive
Party on hearing of the action of
the House of Representatives in
expelling two of its members after
secret hearing, James A. Struck
meyer, State Chairman of the Pro
gressive Party last week pointed
out that Adolf Hitler’s first act
when coming to power was the de
struction of labor organizations
and the dissolution of the Reich
stag. Struckmeyer charged that
the reactionary elements in the
legislature had “framed” the
charges against Kartus and Robles,
as Hitler framed charges against
the liberal elements in Germany.
Commenting on' published ac
counts of the proceedings, Struck
meyer further said, “Kartus and
Robles are charged basically with
‘obstructionism.’ It is true that
Kartus and Robles have ‘obstruct
ed’ the plans of the copper-power
cattle selfish interests. Tlie two
have ‘aroused passion ana preju
dices of groups which are unable
to talk for themselves.’ It is these
groups—minority groups—w h i c h
have made our country the great
power which it is tod^”
Struckmeyer said, *T know of
my own knowledge that this step
has been planned by the reaction
ary elements in the legislature for
over a year. The plan now has
been carried into effect, striking a
blow at the democratic processes
in our state. If the action of the
legislature is allowed to go un
challenged, there is no reason why
the Democrats, having a majority
of the legislators, could not expel
all Republican members of the leg
islature.”
The death struggle between re
action and liberalism has begun,
he said. Mr. Struckmeyer thinks
that every citizen in this state
should let his or her representa
tive immediately know of the
grave alarm with which liberals
view the star chamber proceedings
instituted and conducted by the
servants of reaction.
Garvey Reiterates
Stand on Civil Rights
Steve Langmade, Chairman of
the Ctate’s Central Committee of
the Democratic Party, in an inter
view with the Arizona Sun, quoted
Governor Garvey as saying, “I re
iterate my stand on civil rights as
I stated before September Pri
mary.”
Mmi m 1 .lit
:-y.
mA MIiMM
Mr. Garvey’s stand is as follows:
“All questions regarding civil
rights, fair employment and treat
ment of minority groups are most
readily simply answered by
reiteration of the statement that
I have made again and again and
again, and in .which I thoroughly
believe: “No citizen of this state,
while I am Governor, shall ever be
penalized or discriminated against
because of race, creed or color; un
der the Bill of Rights of the Amer
ican Constitution all are entitled
to equal opportunity and the same
fairness of treatment; capability
and competence, rather than race,
color or religion, shall comprise
the yardstick by which the indi
vidual is measured. More than to
any other factor, it is to our facili
ty to tolerate and assimilate, that
we are indebted for the greatness
of this great nation.”

various slogans to end “jim crow.”

thaniel Moton, it started when
a mixed group of Young Progres
sives of America were not allowed
to sit together at the theatre be
cause some among them were Ne
groes. The manager, when asked
by the party, if this is the polidy
of the theatre, said, it is. The
young people demanded their
money bayk and began a campaign
among their friends to boycott the
theatre until it quits discriminat
ing. The response was so great,
Moton said, that they decided to
broaden the campaign and demon
strate Tn front of the theatre.
Their objective is to pass a civil
rights law which would forbid dis
crimination by any public estab
lishment in Arizona, the spokes
man said. Any violation would be
punishable by fine or jail sentence.

izations throughout the city have
pledged their support to fight for

Negro Lawyer To
Practice Here
The second Negro in the history
of the State will establish law
practice in Phoenix soon.
Attorney Hayzell Daniels, a na
tive Texan moved to Tucson when
quite young. He received his early
education in public schools and the
University of Arizona where he
completed his law course also. It
is reported Mr. Daniels is the first
Negro in the State’s history to
pass the bar.
The other lawyer was Robert
Fortune who practiced law here
for several years until his death
about 10 years ago. During his
time bar examinations were not
required.
It is learned Mr. Daniels, on his
return from a short visit in the
East where he is visiting relatives
will establish his office in down
town Phoenix.
He is the husband of Mrs. Grace
Daniels, well known in the city
who has been teaching in the city
schools for the last 4 years.

The Mysterious Train
Accident Still Unsolved
A 66-YEAR-OLD WOMAN STILL IN CRITICAL CON
DITION IN COUNTY HOSPITAL
Maricopa County sheriff’s depu
ties and railroad officials still are
investigating the strange circum
stances under which a s passenger
fell from the speeding Sunset Lim
ited, 28 miles west of Buckeye, and
suffered the loss of her left foot,
cut off at the ankle.
Found at 7 a. m., last Saturday,
by Southern Pacific Railroad work
ers beside the tracks near Crag, a
section house five miles west of
Arlington, she was not identified
until Sunday, as Mrs. Hattie Cross,
66, a Negro, who had been enroute
from Alexandria, La., to her home
in Los Angeles.
In addition to the severed foot,
her right leg was broken, and her
condition was critical as she un
derwent surgery, at the Maricopa
County Hospital.
Railroad officials estimated that
eight hours elapsed from the time
she plunged into darkness beneath
the train wheels until medical
treatment began.
The Opinion Hailed As

Ariz. Voters League
Secure Permanent
Headquarters
The Arizona Voters League have
set up permanent office at 1149
East Jefferson Street.
This organization plgns to put
on an extensive program to sup
port candidates who will support
the following objectives:
1. To secure 'the passage of a
permanent F. E. P. C. bill in local
and state government.
2. To work for legislation to ad
mit all persons regardless of race
or coloij*, to restaurants, theaters,
hospitals, and other places of pub
lic accommodations.
3. To work for legislation de
signed to prohibit racial segrega
tion and discrimination wherever
state funds are used in education,
health and employment.
4. To secure employment and
job opportunities in all govern
mental departments in the state
of Arizona, without regard to race,
creed, color, or national origin.
5. To promote integration of
qualified citizens in all branches of
the National Guard without dis
crimination.
6. To work for repeal of all
present legislation detrimental to
organized labor.
7. To seek full benefit and par
ticipation in all phases of the G. I.
rehabilitation program.
Interested citizens wishing to
join, may stop by the office at 1149
East Jefferson Street.
School Trustee
Election To Be Held
On October 30
School trustee elections will be
held Oct. 30 in the elementary and
high school districts of Maricopa
County, according to John H. Bar
ry, county school superintendent.
trustee will be elected in the
elementary districts and the regu
lar high school districts for three
year terms beginning January 1,
while in the union high school dis-
the trustee is elected for a
five-year term, the superintendent
pointed out.
“A qualified voter for electing a
school trustee must be a registered
voter in Arizona at time of vot
ing.” Superintendent Barry said.
“He must have resided in the dis
trict for 30 days prior to the day of
election.
“He must be the parent or
guardian of a minor child residing
in the district or must have paid
a state or county tax during the
preceding year. Either spouse is
deemed a taxpayer on community
property.

The accident was reported to
the sheriff’s office at 7:30 a. m.
Saturday as “a dead body found.”
at 9 a.m. D. L. McGovney, head of
the sheriff’s identification bureau,
arrived at the scene and found the
injured woman living. He sum
moned a Buckeye ambulance and
it was 10:30 o’clock before she
could be admitted to the hospital.
Leo Sizemore, claims adjuster
for the Southern Pacific Railroad,
said that she must have fallen
from a coach on the passenger
train at about 2 a. m.
He said she apparently opened
the coach door and stepped out.
Fellow passengers were asleep
at the time of the accident ahd did
not miss her until later Saturday.
Mrs. Cross was reported missing
when the train reached Los Ange
les. She was identified at the hos
pital by a daughter, Mrs. Annie
Mae Lacefield, who is here at her
mother’s bedside.

Los Angeles, Oct. I.—ln a 4-3 de
cision, the State Supreme Court
today ruled the state law banning
marriages of whites and Negroes
and other so-called “non-Cauca
sians” was unconstitutional.
The 7-page opinion was hailed
here as a “magnificent decision”
by Atty. Dan Marshall, chairman
of the Catholic Inter-racial Coun
cil, who submitted the brief result
ing in the decision.
Marshall challenged the state
law on behalf of Andrea Peraz,
a white girl, and Sylvester Davis
Jr., Negro, whi were prevented
from marrying by the state ban.
The State Supreme Court has
been considering the case for a
year.
Marshall said the decision “sets
a tremendous precedent for the
entire nation and is a big step to
ward wiping out Hitler ideas of
race superiority.”
From the Southern California
Young Progressives came the fol
lowing:
“The Southern California Young
Progressives hail the decision of
the California Supreme Court hold
ing unconstitutional the law out
lawing mixed marriages as a maj
or victory in its campaign in con
junction with other progressive
organizations against the meas
ure,” said Letitia Innes, Southern
California regional director, Young
Progressives.
“At our founding convention this
month in Los Angeles we passed a
resolution against the measure and
resolved to work for its repeal,”
she said.
Justice Roger Traynor, in the
state court’s majority opinion, said
the “racial marriage” ban was “ar
bitrary and unreasonable discrimi
nation against certain racial
groups.”
Justice Jesse Carter, in a con
curring opinion, said the ban on
intermarriage was *the “product of
ignorance, prejudice and intoler
ance.”
Taylor Boys Get
Life In Prison
Life terms in the state prison at
Florence were imposed Wednesday
on Otis Taylor, 36 years old, and
his brother, Alfred, 22,- Phoenici
ans, convicted of first-degree mur
der in the knife and hammer slay
ing of Walter Pratt, 37, a South
Central avdhue service station at
tendant.
Superior Judge Walter Thalhei
mer’s sentence was a formality,
since the jury which found the
brothers guilty also decreed the
penalty. Pratt was slain in a dis
pute over change from a dollar
involved in a' gasoline purchase
by the brothers May 25.
Brockett Pleads
For Equal Rights
Tucson, Oct. 3. —Bruce Brockett,
Republican candidate for governor
called for an end to racial intoler
ance and class distinction, in a
speech to more than 500 members
of the Tucson Young Republican
Club her£ today.
Brockett said, “class hatred has
been pushed out before us at every
step of the road by the New Deal
politicians.”
Republicans appearing on the
platform with Brockett were: Al
bert R. Buehman, candidate for U.
S. Representative from District 2,
Marvin Clemens, candidate for
sheriff of Pima county, Edward
W. Scruggs, candidate for county
attorney. Brockett was introduced
by Mac Marshall, president of the
.club.

Red Cross Chapter
Motor Corps Is
Reorganized Here
Mrs. Arthur Keith, Motor Corps
Chairman, of the Putnam Chapter
American Red Cross, announced
at a recent meeting of the Board
of Directors that she has reor
ganized the corps and the fol
lowing people have volunteered
their services: Mrs. Edward Burt,
Mrs. Dorothy Dawley, Mrs. Mil
dred Dean, Mrs. Joel Douglas,
Mrs. R. Dinolt, Mrs. Ruth Gagne,
Mrs. Wida Gerardi, Mrs. Hazel
Laun, Mrs. Juliet Mandler, Mrs.
Ruth Nelson, Mrs. Estelle Phan
euf, Mrs. Louise Phillips, Miss
Alice Wilder Smith and Mrs. Ber
nice Delaney.
The reorganization has been
made necessary because of the
greater demand for motor corps
service during the past several
months. Transportation has been
provided to Newington for child
ren in addition to the services
which are the usual duties of the
Corps.
World Federalists
To Hear Facts
On Referendum
Arthur Hostage, acting execu
tive director for the United World
federalists of Connecticut, leading
American proponents of world
federation, will speak tonight at
3 o’clock in the First Congrega
tional Church at Storrs on “The
United Nations: Beginning or
End?”'
Mr. Hostage, a veteran of World
War II, with service in the Europ
ean theatre, studied economics at
Yale and was active in the Yale
chapter of UNF. He has served
as a member of the state execu
tive council, has been prominent 1
amang those conducting student
institutes and has spoken to nu- j
merous student groups, civil clubs 1
and public meeting throughout
southern New England and New
York.
United World Federalists, the
group he represents, is a non-pro
fit organization, with more than
100,000 signed adherents and 30,
000 active members in 420 chap-
ters throughout the nation. Its
president is Cord Meyer Jr. The
student division, directed by stu
dents, has 10,000 members in 220
high schools and college chapters.
The meeting is being sponsored
by the Mansfield chapter of Unit
ed World Federalists so that
voters may have the facts on the
referendum for strengthening the
United Nations before election.
The public is invited to attend this
timely meeting.

Catholic War Veterans To Elect
New Officers At Annual Meeting
Representatives Of i
4 Area Schools At
Classical Meeting
Four schools in the Putnam
area were represented at the an
nual meeting of the Connecticut
section of the Classical Associa
tion of New England held Satur
day at Fairfield University in
Fairfield.
The following were represent
ed:
Putnam High School: Francis
Curran, principal and head of the
Latin Department.
Putnam Catholic Academy: Sis
ter Louise Sophie.
Annhurst College, South Wood
stock: Sister Marguerite Felieie.
Marianapolis Academy, Thomp
son: Rev. John C. Petrauskas,
M.I.C., registrar.
Miss Barbara Rogers, a recent
Annhurst graduate, now a teacher
in Wallingford, and Arthur P.
Bove, editor of The Observer,
were among other members of the
association present for the annual
meeting.
The Connecticut group accepted
the invitation of Marianapolis
Academy to hold the next annual
meeting at Thompson in October,
1949.
Rev. John C. Petrauskas,
of Marianapolis was
named a member of the exe
cutive committee of the Con
necticut Section.
Four papers on classical sub
jects were presented at the meet
ing.
Rev. James H. Dolan, rector of
Fairfield University welcomed the
group comprising teachers and in
structors from high and classical
schools. Professor Leroy C. Bar
rett of Trinity College read a pa
per “Latin Becomes France” and
“Pre-Christian Virgil” was the
subject of a paper read by Rev.
Francis A. Sullivan, S.J., of St.
Andrews-on-the-Hudson, Pough
keepsie, N.Y.
Rev. William J. Power, S.J., of
Fairfield University, led a discus
sion on teachers’ problems and
Miss Anita Flannagan of West
Haven High School gave a report
on the association-sponsored state
Latin contest.
Speakers at the afternoon ses
sion included Very Rev. William
E. Fitzgerald, S.J., rector of Chev
erus School, Portland, Me., and
Professor C. Bradford Welles, of
Yale University, brother of Miss
Esther E. Welles, Putnam public
health nurse.
Miss Frances E. Najako of Mid
dletown High School, and Miss
Edith Plumb of Bulkeley High
School, Hartford, were elected i
president and secretary respec-
tively for 1948-49.
VA To Discontinue J
Itinerant Service j
The Veterans Administration
announced today that itinerant
contact service that had been fur
nished to Danielson and Putnam
would be discontinued effective
November 1.
Service to these communities
had been established in order to
rendet assistance to veterans and
their dependents. Contact service
[0 both locations was formerly
rendered on a one day each week
schedule. Because of the small
number of veterans calling upon
the VA representative, the service
was reduced to one half day each
week to each point, and later fur
ther curtailed to one half day
every other week to each com
munity.
The number of contacts on the
latter schedule has now reached
such a low level that continuance
of the service seems inadvisable.
The Veterans ’Administration
maintains a full time office in the
Post Office Building, Norwich, in
charge of A. G. Dahinden, and j
every Thursday has a contact re
presentative at the Veterans Ad
visory Center in Willimantic.
■Short Line Bus
Fares Undecided
The State Public Utilities Com
mission Wednesday reserved de
cision on a petition of the Short
Line, Inc., of Providence, R.I., to
increase its bus fares in Connecti
cut.
The company wants to' raise
fares about 15 per cent. It oper
ates routes from Hartford to New
London, Hartford to Norwich and
from the Massachusetts-Connecti
cut line to Putnam.

Talk At PHS

Putnam—
Mrs. Nellie Decorow of Bel
fast, Me., is visiting her sister-in
law, Mrs. Annie May Ripley of
Grove Street
Terence McGarry, present com
mander of the St. Mary’s Post No.
1213, Catholic War Veterans, an
nounced today that a nominating
committee has been appointed to
select a slate of post officers for
the coming year. The committee
will present its list of nominees
at the next regular meeting on
Monday, November 1.
The first meeting of the post
year, which starts in October, was
held Sunday morning in St.
Mary's Hall. Meetings had been
suspended for the summer
months. A program for the com
ing year was discussed.
St. Mary’s Post of Putnam is
one of the largest and most active
posts in the state. It was charter
ed in February, 1947 with 40 char
ter members. It has grown since
then to an organization of over
100 members.
Officers for the coming year will
be elected on November 1, and
the installation ceremonies will
be held at a special dinner meet
ing at a later date.
City Jews Mark
Sukkot, Season
Of Thanksgiving
Sukkot, holiday season of
thanksgiving, opened Sunday
night with brief services in the
local synagogue.
It has both a seasonal and his
torical significance. In ancient
Israel, it was an agricultural festi
val, when the Jews traveled to
Jerusalem with offerings and ex
pressed their gratitude for the
bounties of nature.
“Sukkot” is the name for the
booths, or tents, in which the
Israelites lived during their wan
derings in the desert after the
Egyptian bondage.
Decorations in the synagogues
during Sukkot Week include
many fruits and flowers. Chief
symbols of Sukkot are the palm
branch, myrtle, willow and citron. I
Booths of elaborate design are of- I
ten erected for Jewish worship in
this period.
In spirit, there is a close -re
semblance between this harvest
festival and the American thanks
giving.
Program For
Protection Of
Pedestrians
The fourth annual “Connecti
cut Pedestrian Protection Project”
opened Friday and will continue
through November 30. I
The State Highway Safety
Commission sponsors the project i
which is designed to reduce pe-
destrian traffic accidents.
Endorsing the program, Mayor
William Perry Barber this week
issued the following proclama
tion:
"In cooperation with the Con
necticut Highway Safety Commis
sion, I pledge support of all offi
cial agencies in promoting the
1948 Pedestrian Protection Pro
gram, which will continue
throughout the state to November
30.
“This special program is spon
sored by the Connecticut Chiefs
of Police Association, the Connec-
ticut Association of Street and
Highway Officials, the Connecti-1
cut Association of Public School
Superintendents and the State De
partments of Education, High-1
ways. Motor Vehicles, Police and j
the Highway Safety Commission, j
“We are grateful that our com
munity is a safe place for you and
me and other citizens to proceed
about their business.
“To date no fatal pedestrian ac
cidents have been charged against I
us this year. I, therefore, urge
each and everyone to use the ut
most caution, whether walking
or driving, that our record may
be unblemished.”

Congressman Horace Seely
Brown, Jr., has announced that j
the Coast Guard will hold com
petitive examinations February
23-24, 1949 for entrance to the
Coast Guard Academy.
High School graduates of sound
moral character, with good stand
ing in the community, in excel
lent physical condition and inter
ested in a career as Coast Guard
officer, should write without delay
to the Commandant, United States
Coast Guard, Washington 25, D C.
Congressman Seely-Brown is a
member of the Subcommittee on
Coast Guard Activities of the
House Committee on Merchant
Marine and Fisheries.

Etta Beals Dies
Funeral Friday
Miss Etta May Beals, 82-year-old
Bluffton resident died Wednesday
morning at 2:30 o’clock at the Cgn
muity hospital here where she l^d
been a patient for the past year.
The body is at the Basinger fufc
eral home where funeral services v^l
be held Friday afternoon at 2 o'clock.
Officiating will be Rev. E. J. Pen
horwood of Lima South Side Church
of Christ, former pastor of the
Bluffton Church of Christ of which
she was a member. Interment will
be in Maple Grove cemetery.
She was bom in Antwerp, Ohio,
July 10, 1866, the daughter of Mar
ion and Samantha (Reed)
Most of her life was spent in
ton. She was a sister of
Beals who died last March.
BLUFFTON MAY GET
BUILDING CODE FOR
RESIDENTIAL AREA
Council Studies Advantages of
Setting Up Regulations on
Residential Building
Town Ordinances Now Provide
Only Controls of Construc
tion
If the ordinance is drafted it will
be the first move by the town to
regulate building activity in residen
tial sections of the town, altho the
business district has since 1927 been
subject to a building code.
A study of building ordinances of
other cities is being made by council
men, to determine what regulations
would be applicable to Bluffton, with
a final decision on the matter ex
pected this month.
Controls in Business District
Bluffton’s only present controls
over building activity apply to the
business district, established in a fire
zone ordinance first passed in 1927,
and re-drafted in 1931.
In the ordinance, the limits are
established in a district bordered by
College avenue on the south Big
Riley creek on the east Washington
street on the north and Elm street
on the west.
Outside of this district there have
been no restrictions of any kind on
building activities.

Announce Evening
School Of Religion
A training school for Sunday
school teachers and others interested
will be sponsored this fall by the
Bluffton Ministerial association, it
was announced the first of the week.
Classes will be held at the high
school building from 8 to 10 o’clock
for five consecutive Thursday even
ings, beginning October 14.
The administrative board consists
of Rev. Paul Cramer, dean Prof.
Paul Shelly, Dan Alspach, Nelson
Steiner, Carl Derringer and Carl
Smucker. Faculty and classes will
be announced later.

High came roaring back to win their
first football game since 1945 whan

Saturday.
The first quarter looked like any
thing but a Harlem victory aa the
Sunburst squad scored at will and
the Cats spent most of their time
fumbling the bull, but during the
latter part of the second quarter tho

circled right end to score on a 25
ynid spring to pay dirt.,

of thrills as the Wildcat* became Irig

scoring from there on. 11l tho third

from the Harloirf 25 yard line. Zinn
came crashing through to bat tbs*

grabbed the ball out of thin air nn’l
sriampered 55 yards to the goal line.
Bigby ’h drop kick was good for two
points, Mid that later proved to be
tlie winning margin for the game.
In the final quarter Harlem was held
for downs on the Sunburst 5 yard
line. Bigby took their pant on tVs
mid-field stripe and out ran the
field for the final scoring of He
game.

Bank of Commerce
Reports 1947 Earnings
Of $36.33 a Share
By Nelson M. Shepard
Net earning! of the Bank of Com
merce and Savings for 1947 were
equivalent to $36.33 per share, a
return slightly in excess of the pre
vious year and
“best In the his
tory of the
bank,” Thomas
J. Groom, presi
dent, disclosed
today in hia re
port at the an
nual stockhold
ers' meeting.
Looking con
fidently toward
the end of the
current year, he
assured stock
holders that the
1 9 4 8 earnings
would "compare
T. J. Groom.
favorably" and possibly exceed last
year's record by a slight margin.
The steadily improved condition
and growth of the Bank of Com
merce and Savings in recent years
resulted in Initial steps being taken
to provide for a third branch, be
sides new and larger quarters for
the two existing branches. A site
has been acquired for a branch at
Fourth street and Rhode Island
avenue N.E., Mr. Groom reported,
but because of the scarcity and un
certainty of building materials, the
directors have not deemed it ad
visable to begin new construction.
New Branch Planned.
Meanwhile. Mr. Groom said, efforts
are being made to find a suitable
temporary location for the proposed
Northeast branch.
The two existing branches of the
bank continue to render ‘useful
and convenient facilities” in the
communities in which located, the
report said. Construction of new
quarters for the Brightwood office
began on June 21 and the work is
scheduled for completion about
January 1.
A site also has been acquired for
the H street branch at North Capi
tol and H streets N.W. Construc
tion of the new building is much
needed, the president said, and it
is expected work will get underway
aoon.
Mr. Groom returned for the meet
ing this morning from Detroit where
he had attended the American
Bankers Association Convention.
Pledge* Fight on Inflation.
Pledging co-operation in measures
to check inflation, he told the stock
holders: ‘‘Probably the greatest prob
lem confronting the country today
Is the steadily rising spiral of prices.
The welfare of all our citizens is
threatened. To check inflation and
prevent it from running its inevit
able course calls for the fullest co
operation of every segment of our
economy.”
The following directors were re
elected: George B. Burrus. William
L. Bush, Theodore Cogswell, James
E. Colliflower, A. E. Giegengack,
Thomas J. Groom, Harry J. Kane,
,1r.; Frank Owdngs, George M. Quirk,
Donald F. Roberts, Leo A. Rover
and J. Sanders. The officers for the
ensuing year will be elected at a
meeting of directors October S.
Beginning this week, the Wash
fngton Stock Exchange announced
today it will open on Saturdays from
11 am. to 12 noon for sales or ex
tended trading only. The Saturday
session conforms to the new schedule
of the New York Stock Exchange.

Reserve Board.

ended September 25 averaged 5,543,
100 barrels daily vs. 5.494,350 in the,
previous week and 5,196,000 in the
like 1947 week—American Petroleum
Institute.

cars, a drop of .2% from the previous
week and 3.2% below the like 1947
week—Association of American Rail
roads.

Stock Market Turns
Slightly Lower as
Early Demand Fades
By th« Associated Press
NEW YORK, Sept. 30.—Early de
mand faded away in the stock mar
ket today and fractional gains in
numerous cases were converted into
small losses.
A fair amount of business was
done, but professional traders again
accounted for most of the activity.
Public interest in stocks remained
at low' ebb.
Wall Street continued to take its
trading cue from the international
news. Most reports from the domes
tic business front were encouraging,
and prospects for the immediate
future at least are regarded favor
ably, but these influences failed to
stir up much enthusiasm.
Marked down fractions to around
a point were United States Steel.
Republic Steel, Chrysler, American
Telephone, Cerro de Pasco, Ameri
can Smelting, Westinghouse Elec
tric, American Woolen, Nickel Plate
common. Santa Fe, Texas Co. and
International Paper.
Still ahead were Goodyear, Fire
stone. Deere, United Aircraft. Elec
tric Power Light, Kennecott Cop
per. Allied Chemical. Texas Gulf
Sulphur, Nickel Plate preferred,
Union Pacific and Pacific West
ern Oil.
Revere Copper and Brass moved
up about a point at one time fol
lowing news of a boosted dividend.
Announcement of an extra divi
dent helped Burlington Mills.

Chicago Grain
CHICAGO. Sept. 30 UP.—Grain pricas
herd about even with yesterday's close
during the early part of today s Board of
Trade session.
Department of Agriculture indications
of support prices, as well as corn alloca
tions. made over night, had been largely
discounted in previous trading.
Business in all pits was light and in
most cases prices were lower by minor
fractions
WheaT traders said flour business con
tinues to be disappointing and Government
purchase for export is having little effect
on the market.
Prospects of heavy Government export
of corn failed to stimulate that grain.
Estimated corn receipts for today were
placed at 103 cars.
During the first hour wheat was un
changed to lower; December. '2.24%;
corn was *«-V« lower; December. J .383«,
•nd oats were unchanged to U lower: De
cember. 738a. Soybeans were unchanged
to 1J4 lower, November. 2.4o34.
The Department of Agriculture has in
dicated price support for corn at 1.44 a
bushel on a national basis. The Govern
ment is required to support lower prices
of grain at not less than 00 per cent of
parity as of October l Figures released
after the close ot yesterday's trading
shows the parity price of corn at J .00
on larms. about in line with trade expec
tations.
Announcement of the entire support
program is expected after trading today
or tomorrow. Government officials expect
upwards of 500.000,000 bushels of this
year's com crop to be stored under the
price piogram.
Another overnight development was the
rnnouncement of export corn allocations
for the fourth quarter. The department
placed the figure *at 19,000.000 bushels,
about what the trade had expected.
Allocations for export are expected to
be stepped up sharply after the first of
the year. Unconfirmed reports say the
Government may allot upwards of 150.
ooo.ooo bushels of this year’s corn crop
ro shortage areas abroad.
The Board of Trade said that notice of
intentions to deliver on September wheat
and corn contracts today covered the out
j-taning obligations. 7,000 bushels of wheat
and 5*9.000 bushels of corn.
Cloudy weather with some rain was in
prospect for the corn belt today.

NEW YORK Sept. 30 UPV—Blow trad

today. Deferred positions were under
pressure of commission house liQUldatlon.
The selling followed news of a lower
mid-September cotton parity as compared
with a month ago.

be attracted to the market on Wednes
days Washington's announcement that
there will be no production controls on
the 1949 cotton crop.
Short covering was a steadying influenee

higher than the previous close. October

ington Stock Exchange in the next

common and the two preferred, Na
tional Savings Trust. Washington
