mainly to the yield of better than
6 per cent.
Bank Assets Off in Week.
Combined assets of Washington
banks totaled $977,222,000 on Octo
ber 20, a drop of $20,062,000 from
October 13, the Richmond Reserve
Bank reports. Total investments
revealed practically the same de
crease during the week, the report
showed, a reduction in amount of
Treasury' bills held being the main
reason.
Commercial loans Were down about
$97,000 during the w’eek while real
estate loans showed a gain of $125,
000. Total loans were up more than
$400,000. Demand deposits of $707,
560.000 were down $22,411,000 for the
week. Savings gained slightly.
Beverage Control Held Vital.
“Control of beverage business Is
Often the critical factor in decid
ing ultimate profit or loss to the
average hotel,” Arnold Jorgensen,
CPA, told the Hotel Accountants'
Association of Washington las',
night at the Statler. Mr. Jorgensen
has for 30 years headed the local
office of Horwath Horwath, hotel
accountants.
The first conference of Washing
ton Chapter, Association of Amer
ican Executives, will be held to
morrow at the Statler, starting with
a luncheon. Among the speakers
will be Leonard W. Trester. presi
dent. American Public Relations
Association: Dean J. Freeman Pyle,
University of Maryland: Arthur E.
Bums, George Washington Univer
sity, and W.‘ H. Stauffer. Virginia
Chamber of Commerce. Officers of
Washington Chapter will be elected.
D. C. Insurance Tops Nation.
Washington families carry an av
erage of $7,400 in life insurance,
highest in the Nation, according to
the Institute of Life Insurance. The
national average is $4,700. New
York and Delaware have the sec
ond highest average. $7,100.
Robert L. Cudd, second vice pres
ident of the Chase National Bank,
will address a dinner meeting of the
fiduciaries section of the District
Bankers’ Association next Wednes
day at the Mayflower, with Chair
man Bruce Frantz presiding. He
will speak on taxes and estate plan
ning.
M. Szymczak. Reserve Board
member, says further credit controls
are being studies by the board. The
international situation may force
higher taxes, he said in a Buffalo
address.
Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner
Beane is distributing a broad survey
of the air transportation industry.
Business Briefs
Bank Clearings in 25 leading cities
declined ’to *14,373,776,000 in the
week ended October 27 vs. #15,841,
919.000 in the preceding week and
$13,427,618,000 in the like 1947 week.
—Dun Bradstreet.
Commercial, Industrial and Farm
Loans of member banks in leading
cities declined to *15,374,000,000 on
October 20, a drop of $84,000,000
from a week earlier, but remained
$1,712,000,000 above a year earlier—
Federal Reserve Board.
Crude Oil Production set a new
record for the third week in a row
in the week ended October 23. aver
aging 5,595,950 barrels daily vs.
5.586.350 in the previous week and
6.295.350 in the like 1947 week—
American Petroleum Institute.
Engineering Constructon awarded
this week totaled $179,052,000 vs.
#129.008.000 in the previous week
and $82,237,000 in the like 1947 week
—Engineering News-Record.
Transcontinental Gas Pipeline
Corp. registered with the SEC 265,
000 common shares of cumulative
preferred stock and 265.00 common
shares for public offering. An addi
tional 2.250 000 common shares also
were listed for distribution to pre
sent holders at the rate of three
new shares for each one held.
Carnegie-Ulinois Steel Corp. and1
National Tube Co., subsidiaries of
United States Steel, announced!
price increases on steel products
combined with zinc.
International Silver Co. declared
a dividend of $1.50 and a special of
$1 a common share, both payable
December 1.
Freeport Sulphur Co. declared a
quarterly dividend of 75 cents, pay
able December 1. Previous pay
ment swere 62'2 cents.
Cessna Aircraft Co. declared a di
vidend of 25 cents, payable Decem
ber 8. The previous and only pay
ment for 1947 was 20 cents last De
cember 5.

Stock Market Turns
Lower Alter Early
Attempt to Rally
ly th« Associated Press
NEW YORK, Oct. 28.—The stock
market turned on its heel after an
early try at higher prices today.
Morning gains were wiped out in
many cases or changed into.small
losses. The market tended to level
off around noon when selling pres
sure became less insistent.
Activity Increased with the step
up in selling.
The setbatflc coincided with Pre
mier Joseph Stalins declaration
that the Western Powers are.sup
porting a policy which can lead to
the “unleashing of a new war."
Brokerage quarters suggested that
the Stalin statement may have had
a minor affect on the market, but
that profit-taking operations by
professional traders had a greater
influence on the price trend.
National Steel lost 2 points and
smaller losses were posted for
United States Steel, Bethlenem
Steel, General Motors, Chrysler,
Goodrich, International Harvester,
Douglas Aircraft, North American
Co., Kennecott Copper, Westing
house Electric, Allied Chemical,
Santa Fe, Decca, Pennsylvania,
Texas Co., Gulf Oil and Loew's.
Clinging to small gains were
Packard, United States Rubber,
Montgomery Ward, Sears Roebuck,
Cerro de Pnsco, J. C. Penney,
Owens-Illinois, Great Northern pre
ferred. Chesapeake <fc Ohio, Union
Pacific and Twentieth Century-Fox.

Chicago Grain
CHICAGO. Oct 2K <P>.—Corn recorded
small losses on the Bosrd of Trade to
day in fairlv active dealings. Other
grain* did not move far in either direc
tion. but the volume of trading was fairly
large
Some commission house selling came
Into corn following yesterday’s upturn.
The trade was still waiting to see if
the Commodity Credit Corp. obtained any
of this grain in yesterday’s market
Figures on *uch purchases were expected
to be made public some time today.
Wheat near the end of the flrs; hour
was cent lower to higher: December.
82.285*: corn was U-3« lower: December,
781 ftfa. and oats were unchanged to
lower: December. 78s*. Soybeans were
lVs to 2 cents lower: November. *2.53‘«
Corn receipts here were estimated at
542 cars, a fact which had some influence
in causing early selling Some parts of
the Midwest have reported boxcar short
ages were holding down the movement of
corn to terminals.
The corn-hog ratio at Chicago this
week was placed at 17.0 compared with
15 0 a week ago and 11.5 a year ago.
Such a ratio was extremely favorable for
feeding corn to. hogs, which ordinarily
might result in holding hogs back for
tha purpose. However, hog prices de
cline again today.
New-crop wheat deliveries showed some
easiness at the start. Light showers fell
In Kansas and the Texas Panhandle over
night More were forecast for Western
Kansas. Western Oklahoma and Western
Texas for today or tonigh:
These were expected to be light, scat
tered showers and not the kind of steady
rain which this winter <#t territory
needs. However, the moisture forecast
was enough to discourage any early ag
greasive buying of 1848 wheat crop de
liveries
There was a tendency upon the part
of professional traders to go slow In De
cember wh#pt. which has advanced above
the Government loan level. With cash
wheat also above or around the loan
point, many traders wanted to wait and
see If producers would sell their train.

VOTE FOR FRANK A. METCALF
Dear Friends and Others:
You have already learned my aims in seeking the
office of Highway Engineer so I shall merely highlight
them for your now. My opponent who ran against me
as a Democrat in the Primaries and now seeks this
office as a Republican has stressed a second Highway
to the states. I agree; that would be a highly com
mendable undertaking, but definitely not with Terri
torial Funds nor is that within the province of a Terri
torial Highway Engineer. We need roads within our
Territory; Short roads leading to existing highways
and long roSds reaching out to new regions to accom
modate the homesteader, the miner and the pros
pector. Our fishing fleets, the backbone of our eco
nomy, need more and better facilities. Air travel in
the Territory must be made safer by more and
larger landing fields. These things I have pledged my
self to construct, allocating the Territorial funds at
my disposal as equally as is humanly possible.
My opponent would abolish the Highway Patrol.
That, my friends, must not come about. The trav
elers on our highways of which there were last sum
mer several thousand, must be protected from drunk
en drivers. They must have the assistance furnished
them by a courteous force of Patrolmen.
Do you know that every Highway Patrol carries a
full first-aid equipment for men and cars? They carry
in addition a large first aid kit rations for one week,
extra gas and oil, a tow chain, a shovel, an ax and
extra jack and other things that might be needed to
assist an unfortunate motorist.
Many a letter has come into the office commenting
on the efficiency and welcome assistance of the High
way Patrol Force. This force I shall maintain, if you
elect me, and, as the need arises, I shall increase it.
As in the States our highways here must be protected.
As to the qualifications for this office I am seeking,
I am a graduate Engineer with a Master’s degree, and
I am a registerd engineer in the Territory of Alaska.
I have followed this profession exclusively with no
branchings out into other fields. I was for years a
Locating Engineer for the Milwaukee Railroad. 1
have always been a doer and not a dreamer. 1 will give
you an honest, capable administration with a fair al
location of the Territorial funds at my disposal. Since
coming to Alaska in 1913, with time out for service
with the U. S. Engineers in World War 1, I have sur
veyed homesteads and mining claims. I have laid out
townsites and designed bridges. I have dredged har
bors and laid out airfields.
You will not do wrong to elect me to the office of
Territorial Highway Engineer.
Sincerely yours,
FRANK A. METCALF.

Minnesota Backers
Cautious but Expect
Dewey to Win State
By Gould Lincoln
Star Staff Correspondent
ST. PAUL, Minn., Sept. 25.—
Republican organization leaders
in Minnesota wear long faces
when they talk about the presi
dential and senatorial races—
overconfidence is not their be
setting sin. Privately and when
pressed, they admit they believe
that both Dewey-Warren ticket
and Senator “Joe” Ball will carry
the state.
As for the Republican State ticket,
headed by popular Gov. Luther M.
Youngdahl, they feel certain it will
go across by a big margin over the
Democratic-Farmer Labor ticket.
Democratic-Farmer Labor Party
leaders, for their part, insist they
have a winner in their Senatorial
candidate, the dynamic young
Mayor of Minneapolis. Hubert Hum
phrey—and they hope to win the
State’s electoral votes for President
Truman. This is, however, just a
hope.
Once Strong for Wallace.
A few months ago Minnesota!
looked like a happy hunting ground
for Henry A. Wallace and his new
Progressive Party. Mr. Wallace had
tha support of the Benson left wing
of the old Farmer-Labor Party.
The former Senator and Governor,
Elmer Benson, was promising to
take control of the Democratic
Farmer Labor Party away from the
Trumanites and place it in the
hands of Mr. Wallace. He failed j
miserably. Mr. Wallace has been
going down hill here ever since,
.until now the political wiseacres are
giving him no more than 35,000 votes
in the presidential race. Whatever
he gets, whether it be the 35,000
or trie 100,000 his more optimistic
supporters suggest, will be taken
away from Mr. Truman.
The probabilities are that Gov.;
Dew'ey will carry the State. There j
remains, however, a lot of hard
work to be done. The farmers, who
usually vote Republican, must be
prevailed upon to turn out in large
numbers. They must be convinced
that Gov. Dewey and a Republican
administration will be friendly to
agriculture. A good deal of propa
ganda to the effect that Gov. Dewey
was unfriendly to the rural elec
trification administration and to
governmental price supports for
farm products was spread in 1944—
and its effects have not entirely
worn off. Further, just as in 1944 I
when the voters were urged not to
change horses in the midst of a j
war, the Democrats and Farmer- j
Laborites (united in one party) are
saying it’s no time to change horses
when the farmers are getting the
highest prices on record for their
products. It may be an effective
argument. It's also an argument
the Republicans must combat
throughout this western territory, j
Lost to Roosevelt by 62.000.
Four years ago, Gov. Dewey lost I
to the late President Roosevelt by1
62,000 votes. This was not a large
margin In a total vote of more than
a million. It looks like a margin
that could easily be overcome
against Mr. Truman, particularly
with a third candidate in the race
Who will cut into the Truman vote.
The Wallace Progressives have
atill to get their presidential elec
tors on the ballot. They are cir
culating the necessary petitions, and
since they require only 2.000 sig
natures, there seems no reason to
believe that, Mr. Wallace's name
Will be left off.
The Republican organization Is
strongly united back of Gov. Dewey
and Senator Ball while the Demo
crats and Farmer Laborites are
divided, with a minority favoring;
Mr. Wallace. Four years ago, Sena-1
tor Ball opposed the election of
Gov. Dewey and supported Mr.
Roosevelt. This both enraged the!
leaders of the Republican organiza-!
tion and cut into Republican and
Independent vote support of Gov.
Dewey. Today, however, this is being
forgiven in the interest of party
unity and the need of holding on
to a Senate seat to maintain a
Republican majority in the upper
house in the next Congress.
Favored Stassen.
Minnesota Republicans were
greatly disappointed when the Re
publican National Convention turned
its back on former Gov. Harold E.
Stassen and nominated Gov. Dewey.
Some of them are still inclined to
belittle Gov. Dewey's strength in
this great farm area. But the man
ner in w'hich Mr. Stassen himself
has leaped into the campaign in
support of the Dewey-Warren ticket
has helped to allay the very definite j
feeling that the Stassen enthusiasts;
had against the New York Governor.!
The recent primary vote in the.
State gave the Republicans a lift!
In spirit. Before the primary, polls
taken by the Minneapolis Tribune
and by others indicated the Repub
lican National ticket and Senator
Ball might not fare well. In the
primary, however, the Republicans
did so well—as compared to the
number of votes cast for the Demo- i
crat Farmer Labor candidates that
a somewhat defeatist attitude on
the part of the Republicans has dis
appeared. For example, the Re- j
publicans cast 371.000 votes in the1
Gubernatorial primary and the
Democrat-Farmer Labor Party, only
161,000—which is a lead for the Re
publicans of more than 200,000.
Primaries Not Certain Barometer.
Primary votes, when the percent-:
age of voters who take the trouble i
to go to the polls is approximately
60 per cent of the total vote ex
pected in the general election, are
by no means conclusive evidence of
what may be expected in November.
F’urther, Democratic-Farmer Labor

didates had not been named, and the

would win and 22 per cent that the!
Democrats would win. Undecided1
were 17 per cent, and 1 per cent
thought the Wallace third party
would win. While the new poll
shows a slight beUerment for Mr.
Truman, it is so slight as not to be
material.
Dewey Plans Speeches.
The Republican high command is
by no means overlooking Minnesota.
Gov. Dewey himself is slated to;
come to St. Paul October 15 to de
liver a major speech. Mr. Stassen
will deliver a series of speeches in
the State, and Gov. Warren of
California, Gov. Dewey's running
mate, will make several speeches
here, too.
Gov. Warren, a Scandinavian by!
descent, is regarded as a logical
campaigner for this State, which
has a large Scandinavian population.
One of his speeches will be deliv
ered in Hibbing in the iron ore
country, where the opposition has
considerable strength and where
William A. Berlin, a young Hibbing
war veteran, is seeking to take from
Representative Blatnik the only
I House seat held by the Democratic
Farmer Labor Party,
j While the Republicans must fight
hard to retain the 3rd and 4th
districts, located respectively in
Minneapolis and St. Paul, they are
j expected to hold onto what they
have.

He had no fault to find with this
spending, except to charge that some
of the foreign aid money spent in
the first 2 4 years after V-J Day
was "wasted behind the Iron Cur
tain."
Supports ECA Program.
He drew a distinction between
that spending and the current bi
partisan European recovery pro
gram, which he called a "sound in
vestment in peace."
His third cause for present high
prices, however, he laid at the ad
ministration's door. It follows:
"The third cause of high prices
and inflation is the fact that both
before and since the war, our Na
tional administration has for years
been openly, avowedly, deliberately
discouraging production and trying
to raise prices. Those policies have
reflected the administration's basic
lack of understanding of the simple
fundament^* of our economic sys
tem and itJwagic lack of faith in
the future of America. The un
happy results of those policies are
continuing even to this very day.
They are the sole responsibility of
the present National administration
and it cannot pass the blame to any
one else."
Proposes Six Steps.
Gov. Dewey then proposed six
steps he wouid take to curb infla
tion Boiled down, they follow:
1. Bring to Washington a new ad
ministration.
2. Put "a brake on unnecessary
Government spending.”
3. Systematic payments on the
public debt.
4. A promise that "in your next
National administration there will

didates of minor political groups

which candidates for President and
Vice President or presidential elec
tors other than those proposed by
major parties can be placed on the

Warren Urges Unity
To Inspire Free Lands
By th# Aisociat»d Pr#*$
BUFFALO, N. Y„ Sept. 25.—Gov.
Earl Warren tonight appealed for
American unity in the election cam
paign as an inspiration to the free
nations behind the “Iron Curtain.”
It must be proof to “cynical eyes”
that the American people can rise
above hatred and discriminiation, he
declared.
It was both an appeal for moder
ation and a warning that the out
come of the election is bound to
give “aid and comfort” to one or the
other of the world's two great groups
—those who believe in democracy,
or the totalitarian nations.
“I believe there is no Iron Curtain
so heavy or so impervious to light
that it can shut out the inspiration
that comes from good example,” he
said.
“I believe that knowledge of
everything that is fine and good in
this country will some way or other
filter through the Iron Curtain as
certainly as any bad examples pass
through it.”
For this reason, he said, “I hope
that this National campaign can be
kept on a high plane of American
ism—without hatred, without bit
terness, and without appeal to the
prejudices of any class, race, group
or section of our country.”

Alabama May Stand
Alone in South on
Truman Ballot Ban
ly th» Atiociatxi Prtu
MONTGOMERY, Ala., Sept. 25
Alabama, which has supported every
Democratic candidate since 1872.
may be the only State this year
where the voters can’t mark their
ballots for President Truman.
The question hasn’t been settled
yet in Louisiana and Georgia, but
every other Southern State has a
place on the November ticket for Mr.
Truman or electors pledged to him.
Even in Mississippi, where the
States’ Rights Democratic Party was
bom out of resentment toward the
President’s racial policies, the voters
can vote for him if they like. The
regular Democratic Party candidates
are committed to the Thurmond
Wright slate, but a separate slate
of Truman electors will be on the
election list too.
The President's name was dropped
from the ballot in Louisiana, also,
and Governors J. Strom Thurmond
of South Carolina and Fielding
Wright of Mississippi were desig
nated the official candidates for
President and Vice President.
Gov. Earl K. Long called the
Legislature into special session,
however, to get Mr. Truman back in
the running. A bill passed by the
Louisiana House and now pending in
the Senate would let the President's
supporters put him on the ballot as
long as they do not call him a
Democrat.
Georgia's Gov. M. E. Thompson
also today called a special session
to straighten out the election tangle
in that State. The legislators will
meet Monday. The State Democratic
Committee is scheduled to convene
later to name the Democratic elec
tors. Whether they will be com
mitted to any candidate remains
to be seen.
Both Truman Democrats and the
States’ Righters are expected to be
on the ballot in Tennessee, but
neither has been certified officially.
Three of the 12 regular party elec
tors have promised to support the
Thurmond-Wrlght ticket and the
State Democratic Committee has
taken steps to replace them but
nothing final has developed.
Elsewhere in the South, the issue
is more clear-cut.
The States' Rights Democrats
have been ruled out in Oklahoma
in favor of Truman backers. The
Attorney General’s office, which de
cided the question, has suggested
the Thurmond-Wright followers
take the matter into court, but
nothing like that has happened yet.
Pro-Truman and anti-Truman
Democrats will share the ballot in
seven States—Mississippi, Arkansas,
North Carolina, Florida, Kentucky,
Virginia and Texas.
South Carolina has no State bal
lot, each political party distributing
its own. The regular Democratic
ticket is supporting Gov. Thurmond
and Wright, but a pro-Truman fac
tion says it will pass out ballots, too.
Name Banned on Bauoi.
Alabama's Democratic electors
said even before Mr. Truman was
nominated that they wouldn't vote
for him or any candidate who ran
on the explosive civil rights plat
form. The President's n^me won't
be on the ballot—only those of the
11 electors.
Truman followers, led by State
Senator Joseph N. Langan of Mo
bile, first tried to get a separate
slate of electors on the ballot, but
changed their mind.
Instead, Senator Langan has said
he will seek a Federal court order
to make the regular 11 Democratic
electors support the national party
nominee. That won't come, however,
until after the November 2 election.
The legislature of 1945 passed a
bill to require electors to vote for
the national party candidate, but
the State Supreme Court said in
an advisory opinion that the law is
unconstitutional. Electors, the court
said, are free agents.

home games.

Democratic Rally Hears Austin
BlameG.O.P. for High Prices
F. Byrne Austin, of Takoma Park, Democratic congressional
candidate from the 6th Maryland District, chats with Mrs. John
L Whitehurst, Democratic national committeewoman for the
State, at a rally last night at the Meadowbrook Recreation
Center in Montgomery County. —Star Staff Photo.
F. Byrne Austin, Democratic can
didate for Congress from the 6th
Maryland District, last night blamed
the "do-nothing Republican 80th
Congress” for high prices.
He spoke at a Democratic rally
attended by an estimated 3,000 per
sons in the Meadowbrook Recreation
Center at East-West highway and
Beach drive, Montgomery County.
He seeks the seat now held by
Representative Beall, Republican.
Mr. Austin, who lives in Takoma
Park, said 6th District residents "are
convinced that the Republican 80th
Congress removed price controls for
the profit of the few and at the ex
pense of the many."
"In 1946.” Mr. Austin declared.
“The Republicans said they would
take off price controls and natural
competition would bring down
prices. What happened? Today, it
is difficult for most of us to buy all
of the things we need
He recalled that President Tru
man. in an attempt to control prices,
asked the recent special session of
Congress to re-establish excess pro
fits taxes and to regulate specula
tion.
He also accused the Republicans j
of "playing politics with atomic j
energy” by putting their knife "in-1
to its nonpartisan administration." j
In urging a “decisive” vote for
himself and the national ticket, Mr.
Austin $aid:
“Our Democratic goals have
brought our country from despair
and fear in 1932 to prosperity and,
the opportunity to create a world
of justice and peace. Under a Dem
ocratic president the United Nations
was established.”
Other speakers included Gov. Lane
of Maryland, who made a brief
statement; Will Allen, who opposed
Mr. Austin in the primaries; J. In
gram Medley, vice chairman of the
Democratic State Central Commit- j
tee for the county; Mrs. Anna
Roach, prominent Bethesda Demo
cratic worker, and Mrs. John L..
Whitehurst, of Baltimore, State'^
Democratic national committee
woman.

A—10 TUESDAY, September 28, 1948

Where We Stand
In his strong and admirable address to
the General Assembly, British Foreign
Secretary Bevin said that in view of the
policies which the Russians have followed
“we don’t know where we stand.” That
is a statement which ought not to be taken
too literally for while it may be true
enough in the limited sense in which Mr.
Bevin was speaking, we certainly do know,
or ought to know, where we stand in the
fundamental matter of our relations with
the Soviet Union.
Mr. Bevin was using the “we” collectively
in reference to the nations West of the
Iron Curtain. And that Mr. Bevin is in
no real doubt as to where these nations
stand is indicated by the following excerpt
which he read from a statement by Lenin,
later incorporated in a book by Stalin:
“We are living not merely in a state but
in a system of states and the existence of
the Soviet Republic side by side with im
perialist states for a long time is unthink
able One or the other must triumph in
the end. And before that end supervenes
a series of frightful collisions between the
Soviet Republic and bourgeois states will
be inevitable. That means that if the
ruling class—the proletariat—wants to hold
away it must prove its capacity to do so by
military organization also.”
It has been hard for the people in the
West to bring themselves to believe that
these words mean what they say. Just
as the political leaders of a decade ago
persuaded themselves that Hitler did not
really mean what he said, their successors
have found it difficult to believe that this
Lenin-Stalin doctrine is really the doctrine
of the men who direct the policies of the
Soviet state today.
But the cumulative evidence to the
contrary is overwhelming. In the light of
what has happened since the end of the
war we would be as foolish as barnyard
geese not to assume that Stalin and his
colleagues fully believe in the inevitability
of an ultimate clash between Communism
and the West, and that they are preparing
accordingly.
No one can say with any certainty
whether, as a matter of fact, the collision
is inevitable. But if we are capable of
learning anything from experience we
ought to realize that the danger of war
will be increased by weakness on our part
and, conversely, that it will diminish to
the extent that we are strong. It is a sorry
prospect if we must deal with Russia at
arm’s length, preparing meanwhile for the
worst. Yet in the‘circumstances as we see
them today it would be suicidal to do any
thing else.__
Diminishing Illiteracy
There is cause for real satisfaction in
the latest report by the Census Bureau on
the decide of illiteracy in the United
States. That illiteracy definitely is on its
way out is indicated by the bureau esti
mates of the number of citizens who could
not read or write in 1947. Comparison of
the figures with those of 1930, when the
last survey was conducted, and with those
of earlier reporting periods, shows that
each succeeding generation of Americans
is better educated than the previous one.
Last October, when the new estimates
were made, about 2,800.000 or 2.7 per cent
of persons fourteen or over could neither
read nor write. In 1930 the total was more
than four million, or 4.3 per cent, and in
1920, nearly five million, or 6 per cent. The
most encouraging aspect of the situation
Is that the proportion of illiterates is
lower with each successively younger age
group. Thus, whereas there are approxi
mately seven illiterates in every hundred
persons aged sixty-five or over, only one
illiterate on an average is to be found
among every hundred persons fourteen to
twenty-four years old. As the older illit
erates pass on, the outlook is for a drastic
reduction in illiteracy percentages.
In line with the findings in past years,
the greatest percentage of illiteracy pre
vailed among non-whites, including not
only Negroes but American Indians and
Asiatics. Eleven per cent of non-whites
were found to be lacking ability to read
or write, as compared with 1.8 per cent
of the whites. But the bureau pointed out
that dramatic progress has been made in
educating the younger non-white age
groups. The figures show this advance
clearly. While one out of three non-whites
over sixty-five could not read or write last
year, only 4 per cent of non-whites in the
fourteen-to-twenty-four age group were
illiterate.
Here is proof that more and better edu
cational opportunities are being made
available to each new generation of
Americans, with especially noteworthy
progress being made among the colored
population. The bureau even credits selec
tive military service with part of the ad
vance observed in recent years. At the
outset of World War II the Army would
not accept illiterates, but as manpower
shortages grew, thousands were drafted
and given schooling in the primary three
R’s. As a result, many veterans who were
illiterate upon enrollment now are able to
read and write for the first time in their
lives.
Another factor in improved conditions
Is the larger appropriations for schools in
many areas which formerly lagged in this j
respect. Compulsory education laws also
are more vigorously enforced than hereto
fore, particularly in rural sections. How
ever, there will be time for complacency
over our recent educational strides only
when illiteracy becomes as rare in this
country as it is in Finland, Switzerland
and a few other places where it is vir
tually unknown.
Red Gains in China
Communist capture of Tsinan, chief city
in Shantung Province, is the latest in a
series of successes which appear to be
shaking the grip of the National govern
ment throughout Northern China. No
where are the Nationalist armies able to
make headway against a concerted Red
offensive which is slashing vital rail com
munication lines and isolating Nationalist
garrisons*supplied only by air. The cumu
lative effect of these reverses, coupled with
the evil effects of galloping monetary in
flation, is to undermine the prestige of
the National government and its leader,
Chiang Kai-shek. The repercussions on
China at large are grave enough to por
tend a major crisis in the long drawn-out
struggle for supremacy which has ragecf
with varying fortunes over so many years.
Of course, it should be understood that
China's interminable civil war cannot be
measured or evaluated by Western stand
ards. China is a vast, sprawling country,
large as the United States, nearly thrice
as populous, and for the most part with
primitive communications. On such a
huge battleground, the loss of a city or
even a province is merely an episode in a
complex, slow-motion drama. Nevertheless,
the general trend is obviously adverse to
the National government. Unless this
trend be stayed or reversed, the eventual
collapse of Chiang’s regime or its dis
integration into local factions is clearly
indicated.
Such an eventuality would be extremely
unfortunate for the West in general and
the United States in particular. Commu
nist domination over all or a large part of
China would upset an already precarious
balance of forces, not in China alone but
over most of Asia. It would add new life
to the current Communist offensive in the
lands to the southward of China and would
presumably have grave effects upon India.
All this should be kept in mind at a
time when the spotlight of public interest
is focussed upon Europe and the crisis
over Berlin. We should not forget that
Communist strategy is literally world-wide,
and that one of its favorite tactics is a
diversionary shift to some relatively neg
lected sector. Many experts on the Orient
warn us that conditions there favor com
munism more than in Europe. And Com
munist triumphs in Asia would, in the
long run, be only a trifle less disastrous
than similar gains in the European theater.
The difficulty is that, no matter how cor
rect this diagnosis, it is hard to see how,
in the present situation, we can divert the
strength needed to cope with a deterio
rating trend in the Far East. The one
ameliorating aspect is the slower tempo of
change in the Orient, especially in China.
This may give us a time margin not
vouchsafed us in Europe. Yet, sooner or
later, the Far Eastern situation will have
tp be faced by uif and the Western world.
It cannot be ignored or permitted to go
by default.
Free Vote in Virginia
A situation that is not without its
inherent touch of irony has been created
by the apparent decision of the Virginia
Democratic State Central Committee to
sit on the fence in the forthcoming presi
dential election.
There is some, but not much, room for
quibbling as to the intent and meaning of
the resolutions adopted by the committee
at its meeting in Richmond on Saturday.
The first resolution is aimed at "getting
out the full Democratic vote” on election
day. The second directs that certain funds
"be used in the interest of the Democratic
Party.” Nowhere was there mention or
indorsement of the Truman-Barkley
ticket, and a Truman supporter who
wanted a specific indorsement of the
national ticket was denied the floor on
the ground that he was not a committee
member. (A similar ruling had been in
voked when another non-member sought
to speak in behalf of the Thurmond States’
Rights ticket.)
The fair inference from all of this is
that the State Central Committee is
opposed to the re-election of President
Truman. This is an inference which can
not be broadened to apply To all of the
leaders of the dominant political organ
ization in Virginia, for some of them,
however unenthusiastically, will publicly
support the Truman-Barkley ticket. But
it is the reasonable interpretation to put
upon what the committee did and said,
and, this being so, it can be argued that
there is reason to regret that the com
mittee was not more courageous and forth
right.
At the same time, it is difficult to sym
pathize with some of the complaints that
are being heard. The Richmond Times
Dispatch, commenting editorially on the
action, concludes that the Democrats of
Virginia are now "free” to vote for Presi
dent Truman, Governor Thurmond or
Governor Dewey. The Times-Dispatch
does not say whether it thinks this is good
or bad. But the plain implication of the
political critics of the State Commit
tee—those who are generally described as
constituting the liberal wing of the Virginia
Democratic Party—is that it is bad. And
there is at least a touch of irony in finding
this group among the complainants be
cause the Democratic voters are being
given a free choice this year by what it
likes to describe as the ruthless Byrd-Tuek
1 machine.
Actually, there is reason to believe that
this sort of thing is good for the political
future of the South. The State Committee,
of course, is not thinking along this line,
and if the Democrats had nominated a
presidential candidate to its liking the
committee would be working to get out
the vote in his behalf. But the present
motives of the State Committee have little
bearing on the proposition that the South
would be better off if its voters could
achieve a real measure of political inde
pendence. There is something basically
j and inherently wrong with the assumption
that any State Committee of any party
can or should be able to swing the voters
one way or another. And if there is to be
an emancipation of the voters in Virginia
this year, if they are to throw off the fet
ters of prejudice and tradition which have
bound them to the Democrat Party, then
it may be said that the State Committee,
wittingly or otherwise, has struck a blow
for a better political future in the South.
Pity the Secret Service Man
It is a safe bet that at least one section
of President Truman’s entourage does not
approve of their boss’ campaign tactic of
getting close to the people in his rear
platform appearances. To the Secret
Service men Mr. Truman’s down-to-earth
mingling with station crowds is a viola
tion of the rule book which governs their
job of protecting the President and his
family from harm. It is no secret that
the Secret Service is very unhappy about
the whole business.
Dispatches from news correspondents
aboard the Truman special train, the
“Ferdinand Magellan,” show why the
dapper, sharp-eyed Treasury agents are
biting their fingernails and talking to
themselves. The stories tell of Mr. Tru
man’s folksy habit of pushing right into
the midst of throngs and shaking hands
right and left, with the ever-present
Secret Service operatives on the fringe,
trying frantically to keep their eyes on
everybody in the crowd. Presidents are
not supposed to do things like that. In
fact, the Secret Service is under orders to
prevent such fraternization—if they can.
But the President, after all, is boss. So,
at whistle stop after whistle stop, Mr.
Truman exuberantly continues to pump
arms and indulge in small talk with his
admirers, strangers as well as acquaint
ances. And that is not all. Frequently
he invites the strangers to step aboard his
private car and meet Mrs. Truman and
daughter Margaret. Reporters estimate
that more persons have seen the interior
of the presidential car in recent weeks
than on any previous trip.
This is all very thrilling and pleasant
to the favored crowds, but it must be a
harrowing ordeal for the men whose legal
responsibility it is to keep cranks and
suspicious persons away from the Presi
dent. For all the Secret Service knows,
many a crank and suspicious person may
have accepted Mr. Truman’s invitation to
shake hands and inspect his private car.
The Secret Service men can only frown
and redouble their efforts to scrutinize all
who get close to their chief. They deserve
a lot of sympathy as well as admiration
for their tireless efforts in the face of
difficulties inherent in the President’s
determination to meet as many “little
people” as possible.
The drugstore price of the constitutent
chemicals in the human body has risen
to around $5, though the careful shopper
might find himself for less in the cut rates.
Rubber street paving, tried out in Akroif,
opens new vistas. Thus the troublemaker,
tossed out of one bar by the bouncer, may
land in another on the first hop.
If to err is human, some of the mightiest
of the world’s diplomats must be people.
This and That
By Charles E. Tracewell
“Never give away anything—you may need
It?’
This ancient wisdom comes home, now and
then, to the person who is feeding the birds.
He may find out that an old broken bridge
lamp standard was something to regret.
But how could he have known it when he
gave it*away? x
The inclination of the moment is to get rid
of many things. The tyranny of things, some
one has said, is the worst tyranny of all. Every
one knows how it is. The attic is full of broken
down furniture. The basement has its quota
of objects no longer in use, but too good to
throw away. Even the garage has corners
and shelves laden down with many things.
Those who believe in keeping everything
(and these include some very famous people)
usually love paper boxes and old bottles.
Nothing goes more against their grain than
to destroy or throw away a neat cardboard
box or prized bottle. Jars in which food has
been packed are serious temptations to them.
So rows and rows of these accumulate, each
one a treasure or a nuisance according to the
point of vtew.
Every household has its own list of discarded
furniture. Other people may have bright ideas
as to what the owner should do with them.
But such a wise one should look first in his
own attic.
Who would imagine that an old bridge lamp
would be worth keeping for 10 years?
The owner did not, so he gave it away.
It was after this that he took up the pleasant
sport of bird watching and feeding.
He bought several feeding stations and put
them around the yard. He bought seed and
suet. He began to read about the birds. Car
dinals began to be topics of conversation.
ik
One of the stations he hung in a tree, but
for some reason the birds did not take to it
there.
The owner decided to move it, and wondered
what he would hang it from, since it was
suspended by a wire.
Immediately to his mind came the idea
of something upright with an arm swung out.
That old bridge lamp standard! It would be
just the thing.
It would have been just the thing, sure
enough, but it wasn’t there.
The whole household looked for it, some in
the attic, some in the garage, some in the
basement.
There was no lamp, and for good reason—
the bird watcher suddenly remembered what
he had done with it. It was the old perversity
of inanimate things. For 10 years nobody
wanted it, and nobody had given it a thought,
and it was there.
When some one wanted it, and thQught of
it, it was gone.
How nice it is to be able to place the blame
on an inanimate thing. They are such pests.
A thing can kick around a house lor years,
always in the way, but just let ii be put into
the trash, a use immediately arises for it.
If the old lamp standard had been on duty,
the feeding station would have been used in
a few minutes. Its sprawly feet would have
sunk into the ground firmly and its arms would
have received the feeder with "birdylike pre
cision,” as the song says.
That old lamp standard would have taken
on a new lease of life, once more serving a good
purpose.
This particular householder there and then
decided that the best thing to do is to keep
everything one ever gets, whether there seems
to be a present use for it or not. The point
is that no one can tell. The lamp was pur
chased and used and discarded. When it
stood around the house for years, there was
no one there then interested in feeding wild
birds. At that time they may even have
thought birds nuisances.
It would take an analytical mind to foresee
all possible uses for anything. This lamp in
stance shows the impossibility of it. A mind
would have to be more than human if under
such circumstances it said to Raelf, “Lamp
standard—let me see, what can ba done with it
some day? Yes, I can foresee a neat little
birdhouse, or a feeding station suspended by
a wire from the center of its roof, hanging to
this very arm.”
It fcannot be done that way, and, since it
cannot, the only thing to do is to keep every*
thing, in the hope and belief it will come in
handy some day.
Letters to The Star
For a Practical Health Program
to ttat Editor of Hie 8Ur:
Intrude the subject “socialized medicine” in
a debate among doctors, and they loose their
habitual objectivity. Like the epithet “scab"
in a labor debate, a “socialized-medicine" tag
is guaranteed unfavorably to prejudge any
health security insurance proposal.
The Star s trenchant editorial on “The Doc
tors' Dilemma” performs a much needed serv
ice for the medical profession. In friendly
fashion, it permits us to see ourselves as others
see us. Though it is our business to diagnose
and heal the ills of others, we resent having
the roles reversed. But as a respected expert
in public relations and social evolution The
Star warns us to pause and reorient. We are
headed for avoidable discomfiture.
This provocative editorial was occasioned by
the worrietd query of a keen lay-observer and
analyist of the medical scene. Writing in
our local medical publication, the Medical
Annals of the District of Columbia, he asks:
"Will Governor Dewey remain unaffected by
the views of his running mate?”
Though they are political opponents, Presi
dent Truman and Governor Earl Warren are
on record as favoring compulsory health in
surance. But Governor Dewey emphatically
has said: "Compulsory socialized medicine
is no good. "Then despite this seeming con
tradiction all three agree they want no parts
of socialized medicine.
The Star and the (writer in the Medical An
nals propose to resolve this political paradox
by having doctors agree on a precise definition
of "socialized medicine.” Unless we do, The
Star warns that a debt-crushed public in des
perate need of financial relief from the bank
rupting costs of health hazards, may define
it for us. And its definition may not be to
our liking.
Obviously, debating minds only can meet by
using the same yardstick. That yardstick
in the present instance must possess precise
social and economic values capable of stan
dardizing a workable national health security
program. The point is: Will agreement on
a definition of socialized medicine provide such
a yardstick?
The frustration and fratricide of the last,
decade would incline one to believe to the
contrary. We have wasted valuable time and
opportunity bitterly protesting that which
all agree we do not want. «We do not want
"socialized medicine.” Why not facilitate a
meeting of minds by affirmatively and pre
cisely defining what we do want? Concen
trate on fruitful areas of agreement and ignore
the discarded and unwanted. In other words
forget “socialized medicine” and concentrate
the indivisible unity of pragmatic altruism on
national health security insurance.
Already we have made marvelous progress to
this end. As Mrs. Agnes E. Meyer so succinctly
has stated: “The success of the National
Health Assembly was a triumph of democratic
process over tensions that appeared to be in
soluable.”
At NHA it unanimously was agreed that our
health security program should be national
in character; that it should be financed by
the solvent principle of contributory insurance;
and that its administration must conform to
patterns of community need and the free
choice and preference of the individual patient
and doctor.
This means a practical health security pro
gram should provide character-building self
help for all rather than indulgent paternalism
for the few. It means that both fair costs and
adequate benefits will be determined by mathe
matics rather than political preference and
patronage. But most important of all it
means that our Nation agrees we are our
brother's keeper. The common burden is light
ened by spreading it to the willing shoulders
of all. To the superior mind and the unsur
passed productivity of competitive capitalism
we add the understanding heart of dependable
social justic. Communism has no chance
against capitalism with an alert, unselfish
social conscience.
THOMAS E. MATTINGLY, M. D.
Assessor’s Procedures Criticized
To the Editor of The Star:
The item in The Star of September 21, relat
ing to the method used by the District of
Columbia assessor in appraising property in
homes and apartments raises the question
whether the assessor is following the provisions
of the tax statutes. The statutes give the
householders an exemption of $1,000, but the
assessor in many cases is appraising household
effects in excess of $1,000, leaving a balance on
which a tax is payable. The assessor is not
basing his appraisals on inspections of the
property but upon the mean or average value
of household effects reported by owners in
similar groups.
Of course, Congress could not enact any tax
law on any such basis, because it would result
in a tax in many cases on property which
householders do not own. It is unreasonable
to say that every householder in a group has
the same amount of household effects. It thus
appears that the assessor is doing indirectly
what Congress cannot do directly.
It is true that if a householder be dissatisfied
with this method of appraisal, he may ask for
a reappraisal, but in numerous cases the tax
is so small that the householder would rather
pay the tax than contest it. Especially is this
true since it may be presumed that the predi
lection of the assessor upon inspection would
be to sustain his original appraisal.
It is not the duty of the assessor to multiply
the exemptions provided in the tax statutes by
excessive appraisals. Nor is it his duty to pro
vide for collections of small amounts from
householders on the theory that they will not
find it worthwhile to resist improper appraisals.
It might well for the assessor to ponder
a statement by Frank E. Holman, the Incoming
president of the American Bar Association,
pledging the association “to a defense of indi
vidual rights against arbitrary powers and
against bureaucratic centralizations which
break down the impartial rule of law and sub
stitute uncontrolled official discretions.”
R. WILSON.

In Behalf of Teachers
To th» Editor of The Star:
May I make a plea for the many fine teachers
and officers who are giving conscientious and
valuable service to the education of children
in the Washington public schools? These men
and women are interested in the growth and
development of children and are devoting their
lives to the youth of our city. The public
school system is not a closed corporation, thank
goodness, and the Board of Education has been
able to choose and secure the services of out
standing educators throughout the Nation.
This has helped strengthen rather than weaken
the system.
The “Mother of a Teacher” who has the real
“low down” on what is going on in the public
schools makes one wonder why so many teach
ers day after day put up with such undemo
cratic procedures. One example does not prove
the rule, however. I know of teachers in the
Washington public schools who work demo
cratically, sharing and co-operating one with
the other, under the leadership of an "out of
town appointed” administrator who happens
to have a real love for, and interest in, chil
dren, and who plans and works together with
her teachers for the general welfare, and other
teachers under administrators who have “come
up from the ranks” who work pleasantly and
co-operatively with and for children.
There are hundreds of teachers teaching the
three Rs in the District public schools every
day. They are not only “allowed” to teach
reading, writing, arithmetic, spelling, history,
geography, etc., but are encouraged and di
rected by their principals and other school
officers to do so. Courses of study on these
subjects, set up by the Curriculum Committee
working together with the teachers, are readily
available to those who wish to see them.
A teacher’s life is not an easy one and she
needs the encouragement and support of the
parents, if she is to help the children success
fully. The old saying that teachers can only
“teach” children, they cannot “learn” them,
Is significant today more than it was some
years ago. Children are coming to school who
do not know what it means to obey, who have
never had any real responsibility and who have
never had to do anything they did not wish
to do. This makes teaching a very difficult
task, especially when the class is large and
the teacher inexperienced; and there are many
Inexperienced teachers in classrooms in the
District and throughout the country today.
This condition needs to be remedied, but the
kind of letters that have been appearing in
The Star lately will not help remedy it. They
are doing real harm by breaking down the
morale of the experienced teacher who is trying
to help children learn in spite of the handicaps
under which they come to school.
There is a tendency on the part of parents
throughout the country to take sides against
the teachers and principals, thereby blocking
any help that the teachers wish t<1 give their
children. If they realized that this an
tagonism only worked to the detriment of the
children, they would work with the teachers
instead of against them.
Let us work together for the good of the
children.. Sarcasm and name-calling will not
help. Understanding and co-operation will.
A TEACHER.
Single Girls in Apartments
To the Editor of The Star:
We hear much of the housing shortage.
Many young married couples are unable to get
shelter of any kind, while many apartments are
occupied by so-called "bachelor girls” and un
married men. During the war many of these
single girls secured apartments stating they
were going to be married as soon as their boy
friends came back from overseas. Many of
these girls had no boy friends overseas or any
where else; and they still are in apartments
financed with the aid of Government funds for
"American families.” Landlords don’t mind
renting to these girls as they don’t have chil
dren to contend with.
The Government should insist that apart
ments financed with Government funds be
rented to families atid that all apartments
occupied by single people be vacated. There
are plenty of vacant sleeping rooms and board
ing houses for these single people who want to
enjoy the benefits of married life without as
suming its responsibilities.
W. H. CORKREAN.

I
The Political Mill
Job Is Underway To Sell'
Dewey to North Midwest
Farmers in Area Are Prosperous and
‘Don’t Want Boat Rocked*
By Gould Lincoln
HELENA, Mont., Sept. 38.—The great NortA
Midwest, with its prairies, its grain and i«
cattle, is the country in which Gov. TTioswa
E. Dewey of New York has to be sold as the
farmers’ candidate if he is to capture tbe
presidential electoral votes of its States. These
people fie prosperous as they have never been
before, notwithstanding high prices they pay
for what they need. Their production is enor
mous and the prices they receive out of the
world. It’s a boat they don’t want rocked,
if they can help it.
The job is underway, Dewey, the Governor
who has done much for the farmers of his
own State; Dewey, the farmer himself with his
300-acre dairy farm, his only real home, the
sole bit of land he owns, is being spread before
the eyes of the farmers of Minnesota. Alfred
D. Stedman, associate editor of the St. Paul
Pioneer Press, after a visit to Gov. Dewey at
his Pawling, (N. Y.) farm, writes in his news
paper:
“Dewey turned to farming as a natural sup
plement to his public career. Such overlapping
of interest of leaders between public service and
agriculture has profoundly affected United
States history.” Mr. Stedman predicts that,
with Gov. Dewey in the White House, it will
again.
Know* Food Problems.
“The Pawling farm affords Dewey personal
contacts that are priceless.” He continues.
“The Nation’s and the world's all-important
food problems are brought home to him on his
own farm. Dewey’s farm experience (he has
had the farm for ten years) bulks large in his
presidential background. Others who had farms
in their backgrounds have turned out to b*
great Presidents. Why not Dewey?”
This midwest farm territory and its people
have had Republicanism in their bones—and
when the pendulum started swinging away
from the New Deal in 1938 and 1940, it was the
first to move over. It was the section of the
country which gave Oov. Dewey nearly all the
99 electoral votes he captured in 1944.
Minnesota was a hold out. It voted for the
fourth time for the late President Franklin D.
Roosevelt. It is still a battleground, into which
Gov. Dewey and his running mate, Governor
Warren of California, its own former Governor
Stassen and other big guns of the Republican
party will carry the Republican fight. The
public opinion polls so far published still give
President Truman the edge—but they are by
no means conclusive, showing Mr. Truman with
only 51 per cent of the vote in the samples
taken. Mr. Roosevelt carried the State four
years ago with 52.8 per cent of the major party
vote cast—a margin of 62,000 votes in a total
of upwards of 1,000,000. Similar polls have
placed Senator Ball, Republican and a par
ticular hate of organized labor leaders, behind
Mayor Hubert Humphrey of Minneapolis, the
Democrat-Farmer Labor candidate. The sign*
are, however, that both Gov. Dewey and Sen
ator Ball are coming up. It will be unlikely
that Minnesota stands out like a Democratic
sore thumb, after the election, if Gov. Dewey
goes over with a bang in the other great agri
cultural states.
Labor Leaders In Fight
In Minnesota, however, the labor leader allies
of Mr. Truman are exerting all their efforts
to defeat Gov. Dewey and Senator Ball, mostly
because of their hatred of the Taft-Hartley
Act, which Senator Ball helped to write. They
have promised, it is reported, to spend $350,000
in the Minnesota campaign, and are relying
on the labor vote in the twin cities and Duluth
and the iron range, plus scads of anti-Dewey
propaganda passed out to the farmers.
Minnesota is tremendously interested In a
suggestion that her Senator Thye and former
Governor be made Secretary of Agriculture in
the Dewey cabinet. It is known that some of
the most important leaders of the farm organ
izations are interested in such an appointment.
Representative Clifford Hope of Kansas, chair
man of the House Agriculture Committee, has
been most in position. A third man who is
said to be under consideration is Milton Elsen
hower, president of a Kansas college and
brother of Gen. “Ike” Eisenhower. Any assur
ance that Senator Thye, who was greatly pop
ular as Governor, would run the federal De
partment of Agriculture, would have its affect
on Minnesota beyond a doubt.
Questions and Answers
A reader can set the answer to anr question of
fact by writing The Evening Star Information
Bureau. 316 Eye street N.E.. Washington 2. D. C.

BY THE HASKIN SERVICE.
Q. When did the custom of flipping a coin
originate?—F. D. F.
A. The custom of flipping a coin dates from
the time of Julius Caesar. His head was en
graved on one side of all Roman coins. If there
was anything to be decided a coin was tossed.
If the head turned up, the person or decision
represented by the “head” was correct.
Q. When did Franklin D. Rooeevelt address
the French people over the air?—E. C. S.
A. President Roosevelt broadcast In French
directly to the people of France by short wave
on November 7, 1942. He urged them to con
tinue to help the Allies In all possible ways.
Q. How many different kinds of flsh are
there?—B. W. Y.
A. There are more than 40,000 different
kinds of flsh. They live in all parts of the
world and there are few waters that do not
contain at least one kind.
Q. How were American troops supplied with
muskets during the Revolutionary War?—
P. W. N.
A. The Colonial Government relied for a
supply of muskets mainly on the Colonists
themselves. Some military equipment was
purchased in France. There were many gun
smiths in the colonies and it has been esti
mated that there was a capacity for manu
facturing 100,000 stands of muskets a year in
1775.
Q. Who was the first woman to receive a
patent?—F. N.
A. The first recorded patent granted to a
woman was received by Mary Kies in 1809
for a method of weaving straw with silk or
thread.
Q. What is the meaning of Rebecca Eureka?
—O. O. S.
A. This was a radar device for para troop
operations. The Rebecca set. in the troop
carrier, triggers the Eureka act strapped to
the leg of a paratrooper on the ground. It
permlta exact spotting and the parachuting of
additional troops or supplies.
Q Why is a certain type of candy called
"praline”?—B. M. J. w _
A. The word is said to have been derived
from the name of a French Marshal and diplo
mat, Cesar du Plessis-Praslin, whose chef, in
an effort to please him, made a confection of
flimmwu dipped in boiling sugar. The nrroe
Praslin is pronounced pra-ian.

CATHERINE HAYDON JACOBS.

Brownell Sees 4 to 1
Dewey Margin, G.O.P.
Control of Congress
By J. A. O'Leary
Election of the Dewey-Warren
ticket by a 4-to-l ratio of electoral
votes was the prediction of Herbert
Brownell, Jr., Republican campaign
manager, as he bade farewell to his
headquarters here today and head
ed back to New York.
Exuding confidence, Mr. Brownell
•Iso made a flat forecast of a Re
publican Congress. He estimated
the GOP will add between 11 and 20
new seats to the 246 it holds in the
present House and will keep control
of the Senate by at least the present
margin of 51 to 45.
The genial, efficient young law
yer, who has helped Gov. Dewey
through all of his political battles,
said he based his prophesies on a
telephone checkup with Republican
officials in 42 States in the last
two days.
Declines to Name-States.
Mr. Brownell gives President Tru
man not more than four States out
side of the South and said he ex
pects Gov. Dewey to, carry "one or
more" in Dixie.
He declined to name the States
he thinks each candidate will carry.
The nearest he came to it was to
say the South will be split three
ways—among Dewey, Truman and
Thurmond (Governor of South Car
olina and the States' Rights nomi
nee!.
Mr. Brownell told his last Wash
ington press conference he expects
the popular vote to be about 10 per
cent above 1944 or around 52,000,000,
and predicted Gov. Dewey would
poll a popular majority in excess of
3,000,000.
As the Republican campaign
manager packed his bags to be with
Gov. Dewey in New York for the
windup, observers here were con
vinced Mr. Brownell has no ambi
tion to enter the Government serv
ice.
Lease on House Expiring.
A correspondent jokingly told Mr.
Brownell his neighbors in McLean,
Va., where he has been living dur
ing the campaign, are saying he
rented the house on a month-to
month basis, but has a four-year
option on it.
“I'm leaving Washington tomor
row and the lease expires Novem
ber 15,” Mr. Brownell said with a
smile. “I'm packing my trunks to
be with Gov. Dewey in Madison
Square Garden, where he will deliv
er one of the most important

night.”

By the Associated Press
NEW YORK, Oct. 29.—The House
Committee on Un-American Activ
ities plans to hold hearings within

tain Negro organizations.”

He also said the committee soon

"The first phase brought out the

actors who are pretty well known.”
Mr. Thomas’ reference to “cards”
presumably was to Communist mem
bership cards.

Dewey Falsely Called
Foe of Civil Service,
Republicans Charge
•y *h» Auocioud Prtis
The Republican National Com
mittee yesterday accused the Dem
ocratic National Committee of dis
tributing a falsified document to
portray Gov. Dewey as a foe of civil
service.
The Democratic National Com
mittee then issued a statement in
which the signer of the document
denied that it was falsified.
The disputed document quoted
the GOP presidential nominee as
telling members of the Brooklyn
council of the Veterans of Foreign
Wars that all civil service employes
are “mediocre at their best” and as
saying:
“If I had my way I'd abolish the
civil service.”
Gov. Dewey was alleged to have
made the remarks last February in
discussing the VFW legislative pro
gram in his Albany office. A num
jber of those present at the meet
ing said Gov. Dewey said no such
thing.
Democrats Stand Firm.
Democratic headquarters said in
a statement October 16 that an
"official report” on Gov. Dewey’s
purported remarks was made to
the VFW Brooklyn Council and
“proved” that Gov. Dewey did make
the derogatory comment on civil
service workers.
The report was signed by Maj.
James J. Mackin, chairman of the
Council's Legislative Committee,
who said he was present at the Al
bany meeting. The Democratic
Committee also issued photographic
copies of Maj. Mackin’s alleged re
port.
GOP headquarters announced yes
terday it has received a letter from
the Brooklyn Council stating "the
alleged report of James J. Mackin
and all press releases relative
to the matter are firmly re
pudiated.”
The Council's letter, signed by
County Comdr. Barclay A. Farenga.
said Maj. Mackin’s report was never
presented to the Council for ratifi
cation and adoption and was not
even filed in the Council’s records'
The statement further disowned
"the irresponsible actions and utter
ings of unauthorized persons.”
Republican Statement.
Republican headquarters said in
yesterday's statement:
j "The Brooklyn council letter
j stamps the fjhotostatic copy as false
i and indicates the scribbled nota
tion on the Mackin report purport
ling to show that it was accepted
and filed, was faked.”
The Republican National Com
mittee identified Maj. Mackin as
an assistant district attorney in
Brooklyn.
Later, the Democratic National
Committee issued a statement quot
ing Maj. Mackin as saying his re
port was read into the minutes of
a Brooklyn VFW council meeting on
March 15 "and filed with other rec
ords of the meeting.”
Maj. Mackin’s statement said:
“I emphatically deny any al
legations that the letter (report) as
reproduced by the Democratic Na
tional Committee is a fake or
forgery.
"I call on any one who believes
m,v report was inaccurate or that
the letter was faked or forged to
swear to such allegations under
oath.”

Approved for Gl VETERAN TRAINING
Globe-Circling Aviatrix
Ready for Aleutian Hop
By the Associated Press
CHITOSE AIRBASE, Hokkaido,
Oct. 29.—Mrs. Richard Morrow-Tait,
British housewife, landed her single
engine plane here today and expects
to take off tomorrow for Shemya
Island in the Aleutians on her pro
jected around-the-world flight.
American Air Force officials in
Tokyo said she would be given an
escort of a B-17 air-sea rescue plane
on the 1,778-mile flight. She had
said she would make the flight
without escort. But American of
ficials said she would be escorted
under the routine procedure for all
small planes making the same
flight.
Her navigator is former Royal Air
Force Pilot Michael Townsend.

Truman Actions Seen
Indicating Bipartisan
Policy Doesn't Exist
Han for Vinson Mission
Cited as Example of How
President Acts on Own
By David Lawrence
The debate between Gov. Dewey
and President Truman on the sub
ject of bipartisan foreign policy and
particularly as to who gets the
credit for starting it presupposes
the existence of something of the
sort. The truth is there never
really has been nor is there any
such thing as a bipartisan foreign
policy in the literal sense of the
phrase.
Actually what has been called a
bipartisan foreign policy is merely
a commendable restraint in both
parties—a form of political truce—
which keeps them from exploiting
to the fullest political advantage
the sins of omission or commission
of one or the other of the parties.
Thus, the isolationist and nonco
operationist attitude of a sub
stantial segment of the Republican
Party in Congress with respect to
European aid is a well-known fact.
The Democrats have hammered
away at it but not with any real
emphasis. They may be. afraid of
reprisals.
Similarly, the fantastic blunder
ing of President 'fruman and his
Department of State in handling
the Russian problem never has been
fully emphasized by the Republi
cans. They could say it has brought
us to the verge of war.
Impression Is False.
Actually, the impression that
Republicans and Democrats sit
down together and shape foreign
policy is just a piece of camou
flage devised to keep foreign powers
from exploiting our own differences
of opinion. The fact that Chair
man Vandenberg of the Senate
Foreign Relations Committee once
in a while sees Secretary Marshall,
or that John Foster Dulles, Gov.
Dewey's close friend and probable
Secretary of State, goes to Paris
as a member of the American dele
gation to the United Nations, is
merely window-dressing for the bi
partisan idea.
For the truth is that, under the
American Constitution, there can
be no bi-partisan control or re
sponsibility for what is happening
in the, Executive Branch of the
Government. President Trumafr was
well within his rights in thinking
up the scheme to send Chief
Justice Vinson to Moscow without
consulting any Republican.
As a matter of fact he was well
within his rights in asking forj
radio time for a “nonpolitical” an-j
nouncement when he planned to j
send the Chief Justice abroad with
out even consulting his own Secre
tary of State. He was legally right j
but politically wrong.
Nor did Secretary Marshall dis
cuss the Vinson plan with his Re- I
publican colleagues in the delega
tion before he left Paris to come
home to talk about it with Mr.
Truman. His excuse was that he
thought the plan dead. But, dead
or not, any partner in a business
enterprise who holds his secret
planning or information from an
other partner would not long retain
the confidence or trust of the other.
It was even worse for Mr. Marshall’s
Republican partners in the delega
tion to leam about it first from the
newspapers.
Wallace Incident Recalled.
President Truman did not err j
just once on this sort of thing;
cither. He had read ahead of j
t ime Henry Wallace’s sensational j
speech on foreign policy, delivered'
at Madison Square Garden. He j
gave it his approval while not only,
his own Secretary of State, James;
F. Byrnes, at Paris in the midst of j
critical negotiations, but also Sen
ator Vandenberg, were unaware of j
what was happening In the White!
House to shape the course of
American foreign policy.
In both the Wallace and Vinson
episodes, America was embarrassed
abroad. But the Republicans have
not exploited either incident to the
fullest extent.
That sort of restraint may be
construed as bipartisan considera
tion for America's best interests
abroad. The President's behavior,
however, in both cases, and his
blundering rather than the misuse
of the facts by foreign powers were
what has been mostly covered up
and prevented from doing further;
damage.
What the average citizen prob
ably would like, and thinks perhaps
actually exists, is a coalition gov
ernment—a bipartisan council that
directs and manages foreign policy.
This implies iptimate familiarity
day by day by both parties with all
phases of our international rela- j
tions.
•It might work under a parliamen
tary democracy where the people
have an instant check on their j
chosen representatives in the cab
inet, but It could hardly be as ef
fective in a system where for four;
years a President has complete
responsibility for what goes on in
the Executive Branch of Govern-;
ment, and cannot be called into
question by the people in the
Interim.
For, no matter how many blund-!
ers a President makes, there is
nothing the opposing party can do;
about it till the next election—and
very little can be said in criticism,
if It’s a matter of foreign policy,
without giving to other governments
an .impression of disunity.

This Changing World
White House Order to Ship Arms to China
May Be too Late to Help Chiang
By Constantine Brown
The White House flashed the
green light last week for the ship
ment of $35,000,000 worth of war
materials to China.
It is assumed
that since the
Chief Executive
himself signed
the order that
machine guns,
tommy guns,
rifles and am
munition be
rushed to the
Nanking govern
ment, the long
standing tempo
rizing policies of
the Par Eastern
Division of the'
State Depart
ment will be
abandoned.
Cnitutlu Brown.
Observers who have followed the
tragedy of China for the last two
years and have urged this Govern
ment to .send weapons to the Na
tionalist forces fear that the ad
ministration's gesture may come too
late to put a halt to the military
victories of the Moscow-sponsored
Communist armies.
The once well-equipped and
American-trained forces of the Nan
king government have dwindled to a
small and loosely knit body of men
who look helplessly at the great
strides of the Communists, whose
strength is growing daily, thanks to
Moscow's unstinting support.
Export Licenses Refused.
During the last two years our Gov
ernment not only has refused to
send arms and ammunition to re
place the Chinese governent's losses,
but we have also refused to issue ex
port licenses to the government of
Generalissimo Chiang Kai-shek. He
wanted to use China's own funds
to purchase equipment with which
to fight the Communists.
Under these circumstances, it
was inevitable that the Nationalist
army, which originally was imbued
with a fine fighting spirit, should
lose heart and surrender to its
enemies.
Although Congress has appro
priated funds for military assist
ance to China—first a token sum
last December, later about $125,
000,000 against the State Depart
ment’s opposition—only a negligible
part of the equipment to be pur
chased with this money has been
made ready for shipment across the
Pacific. State Department red
tape nullified Congress’ action.
Two weeks ago, however, the
White House decided, for as yet un
explained reasons, to speed up as
sistance to China and the order
was issued that $35,000,000 worth
of essential war materials should
be sent to Chinese ports still held
by the Nationalists. These ma
terials Include small arms and
necessary ammunition, but they
have to be crated and shipping
must be arranged.
Move May Be Too Late.
In spite of the White House’s
formal order, it appears doubtful
now that this equipment will reach
China in time to enable the forces
of Gen. Chlang to improve their
situation.
Because of these inevitable de
lays it was suggested that Gen.
MacArthur be asked to rush from
Japan some of the war 'material
he has available there. But Gen.
MacArthur, although keenly aware
of the threat which would face this
country in the event of a Com
munist victory in China, is reported
to have replied that in view of the
tenseness of the international sit
uation, and the role his forces
might have to play in the event of
aggression, he could not spare a
single machine gun.
Meanwhile, however, the Defense
Department, acting on the White
House order, is proceeding to
gather war material for transpor
tation to Shanghai and Tientsin.
Most of our Chinese experts, who
are sympathetic to the idea of
strengthening the Nanking govern
ment against the Communist as
sault, are doubtful, however, the
war material will arrive in time to
be of real we to the remainder of
jGren. Chiang's forces.

Norman Thomas Held Only Candidate
Who Saw Folly of U. S. War Policies
By Dorothy Thompson
Leaders of ancient Rome were
accustomed, when their policies
failed, to end their lives. Leaders
of modern democracies when their
policies fail, ask
touae elected.
President Tru
man does not
seem to know
that his postwar
foreign policy
has failed. Presi
dent Roosevelt,
before his death,
had grave pre
monitions that
he had done
some wrong
things. But Mr.
Truman seems
to have no
doubts that his
Derothy Thompsoa.
every step has been taken with
prescienc.e.
Yet we are in the gravest crisis
of our history, which has its center
in the German capital. This crisis
is the result of a chain of actions
whose results could be, and were,
foreseen by some but not by Presi
dent Truman, GOv. Dewey or Henry
Wallace. The only candidate for
President who can stand on a
record of foresight and .principle is
Norman Thomas. He, alone, saw
how false policies logically must
turn out.
Steps in Crisis.
The steps that led to the present
erisis were: Casablanca and “un
conditional surrender": Teheran
and Yalta, where the principles of
the Atlantic Charter were scrapped
and Russia accorded Immense ex
tensions of power; Potsdam and its
directives for Germany; the drop
ping of the atomic bomb on Japan;
gnd the creation of a United-Na
tions, hamstrung by the veto and
without law, judge or policeman.
"Unconditional surrender” pro
longed the war, dealt a death blow'
to the anti-Nazi movement within j
Germany, prevented any possibility
of a general mutiny, accelerated the
massacre of Jews, and brought
about the execution of those Ger
mans who could best have recon
structed a peaceful Germany con
tributing to the rebirth of Europe.
Neither Gov. Dewey nor Mr. Wal
lace vigorously questioned this
formula.
The Republicans were skeptical
over Teheran and Yalta, but there
was little anybody could do about
decisions made before either Con
gress or the American people knew
anything about them. Yet no
Republican leader grasped from

the outset the full portent of these
meetings.
President Truman himself signed
the Potsdam Agreement. And the
crisis in Berlin is its direct and
inevitable result. The partition of
Germany occurred then—in 1946.
not in 1948. The idiotic idea that
a defeated country could be gov
erned down to its courts, educa
tional system and economy by four
separate military governments—
one of which was a revolutionary
power, and each of which had diver
gent interests—without a clash aris
ing between them, was adopted
then. There was no indication' that
President Truman had the slightest
qualm about the document he had
signed. He returned home grin
ning.
Lack of Restraint Hit.
He grinned again when the
atomic bomb was dropped on Hiro
shima and Nagasaki.
On this occasion he l\ad all the
Republican leaders and Henry
Wallace with him. That Germany
already was knocked out, that
Japan was making peace overtures,
and that atomic energy was an in
calculable force did not lead the
President to exercise restraint.
Nor did he see that we would
damage our moral position and in
still world-wide fears by demon
strating that the United States re
garded the atomic bomb as a legiti
mate instrument of warfare. Nor
had he—nor the Republicans nor
Mr. Wallace—the imagination to
see that a country exclusively pos
sessing a decisive weapon in an
almost-won war needs only to
demonstrate it (during a 24-hour
truce) in an unpopulated area to
win the war and secure the confi
dence of all mankind.
Today the American bad con
science about Hiroshima paralyzes
the national will, as does nothing
else. But Mr. Truman actually re
ferred, in his St. Paul address, to
Hiroshima, among other things, as
an "act of faith"—of faith "in the
soul of man and hqman rights.’’
That was when I decided not to
vote for Mr. Truman, for he does
not display even hindsight. I would
like to vote for Mr. Thomas, who
foresaw the result of all these
policies, but it is hard—and even
harder because I am not a Socialist.
As I see it, the issue in the world
is not between socialism and capi
talism, but between civilization and
barbarism. On this issue I feel that
now, if ever, a yes must be a yes,
and a no a no.

Republicans Write Off Senate Seat;
Dewey Expected to Lose State
By Dorts Fleeson
OKLAHOMA CITY, Oct. 18.—
Republicans here have written off
their Senate race, which whittles
the party’s current Senate margin
to three, and
they also antic
ipate that Pres
ident Truman
will carry the
State by a less
er plurality.
But just in
case things
should be really
tough for the
GOP in the
Senate, with
bad news else
where matching
their own, they
have begun a
quiet investlga
