Kerr, the probable next Senator,
won all those friends In three ex
pensive contests—primary, run-off
and general election.
The grapevine has it that if any
real evidence is uncovered, the
Senate will be asked to hold up
Mr. Kerr’s seating as it will that
of Representative Lyndon Johnson
of Texas.
Although Oklahoma is Mrs.
Dewey’s home State, the Deweys
cannot help themselves or their
Senate nominee, Ross Rizley, very
much. First and foremost, this is
a Democratic State and the Demo
cratic Party still fits its somewhat
free-wheeling temper better than
the party of the Eightieth Con
gress. Public power, reclamation and
rural electrification, hallmarks of
the New Deal, have done much for
the State’s arid regions.
Rixley Can’t Help.
And, while Mrs. Dewey is the girl
who made good in the big city, her
home town of Sapulpa, which col
lected the funds for the musical
education leading to her successful
marriage, says she largely forgot
them until the Deweys hit the na
tional stage. In fact, Sapulpa some
times yields to the human tempta
tion to shout the above tidings.
Oklahomans add also that the cool
ish Gov. Dewey is not an Oklahoma
type.
Nor can Mr. Rizley help the
presidential nominee, as he is the
prototype of the unpopular Eighti
eth Congress, who identified himself
completely with the Martin-Halleck
Taber leadership and threw in a
rate-raising natural gas bill of his
own for good measure. His isola
tionist war record—he voted against
lend-lease, the draft, etc.—also is a
target.
In a desperate effort to retrieve
his fortunes, Mr. Rizley attempted
to capitalize on the States’ rights
issue in the Southern counties
which are Democratic bastions.
This lost him every Negro vote ir.
the cities and angered many Re
publicans. Gov. Dewey complained
about it, too, while Gov. Warren
said plainly that if he had known
that Mr. Rizley was doing this, he
would not have mentioned his name.
Expect to Keep Rizley Seat.
Republicans believe they will re
tain Mr. Rizley’s House seat and
re-elect Representative Schwabe.
Democrats contend they will beat
Mr. Schwabe with an anti-New
Deal Democrat. The probable
Democratic nominee for the Senate
in ’60—Representative Monroney—
is safe and so are the other fire
Democratic Congressmen.
Democrat Kerr is a popular for
mer Governor and millionaire oil
man. He is a tall and impressive

Colorado Is Expected
To Vote for Dewey
By Huge Majority
By Gould Lincoln
Star Staff Corratpondant
DENVER, Colo., Oct. 5.—Colo
rado has had a checkered career
in national politics, favoring
Democrats and Republicans in
turn. In the last two presi
dential elections it has gone Re
publican. This year is will plunk
for the Dewey-Warren ticket.
Democrats here continue to insist
they have a chance to win the
State for Preeident Truman, but
when they are asked, “Why should
Truman be a stronger candidate
against Dewey than was Roosevelt
four years ago. in the midst of a
war," they flounder around.
One of them came up with a sug
gestion that this year the veterans’
vote might turn the trick for their
candidate. They seemingly overlook
the fact that President Roosevelt,
in 1944, had the vote of the soldiers
who now are the veterans.
Republican Leaders Confident.
President Truman was here not
long ago, and drew a big open-air
crowd In the noon hour, which
tome of the more enthusiastic esti
mated at 100,000, but which compe
tent newspapermen cut down to
35.000. Further, and it may be sig
nificant, hundreds drifted away
when Mr. Truman had been speak
ing onlv a few minutes.
The Republican leaders have no
doubt about a Dewey victory. What
worries them are the contests for
Senator and Governor.
The senatorial seat, now held by
the miraculously successful Senator
"Ed” Johnson, Democrat, is a prize
of great importance in view of the
probable closeness of the political
makeup of the Upper House in the
next Congress. If the Republicans
can win this one, taking it away
from the Democrats, they are vir
tually sure of hanging on to the alim
margin they now have in the Senate.
Senator Johnson Popular.
Senator Johnson, former Governor
and for 12 years a Senator, is im
mensely popular. He has done favors,
big and little, for thousands of his
constituents. Republicans as well as
Democrats, he is almost as great a
letter writer as was Jim Farley
when Mr. Farley was running the
Democratic National Committee and
campaigns for President Roosevelt
Thousands of Republicans have
voted for Senator Johnson in the
past.
In 1942. when the Republicans
were beginning their return from
the depths into which they had
been plunged by the Roosevelt land
slides, they thought they had a
chance of defeating Senator John
son. He was up for re-election.
Their candidate was Gov. Ralph
Carr.
When the votes were counted. Sen
ator Johnson had won by approxi
mately 3,600 votes. Gov. Carr had
run far behind the Republican ticket
In strong Republican districts. This
was due in. part, but ohljj£aipfii*4o
the fact mat Gov. Carr had anfaj*
onized a good part of me Republican
organization.
In that same year, Senator Mini
kin, running for the short term Sen
ate seat to fill a vacancy caused by
the death of Senator Alva Adams-,
won out by 40,000 votes. SenatK
Millikin carried BoulddrCountyWf
4.000 votes and Gov. f'.or* Tftrt'lf gy
*00. There were other equallyg!fer
ing instances of Republican support
of Senator Johnson.
So the Democrats are confident
Senator Johnson will win again.
He may well do so. However, the
Republicans have not given up hope.
They figure it this way. If Gov.
Dewey could carry- Colorado against
President Roosevelt by 34,000 votes,
as he did, he will carry the State
against President Truman by a
minimum of 50.000, and they are
hoping for 75,000. In that event
they figure the National ticket will
pull their senatorial candidate, Will
P. Nicholson, along with it to vic
tory.
Bet on Lead of 50,000.
Professional gamblers are betting
even money today that Gov. Dewey's
lead will be 50.00&—and mere are
few takers. A State-wide poll, taken
In September by Research Services,
Inc., for the Denver Post, showed
Gov. Dewey with 50 per cent of the
vote, President Truman with 47 and
Henry A. Wallace with 3 per cent.
This does not give Gov. Dewey as
wide a margin as the gamblers and
political observers expect.
The poll, however, showed a lot of
voters answering they had not made
up their minds. If reports are cor
rect, Gov. Dewey is not going down
hill in Colorado. This may account
for the discrepancy.
On the other hand, Mr. Wallace
and his Progressive Party are not
doing well. They are dogged here
by the Communist blight which has
caught up with them in other
States. The Democrats’ greatest
danger in the presidential race is
that many of the voters who are
unenthusiastic about Truman will
not vote.
Newcomer to Politics.
The Republican opposing Senator
Johnson is a newcomer to politics.
Mr. Nicholson is a 48-year-old vet
eran of two wars. He is a success
ful businessman. Physically, he is
almost in the giant class, standing
6 feet 4'2 inches. He announced
himself a candidate for the sen
atorial nomination last year, a pro
cedure almost unheard of in this
State. He has been hard at it ever
since, and in the primary he de
feated former Gov. Vivian.
Issues in the senatorial campaign
are Mr. Nicholson's internationalism
and strong position for increased
national defense, and Senator
Johnson's rather isolationist stand.
The Taft-Hartley Act also has been
made an issue.
Republics hold three of Colorado's
House seats. They are trying hard
to hold what they havd and take
the fourth, the seat in the Denver
district, now occupied by Represent
ative Carroll, Democrat. The prob
abilities are they will re-elect the
Republican incumbents. They will
have a tough job in defeating Mr.
Carroll. The Republican candidate
against him is Christopher Cusack,
an advertising man.
Hill Appears Easy Winner.
Representative Hill, Republican,
will carry the second district in a
walk. Both Representatives Cheno
weth and Rockwell in the third and
four districts are likely winners.
Denver has been a Democratic
stronghold. Mr. Carroll, whose story
sounds like an Horatio Alger tale,
is popular. He is a 100 per cent
New Deal Democrat. At 16 years
of age he pried his way into the
Army and served in World War I.
When he returned he went on the
police force, studied law at night
and became district attorney. Then
he was elected to Congress.
The Progressives recently an
nounced their support of Mr. Car
roll. which he immediately rejected.
The Communist issue is red hot
here, particularly as three Reds who
refused to testify before a grand
jury have been placed in jail.
Gov. William Lee Knous, Demo
crat, is another white hope of the
party. The Democratic leaders
count on re-electing him because
be, like Senator Johnson, is greatly
popular. Tne Republicans have
nominated David A. Hammll. a
cattleman and rancher, and they
have £y no means given up hope
of unieating Gov. Knous.

Gen. McLain to Speak
At Chest Kickoff Rally
Lt. Gen. Ray McLain, chief of
information of the Army, will be
principal speaker at the kickoff
breakfast of the Alexandria Com
munity Chest campaign at 8 am.
tomorrow at the George Mason
Hotel.
All campaign workers for the
Chest have Deen invited to the
breakfast, which is being sponsored
by the Alexander Junior Chamber
of Commerce.
At 8:30 p.m. tomorrow In the
Alexander Recreation Center, Pen
dleton and Patrick streets, the resi
dential 2 division will hold a rally.
Warren Making Tour
Of West Virginia to
Bolster Revercomb
•y th« Auvciatad Press
EN ROUTE WITH WARREN TO
CHARLESTON. W. Va„ Oct. 5
Oov. Warren of California. Repub
lican vice presidential candidate,
moved into West Virginia’s hot
senatorial race today to carry the
prestige of the Republican national
ticket to the support of Senator
Revercomb.
Hie California Governor was ac
companied from Pittsburgh on the
day’s West Virginia campaign swing
by Senator Revercomb, Walter S.
Hallanan, Republican national com
mitteeman, and West Virginia's top
Republican leaders.
With Republicans stepping up
their State and district campaigns
to insure control of the next Con
gress, particular Interest attached
to the West Virginia race in which
Senator Revercomb is engaged by
former Senator Matthew M. Neely,
the Democratic nominee.
Senator Revercomb has been at
odds with Gov. Dewey, the Republi
can standard bearer. Gov. Dewey
asked the West Virginian to modify
his position on the displaced per
sons bill then before Congress, and
Senator Revercomb refused. Gov.
Dewey's resultant coolness has had
its political repercussions in West
Virginia.
Gov. Warren today, however, had
on his schedule a series of appear
ances starting at Grafton at 8 a m.
and continuing with Clarksburg,
Parkersburg, Point Pleasant and
ending up at Charleston tonight in
a major campaign speech.
At Charleston, the vice president
ial candidate will be introduced by
Senator Revercomb when he ap
pears in municipal auditorium at
9:30 p.m. A State radio network
will carry his talk.
Last night in Pittsburgh. Gov.
Warren called for full production
as the only answer to the ‘twin
challenge” of inflation and the cost
of arming the United States against
aggression.
"The cause of this inflation is
deeply imbedded and widespread.
It cannot be reached with paper
rules and regulations. It can be
reached only with machines, bull
dozers, steam shovels, plows, rails,
plus organized energy and know
how
"By this I mean nothing more or
less than all-out production equal
to our every need.”

Barkley Says 6.0. P.
Is in for Surprises
On Election Day
ty Hi* Associated Pros*
EN ROUTE WEST WITH SENA
TOR BARKLEY, Oct. 6.—Senator
Barkley pushed to the foot of the
Rockies in his quest for Democratic
votes.
As he left the Grain Belt after
two days of Intensive airborne cam
paigning, the Democratic vice presi
dential nominee expressed confi
dence that "the Republicans are in
for some surprises" on election day.
In both Minnesota, where he spoke
Saturday, and Iowa, where he ad
dressed large crowds yesterday at
Mason City and Ottumwa, the Ken
tuckian has met with enthusiastic
receptions.
His private talks with party lead
ers in both States have convinced
Senator Barkley personally that the
Democrats will pick up Senate seats
in both and that President Truman
and himself will win Iowa’s 10 and
Minnesota's 11 electoral votes.
“I am elated over the prospects
in the two States,” the Senator told
newsmen as he left for Denver and
a speech at Greeley, Colo.
The Democratic high command is
counting heavily on winning two
Senate seats at stake in Minnesota
and Iowa in the party’s bid to re
gain control of the Senate. Both
States now are represented by Re
publican Senators.
Senator Barkley’s foray into Colo
rado is scheduled for a dual pur
pose. It is to whip up support for
the entire National ticket and for
Democratic Senator Johnson.

are to be made between Barcelona
and Pari*.

South China's Death Toll
In Typhoon Put at 800
■y *K« All»ciat»d frtf
CANTON, China, Oct. 8. —Press
dispatches reaching here today said
800 persons were killed when a
typhoon struck Leichow Peninsula
and the Pakhoi, Kwangchouwan
and Holhow areas September 27.
The reports said 800 persons
perished when the typhoon sank
more than 100 fishing boats near
Leichow. Another 200 were killed
when 70 salt-laden sailing junks
were destroyed near Pakhoi.
Heavy damage and casualties also
were inflicted by torrential rains
which inundated the area around
Hainan Island.

Royall Congratulates Press
For Free Flow of Facts
The Nation’s press was congratu
lated today by Secretary U the
Army Royall in a statement mark
ing observance of National News
paper Week, October 1-8.
“Pew things are more important
to the peoples of a democracy than
a free and unadulterated flow of
facts,’* he said.
“In America this precious privi
lege was early incorporated in the
Bill of Rights, and freedom of the
press has always been one of the
most zealously guarded of gifts,"
he continued. "This week through
out the Nation, National News
paper Week is being celebrated un
der the slogan: ‘Your right to know
is the key to all your liberties”*
“In these days when people 1
throughout the world are thirsting*
for peace this slogah is apt, indeed.”

Ever sip cider through a straw?
Works fine... with a few straws and a
little fair play. But it’s no party when
one person sips more than his share.
What’s true of a party is also true of
a party line. When everybody works
together, there’s plenty of telephone
service for all. But when someone
makes a series of calls one right after
the other, it deprives the other fellow
of his fair share of service.
Most folks on party lines realize
this. They “space’* their calls so that
others have a chance to use the line.
They make it a point to answer the
telephone promptly. And they never
interrupt when the line is in use,
except in a case of emergency.
When you do these neighborly
things, you’re helping yourself and
others to better telephone service.

Probers' Spy Report
Is Political Slander on
Truman, Barkley Says
By th« Associated Press
NIAGARA FALLS, N. Y., Sept. 30.
Senator Barkley says the House Un
American Activities Committee's re
cent report on spies in the Govern
ment is a slander on President Tru
man “brought forward for political
purposes.’’
The Democratic vice presidential
nominee said last night the commit
tee in its report was "undertaking
to show that President Roosevelt
and President Truman connived at
the infiltration of Government by
Communists.”
Senator Barkley spoke to about
300 at the high school auditorium in
the last address of his upstate New
York campaign. He spoke earlier
at Buffalo and North Tonawanda.
GOP Assailed for ‘^Reaction.”
The Senator said here that the
Republican Party in the past has
put the Nation into “despondency
and despair” and has been the par
ty of “reaction and fatalism,” add
ing:
“The Republican Party never
knows where it is going. It only
knows where it has been.”
Senator Barkley suggested “as one
songster to another," that Gov.
Dewey, Republican presidential nom
inee, adopt as his theme song “I
Have Plenty of Nothing.”
During the administration of
Harding, Coolidge and Hoover, Sen
ator Barkley said, “not a single law
was enacted that could be called
constructive, progressive or liberal,”
and the GOP “threw' our Nation
into fits.”
Begins Western Tour Tomorrow.
“I understand the Governor of
New Yorks has a great voice,” the
Democratic vice presidential nom
inee said. “I understand that the
Governor of New York used to sing
in a choir. I don’t hold that against
him. I admire him for it. I used to
think I was sort of a baritone my
self.”
It was then that he suggested
that Gov. Dewey adopt the theme
song.
Senator Barkley left by special
plane for Washington. Tomorrow
he begins a speaking tour that will
take him to the West Coast.
Royall Says Wallace
Hurts Hope for Peace
By the Associated Press
ST. LOUIS, Sept. 30.—Secretary
ol the Army Royall says that Henry
Wallace has hurt the chances for
peace.
Secretary Royall also told the 70th
National Guard Association con
vention last night that he doesn't
think war is imminent, but that
the decision of war or peace rests
with a small group of Communist
leaders.
Speaking of the Progressive
Party's presidential candidate, he
said:
"There can be no doubt in the
mind of any reasonable man that
preachments of one of the candi
dates for the presidency has
strengthened the hands and wills
of our enemies and has added to
difficulty in Berlin.
"Henry Wallace has hurt chances
for peace, not helped it.”
Secretary Royall said that "de
termination of war is not in our!
hands, but in the hands of a few
despotic and powerful men in the
Kremlin and'it is timing only, notj
the ultimate pry-pose of these men,!
that is uncertain.”
Pardon for Mee's Slayer
Reported Opposed by Court
By the Associated Press
HAVANA, Sept. 30.—The Havana
Criminal Court yesterday opposed
a presidential pardon for Patricia
(Satira) Schmidt on the ground
that she has shown no signs of
repentance for shooting her lover,
Jack Lester Mee.
The court, which sentenced the
Toledo (Ohio) dancer to 15 years
in prison for killing the Chicago
attorney aboard his yacht Satira
in April, 1947, expressed its view’s
in reply to a request from the Min
istry of Justice.
It is usual for the Criminal Court
to report unfavorably on all pardon
requests and for this reason the
court's opposition is not expected
to influence the decision of Presi
dent Ramon Grau San Martin.

French Reds Back Arms Plan.
France’s Communists joined those
of Britain in backing Russia's dis
armament proposals.
The party's political bureau is
sued a communique thanking the
Soviet delegation for proposing a
one-third reduction in the arms of
the major powers and a ban on
atomic weapons. The British Com- j
munist Executive Committee issued j
a similar statement Tuesday.
“The people of France,” the com
munique said, “denounce the con
stitution of an anti-Soviet military
bloc led by American imperialists
and grouping under the command
of English Marshal Montgomery the
armed forces of Great Britain,
France, Belgium, Holland and Lux
embourg.”
This referred to the Brussels
Alliance defense plans.
“The people of France will not
and will never wage war against the
Soviet Union,” the statement said.

brought into the Federal courts to!
keep peace in the coal pits.
Paul Douglas of Chicago, Demo
cratic candidate for the Senate seat
held by Wayland Brooks, Republi
can, of Illinois and Mr. Truman
both addressed a crowd this morn- j
ing at Mt. Vernon, where the Presi- j
dent left his train to travel by auto-1
mobile.
In a brief talk at Benton before
6.000 to 7,000 persons, the President
attacked Congress for passing the
Taft-Hartley law. He also declared:
“I’ve been trying to get Congress J
to help me fight inflation, but^
they’re not interested."
At West Frankfort, he placed a
wreath on a monument to soldier1
dead in the presence of a crowd of'
10,000.
At Herrin, a crowd estimated at
12.000 heard the Chief Executive
characterize the issues of the cam
paign as “simply a fight between
the economic royalists and the i
people.”
Mr. Truman spoke at Southern
Illinois Normal University's Shryock
Auditorium at Carbondale. An es
timated 4.500 crowded the audi
torium and several thousand more
heard the speech over loud speakers
on the campus outside.
Mr. Truman asked for Democratic
votes to stop “big business” Re
publicans from continuing to “nail
the American consumer to the wall
with spikes of greed.”
Offers "Era of Hope.
He offered them a choice between
"another era of fear under the
Republican reactionaries and "an
other era of hope with liberal de- j
mocracy.”
The President assailed the Re
publican-controlled Eightieth Con
gress as "the puppet" of big busi
ness. and the real estate, power and
grain speculators’ lobbies and
rapped its passage of the "reac
tionary Taft-Hartley Act” over his
veto.
“Instead of improving the Wag
ner Act as I recommended,” Mr.
Truman declared, "they cut and
hacked away at the workers' newly
won rights. The Taft-Hartley Act
is the way backward, not the way
forward. That’s the .Republican
way.”
The President said the Democrats
have raised the income of the
farmer to 10 times i\hat it was in
1932, haved millions of home and
farm owners from eviction, rescued
the banking system, established de
posit insurance and "brought truth
to the sale of securities.”
Will Speak in Albany.
Tomorrow he will be traveling the
bituminous fields of Kentucky and
West Virginia and within the next
10 days the anthracite miners of
Pennsylvania will get a taste of Tru
man campaigning. On the swing,
which was just announced yester
day, the President also will drop by
the door of his presidential foe. Gov.
Thomas E. Dewey, when he talks at
Albany, and he also will move
through the upstate New York dis
trict of Representative Taber, Re
publican, chairman of the House
Appropriations Committee, whom
Mr. Truman frequently has charac
terized as a mossback in his current
push against the GOP.
The President moved into Illinois
today after winding up two days of
rugged campaigning in Oklahoma
yesterday with a dozen talks and a
couple of parades, and then topping
that off with more oratory in South
west Missouri last night.
After boarding his train again at
Carmi this afternoon, the President
goes to Evansville, Ind., for a talk,
then over to Louisville where he
speaks tonight (10 p.m. EST) at the
Armory.
Domestic Policies Topic Tonight.
Charles G. Ross, presidential sec
retary, said the Louisville speech
will deal with domestic policies. He
said it will hit hard on high prices
and housing and get into the sub
ject of monopolies.
The President is nearing the end
of the long swing to the West Coast
enthusiastic over the crowds that
have turned out to greet him. Fre
quently ne referred to that as his
train rolled through Oklahoma and
Missouri yesterday and last night,
flailing away at the GOP. telling of
the prosperity he claimed as a
Democratic accomplishment, and
calling for the election of a straignt
Democratic ticket in November.
At Tulsa, Okla., where the Presi
dent had one of the largest turn
outs of his trip, he called bluntly
for the defeat of Representative
George B. Schwabe. Republican, of
the First District, declaring “He has
one of the worst records in Congress
and he’s got a brother up in Mis
souri with the same kind of record.”
The reference was to Representative
Max Schwabe, Republican, of Mis
souri’s Seccgid District.
Tulsa, w'hich is in rock-ribbed
Republican territory, had never seen
a President parade through the
streets before President Roosevelt
make a quickie war plant inspection
tour there in 1943, and reacted ac
cordingly.
Police estimated that about 100,
000 persons lined the streets in the
business district as the presidential
motorcade moved through in late
afternoon under a shower of ticker
tape, and an audience of around
17.000 was on hand at Skelly
Stadium, the athletic field of Tulsa
University, where Mr. Truman spoke.
A holiday had been declared in
the schools, and there was a liberal
sprinkling of juveniles in the gath
ering.
The Tulsa speech also brought
the President face to face with
"Old Man Moore,” as he char
acterized ihe retiring Republican
Senator Moore of Oklahoma in a
talk Tuesday when he said the leg
islator never was any good.
Senator Moore was on the offi
cial committee to greet the Presi
dent, and as the Truman party
moved through the line at Union
Station, the Senator stepped out,
held out his hand and said, “Hello,
Harry. The President grasped the
proffered hand and responded.
“Hello. Senator. How are you?
It’s nice ta see you.”
As the committee followed the
President, the Senator went along,
and was on the platform when Mr.
Truman made his 102d attack on
the GOP since leaving Washington
September 17.
A little earlier, the President took
part in another parade and demon
stration at Muskogee, where a large
part of the male population now
is wearing beards in a centennial
celebration commemorating the set
tling of the five civilized tribes in
old Indian Territory.

warning to the world not to misin

Gov. Dewey took a detour of sev
eral hundred miles into the north
ern part of Montana to speak at
Great Falls, and he found an en
thusiastic crowd of 4,000 waiting in

Backs State Candidates.
At Great Falls he appeared for

picked by the Republicans to oppose

paigning across Montana for nearly

tana because the senatorial candi

ate and for the re-election of Gov.

Ford will for a third term as Gov
ernor. Gov. Dewey went to bat hard

people closer together so that they
can realize their great future andj

And let no dictator or trigger- j
happy militarist anywhere make
any mistake about that purpose.”
Turning to economic problems at
home, Gov. Dewey promised that, if
elected, his administration would
“reject the defeatist doctrine that
our country is fated to swing wildly

confidence that the depression con
dtions of the 1930s can be avoided.

<9:30 p.m. EST) on a Nation-wide

Close U. S. Co-operation
With Alliance Expected
By Iht Associated Press
American officials yesterday fore
cast the closest possible co-operation
with the newly formed military com
mand of the Western European
Union. They said, however, that it
would be months before this country

PAPER LEFT BY PEARY—This brief report by Comdr. Robert E. Peary was found by a Navy
and Coast Guard task force last summer in a cairn on bleak Cape Sheridan, 450 miles south of
the North Pole. The report, found in a whisky bottle, tells of the arrival of Comdr. Peary's
ship, the Roosevelt, at the cape In 1905 and the discovery of papers left there 20 years before
by a British expedition. —Official Navy Photo..
Warren Declares U. S.
Cannot Afford to Keep
Embittered Regime
By the Associated Press
NEW YORK, Sept. 30.—Gov. Earl
Warren says New Jersey—which
four times gave majorities to Frank
lin D. Roosevelt—will go Repub
lican this year.'
Before a cheering, near-capacity
audience in Newark, N. J„ last
night, the GOP vice presidential
nominee said he now is confiident
the Dewey-Warren ticket will carry
the State.
From Newark Gov. Warren came
back to New York City for a round
of party gatherings and a speech
tonight.
Gov. Warren told his Newark
audience of almost 4,000 that after
“looking over” a score of States he
is sure Gov. Thomas E. Dewey will
“carry the Nation.”
The United States, the Califor
nian declared, "cannot afford to
continue a shattered, complaining
administration at the helm.”
He said a unified leadership is
required if America is to show it is

"No real progress can possibly be
made as long as we have a national
administration that is not only di
vided but bitter—an administration
so tired that it is losing itself in
abuse.”
Gov. Warren, speaking in the
heart of a heavily industrialized
area, said, “We must show that we
can develop a sound, co-operative
relationship between workers and
management. If our laws affecting
the collective bargaining process are,
after experience, found to be un»
necessary or oppressive, then they
can and should be changed.” He
made no direct reference to the
Taft-Hartley Act.
“We must make sure,” Gov. War
ren added, “that this great, dynamie
economy of ours, which has been
built through the energy of a free
people, does not grow in such a way
that it grinds down the human be
ings who are a part of it—both as
workers and consumers.”
